The derived noun ~Anpassung~ ("adaptation") is the standard German translation of Darwin's concept of adaptation — a Latin-rooted verb with an Arabic-influenced French intermediary, now wearing a Germanic prefix and suffix to discuss evolutionary biology. Cognate with Dutch ~aanpassen~.
en.anregen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~regen~:

- ~regen~: From MHG ~regen~ ("to make protrude, to erect, to excite, to move"), from Proto-Germanic *~hragjaną~ ("to make stand, to wake up, to incite"), a causative formation from *~hreganą~ ("to jut out"), from PIE *~krek-~ ("to project, to protrude").
- ~an-~: From OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana-~, from PIE *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds a sense of initiating or stimulating action.

The verb ~anregen~ means "to stimulate, to inspire, to suggest," carrying a sense of setting something into motion intellectually or physically. The causative origin is visible: Proto-Germanic *~hragjaną~ meant "to cause to stand up or project," and this evolved through Middle High German into the more abstract sense of stirring someone to action or thought. The noun ~Anregung~ ("suggestion, stimulus, inspiration") is widely used in both casual and academic German. The related verb ~sich regen~ ("to stir, to move") preserves the older physical sense. In modern German, ~anregend~ ("stimulating") can describe a conversation, a cup of coffee, or an idea equally well, while the homograph ~Regen~ ("rain") is an entirely unrelated word from a different Proto-Germanic root (*~regną~).
en.anrufen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~rufen~:

- ~rufen~: From MHG ~ruofen~, from OHG ~ruofan~ (also ~hruofan~), from Proto-West Germanic *~hropan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hropanan~. The Proto-Germanic origin is unclear; it may be distantly related to *~hrothiz~ ("glory, fame"), but the exact connection is debated. Cognate with Dutch ~roepen~, English dialectal ~roop~.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana~ ("on, at"), from PIE *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). This prefix adds a directional quality, turning a general shout into a call directed at someone.

The most common modern use of ~anrufen~ is "to telephone," a meaning that emerged only in the 20th century with the spread of telephone technology. Before that, ~anrufen~ meant simply "to call out to someone" or "to invoke" (as in a legal or religious context: ~das Gericht anrufen~, "to appeal to the court"). The initial ~h-~ in OHG ~hruofan~ (reflecting Proto-Germanic *~hr-~) was lost during the transition to Middle High German, as happened with many words beginning with ~hr-~, ~hl-~, ~hn-~. Interestingly, ~rufen~ historically wavered between strong and weak conjugation; weak forms persisted until the 19th century, and the lack of umlaut in the present tense (~du rufst~, not *~du rüfst~) is a relic of this weak-verb past.
en.ansagen:
From Middle High German ~anesagen~, from Old High German ~anasagēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~anasaggjan~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~sagen~:

- ~sagen~: From MHG ~sagen~, from OHG ~sagēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~saggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sagjaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~sekʷ-~ ("to say"). Cognate with English ~say~, Dutch ~zeggen~, Danish ~sige~, Swedish ~säga~.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~an~ ("on, at"), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix conveys direction -- saying something ~to~ someone, making an announcement.

The PIE root *~sekʷ-~ also produced Latin ~inquit~ ("says") and Old Irish ~insce~ ("speech"). In modern German, ~ansagen~ has found new life beyond its traditional meaning of "to announce": it is widely used in the colloquial expression ~angesagt sein~ ("to be trendy, in vogue"), a usage that emerged in the late 20th century. The word ~Ansager~ ("announcer") was the standard term for a television presenter before the English loanword ~Moderator~ largely replaced it.
en.anschauen: Formed from prefix ~an-~ (from PIE *~h₂en~, "on, onto") and ~schauen~, from MHG ~schouwen~, from OHG ~scouwōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~skawwōną~ ("to look, see, watch"), from PIE *~(s)kewh₁-~ ("to heed, look, feel, take note of"). The prefix transforms "to look" into "to look at" with directed, attentive focus: to gaze upon something. A near-synonym ~ansehen~ (~sehen~ from MHG ~sëhen~, from OHG ~sëhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwaną~, from PIE *~sekʷ-~ ("to see, notice")) can be interchangeable, but ~anschauen~ tends to carry a slightly more contemplative or visual-aesthetic sense — one ~schaut~ a painting or a landscape ~an~. Note the German idiom ~Schau an!~ ("Well, look at that!") — a trace of the older sense of directed, surprised gaze.
en.anschließen:
From Middle High German ~aneslieȥen~, equivalent to ~an-~ + ~schließen~:

- ~schließen~: From MHG ~slieȥen~, from OHG ~slioȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sleutaną~, from PIE *~klew-~ ("to close, shut").
- ~an-~: From PIE *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds the sense of attachment or connection.

The semantic range of ~anschließen~ runs from the physical ("to connect, to plug in" — ~den Computer anschließen~) to the social ("to join" — ~sich einer Gruppe anschließen~, "to join a group") to the temporal ("to follow" — ~anschließend~, "subsequently"). All these meanings share the image of closing something onto something else — locking one thing to another. The reflexive ~sich anschließen~ is particularly productive: one can attach oneself to a group, an opinion, or a movement. The derived adjective ~anschließend~ ("subsequent, following") has become one of German's most common temporal connectors, its etymological sense of "locking on after" faded to a mere "and then." Cognate with Dutch ~aansluiten~.
en.ansehen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~sehen~:

- ~sehen~: From MHG ~sëhen~, from OHG ~sëhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwaną~, from PIE *~sekʷ-~ ("to see, notice"). Cognate with Dutch ~zien~, English ~see~, Gothic ~saihwan~.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana-~, from PIE *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). This separable prefix directs the base verb's action toward a goal or surface.

The ~an-~ prefix gives ~sehen~ a focused, directed quality: where ~sehen~ is "to see" in general, ~ansehen~ is "to look at" something specifically. The reflexive ~sich ansehen~ ("to watch, to look at for oneself") extends this to viewing as an activity — watching a film, inspecting a site. The noun ~Ansehen~ ("reputation, standing") developed from the sense of "how one is looked upon" — social regard crystallized from the directed gaze. Cognate with Dutch ~aanzien~ ("to regard; reputation"), which preserves exactly the same semantic range.
en.ansetzen:
From Middle High German ~anesetzen~, from Old High German ~anasezzen~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjaną~ ("to set, to place"), a causative of *~sitjaną~ ("to sit"), from PIE *~sodéyeti~, from the root *~sed-~ ("to sit"). Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~, Yiddish ~זעצן~.
- ~an-~: From OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana-~ ("on, onto"), from PIE *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). This prefix adds a sense of attachment, initiation, or application.

The relationship between ~setzen~ and ~sitzen~ mirrors English ~set~ and ~sit~ exactly: both pairs reflect the Proto-Germanic causative pattern where *~-jan-~ turns "to sit" into "to make sit, to place." ~Ansetzen~ is remarkably polysemous in modern German: it can mean "to put on" (a pot), "to schedule" (a meeting), "to begin" (an approach), "to form" (rust), or "to prepare" (a mixture). Compare English ~onset~, which shares the same etymological structure (~on~ + ~set~) and originally meant "an attack" before broadening to "beginning." The PIE root *~sed-~ is one of the most well-attested roots in historical linguistics, producing Latin ~sedēre~ ("to sit"), English ~sit~, ~seat~, ~saddle~, and ~sedan~.
en.ansiedeln:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~siedeln~:

- ~siedeln~: From MHG ~sidelen~, from OHG ~sidalen~, derived from ~sidila~ ("seat"), cognate with Old English ~setl~ ("seat, settle"). Related to ~sitzen~ ("to sit") and hence ultimately to English ~settle~, though not a direct cognate of it.
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~an~ ("on, at"), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds a sense of establishing or fixing in place.

The connection between sitting and settling is ancient and deeply embedded across the Indo-European family: to settle somewhere is, at its most basic, to sit down and stay. OHG ~sidila~ ("seat") developed into ~Siedlung~ ("settlement"), and the verb ~siedeln~ preserves this link between physical rest and permanent habitation. English ~settle~ followed a parallel path from Old English ~setl~ ("seat, place to sit") to the verb ~setlan~ ("to place, establish"). The compound ~ansiedeln~ emphasizes the purposeful act of establishing a settlement, often used reflexively (~sich ansiedeln~, "to settle down").
en.ansprechen:
~an-~ + ~sprechen~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~sprechen~:

- ~sprechen~: From MHG ~sprëchen~, from OHG ~sprëhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekaną~, probably from PIE *~spregʰ-~ ("to crackle, make noise").
- ~an-~: From PIE *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds the sense of direction toward someone or something, initiating contact.

~Ansprechen~ means "to address, to speak to; to appeal to; to respond to (treatment)." The prefix ~an-~ directs the act of speaking toward a target — one ~spricht jemanden an~ by approaching and initiating speech. The secondary sense "to appeal to" (~das spricht mich an~, "that appeals to me") developed from the idea of speech landing on and engaging a listener. The medical sense — ~auf ein Medikament ansprechen~ ("to respond to a medication") — is a 20th-century extension of the same logic: the body "responds" when the treatment "speaks to" it. The PIE root *~spregʰ-~ is onomatopoeic — crackling into speech — making ~ansprechen~ ultimately an echo of the very sound of language itself. Cognate with Dutch ~aanspreken~.
en.anstellen:
From MHG ~anestellen~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand").
- ~an-~: From PIE *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). This separable prefix adds the sense of placing at, turning on, or setting to work.

~Anstellen~ is one of German's most versatile verbs: "to employ" (~jemanden anstellen~), "to turn on" (~das Wasser anstellen~), "to queue up" (~sich anstellen~), and "to behave, to make a fuss" (~sich anstellen~ in its colloquial sense: ~Stell dich nicht so an!~, "Don't make such a fuss!"). The employment sense is the source of ~Angestellte(r)~ ("employee"), one of the most common nouns in German labor law. The PIE root *~stel-~ is enormously productive: it gave Latin ~stāre~ ("to stand"), whence English ~state~, ~station~, ~statue~, and ~install~.
en.anstreben:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~streben~:

- ~streben~: From MHG ~strëben~, from OHG ~strëbēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stribēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~stribāną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)ter(h₁)-~ ("to be stiff, to exert"), the same root that produced Proto-Germanic *~staraz~ ("stiff"). Cognate with Dutch ~streven~ ("to strive"), English ~strive~ (via Old French ~estriver~, itself borrowed from a Germanic source).
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~an~ ("on, at"), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix directs the striving toward a specific goal.

English ~strive~ took a scenic route back to its Germanic origins: it entered English from Old French ~estriver~ ("to quarrel, contend"), which had been borrowed from a Frankish Germanic source related to OHG ~strëbēn~. So ~anstreben~ and ~strive~ are cousins who went on very different journeys before ending up with similar meanings. The PIE root *~(s)ter-~ ("to be stiff, rigid") also produced English ~stare~, ~stark~, ~stern~, and ~stereo~ (via Greek ~stereós~, "solid") -- all sharing the core notion of rigidity or firmness.
en.antreten:
~an-~ + ~treten~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~treten~:

- ~treten~: From MHG ~trëten~, from OHG ~trëtan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tredan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trudaną~ ("to tread, to step on"). Cognate with English ~tread~.
- ~an-~: From PIE *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds the sense of stepping toward, stepping up to, or stepping into position.

~Antreten~ covers a wide semantic range unified by the image of stepping forward. In its military sense, it means "to fall in, to line up for muster" — the sergeant's bark ~antreten!~ commands soldiers into formation. In sports and politics, it means "to compete, to run" (~gegen einen Gegner antreten~, "to compete against an opponent"; ~zur Wahl antreten~, "to run for election"). With a direct object, it means "to begin" a duty or journey (~eine Reise antreten~, "to set out on a journey"; ~ein Amt antreten~, "to take office"; ~ein Erbe antreten~, "to accept an inheritance"). And in its most physical sense, it means "to kick-start" a motorcycle by stepping on the starter. The derived noun ~Antritt~ ("commencement, start") appears in formal compounds: ~Antrittsrede~ ("inaugural address"), ~Antrittsbesuch~ ("introductory visit"). The metaphor of stepping forward to begin something, to face a challenge, or to take one's place runs through every usage.
en.antworten: From Middle High German ~antwürten~, from Old High German ~antwurten~, from Proto-Germanic *~andawurdijaną~, a denominative of *~andawurdiją~ ("answer"), itself from *~anda-~ ("against, in return") + *~wurdiją~ ("speech"), a derivative of *~wurdą~ ("word"). The archaic prefix *~anda-~ ("against, in return") is the same element that appears in Gothic ~anda-~ and Old English ~and-~. By surface analysis, the modern verb looks like a denominative of ~Antwort~ ("answer") + ~-en~, which is how most contemporary speakers parse it. The fascinating parallel with English ~answer~ is not accidental: English ~answer~ comes from Old English ~andswaru~ ("a sworn statement against [a charge]"), from ~and-~ ("against") + ~swaru~ ("affirmation, swearing"). Both ~antworten~ and ~answer~ contain the same ancient prefix meaning "against, in return," and both refer to speech made in response to speech — though German built on *~wurdą~ ("word") while English built on *~swarōną~ ("to swear"). The Proto-Germanic *~anda-~ is cognate with Greek ~ἀντί~ (~antí~, "against, opposite"), which gives English ~anti-~. All three descend from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, front, opposite"), whose comparative form *~h₂énteros~ ("the one facing, the other of two") produced Proto-Germanic *~anþeraz~ — the ancestor of both German ~ander~ ("other") and English ~other~. In English, the nasal was lost before the fricative by the Ingvaeonic nasal spirant law (*~anþeraz~ → *~āþeraz~ → OE ~ōþer~ → ~other~), while German preserved it (~ander~). So ~antworten~, Greek ~anti-~, German ~ander~, and English ~other~ are all siblings from the same PIE root — the one about facing, fronting, and opposing. Cognate with Dutch ~antwoorden~, Afrikaans ~antwoord~.
en.anweisen:
From Middle High German ~anewīsen~. Compound of ~an-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wīsen~, from OHG ~wīsen~ (weak verb until the seventeenth or eighteenth century), a West and North Germanic derivation from the adjective ~weise~ ("wise"), from Proto-Germanic *~wīsaz~, from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see, to know"). Cognate with English ~wise~, Dutch ~wijs~, and related to English ~wit~, ~wisdom~, Latin ~vidēre~ ("to see"), and Sanskrit ~veda~ ("knowledge").
- ~an-~: From OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana-~, from PIE *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). The prefix adds a directional or purposive sense.

The verb ~anweisen~ means "to instruct, to direct, to assign, to transfer (money)." The semantic chain runs from "seeing" (PIE) through "knowing" (Germanic) to "showing the way" (~weisen~) to "directing someone toward a task" (~anweisen~). The noun ~Anweisung~ ("instruction, directive") is ubiquitous in bureaucratic and technical German. The PIE root *~weyd-~ is the source of an astonishing range of descendants: Latin ~vidēre~ ("to see") gave English ~video~, ~vision~, and ~evidence~; Sanskrit ~Veda~ ("sacred knowledge") named the oldest Hindu scriptures; and Germanic *~wīsaz~ produced ~wise~, ~wisdom~, and the German ~-weise~ suffix meaning "in the manner of." The financial sense of ~anweisen~ ("to transfer money") derives from directing funds toward a recipient.
en.anwenden: Formed from prefix ~an-~ (from PIE *~h₂en~, "on, onto") and ~wenden~, from MHG ~wenden~, from OHG ~wenten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wandijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wandijaną~ ("to turn"), from PIE *~wendʰ-~ ("to turn, wind, braid"). Compare Middle Low German ~anwenden~. The prefix adds the sense of directing or turning something toward a purpose: to ~wenden~ (turn) something ~an~ (onto) a task. The English verb ~apply~ (from Latin ~applicare~, "to fold toward") performs exactly the same semantic work by different means.
en.anzeigen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~zeigen~:

- ~zeigen~: From MHG ~zeigen~, from OHG ~zeigōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taigōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taikōną~, from PIE *~deyḱ-~ ("to point out, show"). Cognate with Dutch ~tonen~, Latin ~dīcō~ ("to say, show"), Greek ~deiknúō~ ("to show").
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an-~, from OHG ~ana-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana-~, from PIE *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). This separable prefix directs the base verb's action toward a recipient or surface.

The ~an-~ prefix sharpens the sense of ~zeigen~ from "to show" into "to indicate, point at, display." In legal German, ~anzeigen~ specifically means "to report to authorities" — to point someone out to the state — giving the word a charged legal register absent from the neutral ~zeigen~. The noun ~Anzeige~ covers both "advertisement" (a directed display) and "police report" (a directed accusation), a semantic duality that reflects the verb's core gesture of pointing something out to a recipient. Cognate with Dutch ~aangeven~.
en.anziehen:
Compound of ~an-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhanan~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, to lead"). Cognate with English ~tow~, ~tug~, and ~duke~ (the last via Latin ~dux~, "leader," from the same root). Also related to obsolete English ~tee~ ("to draw, lead").
- ~an-~: From MHG ~an(e)-~, from OHG ~ana~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ana~, from Proto-Germanic *~ana~ ("on, at"), from PIE *~h₂en~ ("on, onto"). This prefix adds the sense of pulling something onto oneself.

The semantic range of ~anziehen~ is remarkably broad: "to put on (clothing)," "to attract," "to tighten (a screw)," and even "to accelerate." All these meanings flow logically from the core image of "pulling onto": you pull clothes onto your body, a magnet pulls iron toward itself, you pull a screw tighter. The Dutch expression ~zich iets aantrekken van~ ("to take something to heart") mirrors the western German idiom ~sich etwas anziehen von~, suggesting a shared metaphorical tradition. English ~attract~ is a Latin calque of the same concept (~ad-~ + ~trahere~ = "to pull toward").
en.arbeiten: From Old High German ~arbeiten~, from Proto-Germanic *~arbaidijaną~, ultimately from PIE *~h₃órbʰos~ ("orphan"). The semantic evolution from "orphan" through "hardship, toil" (the lot of the fatherless) to "work" reflects a shift from social condition to the labor it entailed. Cognate with Dutch ~arbeiden~, Old Saxon ~arvedian~, Gothic ~arbaidjan~, and — from the same PIE root — English ~orphan~ and Greek ~orphanos~.
en.atmen:
From Middle High German ~ātemen~, from Old High German ~ātamōn~, derived from Proto-Germanic *~ēþmaz~ ("breath"), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₁eh₁tmén-~ ("breath"). Equivalent to ~Atem~ ("breath") + ~-en~. Cognate with Dutch ~ademen~ ("to breathe").

The PIE root *~h₁eh₁t-~ connects German breathing to one of the most profound concepts in Indo-European thought: Sanskrit ~ātmán~ ("soul, self, breath") descends from the same source, and in Hindu philosophy, the ~Ātman~ is the true self or soul -- the innermost essence that animates all living beings. The semantic chain from "breath" to "soul" is nearly universal across ancient cultures: breath is the visible sign of life, so the word for breath becomes the word for the life force itself. Latin ~anima~ ("breath, soul") and Greek ~psychē~ ("breath, soul") show the same pattern. The archaic German doublet ~Odem~ ("breath"), now largely poetic, also descends from *~ēþmaz~ via a different phonological path.
en.aufbauen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~bauen~:

- ~bauen~: From MHG ~būwen~, from OHG ~būan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~būan~, from Proto-Germanic *~būaną~ ("to dwell, reside"), from PIE *~bʰuH-~ ("to be, become, grow"). The same PIE root yields English ~be~, Latin ~fuī~ ("I was"), and Greek ~phúō~ ("to grow").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-Germanic *~up-~, from PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). This separable prefix adds an upward or constructive directionality to the base verb.

The ~auf-~ prefix transforms ~bauen~'s basic sense of "to build" into "to build up, construct from the ground up, assemble." The compound is the standard word for constructing something systematically — a building, a business, an argument (~ein Argument aufbauen~). The figurative extensions are productive: ~aufgebaut sein aus~ ("to be composed of"), ~auf etwas aufbauen~ ("to build on something"). Cognate with Dutch ~opbouwen~, Low German ~upbuuen~.
en.aufbewahren:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~bewahren~:

- ~bewahren~: From MHG ~bewarn~, from OHG ~biwarōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~biwarōną~, compound of *~bi-~ ("about, around") + *~warōną~ ("to guard, protect"), from Proto-Indo-European *~wer-~ ("to become aware of, to heed"). Cognate with English ~beware~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The prefix here conveys preservation and safekeeping, with an underlying spatial metaphor of storing something upward or away.

The PIE root *~wer-~ ("to heed, perceive") is the ancestor of an impressive family: German ~wahren~ ("to preserve"), ~wahrnehmen~ ("to perceive"), ~Wahrheit~ ("truth"), English ~wary~, ~aware~, ~beware~, ~guard~ (via French ~garder~, from Frankish *~wardōn~), and ~ward~/~warden~. The double-prefixed ~aufbewahren~ piles up two layers of protective meaning: ~be-~ wraps the guarding around the object, and ~auf-~ stacks the safekeeping upward. The result means "to store, to keep safe, to preserve" -- a word that sounds bureaucratic but carries millennia of watchful vigilance in its roots.
en.aufbringen:
From Middle High German ~ūfbringen~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~bringen~:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringaną~, from a Pre-Germanic compound *~bʰrenḱ-~, blending Proto-Indo-European *~bʰer-~ ("to carry") with *~h₁neḱ-~ ("to bring, reach"). Cognate with English ~bring~, Dutch ~brengen~, Afrikaans ~bring~, Gothic ~briggan~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds an upward, outward, or provocative force.

The etymology of ~bringen~ is unique among Germanic verbs: it is the only verb in Proto-Germanic with a mixed weak-strong past tense (German ~brachte~ shows both the dental suffix of weak verbs and the ablaut vowel change of strong verbs). This hybrid conjugation suggests that *~bringaną~ was already anomalous in Proto-Germanic. The compound ~aufbringen~ has developed several distinct meanings: "to raise, to muster" (funds or courage), "to infuriate" (someone), and in nautical usage "to seize" (a ship). Cognates include Dutch ~opbrengen~ ("to yield, produce") and the archaic English ~upbring~ ("to bring up, to raise").
en.auffallen:
From Middle High German ~ūfvallen~, ~ūffallen~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~fallen~:

- ~fallen~: From MHG ~vallen~, from OHG ~fallan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallaną~, from PIE *~peh₃l-~ ("to fall").
- ~auf-~: From PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The separable prefix adds a sense of upward motion or sudden appearance.

~Auffallen~ means "to stand out, to be noticeable, to attract attention." The literal image is striking: something that ~falls upward~ — that rises against the expected direction — naturally catches the eye. The cognate Low German form ~upvallen~ confirms the compound's age. In modern usage, the verb is impersonal: ~es fällt mir auf~ ("it strikes me, I notice it"), and the derived adjective ~auffällig~ ("conspicuous, striking") is ubiquitous. The verb captures a deep insight about perception: we notice not what falls as expected, but what ~falls up~ — what violates our expectations. Cognate with Dutch ~opvallen~ ("to stand out"), which follows the same metaphor exactly.
en.auffordern:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~fordern~:

- ~fordern~: From MHG ~vodern~, ~vordern~, from OHG ~fordarōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~forþ(a)rōn~ ("to move forward, to bring to the front"), from *~forþar~ ("further"), ultimately from PIE *~per-~ ("to go forth"). Cognate with English ~further~ (from the same Proto-West Germanic stem). Related to German ~fördern~ ("to promote, support"), which is the same word with umlaut.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf~, from OHG ~ūf~, from Proto-Germanic *~upp~ ("up"), from PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). As a prefix, it adds urgency or directedness.

The literal sense is "to demand upward" — to call upon someone to do something. ~Auffordern~ means "to request, to call upon, to challenge" and carries more urgency than ~bitten~ ("to ask") but less than ~befehlen~ ("to command"). The noun ~Aufforderung~ ("request, summons, prompt") is common in bureaucratic and legal German. The relationship between ~fordern~ ("to demand") and ~fördern~ ("to promote") — distinguished only by umlaut — illustrates how a single root meaning "to bring forward" could split into "to demand something be brought forth" and "to push something forward."
en.aufführen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~führen~:

- ~führen~: From MHG ~vüeren~, from OHG ~fuoren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fōrijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fōrijaną~, originally the causative of OHG ~faran~ ("to proceed, travel"). From PIE *~per-~ ("to carry, lead forward"). Cognate with English ~fare~ (the base verb) and, more distantly, ~ferry~ (which shares the same causative formation).
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf~, from OHG ~ūf~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~, from PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below").

The MHG compound ~ūfvüeren~ originally meant "to lead up, bring forward." Its theatrical sense "to perform (a play)" developed from the idea of bringing a work forward onto the stage. The reflexive ~sich aufführen~ ("to behave, conduct oneself") adds yet another dimension, sometimes used disapprovingly: ~Führ dich nicht so auf!~ ("Don't carry on like that!").
en.aufgeben:
From Middle High German ~ūfgeben~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gëben~, from OHG ~gëban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebaną~, from PIE *~gʰebʰ-~ ("to give").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-Germanic *~up-~, from PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds a sense of upward transfer or relinquishment.

The compound's meaning of "to give up, to surrender" derives from the image of giving something upward — releasing it from one's grasp to the sky, as it were. But ~aufgeben~ also means "to post (a letter)" (giving it up to the postal system), "to assign (homework)" (giving a task upward to students from teacher), and historically "to serve food" (giving it up onto the table). These senses all preserve the core gesture of handing something over and away from oneself. The derived noun ~Aufgabe~ ("task, assignment, mission") reframes the act of giving up as a duty received — what is given up by one party becomes the task of another. The phrase ~sich aufgeben~ ("to give up on oneself") is among the most psychologically loaded expressions in German. Cognate with English ~upgive~ (archaic), Dutch ~opgeven~.
en.aufgreifen:
From Middle High German ~ūfgrīfen~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~greifen~:

- ~greifen~: From MHG ~grīfen~, from OHG ~grīfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grīpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grīpaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~gʰreyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). Cognate with English ~gripe~ and ~grip~, Dutch ~grijpen~, Swedish ~gripa~. Also related to Lithuanian ~griẽbti~ ("to seize").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds the sense of picking up or seizing upon.

The PIE root *~gʰreyb-~ has produced a cluster of English words all revolving around grasping: ~grip~, ~gripe~, ~grope~, and ~grab~ (the last via a secondary formation). The compound ~aufgreifen~ means both "to pick up" in a physical sense and "to take up" in an abstract one -- as in taking up an idea or a suggestion. In legal and police contexts, ~aufgreifen~ means "to apprehend, to pick up" a suspect, preserving the original forceful sense of seizing. The Gothic cognate ~greipan~ appears in Wulfila's Bible translation, showing that this verb was already well established across the Germanic world by the 4th century.
en.aufhalten:
From Middle High German ~ūfhalten~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldaną~ ("to hold, keep"). The PIE origin is uncertain; one proposal connects it to *~ḱel-~ ("to hide, conceal"), while others suggest *~kelH-~ ("to rise") or *~kel-~ ("to drive"). Cognate with English ~hold~, Dutch ~houden~, Swedish ~hålla~, Icelandic ~halda~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf~, from OHG ~ūf~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~, from PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below").

Cognate with Dutch ~ophouden~ ("to stop, to cease") and English ~uphold~ ("to maintain, support"), though the three languages have sent the same compound in different semantic directions. German ~aufhalten~ primarily means "to halt, to stop (someone)" and reflexively "to stay, to sojourn" (~sich aufhalten~). The reflexive use with ~mit~ in the negative -- ~sich nicht mit etwas aufhalten~ ("not to bother with something") -- adds the nuance of refusing to be delayed by trivial matters.
en.aufheben:
~auf-~ + ~heben~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~heben~:

- ~heben~: From MHG ~heben~, from OHG ~heffen~, ~heven~, from Proto-Germanic *~hafjaną~, from PIE *~keh₂p-~ ("to grasp, to seize").
- ~auf-~: From PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The separable prefix adds a sense of upward motion or removal.

~Aufheben~ is one of German's most philosophically famous verbs, carrying three seemingly contradictory meanings: "to pick up," "to preserve, to keep," and "to cancel, to abolish." Hegel seized upon this triple meaning to create his concept of ~Aufhebung~ ("sublation"): the dialectical process in which a thesis and antithesis are simultaneously ~negated~, ~preserved~, and ~elevated~ into a synthesis. It is rare for a single word to contain its own opposite (such words are called ~auto-antonyms~ or ~contronyms~), and rarer still for a philosopher to build an entire system on the ambiguity. The three senses are etymologically unified: to lift something ~up~ (~aufheben~) removes it from where it was (cancellation), takes it into one's hands (preservation), and raises it to a higher position (elevation). The colloquial phrase ~gut aufgehoben sein~ ("to be in good hands") uses the preservation sense. Cognate with Dutch ~opheffen~ ("to abolish, to lift up").
en.aufhören:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~hören~:

- ~hören~: From MHG ~hœren~, from OHG ~hōren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hōrian~, from Proto-Germanic *~hauzijaną~ ("to hear, to listen"), from PIE *~h₂ḱh₂owsyéti~ ("to be sharp-eared"), a compound of *~h₂eḱ-~ ("sharp") + *~h₂ows-~ ("ear") + *~-yéti~ (denominative suffix). Cognate with English ~hear~, Dutch ~horen~, Danish ~høre~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf~, from OHG ~ūf~, from Proto-Germanic *~upp~ ("up"), from PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys upward motion, opening, or cessation.

The compound means "to stop, to cease, to quit" — ~Hör auf!~ ("Stop it!"). The connection between "listening" and "stopping" is not immediately obvious, but it likely arose from the idea of heeding a command: when you "listen up" (~aufhören~), you stop what you are doing and pay attention. The PIE etymology of ~hören~ is itself remarkable: *~h₂ḱh₂owsyéti~ literally means "to have sharp ears," a compound that reveals the Proto-Indo-European speakers understood hearing as a sharpening of the ear's attention. The PIE root *~h₂ows-~ ("ear") survives in Latin ~auris~ ("ear"), whence English ~aural~ and ~auricular~. The expression ~Da hört sich alles auf!~ ("That's the limit!" literally "Everything stops hearing itself there!") is a characteristically German way of expressing indignation at something beyond the pale.
en.aufklären:
From ~auf-~ + ~klären~, a calque of Middle Low German ~upklāren~. Compound of a Germanic prefix and a Latin-derived verb:

- ~klären~: From MHG ~klæren~, from the adjective ~klar~, which entered German from Middle Low German or Middle Dutch ~claer~, reinforced by Old French ~cler~, all ultimately from Latin ~clārus~ ("bright, clear, famous").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The prefix conveys clearing up, illumination, or resolution.

The word ~aufklären~ is historically inseparable from the ~Aufklärung~ -- the German Enlightenment of the 18th century. Immanuel Kant's famous 1784 essay "Was ist Aufklärung?" defined it as humanity's emergence from self-imposed immaturity. The word itself mirrors this intellectual program: ~auf-~ ("up") + ~klären~ ("to clear") = "to clear up, to enlighten." Compare Dutch ~opklaren~ ("to clear up," especially of weather). The meteorological sense of clearing up likely preceded the philosophical one, and ~aufklären~ still retains both: the weather clears up (~Das Wetter klärt auf~) and a detective clears up a crime (~einen Fall aufklären~). Latin ~clārus~ also gave English ~clear~, ~clarify~, and ~declare~.
en.aufkommen:
From Middle High German ~ūfkomen~, from Old High German ~ūfqueman~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~kommen~:

- ~kommen~: From MHG ~komen~/~quëmen~, from OHG ~quëman~ (rarer ~cuman~), from Proto-West Germanic *~kweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~kwemaną~, from the aorist subjunctive of Proto-Indo-European *~gʷem-~ ("to step, to arrive"). Cognate with English ~come~, Dutch ~komen~, Danish ~komme~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The prefix conveys emergence or arising.

The OHG form ~quëman~ preserves the ancient ~kw-~ cluster (written ~qu-~) that was later simplified to ~k-~ in most German dialects -- a sound change called labiovelar simplification. Gothic ~qiman~ and Old English ~cuman~ show the same root. The compound ~aufkommen~ has branched into several meanings: "to arise, to emerge" (of trends or ideas), "to bear the cost" (~für etwas aufkommen~, "to pay for something"), and "to recover" (from an illness). The PIE root *~gʷem-~ ("to step") also produced Latin ~venīre~ ("to come"), Greek ~baínein~ ("to go"), and Sanskrit ~gámati~ ("goes") -- making ~kommen~ a cousin of English ~advent~, ~venue~, and ~acrobat~.
en.auflösen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~lösen~. Compare Dutch ~oplossen~:

- ~lösen~: From MHG ~lœsen~, from OHG ~lōsen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lausijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lausijaną~ ("to make loose, release, set free"), a factitive derivation from *~lausaz~ ("loose, free"), from PIE *~lewh₁-~ ("to loosen, cut apart"). Cognate with English ~loose~ (via Old Norse ~lauss~), obsolete English ~leese~ (from Old English ~līesan~), Danish ~løse~, Swedish ~lösa~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf~, from OHG ~ūf~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~, from PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below").

The Dutch cognate ~oplossen~ has specialized further than its German cousin: it is the standard word for "to solve" (a problem), "to dissolve" (a substance), and even "to resolve" (a conflict). German ~auflösen~ covers similar ground but tends more toward "to dissolve, to disband, to break up." The expression ~sich in Luft auflösen~ ("to dissolve into thin air") captures the most dramatic end of this semantic spectrum.
en.aufnehmen:
From Middle High German ~ūfnemen~, from Old High German *~ūfneman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uppneman~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nëmen~, from OHG ~nëman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemaną~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due"). A class IV strong verb.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf~, from OHG ~ūf~, from Proto-Germanic *~upp-~, from PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). This separable prefix adds a sense of upward motion, opening, or reception.

The compound's range of meanings — "to pick up," "to record," "to admit (a member)," "to photograph," "to receive (a signal)," "to absorb (a shock)" — illustrates how the directional ~auf-~ prefix transforms the basic act of taking into a rich array of receptive and upward-oriented actions. Dutch ~opnemen~ and Old English ~upniman~ are parallel formations. Cognate with Dutch ~opnemen~.
en.aufpassen:
From ~auf-~ + ~passen~, a calque of Dutch ~oppassen~. Compound of a Germanic prefix and a Romance-derived verb:

- ~passen~: Borrowed from French ~passer~ ("to pass"), ultimately from Latin ~passus~ ("step, pace"). Most closely related to German ~passieren~ ("to happen, to pass").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds a sense of alertness and upward attention.

The sense of "to be attentive, to pay attention" in ~aufpassen~ is not inherited from the French source but is a native Germanic semantic development within Dutch, from which it was calqued into German. French ~passer~ means simply "to pass," but Dutch ~oppassen~ and its German calque ~aufpassen~ developed the meaning "to watch out, to be careful" -- perhaps from the idea of stepping up to alertness. The phrase ~pass auf!~ ("watch out!") is one of German's most common warnings. Curiously, ~passen~ in German also means "to fit" (as in clothing), a sense that developed independently from the idea of a step or measure being correct.
en.aufrufen:
~auf-~ + ~rufen~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~rufen~:

- ~rufen~: From MHG ~ruofen~, from OHG ~ruofan~, ~hruofan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hrōpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrōpaną~ ("to cry out, call, shout"). The Proto-Germanic etymology is uncertain; a traditional connection to *~hrōþiz~ ("glory, fame") is phonetically problematic. Cognate with Dutch ~roepen~.
- ~auf-~: From PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds the sense of calling upward or outward — summoning to action.

~Aufrufen~ means "to call (someone's name), to call upon, to summon, to invoke." In modern German it has gained a strong technical sense: ~eine Webseite aufrufen~ ("to call up a webpage"), ~eine Funktion aufrufen~ ("to invoke a function"). The older sense of publicly summoning someone by name persists in legal and administrative contexts (~die Zeugen aufrufen~, "to call the witnesses"). A curious linguistic footnote: ~rufen~ originally had both strong and weak conjugation forms coexisting, with the weak pattern attestable into the 19th century. The modern umlautless present-tense conjugations (~du rufst~, ~er ruft~, not the obsolete ~du rüfst~, ~er rüft~) are a relic of this weak-verb influence, as lack of umlaut on rounded diphthongs before labials is regular in Upper German but has only become standard in ~rufen~, not in ~laufen~ or ~saufen~.
en.aufstellen:
Equivalent to ~auf-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stallijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stallijaną~, from PIE *~stelh₂-~ ("to put, to stand").
- ~auf-~: From PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds the sense of placing something upright or establishing something.

~Aufstellen~ means "to set up, to erect, to establish" — covering everything from physical placement (~ein Zelt aufstellen~, "to pitch a tent") to abstract establishment (~eine Theorie aufstellen~, "to put forward a theory," ~einen Rekord aufstellen~, "to set a record") to military formation (~Truppen aufstellen~, "to deploy troops"). The prefix ~auf-~ lifts the act of placing into an upright, visible, public position. The derived noun ~Aufstellung~ means "lineup, formation, arrangement" — the starting eleven in a football match is the ~Aufstellung~. Cognate with Dutch ~opstellen~.
en.aufsuchen:
From ~auf-~ + ~suchen~:

- ~suchen~: From MHG ~suochen~, from OHG ~suohhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sōkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sōkijaną~ ("to seek"), from Proto-Indo-European *~seh₂g-~ ("to seek out"). Cognate with English ~seek~, Gothic ~sōkjan~, Old Saxon ~sōkian~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The prefix adds purposefulness to the seeking.

The PIE root *~seh₂g-~ also produced Latin ~sāgīre~ ("to perceive keenly") and Old Irish ~saigim~ ("to seek, pursue"), suggesting that the original sense was more about keen perception than mere searching. The compound ~aufsuchen~ means "to seek out, to visit, to call on" -- it implies a deliberate, purposeful visit rather than a casual encounter. Compare English ~upseek~, an archaic cognate. The related noun ~Suche~ ("search") and compound ~Versuch~ ("attempt, experiment") both stem from the same root, showing how "seeking" branches into "trying" and "testing."
en.auftauchen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~tauchen~:

- ~tauchen~: From MHG ~tūchen~, from OHG ~-tūhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dūkan~, from Proto-Germanic *~dūkaną~ ("to duck, dive under"). The ultimate PIE origin is unclear; one theory connects it to *~dʰugʰnéh₂-~, from a root meaning "to fall," which might also be related to *~duganą~ ("to be fit"). Cognate with Dutch ~duiken~, Swedish ~dyka~. A derivative with Low German consonantism is ~ducken~, cognate with and synonymous with English ~duck~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf~, from OHG ~ūf~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~, from PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below").

The word ~tauchen~ had become obsolete in Upper German dialects but was reintroduced from Central German ~dūchen~/~tūchen~ and Middle Low German ~dūken~. In this process, the originally strong verb became weak. The compound ~auftauchen~ literally means "to dive up," describing a submarine, a whale, or a diver surfacing. Its figurative use -- "to turn up, to appear" -- captures the sudden, often unexpected quality of something emerging from hidden depths.
en.aufteilen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~teilen~:

- ~teilen~: From MHG ~teilen~, from OHG ~teilen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dailijan~ ("to divide, part, deal"), from Proto-Germanic *~dailijaną~, from PIE *~dʰoyl-~ ("part, watershed"). Cognate with English ~deal~ and Dutch ~delen~.
- ~auf-~: From PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). As a separable prefix, it means "up, open, on."

~Aufteilen~ means "to divide up, to partition, to distribute" — literally "to part open," splitting something into portions. The ~auf-~ prefix adds a sense of opening or distributing that plain ~teilen~ ("to share, to divide") lacks: ~teilen~ can mean sharing (without separating), but ~aufteilen~ always implies breaking something into distinct portions. The PIE root produced English ~deal~ (exact cognate), ~dole~ (a distributed share), and ~ordeal~ (originally "a judgment," from the act of distributing or meting out justice).
en.auftragen:
From Middle High German ~ūftragen~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganą~ ("to draw, drag"), from Proto-Indo-European *~dʰregʰ-~ ("to pull, draw"). Cognate with English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Dutch ~dragen~ ("to carry"), Portuguese ~trazer~ ("to bring").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The prefix conveys applying something onto a surface or assigning a task upward.

The semantic shift from "to pull, drag" to "to carry, bear" happened within Germanic: what was originally dragged along the ground came to be carried. The compound ~auftragen~ has developed several distinct meanings that seem unrelated on the surface: "to apply" (paint, cream), "to assign, commission" (a task), and "to serve" (food). The connecting thread is the idea of placing something onto or upon: paint onto a canvas, a duty onto a person, food onto a table. English ~draw~ and ~drag~ are doublets from the same Proto-Germanic root, split by dialect: ~drag~ entered English from Old Norse ~draga~, while ~draw~ continued the native Old English ~dragan~.
en.auftreten:
From Middle High German ~ūftreten~. Compound of ~auf-~ + ~treten~:

- ~treten~: From MHG ~trëten~, from OHG ~trëtan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tredan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trudaną~. A class V strong verb cognate with English ~tread~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-Germanic *~up-~, from PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). As a separable prefix, ~auf-~ adds upward directionality or the sense of opening, beginning, or public emergence.

The compound's primary senses — "to appear (on stage)," "to occur," "to kick" — all derive from the image of stepping upward into visibility. An actor who ~auftritt~ steps forward onto the boards; a problem that ~auftritt~ steps up into one's awareness; an aggressor who ~auftritt~ puts a foot forward in force. The ~Auftritt~ ("appearance, scene, entrance") and the theatrical term ~Auftreten~ ("manner of conducting oneself") preserve the original footstep metaphor. Notably, the ~treten~-based family accounts for nearly every German word for social or institutional entry: ~eintreten~ ("to enter, to join"), ~betreten~ ("to step on"), ~vertreten~ ("to represent"), and ~auftreten~ itself — German social life, from stage to parliament, is expressed through the vocabulary of footsteps. Cognate with Dutch ~optreden~, English ~tread~.
en.aufwachsen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~wachsen~:

- ~wachsen~: From MHG ~wahsen~, from OHG ~wahsan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wahsan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wahsijaną~ ("to grow"), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂weg-~ ("to grow, increase"). Cognate with English ~wax~ (as in "to wax and wane"), Dutch ~wassen~ ("to grow"), Swedish ~växa~, Gothic ~wahsjan~.
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~up~, from Proto-Germanic *~up~ ("up"), from Proto-Indo-European *~upó~ ("up, from below"). The prefix emphasizes the upward trajectory of growth.

The PIE root *~h₂weg-~ ("to grow, increase") also produced Latin ~auxilium~ ("help," originally "increase, reinforcement"), Lithuanian ~áukštas~ ("high"), and Greek ~auxánein~ ("to increase") -- the source of the modern biological term ~auxin~ (a plant growth hormone). English ~wax~ in the sense of "to grow" (as the moon waxes) preserves the old meaning, though it has been largely displaced by ~grow~ in everyday speech. The compound ~aufwachsen~ means specifically "to grow up" in the sense of childhood and maturation, reinforcing the upward metaphor with ~auf-~. The unrelated homophone ~wachsen~ meaning "to wax, to polish" (from ~Wachs~, "wax") is an entirely different word with a different Proto-Germanic root.
en.aufweisen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wīsen~, from OHG ~wīsen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wīsijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wīsijaną~, from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see, to know").
- ~auf-~: From PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). This separable prefix adds a sense of displaying, presenting, or bringing upward to view.

~Aufweisen~ means "to show, to exhibit, to feature" — literally to point something upward so it can be seen. It is used in formal or academic registers: ~Das Gebäude weist Schäden auf~ ("The building exhibits damage"). The PIE root *~weyd-~ ("to see, to know") connects ~weisen~ to a vast family of cognates: English ~wise~, ~wit~, and ~vision~ (via Latin ~vidēre~), Sanskrit ~véda~ ("knowledge," as in the Rigveda), and Greek ~εἶδος~ ("form, appearance," whence English ~idea~). The journey from "seeing" to "knowing" to "showing" is preserved in ~aufweisen~: what you point out is what you have seen, and what you have seen is what you know.
en.aufzeigen:
Compound of ~auf-~ + ~zeigen~:

- ~zeigen~: From MHG ~zeigen~, from OHG ~zeigōn~, an intensive form of ~zīhan~ (whence modern ~zeihen~ "to blame, accuse"), ultimately from PIE *~deyḱ-~ ("to show, point"). Cognate with English ~teach~, Latin ~dīcere~ ("to say"), Greek ~deiknunai~ ("to show").
- ~auf-~: From MHG ~ūf-~, from OHG ~ūf-~, from Proto-Germanic *~upp-~, from PIE *~upó~ ("up, from below"). As a prefix, it conveys "up, upward, open."

The PIE root *~deyḱ-~ produced a remarkable family: Latin ~dīcere~ ("to say"), ~digitus~ ("finger" -- the pointer), ~index~ ("indicator"), and ~iūdex~ ("judge" -- one who shows the law). In Germanic, the root went in a different direction: English ~teach~ (originally "to show") and German ~zeigen~ ("to show"). Also related is ~Zeichen~ ("sign, mark"). In Modern German, ~zeigen~ absorbed the synonymous but entirely unrelated MHG ~zöugen~ (cognate with Gothic ~ataugjan~), which is why variant spellings like ~zäugen~ once appeared. The prefix ~auf-~ adds the sense of raising something to visibility: to point out, demonstrate, highlight. In Austria and far-western Germany, ~aufzeigen~ also means "to raise one's hand" in school -- literally showing upward.
en.ausbauen:
~aus-~ + ~bauen~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~bauen~:

- ~bauen~: From MHG ~būwen~, from OHG ~būan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~būan~, from Proto-Germanic *~būaną~ ("to dwell, reside"), from PIE *~bʰuH-~ ("to be, become, grow").
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of expansion or development outward.

~Ausbauen~ means "to expand, to develop, to extend" — literally "to build outward." Where ~bauen~ alone means "to build, to construct," the prefix ~aus-~ directs the building process outward from an existing structure: one ~baut~ a road network ~aus~ (expands it), ~baut~ a lead ~aus~ (extends it in competition), or ~baut~ an attic ~aus~ (finishes it for habitation). The verb thus straddles the literal and figurative: from physically enlarging a building to abstractly developing a strategy, a relationship, or a competitive advantage. The derived noun ~Ausbau~ ("expansion, development, extension") appears frequently in planning and policy contexts. Cognate with Dutch ~uitbouwen~.
en.ausbilden:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~bilden~:

- ~bilden~: From MHG ~bilden~, ~bileden~, a merger of OHG ~biliden~ (from Proto-West Germanic *~biliþijan~) and OHG ~bilidōn~ (from Proto-West Germanic *~biliþōn~), both derived from *~biliþī~ ("image, likeness"), whence modern ~Bild~ ("picture, image"). The deeper origin is Proto-West Germanic *~bil~ ("equal, alike").
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz~, from OHG ~ūz~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~ ("out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds a sense of full development or completion.

The literal sense is "to form out" — to shape someone or something into full development. ~Ausbilden~ means "to train, to educate" (especially in vocational or professional contexts), and the noun ~Ausbildung~ ("training, education") is a cornerstone of the German dual-education system, in which apprentices alternate between workplace training and classroom instruction. The MHG predecessor ~ūzbilden~ is already attested. The semantic journey from "image" (~Bild~) to "to form" (~bilden~) to "to train" (~ausbilden~) follows a coherent metaphorical chain: to train someone is to shape them according to an ideal image. Cognate with Dutch ~afbeelden~ ("to depict") and Swedish ~bilda~ ("to form, educate").
en.ausdrücken:
From Middle High German ~ūzdrücken~. In the sense "to express," a calque of Latin ~exprimere~ ("to press out"). Compound of ~aus-~ + ~drücken~:

- ~drücken~: From MHG ~drücken~, from OHG ~drucchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þrukkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þrukkijaną~ ("to press on, crowd"), from PIE *~trewk-~ ("to press on and break"). Cognate with Welsh ~trychu~ ("to cut"), Lithuanian ~trū́kti~ ("to tear into pieces"). Doublet of ~drucken~ ("to print"), which was originally a southern German variant that later specialized in meaning.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz-~, from OHG ~ūz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt-~, from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out").

The physical meaning "to squeeze out" (juice from a lemon, a cigarette stub) and the abstract meaning "to express" (a thought, an emotion) coexist in the same verb -- the metaphor of pressing inner content outward into the world. Compare Dutch ~uitdrukken~, which underwent the same semantic split. The Latin model ~exprimere~ (literally "to press out") that inspired the abstract sense also gave English ~express~, making ~ausdrücken~ and ~express~ etymological mirror images -- one Germanic, one Romance, both built on the same metaphor. The split between ~drücken~ ("to press") and ~drucken~ ("to print") is a rare case of a single verb differentiating into two standard forms, each with its own semantic domain.
en.auseinandersetzen:
From ~auseinander-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjaną~, from PIE *~sodéyeti~, the causative of *~sed-~ ("to sit"). Thus ~setzen~ is literally "to cause to sit," i.e., "to set, to place." Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~, Yiddish ~zetsn~.
- ~auseinander-~: From ~aus~ ("out") + ~einander~ ("one another"), from PIE *~úd~ ("out") + *~h₁én~ ("in") + *~h₂enteros~ ("other"). The adverb means "apart, asunder."

The reflexive ~sich auseinandersetzen mit~ ("to engage with, to come to grips with, to examine critically") is the most common modern usage, literally meaning "to set oneself apart with" a topic, implying thorough, often difficult engagement. The non-reflexive ~auseinandersetzen~ means "to explain in detail" or "to take apart." This is one of German's celebrated long compound verbs, combining four etymological layers into a single separable verb. The causative relationship between ~sitzen~ ("to sit") and ~setzen~ ("to set, place") mirrors exactly the English pair ~sit~/~set~ -- both pairs descend from the same PIE causative formation *~sodéyeti~ ("to cause to sit"), making this a grammatical pattern that has survived intact for over five millennia. English ~asunder~ is a cognate of ~auseinander~, sharing the same Germanic root for "apart."
en.ausfallen:
From Middle High German ~ūzvallen~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~fallen~:

- ~fallen~: From MHG ~vallen~, from OHG ~fallan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fallan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallaną~, from PIE *~pōl-~, possibly a compound of *~h₂epo~ ("off, away") + *~h₃elh₁-~ ("to fall"). Cognate with English ~fall~, Dutch ~vallen~, Danish ~falde~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz-~, from OHG ~ūz-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ūt~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~, from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). As a verbal prefix, it often conveys motion outward or cessation.

The compound ~ausfallen~ has developed a remarkable range of meanings: "to fall out" (hair, teeth), "to be cancelled" (a class, an event), "to fail" (a machine), and "to turn out" (a result). The cancellation sense — where something ~falls out~ of the schedule — is perhaps the most distinctive, since English uses the unrelated "cancel" (from Latin ~cancellāre~). The PIE etymology of ~fallen~ is debated: the compound-root theory connecting it to Latin ~aboleō~ ("to destroy") and Lithuanian ~pùlti~ ("to fall") is attractive but uncertain. Cognate with Dutch ~uitvallen~.
en.ausführen: From Middle High German ~ūzvüeren~, Old High German ~ūzfuoren~. By surface analysis, ~aus-~ (from PIE *~úd~, "upwards, out") and ~führen~, from MHG ~vüeren~, from OHG ~fuoren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fōrijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fōrijaną~, from PIE *~per-~ ("going, passage"). Cognate with Dutch ~uitvoeren~. The range of meanings is characteristic of Germanic prefix-verb productivity: to carry out (execute a plan), to lead out (escort someone), to export (carry goods out), or to elaborate (develop an argument out to its conclusion). Dutch ~uitvoeren~ covers the same semantic territory, showing how deeply the compound is shared across West Germanic.
en.ausgeben:
From ~aus-~ + ~geben~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gëben~, from OHG ~gëban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebaną~, from PIE *~gʰebʰ-~ ("to give").
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of giving outward, distributing, or expending.

~Ausgeben~ means "to spend (money)" — ~Geld ausgeben~ — but also "to dispense" (medication), "to issue" (banknotes, stamps, orders), "to output" (data, in computing), and, colloquially, "to buy someone a drink" (~eine Runde ausgeben~, "to buy a round"). The reflexive ~sich als etwas ausgeben~ ("to pose as, to impersonate") is a fascinating extension: one "gives oneself out" as something one is not, distributing a false identity to the world. The derived adjective ~ausgiebig~ ("extensive, thorough, generous") preserves the sense of giving freely and fully — an ~ausgiebiges Frühstück~ is a lavish breakfast where nothing is held back. The pair ~ausgeben~ (to give out, to spend) and ~eingeben~ (to input, to enter) form a natural antonym in computing terminology. Cognate with English ~outgive~ (archaic "to give out, to distribute"), Dutch ~uitgeven~ ("to spend, to publish" — the Dutch sense of "to publish" shows another direction of "giving out": releasing a book into the world).
en.ausgehen: From Middle High German ~ūzgēn~, ~ūzgān~, itself from Old High German ~ūzgangan~, ~ūzgān~, ~ūzgēn~. Equivalent to prefix ~aus-~ (from PIE *~úd~, "upwards, out") and ~gehen~, from MHG ~gan~, ~gen~, from OHG ~gan~, ~gen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gān~, from Proto-Germanic *~gāną~, from PIE *~ǵʰeh₁-~ ("to leave"). Cognate with Dutch ~uitgaan~, Low German ~utgahn~, English ~outgo~, and Swedish and Danish ~utgå~. The compound captures a nuance that German preserves through prefix: motion that takes one out of a bounded space. The same PIE root that gives "go" in English gives ~gehen~ in German, and the ~aus-~ prefix turns an abstract motion into a departure from enclosure — going out to socialize, going out (of a flame), or running out (of stock).
en.ausgleichen:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~gleichen~:

- ~gleichen~: From MHG ~gelīchen~, from OHG ~gilīhhon~, from Proto-Germanic *~galīkōną~ ("to make alike, to compare"). The adjective ~gleich~ ("equal, same") descends from OHG ~gilīh~, a compound of ~ga-~ ("together," from PIE *~ḱóm~) + ~līk-~ ("body, form"), related to ~Leiche~ ("corpse," originally "body").
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz-~, from OHG ~ūz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~, from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of completion, balancing out, or evening.

The original meaning of ~gleich~ was "having the same body/form," a compound that literally said two things looked alike because they shared the same shape. The element ~-līk-~ also produced the English suffix ~-ly~ (originally "having the body/form of") and the word ~like~ (same root). English ~like~, German ~gleich~, and the suffix ~-lich~/~-ly~ are all siblings from the same Germanic root. The compound ~ausgleichen~ means "to equalize, compensate, balance out," carrying the sense of bringing two unequal things into the same form. The sports term ~Ausgleich~ ("equalizer") captures this precisely: the goal that makes the score equal again. The noun ~Gleichgewicht~ ("balance, equilibrium") and the adjective ~gleichgültig~ ("indifferent," literally "equally valid") are further compounds showing the productivity of this root. The morbid origin in ~Leiche~ ("corpse") is long forgotten, but it reminds us that the concept of likeness began with the physical human body.
en.auslegen:
From Middle High German ~ūʒlegen~ ("to display, set out; interpret; lend money"). Compound of ~aus-~ + ~legen~:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjaną~ ("to lay, place"), the causative of *~ligjaną~ ("to lie"), from PIE *~logʰéyeti~, causative of *~legʰ-~ ("to lie down"). Cognate with English ~lay~, Dutch ~leggen~, Danish ~laegge~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūʒ~, from OHG ~ūz~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ūt~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~, from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out").

The MHG verb already had a remarkably wide semantic range: display, interpret, and lend money. All three senses survive in modern German. ~Auslegen~ means "to lay out, display" (goods in a shop), "to interpret, construe" (a text, a law, a remark), and "to advance money" (paying on someone's behalf). Cognate with Dutch ~uitleggen~ ("to explain") and Yiddish ~אויסלייגן~ ("to spell out; interpret; lend money"), both preserving a similar range.
en.ausliefern:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~liefern~:

- ~liefern~: From MHG ~lieveren~ (attested before 1350), probably borrowed via Middle Dutch and Middle Low German ~lēveren~ from Old French ~livrer~ ("to deliver"), from Latin ~līberāre~ ("to set free, hand over"), from ~līber~ ("free"). The vocalism ~-ie-~ (instead of the expected ~-ē-~) is attributed to hypercorrection. Low German definitely contributed to the spread of the word, which was part of the technical vocabulary of the Hanseatic League. Cognate with Dutch ~leveren~, Swedish ~leverera~, and French ~livrer~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz~, from OHG ~ūz~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~ ("out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys movement outward, completion, or distribution.

The compound ~ausliefern~ means "to deliver, hand over, extradite" — to send something ~aus~ ("out") by ~liefern~ ("delivering") it. The root ~liefern~ is one of the few German verbs with a Romance etymology that entered the language not through courtly culture but through trade: the Hanseatic League's commercial network spread the word from Low German ports across the German-speaking world. The Latin ancestor ~līberāre~ ("to set free") — which also gives English ~liberate~ and ~liberty~ — underwent a remarkable semantic narrowing in its passage through Old French into Germanic: from "freeing" to "handing over goods." English ~deliver~ traveled the same Old French path but kept a wider semantic range.
en.auslösen:
~aus-~ + ~lösen~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~lösen~:

- ~lösen~: From MHG ~lœsen~, ~lōsen~, from OHG ~lōsen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lausijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lausijaną~, a factitive verb derived from *~lausaz~ ("loose, free"), from PIE *~lewh₁-~ ("to loosen, cut apart").
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds a sense of releasing outward, completing a process of liberation.

~Auslösen~ means "to trigger, to initiate, to cause; to ransom" — two senses united by the image of releasing something from constraint. The triggering sense (~eine Reaktion auslösen~, "to trigger a reaction"; ~den nächsten Börsencrash auslösen~, "to trigger the next stock-market crash") envisions a mechanism held in check that is suddenly set free. The ransoming sense (~einen Gefangenen auslösen~, "to ransom a prisoner") is the older meaning: to pay the price that frees someone from captivity. The derived noun ~Auslöser~ means both "trigger" (on a camera or a gun) and, figuratively, "the thing that set events in motion." The English cognate ~loose~ descends from Old Norse ~lauss~ (from the same Proto-Germanic *~lausaz~), while ~lose~ comes from a different path through Old English ~losian~. German ~lösen~ and English ~loose~ are thus cousins, not siblings — arriving at similar sounds from slightly different routes through the same Proto-Germanic family.
en.ausmachen:
From ~aus-~ + ~machen~. Compound of:

- ~machen~: From MHG ~machen~, from OHG ~mahhōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~makōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~makōn~, from *~mak~ ("fit, suitable") + *~-ōn~, ultimately from PIE *~mag-~ ("to knead, mix, make"). Cognate with English ~make~, Dutch ~maken~, Low German ~maken~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz-~, from OHG ~ūz-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ūt~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~, from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). As a verbal prefix, it can convey completion, extinction, or determination.

~ausmachen~ is a lexical chameleon with at least five major senses: "to turn off" (a light), "to make up, constitute" (a sum), "to matter" (~es macht nichts aus~ = "it doesn't matter"), "to agree upon, arrange" (a meeting), and "to make out, discern" (something in the distance). This polysemy makes it one of the trickier German verbs for learners. The "turn off" sense — extinguishing something ~out~ — parallels English "put out" (a fire), while the "constitute" sense reflects the idea of ~making out~ a total. The "to matter" sense is perhaps the most idiomatic: ~macht dir das etwas aus?~ ("does that bother you?") literally asks whether something "makes something out" of the situation for you. The PIE root *~mag-~ ("to knead") points to the most physical origin of all: shaping dough with one's hands.
en.ausprobieren:
From ~aus-~ + ~probieren~. ~Probieren~ derives from Latin ~probāre~ ("to test, to inspect, to approve, to prove"), a denominal verb from ~probus~ ("good, fit, virtuous"), from PIE *~pro-bʰuH-s~ ("being in front"), from *~pro-~ ("forward") + *~bʰuH-~ ("to be"). ~Probieren~ is a doublet of both ~proben~ ("to rehearse") and ~prüfen~ ("to examine, to test"), all from the same Latin root but via different routes.

- ~probieren~: Directly from Latin ~probāre~. The sense in German is "to try, to taste, to sample" — not "to probe" in the clinical English sense. This is a subtle false friend: English ~probe~ (also from ~probāre~) has narrowed to mean investigation or intrusion, while German ~probieren~ kept the lighter sense of tasting or trying.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūȥ~, OHG ~ūȥ~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ūt~, from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). Cognate with English ~out~. The prefix here adds the sense of thoroughness or completion — testing something all the way through.
en.ausreichen:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~reichen~:

- ~reichen~: From MHG ~reichen~, from OHG ~reihhen~, ~reichen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raikijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~raikijaną~ ("to reach, stretch out, extend"). Cognate with English ~reach~ and Dutch ~reiken~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz~, from OHG ~ūz~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~ ("out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys sufficiency or completion.

The compound literally means "to reach out sufficiently" and has come to mean simply "to suffice, to be enough": ~Das Geld reicht aus~ ("The money is enough"). The adjective ~ausreichend~ ("sufficient, adequate") is also used as a school grade in Germany — equivalent to a D, the lowest passing mark, which tells students their performance ~reicht gerade aus~ ("just barely suffices"). The root verb ~reichen~ preserves both the physical sense ("to hand, to pass") and an older sense of sufficiency (~Das reicht!~ "That's enough!"). English ~reach~ underwent the opposite semantic narrowing, losing the sufficiency meaning and retaining only the physical extension. The PIE ancestor *~raikijaną~ meant "to stretch out the hand," an image that survives whenever a German speaker says ~Reichen Sie mir bitte das Salz~ ("Please pass me the salt").
en.ausrichten:
From Middle High German ~ūzrihten~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~richten~:

- ~richten~: From MHG ~richten~, from OHG ~rihten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rihtijan~ ("to right, to direct").
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The separable prefix adds a sense of outward direction or completion.

~Ausrichten~ carries a remarkable range of meanings: "to align, to orient, to deliver (a message), to organize (an event), to accomplish." The root ~richten~ means "to straighten, to direct, to judge" — cognate with English ~right~ — and the prefix ~aus-~ pushes this straightening outward, toward completion. When you ~eine Nachricht ausrichten~ ("deliver a message"), you are directing words outward to their recipient. When you ~ein Fest ausrichten~ ("host a festival"), you bring an event into proper arrangement. The MHG compound ~ūzrihten~ already carried the sense of equipping or furnishing, showing that the "arranging outward" metaphor is centuries old. Cognate with Dutch ~uitrichten~.
en.ausschalten:
From Middle High German ~ūzschalten~, equivalent to ~aus-~ + ~schalten~:

- ~schalten~: From MHG ~schalten~, from OHG ~scaltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaldan~ ("to push, shove"). Further etymology is obscure; according to Pokorny, possibly from PIE *~(s)kelH-~ ("to split, separate"), in which case related to English ~shell~. Cognate with Old Saxon ~skaldan~ ("to push").
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz~, from OHG ~ūz~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~ ("out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix conveys removal or deactivation.

The literal sense is "to switch out" — to push something out of its active state. ~Ausschalten~ means "to switch off, to turn off, to disable; to eliminate, neutralize." The second, figurative sense ("to eliminate a rival, to neutralize an opponent") reveals a darker dimension: one "switches off" a person as though they were a machine. The root ~schalten~ originally meant "to push" or "to shove" in Old High German, and its modern meaning "to switch" preserves the physical gesture of pushing a lever or mechanism. The compound ~Schalter~ ("switch; counter, window at a service desk") unites both the electrical and bureaucratic senses through the same image of a mechanism that controls access. Dutch ~uitschakelen~ is the exact cognate.
en.ausschließen: Formed from prefix ~aus-~ (from PIE *~úd~, "upwards, out") and ~schließen~, from MHG ~slieȥen~, from OHG ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slūtaną~, *~sleutaną~ ("to lock, shut"). The root ~schließen~ descends from Middle High German ~slieȥen~, Old High German ~sliozan~, Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, and Proto-Germanic *~slūtaną~ / *~sleutaną~, meaning "to lock, shut." Cognate with Dutch ~sluiten~, and — strikingly — English ~slot~. To "slot out" is a false friend here: English ~slot~ kept the narrow physical sense (a groove or opening), while German ~schließen~ and ~ausschließen~ developed the abstract sense of closing off and excluding.
en.aussehen:
From Middle High German ~ūzsehen~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~sehen~:

- ~sehen~: From MHG ~sëhen~, from OHG ~sëhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwaną~, from PIE *~sekʷ-~ ("to see, notice"). Cognate with Dutch ~zien~, English ~see~, Gothic ~saihwan~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz-~, from OHG ~ūz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ut-~, from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This separable prefix adds an outward or emergent sense to the base verb.

The compound's literal sense is "to look out" — as through a window — and from this developed the dominant modern meaning "to appear, to look (a certain way)": the impression projected outward toward the viewer. The parallel Yiddish ~אויסזען~ (~oyszen~) and Dutch ~uitzien~ preserve identical structure and meaning, while Scandinavian equivalents (~se ut~ in Swedish and Norwegian, ~se ud~ in Danish) express the same idea with postverbal ~ut/ud~. A false friend for English speakers: "he looks good" is ~er sieht gut aus~, not *~er schaut gut~.
en.aussetzen:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjaną~, a causative of *~sitjaną~ ("to sit"), from PIE *~sed-~ ("to sit").
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of outward placement, exposure, or cessation.

~Aussetzen~ unfolds into a striking range of meanings, all derived from "setting something out": to expose (someone to danger — setting them out in the open), to abandon (a child or animal — setting them out and leaving), to release (a captive animal back into the wild — setting it out to freedom), to suspend (an activity — setting it outside the flow of events), to stop (of a machine or heartbeat — ceasing to operate), and to miss a turn (in a game — being set outside the round). The abandonment sense is perhaps the most emotionally charged: ~ein Kind aussetzen~ evokes the ancient practice of infant exposure, known in German legal history as ~Kindesaussetzung~. The word ~ausgesetzt~ ("exposed") functions as both past participle and adjective, describing vulnerability itself. Cognate with Dutch ~uitzetten~, Luxembourgish ~aussetzen~.
en.aussprechen:
From Middle High German ~ūzsprëchen~, from Old High German ~ūzsprehhan~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~sprechen~:

- ~sprechen~: From MHG ~sprëchen~, from OHG ~sprëhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekaną~ ("to speak"), probably from PIE *~spregʰ-~ ("to crackle"). Cognate with English ~speak~, Dutch ~spreken~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz-~, from OHG ~ūz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt-~, from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). As a prefix, it conveys the sense of "out, outward, completely."

The connection between "cracking/crackling" and "speaking" in the PIE root *~spregʰ-~ suggests that the earliest Germanic conception of speech was of sounds bursting forth -- noises that crack the silence. The ~aus-~ prefix in ~aussprechen~ reinforces this outward motion: to "speak out" a word is to pronounce it fully, to articulate it. The verb also carries the sense of "to utter" something otherwise kept silent, and reflexively (~sich aussprechen~) means "to pour one's heart out" or "to declare oneself in favour of something." In its intransitive use (~Lass ihn aussprechen!~), it means "to finish speaking" -- to let someone speak completely out to the end.
en.ausstatten: Formed from prefix ~aus-~ (from PIE *~úd~, "upwards, out") and the now-obsolete verb ~statten~. The core verb ~statten~, from Middle High German ~staten~ ("to help do something; to add"), appears to be a denominal formation from Proto-Germanic *~stadiz~ ("place, location"), from Proto-Indo-European *~stéh₂tis~ ("standing, position"). The related noun ~Statt~ (now chiefly surviving in compounds like ~Statthalter~ and the preposition ~statt~ "instead of") is a doublet of ~Stadt~ ("city") and cognate with English ~stead~ — as in ~homestead~ and ~instead~. To "outfit" someone is thus, at root, to "place out" a full complement of equipment or furnishings.
en.ausstellen:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, to position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, to put, to post, to stand"). Cognate with English ~stall~ and ~still~.
- ~aus-~: From OHG ~ūz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt-~, from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix conveys the sense of putting forth or outward.

The verb ~ausstellen~ has two principal meanings that developed from the same image of "placing outward": first, "to exhibit, to display" (as in a museum exhibition, ~Ausstellung~), and second, "to issue" (a document, certificate, or receipt). In both cases, something is being put out into the world for others to see or use. The PIE root *~stel-~ is remarkably productive, giving rise to German ~Stelle~ ("place, position, job"), ~Gestalt~ ("form, figure"), English ~install~, ~stall~, and Greek ~stēlē~ ("upright stone slab"). The noun ~Ausstellung~ ("exhibition") is one of the most common German cultural terms, used for everything from art shows to trade fairs. The double meaning of ~ausstellen~ occasionally creates ambiguity: ~einen Pass ausstellen~ means "to issue a passport," not "to exhibit" one.
en.aussuchen:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~suchen~:

- ~suchen~: From MHG ~suochen~, from OHG ~suohhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sōkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sōkijaną~, from PIE *~seh₂g-~ ("to seek out"). Originally an Upper German form without umlaut on the long rounded vowel before a velar consonant, reinforced against the umlauted Central German form (compare Luxembourgish ~sichen~) by the umlautless past forms. Cognate with English ~seek~, Dutch ~zoeken~, Low German ~söken~, Danish ~søge~, and Gothic ~sokjan~.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz~, from OHG ~ūz~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~ ("out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys selection or extraction from a group.

The compound literally means "to seek out," i.e., to pick or choose from available options. While ~suchen~ means simply "to search," ~aussuchen~ implies a completed selection: ~Such dir etwas aus!~ ("Pick something out for yourself!"). The reflexive ~sich etwas aussuchen~ is the everyday German equivalent of English "to choose." The English cognate ~seek~ shares the same PIE root *~seh₂g-~, which also produced Latin ~sagīre~ ("to perceive keenly"), the ancestor of English ~sagacious~ and ~presage~. A seeker, in the Indo-European mind, was someone with keen perception.
en.austauschen:
~aus-~ + ~tauschen~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~tauschen~:

- ~tauschen~: From MHG ~tiuschen~, derived from ~tūschen~, related to ~täuschen~ ("to deceive"). The semantic shift ran from "to deceive" to "to trick someone into buying one's goods" to "to trade, to exchange" — a revealing commentary on medieval market ethics. The further origin of ~tūschen~ is uncertain.
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of completeness or thoroughness — a full exchange, in and out.

~Austauschen~ means "to exchange, to replace, to substitute" — a verb of symmetric transfer: what goes out is matched by what comes in. One ~tauscht~ ideas ~aus~ (exchanges them in conversation), ~tauscht~ a defective part ~aus~ (replaces it), or ~tauscht~ prisoners ~aus~ (swaps them between sides). The derived noun ~Austausch~ ("exchange") appears in contexts from student exchanges (~Schüleraustausch~) to information sharing (~Informationsaustausch~). The kinship between ~tauschen~ ("to swap") and ~täuschen~ ("to deceive") — both from the same Middle High German root — is one of German etymology's most entertaining revelations: to trade and to trick were once, at some level, the same act.
en.austragen:
From Middle High German ~ūztragen~, from Old High German ~ūztragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ūtdragan~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganą~ ("to draw, drag"), from PIE *~dʰregʰ-~ ("to pull, draw, drag"). Cognate with Dutch ~dragen~, English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Danish ~drage~, Icelandic ~draga~.
