- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz~, from OHG ~ūz~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~ ("out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix conveys outward movement or completion.

The compound means "to carry out, to deliver (mail); to bear (a child to term); to settle (a dispute); to hold (a competition)." Each sense extends the image of carrying something to its destination or conclusion. The mail carrier (~Briefträger~) ~trägt die Post aus~ ("carries out the mail"), while a pregnant woman ~trägt ein Kind aus~ ("carries a child to term"). The connection between German ~tragen~ ("to carry") and English ~draw~ / ~drag~ is not immediately obvious but perfectly regular: the semantic shift from "to drag" to "to carry" happened within Germanic, while English preserved the older "pulling" sense. Portuguese ~trazer~ ("to bring") is also cognate, having inherited the same root through Latin.
en.ausweisen:
From Middle High German ~ūzweizen~, from Old High German ~ūzwīsen~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wīsen~, from OHG ~wīsen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wīsijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wīsijaną~ ("to show, direct"), a causative derived from *~wīsaz~ ("wise, knowing"), from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see, to know"). The adjective ~weise~ ("wise") is cognate with English ~wise~, Dutch ~wijs~, and Latin ~vidēre~ ("to see") — all from the same root, which links knowledge to vision.
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz~, from OHG ~ūz~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~ ("out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix conveys outward direction or proof.

The compound means "to expel, to deport; to identify, prove" and reflexively ~sich ausweisen~ means "to identify oneself." The semantic bridge between "showing the way out" (expulsion) and "showing who one is" (identification) lies in the root ~weisen~ ("to show, to point"). The ~Ausweis~ ("identity card") is literally "that which shows outward" — an external proof of who one is. The connection between seeing, knowing, and wisdom runs deep in Indo-European languages: Latin ~vidēre~ ("to see") shares the same PIE root *~weyd-~ as German ~wissen~ ("to know") and ~weise~ ("wise"). To the Indo-Europeans, knowledge was something seen.
en.auswerten:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~werten~:

- ~werten~: A verbal derivation from the adjective ~wert~ ("worth, valuable"), from Proto-Germanic *~werþaz~ ("worthy, valuable"), from PIE *~wert-~ ("to turn"). Compare English ~worth~.
- ~aus-~: From OHG ~ūz~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~ ("out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix means "out" in the sense of extracting or deriving results.

~Auswerten~ means "to evaluate, to analyze, to interpret (data)" — literally "to value out," extracting meaning or value from raw material. It is the standard German verb for data analysis: ~Daten auswerten~, ~Ergebnisse auswerten~, ~Statistiken auswerten~. The PIE root *~wert-~ ("to turn") connects value to turning: the original sense was "to turn toward," hence "to regard, to esteem," hence "worth." The same root produced Latin ~vertere~ ("to turn," whence English ~convert~, ~reverse~, ~versus~). The noun ~Auswertung~ ("evaluation, analysis") is indispensable in scientific and statistical German.
en.auswirken:
From Middle High German ~uzwurken~. Compound of ~aus-~ + ~wirken~:

- ~wirken~: From MHG ~wirken~, from OHG ~wirken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wirkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wirkijanan~, a denominative verb from *~werkan~ ("work"), from PIE *~werg-~ ("to do, to work"). Cognate with English ~work~, Dutch ~werken~, and Greek ~ergon~ ("work," as in ~energy~).
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~uz-~, from OHG ~uz-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ut-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ut~ ("out"), from PIE *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of "out" or "thoroughly" to the base verb.

In MHG there were actually two competing forms: ~wirken~ (with ~i~, prevailing in ~Central German~) and ~wurken~ (with ~u~, prevailing in ~Upper German~). The ~i~-form won out in the written standard by the 18th century. Today ~sich auswirken~ is almost always used reflexively, meaning "to have an effect," while the base verb ~wirken~ retains meanings like "to work, to have an effect, to knit." The English cognate ~work~ preserves the broader semantic scope that ~wirken~ has gradually narrowed.
en.auswählen: Formed from prefix ~aus-~ (from PIE *~úd~, "upwards, out") and ~wählen~, from MHG ~weln~, from ~welen~, ~wellen~, from OHG ~wellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~walljan~, from Proto-Germanic *~waljaną~, from PIE *~welh₁-~ ("to wish, choose"). The semantic logic is identical to English ~pick out~: to single out from among alternatives. The prefix ~aus-~ here has a selective, extractive sense — lifting one item out of a set — rather than the directional or exhaustive sense it carries in other compounds.
en.auszeichnen:
Equivalent to ~aus-~ + ~zeichnen~:

- ~zeichnen~: Derived from the noun ~Zeichen~ ("sign, symbol"), from MHG ~zeichen~, from OHG ~zeihhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taikn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taikną~, from PIE *~deyḱ-~ ("to show, point").
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of singling out or distinguishing.

~Auszeichnen~ means "to distinguish, to honor, to award" and also "to mark, to price" (in retail: ~Waren auszeichnen~, "to price goods"). The reflexive ~sich auszeichnen~ means "to distinguish oneself, to excel." The derived noun ~Auszeichnung~ covers both senses: an award or honor, and a price tag. The semantic link is illuminating: to ~auszeichnen~ is to "mark out" — whether marking a person for distinction or marking a product with its price, the underlying gesture is the same: making something visible by putting a sign (~Zeichen~) on it. The PIE root *~deyḱ-~ ("to show, point") that underlies ~Zeichen~ also gives Latin ~dīcere~ ("to say") and ~index~ ("pointer"), making ~auszeichnen~ and ~indicate~ distant PIE cousins. Cognate with Dutch ~uitblinken~ (a different compound for the distinction sense).
en.ausüben:
Compound of ~aus-~ + ~üben~:

- ~üben~: From MHG ~üeben~, ~uoben~, from OHG ~uoben~, from Proto-Germanic *~ōbijaną~ ("to do, practice"), the causative-iterative formation to a lost strong verb *~afaną~, from PIE *~h₃ep-~ ("to labor, work").
- ~aus-~: From MHG ~ūz-~, from OHG ~ūz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt-~, from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix conveys thoroughness or the outward-directed performance of an action.

The base verb ~üben~ means "to practice, drill" -- to perform something repeatedly for improvement. The prefix ~aus-~ transforms this into "to exercise, exert, practice" in the sense of carrying out an activity in the real world: ~einen Beruf ausüben~ ("to practice a profession"), ~Druck ausüben~ ("to exert pressure"), ~Macht ausüben~ ("to wield power"). The shift from repetitive practice to active performance is the key semantic contribution of ~aus-~. The PIE root *~h₃ep-~ ("to labor, work") also gave Latin ~opus~ ("work") and ~opera~, making ~üben~ and ~opera~ distant cousins -- both tracing back to the fundamental human concept of purposeful labor.
en.backen:
From Middle High German ~backen~, from Old High German ~backan~, an originally weak verb and geminated variant of an older strong verb: MHG ~bachen~, from OHG ~bahhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bakan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bakaną~, from PIE *~bʰeh₃g-~ ("to roast, bake"). The two verbs -- strong ~bachen~ and weak ~backen~ -- blended early, each developing both weak and strong forms. The strong form ~bachen~ was originally predominant in Upper German dialects, but ~backen~ prevailed in the modern standard because it was common in Central German and aligned with Middle Low German ~backen~.

Cognate with English ~bake~, Dutch ~bakken~, Danish ~bage~, Swedish ~baka~, and also distantly with Greek ~phōgō~ ("to roast"). The verb retains optional strong conjugation in modern German: alongside the regular weak past tense ~backte~, the strong form ~buk~ (and even ~büke~ in the subjunctive) survives, particularly in literary and regional usage. This dual conjugation pattern is a living fossil of the historical merger of the two verbs. The English cognate ~bake~ has lost all trace of its strong-verb heritage and conjugates entirely regularly. The PIE root *~bʰeh₃g-~ points to a deep antiquity for this culinary technology -- the Indo-Europeans were already baking over five thousand years ago, and the word for it has survived relatively intact across millennia.
en.basieren:
Borrowed from French ~baser~ ("to base, to have as a foundation"), from ~base~ ("base, foundation"), from Old French ~base~, from Latin ~basis~, from Greek ~básis~ ("step, pedestal, base"), from PIE *~gʷm̥-ti-~ ("a stepping"), from *~gʷem-~ ("to come, to go, to step"). The same PIE root gives English ~come~, German ~kommen~, Latin ~veniō~ ("I come"), and Greek ~baínō~ ("I walk, I step").

~Basieren~ is used both transitively ("to base something on") and intransitively ("to be based on"): ~der Film basiert auf wahren Begebenheiten~ ("the film is based on true events"). The word entered German through the prestige of French intellectual and scientific discourse in the 17th and 18th centuries, when French was the language of European courts and academies. The ~-ieren~ suffix marks it as a Romance adaptation. The underlying metaphor — a building resting on its base — runs from Greek architecture through Latin engineering to modern German argumentation: one's argument, like one's building, must ~auf etwas basieren~.
en.basteln:
An Upper German verb, from earlier ~besteln~, an iterative (frequentative) of MHG ~besten~ ("to tie, to bind, to lace"), from Proto-West Germanic *~bastijan~ ("to plait, to sew, to weave"). The vowel ~-a-~ may reflect dialectal influence or readaptation to the underlying noun ~Bast~ ("bast, inner bark"), from MHG and OHG ~bast~ ("inner bark of trees").

~Basteln~ means "to do handicrafts, to tinker, to do DIY" — to make things by hand in an amateur, creative fashion. In medieval usage, the word carried a slightly pejorative connotation: it referred to the work of craftsmen without guild membership — unsanctioned handwork. The modern sense has shed this stigma entirely; ~basteln~ now evokes cheerful creativity, especially children's arts and crafts (~Bastelnachmittag~, "crafting afternoon") or hobby electronics (~an einem Radio basteln~, "to tinker with a radio"). The noun ~Bastler~ ("hobbyist, tinkerer, DIY enthusiast") is affectionate. The connection to ~Bast~ ("bast fiber") is fitting: bast was one of the earliest materials humans worked with their hands — binding, weaving, and plaiting the fibrous inner bark of linden and willow trees into rope, mats, and baskets.
en.bauen: From Middle High German ~būwen~, from Old High German ~būan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~būan~, from Proto-Germanic *~būaną~ ("to dwell, reside"), from PIE *~bʰuH-~ ("to be, become, grow"). The semantic journey from "to be, exist" to "to dwell" to "to build" reflects how habitation and construction are inseparable: one builds where one lives. The same PIE root *~bʰuH-~ is extraordinarily productive across Indo-European, yielding English ~be~ (via PGmc *~beuną~), Latin ~fuī~ ("I was"), Greek ~phúō~ ("to grow, bring forth"), and Sanskrit ~bhavati~ ("to be, become"). In the Germanic branch, the original sense of "dwelling" survives in ~Bauer~ ("farmer," literally "one who dwells on the land"), ~Nachbar~ ("neighbor," literally "one who dwells nearby"), and ~Bau~ ("structure, building"). Cognate with Dutch ~bouwen~, English ~bower~ ("dwelling"), ~neighbor~ (from Old English ~nēahgebūr~, "near-dweller"), Danish ~bygge~.
en.beachten:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~achten~:

- ~achten~: From MHG ~ahten~, from OHG ~ahtōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ahtōn~, a denominative verb from *~ahtu~ ("consideration, attention"), of uncertain pre-Germanic origin.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one, here applying attention directly to an object.

The ~be-~ prefix shifts ~achten~ ("to consider, heed generally") into the specifically transitive ~beachten~ ("to pay attention to, to observe, to comply with"). The distinction is visible in legal and instructional registers: ~beachten Sie~, not *~achten Sie~, is the standard formula in German instruction manuals and official notices. The negative ~unbeachtet~ ("unheeded, unnoticed") collapses back toward ~achten~'s older sense of ignoring. Cognate with Dutch ~beachten~ (archaic), Dutch ~in acht nemen~ (the functional equivalent).
en.beantragen:
From ~be-~ + ~Antrag~ + ~-en~, equivalent to ~be-~ + ~antragen~, which is ~an-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~Antrag~: From MHG ~antrac~, a verbal noun derived from ~antragen~ ("to carry toward, to propose"), itself composed of ~an-~ (from PIE *~h₂en~, "on, onto") + ~tragen~ (from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganą~, from PIE *~dʰregʰ-~, "to pull, draw").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive: to direct a formal request at someone.

~Beantragen~ means "to apply for, to formally request, to propose" — exclusively in bureaucratic and legal contexts. It is the verb of official paperwork: one ~beantragt~ a visa, a building permit, a divorce. The word perfectly encodes the German administrative concept that formal proposals must be ~carried toward~ (~antragen~) an authority and ~directed at~ (~be-~) the relevant office. The derived noun ~Antrag~ itself has drifted from its literal meaning of "carrying toward" to "application, motion, proposal" — and in dated usage, even "marriage proposal" (~einen Antrag machen~). Cognate formations exist in Dutch (~aanvragen~, using a different root but the same structure).
en.beantworten: ~beantworten~ is not simply ~be-~ + ~antworten~ in the usual prefixation sense but a denominal verb formed directly from ~Antwort~ ("answer") with ~be-~ and the infinitive suffix ~-en~. ~Antwort~ comes from Middle High German ~antwürte~, from Old High German ~antwurti~, from Proto-Germanic *~andawurdiją~ — itself from *~anda-~ ("against") and *~wurdiją~ ("speech, conversation"), a derivative of *~wurdą~ ("word, speech"). The noun's gender shifted from neuter to feminine through semantic association with ~Frage~ ("question"). A grammatically significant contrast: ~antworten~ ("to answer") governs the dative — ~jemandem antworten~ ("to answer someone") — while ~beantworten~ governs the accusative — ~eine Frage beantworten~ ("to answer a question"). The prefix ~be-~ here adds transitivity, shifting the object from the person answered to the thing answered. Cognates include Dutch ~antwoord~ and Afrikaans ~antwoord~.
en.bearbeiten: From ~be-~ + ~arbeiten~ ("to work"). The prefix ~be-~ (from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near") makes the base verb transitive. ~Arbeiten~ derives from Old High German ~arbeiten~, from Proto-Germanic *~arbaidijaną~, ultimately from PIE *~h₃órbʰos~ ("orphan") — semantic evolution from "orphan" through "hardship, toil" (the lot of the fatherless) to "work." ~Bearbeiten~ intensifies and directs that labor: to process, to work on, to treat a material or topic. The prefix strips away intransitivity and focuses the action on an object — ~er arbeitet~ ("he works") becomes ~er bearbeitet den Text~ ("he processes the text"). Cognate with Dutch ~bewerken~.
en.beauftragen:
Derived from ~be-~ + ~Auftrag~ + ~-en~. Notably, this verb is not derived from the strong verb ~auftragen~ ("to apply, to serve food") but directly from the noun ~Auftrag~ ("commission, assignment, order"):

- ~Auftrag~: A deverbal noun from ~auftragen~ ("to carry upon, to apply"), from MHG ~ūftragen~, composed of ~auf-~ ("up, upon," from PIE *~upó~, "up from below") + ~tragen~ ("to carry," from MHG ~tragen~, OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganą~, "to draw, drag"). Cognate with English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Dutch ~dragen~.
- ~be-~: From Proto-Germanic *~bi~ ("by, near"), from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). Turns the noun into a transitive verb.

The derivation from the noun rather than the verb explains why ~beauftragen~ conjugates weakly (~beauftragte~, ~beauftragt~) rather than with the strong ablaut pattern of ~tragen~ (~trug~, ~getragen~). This is a common German pattern: when a verb is derived from a noun (even if that noun itself came from a strong verb), the result is always a weak verb. The semantic chain runs from "to carry up" (~auftragen~) to "the thing carried up / laid upon someone" (~Auftrag~, a task or commission) to "to give someone a commission" (~beauftragen~). English ~draw~ is the cognate of ~tragen~, both from PIE *~dʰregʰ-~, with the semantic split happening early: the Germanic branch kept "to carry," while the English branch specialized toward "to drag, to draw."
en.bedanken:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~danken~. Apparently not attested before Modern German (thus after 1400):

- ~danken~: From MHG ~danken~, from OHG ~dankōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þankōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~þankōną~ ("to thank"), a denominative from *~þankaz~ ("thought, gratitude").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This inseparable prefix makes the verb reflexive-transitive: while ~danken~ takes a dative ("to thank someone"), ~sich bedanken~ emphasizes the act from the thanker's perspective.

~Sich bedanken~ means "to express one's thanks, to say thank you" and is slightly more emphatic or formal than simple ~danken~. The ~be-~ prefix transforms the dative construction (~jemandem danken~) into an accusative-reflexive one (~sich bei jemandem bedanken~), shifting the grammatical focus to the grateful person's active expression. The root noun *~þankaz~ ("thought") reveals that Germanic gratitude is literally "thinking of" someone — a semantic parallel to English ~thank~ (from ~think~). The connection between thought and gratitude is preserved in the German phrase ~Ich denke an dich~ ("I think of you"), where ~denken~ and ~danken~ share the same Proto-Germanic ancestor. Cognate with Dutch ~bedanken~, English ~thank~, Danish ~takke~.
en.bedenken:
From Middle High German ~bedenken~, from Old High German ~bidenken~, ~pidenken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biþankijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~biþankijaną~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~denken~:

- ~denken~: From MHG ~denken~, from OHG ~denken~, ~thenken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þankijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þankijaną~, from PIE *~tong-éye-ti~, causative of *~teng-~ ("to think, feel, perceive, know").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive.

~Bedenken~ means "to consider, to reflect upon, to have reservations about." The prefix ~be-~ takes the intransitive act of thinking and gives it a direct object: one does not merely ~denken~ (think) but ~bedenkt~ (thinks over, considers) something specific. As a noun, ~Bedenken~ means "misgivings, reservations" — one's considered doubts. Cognate with English ~bethink~ (archaic, "to think about, to consider"), Dutch ~bedenken~ ("to think up, to consider"), and more distantly with Latin ~tongeō~ ("to know"). The English cognate ~think~ underwent a curious phonetic shift: Proto-Germanic *~þ~ became English ~th~ but German ~d~ (via the High German consonant shift), making ~denken~ and ~think~ less obviously related than they truly are.
en.bedeuten:
From Middle High German ~bediuten~, ~betiuten~, from Old High German ~bidiuten~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~deuten~:

- ~deuten~: From MHG ~diuten~, from OHG ~diuten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þiudijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þiudijaną~, derived from Proto-Germanic *~þeudō~ ("people, nation"), the same root as Old English ~þēod~ ("people").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The original sense of ~deuten~ was "to explain to the people" — to interpret, make intelligible. The surprising etymological kinship between ~bedeuten~ ("to mean") and ~Deutsch~ ("German") reflects their shared root: both trace to the Proto-Germanic word for "people," *~þeudō~. Cognate with Dutch ~beduiden~, Icelandic ~þýða~ ("to translate"), Swedish ~tyda~, Danish ~tyde~.
en.bedienen: From ~be-~ + ~dienen~ ("to serve"). The prefix ~be-~ (from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near") makes the base verb transitive. ~Dienen~ derives from Old High German ~dionōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~þewanōną~ ("to serve"), a denominative of *~þewaz~ ("servant, slave"). The compound ~bedienen~ adds the transitivity that ~dienen~ (which takes the dative) lacks: ~jemandem dienen~ ("to serve someone") vs. ~jemanden bedienen~ ("to attend to someone, operate a machine"). This shift reflects the modern range of the verb: waiting tables, operating machinery, serving a customer. The Proto-Germanic noun *~þewaz~ also underlies Old English ~þegn~, which elevated from "servant" to "warrior" to the Scottish title ~thane~, immortalized in Shakespeare's ~Macbeth~ — a social ascent that ~bedienen~ has not shared. Cognate with Dutch ~bedienen~.
en.bedrohen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~drohen~:

- ~drohen~: From MHG ~drōn~, from OHG ~drouwen~, related to ~dräuen~ ("to threaten"), from Proto-West Germanic *~þrauwjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þrawjaną~ ("to desire, suffer, threaten"), from the noun *~þrawō~ ("suffering, longing"), from PIE *~trewh₁-~ ("to endure, suffer"). Cognate with Old English ~þrēagan~ ("to threaten, rebuke") and Old Norse ~þreyja~ ("to long for").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the action toward a specific object.

The prefix ~be-~ converts intransitive ~drohen~ ("to threaten," with a dative) into transitive ~bedrohen~ ("to threaten someone/something," with an accusative). The semantic path from PIE "to endure suffering" to "to threaten" is instructive: the one who causes suffering came to be understood as the one who threatens, an agent-patient reversal that occurred within the Germanic branch. The noun ~Bedrohung~ ("threat, menace") is the standard term in both everyday and legal German. English ~threat~ is not from the same root — it descends from Old English ~þrēat~ ("crowd, pressure"), from a different PIE stem.
en.bedürfen:
From MHG ~bedürfen~, ~bedurfen~, from OHG ~bidurfan~ ("need, require"). A compound of ~be-~ + ~dürfen~.

- ~dürfen~: From MHG ~durfen~, OHG ~durfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þurban~. Cognate with archaic English ~tharf~ ("to need").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix transforms the base verb and shifts its argument structure — ~bedürfen~ is one of the rare modern German verbs that governs the genitive case (~Wir bedürfen eines starken Mannes~), a construction now otherwise largely extinct.

The modern meanings of ~dürfen~ ("to be allowed to, may") and ~bedürfen~ ("to require, to need") have diverged sharply from each other. In older German, ~dürfen~ itself could mean "to need" (the genitive construction seen in Luther's Bible translation). ~Bedürfen~ preserved that older sense by adding the transitivizing ~be-~ prefix, while bare ~dürfen~ shifted to expressing permission. ~Bedürfnis~ ("need, desire") keeps the older meaning alive.
en.beeindrucken:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~eindrücken~ ("to press in, to impress"):

- ~drücken~: From MHG ~drücken~, from OHG ~drucchen~, ~drukken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þrukkijan~ ("to press"). A doublet of ~drucken~ ("to print"), which has Upper German lack of umlaut before a velar geminate — the first German printers worked in southern Germany.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the impressing action onto a person.
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). Conveys inward direction.

~Beeindrucken~ means "to impress" — literally to press an image into someone's mind. The noun ~Eindruck~ ("impression") is itself a calque of Latin ~impressiō~ (from ~in-~ + ~premere~, "to press into"). The parallel is strikingly exact: both the Latin and Germanic compounds use "in" + "press" to describe how experiences stamp themselves upon consciousness. The doublet ~drucken~/~drücken~ is a fascinating case of semantic specialization through dialect: Proto-West Germanic *~þrukkijan~ meant simply "to press," but when southern German printers adopted the technology in the 15th century, their local dialect form ~drucken~ (without umlaut) became the technical term for printing, while the umlauted ~drücken~ retained the general sense of pressing and pushing.
en.beeinflussen: ~beeinflussen~ is a denominal verb formed from ~Einfluss~ ("influence") with the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near") and the infinitive suffix ~-en~, meaning "to influence, to have an effect on." ~Einfluss~ itself is a calque of Latin ~influentia~ (from ~influere~, "to flow in"), rendered as ~ein-~ ("in-") + ~Fluss~ ("flow, river"), from Middle High German ~vluz~, from Old High German ~fluz~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fluti~, from Proto-Germanic *~flutiz~, from PIE *~plewd-~ ("to flow, run"). Note that German ~Fluss~ and Latin ~fluvius~ are not cognates — Latin took the branch *~bʰlewH-~ while Germanic took *~plewd-~. No simplex *~einflussen~ exists in German; ~beeinflussen~ is formed directly from the noun ~Einfluss~, making it a beautiful example of double prefixation: ~be-~ + ~ein-~ + ~Fluss~ + ~-en~. The concept of influence as a "flowing in" — originally astrological, describing stellar emanations flowing into human affairs — passed through Latin, was calqued into German, and then verbalized with the ~be-~ prefix that adds the transitive sense of directing that flow upon something.
en.beeinträchtigen:
A denominative verb formed from ~Eintracht~ ("concord, harmony, unity") with the prefix ~be-~ and verbal suffix ~-igen~, yielding the sense "to act against someone's harmony." The core noun ~Eintracht~ derives from Middle High German ~eintraht~ ("agreement, accord"), which developed from the MHG phrase ~über ein tragen~ ("to come to agreement, to agree"), literally "to carry into one." The MHG noun is a borrowing and phonetic adaptation from Middle Low German ~ēndracht~, analyzable as ~ein~ ("one") + ~Tracht~ ("bearing, carrying"). ~Tracht~ itself comes from MHG ~traht~, from OHG ~trahta~, from Proto-Germanic *~drahtuz~, from the verb *~draganą~ ("to drag, to carry"), whence modern ~tragen~. Cognate with Dutch ~dracht~ and English ~draught~.

The prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near") makes the verb transitive, and the overall meaning of ~beeinträchtigen~ is "to impair, to detract from, to prejudice" -- literally, to act upon someone's concord. This is a beautiful example of German's talent for building complex concepts from transparent components. The related ~Eintracht~ lives on as the name of several German football clubs, most famously ~Eintracht Frankfurt~, where it retains its original sense of unity. Note that despite superficial similarity, ~Eintracht~ is historically unrelated to ~trachten~ ("to strive"), which comes from Latin ~tractāre~ via OHG ~trahtōn~, though modern speakers often associate the two.
en.beenden: ~beenden~ combines the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near," making the verb transitive) with ~enden~ ("to end"), from Middle High German ~enden~, from Old High German ~entōn~. ~Enden~ is itself a denominal verb formed from ~Ende~ ("end"), which comes from Old High German ~enti~, from Proto-West Germanic *~andī~, from Proto-Germanic *~andijaz~, from PIE *~h₂entíos~ ("front, forehead, tip") — the same root that gave Latin ~ante~ ("before"). The PIE sense of "forehead" developed into "boundary, edge, end" as the front of something became conceptually the limit of it. The prefix ~be-~ adds a sense of completion and transitivity: while ~enden~ can be intransitive ("the road ends"), ~beenden~ always takes a direct object ("to finish, conclude something"). A subtle semantic note: ~beenden~ implies a deliberate, willed conclusion, while ~enden~ is neutral — a process can ~enden~ on its own, but a person ~beendet~ it.
en.befassen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~fassen~:

- ~fassen~: From MHG ~vazzen~, from OHG ~fazzōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fatōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fatōną~ ("to fetch, to hold, to grasp"). Cognate with English ~fetch~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This inseparable prefix makes the verb reflexive-transitive — directing the action of grasping upon a subject.

~Sich befassen mit~ means "to deal with, to occupy oneself with, to engage with" — ~sich mit einem Problem befassen~ ("to deal with a problem"). The physical root is still visible: ~fassen~ means "to grasp, to seize," and ~befassen~ extends this into the intellectual domain — grasping a subject with the mind. The cognacy between German ~fassen~ and English ~fetch~ is a surprising one: both derive from Proto-Germanic *~fatōną~, but the semantics diverged — German kept the "grasp, hold" sense while English developed "go and get." The ~be-~ prefix performs its classic function of turning an intransitive into a transitive-reflexive construction: one does not merely grasp (~fassen~), one actively engages oneself with a topic (~sich befassen~). The word is characteristic of administrative and academic German: committees ~befassen sich mit Anträgen~ ("occupy themselves with applications"), scholars ~befassen sich mit Forschungsfragen~ ("engage with research questions").
en.befestigen:
From Middle High German ~bevestigen~, equivalent to ~be-~ + ~festigen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~festigen~:

- ~festigen~: From ~fest~ + ~-igen~, where ~fest~ descends from MHG ~vest~, from OHG ~festi~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fast~, from Proto-Germanic *~fastuz~ ("firm, solid"). Cognate with English ~fast~ (originally meaning "firm, fixed," as in ~hold fast~ or ~steadfast~, before acquiring its speed sense through the idea of running firmly or decisively).
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive: "to fasten something onto."

The semantic range of ~befestigen~ spans from the physical ("to attach, fasten, secure") to the military ("to fortify"), preserving the original Germanic sense of making something firm and immovable. A ~Befestigung~ can be both a wall bracket and a fortress. English ~fast~ and German ~fest~ are doublets that drifted apart dramatically: English ~fast~ primarily means "quick" today, while German ~fest~ still means "firm, solid, fixed." The PIE root also yielded Old Norse ~fastr~ and Gothic ~fastan~ ("to hold firm"), the latter giving rise to the religious practice of ~fasting~, originally meaning "to hold firmly to a discipline."
en.befinden:
From Middle High German ~bevinden~, from Old High German ~bifindan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bifinþan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~finden~:

- ~finden~: From MHG ~vinden~, from OHG ~findan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~finþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~finþaną~, from PIE *~pent-~ ("to go, pass; path, bridge").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The semantic development from "to find (transitively)" to "to be located; to judge, deem" reflects the ~be-~ prefix's tendency to shift meaning toward a state or condition. The reflexive ~sich befinden~ ("to be located, to feel") developed in Early New High German.
en.befragen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~fragen~:

- ~fragen~: From MHG ~vrāgen~, from OHG ~frāgēn~, ~frahēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frāgēn~, a denominative verb from *~frāgu~ ("question"). Cognate with Saterland Frisian ~fräigje~, Dutch ~vragen~, Low German ~fragen~, English ~frain~ (archaic "to ask, inquire"), Swedish ~fråga~, and Icelandic ~fregna~ ("to hear of, learn about").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This inseparable prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the asking at a specific person or topic.

Where simple ~fragen~ means "to ask" in general, ~befragen~ means "to question, to interrogate, to consult" -- the ~be-~ prefix directs the act at a particular person and implies a more thorough, structured inquiry. One ~fragt~ a friend casually, but one ~befragt~ a witness, a database, or an expert. The derived noun ~Befragung~ ("survey, interrogation") is standard in social-science and legal contexts.
en.befreien:
~be-~ + ~freien~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~freien~/~frei~:

- ~frei~/~freien~: From MHG ~vrī~, from OHG ~frī~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frī~, from Proto-Germanic *~frijaz~ ("free, beloved"), from PIE *~preyH-~ ("to love, to please"). The denominative verb ~freien~ ("to woo, to court") is from Proto-Germanic *~frijōną~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the adjective-derived verb transitive — one ~befreit~ someone or something.

~Befreien~ means "to liberate, to free, to release." The etymology of ~frei~ conceals one of the most remarkable semantic shifts in Indo-European: PIE *~preyH-~ meant "to love, to please," and *~priyós~ meant "dear, beloved" (compare Sanskrit ~priyá-~ "dear, favorite"). In the Germanic and Celtic branches alone, this word for "beloved" acquired the meaning "free" — the idea being that the beloved, the members of one's own tribe and family, were the free people, as opposed to slaves and outsiders. Proto-Germanic *~frijaz~ thus meant both "beloved" and "free," and the noun *~frijōndz~ ("lover, friend") became English ~friend~ and German ~Freund~. The goddess ~Freyja~ / ~Frigg~ bears a name from the same root. ~Befreien~ thus carries within it an ancient equation: to liberate someone is to restore them to the status of a loved one, a member of the community.
en.befördern:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~fördern~. The verb is either a ~be-~ prefixed form of ~fördern~ ("to promote, to advance") or a derivation from ~vorder~ ("front, fore"):

- ~fördern~: From MHG ~vürdern~, from OHG ~furdiren~, a derivation from ~vorder~/~furdir~ ("further, forward"), from Proto-Germanic *~furþer-~ ("further"), from PIE *~per-~ ("forward, through"). Cognate with Old English ~fyrðran~ ("to further"), English ~further~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

Before the 16th century, ~befördern~ was used like its root ~fördern~ in the general sense of "to advance." From the 16th century onward, it developed the specific meanings "to transport" and "to support." In the 18th or 19th century — sources disagree on the exact date — it acquired the meaning "to promote in rank" as a German replacement for the French loanword ~avancieren~ ("to advance"). It also came to mean "to ship, to forward goods" as a replacement for ~spedieren~. The root ~fördern~ itself is related to English ~further~: both derive from the same Proto-Germanic comparative form meaning "more forward." The original nebenforms ~bevordern~ and ~befürdern~ show the vowel variation typical of Middle High German dialects.
en.befürchten:
From Middle High German ~befürhten~, ~bevürhten~, from Old High German ~biforhten~, ~bifurihtōn~, ~biforhtōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bifurhtijan~, equivalent to ~be-~ + ~fürchten~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~fürchten~:

- ~fürchten~: From MHG ~vürhten~, from OHG ~furihtan~, synchronically analyzable as ~Furcht~ + ~-en~. The noun ~Furcht~ descends from MHG ~vorht~, OHG ~forhta~, from Proto-Germanic *~furhtō~ ("fear"), from PIE *~pr̥k-~ ("to fear"). The modern vowel ~-ü-~ is due to umlaut influence from the verb.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, allowing a direct object: one ~befürchtet~ a specific outcome.

Cognate with Middle Low German ~bevrüchten~ and the obsolete English ~befright~ ("to terrify"). The PIE root *~pr̥k-~ also produced English ~fright~ (though via a different Germanic stem, *~furhtį̄~). The semantic distinction between ~fürchten~ and ~befürchten~ is subtle but important: ~fürchten~ expresses a general emotional state of fear, while ~befürchten~ implies a rational apprehension about a specific, anticipated negative outcome. One ~fürchtet~ the dark but ~befürchtet~ that it will rain. This distinction between visceral fear and reasoned worry has no neat parallel in English, which uses ~fear~ for both.
en.begeben:
~be-~ + ~geben~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gëben~, from OHG ~gëban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebaną~ ("to give"), from PIE *~gʰebʰ-~ ("to give, move"). Cognate with English ~give~ (from the Old Norse descendant rather than the native English reflex).
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

~Begeben~ is a formal, almost literary verb with several distinct meanings. The reflexive ~sich begeben~ ("to betake oneself, to go, to proceed") carries an air of deliberate, purposeful movement: ~er begab sich auf den Heimweg~ ("he made his way home"). The impersonal construction ~es begab sich~ ("it came to pass") is the opening formula of biblical narrative in Martin Luther's translation — ~und es begab sich zu jener Zeit~ — and sounds archly archaic in any other context. A third meaning, "to issue" (a financial instrument), belongs to the world of banking: ~eine Anleihe begeben~ ("to issue a bond"). And the reflexive ~sich eines Rechts begeben~ ("to relinquish a right") adds yet another layer. This semantic diversity — going, happening, issuing, renouncing — all radiates from the core image of ~geben~: to give oneself over to movement, to give forth a story, to give up a claim. Cognate with the now-archaic English ~begive~.
en.begegnen:
From Middle High German ~begegenen~, ~begagenen~, from Old High German ~bigaganan~ ("to meet, to encounter"), with prefix from Proto-West Germanic *~gaginijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~gaginjaną~. Equivalent to ~be-~ + ~gegnen~ ("to encounter"). Cognate with Dutch ~bejegenen~.

Despite the surface resemblance to a standard ~be-~ compound, ~begegnen~ has a unique history. The root ~gegnen~ (now archaic in isolation) descends from the Proto-Germanic adverb/preposition *~gagin~ ("against, toward"), cognate with English ~again~ and ~against~ (from Old English ~ongēan~, "toward, against"). To meet someone was, etymologically, to go toward them — to come face to face. The verb takes the dative case (~ich begegne dir~, not ~*ich begegne dich~), a relic of its original directional sense: one encounters someone by moving toward them. The formal ~begegnen~ and the informal ~treffen~ ("to meet," from "to hit, to strike") offer an illuminating contrast: ~begegnen~ emphasizes the chance or directional quality of an encounter, while ~treffen~ emphasizes the impact — you cross paths versus you collide. Cognate with Dutch ~bejegenen~ ("to treat, to deal with"), which has shifted to a purely interpersonal meaning.
en.begehen:
From Middle High German ~begēn~, ~begān~, from Old High German ~bigēn~, ~bigān~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bigān~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gān~, ~gēn~, from OHG ~gān~, ~gēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gān~, from Proto-Germanic *~gāną~, from PIE *~ǵʰeh₁-~ ("to leave"). Cognate with English ~go~, Dutch ~gaan~, Swedish and Danish ~gå~.
- ~be-~: A prefix that typically makes an intransitive verb transitive, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~ ("by, near"), from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near").

The ~be-~ prefix transforms the intransitive "to go" into a transitive verb meaning "to walk upon" or "to commit (a crime, a celebration)." Cognate with Dutch ~begaan~ and the archaic English ~bego~. The dual meaning is striking: ~eine Straße begehen~ means "to walk along a street," while ~ein Verbrechen begehen~ means "to commit a crime" and ~ein Jubiläum begehen~ means "to celebrate an anniversary." All three senses share the underlying idea of "going through" something, whether a path, an act, or an occasion. The spelling with silent ~-h-~ in ~gehen~ was introduced by analogy with ~sehen~, in which the ~h~ had become mute but was preserved in writing.
en.begeistern:
From ~be-~ + ~Geist~ + ~-ern~, a denominal verb meaning literally "to fill with spirit, to inspire":

- ~Geist~: From MHG ~geist~, from OHG ~geist~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gaist~, from Proto-Germanic *~gaistaz~, from PIE *~ǵʰóysdos~ ("anger, agitation"). Cognate with Dutch ~geest~, West Frisian ~geast~, English ~ghost~, Swedish ~gast~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the denominal verb transitive — it directs the filling of spirit onto an object.

The etymological link between ~Geist~ ("spirit, mind") and English ~ghost~ is one of Germanic's most dramatic semantic divergences. Both descend from PGmc *~gaistaz~, but where German elevated ~Geist~ to mean "intellect, spirit, mind" (as in ~Zeitgeist~, "spirit of the age," or ~Geisteswissenschaften~, "humanities"), English narrowed ~ghost~ to mean a spectral apparition. The PIE root *~ǵʰóysdos~ originally meant "agitation, fury" — the ~Geist~ was a state of being seized by something beyond oneself. ~Begeistern~ preserves this ancient sense beautifully: to be ~begeistert~ is to be filled with spirit, seized by enthusiasm, possessed by excitement. The suffix ~-ern~ (a verbal derivation pattern also seen in ~klettern~, ~wandern~) creates the denominal verb from the noun ~Geist~, and the ~be-~ prefix makes it transitive. The derived noun ~Begeisterung~ ("enthusiasm, excitement") is among the most expressive words in the German emotional vocabulary.
en.beginnen: From Middle High German ~beginnen~, from Old High German ~biginnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biginnan~, from Proto-Germanic *~biginnaną~. The etymology of the simplex root *~ginnaną~ is debated: some scholars connect it to PIE *~gʰen-~ ("to gape, yawn, open"), suggesting an original sense of "to open" and hence "to begin." Others consider it a substrate word with no secure Indo-European etymology. A class III strong verb with the nasal-infix ablaut pattern (~beginnen~, ~begann~, ~begonnen~). Cognate with Low German ~beginnen~, Dutch ~beginnen~, West Frisian ~begjinne~, English ~begin~.
en.begleiten:
From Middle High German ~begleiten~ (14th century), from an underlying *~begeleiten~, a confluence of ~beleiten~ and ~geleiten~, both prefixed forms of ~leiten~ ("to lead"). Compound of ~be-~ + ~Geleit~ ("escort, convoy") + ~-en~:

- ~Geleit~ (root): A nominalization of ~geleiten~ ("to escort, conduct"), itself from MHG ~geleite~, a compound of ~ge-~ + ~leiten~. ~Leiten~ ("to lead") descends from OHG ~leitan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laidijan~, a causative verb formed from PGmc *~laidijaną~, from *~līþaną~ ("to travel, go") + causative suffix *~-janą~. The root *~līþaną~ stems from PIE *~leyt-~ ("to go forth, depart"), the same root that underlies Gothic terms for dying and departing.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The critical warning for learners: ~begleiten~ does NOT descend from ~gleiten~ ("to glide"), despite looking like it should. The ~gl-~ sequence is a coincidence — the ~g~ belongs to the old collective prefix ~ge-~ and the ~l~ begins ~leiten~ ("to lead"). The underlying idea is "to provide someone with a Geleit (escort)," not "to glide alongside." Cognate with Dutch ~begeleiden~.
en.begreifen:
From Middle High German ~begrifen~, from Old High German ~bigrīfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bigrīpan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~greifen~:

- ~greifen~: From MHG ~grīfen~, from OHG ~grīfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grīpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grīpaną~ ("to grab, grasp"), from PIE *~gʰreyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). Cognate with English ~grip~, ~gripe~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~ ("by, at, around"), from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). As a prefix, ~be-~ makes intransitive verbs transitive or adds a thoroughgoing sense.

The semantic development from "to grasp physically" to "to comprehend mentally" is one of the most universal metaphors in language. English ~comprehend~ follows the exact same pattern: Latin ~comprehendere~ means "to seize, grasp together." Similarly, English ~grasp~ itself moved from physical to mental. German preserves both layers: ~greifen~ remains physical, while ~begreifen~ is almost entirely intellectual. Cognate with Dutch ~begrijpen~ ("to understand") and Low German ~begriepen~. The related noun ~Begriff~ ("concept, notion") literally means "that which has been grasped."
en.begrenzen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~grenzen~, from the noun ~Grenze~:

- ~Grenze~: From MHG ~grenize~, borrowed from Old Polish ~granica~ ("border, frontier"). This Slavic loanword spread westward through German in the fifteenth century and was reinforced by Luther's Bible translation. It replaced older Germanic terms for "border." Doublet of ~Granne~ ("awn, bristle").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near").

~Grenze~ is one of the most important Slavic loanwords in German, reflecting centuries of contact along the eastern frontier. Before its arrival, German used words like ~Mark~ (as in ~Dänemark~, ~Ostmark~) and ~Rain~ for borders. The borrowed word conquered the entire semantic territory. ~Begrenzen~ adds the ~be-~ prefix to make the noun into a transitive verb: "to limit, to restrict, to mark the boundary of."
en.begründen:
Equivalent to ~be-~ + ~gründen~:

- ~gründen~: A denominal verb from ~Grund~ ("ground, reason, foundation"), from MHG ~grunt~, from OHG ~grunt~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grundu~, from Proto-Germanic *~grunduz~, from PIE *~gʰrendʰ-~ ("to grind").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the act of grounding onto an argument or institution.

~Begründen~ means "to found, to establish" and "to justify, to give reasons for" — a dual meaning rooted in the polysemy of ~Grund~ itself, which means both "ground" (physical foundation) and "reason" (logical foundation). One ~begründet~ a company (founds it on solid ground) or an argument (gives it reasons). The derived noun ~Begründung~ ("justification, reasoning") is standard in legal and academic German. The PIE root *~gʰrendʰ-~ ("to grind") connects to the physical sense of ground — what has been ground down, the bottom layer. English ~ground~ is a direct cognate of ~Grund~, and both preserve the same literal-to-figurative extension: from the earth beneath your feet to the basis of your argument. Cognate with Dutch ~begronden~.
en.begrüßen: From Middle High German ~begrüezen~, from Old High German ~bigruozen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bigrōtijan~; equivalent to ~be-~ + ~grüßen~. The prefix ~be-~ (from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near") intensifies and makes the greeting act outwardly directed. ~Grüßen~ itself derives from Middle High German ~grüezen~, from Old High German ~gruozzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grōtijan~. The noun ~Gruß~ ("greeting") is a deverbal form from Middle High German ~gruoȥ~. Cognate with German Low German ~begröten~, Dutch ~begroeten~, West Frisian ~begroetsje~, and English ~greet~ (from the same Proto-West Germanic root, without the ~be-~ prefix). The English cognate ~begreet~ exists but is archaic. A greeting, etymologically, is an act of touching or addressing — the root sense in Proto-West Germanic was likely physical approach or hailing.
en.behalten: ~behalten~ combines the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near," making the verb transitive) with ~halten~ ("to hold, keep"), from Middle High German ~halten~, from Old High German ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldaną~, of uncertain PIE origin — possibly from *~ḱel-~ ("to hide, conceal"). ~Behalten~ appears in Old High German as ~bihaltan~ and in Middle High German as ~behalten~. Its cognates across Germanic are striking: Dutch ~behouden~ ("to keep, preserve"), English ~behold~ — which preserves the older sense of "to hold in view, to grasp with the eyes" — and the Scandinavian forms Danish and Norwegian ~beholde~, Swedish ~behålla~. The English ~behold~ and German ~behalten~ are thus direct cognates, though ~behold~ has narrowed to visual perception while ~behalten~ means "to keep, retain." The prefix ~be-~ intensifies the holding, giving it the sense of retaining something in one's possession.
en.behandeln:
From Middle High German ~behandeln~ ("to extend one's hand to; to deal with"), from Old High German ~bihantalōn~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~handeln~:

- ~handeln~: From MHG ~handeln~, from OHG ~hantalōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~handulōn~, a denominative verb from Proto-Germanic *~handuz~ ("hand") with the frequentative suffix *~-lōną~. Cognate with Dutch ~handelen~, English ~handle~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically extends the action of the base verb onto an explicit object.

The verb's original MHG meaning — "to extend one's hand to someone" — is strikingly physical: the medical sense of "to treat a patient" preserves exactly this original gesture, the physician's hand laid upon the sufferer. From the same handshake root comes "to deal with a subject" (~ein Thema behandeln~) and "to treat someone a certain way" (~jemanden respektvoll behandeln~). The word ~Behandlung~ thus covers medical treatment, thematic coverage, and interpersonal conduct — all radiating from the extended hand. Cognate with Dutch ~behandelen~.
en.behaupten: ~behaupten~ is a denominal verb formed from ~Haupt~ ("head, chief") with the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near") and the infinitive suffix ~-en~, meaning "to assert, claim, maintain." The original sense was "to maintain as the main point, to hold as chief." ~Haupt~ comes from Middle High German ~houbet~, from Old High German ~houbit~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haubud~, from Proto-Germanic *~haubudą~, from PIE *~káput~ ("head") — the same root that produced Latin ~caput~ ("head"), English ~head~, and the anatomical term ~capital~. The ~-au-~ in ~Haupt~ reflects Upper German dialectal development where ~-ou-~ did not undergo umlaut before labials; the Central German umlaut form survives in ~Häuptling~ ("chieftain") and the phrase ~zu Häupten~ ("at the head of"). The semantic journey from "head" to "to assert" — through the intermediate step of "to hold the chief position, to maintain as one's main claim" — is a vivid example of how body-part metaphors generate abstract intellectual vocabulary.
en.beheben:
From Middle High German ~beheben~ ("to take away"), equivalent to ~be-~ + ~heben~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~heben~:

- ~heben~: From MHG ~heben~, ~heven~ (rarer ~heffen~), from OHG ~heffen~, ~heven~, from Proto-West Germanic *~habbjan~. Cognate with Dutch ~heffen~, English ~heave~, and Danish ~haeve~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, allowing a direct object like a problem or defect.

The semantic journey of ~beheben~ is striking: it originally meant "to take away, remove" in Middle High German, and this sense of removal persisted into the modern meaning "to fix, repair, remedy." Fixing a problem is, after all, removing it. The ~-b-~ in ~heben~ is itself a product of regularization; in Middle High German, the paradigm alternated between fricative ~-v-~ and stop ~-b-~, and the ~-b-~ won out. The original fricative survives in the related noun ~Hefe~ ("yeast," literally "that which heaves or rises"). The original past forms ~hub~ and ~gehaben~ were retained into Early Modern German before being replaced. English ~heave~ is a cognate from the same source. In Austrian German, ~beheben~ has an additional meaning not found in Germany: "to withdraw" (money from an account), preserving the older "take away" sense in a financial context.
en.beherrschen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~herrschen~:

- ~herrschen~: From MHG ~hērschen~/~hērsen~, from OHG ~hērrisōn~/~hērisōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hairisōn~ ("to be strong, dominate"), from the root of ~hehr~ ("sublime, awe-inspiring"), from Proto-Germanic *~hairaz~ ("grey, hoary"), from PIE *~(s)k̂eh₃i-~, an extension of *~(s)k̂eh₃-~ ("grey"). The connection between "grey-haired" and "lordly" reflects a culture that equated age with authority. The first element was later remodeled after ~Herr~ ("lord, gentleman") during the Middle High German period. Cognate with English ~hoar~ ("grey").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~ ("by, at, around"), from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). As a prefix, ~be-~ typically makes intransitive verbs transitive or adds an exhaustive sense.

The ~be-~ prefix makes the intransitive ~herrschen~ ("to rule over") into a transitive verb: ~beherrschen~ means "to master" or "to dominate" something directly. The reflexive ~sich beherrschen~ means "to restrain oneself" -- literally to rule over oneself. Cognate with Dutch ~beheersen~ and Yiddish ~באַהערשן~.
en.beinhalten: ~beinhalten~ is a denominal verb formed from ~Inhalt~ ("content, contents") with the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near") and the infinitive suffix ~-en~, meaning "to contain, to entail." ~Inhalt~ breaks down by surface analysis as ~in-~ + ~Halt~ (a nominalized form of ~halten~, "to hold"), giving the sense of "that which is held within." ~Halten~ derives from Proto-Germanic *~haldaną~, of uncertain PIE origin. The prefix ~be-~ here does not add its usual transitivity — ~beinhalten~ is already transitive — but instead adds a formal or slightly elevated register: while ~enthalten~ ("to contain") is the everyday word, ~beinhalten~ appears in legal, academic, and official prose. A common learner's error is to misparse the word as ~bei-~ + ~nhalten~, but ~bei-~ is not a verbal prefix in modern German in this function; the correct segmentation is ~be-~ + ~Inhalt~ + ~-en~. No simplex *~inhalten~ exists as a standard verb — ~beinhalten~ is fully lexicalized as a whole.
en.beitragen:
Compound of ~bei-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganą~ ("to draw, drag"), from PIE *~dʰregʰ-~ ("to pull, draw, drag"). A class VI strong verb. Cognate with English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Dutch ~dragen~.
- ~bei-~: From MHG ~bī-~, from OHG ~bī-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). As a separable prefix, ~bei-~ conveys accompaniment, proximity, and the idea of adding something alongside something else.

The compound's meaning — "to contribute, to lend to a cause" — is a clean metaphor: one carries (~tragen~) something alongside (~bei-~) the efforts of others. The noun ~Beitrag~ ("contribution, fee, article, premium") covers everything from a financial subscription to an essay in a journal to an insurance payment, always carrying the sense of something carried alongside a collective effort. That ~tragen~ is cognate with English ~draw~ and ~drag~ is not immediately obvious — the High German consonant shift converted the initial ~d~ to ~t~, obscuring the relationship. Cognate with Dutch ~bijdragen~.
en.bekennen:
~be-~ + ~kennen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~kennen~:

- ~kennen~: From MHG ~kennen~, from OHG ~kennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kannjan~, a causative of *~kunnan~ ("to know"), from Proto-Germanic *~kannijaną~ ("to make known"), from PIE *~ǵneh₃-~ ("to know").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix here makes the verb transitive and intensifies the action — from mere knowing to active declaration.

~Bekennen~ means "to confess, to profess, to avow" — to make one's knowledge or belief public. The prefix ~be-~ transforms interior knowing (~kennen~) into outward declaration. The compound appears already in Old High German as ~bikennen~ ("to recognize, acknowledge"). The derived noun ~Bekenntnis~ ("confession, creed") carries deep religious weight in German: the ~Augsburger Bekenntnis~ (Augsburg Confession, 1530) was the foundational document of Lutheranism. The reflexive ~sich bekennen zu~ ("to profess, to declare allegiance to") remains one of the most forceful verbs of commitment in German. Cognate with Dutch ~bekennen~ ("to confess").
en.bekommen:
From Middle High German ~bekomen~, from Old High German ~biqueman~, ~bikweman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bikweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~bikwemaną~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~kommen~:

- ~kommen~: From MHG ~quemen~, ~komen~, ~kumen~, from OHG ~queman~ (rarer ~cuman~, ~chuman~), from Proto-West Germanic *~kweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~kwemaną~, from the aorist subjunctive of PIE *~gʷemt~ ("to take a step, arrive"), from *~gʷem-~ ("to step").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

Despite the compound's formal resemblance to English ~become~, the two have diverged sharply in meaning: German ~bekommen~ means "to receive, get," while English ~become~ means "to come to be." This false-friend pair is a famous pitfall for learners in both directions. Cognate with Dutch ~bekomen~.
en.bekämpfen:
From Middle High German ~bekempfen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~kampfen~:

- ~kampfen~: From MHG ~kempfen~, from OHG *~kempfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kampijan~, a denominative verb from *~kamp~ ("field, battlefield"), itself borrowed from Latin ~campus~ ("field"). The semantic journey from "open field" to "battlefield" to "fight" reflects how combat in the ancient world took place on open terrain. Cognate with English ~camp~ (which retained the spatial sense) and ~champion~ (via Old French from Latin ~campio~, "fighter on the field").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~ ("by, at, around"), from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, turning the intransitive "to fight" into "to fight against something."

The word ~Kampf~ is one of several Latin military loans that entered Germanic languages during the centuries of Roman-Germanic contact along the ~limes~, the empire's northern frontier. Dutch ~kampen~ ("to fight, to struggle") is a close cognate, while English ~combat~ arrived later from French, covering similar semantic ground through an entirely different route.
en.belasten:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~Last~ + ~-en~:

- ~Last~: From MHG ~last~, from OHG ~last~, ~hlast~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlasti~ ("load, burden"), from PIE *~kleh₂-~ ("to put, lay out"). The initial ~hl-~ cluster, still visible in OHG, was simplified to ~l-~ in later German. Cognate with English ~last~ (in the sense of a unit of weight/cargo) and ~laden~ ("to load"); English ~ballast~ is a compound containing the same root.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near").

The semantic range of ~belasten~ maps neatly onto different kinds of burden: physical ("to load, to stress"), psychological ("to burden, to weigh on"), legal ("to incriminate"), and financial ("to debit, to charge an account"). The related ~belästigen~ ("to harass, to bother") looks similar but derives from the adjective ~lästig~ ("annoying") and carries a distinctly different meaning -- an annoyance rather than a load.
en.belaufen:
From Middle High German ~beloufen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~laufen~. Cognate with Dutch ~belopen~:

- ~laufen~: From MHG ~loufen~, from OHG ~hloufan~, ~loufan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaupan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaupaną~ ("to jump forward, to leap"), from PIE *~klewb-~ ("to spring, to hop"). Cognate with Dutch ~lopen~, English ~leap~ and ~lope~, Danish ~løbe~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The reflexive ~sich belaufen auf~ means "to amount to, to total" — an oddly physical metaphor for a numerical concept, as though a sum "runs itself" to a certain point. The root verb ~laufen~ underwent a dramatic semantic shift: Proto-Germanic *~hlaupaną~ meant "to leap, to jump," and this sense is preserved in English ~leap~, while German ~laufen~ gradually broadened from "leaping" to "running" to simply "going on foot." The initial ~hl-~ cluster, still visible in Old High German ~hloufan~, was simplified to ~l-~ during the Middle High German period — a common fate for initial clusters throughout Germanic. Lithuanian ~klùpti~ ("to stumble") is a distant Baltic cousin from the same PIE root.
en.belegen: ~belegen~ combines the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near," making the verb transitive) with ~legen~ ("to lay, place"), from Middle High German ~legen~, from Old High German ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjaną~, from PIE *~logʰéyeti~ — a causative form of the root *~legh-~ ("to lie, lay down"). The prefix ~be-~ shifts the meaning from the intransitive placement to covering or occupying a space entirely. Cognates of ~legen~ include English ~lay~, Danish ~lægge~, and the English prefix verb ~belay~ — the nautical term for securing a rope, which is the direct English cognate of ~belegen~. A delightful false friend: ~belegen~ can mean "to book" (a seat, table, or room) as well as "to document" or "to coat" — contexts where laying something upon a surface shifts into reservation or proof.
en.beleuchten:
From Middle High German ~beliuhten~, from Old High German ~biliuhten~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~leuchten~:

- ~leuchten~: From OHG ~liuhten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~liuhtijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~liuhtijaną~, a denominative or factitive verb from *~leuhtą~ ("light") or *~leuhtaz~ ("light, bright"), ultimately from PIE *~lewk-~ ("to shine"). Cognate with English ~light~, Dutch ~lichten~, and Latin ~lux~ ("light").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~ ("at, by, near"), from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, turning "to shine" into "to illuminate (something)."

The PIE root *~lewk-~ is one of the most productive roots in Indo-European languages, giving rise not only to ~leuchten~ and ~light~ but also to Latin ~lūcēre~ ("to shine"), whence ~lucid~ and ~Lucifer~ (literally "light-bearer"). The ~be-~ prefix transforms intransitive ~leuchten~ ("to shine, glow") into transitive ~beleuchten~ ("to illuminate, to shed light on"), a pattern found across dozens of German ~be-~ verbs. In figurative usage, ~beleuchten~ also means "to examine" or "to shed light on a topic," mirroring English "to illuminate a subject."
en.belohnen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~Lohn~ + ~-en~:

- ~Lohn~: From MHG ~lôn~, from OHG ~lōn~ ("reward, wages"), from Proto-West Germanic *~laun~, from Proto-Germanic *~launą~, from PIE *~leh₂w-~ ("to capture, enjoy, profit"). Cognate with Old English ~lēan~ ("reward"), Gothic ~laun~, and -- surprisingly -- Latin ~lucrum~ ("profit," whence English ~lucre~ and ~lucrative~) and Greek ~leía~ ("booty, spoils").
- ~be-~: Inseparable prefix from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). Here it functions as a transitivizer, turning the noun ~Lohn~ into a transitive verb meaning "to give a reward to."

~Belohnen~ means "to reward." The deep etymology reveals that ~Lohn~ ("wages, reward") and ~lucre~ ("monetary gain") share a common ancestor in the PIE root *~leh₂w-~, whose original sense was "to seize, to enjoy the fruits of seizure." The semantic path from "captured spoils" to "earned wages" to "reward" reflects the evolution from raiding economies to settled labor. The related Old English word ~lēan~ ("reward") died out in English, replaced by the French borrowing ~reward~ (from Old Norman French ~rewarder~, "to regard, to repay"). German preserved the native Germanic term, making ~belohnen~ a linguistic survivor.
en.bemerken:
From Middle High German ~bemerken~, equivalent to ~be-~ + ~merken~:

- ~merken~: From MHG ~merken~, from OHG ~merken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~markōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~markōną~ ("to mark, to note"), from *~markō~ ("boundary, sign"), from PIE *~merǵ-~ ("boundary, edge").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the act of noting onto a specific object.

~Bemerken~ means "to notice, to remark, to observe" — one ~bemerkt~ something that catches one's attention. The derived noun ~Bemerkung~ ("remark, comment, observation") straddles the boundary between perception and speech: a ~Bemerkung~ can be something you merely notice or something you say aloud. The PIE root *~merǵ-~ ("boundary, edge") connects ~bemerken~ to an unexpectedly concrete origin: to notice something is to mark its boundary, to distinguish it from its surroundings. The same root gives English ~mark~, ~margin~, ~marquee~, and ~march~ (as in "the Welsh Marches" — border regions). Cognate with Dutch ~bemerken~, ~opmerken~.
en.bemühen:
From ~be-~ + ~Mühe~ + ~-en~. Compound of ~be-~ + the denominal verb derived from ~Mühe~:

- ~Mühe~: From MHG ~müeje~, ~müe~, from OHG ~muohī~, from the verb ~mühen~ ("to toil"), from Proto-West Germanic *~mōjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~mōjaną~ ("to tire"), from PIE *~meh₃-~ ("to cause toil, be a burden"). Cognate with Latin ~mōlēs~ ("burden, mass"), Greek ~mōlos~ ("turmoil"), and Slavic *~majati~ ("to detain, tire"). Compare Dutch ~bemoeien~ ("to interfere, to meddle").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive and reflexive: to direct effort at something or someone.

~Bemühen~ means "to make an effort, to take trouble" (reflexive ~sich bemühen~) and, less commonly, "to trouble, to bother" (transitive: ~darf ich Sie bemühen?~, "may I trouble you?"). The polite imperative ~bemühen Sie sich nicht!~ ("don't trouble yourself!") is a standard courtesy formula. The derived noun ~Bemühung~ ("effort, endeavor") appears frequently in formal correspondence. Dutch ~bemoeien~ is a cognate but has shifted toward negative meddling ("to interfere"), while German ~bemühen~ retains the more neutral sense of earnest effort — the two words illustrate how the same compound can drift in opposite evaluative directions across closely related languages.
en.benennen:
From Middle High German ~benennen~, from Old High German *~binemnen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~nennen~:

- ~nennen~: From MHG ~nennen~, ~nemmen~, ~nemnen~, from OHG ~nemnen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~namnijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~namnijaną~ ("to name"), a denominative from *~namô~ ("name"), from PIE *~h₁nómn̥~ ("name").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive with the sense of bestowing a name upon.

~Benennen~ means "to name, to designate, to label" — to bestow a name ~upon~ something or someone. Where ~nennen~ simply means "to call" or "to name," the prefix ~be-~ adds the sense of formal designation or bestowal. The PIE root *~h₁nómn̥~ ("name") is one of the most stable words in all Indo-European languages, barely changing across thousands of years: Latin ~nōmen~, Greek ~ónoma~ (with metathesis), Sanskrit ~nā́man~, Old Irish ~ainm~, and English ~name~ all descend from it. The Old Saxon cognate ~binemnian~ and Old English ~benemnan~ show the same ~be-~ + ~name~ compound across the West Germanic languages. The archaic English ~bename~ ("to name, to designate") is a direct cognate, though it fell out of use centuries ago. Cognate with Dutch ~benoemen~, Swedish ~benämna~, Old English ~benemnan~.
en.benutzen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~nutzen~:

- ~nutzen~: From MHG ~nutzen~, from OHG ~nuzzōn~, a denominative verb from the noun ~Nutz~ ("use, benefit"), from Proto-West Germanic *~nuti~, from PGmc *~nutją~, from PIE *~h₂ewd-~ ("to enjoy, benefit"). The double spelling ~nutzen~/~nützen~ in modern German reflects a North-South dialect split, with ~nutzen~ (without umlaut) prevailing in the North and ~nützen~ in the South.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The ~be-~ prefix adds a transitive, directed quality: while ~nutzen~ can be used intransitively ("es nutzt nichts" — "it is of no use"), ~benutzen~ is always transitive and implies actively putting something to use. Cognate with English ~need~ (via the same PGmc root), Dutch ~benutten~.
en.benötigen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~nötigen~:

- ~nötigen~: From MHG ~nōtigen~, from OHG ~nōtigōn~, a denominative verb ("to compel, necessitate") derived from the adjective ~nötig~ ("necessary"), itself from the noun ~Not~ ("distress, need, compulsion"), from OHG ~nōt~, from Proto-West Germanic *~naudi~, from Proto-Germanic *~naudiz~, from PIE *~neh₂w-tí-s~ — a *~ti-~-stem related to the root of *~nawiz~ ("corpse"). The connection between "need" and "corpse" reflects a grim ancient logic: the PIE root evokes the state of the dead — inertness, absence of vital force — and the Germanic word shifted this toward the desperate need or compulsion that threatens to reduce the living to that state.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive or adjective-derived verb into a transitive one.

The ~be-~ prefix shifts ~nötigen~ (intransitive "to compel") toward the transitive "to need, require." The semantic distinction in German is subtle: ~brauchen~ and ~benötigen~ both mean "to need," but ~benötigen~ carries a more formal register. Cognate with Dutch ~benodigden~, English ~need~ (from the same PIE root via *~naudiz~).
en.beobachten:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~Obacht~ + ~-en~:

- ~Obacht~ (root): From MHG ~ob-~ + ~Acht~. The element ~ob-~ is a preposition meaning "over, upon, above" (cognate with Latin ~ob-~). ~Acht~ here is from the noun of "attention, heed" (OHG ~ahta~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ahtu~) — not from ~acht~ ("eight"). The compound ~Obacht~ thus means "watching over, heedful attention from above" — a watchful, supervisory gaze. The related survival ~Obacht geben~ ("to pay attention") still appears in Southern German and Austrian speech.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The layering is vivid: ~beobachten~ = be- + ob- + Acht + -en = "to apply upward-directed attention to something," a verb built from three ancient strata of meaning. The English near-parallel is ~observe~ (Latin ~ob-~ + ~servāre~, "to watch over"), which uses the same Latin prefix ~ob-~ in the same spatial sense of oversight. Cognate with Dutch ~observeren~ (a later Latin borrowing for the same concept).
en.beraten: ~beraten~ combines the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near," making the verb transitive) with ~raten~ ("to advise, counsel; to guess"), from Middle High German ~rāten~, from Old High German ~rātan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rādan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rēdaną~, from PIE *~Hreh₁dʰ-~ ("to think, arrange"). The same Germanic root produced Old English ~rǣdan~, which became modern English ~read~ — a remarkable semantic journey from "to interpret, counsel" to "to interpret written text." The cognate English ~rede~ (archaic: "counsel, advice") preserves the older meaning. Beraten appears in Old High German as ~birātan~ and in Middle High German as ~berāten~, with the obsolete English ~berede~ and ~beread~ as direct cognates. The noun ~Rat~ ("council, advice") and the city-council chamber ~Rathaus~ descend from the same root. ~Beraten~ adds the transitive force of ~be-~: one ~berät~ a person (gives them counsel), but also ~berät eine Frage~ (deliberates on a question).
en.berechnen:
Equivalent to ~be-~ + ~rechnen~:

- ~rechnen~: From MHG ~rechenen~, from OHG ~rehhanōn~, related to Proto-Germanic *~rekōną~ ("to count, to explain, to reckon").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive and adds the sense of applying calculation to something.

~Berechnen~ means "to calculate, to compute, to charge" — the ~be-~ prefix transforms ~rechnen~ ("to do arithmetic") into a transitive verb that applies calculation to a specific object: one ~berechnet~ a cost, a trajectory, a probability, or a fee. The additional sense "to charge" (~Wir berechnen Ihnen 50 Euro~, "We'll charge you 50 euros") shows the natural extension from counting to billing — what you calculate, you charge. The derived adjective ~berechnend~ ("calculating, scheming") carries a pejorative sense: a ~berechnender Mensch~ is someone who computes every social interaction for personal advantage. English ~reckon~ is a direct cognate of ~rechnen~, and both the German and English words retain the dual sense of mathematical computation and informal estimation (~I reckon~ ≈ ~ich rechne damit~). Cognate with Dutch ~berekenen~.
en.berechtigen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~recht~ + ~-igen~, a denominal derivation from the noun ~Recht~ ("right, law"):

- ~Recht~: From MHG ~rëht~, from OHG ~rëht~, from Proto-West Germanic *~reht~ ("right, straight"), from Proto-Germanic *~rehtaz~, from PIE *~h₃reǵ-~ ("to straighten, to direct"). Cognate with English ~right~, Dutch ~recht~, Danish ~ret~, Icelandic ~réttur~, Latin ~rēctus~ ("straight"), and Sanskrit ~ṛjú~ ("upright").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive: to confer a right upon someone.

The verb means "to entitle, to authorize" — literally "to make right for someone." The ~be-~ prefix provides the crucial transitive frame: ~recht~ alone is an adjective ("right, correct"), but ~berechtigen~ acts upon a person, conferring ~Recht~ upon them. The PIE root *~h₃reǵ-~ ("to straighten, to make right") produced an astonishingly wide family: Latin ~rēx~ ("king," originally "the one who directs"), ~rēgula~ ("rule"), and ~regiō~ ("direction, region"); Sanskrit ~rājan~ ("king"); Irish ~rí~ ("king"); and Germanic ~recht~/~right~. The Indo-European concept of justice was thus inseparable from straightness — what is ~recht~ is what is straight, not crooked.
en.bereichern:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~reicher~ + ~-n~, a deadjectival derivation from ~reich~ ("rich"):

- ~reich~: From MHG ~rīch~, ~rīche~, from OHG ~rīhhi~ ("noble, mighty, of high birth"), a ~ja~-stem adjective from Proto-Germanic *~rīkijaz~, derived from the noun *~rīk-~ ("ruler, prince"). This noun is widely regarded as an early Celtic loanword into Germanic, cognate with Latin ~rēx~ ("king"), both from PIE *~h₃rēǵs~ ("king, ruler"). Cognate with Dutch ~rijk~, English ~rich~, Old Norse ~ríkr~, and Old English ~rīce~ (whence the ~-ric(k)~ in names like ~Fredrick~ and ~bishopric~).
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix creates a transitive verb meaning "to make rich."

The verb means "to enrich" — literally "to make rich upon." The reflexive ~sich bereichern~ ("to enrich oneself") often carries a negative connotation of illicit gain. The adjective ~reich~ preserves a remarkable layer of Celtic-Germanic contact: the Germanic peoples borrowed the word *~rīk-~ from their Celtic neighbors, probably during the La Tene period (roughly 5th to 1st centuries BC). The original meaning was "ruler, chief," not "wealthy," and the semantic shift from "powerful" to "rich" happened gradually within Germanic. The same Celtic root gave English ~rich~ via Old French ~riche~, which itself was a Frankish (Germanic) re-borrowing — making ~rich~ a word that bounced from Celtic to Germanic to Romance and back to Germanic again.
en.bereiten:
From Middle High German ~bereiten~, from Old High German ~bireitan~. By surface analysis, ~bereit~ + ~-en~; historically a denominal verb from the Proto-Germanic adjective *~raidaz~ ("ready, arranged"), with the prefix ~bi-~ (ancestral form of modern ~be-~). The adjective *~raidaz~ derives from PIE *~rēy-~ ("to arrange, prepare"). The derived verb *~raidijaną~ meant "to make ready, to equip."

The word ~bereiten~ is not to be confused with ~reiten~ ("to ride"), despite the similar appearance. While ~reiten~ descends from PGmc *~rīdaną~ (PIE *~reydʰ-~, "to ride"), ~bereiten~ descends from PGmc *~raidaz~ (PIE *~rēy-~, "to arrange"). The two roots are entirely unrelated. Modern German preserves ~bereiten~ primarily in elevated or fixed expressions — ~Freude bereiten~ ("to give joy"), ~Schwierigkeiten bereiten~ ("to cause difficulties") — while the simplex ~reiten~ in the readying sense has vanished, surviving only in the adjective ~bereit~ ("ready") and the compound ~vorbereiten~ ("to prepare"). English ~ready~ is a direct cognate of ~bereit~, both descending from PGmc *~raidaz~: to be ready is etymologically to be "arranged." Cognate with Dutch ~bereiden~, English ~ready~.
en.bereitstellen:
Compound of ~bereit~ ("ready") + ~stellen~ ("to put, place"):

- ~bereit~: From MHG ~bereite~, from OHG ~bireiti~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raidī~, from Proto-Germanic *~raidaz~ ("ready, arranged, prepared"), from PIE *~h₂roy-dʰh₁-~ ("arranged"), from *~h₂rey-~ ("to count, order"). Cognate with English ~ready~.
- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand").

The compound literally means "to place in a state of readiness" -- that is, "to provide, supply, make available." It is a separable-prefix verb (~er stellt bereit~) where the first element is not a traditional prefix but the adjective ~bereit~. The kinship between German ~bereit~ and English ~ready~ is not immediately obvious because the English word lost the ~be-~ prefix through phonological erosion, while German preserved it. Both ultimately trace back to a PIE root meaning "to put in order" -- readiness as the state of being arranged. In modern usage, ~bereitstellen~ is especially common in technical and administrative contexts: ~Mittel bereitstellen~ ("to provide funds"), ~Daten bereitstellen~ ("to make data available").
en.bergen:
From Middle High German ~bërgen~, from Old High German ~bergan~ ("to hide, protect, salvage"), from Proto-West Germanic *~bergan~, from Proto-Germanic *~berganą~ ("to hide, protect"), from PIE *~bʰergʰ-~ ("to take care of, protect, preserve"). A class III strong verb. Cognate with English ~bury~ (originally "to shelter, protect" — the modern sense of interring the dead is a semantic narrowing), Dutch ~bergen~, Old Norse ~bjarga~ ("to save, help"), Gothic ~bairgan~ ("to hide"), and Old Irish ~commairge~ ("protection").

The semantic range of ~bergen~ is remarkable: it means "to rescue" (from a shipwreck or avalanche), "to salvage" (a sunken ship), "to recover" (a body), and "to contain, hold" (~in sich bergen~, "to hold within itself"). The reflexive ~sich verbergen~ ("to hide oneself") preserves the original "hide" sense more transparently.
