The connection between ~bergen~ and English ~bury~ is a striking example of semantic divergence from a shared root: German kept the "rescue/protect" sense while English narrowed to "put underground." The PIE root *~bʰergʰ-~ also produced the Proto-Slavic *~borgъ~ that became Russian ~berech'~ ("to guard, protect"), showing remarkable semantic stability across the Indo-European family. The place name ~Salzburg~ ("salt fortress") uses the related noun ~Burg~ ("castle, fortress") — a fortified place where one is protected.
en.berichten:
From Middle High German ~berihten~ ("to put in order; to instruct, to lecture"), from Old High German ~birihten~ ("to make correct"), from Proto-West Germanic *~birinhtijan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~richten~:

- ~richten~: From MHG ~richten~, from OHG ~rihten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rihtijan~ ("to straighten, set straight"), from Proto-Germanic *~rihtijaną~, from *~rehtaz~ ("straight"), from PIE *~h₃reǵtós~ ("right, straightened"), from *~h₃reǵ-~ ("to stretch out, straighten, direct"). Cognate with English ~right~, Dutch ~richten~, Latin ~regō~ ("I rule, direct").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The prefix ~be-~ shifted the sense of "to straighten" toward "to set right, put in order, instruct" — and from there, "to report" (as in giving a straight account of events). The sense of "to execute" (as in a judicial sentence, still heard in ~hinrichten~) also derives from "to put right according to the law." Cognate with Dutch ~berichten~, English ~beright~ (archaic).
en.berufen:
From Middle High German ~beruofen~, from Old High German ~biruofan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~rufen~:

- ~rufen~: From MHG ~ruofen~, from OHG ~ruofan~, ~hruofan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hrōpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrōpaną~. The origin is uncertain; it may be related to *~hrōþiz~ ("glory, fame"), with the ~-p-~ arising from analogical association with the similar-sounding *~wōpijaną~ ("to cry out"). Cognate with Dutch ~roepen~.
- ~be-~: From OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~ ("by, near"), from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). Makes the verb transitive and adds a sense of direction toward.

The ~be-~ prefix transforms "to call" into "to call upon, to appoint, to summon to an office." In modern German, ~berufen~ carries the sense of formal appointment or vocation: ~jemanden auf einen Lehrstuhl berufen~ ("to appoint someone to a professorial chair"). The related noun ~Beruf~ ("profession, vocation") is literally a "calling," a calque of Latin ~vocātiō~ -- Martin Luther coined this usage to argue that every honest trade, not just the priesthood, constituted a divine calling. The adjective ~berufen~ can also mean "qualified" or "authorized," but superstitiously, ~Ich will es nicht berufen~ means "I don't want to jinx it" -- the idea being that calling attention to good fortune might summon bad luck. This superstitious sense preserves an ancient belief in the magical power of spoken words. The modern present tense notably lacks umlaut (~du berufst~, not *~du berüfst~), because the historical umlaut on the rounded diphthong ~uo~ never established itself here, unlike in most other strong verbs.
en.beruhen:
~be-~ + ~ruhen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~ruhen~:

- ~ruhen~: From MHG ~ruo(we)n~, from OHG ~ruowēn~, derived from the noun ~ruowa~ ("rest, peace"), from Proto-West Germanic *~rōu~, from Proto-Germanic *~rōō~ ("calm, rest"), from PIE *~h₁reh₁-~ ("quiet, calm"). Cognate with the Sanskrit ~rātrī~ ("night").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one, but here it shifts ~ruhen~ ("to rest") toward a more abstract, relational sense: "to rest upon" becomes "to be based on."

~Beruhen~ means "to be based on, to rest upon, to depend on" — exclusively in the figurative sense. One says ~die Anklage beruht auf Indizien~ ("the charge rests on circumstantial evidence") or uses the fixed phrase ~etwas auf sich beruhen lassen~ ("to let something rest, to leave a matter be"). Unlike ~ruhen~ itself, which retains strong physical connotations of sleeping, resting, and lying buried, ~beruhen~ exists entirely in the abstract domain. The ~be-~ prefix has lifted the verb off the bed, so to speak, and onto the plane of logic and causation.
en.beruhigen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~ruhig~ + ~-en~:

- ~ruhig~: From MHG ~ruowec~ ("quiet"), from ~Ruhe~ ("rest, quiet, peace"). ~Ruhe~ descends from OHG ~ruowa~ ("peace, quiet, rest"), from Proto-West Germanic *~rōu~ ("calm, rest"), from Proto-Germanic *~rōō~, from PIE *~h₁reh₁-~ ("quiet, calm"). Cognate with Sanskrit ~rātri~ ("night"), whence Hindi ~rāt~ ("night").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This inseparable prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the calming action at a specific person or situation.

~Beruhigen~ means "to calm, to reassure, to soothe" -- the ~be-~ prefix transforms the adjective ~ruhig~ ("calm, quiet") into a transitive verb. ~Beruhige dich!~ ("Calm down!") is one of the most frequently heard imperatives in German. The reflexive ~sich beruhigen~ ("to calm down") and the noun ~Beruhigung~ ("reassurance, calming") complete the word family. A ~Beruhigungsmittel~ ("sedative," literally "calming-agent") is the pharmaceutical extension. The connection between PIE *~h₁reh₁-~ ("quiet") and Sanskrit ~rātri~ ("night") is poetic: night as the time of quiet, of rest.
en.berücksichtigen:
A triple derivation chain: ~Rück-~ ("back") + ~Sicht~ ("sight, view") → ~Rücksicht~ ("consideration, regard") → be- + ~rücksichtigen~:

- ~rücksichtigen~ (intermediate verb): A now-rare denominative verb "to have consideration for," formed from ~Rücksicht~ + the verbalizing suffix ~-igen~. In modern use it survives almost exclusively in the prefixed form ~berücksichtigen~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix transforms the denominative verb ~rücksichtigen~ into a fully transitive form meaning "to take into account, to consider."
- ~Rücksicht~ (root noun): An 18th-century calque of Latin ~respectus~ ("a looking back; regard, consideration"), from ~re-~ ("back") + ~spectus~ (from ~specere~, "to look"). German translates each Latin element precisely: ~Rück-~ ("back, return") for ~re-~, and ~Sicht~ ("sight, view") for ~spectus~. ~Sicht~ derives from MHG ~siht~, from OHG ~siht~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sihti~, formed from the root of ~sehen~ ("to see"), from PGmc *~sehwaną~, from PIE *~sekʷ-~ ("to see, notice"). Cognate with English ~sight~.

The full semantic chain runs: PIE *~sekʷ-~ ("to see") → Germanic "sight" → German "a looking back" → "regard, respect" → "to take into regard." The derivation mirrors Latin ~respectus~ precisely: ~Rücksicht nehmen~ corresponds to ~respectum habere~, and ~berücksichtigen~ provides a single-verb equivalent of the Latin compound.
en.berühren:
~be-~ + ~rühren~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~rühren~:

- ~rühren~: From MHG ~rüeren~, from OHG ~ruoren~, ~hruoren~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrōzijaną~ ("to stir"), a causative formation from *~hrōzaz~ ("active, stirring, moving"), possibly from PIE *~kroHs-~ (a root connected to stirring and shock). Cognate with Dutch ~roeren~ ("to stir"), and the now-rare English ~rear~ ("to move, to stir").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix directs the action of stirring or touching toward a specific object, making the verb transitive: to touch something.

~Berühren~ means "to touch" in both the physical and emotional sense: ~berühr das nicht!~ ("don't touch that!") and ~ihre Geschichte hat mich tief berührt~ ("her story deeply touched me"). In geometry, ~berühren~ means "to be tangent to" — a circle ~berührt~ a line at exactly one point. The derived noun ~Berührung~ ("touch, contact") appears in the elegant compound ~Berührungsangst~ ("fear of contact, inhibition about engaging with something") — a word that captures reluctance to engage with unfamiliar ideas, people, or technologies. The underlying verb ~rühren~ itself means "to stir" (as in cooking: ~die Soße rühren~) and "to move emotionally" (~zu Tränen gerührt~, "moved to tears"), preserving both the physical and affective senses from which ~berühren~ drew its dual nature. The military command ~rührt euch!~ ("at ease!," literally "stir yourselves!") uses ~rühren~ in yet another sense — permission to move after standing at attention.
en.beschleunigen:
From the circumfix ~be-~ + ~-en~ around the adjective ~schleunig~ ("hasty, swift"):

- ~be-...-en~: The ~be-~ prefix (from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near") combined with the verbal ending ~-en~ creates a verb from an adjective, meaning roughly "to make [adjective]."
- ~schleunig~: From MHG ~sliunec~ ("hastily"), from OHG ~sliumo~, ~sniumo~ ("immediately," literally "moving quickly"), from or related to Proto-West Germanic *~sniumijan~ ("to hurry"), from Proto-Germanic *~sniumijaną~ ("to expedite, hurry"). The ~-l-~/~-n-~ replacement appears to be assimilation influenced by the suffix ~-n-~. The Proto-Germanic form is related to *~snewaną~ ("to rush"), from PIE *~snewH-~ ("to be fleet, flow, run").

The literal sense is "to make hasty" — to accelerate, to speed up. ~Beschleunigen~ is the standard German word for acceleration in both everyday and scientific contexts: ~Beschleunigung~ is the physics term for acceleration. The phonological history is remarkable: the original Proto-Germanic *~sn-~ cluster at the beginning of the word shifted to ~sl-~ through assimilation during the Old High German period, then the ~s~ was lost entirely in Standard German, leaving ~schleunig~ as a distant descendant of a root meaning "to rush." The related English word ~snell~ (dialectal: "quick, clever") preserves the original *~sn-~ cluster that German transformed beyond recognition.
en.beschließen:
From Middle High German ~besliezen~, from Old High German ~bisliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bisleutan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~schließen~:

- ~schließen~: From MHG ~slieȥen~, from OHG ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slūtaną~, *~sleutaną~ ("to lock, shut"). A class II strong verb.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one or intensifies the action.

While ~abschließen~ emphasizes physical closure or contractual completion, ~beschließen~ has specialized almost entirely into the domain of deliberate decision-making: "to decide, resolve, pass (a resolution)." The underlying metaphor is the same — a bolt sliding home — but ~be-~ gives the action a transitive, encompassing quality: the will locks around its object. The Bundestag ~beschließt~ laws; a committee ~beschließt~ a motion. Cognate with Dutch ~besluiten~.
en.beschreiben:
From ~be-~ + ~schreiben~. Compound of:

- ~schreiben~: From MHG ~schrīben~, from OHG ~scrīban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrīban~, from Proto-Germanic *~skrībaną~, an early borrowing from Latin ~scrībō~ ("I write"), from PIE *~(s)kreybʰ-~ ("to scratch, cut, incise"). The word entered Germanic through contact with the Roman world along the Rhine frontier.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The ~be-~ prefix in ~beschreiben~ does double duty: it creates a transitive verb from the originally intransitive ~schreiben~ and adds the sense of covering or filling a surface (ein Blatt Papier ~beschreiben~ — to fill a sheet of paper with writing). The sense of "to describe" then develops naturally from the image of tracing something in writing. Cognate with Dutch ~beschrijven~.
en.beschränken:
~be-~ + ~schränken~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~schränken~:

- ~schränken~: From MHG ~schrenken~ ("to place crosswise, to obstruct"), from OHG ~screnken~ ("to spread out, to lay across, to trip someone up"), from Proto-Germanic *~skrankijaną~, related to *~skrankaz~ ("barrier, grid"), probably from PIE *~(s)ker-~ ("to bend, turn").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes an intransitive verb transitive or intensifies the action.

~Beschränken~ means "to limit, to restrict, to confine." The related noun ~Schranke~ ("barrier, gate, railing") preserves the physical origin: a ~Schranke~ is a crossbar or gate that blocks passage, from the MHG sense of "that which is placed crosswise." A ~Schrank~ ("cupboard, wardrobe") is an enclosed space — originally the railing or barrier around it, then the enclosed furniture itself. The verb ~beschränken~ abstracts this physical barrier into any kind of limitation: one ~beschränkt~ freedoms, budgets, or access. The reflexive ~sich beschränken auf~ ("to limit oneself to, to confine oneself to") is particularly common. The derived adjective ~beschränkt~ can mean either "limited" (neutral) or "narrow-minded" (pejorative) — a semantic split where physical confinement becomes intellectual confinement. Cognate with Dutch ~beperken~ (which uses a different root) and ~verschranken~ ("to cross, to interlock"). The related English word ~shrink~ may share the same PIE root *~(s)ker-~, though this connection is debated.
en.beschädigen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~schädigen~:

- ~schädigen~: Derived from ~Schaden~ ("damage, harm"), from MHG ~schade~, from OHG ~scado~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaþō~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaþô~ ("damage, harm"). Cognate with English ~scathe~, Dutch ~schade~, Danish ~skade~, and Swedish ~skada~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~ ("by, at, about"), from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). As an inseparable verbal prefix, it typically makes an intransitive verb transitive or intensifies the action.

The verb ~beschädigen~ means "to damage, to cause harm to" — ~Das Auto wurde beschädigt~ ("The car was damaged"). The ~be-~ prefix makes the verb fully transitive, focusing the action on a specific object. Without the prefix, ~schädigen~ also means "to damage" but is more often used in abstract or legal contexts (~die Gesundheit schädigen~ "to damage one's health"). The English cognate ~scathe~ survives mainly in the negative ~unscathed~ ("undamaged") and the derived adjective ~scathing~ ("harshly critical"). The Old Norse cognate ~skaða~ ("to harm") gave English its borrowed word ~scathe~ via the Danelaw period. The Proto-Germanic root *~skaþ-~ may be connected to the idea of splitting or severing, making ~Schaden~ an ancient cousin of the concept behind English ~shatter~, though the precise PIE etymology remains debated among historical linguists.
en.beschäftigen:
Compound of ~be-~ + an adjectival root ~schäftig~, from Middle High German ~scheftec~ ("active, busy"), from ~Schaft~ ("shaft; handle; business"). Compound of ~be-~ + ~schäftigen~:

- ~schäftig~ (root): From MHG ~scheftec~, possibly influenced by Low German ~bescheftich~ ("bustling, industrious"). The underlying noun ~Schaft~ derives from MHG ~schaft~, from OHG ~skaft~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaft~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaftaz~, from PIE *~skeh₂p-~ ("rod, shaft, staff; instrument for striking"). The original sense was a physical shaft or handle, but in OHG ~schaft~ already denoted "condition, state, business" (as in the suffix ~-schaft~, cognate with English ~-ship~ in ~friendship~, ~lordship~). This abstract sense — "being engaged in an activity" — gives ~schäftig~ its meaning.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one, and is used to form denominative and deadjectival verbs.

The reflex ~-schaft~ in words like ~Wirtschaft~ ("economy"), ~Gesellschaft~ ("society"), and ~Wissenschaft~ ("science") is the same root. Cognate with Dutch ~bezighouden~ (the functional equivalent).
en.beseitigen:
Formed from ~be-~ + ~Seite~ + ~-igen~, literally "to put to the side, to set aside":

- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near").
- ~Seite~: From MHG ~sīte~, from OHG ~sīta~, from Proto-Germanic *~sīdǭ~ ("side"), derived from *~sīdaz~ ("long"), with a sense development from "long (surface)" to "side." Cognate with English ~side~, Dutch ~zijde~, Swedish ~sida~.
- ~-igen~: A verb-forming suffix from adjectives and nouns.

The literal image -- moving something to the side -- has darkened considerably over time. While ~beseitigen~ can innocently mean "to remove, to dispose of" (obstacles, problems, waste), it also serves as a chilling euphemism for "to eliminate" in the sense of killing. This sinister secondary meaning makes ~beseitigen~ a prime example of how spatial metaphors can acquire lethal force.
en.besetzen:
From Middle High German ~besetzen~, from Old High German ~bisezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bisattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bisatjaną~ ("to set near, set around"). Compound of ~be-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjaną~, from PIE *~sodéyeti~. This is the causative of *~sed-~ ("to sit") — literally "to cause to sit," hence "to set, to place." Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~, Yiddish ~זעצן~ (~zetsn~).
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The range of meanings — "to occupy" (a seat, a territory), "to fill" (a position), "to cast" (a role), "to stud" (with jewels) — all extends from the core notion of placing things ~upon~ or ~around~ something. The political sense of ~besetzt~ ("occupied," as in military occupation) became tragically prominent in the 20th century. The telephone sense ~besetzt~ ("busy, engaged") — where all lines are occupied — is familiar to every German speaker. The causative chain from PIE *~sed-~ ("to sit") through Proto-Germanic *~satjaną~ ("to cause to sit") to ~setzen~ ("to set, place") beautifully illustrates how Indo-European built transitive verbs from intransitive roots. Cognate with Dutch ~bezetten~, English ~beset~, Danish ~besætte~, Swedish ~besätta~.
en.besichtigen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~Sicht~ + ~-igen~ (a verbalizing suffix):

- ~Sicht~: From MHG ~siht~, from OHG ~siht~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sihti~ ("sight, view"), from Proto-Germanic *~sihti-~, derived from *~sehwaną~ ("to see"), from PIE *~sekʷ-~ ("to see, to notice"). Cognate with English ~sight~, Dutch ~zicht~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive — directing the action of seeing onto a specific object.

~Besichtigen~ means "to inspect, to view, to tour" — ~eine Wohnung besichtigen~ ("to view an apartment"), ~Sehenswürdigkeiten besichtigen~ ("to visit sights"), ~den Tatort besichtigen~ ("to inspect the crime scene"). The ~be-~ prefix with ~-igen~ creates a transitivized verb meaning "to apply sight to something thoroughly." The PIE root *~sekʷ-~ ("to see, to notice") is one of the great sensory roots of Indo-European, producing Latin ~sequī~ ("to follow" — originally "to see and track"), whence English ~sequence~, ~consequence~, and ~prosecute~. German preserves this root not only in ~sehen~ ("to see") but in ~Gesicht~ ("face" — what is seen), ~Ansicht~ ("view, opinion"), and ~Aussicht~ ("prospect, outlook").
en.besiegen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~Sieg~ ("victory") + ~-en~:

- ~Sieg~: From MHG ~sige~, ~sic~, from OHG ~sigu~, from Proto-Germanic *~segiz-~, *~segu-~ ("victory"). Cognate with Old English ~sige~, ~sigor~ ("victory"), Old Norse ~sigr~, Gothic ~sigis~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This inseparable prefix makes the verb transitive, directing the victory at a specific opponent.

~Besiegen~ means "to defeat, to conquer, to overcome" -- the ~be-~ prefix transforms the noun ~Sieg~ ("victory") into a transitive verb directed at an adversary. Without the prefix, the denominal verb ~siegen~ means simply "to win, to be victorious" (intransitive); the ~be-~ creates a grammatical object slot for the vanquished. The root ~Sieg~ is one of the most culturally significant nouns in Germanic: it appears in dozens of personal names -- ~Siegfried~ ("victory-peace"), ~Siegmund~ ("victory-protection"), ~Sigrid~ ("victory-beauty") -- reflecting the warrior culture that prized victory above all.
en.besingen:
From Middle High German ~besingen~, from Old High German ~bisingan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bisingwan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~singen~:

- ~singen~: From MHG ~singen~, from OHG ~singan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~singwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~singwaną~, from PIE *~sengʷʰ-~ ("to sing, to recite, to utter"). Cognate with English ~sing~, Dutch ~zingen~, Old Norse ~syngva~, Gothic ~siggwan~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This inseparable prefix transforms the intransitive ~singen~ ("to sing") into the transitive ~besingen~ ("to sing about, to celebrate in song"), directing the act of singing onto a specific object.

Cognate with Dutch ~bezingen~ and, remarkably, English ~besing~. The English word is rare today but genuine — it appears in Old English as ~besingan~ ("to sing about, to enchant") and surfaces occasionally in literary and poetic contexts through the centuries. Shakespeare's contemporary Ben Jonson used it, and it appears in hymns and formal verse where ordinary ~sing~ would lack the transitive force needed to take a direct object: one sings, but one ~besings~ a hero or a victory.

The ~be-~ prefix follows the same transitivizing pattern across Germanic languages: just as ~klagen~ ("to lament") becomes ~beklagen~ ("to lament something"), ~singen~ becomes ~besingen~. German still coins new ~be-~ compounds freely, but English fossilized its stock centuries ago, leaving behind a sprawling graveyard of ~be-~ words that repays exploration.

English ~be-~ verbs fall into several families. The transitivizers work exactly like German ~be-~: ~bemoan~ (moan about something), ~bewail~ (wail about something), ~belabor~ (labor upon a point), ~befall~ (fall upon someone), and ~belie~ (lie against, contradict). The intensifiers mean "thoroughly" or "all over": ~bedazzle~, ~besmear~, ~bespatter~, ~bedeck~, ~bedevil~, ~befuddle~, ~bemuse~, ~beset~, ~besiege~, and ~besmirch~ (to smirch — already meaning "to soil" — with thoroughness). The denominals create verbs from nouns: ~bedew~ (cover with dew), ~befriend~ (make a friend of), ~bejewel~ (adorn with jewels), ~becloud~ (cover with cloud), ~bewitch~ (put a witch's spell on), and — most surprisingly — ~behead~, where ~be-~ means not "furnish with" but "deprive of," a rare privative sense paralleled by German ~berauben~ ("to rob someone of"). The deadjectivals make something into a quality: ~becalm~ (make calm), ~benumb~ (make numb), ~befoul~ (make foul), and ~belittle~ (make little), which Thomas Jefferson coined in 1780 — arguably the last time English productively used ~be-~ to create a word that stuck. The British literary establishment mocked ~belittle~ as an ugly Americanism; it outlasted all its critics. Finally, the most ancient ~be-~ words have become so opaque that speakers no longer parse the prefix at all: ~begin~ (the ~gin~ is from a lost verb meaning "to open"), ~become~, ~believe~ (the ~lieve~ is related to ~love~ and ~lief~), ~belong~ (~long~ here meant "to go along with"), ~beseech~ (from Old English ~besēcan~, "to seek out"), ~beware~, ~beguile~ (from Old French ~guile~, so a hybrid of Germanic prefix and Romance root), and even ~beyond~ (~be-~ + ~yond~). That English speakers say ~begin~, ~believe~, and ~beyond~ daily without any awareness of the ~be-~ inside them is perhaps the surest measure of how deeply this Proto-Germanic prefix shaped the language — and how completely it has receded from active use.

The PIE root *~sengʷʰ-~ is notable for its narrow semantic stability: unlike most PIE roots, whose meanings drifted dramatically across millennia, this one has meant "to sing" in virtually every daughter language for over five thousand years — Greek ~omphḗ~ ("divine voice") being one of the few offshoots that ventured beyond the musical sense.
en.besitzen:
From Middle High German ~besitzen~, from Old High German ~bisizzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bisittjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bisitjaną~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~sitzen~:

- ~sitzen~: From MHG ~sitzen~, from OHG ~sizzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sittjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sitjaną~, from PIE *~sed-~ ("to sit").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The three verbs ~sitzen~, ~setzen~, and ~besitzen~ illuminate a classical Germanic pattern: ~sitzen~ (intransitive, "to sit") → ~setzen~ (causative, "to cause to sit, set") → ~besitzen~ (~be-~ applied to ~sitzen~, "to sit upon, possess"). The ~be-~ prefix transforms the intransitive ~sitzen~ into a transitive verb of possession — one "sits upon" what one owns, reflecting the ancient connection between occupying land and owning it. (Compare English ~sit~ / ~set~ for the same intransitive/causative pair.) Cognate with Dutch ~bezitten~, Swedish ~besitta~, Danish ~besidde~, Gothic ~bisitan~.
en.besorgen:
From Middle High German ~besorgen~, from Old High German ~bisorgēn~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~sorgen~:

- ~sorgen~: From MHG ~sorgen~, from OHG ~sorgēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~surgāną~, from the noun *~surgō~ ("worry, sorrow, care"), from earlier *~swurgō~, from PIE *~swergʰ-~ ("to watch over, worry; be ill, suffer"). Cognate with English ~sorrow~, Lithuanian ~sirgti~ ("to be ill"), Old Irish ~serg~ ("sickness").
- ~be-~: From OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive and shifts the meaning from worrying in general to taking care of a specific matter.

The semantic development is fascinating: the PIE root meant "to worry" or "to be sick with concern," and the base verb ~sorgen~ still means "to worry" or "to take care." Adding ~be-~ shifted the meaning toward the practical result of that concern: "to procure, to obtain, to take care of (a task)." In colloquial German, ~besorgen~ can also mean "to get" something in a casual sense. The related noun ~Besorgung~ means "errand," showing how abstract worry crystallized into concrete action. English ~sorrow~ is a direct cognate of ~Sorge~ ("worry, concern"), though the English word has narrowed to the emotional sense while German preserved the broader caretaking meaning.
en.besprechen:
From Middle High German ~besprechen~, from Old High German ~besprechan~, ~besprehhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bisprekaną~ ("to speak about, discuss, blame"). Compound of ~be-~ + ~sprechen~:

- ~sprechen~: From MHG ~sprëchen~, from OHG ~sprëhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekaną~ ("to speak"), probably from PIE *~spregʰ-~ ("to crackle"). Cognate with Dutch ~spreken~, English ~speak~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix directs the speaking toward a topic — to speak about or over something.

~Besprechen~ means "to discuss, to talk over" and is the standard formal verb for holding a meeting or consultation: ~wir müssen das besprechen~ ("we need to discuss this"). The derived noun ~Besprechung~ ("meeting, discussion, review") is a fixture of German office life. But ~besprechen~ has an older, more magical sense as well: in folk practice, ~eine Krankheit besprechen~ meant "to charm away an illness by speaking incantations over it" — the ~be-~ prefix here means "to speak upon" quite literally, directing words at the afflicted body to heal it. This incantatory meaning survives in dialects and in the compound ~Besprechung~ in its folk-medicine sense. Cognate with English ~bespeak~ (now archaic, "to speak for, to engage in advance") and Dutch ~bespreken~ ("to discuss; to reserve").
en.bestehen:
From Middle High German ~bestān~, ~bestēn~, from Old High German ~bistān~, from Proto-Germanic *~bistāną~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~stehen~:

- ~stehen~: From MHG ~stān~, ~stēn~, from OHG ~stān~, ~stēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stān~, from Proto-Germanic *~stāną~, from PIE *~steh₂-~ ("to stand").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The prefix ~be-~ shifts the intransitive ~stehen~ ("to stand") into a transitive verb with several distinct senses: "to pass" (an exam), "to consist of," "to insist on," and "to exist, endure." Cognate with Dutch ~bestaan~.
en.bestellen:
From Middle High German ~bestellen~, from Old High German ~bistellen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb — the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The OHG and MHG compound meant primarily "to surround, to occupy a place, to arrange" — the ~be-~ prefix giving ~stellen~ the spatial sense of placing things around or setting them in order. The modern sense of "to order (goods)" developed from the intermediate meaning "to arrange, to commission": you place an order for something by directing a craftsman or merchant to set it up for you. The same compound also yields the legal term ~Bestellung~ ("appointment to office"), which preserves the older sense of "placement into a role." Cognate with Dutch ~bestellen~.
en.bestimmen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~stimmen~:

- ~stimmen~: From MHG ~stimmen~, from OHG *~stimmen~, a denominative from ~Stimme~ ("voice, vote"). ~Stimme~ derives from Proto-West Germanic *~stebnu~, from Proto-Germanic *~stebnō~, from PIE *~stomen-~ ("mouth, muzzle").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The root verb ~stimmen~ bears three distinct but historically connected senses: "to tune" (an instrument), "to vote," and "to be correct" (~das stimmt~). All three revolve around the idea of bringing something into alignment with a standard — voices tuned together, voices cast collectively, a fact that matches reality. When ~be-~ is added, the verb becomes fully transitive and the sense sharpens: "to determine" means to fix or settle something authoritatively, as if tuning it to a definitive pitch. The abstract noun ~Bestimmung~ carries the full weight of this — "determination," "destiny," "definition" — suggesting that to determine something is to give it its voice, its fixed note in the larger order. Cognate with Dutch ~bestemmen~, English ~stem~ (in the sense of to derive from a source, sharing the PIE root).
en.bestreiten:
From Middle High German ~bestrīten~ ("to fight against"), from Old High German ~bistrītan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~streiten~:

- ~streiten~: From MHG ~strīten~, from OHG ~strītan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~strīdan~. Cognate with Low German ~striden~, Dutch ~strijden~ ("to fight, to struggle"), and English ~stride~.
- ~be-~: From OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive, shifting from fighting in general to fighting against something specific.

The modern meanings of ~bestreiten~ have diverged strikingly from the original martial sense. Today it primarily means either "to dispute, to contest, to deny" (e.g., ~eine Behauptung bestreiten~, "to contest a claim") or "to finance, to bear the cost of" (e.g., ~die Kosten bestreiten~, "to cover the expenses"). The financial sense developed from the metaphor of "being able to cope with" or "to fight through" an obligation. The English cognate ~bestride~ (from the same Proto-West Germanic root *~bistrīdan~) took yet another path, meaning "to straddle" or "to stand over." Dutch ~bestrijden~ ("to combat, to fight against") preserves the original combative sense most faithfully. The divergence of meaning among these cognates -- German "to deny / to finance," English "to straddle," Dutch "to combat" -- is a textbook example of semantic drift across sibling languages.
en.bestätigen:
A denominal verb built on a three-layer derivation chain: the PIE root *~steh₂-~ ("to stand") gives Old High German ~stān~ ("to stand"), which yields the deadjectival form ~stet~ (OHG ~stāti~, "steady, firm, constant"), which in turn generates the MHG adjective ~stætec~ ("firm, constant, stable") — the ancestor of modern ~stetig~. The verb ~bestätigen~ is then formed by adding the transitivizing prefix ~be-~ to a deadjectival stem *~stätig-en~ derived from this adjective:

- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive or adjectival-derived verb into a transitive one.
- *~stätig-~ (root): From MHG ~stætec~, from OHG ~stāti~ ("firm, constant"), ultimately from the root of ~stehen~ ("to stand"), from Proto-Germanic *~stāną~, from PIE *~steh₂-~ ("to stand").

~bestätigen~ therefore means, at its etymological core, "to make something stand firm" — to render a fact, a claim, or an appointment as steady and settled. The word's journey from PIE *~steh₂-~ through OHG and MHG to the modern verb passes through three derivational layers that English rarely matches: a strong verb becomes a deadjectival form, which becomes an adjective, which becomes a denominal causative. The result is that ~bestätigen~ ("to confirm") and ~stehen~ ("to stand") are cognates — separated by two millennia and three steps of derivation, but sharing the same ancestral idea: the thing that is confirmed is the thing that has been made to stand. Cognate with Dutch ~bevestigen~ (which follows a parallel derivational path from ~vast~ "firm").
en.besuchen:
From Middle High German ~besuochen~ ("to seek, use, visit, investigate"), from Old High German ~bisuohhen~ ("to examine, check, try, inquire"), from Proto-West Germanic *~bisōkijan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~suchen~:

- ~suchen~: From MHG ~suochen~, ~süechen~, from OHG ~suohhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sōkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sōkijaną~, from PIE *~seh₂g-~ ("to seek out").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The semantic narrowing from "to seek, investigate, try" (Old High German) to "to visit" (Modern German) reflects a shift from the general act of seeking to its most socially common purpose: seeking out another person at their location. English ~beseech~ — "to beg, implore" — is a cognate that preserved a different strand of the same compound's original range of "seeking earnestly." The English false friend ~visit~ takes a wholly different route, from Latin ~vīsitāre~ ("to go to see"), from ~vīdēre~ ("to see"). Cognate with Dutch ~bezoeken~, Swedish ~besöka~, English ~beseech~.
en.beteiligen:
Denominative verb derived from ~Teil~ ("part, share"). Formed in early modern German with the prefix ~be-~, the adjectival suffix ~-ig~, and the verbal ending ~-en~: ~be-~ + ~teil~ + ~-ig~ + ~-en~, meaning literally "to make someone a part of something."

- ~Teil~: From MHG ~teil~, from OHG ~teil~, from Proto-West Germanic *~daili~, from Proto-Germanic *~dailiz~, tentatively from Proto-Indo-European *~dʰ(h₁)-oy-lo-~ ("part, watershed"), itself possibly from *~dʰeh₁-~ ("to put, do"), though the morphological derivation is uncertain. Cognate with English ~deal~ (as in "a great deal"), Dutch ~deel~, Swedish ~del~.
- ~be-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The inseparable prefix ~be-~ makes an intransitive verb transitive or a denominal verb causative.

The noun ~Beteiligung~ ("participation, stake, share") follows naturally from the verb. The same PIE root underlies the English word ~deal~ — a semantic cousin preserved in phrases like "deal out" and "new deal," where division and distribution remain central.
en.beten:
From Middle High German ~beten~, from Old High German ~beton~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bedon~, from Proto-Germanic *~bedan~ ("to pray, to ask"), closely related to *~bidjan~ ("to ask, to request"), from PIE *~gwhedh-~ ("to ask for, to beseech"). Cognate with English ~bid~ (via Old English ~biddan~) and Dutch ~bidden~ ("to pray"), Danish ~bede~ ("to ask, to pray").

The semantic narrowing from "to ask, to request" to specifically "to pray" is a Christianization phenomenon: when Germanic peoples adopted Christianity, their word for "requesting" was pressed into service for the specific act of requesting from God. English ~bid~ took the opposite path, retaining the secular sense and losing the religious one. The related noun ~Gebet~ ("prayer"), from OHG ~gibet~, uses the ~ge-~ prefix in its collective/completive sense. The further related noun ~Bitte~ ("request, plea") was reshaped by analogy with ~bitten~ ("to ask"), showing how the ~beten~/~bitten~ pair has remained intertwined throughout their history. The verb is attested in Old High German since the 8th century, making it one of the earliest documented German words associated with Christian practice.
en.betonen: ~betonen~ is a denominal verb formed from ~Ton~ ("tone, stress, accent") with the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near") and the infinitive suffix ~-en~. ~Ton~ in this sense comes from Middle High German ~tōn~, borrowed from Latin ~tonus~, itself from Ancient Greek ~τόνος~ (~tónos~), from Proto-Hellenic *~tónos~, from PIE *~ten-~ ("to stretch"). The Greek word referred to the stretching of a string — pitch and tension were the same concept for the ancient world. German has two homophones spelled ~Ton~: one meaning "clay" (from Proto-Germanic *~þanhǭ~, PIE *~tenk-~, "to thicken") and one meaning "tone, sound" — the one that gives ~betonen~. The prefix ~be-~ here marks the action of placing stress upon a syllable or emphasizing a point, turning the noun for sound into a verb for linguistic and rhetorical accent.
en.betrachten:
From Middle High German ~betrahten~, from Old High German ~bitrahtōn~, ~bitrahten~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~trachten~:

- ~trachten~: From MHG ~trahten~, from OHG ~trahtōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~trahtōn~, a borrowing from Latin ~tractāre~ ("to drag, handle, manage, treat"), the frequentative of ~trahō~ ("I drag, pull"). In OHG, ~trahtōn~ shifted from the Latin physical sense of handling toward mental handling — pondering, considering, striving toward.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one, and adds a sense of directed, focused attention.

A remarkable Latin-to-German journey: Latin ~tractāre~ (physical: "to drag repeatedly, handle") enters OHG as "to consider, strive for" (~trachten~), and with ~be-~ becomes "to look at, contemplate" (~betrachten~). The verb is a doublet of ~traktieren~ (modern German "to mistreat"), which preserves more of the original Latin physical sense. The shared root ~trahō~ is also the ancestor of English ~tract~, ~tractor~, and ~attract~. Cognate with Dutch ~betrachten~.
en.betragen:
Inherited from Middle High German ~betragen~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from PGmc *~draganą~ ("to draw, drag"), from PIE *~dʰregʰ-~ ("to pull, draw, drag"). Class VI strong verb. Cognate with English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Dutch ~dragen~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

~Betragen~ carries two distinct modern senses: "to amount to" (as in "die Summe beträgt 100 Euro") and "to behave" (as in "sich gut betragen"). Both trace back to "carrying": a sum "carries itself" to an amount, and a person "carries themselves" in a certain manner. The reflexive noun ~das Betragen~ ("conduct, behavior") preserves the older sense of personal carriage. The same metaphor of conduct as self-carriage appears in English ~comportment~ (Latin ~comportāre~, "to carry together") and ~bearing~. Cognate with Dutch ~bedragen~.
en.betreffen:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~treffen~:

- ~treffen~: From MHG ~treffen~, from OHG ~treffan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~drepan~, from Proto-Germanic *~drepaną~ ("to strike, hit").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The prefix ~be-~ here adds a sense of direction and full engagement: where ~treffen~ is hitting or meeting in general, ~betreffen~ is hitting upon something specifically — "to concern, affect, pertain to." The common phrase ~was mich betrifft~ ("as far as I'm concerned," literally "what hits/touches me") preserves this tactile metaphor vividly. The related adjective ~betroffen~ ("affected; shocked, taken aback") illustrates how being "struck" by something can convey both relevance and emotional impact. Cognate with Dutch ~betreffen~.
en.betreiben:
A calque of Middle Low German ~bedrīven~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~treiben~:

- ~treiben~: From MHG ~trīben~, from OHG ~trīban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~drīban~, from PGmc *~drībaną~, from PIE *~dʰreybʰ-~ ("to drive, push"). Class I strong verb. The English cognate is ~drive~; Dutch ~drijven~, Danish ~drive~, and Swedish ~driva~ are all close relatives.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

~Betreiben~ extends the forceful root sense into abstract domains: "to operate" (a business or machine — you ~drive~ it into action) and "to pursue" (an activity — you ~drive~ at it persistently). The cognate English ~drive~ has undergone exactly the same extension from physical propulsion to abstract pursuit. ~Betreiben~ is also a legal term: ~auf Betreiben von~ means "at the instigation of" — literally "through the driving of." Cognate with Dutch ~bedrijven~.
en.betreten:
From Middle High German ~betreten~, from Old High German ~bitretan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bitredan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~treten~:

- ~treten~: From MHG ~trëten~, from OHG ~trëtan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tredan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trudaną~ ("to tread, step on"). Cognate with English ~tread~. The West Germanic descendants regularized the original ablaut by replacing ~u~ with ~e~ in the present stem.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the intransitive ~treten~ ("to step") into the transitive ~betreten~ ("to step onto, to enter").

The adjective ~betreten~ ("embarrassed") is identical in form to the past participle but developed an independent semantic life. The connection may lie in the image of someone caught in the act of entering a place they should not be -- frozen mid-step, literally "be-trodden."
en.betreuen:
From Middle High German ~betriuwen~, equivalent to ~be-~ + ~treu~ + ~-en~:

- ~treu~: From MHG ~triuwe~, from OHG ~triuwi~, from Proto-West Germanic *~triuwī~, from Proto-Germanic *~triwwiz~ ("faithful, loyal"). Cognate with English ~true~.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This inseparable prefix makes the verb transitive — directing faithfulness onto someone as an act of care.

~Betreuen~ means "to care for, to look after, to supervise" — ~ein Kind betreuen~ ("to look after a child"), ~Kunden betreuen~ ("to attend to clients"). The word is literally "to be true to" or "to be faithful toward" someone, making the act of caregiving an expression of loyalty. The Proto-Germanic root *~triwwiz~ descends from PIE *~deru-~ ("firm, solid," originally referring to wood or trees), which also gives us English ~true~, ~trust~, ~tree~, and ~truce~ — all united by the concept of firmness and reliability. The noun ~Betreuer~ / ~Betreuerin~ ("caregiver, supervisor") is ubiquitous in modern German, from childcare (~Kinderbetreuung~) to academic advising (~Doktorarbeit-Betreuer~). The idea that care is an act of faithfulness rather than mere duty gives ~betreuen~ a warmth absent from its English equivalents.
en.beurteilen:
From early 16th century. Compound of ~be-~ + ~urteilen~:

- ~urteilen~: From MHG ~urteilen~, a denominative from ~Urteil~ + ~-en~. ~Urteil~ descends from MHG ~urteil~, from OHG ~urteil~, ~urteili~, from Proto-Germanic *~uzdailiją~ ("judicial decision, verdict"), literally "an out-dealing." The noun is composed of ~ur-~ (from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~, "out") + ~Teil~ ("part, share"), from MHG ~teil~, from OHG ~teil~, from Proto-West Germanic *~daili~, from Proto-Germanic *~dailiz~, from PIE *~deh₂i-~ ("to divide"). Cognate with Dutch ~oordeel~, English ~ordeal~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one, or intensifies a transitive verb by directing focus onto the object.

While ~urteilen~ means "to judge, pass judgment" in a general or legal sense, ~beurteilen~ means "to assess, evaluate, appraise" — the ~be-~ prefix shifts the focus from the act of judging to the object being judged. One ~beurteilt~ a student's work, a situation, or a wine. The relationship between ~Urteil~ and English ~ordeal~ is a striking case of semantic divergence: the Germanic root meant a formal judicial decision, but in English the word narrowed to mean a trial by fire or water — and eventually any severe test or painful experience — while German kept the original legal meaning intact.
en.bevorzugen:
A modern denominative verb built from ~be-~ + ~Vorzug~ + ~-en~:

- ~Vorzug~: A nominal formation from ~vorziehen~ ("to prefer, to pull forward"), itself from ~vor-~ ("before, forward," from PIE *~preh₂~, "before, in front") + ~ziehen~ ("to pull," from Proto-Germanic *~teuhaną~, from PIE *~dewk-~, "to pull, lead"). ~Vorzug~ literally means "the pulling forward," hence "preference, advantage."
- ~be-~: From Proto-Germanic *~bi~ ("by, near"), from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). Makes the noun into a transitive verb.

The word's structure is a textbook example of German morphological productivity: a PIE root meaning "to pull" became ~ziehen~, which was prefixed to create ~vorziehen~ ("to pull forward, to prefer"), which was nominalized to ~Vorzug~ ("preference"), which was then re-verbalized with ~be-~ and ~-en~ to create ~bevorzugen~ ("to prefer, to favor"). Each step added a layer of abstraction. English ~prefer~ follows a strikingly parallel metaphor from Latin ~praeferre~ ("to carry before, to put forward"), showing that both Germanic and Latin speakers independently conceived of preference as "putting something in front." The fact that ~bevorzugen~ is derived from a noun (~Vorzug~) rather than directly from the verb ~vorziehen~ explains why it conjugates weakly (regularly), despite ~ziehen~ being a strong verb.
en.bewahren:
From Middle High German ~bewarn~, from Old High German ~biwarōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~biwarōną~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~wahren~:

- ~wahren~: From MHG ~warn~, ~waren~, from OHG ~warōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~warōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~warōną~, from *~waraz~ ("cautious, attentive"), from PIE *~wer-~ ("to become aware of, heed"). Related to ~wahr~ ("true"), from Proto-Germanic *~wēraz~, from PIE *~weh₁ros~ ("true"); cognate with Latin ~vērus~ ("true"), Old Irish ~fíor~ ("true").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). Here the prefix intensifies the protective sense of the base verb.

~bewahren~ means "to preserve, protect, safeguard, keep" — a strengthened form of ~wahren~ ("to maintain, guard"). The phrase ~Gott bewahre!~ ("God forbid!," literally "God preserve!") is a common exclamation. Cognate with English ~beware~, which preserves the same ~be-~ + "be watchful" compound but shifted to mean "be on guard against" rather than "protect." The semantic field connecting ~wahren~ ("to guard"), ~wahr~ ("true"), and ~wahrnehmen~ ("to perceive") reflects an ancient cluster of ideas around watchfulness, awareness, and truth — what is carefully observed is what is real. The PIE root *~wer-~ ("to heed") and *~weh₁-~ ("true") may ultimately be related, though the precise connection is debated.
en.bewegen:
~Bewegen~ is two historically distinct verbs that have merged into a single modern form, distinguished only by their past tense:

Etymology 1 — weak (~bewegte/bewegt~, "to move physically"): From MHG ~bewegen~, from OHG ~biwegan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biwegan~, from PGmc *~weganą~ ("to move, carry"), from PIE *~weǵʰ-~ ("to convey, transport"). The same PIE root gives German ~Wagen~ ("wagon, vehicle"), English ~way~ and ~weigh~, and Latin ~vehere~ ("to convey") — the source of English ~vehicle~.

- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix adds transitivizing force: where *~weganą~ was intransitive ("to move"), ~bewegen~ directs that motion onto an object.

Etymology 2 — strong (~bewog/bewogen~, "to motivate, induce"): From MHG ~bewegen~, from OHG ~biweggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biwaggjan~, an iterative-intensive causative of the same PGmc *~weganą~ + causative suffix *~-janą~. This yielded a separate sense: "to set into motion mentally — to move someone to act."

The merger of these two verbs creates a productive ambiguity in Modern High German: "Was hat dich bewogen?" uses the strong past tense and means "What motivated you?" — not "What moved you physically?" The two etyma share PIE *~weǵʰ-~, connecting ~bewegen~ to English ~vehicle~, Latin ~via~ ("road"), and Greek ~ókhos~ ("wagon"). Cognate with Dutch ~bewegen~.
en.beweisen: From ~be-~ + ~weisen~ ("to show, point, direct"). The prefix ~be-~ (from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near") transforms the act of showing into a transitive act of demonstrating or proving. ~Weisen~ derives from Old High German ~wīsen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wīsijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wīsijaną~, a denominative of *~wīsaz~ ("wise, knowing"), from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see, know") — the same root that yields Latin ~videō~ ("I see"), Sanskrit ~veda~ ("I know"), and English ~wise~, ~wit~, and ~vision~. ~Beweisen~ thus literally means "to show thoroughly" — to make something seen beyond doubt. The related noun ~Beweis~ ("proof, evidence") preserves the same ancient connection between seeing and knowing. Cognate with Dutch ~bewijzen~.
en.bewerben:
From Middle High German ~bewerben~, from Old High German ~biwerban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bihwerban~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~werben~:

- ~werben~: From MHG ~wërben~, from OHG ~wërban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hwerban~, from Proto-Germanic *~hwerbaną~ ("to turn, to move about"), possibly from PIE *~kʷerp-~ ("to turn").
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix makes the verb transitive and reflexive.

~Bewerben~ (reflexive: ~sich bewerben~) means "to apply (for a job, a position, a contract)." The root ~werben~ originally meant "to turn, to move about" in Proto-Germanic, and its semantic evolution is fascinating: from physical turning to bustling activity, then to soliciting and recruiting (still visible in ~Werbung~, "advertising"), and finally to courting or wooing. The prefix ~be-~ makes this reflexive: ~sich bewerben~ is to turn oneself ~toward~ something, to present oneself as a candidate. The noun ~Bewerbung~ ("application") and ~Bewerber~ ("applicant") are standard vocabulary of professional life. The Proto-Germanic root *~hwerbaną~ also gave Dutch ~werven~ ("to recruit"), Icelandic ~hverfa~ ("to turn, to disappear"), and the archaic English ~wharve~ ("to turn"). The loss of the initial ~hw-~ in German (via OHG ~wërban~) obscures the connection to English ~whirl~ and ~warp~, which may be distant relatives through the same PIE turning root. Cognate with Dutch ~werven~, Faroese ~hvørva~.
en.bewerten: ~bewerten~ is a denominal verb formed from ~Wert~ ("value, worth") with the prefix ~be-~ (from PIE *~h₁epi~, "on, at, near") and the infinitive suffix ~-en~, meaning "to evaluate, assess, rate." ~Wert~ comes from Middle High German ~wert~, from Old High German ~werd~, from Proto-Germanic *~werþaz~, ultimately from PIE *~wert-~ ("to turn") — the underlying sense being that of exchange, something turned over in trade. The same PIE root gives English ~worth~ and ~weird~ (originally "fate, that which turns"). The regular sound change from Proto-Germanic ~-þ-~ to German ~-t-~ in the uninflected form was then generalized across the paradigm; the older ~-d-~ is preserved in related words ~Würde~ ("dignity") and ~würdig~ ("worthy"). A cognate of particular interest: English ~worth~ and German ~Wert~ are doublets, and the sentence "What is it worth?" maps exactly onto "Was ist es wert?" — same structure, same root.
en.bewirken:
From Middle High German ~bewirken~, ~bewürken~, from Old High German ~biwurken~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~wirken~:

- ~wirken~: From MHG ~wirken~, from OHG ~wirken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wirkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wirkijaną~ ("to make, produce, effect"), from *~werką~ ("work"), from PIE *~werǵ-~ ("to work"). Cognate with English ~work~, Dutch ~werken~, Greek ~érgon~ ("work"), and the organ name in ~Chirurg~ (from Greek ~kheirourgós~, "hand-worker"). An Upper German variant *~wurkijaną~ (OHG ~wurken~, MHG ~würken~) coexisted for centuries; the Central German ~i~-form prevailed in the written language by the 18th century.
- ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). The prefix directs the working toward a result, making the verb mean "to work upon" in the sense of causing.

~Bewirken~ means "to bring about, to cause, to effect" — a formal verb of causation. Where ~wirken~ alone means "to work, to have an effect, to seem," ~bewirken~ focuses on the result: ~die Medizin hat eine Verbesserung bewirkt~ ("the medicine brought about an improvement"). It occupies a register above ~verursachen~ ("to cause") and well above ~machen~ ("to make, to do"). The PIE root *~werǵ-~ is one of the most productive in the Indo-European family: it gives Greek ~érgon~ ("work," whence ~energy~, ~allergy~, ~liturgy~), Latin ~urgēre~ ("to press"), and Old English ~weorc~ (modern ~work~). The connection between German ~wirken~/~bewirken~ and English ~work~ is therefore genuine cognacy, not mere resemblance.
en.bewundern:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~wundern~, from ~Wunder~ ("wonder, miracle"):

- ~Wunder~: From MHG ~wunder~, from OHG ~wuntar~, from Proto-Germanic *~wundrą~, from PIE *~wenh₁-~ ("to wish, desire, strive for, love"). Cognate with English ~wonder~, Dutch ~wonder~, Danish ~under~.
- ~be-~: Inseparable prefix from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). Here it functions as a transitivizer, making the reflexive ~sich wundern~ ("to wonder at") into the transitive ~bewundern~ ("to admire").

~Bewundern~ means "to admire," while the simpler ~sich wundern~ means "to be surprised, to wonder." The prefix ~be-~ transforms passive astonishment into active admiration. The PIE root *~wenh₁-~ ("to desire, strive for") reveals that ~wonder~ was originally an expression of longing or desire: one wondered at what one wished for. The same root produced Latin ~Venus~ (goddess of desire), ~venārī~ ("to hunt," originally "to pursue with desire," whence English ~venison~), and Old English ~wynn~ ("joy, delight"). The German compound ~Wunderkind~ ("child prodigy," literally "wonder child") entered English unchanged.
en.bewähren:
Compound of ~be-~ + ~währen~, a deadjectival derivation from ~wahr~ ("true"):

- ~wahr~: From MHG ~wār~, ~wǣre~, from OHG ~wār~, ~wāri~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wār~, from Proto-Germanic *~wēraz~ ("true, reliable"), from PIE *~weh₁ros~ ("true"), from *~weh₁-~ ("to be true"). Cognate with English archaic ~ware~ ("aware"), Dutch ~waar~ ("true"), Latin ~vērus~ ("true"), and Irish ~fíor~ ("true").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix adds transitivity and intensification.

The reflexive ~sich bewähren~ means "to prove oneself, to stand the test" — literally "to make oneself true." The adjective ~wahr~ descends from a PIE root *~weh₁-~ whose reflexes encode the concept of truth across the Indo-European world: Latin ~vērus~ ("true") gave English ~very~, ~verify~, and ~verdict~ ("a true saying"); Old Irish ~fíor~ means "true"; and Proto-Slavic *~věra~ means "faith, belief" (whence Russian ~вера~). The related German verb ~wahren~ means "to preserve, to maintain," while ~wahrnehmen~ means "to perceive" — suggesting that truth, preservation, and perception were linked in the Germanic mind. English ~aware~ is a distant relative through the same Proto-Germanic adjective.
en.bewältigen:
From Middle High German ~bewältigen~, derived from ~be-~ + ~waltig~ + ~-en~, in which ~waltig~ is an obsolete variant of ~gewaltig~ ("powerful, mighty"). Compound of ~be-~ + the obsolete adjective ~waltig~:

- ~waltig~: From MHG ~waltic~, related to ~walten~ ("to rule, govern"), from OHG ~waltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~waldan~ ("to reign"), from Proto-Germanic *~waldaną~, from PIE *~h₂welh₁-~ ("to rule, be strong"). The noun ~Gewalt~ ("power, force") shares this root, from OHG ~giwalt~, Proto-Germanic *~gawaldą~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bi~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"). This prefix makes the verb transitive: "to overpower" something.

The semantic development from "to exercise power over" to "to master, cope with, overcome" reflects the journey from raw physical dominance to intellectual and emotional mastery. Cognate with English ~wield~, which underwent a similar semantic broadening from ruling to skillful handling. The root also appears in Icelandic ~valda~ ("to cause"), Polish ~wladac~ ("to rule"), and Russian ~vladet'~ ("to own, command"), revealing a pan-Indo-European concept of power. The related noun ~Bewältigung~ plays a prominent role in modern German discourse, notably in the compound ~Vergangenheitsbewältigung~ ("coming to terms with the past"), a word so culturally specific that it is often borrowed untranslated into English.
en.bezahlen:
From Middle High German ~bezaln~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~zahlen~:

- ~zahlen~: From MHG ~zaln~, ~zalen~, from OHG ~zalōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~talōn~, from PGmc *~talōną~ ("to tell, recount; to count, reckon"). Cognate with English ~tale~ and ~tell~ (in the counting sense: "all told" means "all counted"), Dutch ~betalen~, Swedish ~tala~ ("to speak"), Danish ~tal~ ("number").
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix adds a transitive, directed quality — while ~zahlen~ can be used intransitively ("ich zahle" — "I pay"), ~bezahlen~ more explicitly takes a direct object (the thing or person paid).

The conceptual bridge from "counting" to "paying" runs through the ancient practice of counting out coins aloud when settling a debt. English preserves the counting sense in ~teller~ (one who tells/counts — as in a bank teller). German ~Zahl~ ("number") and ~zahlen~ ("to pay") descend from the same root, making every act of paying in German etymologically an act of counting. Cognate with Dutch ~betalen~, Danish ~betale~.
en.bezeichnen:
From Middle High German ~bezeichenen~, from Old High German ~bizeihhanen~, ~bizeihnen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bitaiknijan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~zeichnen~:

- ~zeichnen~: Derived from the noun ~Zeichen~ ("sign, mark"), from MHG ~zeichen~, from OHG ~zeihhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taikn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taikną~ ("sign"), from PIE *~deyḱ-~ ("to point out, show"). A denominative verb: "to make a sign, to mark."
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

The PIE root *~deyḱ-~ is remarkably productive across languages: it yields ~Zeichen~ ("sign"), ~zeigen~ ("to show"), ~zeihen~ ("to accuse"), Latin ~dīcō~ ("I say, point out"), Greek ~deiknumi~ ("I show"), and English ~teach~ (from OE ~tǣcan~, from the same root). The ~be-~ prefix shifts the basic meaning of "to make a mark" toward "to designate, label, characterize." Cognate with Dutch ~betekenen~ ("to mean, signify"), English ~betoken~.
en.beziehen:
From Middle High German ~beziehen~, from Old High German ~biziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biteuhan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from PGmc *~teuhaną~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Class II strong verb. Cognate with Latin ~dūcere~ ("to lead") — the source of English ~duke~ and ~duct~ — and with English ~tow~.
- ~be-~: From MHG ~be-~, from OHG ~bi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bi-~, from PIE *~h₁epi~ ("at, near"). This prefix typically transforms an intransitive verb into a transitive one.

~Beziehen~ has accumulated a striking range of meanings, all traceable to the root idea of "pulling toward or over": to move into (a flat — you "draw" yourself into it), to subscribe to (a journal — you "draw" it to yourself), to source (from a supplier — you "draw" goods from them), to cover (a bed or cushion — you "draw" fabric over it), and to refer (to something — you "draw" a connection). The reflexive ~sich beziehen auf~ ("to refer to") was probably influenced by Latin ~referre~ ("to carry back, refer"). The derived noun ~Beziehung~ ("relationship, connection") shows the same root abstraction — a relationship is a mutual drawing-toward. Cognate with Dutch ~betrekken~.
en.biegen:
From Middle High German ~biegen~, from Old High German ~biogan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~beugan~, from Proto-Germanic *~beuganą~, from PIE *~bʰewgʰ-~ ("to bend"). Cognate with Sanskrit ~bhujáti~ ("to bend, curve").

~Biegen~ is a class II strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~biegen~, ~bog~, ~gebogen~. It means "to bend, to curve, to turn (a corner)." The idiom ~auf Biegen und Brechen~ ("by hook or by crook," literally "by bending and breaking") pairs the verb with its semantic opposite, suggesting that one will either bend something into shape or break it trying. The PIE root *~bʰewgʰ-~ specialized in Germanic for physical bending, while in English the cognate ~bow~ (as in bowing one's head) preserved a more social and gestural sense. The English noun ~bow~ (of a ship) and the word ~elbow~ (literally "arm-bend," from OE ~elnboga~) are also related. Dutch ~buigen~, Danish ~bøje~, and Swedish ~böja~ are immediate Germanic siblings.
en.bieten: From Middle High German ~bieten~, from Old High German ~biotan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~beudan~, from Proto-Germanic *~beudaną~, from PIE *~bʰewdʰ-~ ("to be awake, aware"). A class II strong verb whose PIE root conveys notions of awareness and offering. Cognate with Low German ~bieden~, Dutch ~bieden~, English ~bid~, Danish ~byde~, Swedish ~bjuda~.
en.bilden: From Middle High German ~bilden~, ~bileden~, a merger of two Old High German verbs: ~biliden~ ("to form into an image"), from Proto-West Germanic *~biliþijan~, and ~bilidōn~ ("to copy, represent"), from Proto-West Germanic *~biliþōn~. Both derive from the Proto-West Germanic noun *~biliþī~ (whence German ~Bild~, "image, picture"). ~Bild~ itself traces from OHG ~bilidi~, ~biladi~, attested since the 8th century, first in the sense "model, exemplar" and later "likeness, copy"; its further pre-Germanic origin is obscure — German Wiktionary notes only a Germanic stem *~bil-~ meaning "form," with no secure PIE etymology established. The semantic history of ~bilden~ — from "to give something an image or form" through "to educate, cultivate" — reflects the deep German cultural connection between ~Bild~ ("image") and ~Bildung~ ("education, formation of character"), the latter concept central to German Idealist philosophy. ~Bildung~ is not merely instruction but the shaping of the whole person as if forming a work of art. Cognate with Dutch ~beelden~, Swedish ~bilda~.
en.binden:
From Middle High German ~binden~, from Old High German ~bintan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bindan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bindaną~, from PIE *~bʰendʰ-~ ("to bind, tie"). A class III strong verb with the characteristic ~i~–~a~–~u~ ablaut pattern (~binden~, ~band~, ~gebunden~). Cognate with Low German ~binden~, Dutch ~binden~, English ~bind~, Danish ~binde~, Gothic ~bindan~.

The PIE root *~bʰendʰ-~ has left an extraordinarily wide trail across the Indo-European family. Sanskrit ~bandhati~ ("to bind, to tie") is a direct cognate — and the English loanword ~bandana~ descends from a Hindi derivative of the same Sanskrit root, meaning "a tied thing." The Germanic noun ~Band~ ("ribbon, bond, volume"), English ~band~ and ~bond~, and even ~bundle~ all spring from this same ancient root. To bind a book (~ein Buch binden~) is thus one of the oldest metaphors still alive in everyday German — the same PIE root that described tying physical bonds in the Bronze Age steppe now describes the spine of a paperback.
en.bitten:
From Middle High German ~bitten~, from Old High German ~bitten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biddjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bidjaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~gʷʰedʰ-~ ("to ask for, beseech"). A class V strong verb. Cognate with Low German ~bidden~, Dutch ~bidden~ ("to pray"), English ~bid~ (in the sense "to ask, invite"), Danish ~bede~.

The PIE root *~gʷʰedʰ-~ carries the sense of earnest supplication — the same root that underlies Old Norse ~biðja~, Gothic ~bidjan~, and Sanskrit ~bādh-~ (to press, urge). English ~bid~ is a direct cognate in one of its etymological strands; the modern English word has been complicated by conflation with a second, unrelated Old English verb, ~bēodan~ ("to offer, announce"), from PIE *~bʰewdʰ-~, which contributed the commercial sense "to bid at auction." German ~bitten~ remained uncontaminated by that conflation and preserves the pure sense of asking or requesting. The related noun ~Bitte~ ("request, please") and the polite particle ~bitte~ — the most pervasive politeness word in the German language — descend directly from the same root. Dutch ~bidden~ drifted toward specifically religious supplication, a semantic narrowing that German ~bitten~ avoided.
en.bleiben: From Middle High German ~blīben~, ~belīben~ ("to remain in the same position, pause"), from Old High German ~bilīban~ ("to remain"), from Proto-Germanic *~bilībaną~ ("to remain"), a compound of *~bi-~ + *~lībaną~, from PIE *~leyp-~ ("to stick, adhere"). The semantic development from "to stick" to "to remain" reflects how physical adhesion became a metaphor for staying in place. Cognate with Dutch ~blijven~, English ~belive~ ("to remain, stay").
en.blicken:
From MHG ~blicken~, from OHG ~blicchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~blikk-~, from Proto-Germanic *~bliką~ / *~blikkaz~ ("shine, appearance, look"), from PIE *~bʰleyǵ-~ ("to shine").

The root noun ~Blick~ ("glance, look") is cognate with Dutch ~blik~ ("look, glance; tin"), Danish ~blik~ ("look, glance; tin"), Icelandic ~blik~ ("gleam, shine"), and Old English ~blice~ ("an exposure, manifestation"). The PIE root *~bʰleyǵ-~ also underlies ~bleichen~ ("to bleach"), English ~bleach~, and ~blank~ — all carrying the idea of a bright, shining surface. A ~Blick~ is thus a flash of light turned into a directed gaze, and ~blicken~ retains that original sense of a sudden, luminous perception. In modern colloquial German, ~blicken~ has also developed the meaning "to get it, to understand" (~Du blickst es einfach nicht~ — "You just don't get it") — a semantic extension from visual to mental comprehension.
en.blockieren:
Borrowed in the 17th century from French ~bloquer~ ("to enclose, to block"), from Old French ~bloquer~, derived from ~bloc~ ("block, log"), from Middle Dutch ~blok~ ("tree trunk"), from Proto-West Germanic *~blokk~, from Proto-Germanic *~blukką~ ("beam, log"), from PIE *~bʰelǵ-~ ("thick plank, beam, prop").

~Blockieren~ is a Germanic word that traveled to France and came back. Proto-Germanic *~blukką~ ("beam, log") was borrowed into Old French as ~bloc~, which developed the verb ~bloquer~ ("to obstruct with a log"), which German then re-borrowed as ~blockieren~ in the 17th century. This round trip — Germanic to French to Germanic — is a common pattern for words that gained new semantic layers during their French sojourn. The original concrete image of blocking a road with a log has generalized to any form of obstruction: ~eine Straße blockieren~ ("to block a road"), ~Verhandlungen blockieren~ ("to block negotiations"), ~das Rad blockiert~ ("the wheel locks up").
en.brauchen: From Middle High German ~brūchen~, from Old High German ~brūhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~brūkan~ ("to use, employ, consume"), from Proto-Germanic *~brūkaną~, from PIE *~bʰruHg-~ ("to make use of"). The semantic shift from "to use" (still preserved in the noun ~Brauch~, "custom, usage") to "to need" is a modern development; the older sense persists in formal and legal registers. Cognate with Dutch ~bruiken~, English ~brook~ ("to tolerate, bear"), Latin ~fruor~ ("to enjoy").
en.brechen: ~brechen~ comes from Middle High German ~brëchen~, from Old High German ~brëhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~brekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~brekaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~bʰreg-~ ("to break"). This is the same word as English ~break~: the two are direct cognates, separated only by the High German consonant shift (Proto-Germanic *b remained *b in English but the vowel and consonant system diverged thereafter). The same PIE root underlies Latin ~frangere~ ("to break"), whose past participle ~fractus~ gives English ~fracture~, ~fragment~, and ~fraction~. Cognate with Dutch ~breken~, Old Saxon ~brekan~, and Danish ~brække~. ~Brechen~ covers a remarkable semantic range in modern German: to break physically, to vomit (~brechen~ colloquially), to violate a promise (~ein Versprechen brechen~), and to refract light (~Licht brechen~) — all held together by the PIE root's core sense of sudden separation.
en.brennen:
From Middle High German ~brennen~, a merger of two Old High German verbs: ~brennan~ ("to set on fire," transitive) from Proto-West Germanic *~brannijan~, and ~brinnan~ ("to be on fire," intransitive) from Proto-West Germanic *~brinnan~. The transitive form *~brannijan~ was the causative of *~brinnan~, formed with Proto-Germanic *~-janą~. The intransitive *~brinnaną~ derives from Proto-Indo-European *~bʰrewh₁-~ ("to boil, brew"), making ~brennen~ an unexpected cousin of English ~brew~ and ~broth~. Cognate with English ~burn~, Dutch ~branden~, Swedish ~brinna~, Icelandic ~brenna~.

The merger of two originally distinct verbs into a single paradigm is relatively rare in German. In modern usage, ~brennen~ handles both the transitive sense ("to burn something") and the intransitive ("to be on fire"), a job that required two separate verbs in Old High German. The noun ~Brand~ ("fire, blaze") preserves the ancient strong-verb ablaut grade.
en.bringen: From Middle High German ~bringen~, from Old High German ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringaną~, probably from a pre-Germanic *~bʰrenk-~, a compound root from PIE *~bʰer-~ ("to carry") + *~h₁nek-~ ("to bring") or *~h₂nek-~ ("to reach, attain"). This verb exhibits a mixed conjugation in Germanic: its past tense ~brachte~ combines a strong-verb vowel change (~i~ > ~a~, from the o-grade *~bʰronk-~) with a weak-verb dental suffix (~-te~). It shares this pattern with ~denken~ (~dachte~), though some linguists distinguish ~bringen~'s vowel change as a true ablaut grade shift rather than the umlaut removal seen in ~denken~. Cognate with Low German ~brengen~, Dutch ~brengen~, Afrikaans ~bring~, English ~bring~, West Frisian ~bringe~, Gothic ~briggan~.
en.buchen: ~Buchen~ ("to book, reserve") in its commercial sense is a denominal verb from ~Buch~ ("book"), modelled after English ~to book~. ~Buch~ itself descends from Middle High German ~buoch~, Old High German ~buoh~, Proto-West Germanic *~bōk~, and Proto-Germanic *~bōks~, cognate with English ~book~ and — more surprisingly — English ~beech~, from the same root *~bōks~. Early Germanic writing was scratched onto beech-wood tablets, and the tree name became the word for a written document. There is also a separate, older German verb ~buchen~ meaning "of or relating to the beech tree" (an adjective), from ~Buche~ ("beech"). The commercial sense — booking a hotel, a flight — is thus a modern reborrowing of a concept that traveled from Germanic beech forests to Old English ~bōc~ to modern English ~to book~ and back into German. ~Buchen~ joins English and German in a circular etymological journey spanning fifteen centuries.
en.danken:
From Middle High German ~danken~, from Old High German ~dankōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~þankōną~, a denominative verb derived from *~þankaz~ ("thought, gratitude"), from Proto-Indo-European *~teng-~ ("to perceive, think, feel"). Cognate with Dutch ~danken~, English ~thank~, Danish ~takke~.

The PIE root *~teng-~ is the common ancestor of both German ~danken~ ("to thank") and ~denken~ ("to think"), and likewise of English ~thank~ and ~think~. These are not coincidentally similar words — they descend from the same ancient root. To thank someone originally meant to hold them in thought, to bear them in mind. The Proto-Germanic noun *~þankaz~ carried both meanings: "thought, remembrance" and "gratitude." English ~thank~ and ~think~ (from Old English ~þanc~ and ~þencan~) are doublets from this same source. The phrase ~vielen Dank~ ("many thanks") thus preserves, in every syllable, a word that once meant simply "a thought."
en.darlegen:
Compound of ~dar-~ + ~legen~:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjaną~, the causative of *~ligjaną~ ("to lie"), from PIE *~logʰéyeti~, the causative form of *~legʰ-~ ("to lie down"). Cognate with English ~lay~, Danish ~lægge~.
- ~dar-~: From OHG ~dār~ ("there, thither"), from Proto-West Germanic *~þār~, from Proto-Germanic *~þar~ ("there"). Related to ~da~ ("there") and the English prefix ~there-~ (as in ~therefore~). The prefix conveys presenting something for inspection, placing it "there" before the audience.

The compound means "to set forth, to explain, to present" — to "lay there" an argument or facts for examination. The prefix ~dar-~ is an archaic adverbial form of ~da~ ("there") that survives almost exclusively in a few fixed compounds: ~darlegen~, ~darstellen~ ("to present, to portray"), ~darbieten~ ("to offer"). The root ~legen~ stands in a classic causative relationship with ~liegen~ ("to lie"): ~legen~ means "to cause to lie, to lay," and the pair descends from a PIE construction where adding *~-éye-~ to a verb created its causative. English preserves the same pair: ~lay~ (causative) versus ~lie~ (base).
en.darstellen:
From ~dar-~ + ~stellen~. Compound of:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb meaning "to place, set" — the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~ ("to stand").
- ~dar-~: From Old High German ~dara~ ("thither, to that place"), itself from an extended form of OHG ~thār~/~dār~ ("there"), from Proto-West Germanic *~þār~. The directional -~r-~ suffix marked motion toward a place, distinguishing ~dar~ ("thither") from the static ~da~ ("there"). In Modern German, ~dar-~ is largely fossilized, surviving chiefly in compound verbs like ~darstellen~, ~darlegen~, and ~darbringen~; as a productive prefix before vowels it persists as a variant of ~da-~.

The compound ~darstellen~ — literally "to set forth, place there" — shifted from its concrete spatial meaning to the abstract senses of "to represent, depict, portray," much as English ~present~ shifted from "to place before" to "to introduce, perform." Cognate with Dutch ~voorstellen~ (same semantic development, different prefix).
en.dauern:
~dauern~ carries two entirely separate senses with distinct etymological origins that have been grafted together under one spelling.

~dauern~ "to last, endure": From Middle High German ~dūren~, borrowed from Middle Low German ~dūren~, itself from Latin ~dūrō~ ("to last, endure, harden"), a denominative verb from ~dūrus~ ("hard, lasting"). Latin ~dūrus~ derives from Proto-Italic *~dūros~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dweh₂-~ ("long, far") — the same root underlying Sanskrit ~dūra-~ ("far, distant") and Ancient Greek ~dērós~ ("long"). The path into German ran through Low German contact in the 12th century, gradually displacing the older native form ~weren~ (a forerunner of modern ~währen~). Cognate with Dutch ~duren~, English ~dure~.

~dauern~ "to cause pity, to feel sorry for" (as in ~er dauert mich~, "I feel sorry for him"): From Middle High German ~tūren~, itself an ablaut variant of ~tiure~ ("dear, precious, costly"), the Middle High German ancestor of modern ~teuer~. The original sense was "to be dear, to be costly (to someone)" — hence "to be felt as a loss, to arouse pity." The initial ~d-~ in the modern form results from Low German influence on the High German form. This sense is thus a doublet of ~teuer~ by a different phonological route.
en.deaktivieren:
Compound of ~de-~ + ~aktivieren~:

- ~aktivieren~: From ~aktiv~ + ~-ieren~. ~Aktiv~ is borrowed from Latin ~āctīvus~ ("active, practical"), from ~āctus~, past participle of ~agere~ ("to do, to drive"), from PIE *~h₂eǵ-~ ("to drive"). The suffix ~-ieren~ is the standard German adaptation of Latin/French ~-āre~/~-er~ for creating verbs from borrowed adjectives.
- ~de-~: A Latin-derived prefix meaning "un-, de-," borrowed into German from Latin ~dē~ ("from, down, away"), from PIE *~de-~ ("from"). It reverses the action of the base verb.

The verb means "to deactivate, to disable." It is a fully Latinate creation on German soil — every morpheme traces back to Latin: ~dē~ ("away") + ~āctīvus~ ("active") + ~-āre~ (verbal suffix). The PIE root *~h₂eǵ-~ ("to drive") produced an enormous family across Indo-European: Latin ~agere~ ("to do") yielded English ~act~, ~agent~, ~agenda~, ~agitate~; Greek ~ágein~ ("to lead") yielded ~pedagogue~ and ~demagogue~; and Sanskrit ~ájati~ ("to drive") continued the original sense. German borrowed ~aktivieren~ relatively late, as part of the 18th-century wave of Latinate technical vocabulary. Its antonym ~deaktivieren~ followed naturally.
en.decken: From Middle High German ~decken~, from Old High German ~decchen~, ~thecken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þakkjan~ ("to cover"), from Proto-Germanic *~þakjaną~ ("to cover, to roof"), a causative formation from *~þaką~ ("roof, thatch"), from PIE *~(s)teg-~ ("to cover"). The verb is thus etymologically "to roof" — to place a covering over something, as one covers a house with thatch.
