The PIE root *~(s)teg-~ is extraordinarily productive: it gave Latin ~tegere~ ("to cover"), whence English ~protect~, ~detect~, ~integument~; Latin ~toga~ (a garment that covers); Latin ~tēgula~ ("roof tile"), whence English ~tile~ and German ~Ziegel~; and Greek ~stegos~ ("roof"), whence ~stegosaurus~ (the "roofed lizard," named for its bony back plates). Within Germanic, the same root produced English ~thatch~, Dutch ~dekken~, Swedish ~täcka~, and Danish ~tække~. The semantic range of modern ~decken~ extends far beyond roofing: one ~deckt~ a table (sets it, literally "covers" it with cloth and dishes), ~deckt~ expenses (covers them financially), ~deckt~ an opponent in sports (covers or marks them), and ~deckt~ a mare (covers her — the equestrian breeding term). The noun ~Decke~ ("blanket, ceiling, cover") and ~Dach~ ("roof") are siblings from the same root.
en.definieren: ~Definieren~ ("to define") is borrowed directly from Latin ~dēfīnīre~ ("to set a boundary, mark out limits, define"), from ~dē-~ (an intensifying prefix, here meaning "completely, fully") + ~fīnīre~ ("to limit, bound, end"), from ~fīnis~ ("boundary, end, goal"). The etymology of Latin ~fīnis~ itself is disputed: it may derive from Proto-Indo-European *~dʰeygʷ-~ ("to stick, fix"), which also produced Latin ~fīgere~ ("to fasten") — a boundary marker being literally something stuck in the ground. Alternatively it may trace to *~bʰeyd-~ ("to split"), a boundary being a place of division. English ~fine~ (as in "fine arts," meaning "finished, complete"), ~final~, ~finish~, ~finance~, and ~confine~ all share this Latin ancestor. When one ~definiert~ a concept, one is etymologically "drawing its boundaries completely" — marking where it ends and something else begins.
en.demonstrieren:
From Latin ~dēmōnstrāre~ ("to point out, show, demonstrate"), a compound of ~dē-~ ("from, down") + ~mōnstrāre~ ("to show"), itself from ~mōnstrum~ ("divine omen, portent"), from ~monēre~ ("to warn, advise"), from PIE *~mon-eye-~ (causative of *~men-~ "to think"). Borrowed into German in the early sixteenth century with the meaning "to show, to prove." The protest sense ("to take part in a demonstration") developed after 1850 under influence from the derived noun ~Demonstration~.

The Latin etymon ~mōnstrum~ originally meant a divine omen or warning sign -- something shown by the gods as a portent. It is the same word that gave English ~monster~ (a prodigious or unnatural creature seen as an omen). So ~demonstrieren~ and ~Monster~ are, etymologically, siblings: both descend from the act of divine showing. The PIE root *~men-~ ("to think") is extraordinarily productive: it yielded Latin ~mens~ ("mind"), Greek ~menos~ ("spirit"), Sanskrit ~manas~ ("mind"), English ~mind~, ~mental~, ~monitor~, ~monument~, and ~money~ (from ~Monēta~, an epithet of Juno, in whose temple coins were minted).
en.denken: From Middle High German ~denken~, from Old High German ~denken~, ~thenken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þankijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þankijaną~, from PIE *~teng-~ ("to think, feel"). A mixed-conjugation verb: its past tense ~dachte~ combines a vowel change (~e~ > ~a~, from the removal of the nasal-conditioned umlaut) with a weak dental suffix (~-te~). It shares this pattern with ~bringen~ (~brachte~), though some linguists distinguish ~bringen~'s vowel change as a true ablaut grade shift rather than the umlaut removal seen in ~denken~. The same PIE root yields ~Dank~ ("thanks") and ~Gedanke~ ("thought"), reflecting the deep semantic connection between thinking and thanking in the Germanic languages. Cognate with Bavarian ~denkn~, Dutch ~denken~, Low German ~denken~, West Frisian ~tinke~, English ~think~, Icelandic ~þekkja~.
en.deuten:
From Middle High German ~diuten~, from Old High German ~diuten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þiudijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þiudijaną~ ("to associate with; to interpret; to point out"), derived from Proto-Germanic *~þeudō~ ("people"), from PIE *~tewtéh₂~ ("people"). Cognate with Dutch ~duiden~ ("to indicate, to interpret"), Icelandic ~þýða~ ("to translate"), Swedish ~tyda~ ("to interpret, to decipher"), Danish ~tyde~ ("to interpret").

The semantic path from "people" to "interpret" is one of the most fascinating in Germanic etymology. The original meaning was "to make intelligible to the people" — to translate from learned or sacred language into the common tongue. This is precisely the same word that gives German ~deutsch~ ("German"), literally "of the people," via Proto-Germanic *~þiudiskaz~ ("pertaining to the people"), which distinguished the vernacular Germanic languages from Latin. To ~deuten~ was, in a sense, to speak ~deutsch~ — to render comprehensible in the people's language. The modern meanings — "to interpret" (dreams, omens, texts: ~Träume deuten~) and "to point" (~auf etwas deuten~, "to point at something") — both descend from this original act of making meaning accessible. The derived noun ~Bedeutung~ ("meaning, significance") and the adjective ~deutlich~ ("clear, distinct") preserve the interpretive core. That ~deutsch~ and ~deuten~ share a root is not mere folk etymology but genuine linguistic kinship: both spring from the Proto-Germanic concept of "the people" and their language.
en.dienen: From Middle High German ~dienen~, from Old High German ~dionōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~þewanōną~ ("to serve"), a denominative verb derived from *~þewaz~ ("servant, slave"). The etymology of *~þewaz~ is uncertain; one hypothesis derives it from PIE *~tekʷ-~ ("to run"), with "servant" as "one who runs (errands)"; another connects it to *~tew(h₂)-~ ("to swell, grow"), though neither etymology is secure. The same Proto-Germanic root *~þewaz~ underlies Old English ~þegn~ ("thane, servant"), which by a remarkable social elevation came to mean "warrior, nobleman" and eventually became the Scottish title ~thane~ — immortalized in Shakespeare's ~Macbeth~. Cognate with Dutch ~dienen~, English ~thane~ (via the related noun), Danish ~tjene~.
en.diskutieren: Borrowed into German from French ~discuter~, from Latin ~discutiō~ ("to shake apart, scatter, examine, discuss"), a compound of ~dis-~ ("apart") + ~quatiō~ ("to shake"). Latin ~quatiō~ derives from Proto-Italic *~kʷatjō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)kwot-i-~ ("to shake"). The original sense of ~discutiō~ was entirely physical — "to smash to pieces, disperse" — and only later shifted to the figurative sense of "to examine thoroughly, to sift through arguments." This semantic journey from "shaking apart" to intellectual analysis is a vivid metaphor: a discussion, etymologically, is a shaking-apart of ideas. German received ~diskutieren~ via French contact, using the standard ~-ieren~ suffix that marks French and Latin loanverbs and always produces weak verbs. Cognate with French ~discuter~, English ~discuss~, Spanish ~discutir~, Italian ~discutere~.
en.dokumentieren: A denominative verb derived from the noun ~Dokument~ ("document"), with the productive ~-ieren~ suffix. ~Dokument~ was borrowed from Latin ~documentum~ ("lesson, proof, example"), from the verb ~doceō~ ("to teach, to show"), from PIE *~doḱ-éye-ti~, the causative of *~deḱ-~ ("to accept, to receive, to perceive"). The semantic shift from "a lesson" to "a written proof" happened in Medieval Latin, where ~documentum~ came to mean a charter or official record — something that ~teaches~ you the truth by ~showing~ evidence. The same PIE root *~deḱ-~ produced Latin ~decēns~ ("fitting, proper") — whence English ~decent~ — and ~doctor~ (originally "a teacher"), as well as Greek ~dóksa~ ("opinion, glory") — whence English ~doxology~ and ~orthodox~. German ~dokumentieren~ thus etymologically means "to teach through evidence." Cognate with Dutch ~documenteren~, English ~document~, French ~documenter~.
en.dominieren:
Borrowed in the 14th century from Latin ~dominārī~ ("to rule, to dominate"), from ~dominus~ ("lord, master"), from ~domus~ ("house, household"), from PIE *~dóm-~ ("house"), from the root *~dem-~ ("to build, to put together"). Cognate with English ~dominate~, French ~dominer~, Italian ~dominare~.

~Dominieren~ means "to dominate, to prevail, to be predominant." As a Latin borrowing, it stands alongside native Germanic synonyms like ~beherrschen~ ("to rule over") and ~vorherrschen~ ("to predominate"), but ~dominieren~ carries a specifically forceful connotation — asserting supremacy rather than merely holding authority. The PIE root *~dem-~ ("to build") reveals a domestic origin for the concept of dominion: the ~dominus~ was literally the master of the ~domus~, the household. This root produced an extraordinary range of descendants: Latin ~domesticus~ (whence English ~domestic~), ~domicilium~ (whence ~domicile~), Greek ~démos~ ("the people," originally "district" — whence ~democracy~), Russian ~dom~ ("house"), and English ~timber~ (from Proto-Germanic *~timrą~, "building material"). The journey from "house" to "lord" to "to dominate" traces the expansion of authority from a single household to an empire.
en.drehen:
From Middle High German ~dræjen~, ~dræhen~, from Old High German ~drāen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þrāan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þrēaną~ ("to twist, turn"), from Proto-Indo-European *~terh₁-~ ("to rub, rub by twisting, twist, turn"). Cognate with Bavarian ~drahn~, Dutch ~draaien~, English ~throw~.

The cognate with English ~throw~ is not a false friend but a true one — it is simply that ~throw~ has shifted its meaning dramatically since the Old English period. Old English ~þrāwan~ meant "to twist, turn," not "to hurl," and the act of hurling was expressed by the now-archaic ~weorpan~ (cognate with German ~werfen~). At some point in the Middle English period, ~throw~ displaced ~weorp-~ as the word for tossing and was itself displaced by ~warp~ as the word for twisting — a near-complete swap of semantic roles. The shared ancestry with ~drehen~ is visible in Dutch ~draaien~, Low German ~draien~. The PIE root *~terh₁-~ also underlies Latin ~terere~ ("to rub, wear away"), English ~thread~ (something twisted), and English ~throw~ (in the potters' sense: "to throw a pot" still means "to shape clay on a turning wheel").
en.dringen:
From Middle High German ~dringen~, from Old High German ~dringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þringwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þrinhwaną~, from PIE *~trenkʷ-~ ("to beat, hew, press"). Akin to ~drängen~ ("to push, urge") and ~Drang~ ("urge, drive"). Cognate with dialectal English ~thring~ ("to press, to crowd") and, more distantly, with English ~throng~.

~Dringen~ is a class III strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~dringen~, ~drang~, ~gedrungen~. It means "to press through, to penetrate, to insist urgently." The related causative ~drängen~ ("to urge, to push") has the expected umlaut marking of Germanic causative formation: the base vowel ~a~ from the past tense grade becomes ~ä~ in the derived verb. The noun ~Drang~ ("drive, urge, impulse") is famous from the literary movement ~Sturm und Drang~ ("Storm and Drive"), the late 18th-century revolt against rationalist constraints that launched German Romanticism. English ~throng~ (a pressing crowd) preserves the original sense of people pressing together. The initial Proto-Germanic *~þr-~ cluster regularly became ~dr-~ in Old High German through the consonant shift.
en.drohen:
From Middle High German ~drōn~, ~drōwen~, from Old High German ~drouwen~, ~drōwen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þrauwijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þraujaną~ ("to threaten"). The archaic variant ~dräuen~ preserves the older vowel pattern more faithfully. Cognate with Old English ~þrēagan~ ("to rebuke, threaten"), Old Saxon ~thrōian~, Middle Dutch ~drouwen~.

The shift from ~drouwen~ to ~drohen~ involved a simplification of the diphthong ~ou~ to long ~ō~ and loss of the ~w~, a common phonological development in Middle High German. The older form ~dräuen~ (with umlaut from the ~j~ in *~þrauwijan~) survives in literary German and Luther's Bible translation but is otherwise archaic. The derived noun ~Drohung~ ("threat") and the compound ~bedrohen~ ("to threaten someone directly") are far more common in modern usage than the simplex ~drohen~ in isolation. English lost its cognate ~threaten~ (from ~þrēat~, a different formation) but the related Old English ~þrēagan~ ("to rebuke") died out, leaving no direct modern English reflex of this particular Germanic root.
en.drucken:
From Middle High German ~drucken~, from Old High German ~drucchen~/~drukken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þrukkijan~ ("to press"). This is a doublet of ~drücken~ ("to press, push"), which preserves the expected umlaut. The lack of umlaut in ~drucken~ reflects Upper German dialect phonology, where umlaut before a velar geminate was blocked. Since the first German printers (Gutenberg and his successors) were based in the south, this southern dialectal form became the established technical term for printing in the seventeenth century.

The semantic split is clean: ~drucken~ means "to print" (text, books, newspapers), while ~drücken~ means "to press, push" (a button, a hand). Both descend from the same Proto-Germanic root, but the printing revolution cemented two distinct verbs. The derived noun ~Druck~ can mean both "print" and "pressure," preserving the original unity. Polish ~drukować~ ("to print") was borrowed from German ~drucken~.
en.drängen:
From a merger of two Middle High German verbs: (1) MHG ~drengen~, from OHG ~drengen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þrangwijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þrangwijaną~; and (2) the now-obsolete ~drangen~, from MHG ~drangen~, from OHG ~drangōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þrangōn~. Both derive ultimately from PIE *~trenkʷ-~ ("to beat, hew, press"). Cognate with dialectal English ~thring~ ("to press, push") and, more distantly, with English ~throng~ (a pressing crowd).

The fact that two separate verbs merged into a single modern form is an unusual etymological event. The causative *~þrangwijaną~ ("to cause to press") and the denominative *~þrangōn~ ("to act like a press/crowd") converged because their meanings overlapped so thoroughly. The related strong verb ~dringen~ ("to penetrate, press through") preserves the intransitive sense, while ~drängen~ took over the transitive and causative meanings ("to push, urge, press someone"). The noun ~Drang~ ("urge, drive, pressure"), as in ~Sturm und Drang~ ("Storm and Stress"), the famous 18th-century literary movement, derives from the same root. The English word ~throng~ entered the language from Old Norse ~þrǫng~ ("crowd"), itself from the same Proto-Germanic family, making it a Scandinavian cousin of this purely West Germanic verb.
en.drücken: From Middle High German ~drücken~, from Old High German ~drucchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þrukkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þrukkijaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~trewk-~ ("to press on and break"). Cognate with Dutch ~drukken~, Low German ~drücken~, Old English ~þryċċan~ (surviving in dialectal English ~thrutch~). A striking consequence of the same root is the doublet within German itself: ~drücken~ ("to press") and ~drucken~ ("to print") descend from the same Old High German verb, with the printing sense arising from Gutenberg-era specialization of the pressing gesture. Welsh ~trychu~ ("to cut") and Lithuanian ~trū́kti~ ("to tear, burst") are cognates outside Germanic, illuminating the original sense of forceful rupture.
en.durchführen:
Compound of ~durch-~ + ~führen~:

- ~führen~: From MHG ~vüeren~, from OHG ~fuoren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fōrijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fōrijaną~, the causative of *~faraną~ ("to go, travel"), from PIE *~per-~ ("going, passage"). Cognate with Dutch ~voeren~, English ~fare~ (from the base verb).
- ~durch-~: From MHG ~durch~, from OHG ~duruh~, from Proto-Germanic *~þurhw~, from PIE *~terh₂-~ ("to cross, pass through") combined with *~h₃ekʷ-~ ("to see") — a compound root suggesting "to see across, to look through." Cognate with English ~through~ and ~thorough~, Dutch ~door~, West Frisian ~troch~, Yiddish ~durkh~.

The compound means "to carry out, to execute, to implement" — literally to lead something all the way through to completion. The spatial metaphor is transparent: a plan or project is guided through the full extent of its path from start to finish. The English expression ~carry through~ shares the same spatial-completive logic. Cognate with Dutch ~doorvoeren~.
en.durchlaufen:
Compound of ~durch-~ + ~laufen~:

- ~laufen~: From MHG ~loufen~, from OHG ~hloufan~, ~loufan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaupan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaupaną~ ("to jump forward, to leap"), from PIE *~klewb-~ ("to spring, to hop"). Cognate with Dutch ~lopen~, English ~leap~ and ~lope~, Danish ~løbe~.
- ~durch-~: From MHG ~durch~, from OHG ~duruh~, from Proto-Germanic *~þurhw~, from PIE *~terkʷ-~ ("to cross, pass through"). Cognate with English ~through~. The prefix conveys traversal from beginning to end.

As an inseparable compound (~durchláufen~, stressed on ~-lau-~), it means "to pass through, to go through (a process, a stage)" — ~ein Programm durchlaufen~ ("to go through a program"). As a separable compound (~dúrchlaufen~, stressed on ~durch-~), it means "to run through without stopping" or "to wear through (shoes) by running." The stress distinction changes both meaning and grammar: inseparable ~durchlaufen~ takes no ~ge-~ in the past participle (~hat durchlaufen~), while separable ~durchlaufen~ does (~ist durchgelaufen~). This stress-based split between separable and inseparable verbs is a distinctive feature of German that has no direct parallel in English. The PIE root *~terkʷ-~ ("to cross through") is also the ancestor of English ~through~ and ~thorough~ — originally the same word.
en.durchsetzen:
~durch-~ + ~setzen~. Compound of ~durch-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjaną~ ("to set, place"), the causative of *~sitjaną~ ("to sit"), from PIE *~sed-~ ("to sit").
- ~durch-~: From PIE *~terkʷ-~ ("to cross, pass through"). The prefix adds the sense of pushing through or carrying through to completion.

~Durchsetzen~ is one of those German verbs whose meaning shifts dramatically depending on whether it is separable or inseparable. As an inseparable verb (~durchsetzen~, stress on ~-setz-~), it means "to intersperse, to permeate" — a crowd ~durchsetzt~ with undercover agents, a text ~durchsetzt~ with errors. As a separable verb (~durchsetzen~, stress on ~durch-~), it means "to push through, to enforce, to prevail" — one ~setzt~ a plan ~durch~ or ~setzt sich durch~ ("prevails"). The reflexive ~sich durchsetzen~ is among the most common uses: to assert oneself, to succeed against resistance. The underlying image in both cases is "setting something through" an obstacle, but whether the obstacle is permeated passively or overcome actively depends on stress placement alone. Cognate with Dutch ~doorzetten~ ("to persevere, to push through").
en.dürfen: From Middle High German ~durfen~, ~dürfen~, from Old High German ~durfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þurban~, from Proto-Germanic *~þurbaną~, from PIE *~terp-~ ("to satiate, enjoy"). A preterite-present verb — its present-tense conjugations descend from what was originally a past-tense (perfect) formation, giving it the irregular present paradigm typical of this class (~darf~, ~darfst~, ~darf~ rather than the expected weak endings). The original sense of "to need" (still preserved in ~bedürfen~) shifted in Modern German to "to be allowed to." Cognate with Ancient Greek ~terpo~ ("I delight, please"), English ~tharf~ (archaic, "to need").
en.ehren:
From Middle High German ~ēren~, from Old High German ~ēren~, from Proto-Germanic *~aizāną~, from PIE *~h₂eys-~ ("to respect, to be in awe of"). Derived from the noun ~Ehre~ ("honor"), which descends from OHG ~ēra~ ("honor, fame, sense of honor"), from Proto-West Germanic *~aiʀu~, from Proto-Germanic *~aizō~. Cognate with Old Norse ~eir~ ("pardon, gentleness"), Old Saxon ~ēra~ ("honor, protection, pardon"), Old English ~ār~ ("honor, help, pardon"), and Dutch ~eer~ ("honor").

The verb ~ehren~ means "to honor, to respect, to pay tribute to." The connection to Latin ~aestimāre~ ("to value, to acknowledge"), whence English ~esteem~ and ~estimate~, has been proposed via the PIE root, suggesting that the Indo-European speakers linked the concept of honor with the act of valuation. The proverb ~Ehrlich währt am längsten~ ("Honesty lasts the longest") uses the derived adjective ~ehrlich~ ("honest," literally "honor-like"). The compound ~Ehrenamt~ ("honorary office, volunteer position") reflects the German cultural concept of unpaid civic duty performed for honor rather than payment — a cornerstone of German community life that has no single-word English equivalent. The expression ~jemandem die letzte Ehre erweisen~ ("to pay someone the last honor") refers to attending a funeral, connecting ~Ehre~ to rites of passage.
en.eignen:
From Middle High German ~eigenen~, from Old High German ~eiganen~, from Proto-Germanic *~aiginōną~, a denominative verb derived from *~aiganaz~ ("owned, one's own"), the past participle of *~aiganą~ ("to possess, own"), from PIE *~h₂eh₂óyḱe~, from *~h₂eyḱ-~ ("ability, possession"). The verb ~eignen~ thus originally meant "to make one's own, to appropriate" — a sense still visible in the reflexive ~sich etwas aneignen~ ("to acquire, appropriate something for oneself"). The modern reflexive ~sich eignen~ ("to be suited, to be fit for") developed from the idea of being the proper or fitting owner of some quality or role.

The adjective ~eigen~ ("own, characteristic") shares the same PGmc root and is cognate with English ~own~ and Dutch ~eigen~. The PIE root *~h₂eyḱ-~ ("ability, possession") also underlies Gothic ~aigan~ ("to have") and Old English ~āgan~ ("to own"), the latter giving English ~owe~ (its present tense) and ~ought~ (its past tense). Cognate with Swedish ~ägna~, English ~own~.
en.einbauen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~bauen~:

- ~bauen~: From MHG ~būwen~, from OHG ~būan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~būan~, from Proto-Germanic *~būaną~, from PIE *~bʰuH-~ ("to grow, to become, to dwell").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). This separable prefix adds the sense of inserting or incorporating something into a larger structure.

~Einbauen~ means "to install, to build in, to incorporate" — a concept central to modern engineering and construction. You ~baust eine Küche ein~ ("install a kitchen"), ~baust ein Feature ein~ ("incorporate a feature"), or ~baust einen Motor ein~ ("install an engine"). The PIE root *~bʰuH-~ ("to grow, to become") is astonishingly productive: it yields English ~be~, ~build~, ~booth~, and ~bower~, as well as Greek ~φύω~ ("to grow," whence ~physics~ and ~physiology~) and Latin ~fuī~ ("I was"). The semantic chain from "growing" to "dwelling" to "building" to "building in" shows language faithfully tracking human cultural evolution from nomadic life to settled construction.
en.einbeziehen:
A modern compound of ~ein-~ + ~beziehen~:

- ~beziehen~: From MHG ~beziehen~, from OHG ~biziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~biteuhan~. Compound of ~be-~ + ~ziehen~:
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~īn~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). As a separable prefix, it means "in, into."
  - ~be-~: From PIE *~h₁epi~ ("on, at, near"), makes the verb transitive.
  - ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhaną~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Cognate with English ~tow~ (via Old English ~tēon~), Latin ~dūcō~ ("I lead"), and English ~duke~ (via Latin).

The sense "to refer to" in ~beziehen~ is probably a calque of Latin ~referre~ ("to carry back, to refer"). The compound ~einbeziehen~ ("to include, to involve") thus literally means "to pull into," a vivid spatial metaphor for inclusion. The PIE root *~dewk-~ has produced a remarkable constellation of words across the Indo-European languages: Latin ~dūcō~ gave us ~duke~, ~educate~, ~deduce~, ~conduct~, and ~produce~, while the Germanic branch gave us ~ziehen~, ~tow~, and ~tie~. All go back to the fundamental human action of pulling or leading. Cognate with Dutch ~betrekken~ ("to involve"), which uses a different root verb but the same ~be-~ prefix pattern.
en.einbinden:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~binden~:

- ~binden~: From MHG ~binden~, from OHG ~bintan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bindan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bindaną~ ("to bind, tie"), from PIE *~bʰendʰ-~ ("to tie, bind"). Cognate with English ~bind~, Dutch ~binden~, Danish ~binde~, Sanskrit ~bandhati~ ("binds").
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~īn~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-West Germanic *~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~h₁en~ ("in"). As a verbal prefix, ~ein-~ conveys inward motion or incorporation.

The original concrete sense was "to bind something in," particularly book-binding: ~ein Buch einbinden~ means "to bind a book" in a protective cover. The modern figurative meanings -- "to integrate" and "to involve" -- developed naturally from this: to bind something or someone into a larger whole. ~Jemanden in die Entscheidung einbinden~ ("to involve someone in the decision") treats participation as a kind of binding-in. The noun ~Einbindung~ ("integration, involvement") reflects this abstract usage.
en.einbringen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~bringen~:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringaną~, probably from PIE *~bʰrenk-~, a blend of *~bʰer-~ ("to carry") + *~h₁nek-~ ("to bring, to reach").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). The prefix conveys the sense of bringing something inward, into a system or enclosure.

~Einbringen~ means "to bring in" — covering the harvest (~die Ernte einbringen~, "to bring in the harvest"), financial yield (~Gewinn einbringen~, "to yield profit"), legislative proposals (~einen Gesetzentwurf einbringen~, "to introduce a bill"), and personal contribution (~sich einbringen~, "to contribute, to get involved"). The agricultural sense is likely the oldest: gathering the crop and bringing it inside the barn. The parliamentary sense — introducing a bill into the legislative body — mirrors English ~introduce~ (Latin "to lead inward") and French ~introduire~, all sharing the metaphor of bringing something across a threshold into an enclosed deliberative space. The reflexive ~sich einbringen~ has become a popular buzzword in modern German corporate and civic discourse, meaning to invest oneself actively in a group or project. Cognate with Dutch ~inbrengen~.
en.einfallen:
From MHG ~invallen~, from OHG ~infallan~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~fallen~:

- ~fallen~: From MHG ~vallen~, from OHG ~fallan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fallan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallaną~, possibly from PIE *~h₃elh₁-~ ("to fall") with a prefix *~h₂epo-~ ("off, away"). Cognate with English ~fall~, Dutch ~vallen~, Danish ~falde~.
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). This separable prefix conveys inward motion -- falling into something.

~Einfallen~ means "to occur to (someone), to come to mind" and also "to collapse, to cave in" and "to invade." The cognitive sense -- ~es fällt mir ein~ ("it occurs to me," literally "it falls into me") -- provides a vivid metaphor: thoughts drop into the mind unbidden, like objects falling into a container. The military sense (~in ein Land einfallen~, "to invade a country") is older and more literal: troops falling into enemy territory. The impersonal construction ~mir fällt etwas ein~ ("something occurs to me") is one of the most useful everyday expressions in German, with its opposite ~mir fällt nichts ein~ ("nothing comes to mind") being the student's eternal lament during exams.
en.einfügen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~fügen~. Compare Dutch ~invoegen~.

- ~fügen~: From MHG ~füegen~, ~fuogen~, from OHG ~fuogen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fōgijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fōgijaną~, from PIE *~peh₂ḱ-~ ("to join, attach"). Cognate with Dutch ~voegen~ and the dated English ~fay~ ("to join, fit together").
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~īn-~, from OHG ~in-~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). This prefix adds the sense of inserting or placing into.

The PIE root *~peh₂ḱ-~ connects ~einfügen~ to a remarkable family of words about joining and agreement. Through Latin ~pacisci~ ("to agree, make a covenant"), it produced English ~peace~, ~pact~, ~pacify~, and ~pay~ (originally "to appease"). English ~fair~ is also a distant relative, via the Germanic branch of the root. The reflexive ~sich einfügen~ means "to fit in, to adapt," capturing the social dimension of joining a group. The noun ~Fuge~ ("joint, seam") and the musical term ~Fuge~ ("fugue," a composition where voices are joined in imitation) both descend from the same root. The compound is transparent: to place (~fügen~) something in (~ein~), making it one of those German verbs that is easier to understand etymologically than its Latin-derived English equivalent ~insert~ (from Latin ~inserere~, "to plant in").
en.einführen:
From Middle High German ~īnvüeren~, equivalent to ~ein-~ + ~führen~:

- ~führen~: From MHG ~vüeren~, from OHG ~fuoren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fōrijan~, a causative of Proto-Germanic *~faraną~ ("to travel"), from PIE *~per-~ ("to cross, to go through").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of bringing something inward or initiating.

~Einführen~ means both "to introduce" (a concept, a person, a reform) and "to import" (goods into a country) — the prefix ~ein-~ captures the act of leading something inward, whether into awareness or across a border. The derived noun ~Einführung~ ("introduction") is the standard German word for an introductory text, course, or presentation. The import sense gives ~Einfuhr~ ("import"), a nominalization that pairs neatly with ~Ausfuhr~ ("export," from ~ausführen~) — the in-leading and out-leading of goods. Cognate with Dutch ~invoeren~.
en.eingeben:
From Middle High German ~īngeben~, from Old High German ~ingeban~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gëben~, from OHG ~geban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebaną~, from PIE *~gʰebʰ-~ ("to give"). Cognate with English ~give~ (which replaced the native Old English ~giefan~ only in form, keeping the meaning), Dutch ~geven~, Swedish ~giva~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~īn~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). As a separable verbal prefix, it means "in, into."

The literal meaning is "to give in(to)," yielding two principal modern senses: "to enter, to input" (data into a computer or system) and "to inspire, to suggest" (an idea into someone's mind). The computing sense is a natural modern extension of the older meaning: feeding information into a machine is metaphorically giving something into it. The older sense of divine or creative inspiration (~Eingebung~, "inspiration, intuition") preserves the idea that ideas are "given into" the mind from an external source. Cognate with Scots ~ingive~. The PIE root *~gʰebʰ-~ shows an intriguing semantic ambiguity between "to give" and "to take" across different branches: while Germanic languages settled on "to give," Slavic languages like Russian developed "to seize, to take" from the same root -- suggesting the original sense may have been the neutral physical act of transferring, with the direction depending on perspective.
en.eingehen:
~ein-~ + ~gehen~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gān~, ~gēn~, from OHG ~gān~, ~gēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gān~, from Proto-Germanic *~gāną~, from PIE *~ǵʰeh₁-~ ("to leave").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of going into, entering.

~Eingehen~ is one of German's most semantically branched verbs. As a separable verb it means: "to enter (into an agreement), to go into (a topic), to shrink (of fabric), to wither and die (of plants), to arrive (of mail)." The image in each case is something moving inward — into a contract, into a subject, into smaller dimensions, into death, into a mailbox. The phrase ~auf etwas eingehen~ ("to address, respond to, engage with something") is ubiquitous in academic and professional discourse: one ~geht ein auf~ a question by entering into it. The sense "to wither" (~die Pflanze geht ein~, "the plant is dying") uses the same inward-collapse metaphor: the plant goes into itself, contracts, and ceases. The PIE root *~ǵʰeh₁-~ ("to leave") produces the paradox that "going" originally meant "leaving" — a reminder that direction is always relative to a starting point. Cognate with Dutch ~ingaan~.
en.einhalten:
From ~ein-~ + ~halten~, an alteration of the older ~innehalten~, which originally shared the same meanings but has since narrowed to "to pause, to wait":

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldaną~, from PIE *~kel-~ ("to drive, to herd").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). The prefix conveys the sense of holding something inward, keeping it contained, or maintaining an internal commitment.

~Einhalten~ primarily means "to keep, to stick to, to comply with" — rules (~Regeln einhalten~), deadlines (~Fristen einhalten~), promises (~Versprechen einhalten~), and appointments (~Termine einhalten~). The underlying image is of holding something inside, keeping it intact rather than letting it slip. The older and now rare sense of "to hold back, to contain" survives in literary usage: ~Er konnte die nervösen Pferde nicht mehr einhalten~ ("He could no longer hold back the nervous horses"). A colloquial sense unique to German is "to hold one's pee" — ~Kannst du noch einhalten?~ ("Can you hold it in?") — holding something in quite literally. The dated literary usage ~Haltet ein!~ ("Desist!") preserves the imperative sense of stopping, halting an action by holding it in check. The derived noun ~Einhaltung~ ("compliance, observance") is a staple of legal and regulatory German. Related to ~Einhalt~ ("halt, cessation"), as in ~Einhalt gebieten~ ("to put a stop to"). Cognate with Dutch ~inhouden~ ("to contain, to hold back").
en.einholen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~holen~:

- ~holen~: From MHG ~holen~, ~holn~, from OHG ~holōn~, ~holēn~ ("to fetch, get, draw"), from Proto-West Germanic *~holōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~halōną~, *~hulōną~ ("to call, fetch, summon"), from PIE *~kelh₁-~ ("to call, cry, summon"). Related to English ~haul~ (borrowed into English from Old French ~haler~, which was itself borrowed from a Germanic source).
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). As a separable prefix, it means "in, into."

~Einholen~ has two distinct meanings: "to catch up with" (~einen Vorsprung einholen~ = "to close a lead") and "to fetch, to obtain" (~Erkundigungen einholen~ = "to make inquiries," ~Genehmigungen einholen~ = "to obtain approvals"). The connection between ~holen~ and English ~haul~ reveals a circular borrowing: Proto-Germanic *~halōną~ was borrowed into Old French as ~haler~, which was then borrowed into Middle English as ~haul~. So English ~haul~ is a Germanic word that took a detour through French before returning to the Germanic family.
en.einigen:
From Middle High German ~einegen~, ~einigen~, from Old High German ~gieinīgen~. Compound of ~ein~ + ~-igen~:

- ~ein~: From MHG ~ein~, from OHG ~ein~ ("one"), from Proto-West Germanic *~ain~, from Proto-Germanic *~ainaz~, from PIE *~h₁óynos~ ("one, single"). Cognate with English ~one~, Dutch ~een~, Danish ~en~.
- ~-igen~: A denominal/deadjectival suffix forming verbs from adjectives or nouns.

The verb means "to unite, to agree" and reflexively ~sich einigen~ means "to come to an agreement." The derivation from ~ein~ ("one") is transparent: to ~einigen~ is to make ~einig~ ("united, in agreement"), which is to make parties into "one." The OHG form ~gieinīgen~ shows the ~gi-~ prefix (modern ~ge-~) that was common in Old High German for creating perfective or resultative verbs. The word is inseparable from German political history: ~die (Wieder)vereinigung~ ("the (Re)unification") of 1990 uses the same root, and the motto ~Einigkeit und Recht und Freiheit~ ("Unity and Justice and Freedom") opens the German national anthem. The PIE root *~h₁óynos~ also gave Latin ~ūnus~ ("one"), whence English ~union~, ~unite~, and ~unanimous~.
en.einkaufen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~kaufen~:

- ~kaufen~: From MHG ~koufen~, from OHG ~koufōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kaupōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~kaupōną~, borrowed from Latin ~caupō~ ("innkeeper, petty tradesman, huckster"). Cognate with English ~cheap~ (originally "trade, bargain," from Old English ~cēap~ "bargain, cattle"), Dutch ~kopen~, Swedish ~köpa~, and Danish ~købe~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~in~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~ ("in, into"), from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys movement inward or acquisition.

The compound literally means "to buy in" — to bring purchased goods into the household. ~Einkaufen~ is the standard German word for grocery shopping and general retail purchasing: ~einkaufen gehen~ ("to go shopping"). The etymology of ~kaufen~ is remarkable: it is one of the oldest Latin loanwords in Germanic, borrowed during the Roman trading period along the Rhine frontier. Latin ~caupō~ meant a petty trader or tavern keeper, and the Germanic peoples adopted it to describe the act of commercial exchange itself. English ~cheap~ underwent an even more dramatic shift: from "trade" to "bargain" to "inexpensive." The related German noun ~Kaufmann~ ("merchant") literally means "trade-man" and preserves the original commercial sense. The expression ~Einkaufswagen~ ("shopping cart") and ~Einkaufszentrum~ ("shopping center") are compound-of-a-compound formations typical of German's love of agglutination.
en.einladen:
~ein-~ + ~laden~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~laden~:

- ~laden~: Two distinct verbs converged on this infinitive in Modern German. The weak verb "to invite" — the one active in ~einladen~ — derives from MHG ~laden~, from OHG ~ladon~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laþōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~laþōną~, from *~laþō~ ("invitation"), from PIE *~h₂lot-eh₂~, a root shared with Hittite ~halzai~ ("to invoke, call out"). The strong verb "to load" derives from OHG ~hladan~, from PIE *~kleh₂-~.
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of direction inward or bringing into.

The semantic journey of ~einladen~ from "to load in" to "to invite" is one of German's most charming etymological surprises. The literal sense — loading guests into one's home or vehicle — crystallized as the dominant figurative meaning, completely eclipsing any loading sense in the compound. Today ~einladen~ means exclusively "to invite," while the loading sense requires the strong-conjugation ~laden~ on its own. The Hittite cognate ~halzai~ makes this verb a rare bridge to Anatolian — one of the oldest branches of Indo-European — giving ~einladen~ an etymology that reaches back roughly 4,000 years to the earliest attested Indo-European languages. The noun ~Einladung~ ("invitation") is the result: a word built on "loading" that now means only "an invitation to come in." Cognate with Dutch ~uitnodigen~.
en.einlassen:
From Middle High German ~īnlāzen~, from Old High German ~inlāzan~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lāzen~, ~lān~, from OHG ~lāȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lātan~ ("to let, allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lētaną~ ("to leave behind, allow"), from PIE *~leh₁d-~ ("to let, to leave behind"). Cognate with Dutch ~laten~, English ~let~.
- ~ein-~: Related to ~in~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). The prefix conveys inward movement.

The compound means "to let in, to admit" and reflexively ~sich einlassen auf~ means "to get involved in, to engage with." The reflexive sense carries a connotation of risk — ~sich auf etwas einlassen~ implies committing to something uncertain, letting oneself ~in~ to a situation that may prove difficult. The root ~lassen~ belongs to the seventh class of strong verbs and retains the archaic reduplicating pattern in its past tense (~ließ~, from the old reduplicated form). The PIE root *~leh₁d-~ also produced Latin ~lassus~ ("tired, weary") — to let go is to become exhausted. English ~let~ is the direct cognate, while ~late~ (originally "slow, tardy") may also be related through the sense of "leaving behind, remaining."
en.einlegen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~legen~:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjaną~ ("to lay"), the causative of *~ligjaną~ ("to lie"). Cognate with English ~lay~, Dutch ~leggen~, Danish ~laegge~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~īn~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). As a separable prefix, it conveys the sense of placing something into a container or medium.

~Einlegen~ means "to insert, to put in, to pickle" — literally to lay something in. Its range of meanings is remarkably broad: ~Gurken einlegen~ ("to pickle cucumbers," i.e., to lay them into brine), ~eine Pause einlegen~ ("to take a break," i.e., to insert a pause), ~ein gutes Wort einlegen~ ("to put in a good word"), and ~eine CD einlegen~ ("to insert a CD"). The pickling sense gave rise to the noun ~Einlage~ ("insert, deposit, filling"), used in contexts from banking (a deposit) to soup (the noodles or dumplings added as filling). The PIE root *~legʰ-~ ("to lie, to lay") is one of the most productive in Indo-European, yielding English ~lay~, ~lie~, ~law~, ~ledger~, Latin ~lectus~ ("bed"), and Greek ~lekhos~ ("bed, lair").
en.einleiten:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~leiten~:

- ~leiten~: From MHG ~leiten~, from OHG ~leiten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laidijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~laidijaną~ ("to lead, guide, conduct"), a causative of *~līþaną~ ("to go, travel"). Cognate with English ~lead~, Old Saxon ~lēdian~, Old Norse ~leiða~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~in~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~ ("in, into"), from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys movement inward or initiation.

The compound means "to introduce, to initiate, to begin" — one ~leitet ein Verfahren ein~ ("initiates a proceeding"), ~leitet eine Rede ein~ ("opens a speech"), or ~leitet Maßnahmen ein~ ("introduces measures"). The image is of leading something inward, into existence or action. The noun ~Einleitung~ ("introduction") is the standard term for the opening section of a book, essay, or piece of music. The root verb ~leiten~ is the causative partner of an older verb meaning "to go" (still preserved in ~Leitfaden~ "guideline," literally "guiding thread"): to ~leiten~ is to "cause to go," i.e., to lead. English ~lead~ descends from the same Proto-Germanic causative. The coincidence that English ~lead~ (the verb) and ~lead~ (the metal) are spelled identically is purely accidental — the metal comes from a completely different root.
en.einnehmen:
From Middle High German ~īnnemen~, from Old High German ~inneman~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nëmen~, from OHG ~nëman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemaną~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of taking inward, receiving, or absorbing.

~Einnehmen~ covers a remarkable semantic range, all unified by the notion of taking something in: to take (a meal, medicine), to take up (a position or place), to capture (a fortress), and to win over (a person's affections). The military sense — ~eine Stadt einnehmen~ ("to capture a city") — preserves the oldest and most concrete meaning: taking something inward, under one's control. The derived noun ~Einnahme~ means "income, revenue" (money taken in) as well as "capture" (a stronghold taken in). The gentler sense of ~einnehmend~ ("charming, winning") shows how the metaphor of conquest softened: a person with an ~einnehmendes Wesen~ ("engaging manner") captures your sympathy rather than your city. Cognate with Dutch ~innemen~.
en.einreichen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~reichen~:

- ~reichen~: From MHG ~reichen~, from OHG ~reihhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raikijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~raikijaną~ ("to stretch out, reach"), a causative o-grade derivation from PIE *~Hreyǵ-~ ("to stretch out, reach"). Cognate with English ~reach~, Dutch ~reiken~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~īn~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-West Germanic *~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in").

The literal sense is "to reach something in" -- to extend or hand something into an authority's domain. Modern usage has fully specialized to mean "to submit, to hand in" (a document, an application, a complaint). The underlying spatial metaphor of reaching inward persists: one ~reicht ein~ a ~Klage~ (lawsuit), a ~Bewerbung~ (application), or a ~Petition~ (petition), each time stretching the document across a threshold toward someone who will receive it.
en.einrichten:
~ein-~ + ~richten~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~richten~:

- ~richten~: From MHG ~richten~, from OHG ~rihten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rihtijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rihtijaną~, a denominative of *~rehtaz~ ("straight"), from PIE *~h₃reǵtós~, from *~h₃reǵ-~ ("to stretch out, straighten, direct").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of directing something inward, setting it up inside a space.

~Einrichten~ means "to set up, furnish, arrange, or establish" — the image of straightening things inward, into a space, until they are ordered correctly. ~Eine Wohnung einrichten~ ("to furnish an apartment") captures the core sense: making a space right by placing things within it. The related noun ~Einrichtung~ means both "furnishings" and "institution, facility" — a semantic duality that mirrors the two applications of the verb. The same PIE root *~h₃reǵ-~ underlies Latin ~regō~ ("to rule, direct") and English ~right~, ~reign~, and ~regiment~. To set something right is, in the oldest layer of the language, to straighten it. Cognate with Dutch ~inrichten~.
en.einräumen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~räumen~:

- ~räumen~: From MHG ~rūmen~, from OHG ~rūman~, ~rūmman~, derived from the noun ~Raum~ ("room, space"). ~Raum~ from MHG ~rūm~, from OHG ~rūm~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rūm~, from Proto-Germanic *~rūmą~ ("room, space"), from PIE *~(H)rewH-~ ("to tear open, to make space"). Cognate with English ~room~, Dutch ~ruimte~, Old Norse ~rúm~.
- ~ein-~: Related to ~in~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). The prefix conveys inward placement.

The compound means "to put away (into a cupboard or room); to concede, to admit (a point); to grant (rights or authority)." The semantic leap from "putting things into a space" to "conceding a point" is bridged by the metaphor of making space for someone else's position — to ~einräumen~ an objection is to clear room for it in one's own argument. The root ~Raum~ is cognate with English ~room~: both descend from Proto-Germanic *~rūmą~, and the PIE root *~(H)rewH-~ ("to tear open") reveals the original, physical sense of creating space by clearing or breaking open ground. The adjective ~geräumig~ ("spacious") preserves the direct connection, while the verb ~aufräumen~ ("to tidy up") uses a different prefix to mean "to clear space upward."
en.einschalten:
From ~ein-~ + ~schalten~, perhaps modeled after its antonym ~ausschalten~:

- ~schalten~: From MHG ~schalten~, from OHG ~scaltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaldan~ ("to push, to shove"). The further etymology is obscure; according to Pokorny, it may derive from PIE *~(s)kelH-~ ("to split, to separate"), in which case it is related to English ~shell~. Cognate with Old Saxon ~skaldan~ ("to push").
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~in-~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-West Germanic *~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~ ("in, into"), from PIE *~h₁en~ ("in"). Cognate with English ~in~, Latin ~in~, Greek ~en~.

The original meaning of ~schalten~ was physical: "to push, to shove, to propel." From this developed the sense of operating a mechanism by pushing a lever or switch, which led to the modern meanings "to switch, to shift (gears), to control." The compound ~einschalten~ thus literally means "to push in" or "to switch in," hence "to turn on, to activate." It is also used reflexively (~sich einschalten~) to mean "to intervene, to get involved," as if inserting oneself into a process. The suggestion that ~einschalten~ may have been modeled after ~ausschalten~ is an interesting case of a word being back-formed from its antonym, though both are now completely established in their own right.
en.einschränken:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~schränken~:

- ~schränken~: From MHG ~schrenken~ ("to place crosswise"), from OHG ~screnken~ ("to spread out, throw down"), from Proto-Germanic *~skrankijaną~ ("to cause to shrink; to limit, restrict"). Related to ~Schrank~ ("cabinet, cupboard"), from OHG ~scranc~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrank~, from Proto-Germanic *~skrankaz~ ("grid, barrier"), probably from PIE *~(s)ker-~ ("to bend, turn").
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~īn~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-West Germanic *~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~h₁en~ ("in"). As a verbal prefix, ~ein-~ conveys inward motion or confinement.

The compound literally conveys the idea of placing something within barriers or limits. The related noun ~Schranke~ ("barrier, gate") preserves the concrete sense of a physical limitation, while ~einschränken~ has moved fully into the abstract domain of constraining, limiting, or restricting. The reflexive ~sich einschränken~ means "to economize" or "to tighten one's belt."
en.einschätzen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~schätzen~:

- ~schätzen~: From MHG ~schetzen~, a verbal formation from ~Schatz~ ("treasure"). ~Schatz~ descends from MHG ~schaz~, from OHG ~skaz~, ~scaz~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skatt~ ("cattle, treasure"), from Proto-Germanic *~skattaz~. Cognate with Old Norse ~skattr~ (whence Danish ~skat~, Swedish ~skatt~, all meaning "tax, treasure"), Gothic ~skatts~ ("money"), Dutch ~schat~, English ~scat~ (dialectal: "tax, tribute").
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~īn~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). As a separable prefix here, it adds the sense of placing something into a category or framework.

~Einschätzen~ means "to assess, to estimate, to evaluate" — literally to place a value (~Schatz~) on something. The semantic path from ~Schatz~ ("treasure") to ~schätzen~ ("to estimate, to value") is transparent: to appraise is to determine how much treasure something is worth. The Proto-Germanic *~skattaz~ originally meant "cattle" — the oldest form of wealth in pastoral Indo-European society — before shifting to "money, treasure." This cattle-to-currency shift is paralleled by Latin ~pecūnia~ ("money") from ~pecus~ ("cattle") and English ~fee~ from Proto-Germanic *~fehu~ ("cattle, property").
en.einsehen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~sehen~:

- ~sehen~: From MHG ~sëhen~, from OHG ~sëhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwaną~, from PIE *~sekʷ-~ ("to see, notice"). Cognate with English ~see~, Dutch ~zien~, Danish ~se~, Gothic ~saihwan~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~īn~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys "in, into."

~Einsehen~ means "to look into" literally (a document, a file) and "to realize, to understand" figuratively. The metaphorical leap from "looking into" to "understanding" appears in many languages: English ~insight~ (literally "seeing in"), Latin ~inspicere~ ("to look into," whence ~inspect~), and Greek ~ennoeō~ ("to perceive," from ~en-~ "in" + ~noeō~ "to think"). The derived noun ~Einsicht~ ("insight, realization") perfectly mirrors its English cognate.
en.einsetzen:
From Middle High German ~īnsetzen~, ~insetzen~, from Old High German ~insezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~insattjan~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjaną~ ("to set, to place down"), from PIE *~sodéyeti~. A causative derivation of *~sitjaną~ ("to sit"): literally "to cause to sit," hence "to set, place."
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~in-~, from OHG ~in-~, from Proto-Germanic *~in-~, from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). As a verbal prefix, ~ein-~ adds the sense of "into" or "in place."

The compound's range of meanings — "to insert," "to deploy (troops)," "to stake (money)," "to begin (music)" — all derive from the core sense of placing something into position or into action. Compare English ~inset~, a partial cognate. Cognate with Dutch ~inzetten~, English ~inset~.
en.einsteigen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~steigen~:

- ~steigen~: From MHG ~stīgen~, from OHG ~stīgan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stīgan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stīganą~ ("to climb, ascend"), from PIE *~steygʰ-~ ("to stride, to step, to go"). Cognate with obsolete English ~sty~ ("to ascend"), Dutch ~stijgen~ ("to rise"), Danish ~stige~, and Swedish ~stiga~.
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). As a separable prefix, it means "in, into."

~Einsteigen~ means "to board, to get in, to enter (a vehicle)" — literally "to climb in." One ~steigt ein~ in a bus, a train, a car, or an airplane. Figuratively, it also means "to get involved in, to enter (a business or discussion)": ~in ein Geschäft einsteigen~ ("to enter into a business"). The PIE root *~steygʰ-~ is the ancestor of English ~stair~ (from Proto-Germanic *~staigrī~, "that which one climbs"). The modern German opposite ~aussteigen~ ("to get out, to exit") has developed the colloquial extended meaning of "to drop out" (~aus der Gesellschaft aussteigen~ = "to drop out of society").
en.einstellen:
From Middle High German ~īnstellen~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb — the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~.
- ~ein-~: From MHG ~in-~, from OHG ~in-~, from Proto-Germanic *~in-~, from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). This separable prefix expresses motion inward or placement into a defined space.

~einstellen~ is one of the most semantically productive compound verbs in German, carrying several meanings that look quite unrelated until the spatial metaphor underlying them is understood. To hire someone (~jemanden einstellen~) is literally to place them into the workforce. To adjust a dial (~die Temperatur einstellen~) is to set it into the correct position. To stop an activity (~die Produktion einstellen~) is to put it into a state of rest. To insert a line of text is to place it into a document. All derive from the core image of ~stellen~ with the directional force of ~ein-~: placing something into its proper slot. The reflexive ~sich einstellen~ — "to arrive, to occur" (as when a feeling sets in) — adds yet another dimension: the thing places itself in. Cognate with Dutch ~instellen~.
en.einstufen:
Compound of ~ein-~ + ~Stufe~ + ~-n~:

- ~Stufe~: From MHG ~stuofe~, from OHG ~stuofa~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stōpā~, related to the verb *~stappjan~ ("to tread, to step"). Cognate with Old Saxon ~stōpo~, Dutch ~stoep~ ("stoop, front steps"), and English ~stoop~ (the porch steps of a house, a word borrowed into American English from Dutch).
- ~ein-~: Related to ~in~, from OHG ~in~, from Proto-Germanic *~in~, from PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). The prefix conveys placement into a category.

The verb means "to classify, to rank, to categorize" — to place something or someone into a ~Stufe~ ("step, level, grade"). The noun ~Stufe~ originally meant a physical step or stair and gradually extended to abstract levels and grades. The compound ~Einstufung~ ("classification") is standard in bureaucratic and academic German: ~Einstufungstest~ ("placement test") determines which ~Stufe~ a student enters. The English word ~stoop~ (the raised platform at a house entrance) traveled from Dutch ~stoep~ to New Amsterdam (later New York) in the 17th century, preserving the same Proto-West Germanic root in an unexpected American context. The South African English ~stoep~ ("veranda") is a parallel borrowing from Afrikaans.
en.eintragen:
~ein-~ + ~tragen~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganą~ ("to draw, carry, bear"), from PIE *~dʰregʰ-~ ("to draw, drag").
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of carrying into or inscribing into.

~Eintragen~ means "to enter, to register, to record" — to carry information into a book, a list, or a database. The underlying metaphor is physical: one ~trägt~ (carries, bears) data ~ein~ (into) a written record. The reflexive ~sich eintragen~ ("to sign in, to register oneself") and the noun ~Eintrag~ ("entry, record") are central to German bureaucratic life — from hotel guest books to commercial registers (~Handelsregister~). The verb can also mean "to yield, to bring in" (profit): ~das Geschäft trägt viel ein~ ("the business yields a lot"), where the image shifts from inscription to income flowing inward. Cognate with Dutch ~indragen~ ("to bring in, contribute"). The PIE root *~dʰregʰ-~ connects ~tragen~ to English ~draw~ and ~drag~ — the physical labor of hauling something from one place to another, now abstracted to moving a pen across paper.
en.eintreten:
~ein-~ + ~treten~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~treten~:

- ~treten~: From MHG ~trëten~, from OHG ~trëtan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tredan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trudaną~ ("to tread, to step on"). The West Germanic descendants regularized the verb's ablaut by replacing the original ~u~ vowel with ~e~ in the present tense, but the original vocalism may have survived longer in some High German dialects — the Medieval Latin loanword ~trottō~ ("to trot") likely reflects this older pronunciation. Cognate with English ~tread~.
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). The prefix adds the sense of entering, moving inward.

~Eintreten~ is a remarkably versatile compound. Its core meaning is "to enter, to step in" (~bitte treten Sie ein!~, "please come in!"), but it extends to "to occur, to happen" (~ein unerwartetes Ereignis ist eingetreten~, "an unexpected event has occurred"), "to join" an organization (~in eine Partei eintreten~, "to join a party"), and "to advocate for" (~für die Menschenrechte eintreten~, "to stand up for human rights"). With a direct object and ~haben~, it means "to kick in" (~die Tür eintreten~, "to kick the door in") or "to tread down" (soil). This wide semantic spread — entering, occurring, joining, advocating, kicking — all stems from the physical act of stepping into a space, with each meaning extending the metaphor of crossing a threshold.
en.einziehen:
From MHG ~inziehen~. Compound of ~ein-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhaną~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Cognate with Gothic ~tiuhan~, Old English ~tēon~, Dutch ~tien~ (obsolete), Old Norse ~toga~, and English ~tug~.
- ~ein-~: From PIE *~h₁én~ ("in"). This separable prefix adds the sense of movement inward.

~Einziehen~ means "to move in" (to a new home), "to draft" (into military service), "to collect" (debts, taxes), "to retract" (claws), and "to absorb" (moisture). The semantic thread is always drawing or pulling inward. The housing sense (~in eine Wohnung einziehen~) is the most common in everyday speech and pairs neatly with ~ausziehen~ ("to move out"). The PIE root *~dewk-~ also produced Latin ~dūcere~ ("to lead"), whence English ~duke~, ~educate~, ~conduct~, and ~produce~.
en.empfangen:
From Middle High German ~enphāhen~, from Old High German ~intvāhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~andfanhaną~. Compound of ~emp-~ (variant of ~ent-~) + ~fangen~:

- ~fangen~: From MHG ~vāhen~, from OHG ~fāhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fą̄han~, from Proto-Germanic *~fanhaną~, from PIE *~peh₂ḱ-~ ("to secure, to attach, to fasten").
- ~emp-~: A variant of ~ent-~, from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). Before labials (~f~, ~pf~), ~ent-~ becomes ~emp-~ for phonological reasons. The prefix here conveys the sense of receiving or taking in — facing something and accepting it.

~Empfangen~ means "to receive, to welcome, to conceive (a child)" — senses that span from the formal (~einen Gast empfangen~, "to receive a guest") to the biological. The Old High German form ~intvāhan~ shows the original prefix ~int-~ (= ~ent-~) still intact; the shift to ~emp-~ before ~f~ is the same sound change seen in ~empfinden~ and ~empfehlen~. Cognate with Dutch ~ontvangen~, Old Saxon ~antfāhan~. The PIE root *~peh₂ḱ-~ ("to secure, to fasten") makes the etymological image one of catching and holding — reception as a deliberate act of grasping what comes toward you, which also explains why English ~fang~ (the tooth that seizes prey) shares the same ancestor.
en.empfehlen:
From Middle High German ~enpfelhen~, ~enphelhen~, ~entfelhen~, ~intfelhen~, from Old High German ~intfelhan~, ~intfelahan~. Compound of ~emp-~ + the archaic root ~felhan~:

- ~felhan~: From Proto-Germanic *~felhaną~, of uncertain PIE origin. Two competing theories: if the primary sense was "to go, enter," it may be related to *~fulgāną~ ("to follow"); if "to conceal" was primary, it may connect to a PIE root *~pel-~ ("to cover, wrap"). The Old High German verb ~felhan~ meant "to preserve, entrust, confide in, surrender."
- ~emp-~: A variant of ~ent-~, from MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). The form ~emp-~ (rather than ~ent-~) arose through assimilation before the labial consonants ~f-~ and ~pf-~, the same process that gives ~empfinden~ rather than *~entfinden~.

A critical false friend lurks here: ~empfehlen~ ("to recommend") is etymologically related to ~befehlen~ ("to command, order") — both descend from OHG ~felhan~ — but is ~not~ related to ~fehlen~ ("to be missing, to lack"), which has a different and separately borrowed history (from Old French ~faillir~). The prefix ~emp-~ shifts the archaic sense of "entrusting" into the modern sense of putting someone or something forward as worthy of confidence. Cognate with Dutch ~bevelen~ (via ~befehlen~).
en.empfinden:
From Middle High German ~enpfinden~, ~entfinden~, from Old High German ~intfindan~, equivalent to ~emp-~ (variant of ~ent-~) + ~finden~:

- ~finden~: From MHG ~vinden~, from OHG ~findan~, from Proto-Germanic *~finþaną~, from PIE *~pent-~ ("to tread, walk, find the way").
- ~emp-~: A variant of ~ent-~, from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). Before labial consonants (~f~, ~pf~), the prefix ~ent-~ assimilates to ~emp-~: compare ~empfangen~ (from ~ent-~ + ~fangen~), ~empfehlen~ (from ~ent-~ + ~fehlen/befehlen~).

~Empfinden~ means "to feel, to sense, to perceive through the sense organs" — a class IIIa strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~empfinden~, ~empfand~, ~empfunden~. The semantic logic of the compound is beautiful: ~ent-~ / ~emp-~ in its original sense of "facing, encountering" + ~finden~ ("to find") yields "to find oneself facing something, to encounter a sensation." The word occupies a specific register between physical sensation (~er empfand die Kälte am ganzen Körper~, "he felt the cold all over his body") and emotional perception (~Mitleid empfinden~, "to feel compassion"). The derived noun ~Empfindung~ ("sensation, feeling") and the adjective ~empfindlich~ ("sensitive, touchy") extend this perceptual focus. The adjective ~empfindsam~ ("sentimental") was coined by G. E. Lessing in 1768 as a calque of English ~sentimental~, itself newly minted by Laurence Sterne.
en.enden:
From Middle High German ~enden~, from Old High German ~entōn~ ("to end, to complete, to die"), attested since the 8th century. A denominative verb derived from the noun ~Ende~:

~Ende~ traces to OHG ~enti~, from Proto-West Germanic *~andī~, from Proto-Germanic *~andijaz~, from PIE *~h₂entíos~ ("front, forehead") — itself from *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front").

The semantic journey from "forehead" to "end" is one of the more surprising in Germanic etymology. The PIE root *~h₂ent-~ referred to the front of the head — the face turned outward, the foremost surface of a thing. In Proto-Germanic, this gave *~andijaz~, which came to mean the farthest extent of something — the outermost point, the frontier, the edge. As the word entered OHG it had already shifted from "front" to "extremity," and from "extremity" to "terminus": the end is the point at which a thing faces outward no further. German ~Ende~ and English ~end~ are exact cognates, both descending from this root. The same PIE root *~h₂ent-~ also appears in the German prefix ~ent-~ and in Latin ~ante~ ("before") — a reminder that "front" and "before" are closely allied concepts. The verb ~enden~ is a simple denominative: to end is to bring something to its ~Ende~. Its OHG antecedent ~entōn~ already carried the sense of dying — reaching the ultimate end — a usage preserved in literary German to this day.
en.engagieren: Borrowed from French ~engager~ ("to pledge, to commit, to hire"), with the verb ending replaced by the German ~-ieren~ suffix. French ~engager~ derives from Old French ~engagier~ ("to pawn, to pledge"), from ~en-~ ("in") + ~gage~ ("pledge"), from Frankish *~wadja~ ("pledge"), from Proto-Germanic *~wadjō~ / *~wadją~ ("pledge, guarantee"), from PIE *~wedʰ-~ ("to guarantee, to pledge"). The etymology is thus circular: a Germanic word (*~wadja~) traveled into Old French via the Frankish conquerors of Gaul, evolved through centuries of French phonology into ~gage~ and then ~engager~, and was reborrowed back into German as ~engagieren~ in the 17th century — unrecognizable to German speakers as their own ancestor. The Proto-Germanic root also gives Gothic ~wadi~ ("pledge"), Old English ~wedd~ ("pledge, vow" — whence English ~wed~, originally "to pledge"), and Old High German ~wetti~ ("collateral, security agreement" — whence modern ~Wette~, "bet"). So ~engagieren~ and ~Wette~ are distant cousins reunited after a thousand-year detour through France. In modern German, ~engagieren~ means "to hire (an artist)" and, reflexively, ~sich engagieren~ means "to get involved, to commit oneself (to a cause)" — ~sich politisch engagieren~ ("to be politically active"). The derived noun ~Engagement~ retains its French pronunciation. Cognate with English ~engage~, Portuguese ~engajar~, Romanian ~angaja~.
en.entdecken:
From Middle High German ~endecken~, from Old High German ~intthecken~, ~inthecken~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~decken~:

- ~decken~: From MHG ~decken~, from OHG ~decchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þakkjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þakjaną~, from Proto-Germanic *~þaką~ ("roof, cover"), from PIE *~(s)teg-~ ("to cover"). Cognate with West Frisian ~dekke~, Dutch ~dekken~, English ~thatch~, Swedish ~täcka~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The literal meaning of ~entdecken~ is "to un-cover, to remove a covering" — the ~ent-~ prefix reverses the action of ~decken~ ("to cover"). From this concrete sense, the verb generalized to "to discover," the same metaphor underlying English ~uncover~ ("to discover") and French ~découvrir~ ("to discover," from ~couvrir~, "to cover"). English ~discover~ itself follows the same pattern via Latin ~discooperiō~. The shared PIE root *~(s)teg-~ ("to cover") also shows up in English ~stegosaurus~ (the "roofed lizard") and Latin ~tēctum~ ("roof"). Cognate with Dutch ~ontdekken~, Danish ~opdage~ (different prefix, same root verb).
en.entfallen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~fallen~:

- ~fallen~: From MHG ~vallen~, from OHG ~fallan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fallan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallaną~ ("to fall"), from PIE *~pōl-~ (possibly from a compound of *~h₂epo~ "off, away" + *~h₃elh₁-~ "to fall"). Cognate with English ~fall~, Dutch ~vallen~, Danish ~falde~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~and-~, from Proto-Germanic *~anda-~ ("against, away"), from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). As a prefix, ~ent-~ typically conveys removal, reversal, or separation.

The three modern meanings elegantly reflect the prefix's separative force applied to falling: "to drop away" (something falls from one's hands), "to lapse or be cancelled" (something falls away from the schedule), and "to slip one's memory" (something falls away from one's mind). The last sense offers a vivid metaphor: a forgotten thing has literally "fallen away from" the speaker.
en.entfalten:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~falten~:

- ~falten~: From MHG ~valden~, ~valten~, from OHG ~faldan~, ~faltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~falþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~falþaną~, from pre-Germanic *~polt-~, from PIE *~pel-~ ("to fold"). The irregular forms with ~-t-~ arose through early levelling of ~Grammatischer Wechsel~ (Verner's law alternation) but remained rare in both Old and Middle High German. They likely established themselves by analogy with the several German verbs in ~-alten~ (~halten~, ~schalten~, ~spalten~, ~walten~), as no verbs in ~-alden~ survived. Cognate with English ~fold~, Dutch ~vouwen~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~andi-~ ("away, against"), from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). Here the prefix reverses the base action: unfolding what was folded.

The literal sense is "to un-fold," and the semantic extension to "to develop, to unfurl, to deploy" is natural and vivid. One can ~ein Tuch entfalten~ ("unfold a cloth"), ~Aktivitäten entfalten~ ("develop activities"), or ~seine Persönlichkeit entfalten~ ("unfold one's personality"). The PIE root *~pel-~ also gives Latin ~-plex~ (as in ~duplex~, ~complex~), Greek ~-plasios~ ("-fold"), and English ~-ply~ (as in ~multiply~), making ~entfalten~ a distant relative of ~complicated~ (literally "folded together") and ~simple~ (literally "one-fold"). The English cognate ~fold~ descends from the same Proto-Germanic root but via Old English ~fealdan~, which preserved the original ~-d-~ that German analogically replaced with ~-t-~.
en.entfernen:
From Middle High German ~entverren~, analyzable as ~ent-~ + ~fern~ + ~-en~ (deadjectival verb suffix). Compound of ~ent-~ + ~fern~:

- ~fern~: From MHG ~verren~, from OHG ~ferrana~, from Proto-Germanic *~ferrai~ ("far, distant"), from Proto-Indo-European *~per-~ (locative form *~per-nóy~, "to cross, pass"). The same PIE root *~per-~ also underlies English ~far~, and more distantly Latin ~per-~ and Greek ~peri-~. Cognate with English ~far~, Dutch ~ver~, Danish ~fjern~, Icelandic ~fjær~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The verb literally means "to make distant, to remove into the distance" — the ~ent-~ prefix conveys the movement away, while ~fern~ supplies the notion of distance. The reflexive ~sich entfernen~ ("to move away, leave") is extremely common in modern German, but the transitive form ("to remove something") is equally current. The spatial metaphor — distance as something actively created — is preserved in every use of the word. Cognate with Dutch ~verwijderen~ (the functional equivalent).
en.entgehen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gān~, ~gēn~, from OHG ~gān~, ~gēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gān~, from Proto-Germanic *~gāną~, from PIE *~ǵʰeh₁-~ ("to leave"). Cognate with English ~go~, Dutch ~gaan~, Swedish ~gå~.
- ~ent-~: From PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). As a prefix, it means "away, un-, de-."

~Entgehen~ means "to escape, to avoid, to elude" — literally "to go away from." The dative construction ~jemandem entgehen~ ("to escape someone's notice") is particularly common: ~Diese Tatsache ist mir entgangen~ ("This fact escaped my notice"). The spelling with ~-h-~ in modern German ~gehen~ is a purely orthographic addition modeled on ~sehen~ ("to see"), where the ~h~ was originally etymological. In Old High German, the verb was simply ~gān~ — no ~h~ at all.
en.enthalten:
From Middle High German ~enthalten~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldaną~. Cognate with English ~hold~, Dutch ~houden~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The compound was influenced by ~innehalten~ and ~einhalten~ (literally "to hold inside"), which shaped both its "to contain" sense (compare ~Inhalt~, "contents," from the same root complex) and its reflexive "to abstain" sense (~sich enthalten~, "to refrain"). Cognate with Dutch ~onthouden~ ("to remember, withhold") and ~inhouden~ ("to contain"), Middle Low German ~enthōlden~.
en.entlassen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lāzen~, ~lān~, from OHG ~lāȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lātan~ ("to let, allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lētaną~ ("to leave behind, allow"), from PIE *~leh₁d-~ ("to let, to leave behind"). Cognate with Dutch ~laten~, English ~let~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, ~ant-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~ ("against, facing"), from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). As a prefix, it conveys reversal, removal, or release.

The compound means "to release, to discharge, to dismiss, to lay off." The ~ent-~ prefix adds the sense of releasing from an obligation or bond: a doctor ~entlässt~ a patient from the hospital, an employer ~entlässt~ an employee from their position. The noun ~Entlassung~ covers both the neutral sense of "discharge" and the harsher sense of "dismissal" or "layoff." The word carries considerable weight in German labor law, where ~Entlassung~ triggers formal legal protections (~Kündigungsschutz~, "protection against dismissal") that are among the strongest in Europe. The combination of ~ent-~ ("away from") and ~lassen~ ("to let") creates a precise image: to let someone go away from where they have been held.
en.entlasten:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~lasten~, a denominal derivation from ~Last~ ("load, burden"):

- ~Last~: From MHG ~last~, from OHG ~last~, ~hlast~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlasti~, from PIE *~kleh₂-~ ("to put, to lay out"). The initial OHG ~hl-~ cluster was simplified to ~l-~ during the Middle High German period. Cognate with English ~last~ (in the sense of a unit of weight or cargo) and ~ballast~ (which preserves the noun in a compound). Related to English ~laden~ ("loaded").
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, ~ant-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~ ("against, facing"), from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). As a prefix, it conveys removal or reversal.

The verb means "to relieve, to unburden, to exonerate" — to remove a ~Last~ ("burden") from someone. In legal contexts, ~entlasten~ means "to exonerate" (to remove the burden of guilt), and ~Entlastung~ is the legal term for "exoneration" or "acquittal." In accounting, ~Entlastung~ refers to the formal approval and discharge of a board's management ("ratification"). The ~ent-~ prefix performs its characteristic reversal: where ~belasten~ means "to burden" (using ~be-~ to place a load upon), ~entlasten~ removes the load. This elegant ~be-~/~ent-~ mirror pair — burdening and unburdening — is a productive pattern in German.
en.entnehmen:
From Middle High German ~entnëmen~, from Old High German ~intneman~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nëmen~, from OHG ~nëman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemaną~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
- ~ent-~: From PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). As a prefix, ~ent-~ conveys separation, removal, or extraction — taking something away from where it was.

~Entnehmen~ means "to take out, to remove, to extract" in formal registers — ~Bitte Karte entnehmen~ ("Please remove card") is the phrase every German ATM user knows by heart. Beyond the physical sense, ~entnehmen~ also means "to learn, to conclude, to gather" from a source — ~Ihrem Brief entnehme ich, dass...~ ("From your letter I gather that..."). This abstract extension treats information as something physically extracted from a container (the letter, the document, the silence). The derived noun ~Entnahme~ means "removal, withdrawal, extraction" — ~Blutentnahme~ ("blood draw") and ~Probeentnahme~ ("sample extraction") are standard medical and scientific terms. The ~ent-~ prefix's separative force makes ~entnehmen~ more formal and precise than simple ~herausnehmen~ ("to take out"), lending it a clinical or bureaucratic register. Cognate with Dutch ~ontnemen~ ("to deprive of, to take away").
en.entscheiden:
From Middle High German ~entscheiden~, from Old High German ~intskeiden~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~scheiden~:

- ~scheiden~: From MHG ~scheiden~, from OHG ~sceidan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaiþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaiþaną~, from PIE *~skey-~ ("to split, dissect"). Cognate with Dutch ~scheiden~, West Frisian ~skiede~, English ~shed~, Irish ~scian~ ("knife"), Ancient Greek ~skhízō~ ("to split"), and Sanskrit ~cyati~ ("he cuts off").
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The semantic development of ~entscheiden~ — from "to cut apart, separate" to "to decide" — parallels Latin ~dēcīdere~ (literally "to cut off," from ~caedere~, "to cut"), which also yielded the English word ~decide~. Two languages, one metaphor: a decision is a cut that divides options apart. Cognate with Dutch ~ontscheiden~, Low German ~entscheden~.
en.entschließen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~schließen~:

- ~schließen~: From MHG ~slieȝen~, from OHG ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sleutaną~, *~slūtaną~ ("to lock, shut"). The PIE origin is uncertain; traditionally connected to *~(s)klewd-~ ("to lock"), from *~(s)kleh₂w-~ ("nail, hook, pin, key"), from *~(s)kel-~ ("to crook, bend"). Cognate with Old Saxon ~slūtan~, Dutch ~sluiten~, English ~slot~.
- ~ent-~: From PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix carries a sense of reversal, undoing, or decisive separation.

The literal sense is "to un-lock" — to open a decision that was previously closed. The reflexive ~sich entschließen~ means "to decide, to resolve, to make up one's mind," and the noun ~Entschluss~ ("decision, resolution") is the standard word for a firm resolve. The metaphor is striking: a decision is imagined as a lock being opened, releasing a course of action. The base verb ~schließen~ appears in an extraordinary range of compounds: ~beschließen~ ("to decide, to resolve"), ~abschließen~ ("to lock, to conclude"), ~einschließen~ ("to lock in, to include"), ~ausschließen~ ("to lock out, to exclude"). The English cognate ~slot~ — a narrow opening — preserves the physical sense of the lock mechanism. Latin ~clāvis~ ("key"), whence English ~clavicle~ and ~conclave~, may be a distant cousin if the traditional PIE etymology is correct.
en.entschuldigen:
From Middle High German ~entschuldigen~ ("to absolve, to exonerate"). Surface analysis: ~ent-~ + ~schuldig~ ("guilty") + ~-en~, literally "to de-guilt":

- ~schuldig~: From ~Schuld~ ("guilt, debt, fault"). ~Schuld~ from MHG ~schult~, from OHG ~sculd~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skuldi~, from Proto-Germanic *~skuldō~, from PIE *~skel-~ ("to owe, to be obliged"). Cognate with obsolete English ~shild~ ("debt, fault, guilt"), Dutch ~schuld~, and English ~shall~ (which originally expressed obligation: "I shall" = "I owe it").
