- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, ~ant-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~ ("against, facing"), from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). The prefix conveys removal or reversal.

The verb means "to excuse, to apologize" — literally "to un-guilt." The reflexive ~sich entschuldigen~ ("to excuse oneself, to apologize") is one of the most common polite expressions in German: ~Entschuldigung!~ ("Excuse me!"). The MHG meaning was stronger: "to absolve, to exonerate," a legal and religious term for removing guilt. Over the centuries, the meaning softened from juridical absolution to social courtesy. The connection between ~Schuld~ ("guilt") and ~Schuld~ ("debt") is not coincidental — they are the same word, reflecting the ancient equation of moral guilt with financial obligation. English ~shall~ descends from the same root, preserving the original sense of obligation, and Gothic ~skulan~ ("to owe, to be obligated") confirms the shared Germanic ancestry.
en.entsorgen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~sorgen~, a denominal derivation via the noun ~Sorge~ ("care, worry"):

- ~sorgen~: From MHG ~sorgen~, from OHG ~sorgēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~surgāną~, from PIE *~swergʰ-~ ("to watch over, to worry; to be ill, to suffer"). The noun ~Sorge~ ("care, worry, sorrow") is cognate with English ~sorrow~, Dutch ~zorg~, Danish ~sorg~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, ~ant-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~ ("against, facing"), from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). The prefix conveys removal.

The verb means "to dispose of (waste)" — literally "to de-worry," to remove the object of concern. The word is strikingly modern: although its MHG ancestor ~entsorgen~ originally meant "to free from worry," the old meaning fell out of use, and the modern waste-disposal sense emerged only in the 1980s, during the environmental movement. Some commentators have claimed ~entsorgen~ originated as Nazi-era bureaucratic euphemism, but the evidence points instead to its independent reinvention in postwar environmental discourse. The irony is rich: a word that once meant "to relieve anxiety" now refers to the very activity — waste management — that causes considerable anxiety in environmentally conscious Germany, a country that takes its ~Mülltrennung~ ("waste sorting") with legendary seriousness.
en.entspannen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~spannen~:

- ~spannen~: From MHG ~spannen~, from OHG ~spannan~, from Proto-Germanic *~spannaną~, originally a strong verb of the seventh class. The long ~-nn-~ comes from an earlier form *~spanwaną-~. Perhaps ultimately an ablaut form related to *~spinnaną~ ("to spin"), or alternatively from PIE *~spend-~ ("to stretch"), comparing Lithuanian ~spęsti~ ("to snare") and Latvian ~spanda~ ("strap"). Cognate with English ~span~ and ~spin~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~andi-~ ("away, against"), from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). Here the prefix acts as a reversal: undoing the tension.

The literal sense is "to un-tension," i.e., to release from a stretched or strained state, yielding "to relax, to ease." The word works both transitively (~einen Bogen entspannen~, "to unstring a bow") and reflexively (~sich entspannen~, "to relax"). The physical metaphor of releasing a taut bowstring or spring extends naturally to psychological relaxation. English ~span~ (a unit of measurement based on the stretched hand) is the closest cognate, preserving the root sense of stretching. The compound ~Entspannung~ ("relaxation, detente") gained particular political currency during the Cold War as the German equivalent of ~detente~ -- and indeed, French ~detente~ is itself a structural parallel, from ~des-~ ("un-") + ~tendre~ ("to stretch"), from Latin ~tendere~, making it a twin of ~entspannen~ built from completely different etymological material.
en.entsprechen:
From Middle High German ~entsprechen~ (implied), from Old High German ~intsprehhōn~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~sprechen~:

- ~sprechen~: From MHG ~sprëchen~, from OHG ~sprëhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekaną~, probably from PIE *~spregʰ-~ ("to crackle"). The semantic development from "to crackle, make noise" to "to speak" parallels the evolution seen in many languages where words for speech derive from words for sound. Cognate with Dutch ~spreken~, English ~speak~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The compound's semantic development — from "to speak in response to" to "to correspond to, match, comply with" — reflects a characteristic use of ~ent-~ to convey a reciprocal or countering relationship. ~Einer Sache entsprechen~ ("to comply with something") and ~dem Bild entsprechen~ ("to match the image") both capture this sense of meeting or answering to something. Cognate with Dutch ~overeenkomen~ (the functional equivalent, though formed differently).
en.entstehen:
From Middle High German ~entstēn~, ~entstān~, from Old High German ~intstantan~, ~intstantēn~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~stehen~:

- ~stehen~: From MHG ~stān~, ~stēn~, from OHG ~stān~, ~stēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stān~, from Proto-Germanic *~stāną~, from PIE *~steh₂-~ ("to stand").
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The compound's semantic development from "to stand away/forth" to "to come into being, arise" reflects how the ~ent-~ prefix here conveys emergence or inception rather than its more common sense of removal.
en.enttäuschen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~täuschen~ ("to deceive"). Coined in the 19th century as a German replacement for the French loanwords ~desabusiren~ (from French ~désabuser~) and ~detrompiren~ (from French ~détromper~):

- ~täuschen~: From MHG ~tiuschen~, derived from ~tūschen~ ("to exchange surreptitiously"), related to ~tauschen~ ("to trade, to exchange"). The semantic development was "to swap" > "to trick someone into a trade" > "to deceive" (DWDS).
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, ~ant-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~ ("against, facing"), from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). The prefix conveys reversal.

The verb means "to disappoint" — but its literal, etymological meaning is "to un-deceive," to tear away a deception and reveal the truth. This is the opposite of what "disappoint" feels like: one would expect "un-deceiving" to be positive (and in fact, the original 19th-century sense was neutral — simply "to free from an illusion"). Over time, however, the word shifted from describing the removal of delusion to describing the emotional pain that follows when reality falls short of expectation. The underlying ~täuschen~ ("to deceive") derives from ~tauschen~ ("to exchange"), with the semantic pathway "to exchange" > "to palm off, to trick into a swap" > "to deceive generally." This connection between trading and trickery is ancient and cross-linguistic: English ~trick~ may derive from Old French ~trichier~ ("to cheat"), and Latin ~dolus~ means both "cunning" and "fraud."
en.entwerfen:
From Middle High German ~entwerfen~. Compound of ~ent-~ + ~werfen~:

- ~werfen~: From MHG ~wërfen~, from OHG ~werfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~werpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~werpaną~, from PIE *~wergʷ-~ ("to bend, spin, warp"). The ~-p-~ in the Germanic form likely arose after resonants in words beginning with labial consonants. Cognate with English ~warp~ (originally "to throw"), Dutch ~werpen~, and via Greek ~rhembō~ ("to turn in circles"), the source of English ~rhombus~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~andi-~ ("away, against"), from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This inseparable prefix adds a sense of reversal, removal, or inception to the base verb.

The literal sense is "to throw out" or "to cast forth," and the semantic development to "to design, to draft, to sketch" reflects the idea of casting forth an idea into physical form. Cognate with Dutch ~ontwerpen~ ("to design"), which preserves the same prefix-root combination. The relationship between ~werfen~ ("to throw") and English ~warp~ illustrates a dramatic semantic divergence from a shared ancestor: German kept the concrete "throw" meaning, while English ~warp~ shifted from "to throw" to "to twist, to distort" (with the weaving sense of "warp threads" reflecting the PIE meaning "to bend"). The noun ~Entwurf~ ("design, draft, blueprint") is the corresponding deverbal noun, parallel to how English ~draft~ originally meant "something drawn or pulled."
en.entwickeln:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~wickeln~. Calque of French ~développer~.

- ~wickeln~: From MHG ~wickeln~ ("to wind, wrap around"), a denominative of ~wickel~, from OHG ~wickili~ ("fibres wound around the distaff"), from Proto-Germanic *~wikaną~ ("to bend"), from PIE *~weg-~ ("to weave"). Related to ~Wickel~ ("anything wrapped, especially a cloth around a poultice"). The OHG noun is a diminutive of ~wiohha~ ("bundle of fibres, wick"), cognate with Dutch ~wiek~, English ~wick~, and Latin ~velum~ ("cloth, veil").
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-Germanic *~and-~, from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, un-, or de-.

The semantic development from "to unwrap" to "to develop" parallels French ~développer~ (from ~des-~ + Old French ~voloper~, "to wrap"), which ~entwickeln~ was coined to translate. Both languages arrived at "develop" via the metaphor of unfolding something hidden.
en.entziehen:
Compound of ~ent-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhaną~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Cognate with Low German ~tehn~, Gothic ~tiuhan~, Old English ~teon~, and Old Norse ~toga~.
- ~ent-~: From MHG ~ent-~, from OHG ~int-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~and-~, from Proto-Germanic *~andi-~, from PIE *~h₂ent-~ ("face, forehead, front"). This prefix conveys separation, removal, or reversal.

The compound literally means "to pull away from," yielding the modern senses of "to withdraw, deprive, extract." The reflexive form ~sich entziehen~ means "to evade, to escape from," adding a layer of agency to the withdrawal. The PIE root *~dewk-~ is remarkably productive: it yielded Latin ~ducere~ ("to lead"), giving English ~duke~, ~conduct~, ~produce~, ~reduce~, ~educate~, and ~deduce~. English ~tie~ is also from the same root, via the idea of pulling tight. English ~tug~ and ~tow~ are further relatives. The German noun ~Entzug~ ("withdrawal") is widely known in the compound ~Entzugserscheinungen~ ("withdrawal symptoms"), one of those long German compound nouns that compresses into a single word what English needs a phrase to express. The obsolete English verb ~tee~ ("to draw, lead") was a direct cognate of ~ziehen~ that did not survive.
en.erarbeiten:
~er-~ + ~arbeiten~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~arbeiten~:

- ~arbeiten~: From MHG ~arbeiten~, from OHG ~arabeitōn~, ~arbeitōn~, from OHG ~arabeit~ ("labor, hardship"), from Proto-West Germanic *~arbaiþi~, from Proto-Germanic *~arbaidiz~ ("work, hardship"), possibly from PIE *~h₃erbʰ-~ ("to change status, to orphan").
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of achievement through effort or completion of a process.

~Erarbeiten~ means "to achieve through work, to develop through effort, to work out." The ~er-~ prefix transforms the simple act of working into the completed result of that work — one does not merely ~arbeitet~, one ~erarbeitet~ a result, a plan, a competence. The underlying etymological image is almost poignant: if ~arbeiten~ indeed descends from a root meaning "orphan, hardship" (as suggested by the connection to PIE *~h₃erbʰ-~ and Gothic ~arbaiþs~, "toil, distress"), then ~erarbeiten~ captures the full arc of labor as suffering that yields an outcome. The reflexive ~sich etwas erarbeiten~ ("to earn something through hard work") emphasizes the personal investment required. There is no shortcut implied — unlike ~verdienen~ ("to earn," which can be passive), ~erarbeiten~ insists on active, sustained effort.
en.erbauen:
From Middle High German ~erbouwen~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~bauen~:

- ~bauen~: From MHG ~buwen~, from OHG ~buan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~buwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~buwaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~bhuH-~ ("to be, become, grow, dwell"). The semantic journey from "to dwell" to "to build" reflects the Germanic settlers' deepening attachment to fixed homesteads. Cognate with English ~build~ (via a different branch), Dutch ~bouwen~, and English ~be~, ~been~ (via the same PIE root).
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of achievement or completion to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The ~er-~ prefix intensifies the base verb, lending ~erbauen~ the elevated sense of "to construct, to edify." In modern German, the reflexive ~sich erbauen~ means "to be edified" in a spiritual or emotional sense, a meaning that grew from the literal act of building churches and monuments.
en.erbringen:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~bringen~:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringaną~ ("to bring"), probably from a Pre-Germanic compound *~bʰrenk̂-~, combining PIE *~bʰer-~ ("to carry") and *~h₁nek̂-~ ("to bring, reach"). Cognate with English ~bring~, Dutch ~brengen~, Gothic ~briggan~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of achievement or completion to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The ~er-~ prefix transforms "to bring" into "to bring about, to produce, to yield" -- conveying the successful result of bringing. It appears most often in formal or bureaucratic contexts: ~einen Beweis erbringen~ ("to furnish proof"), ~eine Leistung erbringen~ ("to render a service"), ~ein Opfer erbringen~ ("to make a sacrifice"). The verb ~bringen~ itself is unusual among Germanic verbs in having a mixed weak-and-strong past tense (~brachte~, ~gebracht~), a pattern unique in Proto-Germanic.
en.erfahren:
From Middle High German ~ervarn~, from Old High German ~irfaran~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀfaran~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~fahren~:

- ~fahren~: From MHG ~varn~, ~varen~, from OHG ~faran~, from Proto-West Germanic *~faran~, from Proto-Germanic *~faraną~, from PIE *~per-~ ("going, passage"). A class VI strong verb.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The completive ~er-~ prefix transforms the base sense of "to travel" into "to find out, learn through experience" — one discovers by traveling through, by undergoing a journey. This semantic link between physical travel and the acquisition of knowledge is beautifully preserved in the noun ~Erfahrung~ ("experience"), literally "a traveling through." Cognate with Old English ~āfaran~ (whence obsolete English ~afare~, "to depart, set out").
en.erfassen:
From ~er-~ + ~fassen~:

- ~fassen~: From MHG ~vazzen~, from OHG ~fazzōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fatōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fatōną~ ("to fetch, hold"), ultimately cognate with English ~fetch~. The connection is one of the more surprising Germanic pairs: both descend from a root meaning "to get, obtain."
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This completive prefix transforms the act of grasping into a fully achieved comprehension or registration. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The compound means "to grasp, to seize, to comprehend, to record (data)" — the ~er-~ prefix driving the simple act of grabbing through to completion: one doesn't merely touch but fully takes hold, in the physical sense (~einen Dieb erfassen~, "to apprehend a thief") and the cognitive sense (~eine Idee erfassen~, "to grasp an idea"). In modern administrative and statistical language, ~erfassen~ has become the standard term for recording and registering data — "to capture" in the technical sense, as in ~Daten erfassen~. The related ~Fassung~ ("composure; version") and ~unfassbar~ ("incomprehensible," literally "ungraspable") show the root's wide idiomatic reach. Cognate with Dutch ~vatten~.
en.erfinden:
From Middle High German ~ervinden~, from Old High German ~irfindan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀfinþan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~finden~:

- ~finden~: From MHG ~vinden~, from OHG ~findan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~finþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~finþaną~, from PIE *~pent-~ ("to go, pass; path, bridge"). Cognate with English ~find~, Dutch ~vinden~, Danish ~finde~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of achievement or completion to the base verb.

The semantic journey from "to find completely" to "to invent" is a remarkable one: ~erfinden~ moved from the idea of thorough discovery to the creation of something entirely new. The PIE root *~pent-~ originally meant "to tread a path," so ~finden~ traces back to the notion of coming upon something while walking, and ~erfinden~ extends that to arriving at something that did not exist before. The noun ~Erfindung~ ("invention") and ~Erfinder~ ("inventor") are among the most productive derivatives in modern German.
en.erfolgen:
From Middle High German ~erfolgen~, from Old High German ~irfolgēn~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~folgen~:

- ~folgen~: From MHG ~volgen~, from OHG ~folgēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~folgēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fulgāną~. Cognate with Dutch ~volgen~, Low German ~folgen~, English ~follow~, Danish ~følge~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The compound's primary modern sense — "to occur, to result, to ensue" — reflects the completive force of ~er-~: an outcome that follows through to its conclusion. The cognate relationship between ~folgen~ and English ~follow~ is not immediately obvious in Modern German spelling, but the OHG form ~folgēn~ makes the shared Proto-Germanic origin transparent. Cognate with Dutch ~evolgen~ (archaic).
en.erfordern:
From Middle High German ~ervordern~, from Old High German ~irfordarōn~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~fordern~:

- ~fordern~: From MHG ~vordern~, from OHG ~fordarōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~furþrōną~, from Proto-Germanic *~furþrōną~, from *~furþraz~ ("further, forward"), from PIE *~per-~ ("to cross, go through").
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix emphasizes the full extent of what is demanded.

The distinction between ~fordern~ ("to demand") and ~erfordern~ ("to require") is subtle but important: ~fordern~ implies an agent making a demand (~der Chef fordert Pünktlichkeit~ — "the boss demands punctuality"), while ~erfordern~ implies an impersonal, situational necessity (~die Situation erfordert Geduld~ — "the situation requires patience"). The ~er-~ prefix strips away the personal element, transforming a demand into a requirement — from something one person asks of another to something a situation asks of everyone. Cognate with Dutch ~vereisen~ (a different compound with the same meaning).
en.erforschen:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~forschen~:

- ~forschen~: From MHG ~vorschen~, from OHG ~forscōn~ ("to ask, to inquire, to investigate"), from Proto-West Germanic *~fraskōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fraskōną~, likely related to *~frehnaną~ ("to ask"), from PIE *~preḱ-~ ("to ask, to request"). Cognate with Latin ~poscō~ ("I demand"), ~precor~ ("I pray"), Sanskrit ~pr̥ccháti~ ("he asks"), and English ~pray~ (via Old French ~preier~, from Latin ~precārī~).
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds the sense of thorough completion — researching something to the end.

~Erforschen~ means "to explore, to investigate, to research thoroughly" — ~den Weltraum erforschen~ ("to explore outer space"), ~die Ursachen erforschen~ ("to investigate the causes"). The noun ~Forscher~ ("researcher, explorer") and ~Forschung~ ("research") are central to German academic and scientific vocabulary. The PIE root *~preḱ-~ ("to ask") connects scientific inquiry with prayer and supplication in a profound etymological circle: to research and to pray both begin with asking. The semantic shift from "to ask questions" to "to conduct systematic investigation" occurred within German itself during the medieval period, as ~forschen~ moved from casual inquiry to the structured questioning that defines the scientific method.
en.erfreuen:
From Middle High German ~ervröuwen~, ~ervröwen~, from Old High German ~irfrewen~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~freuen~:

- ~freuen~: From MHG ~vröuwen~, ~vreuwen~, from OHG ~frewen~, ~frouwen~, derived from OHG ~frō~, ~frao~ ("glad"). The adjective ~froh~ descends from Proto-Germanic *~frawaz~ ("quick, glad"). Equivalent to ~froh~ + ~-en~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of achievement or completion to the base verb.

The ~er-~ prefix intensifies ~freuen~ ("to please, to be glad") into a sense of causing or experiencing full delight. While ~sich freuen~ means "to be happy," ~erfreuen~ means "to delight" someone else, or in its reflexive form ~sich erfreuen~ with the genitive, "to enjoy" something. English ~frolic~ is a distant cousin, borrowed from Dutch ~vrolijk~ ("merry"), which shares the same Proto-Germanic root *~frawaz~. The Old High German vowel ~ö~/~öu~ in ~vröuwen~ reflects the Germanic umlaut triggered by the ~-j-~ in the verb derivation, a phonological process that left its fingerprints all over German's vowel system.
en.erfüllen:
From Middle High German ~ervüllen~, from Old High German ~irfullen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀfullijan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~füllen~:

- ~füllen~: From MHG ~vüllen~, from OHG ~fullen~, from Proto-Germanic *~fullijaną~, a denominative of *~fullaz~ ("full"), from PIE *~pleh₁-~ ("to fill"). Cognate with Dutch ~vullen~, English ~fill~, Danish ~fylde~, Swedish ~fylla~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or achievement. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The ~er-~ prefix gives ~erfüllen~ its characteristic sense of complete, achieving fulfillment — not merely filling, but filling to completion, satisfying entirely. A wish, a promise, a quota: all ~erfüllt~ when fully accomplished. The PIE root *~pleh₁-~ is also the ancestor of English ~full~, Latin ~plēnus~ ("full"), and Greek ~plḗrēs~ ("full"). Cognate with Dutch ~vervullen~.
en.ergeben:
From Middle High German ~ergeben~, from Old High German ~irgeban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀgeban~, from Proto-Germanic *~uzgebaną~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gëben~, from OHG ~gëban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebaną~, from PIE *~gʰebʰ-~ ("to give"). Cognate with Dutch ~geven~, English ~give~, Swedish ~giva~/~ge~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The semantic range of ~ergeben~ is notably wide: "to yield" (literally, to give out completely), "to result in" (the calculation yields a result), and — as the reflexive ~sich ergeben~ — "to surrender" or "to turn out to be." The sense of ~ergeben~ as an adjective ("devoted, submissive") derives from the same completive force: one who has wholly given themselves over. Cognate with obsolete English ~agive~, Dutch ~overgeven~.
en.ergreifen:
From Middle High German ~ergreifen~, from Old High German ~irgreifōn~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~greifen~:

- ~greifen~: From MHG ~grīfen~, from OHG ~grīfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grīpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grīpaną~, from PIE *~gʰreyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). Cognate with English ~grip~, ~gripe~, Dutch ~grijpen~, Swedish ~gripa~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). Adds a sense of successfully completing the action.

The ~er-~ prefix transforms "to grasp" into "to seize successfully, to take hold of" -- implying a completed act of grasping. The verb has developed rich figurative meanings: ~Maßnahmen ergreifen~ ("to take measures"), ~die Flucht ergreifen~ ("to take flight"), ~einen Beruf ergreifen~ ("to take up a profession"), and ~ergriffen sein~ ("to be deeply moved"). This last sense -- emotional seizing -- shows how physical grasping became a metaphor for being seized by emotion. The English cognate is the humble ~grip~, which shares the same Proto-Germanic ancestor *~grīpaną~. The word ~gripe~ (originally "to grasp," now "to complain") is another English sibling, its semantic shift from clutching to complaining perhaps reflecting the clenched frustration of one who gripes.
en.ergänzen:
From ~er-~ + ~ganz~ with the verbal suffix ~-en~, producing the original form *~ergänzen~ ("to make whole, to complete, to supplement"):

- ~ganz~: From MHG ~ganz~, from OHG ~ganz~ ("whole, sound, healthy, complete"), from Proto-West Germanic *~gant~ ("whole, sound"). The ultimate PIE source is uncertain; the form is attested only in West Germanic. Cognate with Dutch ~gans~ (archaic "whole"), Low German ~ganz~.
- ~er-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This inseparable prefix adds a sense of achievement or completion to a derived verb.

The ~-ä-~ in modern ~ergänzen~ is the result of umlaut: the verb was originally denominalized as *~gänzen~ (with fronted vowel from adjectival inflection), then prefixed with ~er-~. The completive prefix is semantically apt — ~ergänzen~ does not merely add, but adds in a way that makes something whole. The noun ~Ergänzung~ ("supplement, complement") is a standard term in mathematics and linguistics.
en.erhalten:
From Middle High German ~erhalten~, from Old High German ~irhaltan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldaną~. Cognate with English ~hold~, Dutch ~houden~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The semantic development from "to hold (up/out)" to "to receive" and "to preserve" reflects the completive force of the ~er-~ prefix. Cognate with Dutch ~erhouden~ (archaic).
en.erheben:
From Middle High German ~erheben~, from Old High German ~irheffen~, ~irheven~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀhabbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~uzhabjaną~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~heben~. Cognate with Middle Dutch ~erheffen~ (whence Dutch ~verheffen~), Old Saxon ~āhebbian~, Old English ~āhebban~, Gothic ~ushafjan~.

- ~heben~: From MHG ~heben~, ~heven~, from OHG ~heffen~, ~heven~, from Proto-West Germanic *~habbjan~, from Proto-Indo-European *~keh₂p-~ ("to seize, take hold of"). A class VI strong verb; the past tense ~hob~ and participle ~gehoben~ descend from the strong ablaut paradigm. Cognate with English ~heave~, Dutch ~heffen~, Danish ~hæve~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). The completive prefix reinforces the inherent upward thrust of ~heben~. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The curious shift from "to seize" (PIE) to "to lift" (Germanic) reflects the same conceptual move as in English ~heave~ — the act of taking hold of something is inseparable from lifting it. ~Erheben~ takes this a stage further: the ~er-~ prefix pushes ~heben~ to completion, yielding meanings that span the physical ("to raise") and the institutional ("to levy a tax," "to lodge a complaint," "to elevate to nobility"). A tax is ~erhoben~; a glass is ~erhoben~ in a toast; a voice is ~erhoben~ in protest — all are acts of lifting. The derived noun ~Erhebung~ covers both a geographic rise (an elevation in a landscape) and a political one (an insurrection, literally a "raising up"). A notable cognate surprise: English ~heave~ and German ~heben~ diverge from the same Proto-West Germanic root, making a sailor heaving a rope the etymological twin of a German speaker raising their glass. Cognate with Dutch ~verheffen~, English ~heave~.
en.erholen:
From Middle High German ~erholn~, from Old High German ~irholōn~, ~irhalōn~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~holen~:

- ~holen~: From MHG ~holen~, from OHG ~holōn~ ("to fetch, get"), from Proto-West Germanic *~holōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~halōną~ ("to call, fetch, summon"), from PIE *~kelh₁-~ ("to call, summon").
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix carries a restorative sense — fetching oneself back from a depleted state.

~Sich erholen~ means "to recover, to recuperate" — almost exclusively used as a reflexive verb. One ~erholt sich~ from illness, exhaustion, a shock, or a financial setback. The literal image is fetching oneself back — summoning one's strength out of depletion and back into wholeness. The noun ~Erholung~ ("rest, recreation, recovery") is the standard word for leisure in the restorative sense: ~Erholungsgebiet~ ("recreation area"), ~Erholungsurlaub~ ("recuperative vacation"). The progression from "to fetch" (~holen~) to "to fetch back" (~erholen~) to "to recover oneself" (~sich erholen~) is a beautiful example of how German prefixes can redirect a concrete physical verb toward an abstract psychological process.
en.erhöhen:
From Middle High German ~erhœhen~, ~erhœgen~, from Old High German ~irhōhen~, ~irhohen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀhauhijan~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~hoch~ + ~-en~:

- ~hoch~: From MHG ~hōch~, from OHG ~hōh~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hauh~, from Proto-Germanic *~hauhaz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~kewk-~, a suffixed form of *~kew-~ ("to swell, be strong, be vigorous"). Cognate with Dutch ~hoog~, English ~high~, Swedish ~hög~, Danish ~høj~.
- ~er-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix here reinforces the inherent upward directionality of the root.

The irregular stem alternation in modern ~hoch~ vs. inflected ~hoh-~ (as in ~höher~, ~Hoheit~) reflects a Middle High German sound change: OHG ~-h-~ became /x/ in coda position but /h/ elsewhere, and most such alternations were later leveled — except in ~hoch~, which Standard German has preserved in isolation. Cognate with Old Dutch ~irhōen~, Middle Low German ~erhȫgen~.
en.erinnern:
From Middle High German ~erinnern~, ~erinneren~, from Old High German ~innarōn~ ("to make someone perceive inwardly") with the later addition of the completive ~er-~ prefix. Compound of ~er-~ + ~innern~, from ~inner~ ("inner"):

- ~innern~: Denominative of ~inner~ ("inner"), from OHG ~innar~, from Proto-Germanic *~inn-~, from PIE *~h₁en~ ("in"). The ~-ar~ suffix is a comparative formation meaning "more inward."
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or achievement. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The semantic structure is thus "to bring something fully inward (into the mind)" — the ~er-~ prefix marking completion of the act of interiorizing. The reflexive ~sich erinnern an~ ("to remember") became the dominant construction in Early New High German; the transitive non-reflexive ~jemanden an etwas erinnern~ ("to remind someone of something") preserves the older causative sense of "to make someone bring inward." English ~remind~ and ~remember~ cover the same semantic territory through entirely different routes. Cognate with Dutch ~herinneren~ (with the parallel ~her-~ prefix), English ~inner~.
en.erkennen:
From Middle High German ~erkennen~, from Old High German ~irkennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀkannijan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~kennen~:

- ~kennen~: From MHG ~kennen~, from OHG ~kennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kannijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~kannijaną~, a causative of *~kunnaną~ with the suffix *~-janą~. *~kunnaną~ derives from PIE *~ǵn̥néh₃ti~, from *~ǵneh₃-~ ("to know").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The completive force of ~er-~ transforms the simple acquaintance of ~kennen~ ("to know, be acquainted with") into the achievement of recognition: ~erkennen~ is "to come to know, to recognize." This distinction — between ongoing familiarity and the moment of recognition — has legal weight in German: ~erkennen~ is the verb used when courts and authorities make formal determinations (~ein Urteil erkennen~). Cognate with Dutch ~erkennen~.
en.erklären:
From Middle High German ~erklæren~, from ~er-~ + ~klären~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~klären~:

- ~klären~: From MHG ~klæren~, a denominative verb derived from the adjective ~klar~ ("clear"). ~klar~ descends from MHG ~klār~, ~clār~, borrowed from Middle Low German ~klār~ or Middle Dutch ~claer~ and reinforced by Old French ~cler~, both from Latin ~clārus~ ("bright, clear, famous"). Latin ~clārus~ is related to ~clāmāre~ ("to call out") and ultimately from PIE *~kelh₁-~ ("to call, shout").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The semantic development from "to make clear" (a liquid, a room) to "to explain" (a concept) follows the same metaphor in many languages — English ~clarify~ and ~explain~ both draw on light and space as cognitive metaphors. Cognate with Dutch ~verklaren~, English ~clarify~ (from the same Latin root).
en.erkranken:
A denominative verb from ~krank~ ("sick") with the prefix ~er-~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~krank~ + ~-en~:

- ~krank~: From MHG ~krank~, from OHG *~krank~, from Proto-West Germanic *~krank~, from Proto-Germanic *~krangaz~, *~krankaz~ ("crooked, weak"). Cognate with English ~crank~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix marks the onset or inception of a state -- becoming sick rather than merely being sick.

The semantic shift from "crooked, bent" to "sick" parallels the metaphor found in many languages of illness as distortion or weakness. English ~crank~ preserves the original spatial meaning of something bent or twisted, while German generalized the sense to physical debility. The ~er-~ prefix here functions inceptively: ~krank sein~ is "to be sick," but ~erkranken~ is "to fall ill" -- the transition into sickness.
en.erkunden:
From ~er-~ + ~kunden~, a denominative verb from ~kund~ ("known, manifest"). Compound of:

- ~kund~: From MHG ~kunt~, from OHG ~kund~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kunþ~, from Proto-Germanic *~kunþaz~, originally the past participle of *~kunnaną~ ("to know"), from PIE *~ǵneh₃-~ ("to know"). Cognate with English ~couth~ (archaic past participle of "can/know"), and related to English ~can~, ~know~, ~ken~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix adds a sense of thorough investigation — making something ~fully~ known.

~erkunden~ means "to explore, to reconnoiter, to investigate" — to make the unknown ~known~. The military sense of reconnaissance (~Erkundung~) is prominent, but the verb also applies to exploring a city or investigating a matter. The same PIE root *~ǵneh₃-~ is one of the most productive in Indo-European, yielding Latin ~gnōscere~ / ~cognōscere~ (whence English ~cognition~, ~recognize~), Greek ~gignṓskein~ (whence ~gnosis~, ~diagnosis~), and Sanskrit ~jñā-~ (whence ~jñāna~, "knowledge"). The English word ~uncouth~ preserves the negative form of this same participle — literally "unknown," hence "unfamiliar, strange."
en.erlangen:
From Middle High German ~erlangen~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~langen~:

- ~langen~: From MHG ~langen~, from OHG ~langēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~langōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~langōną~. Often connected to *~langaz~ ("long"), with semantic development from "long length" to "reach, extend" to "wish to reach." Cognate with English ~long~ (in the archaic sense "to long for, to desire").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of achievement or completion to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The ~er-~ prefix sharpens the reaching sense of ~langen~ into one of successful attainment: "to reach for" becomes "to achieve, obtain, gain." While ~langen~ in modern German is informal and chiefly southern (~mir langt's~ = "I've had enough"), ~erlangen~ remains firmly in the formal register -- one ~erlangt~ a degree, citizenship, or recognition. Not to be confused with the Bavarian city of ~Erlangen~, whose name derives from a river name rather than from this verb.
en.erlassen:
From Middle High German ~erlāzen~, from Old High German ~irlāzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uzlātan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lāzen~, ~lān~, from OHG ~lāzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lātan~ ("to let, allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lētaną~ ("to leave behind, allow"), from Proto-Indo-European *~leh1d-~ ("to let, leave behind"). Cognate with English ~let~, Dutch ~laten~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of completion or result.

The compound literally means "to let out" or "to release completely." Its modern meanings span from "to issue, decree" (a law is "released" into the world) to "to remit, waive" (a debt is "let go of"). This semantic duality -- both promulgating and pardoning -- springs from the same root idea of releasing something from one's control.
en.erlauben:
From Middle High German ~erlouben~, from Old High German ~irlouben~, ~arlouben~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀlaubijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~uzlaubijaną~. Compound of ~er-~ + the extinct root *~laubijaną~:

- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~, from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of achievement or completion — "allowing fully."
- *~laubijaną~: From Proto-Germanic *~laubijaną~ ("to allow, approve of, commend"), from *~laubō~ ("permission"). From PIE *~lewbʰ-~ ("to desire, love, praise, care for").

The root *~laubijaną~ is extinct as an independent verb in modern German — it survives only inside compounds like ~erlauben~ and ~glauben~ ("to believe"). The believe connection is one of the most surprising in the Germanic lexicon: ~glauben~ descends from *~galaubijaną~ ("to praise, commend, allow, believe"), which is the same *~laubijaną~ root with the collective prefix *~ga-~. The semantic path runs from "to care for, value" → "to hold dear" → "to trust" → "to believe." English ~believe~ follows the same route (from Old English ~ġelīefan~, from *~galaubijaną~). German ~erlauben~ ("to permit") and English ~believe~ are therefore distant cousins: both descend from PIE *~lewbʰ-~, one through the "granting approval" branch, the other through the "placing trust" branch. The English cognate of ~erlauben~ specifically is the archaic verb ~leave~ (as in "by your leave"), meaning "permission." Cognate with Old English ~ālīefan~, ~ālȳfan~ ("to allow"), Dutch ~verloven~ (archaic "to permit").
en.erleben:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~leben~:

- ~leben~: From MHG ~lëben~, from OHG ~lebēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~libbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~libjaną~, from PIE *~leyp-~ ("to leave, cling, linger"). Cognate with Dutch ~leven~, English ~live~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The compound's semantic development from "to live through (something)" to "to experience" reflects the completive force of ~er-~: what one fully lives through, one has experienced. The noun ~Erlebnis~ ("experience, adventure") derives from this verb. ~Erleben~ conveys a more vivid, personal, emotionally charged experience than the parallel ~Erfahrung~ (from ~erfahren~), which emphasizes accumulated knowledge — a distinction felt by every German speaker.
en.erledigen:
From ~er-~ + ~ledig~ + ~-igen~, a deadjectival verb meaning "to free from, to settle, to take care of":

- ~ledig~: From MHG ~lëdic~, ~lëdec~ ("free, unoccupied, unmarried"), from OHG *~lidig~, from Proto-Germanic *~liþugaz~ ("flexible, free"). Related to ~Glied~ ("limb, member") and ultimately to PIE *~leyH-~ ("to let go, release").
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix emphasizes finishing or resolving something fully.

~Erledigen~ means "to take care of, to settle, to finish off" — etymologically, "to make free," to resolve something so that it is no longer pending. The colloquial sense "to finish off, to exhaust" (~Ich bin erledigt~ — "I'm done for, I'm wiped out") extends the metaphor: what is ~erledigt~ is so thoroughly freed from obligation that it has no energy left. The base adjective ~ledig~ survives primarily in the sense "unmarried, single" (~ledig~ on official forms), preserving the older meaning of "free, unattached." The semantic path from "free" to "taken care of" is counterintuitive until you consider the German perspective: to ~erledigen~ a task is to free yourself from it. Cognate with Dutch ~afhandelen~ (a different compound with similar meaning).
en.erleichtern:
From ~er-~ + ~leicht~ + ~-ern~, a deadjectival verb meaning "to lighten, to ease, to relieve":

- ~leicht~: From OHG ~līht~, from Proto-West Germanic *~līht~, from Proto-Germanic *~linhtaz~, from PIE *~h₁lengʷʰ-~ ("light, not heavy"). Cognate with English ~light~ (not heavy), Dutch ~licht~, Danish ~let~.
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix emphasizes achieving the lightened state.

~Erleichtern~ means "to make easier, to relieve, to facilitate" — etymologically, "to make light." The reflexive ~sich erleichtern~ can mean "to relieve oneself" (a euphemism for urination), while the nominal ~Erleichterung~ ("relief") is one of German's most common emotional nouns: the feeling when a burden is lifted. The adjective ~leicht~ and its English cognate ~light~ descend from the same Proto-Germanic *~linhtaz~, which itself derives from PIE *~h₁lengʷʰ-~ — making the connection between German ~erleichtern~ and the English phrase "to lighten the load" a genuine cognate parallel, not a coincidence. The ~-ern~ suffix (the same verbal derivation pattern seen in ~begeistern~, ~verbessern~) converts the adjective into a causative verb: "to cause to become light."
en.erleiden:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~leiden~:

- ~leiden~: From MHG ~līden~, from OHG ~līdan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~līþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~līþaną~ ("to go, to travel"). Cognate with Dutch ~lijden~, English ~lithe~ (archaic: "to go"). The meaning shifted from "go, travel" through "endure" to "suffer," reinforced by MHG ~leiden~ ("to be sorry, to suffer"), from OHG ~leidēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laiþijan~, and the originally unrelated noun ~Leid~ ("sorrow"), cognate with English ~loath~.
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of enduring a process to its completion — suffering through something fully.

~Erleiden~ means "to suffer, to sustain, to undergo" — always something negative: ~einen Verlust erleiden~ ("to suffer a loss"), ~eine Niederlage erleiden~ ("to sustain a defeat"). The ~er-~ prefix intensifies the suffering, marking it as a completed experience rather than an ongoing state. The semantic history of ~leiden~ is a remarkable case of contamination: Proto-Germanic *~līþaną~ meant simply "to go, to travel," but the hardships of medieval travel — and the phonetic similarity to the unrelated *~laiþaz~ ("hateful, hostile," whence English ~loath~) — gradually pulled the verb from motion into misery. English preserves the original motion sense in the archaic ~lithe~ ("to go") and the word ~lithesome~ ("moving gracefully").
en.erlernen:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~lernen~:

- ~lernen~: From MHG ~lernen~, from OHG ~lernēn~, ~lirnēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~liʀnēn~, from a PIE mediopassive causative formation *~lis-n̥h₂-tór~ from the root *~leys-~ ("to trace, to track"). Cognate with English ~learn~ (from Old English ~leornian~). Related to German ~lehren~ ("to teach"), from Proto-Germanic *~laizijaną~, the active causative of the same root.
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of achievement or completion — reaching the end of a process.

The literal sense is "to learn completely" — to master, to acquire fully. While ~lernen~ means simply "to learn" (an ongoing process), ~erlernen~ emphasizes the completed achievement: ~eine Sprache erlernen~ means to master a language, not merely to study it. The PIE root *~leys-~ ("to trace, to track") reveals a profound metaphor: learning is tracking — following trails of knowledge, tracing patterns until they become familiar. This root also produced Proto-Germanic *~laizō~ ("teaching, lore"), whence Old English ~lār~ (modern English ~lore~), and *~listiz~ ("art, cunning"), whence German ~List~ ("cunning, ruse") and English ~list~ (in the archaic sense of "craft"). The twin siblings ~lehren~ ("to teach") and ~lernen~ ("to learn") derive from the same root via different derivational paths: one causative ("to make someone track"), the other middle-voice ("to track for oneself").
en.erläutern:
From ~er-~ + ~läutern~, a deadjectival verb from ~lauter~ ("pure, clear"):

- ~läutern~: From MHG ~liutern~, ~lūtern~, from the adjective ~lūter~ ("clean, clear, pure"), from OHG ~hlūtar~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlūtr~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlūtraz~ ("clear, pure"). Cognate with English ~luster~ (a distant relative via Latin, not a direct cognate).
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix emphasizes bringing something to full clarity.

~Erläutern~ means "to explain, to elucidate, to clarify" — etymologically, "to make something pure/clear all the way through." The base adjective ~lauter~ survives in modern German with two distinct meanings: as an adjective meaning "pure, sincere" (~lautere Absichten~, "pure intentions") and as an adverb meaning "nothing but, sheer" (~lauter Unsinn~, "sheer nonsense"). The verb ~läutern~ itself means "to purify, to refine" (both physically and morally — one ~läutert~ gold or one's character), while the ~er-~ prefix shifts the meaning from physical purification to intellectual clarification. The derived noun ~Erläuterung~ ("explanation, commentary") is standard in academic and legal German. The old ~hl-~ initial cluster (OHG ~hlūtar~) was simplified to ~l-~ in Middle High German, just as in ~lachen~ from ~hlahjan~.
en.ermitteln:
From ~er-~ + ~Mittel~ + ~-n~, a denominal verb coined in Early New High German. The middle element is the noun ~Mittel~ ("means, medium, remedy"), from Middle High German ~mittel~, from Old High German ~mittil~, from Proto-Germanic *~midlą~ ("middle"), cognate with Dutch ~middel~, English ~middle~.

- ~Mittel~: From the Proto-Germanic base *~medja-~ ("middle"), from PIE *~medhyo-~ ("middle"). The same PIE root underlies English ~mid~, ~middle~, Latin ~medius~ ("middle," source of ~medium~ and ~Mediterranean~), and Sanskrit ~mádhya-~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). The completive prefix frames the use of means as arriving at a result — finding out, determining. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The verb's literal meaning is "to ascertain by means," and it specializes in investigation: detectives ~ermitteln~ facts, courts ~ermitteln~ evidence, statistics ~ermitteln~ values. The noun ~Ermittlung~ ("investigation, finding") is the standard legal and journalistic term for an inquiry. The semantic journey — from "middle" to "means" to "to investigate" — is anchored in the ancient intuition that the middle is the place where things are measured and weighed: a mediator stands in the middle, and the investigator who reaches the truth has found the measure of the matter. The cognate chain connecting German ~Mittel~ to English ~middle~ and Latin ~medium~ passes through the same PIE root *~medhyo-~, making ~ermitteln~ a distant relative of English ~mediate~ and ~immediate~. Cognate with Dutch ~vaststellen~ (conceptually parallel; no direct cognate for this compound).
en.ermöglichen:
Denominal compound of ~er-~ + ~möglich~ + ~-en~. The adjective ~möglich~ derives from ~mögen~ + the adjectival suffix ~-lich~.

- ~möglich~: From ~mögen~ + ~-lich~. ~Mögen~ descends from MHG ~mugen~, OHG ~mugan~, Proto-West Germanic *~magan~, Proto-Germanic *~maganą~, PIE *~megʰ-~ ("to be able, have power").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The compound literally means "to make possible" — the ~er-~ prefix imparts a resultative sense ("to bring about the state of being ~möglich~"). The adjectival suffix ~-lich~ (cognate with English ~-ly~ and ~-like~) converts the modal verb into an adjective, before the verbal suffix ~-en~ reconverts it into a verb: ability → possible → to make possible. Cognate with Dutch ~mogelijk maken~.
en.ernennen:
From Middle High German ~ernennen~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~nennen~:

- ~nennen~: From MHG ~nennen~, ~nemmen~, ~nemnen~, from OHG ~nemnen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~namnijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~namnijaną~, a denominative verb derived from *~namô~ ("name"), from Proto-Indo-European *~h1nomn~ ("name"). Cognate with English dialectal ~nemn~ ("to name"), Swedish ~namna~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds a sense of definitiveness or official result.

The compound literally means "to name out" or "to name forth," hence "to appoint, to designate." The act of naming has carried legal and political force since ancient times -- to name someone to a position was to confer authority. ~Nennen~ is simply "to call, to name," but ~ernennen~ elevates the act to an official appointment. The PIE root *~h1nomn~ is one of the most stable words in the language family, surviving nearly unchanged across thousands of years and dozens of languages.
en.erneuern:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~neu~ + ~-ern~:

- ~neu~: From MHG ~niuwe~, from OHG ~niuwi~, from Proto-West Germanic *~niwi~, from Proto-Germanic *~niwjaz~, from PIE *~néwos~ ("new"). Cognate with English ~new~, Dutch ~nieuw~, Danish ~ny~, Latin ~novus~, Greek ~neos~, Sanskrit ~navas~.
- ~er-~: Inseparable prefix from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here it conveys achievement or completion: making something fully new.

~Erneuern~ means "to renew, to renovate, to restore." The PIE root *~néwos~ is one of the most widely preserved words in the Indo-European family -- virtually every daughter language retains a recognizable reflex: English ~new~, Latin ~novus~ (whence ~novel~, ~novice~, ~innovate~), Greek ~neos~ (whence ~neon~, ~neologism~), Welsh ~newydd~, Russian ~novyj~, Persian ~now~ (as in the holiday ~Nowruz~, "new day"), and Hindi ~nayā~. To ~erneuern~ is to bring something back to its ~neue~ state, a concept so fundamental that the word for it has survived five millennia of linguistic change virtually intact.
en.ernten:
From Middle High German ~ernen~, from Old High German ~arnon~, from Proto-Germanic *~azanōną~. This verb is denominative, derived from the noun *~azaniz~ ("harvest"), from Proto-Indo-European *~seh1-~ ("to sow"). The modern form with ~-t-~ (first attested in MHG ~ernde~) stems from a merger with the related noun OHG ~arnōt~, from Proto-Germanic *~azanōdaz~.

Cognate with English ~earn~, which shares the same Proto-Germanic ancestor *~azanōną~ but diverged dramatically in meaning. The connection is that both words originally denoted reaping the fruits of labor: German preserved the agricultural sense ("to harvest"), while English generalized it to "to gain by labor." The PIE root *~seh1-~ ("to sow") also produced Latin ~semen~ ("seed") and English ~sow~ and ~seed~, making ~ernten~ a distant cousin of all of them.
en.ernähren:
From Middle High German ~ernern~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~nähren~:

- ~nähren~: From MHG ~neren~, ~nern~, from OHG ~nerien~, from Proto-West Germanic *~nazjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~nazjaną~. The original OHG meaning was "to rescue, to keep alive," which gradually narrowed to "to nourish, to feed." Cognate with obsolete Dutch ~neren~ ("to feed, to provide for oneself").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix conveys thoroughness or sustaining completion.

The shift from "to rescue" to "to nourish" is intuitive: in a subsistence world, to save someone and to feed someone were nearly the same act. The ~er-~ prefix intensifies this life-sustaining sense, making ~ernähren~ mean "to nourish fully, to sustain." The reflexive ~sich ernähren~ ("to feed oneself, to make a living") preserves the older idea of self-preservation.
en.erobern:
From MHG ~geoberen~, ~oberen~, from OHG ~giobarōn~ ("to overcome, to delay"), ~obarōn~ ("to delay, to cancel"), and ~irobarōn~ ("to get tired, to be exhausted"). Related to ~obere~ ("upper") and ~ober~ ("above"), from OHG ~obar~, from Proto-Germanic *~ubar~ ("over, above"), from PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). The prefix ~er-~ (from OHG ~ir-~, ~ge-~) adds the sense of achievement, of overcoming from above.

~Erobern~ means "to conquer, to capture, to win" — ~eine Stadt erobern~ ("to conquer a city"), ~jemandes Herz erobern~ ("to win someone's heart"). The noun ~Eroberung~ ("conquest") and ~Eroberer~ ("conqueror") are direct derivatives. The etymology reveals a spatial metaphor for conquest: to conquer is to gain the upper position (~ober~, "upper"), to overcome by rising above. This is the same root that gives German ~über~ ("over"), ~Oberfläche~ ("surface," literally "upper surface"), and ~Oberst~ ("colonel" — originally the uppermost-ranking officer). The PIE source *~upéri~ also produced Greek ~hyper~ ("over, beyond"), Latin ~super~ ("above"), and Sanskrit ~upári~ ("above") — making ~erobern~ a distant cousin of English ~super~, ~hyper~, and ~over~.
en.erreichen:
From Middle High German ~erreichen~, from Old High German ~irreihhen~, ~irreihhōn~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~reichen~:

- ~reichen~: From MHG ~reichen~, from OHG ~reihhen~, ~reichen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raikijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~raikijaną~. Cognate with Dutch ~reiken~, English ~reach~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The ~er-~ prefix transforms the base meaning of "to reach, extend" into "to achieve, attain" — the goal is reached to completion. Cognate with Low German ~erreiken~, Dutch ~ereiken~ (archaic).
en.errichten:
From Middle High German ~errihten~, from Old High German ~irrihten~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~richten~:

- ~richten~: From MHG ~rihten~, from OHG ~rihten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rihtijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rihtijaną~ ("to make straight, direct"), from PIE *~h₃reǵ-~ ("to straighten, direct").
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix emphasizes bringing something to its full upright state.

~Errichten~ means "to erect, to establish, to build" — it takes the base sense of ~richten~ ("to direct, to make straight") and, with the completive ~er-~, pushes it to the endpoint: something fully raised, standing upright, brought into being. One ~errichtet~ a building, a monument, a foundation, or a state. The word carries a formality that the simpler ~bauen~ ("to build") lacks — one ~baut~ a house but ~errichtet~ a cathedral or an institution. The PIE root *~h₃reǵ-~ ("to straighten") also gives Latin ~rēx~ ("king," literally "the one who directs"), English ~right~, ~regal~, and ~reign~ — to erect and to rule share a common ancestor in the act of straightening. Cognate with Dutch ~oprichten~.
en.erschaffen:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~schaffen~ (strong verb):

- ~schaffen~ (strong verb): From MHG ~schaffen~, from OHG ~scafan~, ~scaffen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skappjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skapjaną~ ("to create"). Cognate with English ~shape~ and Dutch ~scheppen~.
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix adds a sense of achievement and completion — bringing something into existence.

~Erschaffen~ means "to create" in the highest, most deliberate sense — divine or artistic creation. It exclusively takes the strong conjugation (~erschuf~, ~erschaffen~), unlike the simple verb ~schaffen~, which has split into two: a strong verb (~schuf~, ~geschaffen~ = "to create") and a weak verb (~schaffte~, ~geschafft~ = "to manage, accomplish"). ~Am Anfang erschuf Gott Himmel und Erde~ ("In the beginning God created heaven and earth") is the classic usage. The connection to English ~shape~ reveals that the PIE speakers conceived of creation not as conjuring from void but as forming, carving, shaping raw material.
en.erscheinen:
From Middle High German ~erschīnen~, from Old High German ~irskīnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀskīnan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~scheinen~:

- ~scheinen~: From MHG ~schīnen~, from OHG ~skīnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skīnan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skīnaną~, from PIE *~(s)ḱeh₁y-~ ("to shine, shimmer"). Cognate with Dutch ~schijnen~, English ~shine~, Danish ~skinne~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The completive ~er-~ prefix shifts ~scheinen~'s sense of "to shine, to appear" toward "to appear fully, to emerge into visibility" — ~erscheinen~ describes something coming into view rather than merely glimmering. This explains the verb's dominant modern use for publications: a book ~erscheint~ ("appears, comes out") in the sense of coming fully into the world. The same compound is found in Dutch ~verschijnen~ (with ~ver-~ rather than ~er-~, but the same conceptual result). Cognate with Dutch ~verschijnen~ and Low German ~erschienen~.
en.erschließen:
From the prefix ~er-~ + ~schliessen~:

- ~schliessen~: From MHG ~sliezen~, from OHG ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slutanan~, *~sleutanan~ ("to lock, to shut"). Cognate with Old Saxon ~slutan~, Dutch ~sluiten~ ("to close"), and English ~slot~ (originally the bolt of a lock).
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ur-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion, achievement, or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The compound ~erschliessen~ means "to open up, to develop, to tap (a resource), to infer." At first glance, the combination of a completive prefix with a verb meaning "to close" producing a verb meaning "to open" seems paradoxical. The key lies in the older meaning of ~schliessen~: originally it meant not just "to close" but "to fasten a lock," and by extension "to conclude" (as in English "to come to a conclusion"). With the completive ~er-~ prefix, the sense became "to unlock completely," hence "to open up, to make accessible." The use in the sense of "to infer" (~daraus erschliessen~) parallels ~schliessen~ in its meaning of "to conclude logically." English ~disclose~ captures a similar reversal of "closing" into "opening up."
en.erschweren:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~schwer~ + ~-en~:

- ~schwer~: From MHG ~swær~, from OHG ~swār~, from Proto-West Germanic *~swār~, from Proto-Germanic *~swēraz~, from PIE *~swer-~ ("heavy, grave, serious"). Cognate with Dutch ~zwaar~, Old Norse ~svárr~, and Latin ~sērius~ ("serious," possibly from the same root).
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix adds a causative, intensifying force — making something more so.

~Erschweren~ means "to make difficult, to aggravate, to complicate" — literally "to make heavy." The verb treats difficulty as weight, a metaphor deeply embedded in German (and English). The antonym ~erleichtern~ ("to facilitate, to make easier") employs the same ~er-~ prefix but with ~leicht~ ("light, easy"), forming a perfect heavy/light pair: ~erschweren~/~erleichtern~, as complementary as gravity and buoyancy.
en.ersetzen:
From Middle High German ~ersetzen~, from Old High German ~irsezzen~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjaną~ ("to set, place down"), from PIE *~sodéyeti~. A causative of *~sitjaną~ ("to sit"): literally "to cause to sit."
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or achievement to the base verb.

The ~er-~ prefix transforms ~setzen~ ("to place, set") into ~ersetzen~ ("to replace, compensate") — the completive force of ~er-~ implies that the original is fully displaced and something new set in its place. The legal and financial sense of ~ersetzen~ ("to reimburse, make good for a loss") is older than the everyday sense of "to substitute"; the OHG compound ~irsezzen~ carried the sense of "to restore to its proper position." Cognate with Dutch ~vervangen~ (functional equivalent).
en.ersparen:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~sparen~:

- ~sparen~: From MHG ~sparn~, from OHG ~sparen~, ~sparon~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sparēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~sparōną~, *~sparāną~ ("to save, keep, spare"), from Proto-Indo-European *~sper-~ ("to be productive, to earn"). Cognate with English ~spare~, Dutch ~sparen~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix adds the sense of achieving a result through the base verb's action.

While ~sparen~ means "to save" in the general sense of economizing, ~ersparen~ means "to spare someone something" or "to save up (a specific amount)." The ~er-~ prefix narrows the action to a definite result: not just the habit of saving but the achievement of having saved. The PIE root *~sper-~ originally meant "to be productive," and its semantic descendants span from English ~spare~ (to refrain, to have extra) to German ~sparen~ (to economize) -- complementary facets of the same idea of surplus and restraint.
en.erstatten:
From Middle High German ~erstaten~, derived from ~Statt~ ("place, stead"). Compound of ~er-~ + ~statten~:

- ~statten~: A denominative verb from ~Statt~ ("place, stead"), an orthographic variant of ~Stadt~ ("city"). From MHG ~stat~, from OHG ~stat~, from Proto-Germanic *~stadiz~ ("place"), from Proto-Indo-European *~steh2tis~ ("standing, location"), from the root *~steh2-~ ("to stand"). Cognate with English ~stead~, Doublet of ~Stätte~ and ~Stadt~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix conveys restoration or completion.

The literal sense is "to put back in its place" or "to restore to its proper standing," hence modern meanings of "to reimburse, to refund" and "to file, to render" (as in ~Bericht erstatten~, "to file a report"). The connection to "standing" and "place" runs deep: to reimburse someone is to restore them to the position they occupied before a loss.
en.erstellen:
From ~er-~ + ~stellen~. Compound of two elements:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb, the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~ ("to stand").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or upward result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

~Erstellen~ is a Modern German formation (not attested in OHG or MHG), where the ~er-~ prefix adds the sense of bringing something fully into existence — placing it up into being. Its primary modern meaning is "to create, produce, compile" (a document, report, or structure), reflecting the completive force of ~er-~: not merely placing something, but establishing it fully. The word is especially common in administrative and digital contexts (~eine Datei erstellen~, "to create a file"), where it is the standard synonym for ~anfertigen~ or ~verfassen~. Cognate with Dutch ~opstellen~ ("to draw up, compose," with a different but semantically parallel prefix).
en.erstrecken:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~strecken~:

- ~strecken~: From MHG ~strecken~, from OHG ~strecken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~strakkjan~ ("to stretch, make taut"), from PIE *~(s)treg-~ or *~streg-~ ("stiff, rigid"). Cognate with English ~stretch~, Dutch ~strekken~, Danish ~strække~, Swedish ~sträcka~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). Here the prefix conveys the result or extent of the stretching action.

The verb ~erstrecken~ is used almost exclusively as a reflexive (~sich erstrecken~, "to extend, to stretch, to span"), describing something that reaches or spreads over a given area or period. While ~strecken~ means "to stretch" physically (as in stretching one's limbs), ~erstrecken~ abstracts the notion to spatial or temporal extent. The PIE root *~(s)treg-~ with its "stiff, taut" sense also gave rise to English ~stark~ and ~starch~, all connected through the idea of rigidity and extension. The ~s-mobile~ (optional initial ~s~) in the PIE root is a classic phenomenon where some daughter languages kept the ~s~ and others dropped it.
en.erteilen:
From Middle High German ~erteilen~, from Old High German ~irteilen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀdailijan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~teilen~:

- ~teilen~: From MHG ~teilen~, from OHG ~teilen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dailijan~ ("to divide, part, deal"), from Proto-Germanic *~dailijaną~, from *~dailiz~ ("part, share"), from PIE *~deh₂i-~ ("to divide"). Cognate with English ~deal~, Dutch ~delen~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

In modern German, ~erteilen~ means "to grant, bestow, issue" (permission, a lesson, a command) — one ~erteilt~ an ~Erlaubnis~ (permission) or a ~Lektion~ (lesson). Historically, ~erteilen~ was synonymous with ~urteilen~ ("to judge"), both deriving from the idea of dealing out or apportioning justice. Indeed, ~urteilen~ displaced ~erteilen~ in the judicial sense during the Middle High German period. The noun ~Urteil~ ("judgment, verdict") preserves this original meaning, while ~erteilen~ shifted toward the more general notion of dispensing or conferring. The phrase ~jemandem eine Abfuhr erteilen~ ("to give someone a rebuff," literally "to issue someone a removal") shows the verb at its most formal and decisive. Cognate with English ~ordeal~, which descends from the same Proto-Germanic root *~uzdailiją~ ("judicial decision").
en.erwarten:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~warten~:

- ~warten~: From MHG ~warten~, from OHG ~wartēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wardēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~wardāną~ ("to guard, protect, watch over"), from Proto-Germanic *~warduz~ ("guard"), from PIE *~wer-~ ("to heed, watch"). Cognate with Old Saxon ~wardōn~, Swedish ~vårda~, English ~ward~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The ~er-~ prefix shifts ~warten~ (to wait, to watch) from a state of watchful waiting into a forward-oriented expectation: ~erwarten~ is not merely to wait but to await with anticipation, to count on an outcome. The same PIE root *~wer-~ ("to heed") underlies English ~ward~, ~warden~, and ~aware~; and, borrowed via Old French from Frankish, English ~guard~ traces to the same ancestral root. Cognate with Dutch ~verwachten~ (a parallel compound with ~ver-~ in place of ~er-~).
en.erwecken:
From Middle High German ~erwecken~, from Old High German ~irwekken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uzwakkjan~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~wecken~:

- ~wecken~: From MHG ~wecken~, from OHG ~wecchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wakkjan~ ("to wake up, to awaken"), the causative form of *~wakēn~ ("to be awake"), from Proto-Indo-European *~weg-~ ("to be lively, active"). Cognate with English ~wake~, Dutch ~wekken~.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uz-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). This prefix adds completeness or thoroughness.

The compound intensifies the causative meaning: while ~wecken~ is simply "to wake (someone) up," ~erwecken~ means "to awaken, to arouse, to rouse" -- often in a figurative or spiritual sense. One wakes a sleeping child with ~wecken~, but one ~erweckt~ feelings, memories, or even the dead. The biblical ~Auferweckung~ ("resurrection") is built on this same verb. The PIE root *~weg-~ ("to be lively, active") also produced Latin ~vigil~ ("watchful"), giving English ~vigil~, ~vigilant~, and ~vigor~.
en.erweisen:
~er-~ + ~weisen~. Compound of ~er-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wīsen~, from OHG ~wīsen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wīsijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wīsijaną~ ("to show, point out"), from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see").
- ~er-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds the sense of achievement, completion, or emergence into evidence.

~Erweisen~ means "to prove, to show, to render (a service)." The reflexive ~sich erweisen als~ ("to prove to be, to turn out as") is particularly common: ~er erwies sich als zuverlässig~ ("he proved to be reliable"). The non-reflexive can mean "to render" in formal contexts: ~jemandem einen Dienst erweisen~ ("to render someone a service"), ~jemandem Ehre erweisen~ ("to pay someone honor"). The PIE root *~weyd-~ ("to see") connects seeing, knowing, and showing in a single etymological thread: to ~er-weisen~ is to make something visibly evident, to bring knowledge out into the open. The same root gave Latin ~vidēre~ ("to see"), Greek ~eidos~ ("form"), and Sanskrit ~veda~ ("knowledge") — making ~erweisen~ a distant cousin of English ~wisdom~, ~witness~, and ~video~. Cognate with Dutch ~erwijzen~ (archaic).
en.erweitern:
From ~er-~ + ~weitern~, where ~weitern~ is an obsolete verb derived from the comparative of ~weit~ ("wide, broad, far"):

- ~weit~: From MHG ~wīt~, from OHG ~wīt~, from Proto-Germanic *~wīdaz~. Cognate with Dutch ~wijd~, English ~wide~, Low German ~wied~, Danish ~vid~, Swedish ~vid~.
- ~er-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix transforms "to make wider" into "to expand, to extend fully."

The derivation runs: ~weit~ → comparative ~weiter~ ("wider") → denominative *~weitern~ ("to make wider") → prefixed ~erweitern~ ("to expand, enlarge, broaden"). The intermediate verb ~weitern~ fell out of use, surviving only in this compound and a few dialects. The noun ~Erweiterung~ ("expansion, extension, widening") is used in mathematics (~Erweiterung eines Bruchs~, "extension of a fraction") and in everyday speech (~EU-Erweiterung~, "EU enlargement").
en.erwerben:
From Middle High German ~erwerben~, from Old High German ~irwerban~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~werben~. Compare Dutch ~verwerven~.

- ~werben~: From MHG ~wërben~, from OHG ~wërban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hwerban~, from Proto-Germanic *~hwerbaną~ ("to turn, move around"). A class III strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~werven~ ("to recruit"), Icelandic ~hverfa~ ("to turn, disappear"), Faroese ~hvørva~ ("to disappear"), and — more distantly — English ~whirl~ via a parallel PIE root.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This completive prefix transforms the turning motion of ~werben~ into a fully achieved acquisition. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The semantic journey of ~werben~ is one of German's most striking: from "to turn around" (Proto-Germanic) to "to move about on business" (Old High German), then to "to court, recruit, advertise" (Modern German), and in the compound ~erwerben~ to "to acquire, earn." The connecting thread is the turning motion of commerce — hawking wares, making rounds, drumming up custom. That the same verb underlies both ~Werbung~ ("advertising") and ~erwerben~ ("to acquire") reveals an ancient truth: advertising is the turning that produces getting. The English near-cognate ~whirl~ preserved the literal spinning sense, while the Germanic mercantile senses developed along their own path. A surviving compound that keeps the older sense is ~bewerben~ ("to apply for a job," literally "to turn toward something"). Cognate with Dutch ~verwerven~, Icelandic ~hverfa~.
en.erwischen:
Compound of ~er-~ + ~wischen~:

- ~wischen~: From MHG ~wischen~, from OHG ~wisken~ ("to wipe, to move quickly"), from Proto-Germanic *~wiskijaną~ ("to wipe, to move swiftly"). The original sense was a quick, darting motion — wiping or whisking.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, ~ar-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("out of, from"), from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). As an inseparable prefix, it conveys achievement, attainment, or sudden completion.

The verb ~erwischen~ means "to catch, to nab, to get hold of" — ~einen Dieb erwischen~ ("to catch a thief"), ~den letzten Zug erwischen~ ("to catch the last train"), ~jemanden beim Schummeln erwischen~ ("to catch someone cheating"). The semantic development from "quick wiping motion" to "catching" passed through the idea of a swift grabbing movement: you ~erwischen~ someone the way you might ~wischen~ (swipe) at a fly. The ~er-~ prefix adds the sense of successful completion — not just swiping but actually catching. Colloquially, ~es hat ihn erwischt~ ("it got him") can mean anything from catching a cold to falling in love to dying, depending on context — a remarkable range of fates all described by the same image of being suddenly seized. The related English ~whisk~ (from Old Norse ~viska~, a Germanic cousin of ~wischen~) preserves the sense of rapid sweeping motion.
en.erwähnen:
A 16th-century alteration of the earlier form ~gewähnen~, from Middle High German ~gewähenen~, ~gewahen~, from Old High German ~giwahanen~, ~giwahan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gawahwan~ ("to say, mention"), from Proto-Germanic *~gawahwaną~, from *~ga-~ + *~wahwaną~, from PIE *~wekʷ-~ ("to speak").

The 16th-century reshaping swapped the prefix ~ge-~ for ~er-~, bringing the verb into line with other achievement verbs (the ~er-~ prefix conveys completion: "to bring to mention"). This substitution can create the impression that ~erwähnen~ is related to ~wähnen~ ("to suppose, imagine"), which descends from a different root entirely — Proto-West Germanic *~wānijan~, from a stem *~wōni-~ related to PIE *~wen-~ ("to desire, strive for"). The resemblance is a coincidence of form: the two verbs are not cognate. The true root of ~erwähnen~, PIE *~wekʷ-~, is the same root underlying Latin ~vōx~ ("voice"), ~vocāre~ ("to call"), and English ~voice~ and ~vocal~ — all ultimately from the same "speaking" root. Cognate with Dutch ~gewagen~ ("to mention, refer to").
en.erzeugen:
From ~er-~ + ~zeugen~:

- ~zeugen~: From MHG ~ziugen~, ~geziugen~, from OHG ~giziugon~, a denominative verb from ~Zeug~ ("stuff, gear, equipment, tools"). ~Zeug~ itself derives from MHG ~ziuc~, from OHG ~giziug~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teug~, ultimately a nominal derivative of *~teuhaną~ ("to pull, lead"), which also underlies ~ziehen~ ("to pull, draw"), from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead").
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). The completive prefix turns the act of producing or witnessing into the fully achieved result: something brought into being. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The root ~zeugen~ has two seemingly unrelated modern senses — "to beget, procreate" and "to testify, bear witness" — but both trace to the same image: producing or bringing forward. A witness produces testimony; a parent produces offspring. ~Zeuge~ ("witness") shares this root, as does the archaic noun ~Gezeugs~ ("tools, implements," i.e., things produced). The deeper PIE root *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead") ties ~zeugen~ to Latin ~dūcere~ ("to lead"), the source of English ~duke~, ~duct~, and ~produce~. This makes ~erzeugen~ a distant relative of English ~produce~ — both meaning "to bring something forward" from a common ancestor who simply pulled. ~Überzeugen~ ("to convince"), another compound on the same root, is the rhetorical version of the same act: to pull someone over to one's position through testimony. Cognate with Dutch ~getuigen~ (witness sense), English ~tow~ (via the same PIE *~dewk-~ root shared with ~ziehen~).
en.erzielen:
From ~er-~ + ~zielen~, where ~zielen~ is a denominative verb from ~Ziel~ ("goal, target, aim"):

- ~zielen~: From ~Ziel~ + verbal ~-en~. ~Ziel~: from MHG ~zil~, from OHG ~zil~, from Proto-West Germanic *~til~, from Proto-Germanic *~tilą~ ("goal, mark"). Cognate with Old English ~til~ ("goal"), which survives in the preposition ~till~ (originally "up to the mark").
- ~er-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The completive prefix turns the act of aiming (~zielen~) into the achievement of hitting or obtaining a result.

The word ~Ziel~ itself covers both the physical sense (a target on an archery range) and the abstract sense (a goal in life or sport). ~Erzielen~ specializes in the achievement of quantifiable results: one ~erzielt~ a goal in football (~ein Tor erzielen~), a profit (~einen Gewinn erzielen~), or a record price. The completive ~er-~ is semantically precise: aiming (~zielen~) becomes scoring (~erzielen~). The Old English cognate ~til~ links to the modern preposition ~till/until~, whose core sense was "reaching the mark."
en.erzählen:
