From Middle High German ~erzellen~, from Old High German ~irzellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀtalljan~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~zählen~. Compound of two elements:

- ~zählen~: From MHG ~zel(e)n~, from OHG ~zellan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~talljan~, from Proto-Germanic *~taljaną~, which carried both "to count" and "to recount, tell" from its earliest attestation.
- ~er-~: From MHG ~er-~, from OHG ~ir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀ-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uz-~ ("up, out"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or result to the base verb. Doublet of ~ur-~.

The semantic shift from "counting" to "narrating" — powered by the completive ~er-~ prefix — is one of German's most striking lexical developments. The underlying Proto-Germanic root *~taljaną~ already held both meanings, but ~erzählen~ specialized entirely in the narrative sense: to give a full, complete account. The parallel with English is striking: ~tell~ (from the same root) underwent the same journey from counting to narrating, while ~teller~ at a bank and ~telling beads~ preserve the counting sense. The English word ~recount~ (to narrate) makes the metaphor explicit: to re-count events is to tell them. Cognate with Dutch ~vertellen~ ("to narrate," with a different prefix but the same root).
en.eröffnen:
From Middle High German ~eroffenen~, from Old High German ~iroffanōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uʀupanōjan~. Equivalent to ~er-~ + ~öffnen~:

- ~öffnen~: From MHG ~öffenen~, ~offenen~, from OHG ~offanōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~upanōn~, a denominative from Proto-Germanic *~upanaz~ ("open"), from PIE *~upo~ ("up, up from under"). Cognate with English ~open~, Dutch ~openen~, Danish ~åbne~.
- ~er-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix lends a sense of initiation or ceremonial beginning — "opening up" to completion.

While ~öffnen~ is the everyday word for opening a door or window, ~eröffnen~ carries a more formal or initiatory sense: one ~eröffnet~ a conference, a bank account, a trial, or a new shop. The completive ~er-~ transforms a simple action into an event. The related noun ~Eröffnung~ appears in legal, musical, and commercial contexts — a ~Eröffnungsfest~ is a grand opening, and a symphony's ~Eröffnungssatz~ is its opening movement.
en.essen:
From Middle High German ~ëȥȥen~, from Old High German ~ëȥȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~etan~, from Proto-Germanic *~etaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~h₁ed-~ ("to eat"). A class V strong verb. Cognate with Low German ~eten~, Dutch ~eten~, English ~eat~, Danish ~æde~, Norwegian ~ete~, Swedish ~äta~, Yiddish ~עסן~ (esn), Gothic ~itan~. Non-Germanic cognates include Latin ~edō~, Ancient Greek ~édō~, Sanskrit ~ádmi~, Russian ~yestʹ~, and Scottish Gaelic ~ith~.

Few PIE roots are as conservative as *~h₁ed-~. Across five thousand years and dozens of languages, the word for "to eat" has scarcely changed: Latin ~edō~, Greek ~édō~, Sanskrit ~ádmi~, German ~essen~, and English ~eat~ all reflect the same ancient root, differing only by the regular sound changes of each branch. The PIE root also yields Latin ~edō~, the source of English ~edible~, ~obese~ (from ~ob-esus~, "eaten up"), and ~comestible~ — making ~essen~ a distant relative of all of those. The geminate ~ss~ in the German infinitive reflects a Proto-West Germanic pattern of consonant strengthening. Notably, ~essen~ in Modern German is conventionally reserved for human eating — one says ~fressen~ when animals eat (and, rudely, when humans eat greedily), a distinction without an exact parallel in English.
en.etablieren:
Borrowed in the 17th century from French ~établir~ ("to establish"), from Old French ~establir~, from Latin ~stabilīre~ ("to make firm, to confirm, to establish"), from ~stabilis~ ("firm, steady"), from ~stāre~ ("to stand"), from PIE *~steh₂-~ ("to stand"). The German suffix ~-ieren~ marks the standard adaptation pattern for Romance verbs entering German.

The semantic journey from "to make firm" to "to establish" is straightforward, but the word's social history in German is more interesting. In the 18th and 19th centuries, ~etablieren~ carried a strong commercial sense: a merchant ~etablierte~ a business, and the reflexive ~sich etablieren~ ("to establish oneself") implied achieving financial and social respectability. The derived French loanword ~Etablissement~ took on a seedier coloring in German, often euphemistically referring to a nightclub or house of ill repute — a sense the French original does not share. Modern German has largely rehabilitated ~etablieren~ into neutral usage: political parties ~etablieren sich~ in parliament, scientific theories ~etablieren sich~ in textbooks. The related adjective ~etabliert~ ("established, well-established") carries no negative connotation. Cognate roots appear across the Indo-European spectrum: Latin ~stabilis~ gives English ~stable~, ~establish~, and ~state~; the same PIE *~steh₂-~ yields German ~stehen~, ~Stand~, ~Staat~, and ~stabil~ — making ~etablieren~ a long-distance cousin, returned via French, of words that never left German.
en.existieren: ~Existieren~ ("to exist") is borrowed from Latin ~exsistere~ / ~existere~ ("to step forth, emerge, come into being, exist"), from ~ex-~ ("out, forth") + ~sistere~ ("to cause to stand, to place, to halt"), from Proto-Italic *~sistō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~steh₂-~ ("to stand"). That PIE root is one of the most productive in Indo-European: it produced Latin ~stāre~ ("to stand"), English ~stand~, ~state~, ~station~, ~statute~, ~stable~, and ~substance~, as well as Ancient Greek ~ἵστημι~ (~hístēmi~, "I cause to stand") and Sanskrit ~तिष्ठति~ (~tíṣṭhati~, "he stands"). The Latin compound ~exsistere~ thus literally means "to stand out" — to emerge from non-being into being. English ~exist~ traveled the same route via Old French ~exister~. A philosophically interesting cognate: ~Bestand~ ("existence, stock, inventory") and ~bestehen~ ("to exist, persist, pass a test") share the same PIE root, showing that German preserved the standing metaphor natively while also borrowing the Latin learned form.
en.fahren: From Middle High German ~varn~, ~varen~, from Old High German ~faran~, from Proto-West Germanic *~faran~, from Proto-Germanic *~faraną~, from PIE *~per-~ ("going, passage"). A class VI strong verb whose broad original sense of "to go, travel" narrowed in Modern German to emphasize vehicular travel, while the older general sense of "going" survives in derivatives like ~Erfahrung~ ("experience," literally "a traveling through") and ~Gefahr~ ("danger," literally "a lying in wait on the journey"). Cognate with Dutch ~varen~, English ~fare~, Danish ~fare~.
en.fallen: From Middle High German ~vallen~, from Old High German ~fallan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fallan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallaną~. The ultimate PIE origin is uncertain; one proposed reconstruction connects it to a compound *~h₂peh₃lH-~ ("to fall away, be destroyed"), related to Ancient Greek ~apóllūmi~ ("to destroy, kill") and Lithuanian ~pùlti~ ("to fall, rush"). A class VII strong verb with a reduplicated past tense in Proto-Germanic. The semantic range of ~fallen~ has remained remarkably stable across two millennia — the word for a leaf falling in Old High German is the same word used today for falling in love (~sich verlieben~) or for a soldier falling in battle (~fallen im Krieg~). Cognate with Low German ~fallen~, Dutch ~vallen~, English ~fall~, Danish ~falde~, Icelandic ~falla~, Swedish ~falla~.
en.fangen: ~fangen~ comes from Middle High German ~vāhen~, from Old High German ~fāhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fą̄han~, from Proto-Germanic *~fanhaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~peh₂ḱ-~ ("to secure, attach, fasten"). The original strong verb had the vowel pattern ~fāhan~ / ~fieng~ / ~gefangan~; the modern present stem ~fang-~ was borrowed from the past forms under the influence of Middle Low German ~vangen~, spreading through Central German dialects in the early modern period. The same analogical leveling shaped ~hängen~, though ~hängen~ completed the shift more quickly because it already had two variant verbs reinforcing each other. Cognate with English ~fang~ (originally "that which seizes"), Dutch ~vangen~, West Frisian ~fange~, and — more distantly — Albanian ~peng~ ("to hinder, hold captive"). The PIE root *~peh₂ḱ-~ also produced Latin ~pangere~ ("to fix, fasten") and ~pax~ ("peace, compact"), making ~fangen~ a distant relative of English ~peace~.
en.fassen:
From MHG ~vazzen~, from OHG ~fazzōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fatōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fatōną~ ("to hold, seize"), from PIE *~pod-néh₂-ti~, from *~pod-~ ("to fetch"). Cognate with English ~fetch~.

The connection between ~fassen~ and English ~fetch~ is one of the more startling cognate pairs in the Germanic family: both descend from the same PIE root, yet they diverged dramatically — German keeping the sense of grasping and holding, English developing the sense of going to retrieve. The Proto-Germanic verb *~fatōną~ was a weak class 2 verb meaning "to hold, seize," and its descendants in German spread across an exceptionally rich idiomatic range: ~sich fassen~ ("to compose oneself, pull oneself together"), ~Fassung~ ("composure; version of a text"), ~unfassbar~ ("incomprehensible, literally ungraspable"), ~fassungslos~ ("speechless, stunned"), and the compound ~umfassen~ ("to encompass"). The cognitive metaphor of understanding as physical grasping runs deep: in German one ~fasst~ an idea, in English one ~grasps~ it, and in Latin one ~comprehendit~ it — all three verbs from different roots sharing the same bodily image. The noun ~Fass~ ("barrel, cask") shares the same root — a container that holds things. Note that ~fassen~ is not related to the English verb "to fast" (dietary abstinence), which derives from Proto-Germanic *~fastōną~ ("to hold firm, abstain") — a different root that produced a false-friend surface resemblance.
en.fehlen: From Middle High German ~vælen~, ~velen~, ~valen~, borrowed from Old French ~faillir~ ("to fail, be lacking"), from Latin ~fallō~ ("to deceive, cause to stumble"), from PIE *~(s)gʷʰh₂el-~ ("to stumble"), with a semantic development from "to stumble" through "to deceive." This makes ~fehlen~ one of the relatively few common German verbs that entered the language via Old French rather than through Germanic inheritance — a legacy of medieval courtly and trade contact. The semantic shift from "to deceive" through "to fall short" to "to be missing, to be lacking" follows the trajectory of French ~faillir~: what fails is what is not there when needed. The same Latin root gives English ~fail~, ~false~, and ~fallible~. Cognate with English ~fail~, French ~faillir~, Italian ~fallire~.
en.feiern:
From Middle High German ~vīren~, from Old High German ~fīrōn~, a denominative verb derived from Old High German ~fīra~ (whence modern ~Feier~), itself borrowed from Latin ~fēriae~ ("religious holidays, festivals"). Cognate with Dutch ~vieren~.

Latin ~fēriae~ is from Proto-Italic *~fēsiās~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dʰéh₁s-yo-s~, derived from *~dʰéh₁s~ ("god, godhead, deity"). The same PIE root underlies Latin ~fēstus~ ("festive") — whence English ~feast~ and ~festival~ — and Ancient Greek ~theîos~ ("divine"). ~Feiern~ is thus one of the rare German verbs whose etymology leads directly into Latin and ultimately into the divine: a holiday (~Feiertag~) was originally a day set aside for the gods. The route from Latin into German passed through the early Christian church, which adopted the Roman calendar vocabulary for its own feast days. The modern sense of ~feiern~ — throwing a birthday party, celebrating New Year's — is secular, but the word retains its sacred pedigree. Cognate (distantly) with English ~feast~ and ~festival~ via the Latin root.
en.fertigen: From Middle High German ~vertigen~, ~vertegen~ ("to prepare, to make ready, to keep ready"), equivalent to ~fertig~ + ~-en~. The adjective ~fertig~ ("ready, finished") descends from MHG ~fertec~, from OHG ~fartīg~, from the noun ~fart~ ("journey"). The original meaning of ~fertig~ was "ready for a journey" — prepared, equipped — before broadening to "ready" and then "finished, done." The noun ~Fart~ comes from the same root as ~fahren~: Proto-Germanic *~farą~ ("journey"), from PIE *~per-~ ("to cross, to pass through"). Cognate with Dutch ~vaardig~ ("capable, swift"), Low German ~fardig~, Old English ~fierd~ ("expedition, military campaign"). ~Fertigen~ in modern German means "to manufacture, to produce" — especially in industrial and technical contexts (~Bauteile fertigen~, "to manufacture components"). The semantic arc from "journey-ready" to "manufactured" traces the cultural shift from a travel-based to a production-based economy: what was once preparedness for departure became readiness as a finished product.
en.festhalten:
~fest-~ + ~halten~. Compound of ~fest-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldaną~ ("to hold, to tend, to herd"), from PIE *~kel-~ ("to drive, to herd").
- ~fest-~: From PIE *~pastV~ ("solid, stable"). The prefix adds the sense of firmness, fixedness, or permanence.

~Festhalten~ means "to hold firm, to hold onto, to record, to note down" — a verb whose meanings span from the physical grip to the documentary record. One ~hält~ a railing ~fest~ (grips it tightly), ~hält~ an a belief ~fest~ (clings to it), or ~hält~ observations ~fest~ (records them). The reflexive ~sich festhalten an~ ("to hold onto") is the instruction on every German bus and train: ~Bitte festhalten!~ ("Please hold on!"). The documentary sense — to fix information in place, to write it down — appears in the noun ~Feststellung~ ("determination, establishment of fact") and the participial compound ~festgehalten~ ("recorded, captured"). This semantic extension from physical to abstract is natural: what one grips firmly does not escape, and what one writes down is similarly secured against forgetting. The PIE root *~kel-~ ("to drive, herd") originally described the activity of cattle-herding — keeping animals together and under control — from which the sense of "holding" and "keeping" naturally developed. Cognate with Dutch ~vasthouden~ ("to hold tight").
en.festlegen:
~fest-~ + ~legen~. Compound of ~fest-~ + ~legen~:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjaną~, a causative of *~ligjaną~ ("to lie"), from PIE *~logʰéyeti~.
- ~fest-~: From MHG ~vest~, from OHG ~festi~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fast~, from Proto-Germanic *~fastuz~, probably from PIE *~pastV-~ ("solid, stable"). Cognate with English ~fast~ (in its "firm, fixed" sense, as in "hold fast") and Gothic ~fastan~.

~Festlegen~ means "to fix, determine, establish, pin down" — literally "to lay firm." The combination of "solid" and the causative "to lay" produces the sense of laying something down in a settled, permanent way: a rule, a date, a decision. The reflexive ~sich festlegen~ ("to commit oneself, to be pinned down") has a slightly anxious quality in modern usage — one ~legt sich fest~ when one has to make an irrevocable decision. English ~fast~ in "hold fast," "fasten," and "steadfast" preserves the same Proto-Germanic root *~fastuz~. The ~fest-~ prefix is unusual in that it derives from an adjective rather than a preposition or directional adverb — making ~festlegen~ a rare type of German separable compound built on an adjectival particle. Cognate with Dutch ~vastleggen~.
en.feststellen:
Compound of ~fest-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb — the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~.
- ~fest-~: From MHG ~vest~, ~veste~, from OHG ~festi~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fastī~, from PIE *~pastV-~ ("solid, stable"). Cognate with English ~fast~ (in the archaic sense of "firmly fixed," as in "hold fast"), Dutch ~vast~, Swedish ~fast~.

The literal meaning of ~feststellen~ is "to place firmly" — to put something down in such a way that it stays put. From this spatial metaphor come both of the verb's principal modern senses: "to establish a fact" (to fix it as true, to put it on the record so that it cannot be moved) and "to determine, to ascertain" (to arrive at a settled conclusion). The compound is a relatively modern coinage; MHG and OHG had no single equivalent word, relying instead on circumlocutions. Its closest English functional parallel is "to establish" or "to determine," though neither captures the Germanic concreteness of ~feststellen~'s spatial metaphor as precisely. The same ~fest-~ element appears in ~festhalten~ ("to hold fast"), ~festlegen~ ("to fix, to lay down"), and ~festigen~ ("to consolidate") — a family of verbs all built on the image of gripping or placing something so securely that it cannot shift. Cognate with Dutch ~vaststellen~.
en.filmen:
A denominative verb from ~Film~ + ~-en~. The noun ~Film~ was borrowed from English ~film~, which descends from Old English ~filmen~ ("membrane, thin skin"), from Proto-West Germanic *~filmin-~, from Proto-Germanic *~filmin-~ ("thin skin, membrane"), from Proto-Indo-European *~pel-mo-~ ("membrane"), from the root *~pel-~ ("to cover, skin").

In a charming etymological boomerang, the word traveled from Germanic to English, where it underwent a remarkable semantic shift -- from "membrane" to "thin coating" (1577) to "photographic coating" (1845) to "motion picture" (1895) -- before being borrowed back into German in the early twentieth century. Old English ~filmen~ meant "foreskin" or "caul," and no Anglo-Saxon could have imagined that this humble anatomical term would one day name an art form.
en.finanzieren: From ~Finanz~ + ~-ieren~. ~Finanz~ derives from French ~finance(s)~, from Middle French ~finer~ ("to pay a ransom, to settle a debt"), from ~fin~ ("end"), from Latin ~fīnis~ ("end, boundary, limit"). The semantic chain is elegant: a debt is "ended" by payment, so ~finance~ originally meant "an ending" — specifically, the ending of an obligation through payment. The sense shifted from "ransom payment" (mid-fifteenth century) to "taxation" (late fifteenth century) to "management of money" (eighteenth century). German naturalized the verb with the standard ~-ieren~ suffix, making ~finanzieren~ ("to finance, to fund") feel thoroughly German despite its Romance pedigree. The Latin root ~fīnis~ also gives German ~Finale~, ~Definition~, and ~Infinitiv~ — the last being a grammatical term meaning "the form without an ending," a fitting irony for a verb-conjugation app. Cognate with English ~finance~, French ~financer~, Italian ~finanziare~.
en.finden: From Middle High German ~vinden~, from Old High German ~findan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~finþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~finþaną~, from PIE *~pent-~ ("to go, pass; path, bridge"). The semantic development from "to go, pass" to "to find" parallels similar shifts in Slavic languages. Cognate with Bavarian ~findn~, Low German ~finden~, Dutch ~vinden~, English ~find~, Danish ~finde~.
en.fixieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~fixum~ ("that which is fixed, a fixed sum"), the neuter past participle of ~figere~ ("to fasten, to fix"), possibly influenced by Medieval Latin ~fixāre~ ("to make fast"). The ~-ieren~ suffix is the standard German adaptation of French/Latin ~-ier~/~-āre~ verb endings.

The Latin verb ~figere~ derives from Proto-Indo-European *~dʰeygʷ-~ ("to stick, to fix"). The meanings of ~fixieren~ branched in the sixteenth century when alchemists adopted ~fix~ to mean "stable, non-volatile" (a substance that would not evaporate). From this came the sense of "to make permanent" and then "to stare at fixedly." In modern German, ~fixieren~ can mean "to fix in place," "to stare at intently," or "to define precisely" -- all descendants of the alchemists' quest to make the volatile stable.
en.fliegen: ~fliegen~ comes from Middle High German ~vliegen~, from Old High German ~fliogan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fleugan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fleuganą~, from Proto-Indo-European *~plewk-~ ("to flow, fly"). The PIE root *~plewk-~ is an extended form of *~plew-~ ("to flow, float, swim"), which also produced ~fließen~ ("to flow"), Latin ~pluere~ ("to rain"), and Greek ~plein~ ("to sail"). Motion through a fluid medium — whether water or air — was once a single conceptual domain. The High German consonant shift turned Proto-Germanic *fl- into German fl- unchanged (labial fricatives were unaffected), but the vowel shift from Proto-Germanic *eu to OHG *io to MHG *ie is characteristic of German Class II strong verbs. Cognate with Dutch ~vliegen~, English ~fly~, Danish ~flyve~, and Low German ~flegen~. Compare also Bavarian ~fliagn~, which preserves an older vowel.
en.fliehen:
From Middle High German ~vliehen~, from Old High German ~fliohan~ ("to flee"), from Proto-West Germanic *~fleuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fleuhaną~ ("to flee"), probably from PIE *~plewk-~ ("to fly, flee"), an enlargement of *~plew-~ ("to flow, run"). A class II strong verb. Cognate with English ~flee~ (from OE ~flēon~), Dutch ~vlieden~, and Gothic ~þliuhan~ (with secondary ~þ-~).

The PIE root *~plewk-~ is also the source of ~fliegen~ ("to fly") and ~Flucht~ ("flight, escape"), making ~fliehen~ and ~fliegen~ etymological siblings -- the original root encompassed rapid motion through any medium. The English cognates ~flee~ and ~fly~ mirror this German pair perfectly. Gothic ~þliuhan~ is puzzling: it shows an unexpected ~þ-~ (th-sound) where all other Germanic languages have ~f-~. Linguists debate whether the Gothic form preserves an archaic consonant or reflects a secondary change unique to Gothic. The noun ~Flüchtling~ ("refugee"), derived from ~Flucht~, entered international discourse during the European migration crisis of 2015 -- a word with five-thousand-year-old roots describing an experience as old as humanity itself.
en.fließen: From Middle High German ~vlieȥen~, from Old High German ~flioȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fleotan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fleutaną~ ("to float, to stream, to flow"), from PIE *~plewd-~ ("to flow, to run, to swim"). A class II strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~fließen~, ~floss~, ~geflossen~. The PIE root *~plewd-~ produced an extensive water-vocabulary across Indo-European: Latin ~pluvia~ ("rain"), whence English ~pluvial~; Latvian ~plūdi~ ("flood"); Lithuanian ~pláuti~ ("to wash"); and Greek ~pleō~ ("I sail"). Within Germanic, the root gave English ~fleet~ (originally "a place where water flows," then the ships gathered there), ~float~, ~flood~, Dutch ~vlieten~ ("to flow," archaic/literary), Swedish ~flyta~ ("to float"), and Icelandic ~fljóta~. The zero-grade form *~flut-~ produced German ~Flut~ ("flood, high tide"), English ~flood~, and Dutch ~vloed~. The derived noun ~Fluss~ ("river") is literally "a flowing" — Germanic languages conceptualize rivers as the action of flowing made permanent, whereas Latin ~flūmen~ (from ~fluere~, "to flow," a different PIE root) took the same metaphorical path independently. The compound ~Einfluss~ ("influence") calques Latin ~influentia~ — a flowing-in of one thing upon another.
en.folgen: From Middle High German ~volgen~, from Old High German ~folgēn~ (attested from the 8th century, meaning "to follow, serve faithfully, pursue"), from Proto-West Germanic *~folgēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fulgāną~. The ultimate pre-Germanic origin is uncertain. The Old High German forms reveal an early semantic range that blended physical following, loyal service, and pursuit — reflecting the social structures of early Germanic society in which following a lord was the fundamental act of the ~comitatus~ (the warrior band). German ~folgen~ and English ~follow~ are doublets: both descend from the same Proto-Germanic root, but ~follow~ entered Middle English via Old Norse ~fylgja~ through Old English ~folgian~. Cognate with Dutch ~volgen~, English ~follow~, Danish ~følge~, Swedish ~följa~.
en.fordern:
From Middle High German ~vodern~, ~vordern~, from Old High German ~fordarōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~forþ(a)rōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~furþą~, from PIE *~pŕ̥-to-~, from *~per-~ ("forward, through"). The semantic development from "to push forward" to "to demand, claim" reflects the notion of asserting or advancing a claim. The PIE root *~per-~ is shared with the separable prefix ~vor-~ and with English ~forth~, ~forward~, and ~further~ — all sharing the spatial sense of moving ahead.

German learners must take care to distinguish ~fordern~ ("to demand") from the near-homophone ~fördern~ ("to promote, advance, support"). Despite their near-identical sound, they descend from the same Proto-Germanic root and once overlapped in meaning; the umlaut in ~fördern~ (from MHG ~vürdern~, OHG ~furdiren~, from PWGmc *~furþrijan~) marks its more specifically "forward-moving" sense, while ~fordern~ settled on the sense of making a forward claim or assertion. Cognate with English ~further~ (via the same PIE root), Dutch ~vorderen~.
en.formen: From Middle High German ~formen~, a denominative verb derived from the noun ~Form~, from Middle High German ~form~, borrowed from Latin ~forma~ ("shape, figure, appearance"). The ultimate origin of Latin ~forma~ is uncertain, but it may derive from Etruscan *~morma~, itself from Ancient Greek ~morphe~ ("shape, fashion, outward form"), with dissimilation of *~m-m~ to *~f-m~, as also seen in Latin ~formica~ ("ant") and ~formido~ ("scarecrow, ghost"). This Latin-to-Greek connection means that ~formen~ and English ~morph~ are distant cousins, both tracing back to the Greek concept of visible shape. The German ~-ieren~ suffix was not used here, unlike with many other Latin-derived verbs; instead German created a straightforward denominative from the noun, giving ~formen~ its clean, native-sounding conjugation. English ~form~ (both noun and verb) is a sibling borrowing from the same Latin source, arriving via Old French ~fourme~.
en.formulieren: Borrowed from French ~formuler~, itself from Latin ~fōrmula~ ("a small pattern or mold, form, rule, principle, method"), the diminutive of ~forma~ ("form, shape, figure"). The Latin ~forma~ is of uncertain origin; some scholars connect it to Greek ~morphḗ~ ("form, shape") via metathesis, while others derive it from an Etruscan source. German ~formulieren~ follows the standard ~-ieren~ suffix pattern for verbs borrowed from Romance languages — a productive pattern since the Middle High German period, when courtly culture imported hundreds of French verbs. The diminutive origin is telling: a ~formula~ was not just any form but a ~small~ form, a precise template — and ~formulieren~ preserves this sense of careful, deliberate shaping of language. The related noun ~Form~ entered German directly from French ~forme~, while ~Formel~ ("formula") came through Latin. English ~formulate~ traveled a parallel path, from the same Latin diminutive via French. Cognate with Dutch ~formuleren~, Italian ~formulare~, Spanish ~formular~.
en.fortsetzen:
Compound of ~fort-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjaną~ ("to set, to place"), a causative of *~sitjaną~ ("to sit"), from PIE *~sed-~ ("to sit"). Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~.
- ~fort-~: From MHG ~vort~, from OHG ~fort~ ("forth, onward"), from Proto-West Germanic *~furþ~, from Proto-Germanic *~furþ~ ("forth, forward"), from PIE *~per~ ("to cross; across, before"). As a verbal prefix, ~fort-~ conveys continuation or onward motion. Cognate with English ~forth~.

The compound literally means "to set forth" or "to place onward," yielding the modern sense "to continue." The derived noun ~Fortsetzung~ ("continuation, sequel") is familiar to anyone who has seen the words ~Fortsetzung folgt~ ("to be continued") at the end of a serialized story.
en.fotografieren: Derived from the noun ~Fotografie~, which comes ultimately from Ancient Greek ~phos~ (genitive ~photos~, "light") + ~grapho~ ("I write"), literally "light-writing." The verb was formed by adding the German ~-ieren~ suffix (from French ~-er~ via Latin ~-are~) to the stem ~fotograf-~. The word ~Fotografie~ itself may have been influenced by English ~photography~, which was coined around 1839, likely by Sir John Herschel, though the Franco-Brazilian inventor Hercules Florence also used the term independently. German originally spelled the word with ~Ph-~ as ~Photographieren~, faithfully reflecting the Greek ~phi~; the reformed spelling with ~F-~ was officially adopted in 1996 as part of the German orthographic reform. The shift from ~Ph~ to ~F~ remains one of the most visible and sometimes contested changes of that reform. English ~photograph~ and French ~photographier~ are direct cognates, all children of the same Greek compound.
en.fragen: From Middle High German ~vrāgen~, from Old High German ~frāgēn~, ~frahēn~/~frāhēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frāgēn~ (a denominative derived from *~frāgu~, "question"), from Proto-Germanic *~frēgō~ ("question"), from PIE *~preḱ-~ ("to ask"). The PIE root *~preḱ-~ is widespread and productive: it yields Latin ~precor~ ("I pray, beseech"), Sanskrit ~pṛcchāti~ ("asks"), and Persian ~porsīdan~ ("to ask"). The -~h~- spellings in Old High German (~frahēn~) reflect a retention of the Proto-Germanic laryngeal trace; the ~-g-~ forms ultimately prevailed. Cognate with Saterland Frisian ~fräigje~, Dutch ~vragen~, Low German ~fragen~, English ~frain~ (archaic, "to ask, inquire"), Swedish ~fråga~, Icelandic ~fregna~.
en.freigeben:
Compound of ~frei~ + ~geben~:

- ~frei~: From MHG ~vri~, from OHG ~fri~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fri~, from Proto-Germanic *~frijaz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~prey-~ ("to love, to free"). Cognate with English ~free~, Dutch ~vrij~, Danish ~fri~.
- ~geben~: From MHG ~geben~, from OHG ~geban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebaną~ ("to give"), from Proto-Indo-European *~gʰebʰ-~ ("to give"). Cognate with English ~give~ (via Norse), Dutch ~geven~.

The compound literally means "to give free" or "to release." Its modern uses range from "to release, to approve" (freeing a product or document for distribution) to "to take time off" (~einen Tag freigeben~). The PIE root *~prey-~ originally meant "to love" -- in the ancient Germanic world, a "free" person was one among the beloved kin group, as opposed to slaves and outsiders. Freedom was, at its root, belonging.
en.fressen: From Middle High German ~vrezzen~, from Old High German ~frezzan~ (also ~firezzan~), from Proto-West Germanic *~fraetan~. This is a compound of the prefix ~fr-~ (an unstressed form of ~ver-~) + ~essen~ ("to eat"). The base verb ~essen~ traces from MHG ~ezzen~, OHG ~ezzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~etan~, from Proto-Germanic *~etaną~, from PIE *~h₁ed-~ ("to eat"). Cognate with Dutch ~vreten~, English ~fret~ (in its archaic sense "to eat away, corrode"), Gothic ~fraitan~, and Swedish ~fräta~ ("to corrode"). The prefix ~ver-~/~fr-~ adds a sense of consuming completely or destructively, giving ~fressen~ the connotation of devouring or eating voraciously. In modern German, ~fressen~ is used exclusively for animals eating (or, pejoratively, for humans eating in a rude or greedy manner), while ~essen~ is reserved for polite human eating. This human/animal distinction is a uniquely German innovation not found in other Germanic languages. English ~fret~, though now meaning "to worry," preserves the original sense of gnawing or eating away; a fretted surface is one that has been corroded, and fretwork in architecture involves cutting or eating into wood.
en.freuen: From Middle High German ~vro(u)wen~, ~vre(u)wen~, from Old High German ~frewen~, ~frouwen~, a denominative derivation of Old High German ~fro~, ~frao~ ("glad"). The underlying adjective ~froh~ descends from MHG ~vro~, OHG ~fro~, from Proto-Germanic *~frawaz~ ("quick, glad"). Compare Dutch ~vrolijk~, whence also English ~frolic~.
en.fungieren:
From Latin ~fungor~ ("to perform, discharge, execute") + the German verb-forming suffix ~-ieren~. The Latin ~fungor~ descends from Proto-Italic *~fungor~, from Proto-Indo-European *~bʰewg-~ ("to enjoy, to be of use"), formed with a nasal infix (*~bʰu-ne-g-ti~).

The PIE root *~bʰewg-~ also produced Sanskrit ~bhunakti~ ("he enjoys") and possibly Latin ~fructus~ ("enjoyment, fruit, profit"), though the latter connection is debated. The semantic path from "to enjoy" to "to perform a function" passed through the intermediate sense of "to be occupied with" -- one first enjoys a role, then simply occupies it. In modern German, ~fungieren~ is a formal word meaning "to serve as, to act as," typically describing an official role or capacity.
en.funktionieren: Attested ca. 1800, from ~Funktion~ + the verbal suffix ~-ieren~, modeled on French ~fonctionner~. ~Funktion~ is a borrowing from Latin ~fūnctiō~ ("performance, execution"), itself from ~fungor~ ("to perform, execute, discharge"), from Proto-Italic *~fungōr~, from PIE *~bʰewg-~ ("to enjoy, be of use"). The ~-ieren~ suffix is the standard naturalizer for Latin and French loanverbs in German, entering via Old French contact in the 12th–13th century; it always produces weak verbs. This contrasts sharply with earlier Latin borrowings like ~schreiben~ (from Latin ~scrībō~), which arrived during the Roman period and were fully assimilated into the strong-verb system. Cognate with French ~fonctionner~, English ~function~, Dutch ~functioneren~. The deep PIE root *~bʰewg-~ also underlies English ~brook~ ("to tolerate, make use of").
en.fällen:
From Middle High German ~vellen~, from Old High German ~fellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fallijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallijaną~ ("to make fall"), a causative of *~fallaną~ ("to fall"), possibly from PIE *~h₃elh₁-~ ("to fall, to perish"), with a prefix *~h₂epo~ ("off, away"). Cognate with English ~fell~ (as in "to fell a tree"), Dutch ~vellen~.

The causative relationship between ~fallen~ ("to fall") and ~fällen~ ("to cause to fall, to fell") is a textbook example of the Proto-Germanic ~jan~-causative, exactly parallel to ~sitzen~/~setzen~ ("sit"/"set") and ~liegen~/~legen~ ("lie"/"lay"). The umlaut in ~fällen~ (a to ä) is the footprint of this ancient causative suffix, which contained a front vowel that pulled the root vowel forward. In modern German, ~fällen~ means both "to fell" (a tree) and "to render, to pass" (a judgment: ~ein Urteil fällen~). The judicial sense developed from the image of a decisive, downward stroke -- just as a tree is brought down with finality, so too is a verdict delivered. The PIE root *~h₃elh₁-~ may connect to Greek ~apollȳmi~ ("to destroy, to kill") and Latin ~aboleō~ ("to destroy"), though this etymology remains debated among specialists.
en.fördern: From Middle High German ~vürdern~, from Old High German ~furdiren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~furþrijan~ ("to move forward, to bring to the front"). A denominative of the archaic comparative adverb ~fürder~ ("further, forward"), itself from Proto-West Germanic *~forþar~, from PIE *~per-~ ("before, in front, first") with a comparative suffix. The modern spelling with ~ö~ — rather than the expected ~ü~ — arose through conflation with the related word ~vorder~ (whence modern ~fordern~, "to demand"), probably reinforced by Middle Low German ~vȫrder~ / ~vȫrderen~. The near-homophone pair ~fördern~ ("to promote, support") and ~fordern~ ("to demand") is a persistent pitfall even for native speakers and a nearly untranslatable distinction for learners. Cognate with English ~further~ (both as adverb and as verb "to further a cause"), Dutch ~vorderen~.
en.fügen: From Middle High German ~füegen~, ~fuogen~, from Old High German ~fuogen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fōgijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fōgijaną~, from PIE *~peh₂ḱ-~ ("to join, to attach, to fasten"). ~Fügen~ means "to join, to fit together" and, reflexively, "to submit, to comply" (~sich fügen~) — the person who ~sich fügt~ joins themselves to the arrangement of fate or authority. The noun ~Fügung~ means "decree of fate, providence" — events that have been joined together by a higher hand. ~Sich in sein Schicksal fügen~ ("to accept one's fate") is one of the great stoic phrases of the German language. The PIE root *~peh₂ḱ-~ connects ~fügen~ to a surprisingly wide family: Latin ~pāx~ ("peace" — literally a state of being joined, fastened together) yields English ~peace~, ~pact~, and ~pacify~; Latin ~pangō~ ("to fasten, to fix") gives ~compact~ and ~page~ (a sheet fastened in a book). The archaic English verb ~fay~ ("to fit closely, to join") is a direct Germanic cognate of ~fügen~, from Old English ~fēgan~, though it survives today only in specialized carpentry usage. Dutch ~voegen~ ("to join, to add") is the living cognate. The distance from PIE "to fasten" to German "to accept one's fate" traces a philosophical journey: what is fastened together by destiny cannot be unfastened by human will.
en.fühlen: From northern Middle High German ~vüelen~ (generally spelled ~vulen~), from Old High German ~fuolen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fōlijan~ ("to feel"). The ultimate etymology is uncertain; the root is presumably related to Old Norse ~falma~ ("to grope, feel; to be astonished"), and possibly a secondary causative of a strong verb *~falaną~, from PIE *~pelh₁-~ ("to stroke"). Possible cognates include Latin ~palpō~ ("to stroke, soothe") and Ancient Greek ~psēlaphāō~ ("to feel, grope"). Upper German dialects of Middle High German used ~enphinden~ instead; Modern German ~empfinden~ ("to sense, perceive") has been chiefly confined to higher register as a result, while ~fühlen~ dominates everyday speech. Cognate with Dutch ~voelen~, English ~feel~, Danish ~føle~.
en.führen: From Middle High German ~vüeren~, from Old High German ~fuoren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fōrijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fōrijaną~, the causative of *~faraną~ ("to go, travel"), from PIE *~per-~ ("going, passage"). The causative formation — "to make go," hence "to lead" — follows a productive Germanic pattern in which a long-vowel derivative of a strong verb creates a new weak verb with transitive meaning. Cognate with Dutch ~voeren~, English ~fare~ (from the base verb).
en.füllen: ~Füllen~ ("to fill") descends from Middle High German ~vüllen~, Old High German ~fullen~, and Proto-West Germanic *~fullijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fullijaną~ ("to make full"). This is the causative formation of *~fullaz~ ("full"), from Proto-Indo-European *~pl̥h₁nós~ ("full"). Cognates include Dutch ~vullen~, Danish ~fylde~, Swedish ~fylla~, and English ~fill~ — all from the identical Proto-Germanic causative. English ~fill~ and German ~füllen~ are thus perfect cognates: each is the causative of its native word for "full" (*~full~ / ~voll~), with the characteristic Proto-Germanic causative suffix *~-ijaną~. Latin ~plenus~ ("full") descends from the same PIE root, giving English ~plenty~, ~complete~, and ~replenish~. The simple parallel — "to full" meaning "to make full" — survived intact in both West Germanic branches.
en.fürchten:
From Middle High German ~vürhten~, from Old High German ~furihtan~, a denominative verb derived from the noun ~Furcht~ ("fear"). The noun ~Furcht~ descends from MHG ~vorht~, ~vorhte~, from OHG ~forhta~, from Proto-Germanic *~furhtō~ ("fear"), from PIE *~pr̥k-~ ("to fear"). The modern vowel ~-ü-~ reflects mutual influence between the verb (which had umlauted forms) and the noun (which originally had ~-o-~). Cognate with obsolete Dutch ~vrucht~ (still preserved in ~godsvrucht~, "fear of God"), Middle Low German ~vruchte~, and more distantly with English ~fright~, though that word derives from a different nominal stem (Proto-Germanic *~furhtį̄~). The Old English adjective ~forht~ ("afraid") is the closest English relative of ~Furcht~ itself.

The PIE root *~pr̥k-~ connects fear to a physical response. In Luther's Bible, ~fürchten~ famously appears in the phrase ~Fürchte dich nicht~ ("Fear not"), one of the most frequently repeated commands in Scripture. The reflexive construction ~sich fürchten~ ("to be afraid") is the standard colloquial form, while the transitive ~fürchten~ ("to fear something") is somewhat more literary. This split between transitive and reflexive mirrors a common pattern in German emotion verbs: the transitive form treats the emotion as directed outward, while the reflexive form internalizes it.
en.füttern:
From Middle High German ~vuotern~, from Old High German ~fuotiren~, derived from the noun ~fuotar~ ("fodder"), from Proto-Germanic *~fōdrą~ ("fodder"), from Proto-Indo-European *~peh2-~ ("to guard, graze, feed"). Equivalent to ~Futter~ + ~-n~. Cognate with English dialectal ~to fodder~, Dutch ~voeren~ ("to feed").

There is a second, homonymous ~füttern~ meaning "to line (a garment)," also from MHG ~vuotern~, but derived from a different (though probably distantly related) noun OHG ~fuotar~ ("sheath, wrapping, lining"). The PIE root *~peh2-~ ("to feed, to guard") is remarkably productive: it also gave rise to English ~food~, ~feed~, ~foster~, ~pasture~, and Latin ~pābulum~ ("food, fodder") and ~pāstor~ ("shepherd") -- the shepherd being, at root, the feeder.
en.garantieren:
From French ~garantir~, from Old French ~guarantir~ ("to protect"), from Old French ~guarant~ (whence modern French ~garant~, "guarantor"), from the present participle of an early Romance verb ~garir~ / ~guarir~, from Frankish *~warjan~ ("to protect, to warrant").

The Frankish root *~warjan~ is also the ancestor of English ~warrant~, ~warranty~, and ~ware~ (as in ~beware~) — all carrying the sense of protective vigilance. Old French ~guarantir~ also gave English ~guarantee~ (via Anglo-Norman). So German ~garantieren~ and English ~guarantee~ and ~warrant~ are all cousins, their shared Frankish ancestor having entered both languages via Norman French.
en.geben: From Middle High German ~gëben~, from Old High German ~gëban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebaną~, from PIE *~gʰebʰ-~ ("to give"). Cognate with Dutch ~geven~, English ~give~ (from Old Norse ~gefa~), Swedish ~giva~/~ge~, Danish ~give~.
en.gebieten:
From Middle High German ~gebieten~, from Old High German ~gibiotan~. Compound of ~ge-~ + ~bieten~:

- ~bieten~: From MHG ~bieten~, from OHG ~biotan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~beudan~, from Proto-Germanic *~beudaną~, from PIE *~bʰewdʰ-~ ("to be awake, aware"). Cognate with English ~bid~ (in the sense "to offer, to command"), Dutch ~bieden~, Swedish ~bjuda~.
- ~ge-~: From OHG ~gi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ga-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~ ("together, with"), from PIE *~ḱóm~ ("beside, near, by, with"). In this verb it adds a sense of collective authority or completive force.

Cognate with Old English ~ġebēodan~ and Old Saxon ~gibiodan~. The semantic journey from PIE "to be aware" through Proto-Germanic "to offer, to announce" to modern German "to command" reveals a fascinating power dynamic: awareness became announcement, and announcement became command. English ~bid~ preserves both the "offer" sense ("to bid at auction") and the "command" sense ("to bid someone do something"), having merged two originally separate Old English verbs (~biddan~ "to ask" and ~bēodan~ "to offer"). The related noun ~Gebot~ ("commandment") appears in the Ten Commandments (~die Zehn Gebote~), making this one of the most culturally resonant words in German.
en.gebrauchen:
From Old High German ~gibruhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gabrukan~. Compound of ~ge-~ + ~brauchen~:

- ~brauchen~: From MHG ~bruchen~, from OHG ~bruhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~brukan~ ("to use, employ, consume"), from Proto-Germanic *~brukaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~bʰruHg-~ ("to make use of, to enjoy"). Cognate with English ~brook~ (in the archaic sense "to tolerate, to make use of"), Dutch ~gebruiken~.
- ~ge-~: From OHG ~gi-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ga-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~ ("together, with"), from Proto-Indo-European *~kom~ ("beside, near, by, with"). This prefix here adds a sense of collective completion or thoroughness.

The ~ge-~ prefix intensifies the sense of using something to its full purpose. Dutch ~gebruiken~ is a precise cognate, showing the same prefix pattern. English ~brook~, now archaic outside the phrase "will not brook," once meant "to use, to enjoy" -- the same meaning that German ~brauchen~ has retained for centuries. The shift in English from "to use" to "to tolerate" represents a narrowing from active enjoyment to mere endurance.
en.gebären: From Middle High German ~gebërn~, from Old High German ~gibëran~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gaberan~, from Proto-Germanic *~gaberaną~, a compound of *~ga-~ + *~beraną~ ("to carry, bear"), the latter from PIE *~bʰéreti~, from *~bʰer-~ ("to carry, bear"). The prefix *~ga-~ (the ancestor of modern ~ge-~, from PIE *~ḱóm~, "beside, near, by, with") here marks completion or resultant state. The simplex *~bären~ ("to give birth") is extinct in standard Modern German; ~gebären~ is effectively the only survivor of the family in the birthing sense. English ~bear~ (to give birth) and Latin ~ferō~ ("I carry") are cognates through *~bʰer-~, making ~gebären~, English ~bear~, Latin ~ferō~, and Greek ~pherō~ all distant relatives. One of the oldest and most archaic verbs in the language — its strong ablaut class (class IV) and the now-defunct simplex root attest to enormous age. Cognate with English ~bear~, Dutch ~baren~, Gothic ~bairan~.
en.gefallen:
From Middle High German ~gevallen~, from Old High German ~gifallan~. Compound of ~ge-~ + ~fallen~:

- ~fallen~: From MHG ~vallen~, from OHG ~fallan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fallan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fallaną~, from PIE *~pōl-~ ("to fall"). A class VII strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~vallen~, English ~fall~, Danish ~falde~.
- ~ge-~: From MHG ~ge-~, from OHG ~gi-~, ~ga-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~, from PIE *~ḱóm~ ("beside, near, by, with"). Here the prefix marks completion or arrival at a state — the terminative aspect.

The semantic leap is striking: the root means "to fall" and the prefix marks completion or arrival, yet the compound means "to please." The likely bridge is the idiom of falling into favor — something that "falls" pleasingly to one's taste. Medieval German ~gevallen~ already carried this sense of "coming to please, meeting with approval." The dative construction ~mir gefällt~ ("it pleases me," literally "it falls to me") preserves this spatial metaphor of something landing agreeably within one's sphere. Cognate with Dutch ~gevallen~ ("to please, to fall").
en.gefährden:
From late Middle High German ~gevērden~, derived from the noun ~Gefahr~ ("danger"). The noun ~Gefahr~ descends from MHG ~gevāre~, ~gevære~, from OHG ~fāra~, from Proto-Germanic *~fērō~ ("danger, ambush"). The ~ge-~ prefix (from PIE *~ḱóm~, "beside, near, by, with") here serves as a collective intensifier on the noun, and the verb is formed by adding the ~-en~ suffix to the noun. An alternative derivation treats ~gefährden~ as a prefixed form of the now-obsolete verb ~fährden~ with the prefix ~ge-~.

Cognate with English ~fear~ and Dutch ~gevaar~ ("danger"). The connection between German ~Gefahr~ ("danger") and English ~fear~ is a striking example of semantic parallel: both derive from the same Proto-Germanic root *~fērō~, and both revolve around the concept of threat and peril. The Proto-Germanic word originally meant something like "ambush" or "hostile approach," which naturally split into the danger itself (~Gefahr~) and the emotion it provokes (~fear~). Old English ~fǣr~ meant "sudden danger, calamity" before shifting to the emotional response. The verb ~gefährden~ ("to endanger, to jeopardize") is thus etymologically something like "to put into a state of ambush."
en.gehen: From Middle High German ~gan~, ~gen~, from Old High German ~gan~, ~gen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gān~, from Proto-Germanic *~gāną~, from PIE *~ǵʰeh₁-~ ("to leave"). The conjugation ~gen~ instead of ~gan~ is of Bavarian origin. The ~-h-~ was introduced into the spelling by analogy with ~sehen~, in which it had become mute but was retained in spelling. Cognate with Dutch ~gaan~, Low German ~gaan~, ~gahn~, English ~go~, Swedish and Danish ~gå~.
en.gehören:
From Middle High German ~gehœren~ ("to listen to, obey"), from Old High German ~gihōrian~ ("to hearken to"), from Proto-West Germanic *~gahauʀijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~gahauzijaną~. Compound of ~ge-~ + ~hören~:

- ~hören~: From MHG ~hœren~, from OHG ~hōren~, from Proto-Germanic *~hauzijaną~, from PIE *~h₂ḱh₂owsyéti~ ("to be sharp-eared"), from *~h₂eḱ-~ ("sharp") + *~h₂ows-~ ("ear"). Cognate with Dutch ~horen~, English ~hear~, Danish ~høre~.
- ~ge-~: From MHG ~ge-~, from OHG ~gi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~, from PIE *~ḱóm~ ("beside, near, by, with"). This prefix often adds a collective or completive sense.

The semantic shift from "to listen to, obey" to "to belong to" reflects the medieval feudal sense of owing obedience: what one "hearkens to" is what one is subject to, and hence belongs to.
en.gelangen: From Middle High German ~gelangen~, from Old High German ~gilangōn~, synchronically analyzed in Modern German as an ablaut formation from ~gelingen~ ("to succeed"), though the historical relationship is with ~langen~ ("to reach"). ~Langen~ derives from Middle High German ~langen~, from Old High German ~langēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~langōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~langōną~, a denominative of *~langaz~ ("long"), from PIE *~dlongʰos~ ("long"). The prefix ~ge-~ (from PIE *~ḱóm~, "beside, near, by, with") marks completion or arrival — "to reach all the way to a destination." The semantic path is vivid: to "long" (reach, extend) is to span the distance between oneself and an endpoint, and ~gelangen~ captures that arrival. Cognate with Dutch ~geraken~ (parallel compound), English ~long~ (via *~langaz~).
en.geleiten:
From MHG ~geleiten~, from OHG ~gileitan~, ~gileiten~, from Proto-Germanic *~galaidijaną~. Compound of ~ge-~ + ~leiten~:

- ~leiten~: From MHG ~leiten~, from OHG ~leiten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laidijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~laidijaną~, a causative of *~līþaną~ ("to pass, to go by"), from PIE *~leyt-~ ("to go, depart, die"). Cognate with Old English ~lǣdan~ (whence English ~lead~), Old Saxon ~lēdian~, Old Norse ~leiða~.
- ~ge-~: From PIE *~ḱóm~ ("beside, near, by, with"). This inseparable prefix adds a sense of collective completion -- accompanying thoroughly.

~Geleiten~ means "to escort, to accompany, to conduct" -- to lead someone with care through or toward something. The ~ge-~ prefix intensifies the companionship aspect: not just leading, but accompanying throughout. The word carries a formal, even ceremonial tone: one ~geleitet~ a dignitary to their seat or the deceased to their grave (~das letzte Geleit~, "the last escort"). The derived noun ~Geleit~ ("escort, safe conduct") was a legal term in medieval Germany -- a ~Geleitbrief~ guaranteed safe passage through a lord's territory.
en.gelingen:
From Middle High German ~gelingen~, from Old High German ~gilingan~, a prefixed form of ~lingan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lingwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lingwaną~, from PIE *~h₁lengʷʰ-~ ("lightweight; swift"). Compound of ~ge-~ + the archaic simplex ~lingen~:

- ~lingen~: From OHG ~lingan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lingwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lingwaną~, from PIE *~h₁lengʷʰ-~ ("lightweight; swift"). The simplex ~lingen~ (meaning "to make progress, proceed, succeed") survived into Middle Low German but is no longer a standalone verb in Modern German.
- ~ge-~: From MHG ~ge-~, from OHG ~gi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~, from PIE *~ḱóm~ ("beside, near, by, with"). This prefix often adds a collective or completive sense.

The semantic development from "swift, lightweight" to "to proceed, succeed" reflects a conceptual link between ease of movement and successful outcome — what moves swiftly meets no resistance and therefore succeeds. ~Gelingen~ is an impersonal verb in Modern German: one says ~es gelingt mir~ ("it succeeds for me") rather than constructing a personal subject, a grammatical relic of the verb's impersonal origins. Cognate with Middle Low German ~lingen~, Yiddish ~געלינגען~ (~gelingen~).
en.gelten: From Middle High German ~gelten~, from Old High German ~geltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~geldaną~, possibly from PIE *~gʰeldʰ-~. The semantic range has narrowed over time: in Old High German, ~geltan~ meant "to pay, repay, be worth," while Modern German ~gelten~ centers on "to be valid, to count, to apply." Cognate with Dutch ~gelden~, English ~yield~ (from the same root, with a semantic shift from "paying" to "giving way"), Icelandic ~gjalda~.
en.genehmigen:
From ~genehm~ ("acceptable, agreeable") + ~-igen~ (verb-forming suffix). The adjective ~genehm~ descends from Middle High German ~genaeme~, ultimately from the root of ~nehmen~ ("to take"):

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nemen~, from OHG ~neman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due"). Cognate with English ~nim~ ("to take, filch"), Dutch ~nemen~.

The semantic thread is elegant: something ~genehm~ is literally "takeable" or "acceptable" -- that which one is willing to take. To ~genehmigen~ is therefore "to make acceptable," hence "to approve, to authorize." The PIE root *~nem-~ had the intriguing double meaning of both giving and taking what is owed, which survives in Greek ~nemein~ ("to distribute, to manage") and ~nomos~ ("law, custom") -- the source of English ~economy~, ~nomad~, and ~astronomy~. To approve something is, at its deepest root, to declare it lawfully distributed.
en.generieren: From Latin ~generāre~ ("to beget, to produce, to generate"), with the German ~-ieren~ suffix replacing the Latin infinitive ending. Latin ~generāre~ derives from ~genus~ ("birth, descent, origin, kind"), from PIE *~ǵenh₁-~ ("to beget, to produce, to give birth"). This PIE root is extraordinarily productive across Indo-European languages, yielding Latin ~genus~, ~gens~ ("clan"), and ~gignere~ ("to beget"), Greek ~genesis~ ("origin"), and English ~kin~, ~kind~, ~king~ (literally "son of the clan"), and ~gentle~ (via Old French from Latin ~gentilis~). German borrowed ~generieren~ as a learned term, and it has seen explosive growth in the age of computing: ~generierte Inhalte~ ("generated content") is now commonplace. Related German borrowings from the same Latin root include ~Generation~, ~Generator~, ~generativ~, and ~Genre~ (the last via French). The word ~genieren~ ("to embarrass"), despite its similar appearance, comes from a completely different French source and is a false friend.
en.genießen:
From Middle High German ~geniezen~, from Old High German ~giniozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ganeutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~neutaną~, from PIE *~newd-~ ("to seize, grasp, use"). Compound of ~ge-~ + the archaic simplex ~nießen~ ("to use, enjoy"):

- ~nießen~: From MHG ~nieȥen~, from OHG ~nioȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~neutaną~, from PIE *~newd-~ ("to seize, grasp, use").
- ~ge-~: From MHG ~ge-~, from OHG ~gi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~, from PIE *~ḱóm~ ("beside, near, by, with"). This prefix often adds a collective or completive sense.

The simplex ~nießen~ has largely vanished from Modern German, surviving only in derivatives: ~genießen~ ("to enjoy"), ~Nutznießer~ ("beneficiary"), and the legal term ~Nießbrauch~ ("usufruct," literally "use-enjoyment"). The PIE root *~newd-~ is the same one that underlies ~nutzen~ — both verbs trace to the same ancient idea of taking what is useful. German thus has two descendants of the same root: the everyday ~nutzen~ ("to use") and the more exalted ~genießen~ ("to enjoy"), the latter having acquired a sense of pleasurable fullness through the completive ~ge-~ prefix. Cognate with Dutch ~genieten~, Yiddish ~genisn~.
en.genügen: From Middle High German ~genüegen~, from Old High German ~ginuogen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ganōgijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~ganōgijaną~, from the adjective *~ganōgaz~ ("enough, sufficient"), ultimately from PIE *~h₂eh₂nóḱe~ ("he has reached, he has attained"), the perfective of *~h₂neḱ-~ ("to reach, to attain"). The semantic evolution is elegant: what is "enough" is what has been ~reached~ — the point of sufficiency. The collective prefix *~ga-~ (modern ~ge-~) intensifies the sense: not merely reached but ~fully~ reached. The related adjective ~genug~ ("enough") descends from the same Proto-Germanic adjective *~ganōgaz~. English ~enough~ is a direct cognate (from Old English ~genōg~, from the same source), as are Dutch ~genoeg~, Icelandic ~nog~, and Norwegian ~nok~. The verb ~genügen~ is formally a ~-jan- causative: it meant "to cause to be enough, to satisfy," though in modern German it is simply intransitive ("to suffice, to be enough"). The related adjective ~genügsam~ ("frugal, easily satisfied") preserves the older sense beautifully: someone who is ~genügsam~ is someone for whom the point of ~enough~ is easily reached. Cognate with Dutch ~genoegen~ ("to satisfy").
en.geraten:
From Middle High German ~gerāten~, from Old High German ~girātan~ ("to counsel, decide, turn towards, take care of"), from Proto-West Germanic *~garādan~, from Proto-Germanic *~garēdaną~ ("to have a plan for, to take forethought of, to purpose, to intend"), equivalent to ~ge-~ + ~raten~:

- ~raten~: From MHG ~rāten~, from OHG ~rātan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rādan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rēdaną~ ("to decide, to advise"), from PIE *~Hreh₁dʰ-~ ("to think, arrange").
- ~ge-~: From PIE *~ḱóm~ ("beside, near, by, with"). As a historical inseparable prefix, ~ge-~ originally conveyed collectivity or completion.

~Geraten~ means "to turn out, to succeed; to thrive; to get into (a state)" — senses that have drifted far from the root verb ~raten~ ("to advise, to guess"). The semantic path runs from "to counsel, decide" through "to arrange oneself, to find one's way" to "to turn out (well or badly)." The construction ~in etwas geraten~ ("to get into something") — ~in Schwierigkeiten geraten~ ("to get into trouble"), ~in Vergessenheit geraten~ ("to fall into oblivion"), ~außer Kontrolle geraten~ ("to get out of control") — is among the most productive in modern German. Cognate with Middle Low German ~gerâden~, Dutch ~geraden~ ("to form an assumption, guess"), and Gothic ~garēdan~ ("to provide for, intend"). English ~read~ is a distant cousin: it descends from the same PIE root *~Hreh₁dʰ-~, having shifted from "to advise, interpret" to "to interpret written symbols" — a semantic leap that German ~raten~ never made.
en.geschehen:
From Middle High German ~geschehen~, ~geschēn~, ~geschegen~, from Old High German ~giskehan~, ~scehan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skehan~ ("to spring, come up, emerge, happen"). In contrast to most German ~ge-~ verbs, the ~ge-~ in ~geschehen~ is not a separable or inseparable prefix added to an existing simplex — it fused into the root at the Proto-Germanic level, and no simplex *~schehen~ exists or has ever existed in German. The whole word is the root.

The Proto-West Germanic form derives from a root meaning "to spring, shoot, come quickly into being" — a vivid physical image for the sudden occurrence of events. The same root appears in Old High German ~scehan~ ("to happen"), which co-existed with the prefixed form before the ~ge-~ variant won out entirely. Cognate with Dutch ~geschieden~ ("to happen, to occur; to divorce" — the two senses having diverged from the same root). The related noun ~Geschehnis~ ("occurrence, event") and the participial use of ~das Geschehene~ ("what has happened, the done deed") preserve the verb's core sense of things irrevocably coming to pass.
en.gestalten:
Denominal verb from the noun ~Gestalt~ ("form, shape, figure") with the verbal suffix ~-en~. ~Gestalt~ itself descends from the 14th century as a participial substantive of ~stellen~ — literally "the placed, arranged thing" — from Middle High German ~gestalt~ (past participle used as a noun), from ~ge-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~, from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put").
- ~ge-~: From MHG ~ge-~, from OHG ~gi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~ga-~, from PIE *~ḱóm~ ("beside, near, by, with"). This prefix often adds a collective or completive sense.

German Wiktionary notes that while ~gestalten~ is structurally a conversion from the noun ~Gestalt~, an archaic simplex ~stalten~ once existed independently before vanishing. The noun ~Gestalt~ achieved global fame through Gestalt psychology (~Gestaltpsychologie~), founded in the early 20th century, which took the word's core sense — a whole that is more than the sum of its arranged parts — as its central insight. The verb ~gestalten~ thus means not merely "to form" but "to actively shape into a coherent whole." Cognate with Dutch ~gestalte~ (noun).
en.gestatten:
From Middle High German ~gestaten~, from Old High German ~gistatōn~, related to ~Statt~ ("place, stead"), from MHG ~stat~, from OHG ~stat~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stadi~, from Proto-Germanic *~stadiz~, from PIE *~stéh₂tis~ ("standing, place"). Cognate with English ~stead~.

~Gestatten~ means "to permit, to allow" -- a formal, even courtly alternative to ~erlauben~. The semantic path from "place" to "permission" runs through the idea of granting someone a place or a standing: to ~gestatten~ is, etymologically, to give someone a stead, a place where they may act. The prefix ~ge-~ here functions as a perfective marker (as in OHG ~gistatōn~), completing the act of granting. The polite formula ~Gestatten Sie?~ ("May I?" or "Do you permit?") is still common in formal German, spoken when entering a room or introducing oneself. English ~instead~ preserves the same root: "in (the) stead" means "in (the) place (of)."
en.gewinnen: From Middle High German ~gewinnen~, ~winnen~, from Old High German ~winnan~, ~giwinnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~winnan~, from Proto-Germanic *~winnaną~, from PIE *~wenh₁-~ ("to strive, wish, desire, love"). The PIE root *~wenh₁-~ conveys striving and desire, and its descendants branched widely: Latin ~venus~ ("love, desire") and the goddess ~Venus~ descend from the same root, as does English ~win~. The semantic shift from "to strive, toil" to "to win, gain" reflects how effort became identified with its reward — the same trajectory seen in archaic OHG ~winnan~ ("to struggle, suffer") moving toward Modern German ~gewinnen~ ("to win, to obtain"). The ~ge-~ prefix adds a collective or completive sense. Cognate with Dutch ~winnen~, Low German ~winnen~, Old English ~ġewinnan~ (English ~win~), Norwegian ~vinne~, and Swedish ~vinna~. Also related to English ~Venus~ and Latin ~venus~ via PIE *~wenh₁-~.
en.gewähren:
From Middle High German ~gewern~, from Old High German ~giwerēn~, ultimately from Proto-West Germanic *~warjan~ ("to defend, ward off"), from Proto-Germanic *~warjaną~, from an ~o~-grade ablaut of PIE *~wer-~ ("to heed, watch, guard"). The ~ge-~ prefix (from PIE *~ḱóm~, "beside, near, by, with") adds a completive or collective sense. Cognate with English ~guarantee~ and ~warrant~, both of which entered English via Old French but trace back to the same Frankish Germanic root.

The semantic evolution from "to defend" to "to grant" is a fascinating case of perspective shift: what was originally an act of protection (warding off threats) came to be understood from the beneficiary's perspective as an act of granting safety or permission. English ~ware~ ("cautious"), ~wary~, ~ward~, and ~guard~ (via French, from Frankish *~wardōn~) all descend from the same PIE root *~wer-~. Even ~garage~ is a distant relative, from French ~garer~ ("to shelter"), from Frankish *~warōn~. The modern German phrase ~Gewähr leisten~ ("to guarantee") preserves the original protective sense, while ~jemandem etwas gewähren~ ("to grant someone something") reflects the completed semantic shift from protection to permission.
en.gewährleisten: A compound of ~Gewähr~ ("guarantee, warranty") + ~leisten~ ("to accomplish, perform"). Not a prefixed verb but a verbal noun compound. ~Gewähr~ derives from Old High German (a related form to ~wahren~, "to safeguard"), ultimately connected to Proto-West Germanic *~warōn~ from Proto-Germanic *~warō~ ("care, attention"), possibly from PIE *~wer-~ ("to heed, watch, guard"). ~Leisten~ derives from Middle High German ~leisten~, from Old High German ~leisten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laistijan~, originally "to follow a track" (see *~laistaz~, "footprint"), evolving to "to accomplish, fulfill." The compound literally means "to perform a guarantee" — to carry out the act of warranting something. The noun ~Gewähr~ shares its root with ~wahr~ ("true") and ~wahren~ ("to safeguard"), a cluster of words about truthfulness, reliability, and protection. Cognate with Dutch ~garanderen~ (parallel meaning via different roots), English ~warranty~ (from a parallel Germanic source through Old French).
en.gewöhnen:
From Middle High German ~gewenen~, a byform of ~gewennen~, from Old High German ~giwennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gawannjan~, a prefixed form of *~wannjan~. The base verb traces to Proto-Germanic *~wanjaną~ ("to accustom"), from *~wanaz~ ("accustomed"), from PIE *~wenh₁-~ ("to love, to desire, to strive for"). Cognate with English ~wean~ (originally "to accustom," later narrowed to "to accustom a child away from the breast"), Dutch ~wennen~ or ~gewennen~. The prefix ~ge-~ (from PIE *~ḱóm~, "beside, near, by, with") adds a perfective or collective nuance.

The modern spelling with ~ö~ is a historical curiosity: it results from sporadic rounding of MHG /e/ near labial consonants, reinforced by association with related words that had original /o/, such as ~Gewohnheit~ ("habit"), ~gewohnt~ ("accustomed"), and ~gewöhnlich~ ("usual, ordinary"). Speakers reinterpreted ~gewöhnlich~ as a derivative of ~gewöhnen~, but historically it descends independently from MHG ~gewonlich~. The PIE root *~wenh₁-~ ("to love, to desire") connects ~gewöhnen~ to Latin ~Venus~ (goddess of love) and ~venia~ ("favor"), revealing that at the deepest level, becoming accustomed to something was once understood as growing to love it.
en.gießen:
From Middle High German ~giezen~, from Old High German ~giozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geutan~ ("to pour"), from Proto-Germanic *~geutaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~gʰewd-~ ("to pour").

Cognate with Dutch ~gieten~, Old Saxon ~giotan~, Old English ~geotan~, Old Norse ~gjota~ (whence Danish ~gyde~, Swedish ~gjuta~), Gothic ~giutan~. Further Indo-European cognates include Latin ~fundō~ ("to pour, to smelt"), Greek ~cheo~ ("to pour"), and Sanskrit ~juhoti~ ("he sacrifices" -- by pouring libations). English ~gush~ is a related form.

The sense of "pouring" metals and glass is attested since Old High German times through the participle ~gigozzan~ ("poured, cast"). This makes ~gießen~ one of the oldest technical terms still in daily use: a ~Gießkanne~ ("watering can") and a ~Gießerei~ ("foundry") both preserve the ancient act of controlled pouring. The connection to Latin ~fundō~ (whence English ~found~, as in founding metals, ~foundry~, and ~fusion~) shows that pouring and casting were among the earliest specialized activities to receive their own vocabulary across the Indo-European world.
en.glauben:
From Middle High German ~gelouben~, ~gelöuben~, from Old High German ~gilouben~, from Proto-West Germanic *~galaubijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~galaubijaną~, a compound of *~ga-~ + *~laubijaną~. The root *~laubijaną~ derives from *~laubō~ ("permission, leave"), from PIE *~lewbʰ-~ ("to like, desire, love"). The semantic trajectory runs from "to hold dear, to love" through "to grant leave, to permit" to "to believe, trust" — a striking path from affection to credence. The Upper German form with ~-au-~ (rather than the Central German umlauted variant) was reinforced by the related noun ~Glaube~ ("belief, faith"), while the umlaut survives in derived forms like ~gläubig~ ("devout, credulous") and ~abergläubisch~ ("superstitious"). The connection to love is preserved cross-linguistically: English ~believe~ descends from the same PIE root, and English ~love~ is a direct cognate of the *~lewbʰ-~ family. Cognate with Bavarian ~glaubn~, Dutch ~geloven~, Low German ~glöven~, Gothic ~galaubjan~, and English ~believe~.

The pairing of ~glauben~ and ~believe~ conceals a puzzle: both words share the same Proto-Germanic root *~laubijaną~, yet they wear different prefixes. German chose *~ga-~ (which became ~ge-~, then eroded to the ~g-~ in ~glauben~), while English chose *~bi-~ (which became ~be-~). Both prefixes were available in both languages — Old English had ~gelīefan~ (with ~ge-~) alongside ~belȳfan~ (with ~be-~), and it was ~belȳfan~ that won out. Old High German settled on ~gilouben~ (with ~gi-~, later ~ge-~), and that form prevailed, the prefix gradually wearing down: ~gilouben~ → ~gelouben~ → ~glouben~ → ~glauben~. The two prefixes were not even doing very different work: in early Germanic, both *~bi-~ and *~ga-~ could serve as perfective or intensifying markers on verbs, nudging the meaning from "to hold dear" toward the completed state of "to have come to trust." It is one of those cases where two sister dialects made an arbitrary lexical choice and then each committed to it for a millennium. The result is that German learners of English — and English learners of German — routinely fail to recognize ~glauben~ and ~believe~ as cognates, when in fact they are the same word dressed in different prefixes. Dutch ~geloven~ sided with German (keeping ~ge-~), Gothic ~galaubjan~ likewise, while the Scandinavian languages abandoned both forms in favor of unrelated words (~tro~ in Swedish, Danish, and Norwegian, from Old Norse ~trúa~, cognate with English ~true~ and ~trust~). So the modern Germanic languages offer three different solutions to the same semantic need: German and Dutch built "believe" from "love" with ~ge-~, English built it from "love" with ~be-~, and Scandinavian started over entirely with "trust." The erosion of *~ga-~ to ~g-~ in ~glauben~ is not an isolated accident — the same prefix met the same fate across the Germanic family, and English took the process one step further. English ~enough~ and German ~genug~ are cognates from Proto-Germanic *~ganōgaz~ (*~ga-~ + *~nōgaz~, from *~nahaną~, "to suffice"). In Old English the word was still ~genōg~, transparently prefixed; by Middle English it had weakened to ~inough~ and ~ynough~; modern ~enough~ retains only a silent-looking ~e-~ as the last ghost of the prefix. So where German wore *~ga-~ down to ~g-~ (~glauben~, ~genug~), English wore it all the way to nothing — or nearly nothing. (A tempting parallel is ~yesterday~/~gestern~, but that is a false friend: the ~ge-~ in ~gestern~ is not the prefix at all but part of the root, from PIE *~ǵʰdyes-~ + the comparative suffix *~-tero-~, cognate with Latin ~herī~ and Greek ~khthés~.)
en.gleichen:
From MHG ~gelīchen~, from OHG ~gilīhhon~, ~gilihhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~galīkōną~ ("to make alike, to resemble"), derived from *~galīkaz~ ("alike, similar"), a compound of *~ga-~ (collective prefix) + *~līkam~ ("body, form"), from PIE *~leyg-~ ("image, likeness, similarity"). Cognate with Dutch ~gelijken~, English ~like~ (both the adjective and the verb), Old English ~gelīcian~ ("to please") and ~gelīc~ ("alike"), Old Norse ~glíkr~, Danish ~ligne~.

~Gleichen~ means "to resemble, to be equal to" and is a strong verb (glich, geglichen), one of the few strong verbs derived from a denominal/deadjectival base. The adjective ~gleich~ ("equal, same, alike") lost its prefix ~ge-~ through phonetic erosion in Early New High German, but the verb preserved it as the initial ~gl-~. The underlying metaphor is illuminating: PIE *~leyg-~ meant "body, form" — to be alike was originally to share the same bodily form. This root produced English ~like~, ~alike~, ~likeness~, ~lich~ (a corpse — literally "a body"), the suffix ~-ly~ (originally "having the body/form of"), and ~ilk~ ("same kind"). The word ~like~ in English descends from the same Proto-Germanic *~galīkaz~, meaning that ~gleichen~ and ~like~ are doublets — siblings that traveled different phonetic paths from the same ancestor.
en.glänzen:
From Middle High German ~glenzen~, from Old High German ~glanzen~, from Proto-Germanic *~glantaz~ ("shining, bright"), from Proto-Indo-European *~gʰlond-o-~, from the root *~gʰlend-~ ("to shine, to gleam").

This verb belongs to a rich cluster of Germanic "gl-" words denoting light and vision: ~gleißen~ ("to glitter"), ~glimmen~ ("to glow"), ~glitzern~ ("to sparkle"), ~Glut~ ("embers"), and ~Glas~ ("glass," originally meaning "amber" or "something shiny"). English preserves the same pattern in ~gleam~, ~glitter~, ~glow~, ~gloss~, ~glint~, and ~glisten~. This "gl-" phonestheme -- where an initial consonant cluster carries consistent semantic associations without being a true morpheme -- is one of the most striking sound-meaning patterns in the Germanic languages.
en.gratulieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~grātulārī~ ("to wish joy, to congratulate"), from ~grātus~ ("pleasing, thankful"), from PIE *~gʷerH-~ ("to praise, to welcome"). Cognate with Latin ~grātia~ ("grace, favor"), whence English ~grace~, ~grateful~, ~gratitude~, and ~congratulate~.

~Gratulieren~ is used with the dative case: ~jemandem gratulieren~ ("to congratulate someone"), most commonly ~zum Geburtstag gratulieren~ ("to wish a happy birthday"). The PIE root *~gʷerH-~ ("to praise") is the source of an enormous family of "positivity" words: Latin ~grātia~ produced English ~grace~, ~gracious~, ~grateful~, ~gratis~ (originally "out of kindness"), and ~ingrate~; the same root likely produced Sanskrit ~gūrtí-~ ("praise, welcome").
en.greifen:
From Middle High German ~grîfen~, from Old High German ~grīfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grīpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grīpaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~gʰreyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). A class I strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~grijpen~, Low German ~griepen~, English ~gripe~, and Old Norse ~gripa~.

The PIE root *~gʰreyb-~ is attested in Baltic cognates as well: Lithuanian ~griẽbti~ ("to seize") and ~graibýti~ share the same ancestry, as does Latvian ~gribêt~ ("to wish, desire") — a striking semantic divergence in which the Baltic branch preserved a sense of striving or wanting from what was originally a physical act of grasping. German has developed ~greifen~ into a rich family of compounds: ~begreifen~ ("to comprehend," literally "to grasp around"), ~eingreifen~ ("to intervene," literally "to grasp in"), ~angreifen~ ("to attack, to corrode"), and ~ergreifen~ ("to seize, to move emotionally"). The idea that understanding is a form of grasping — ~begreifen~ — is one of the most striking conceptual metaphors in the language, and it is ancient. The ablaut series ~greift~/~griff~/~gegriffen~ follows the class I pattern, the same as ~reiten~ and ~schneiden~.
en.gründen:
Inherited from Middle High German ~gründen~, from Old High German ~grunten~ ("to fathom, to investigate"). A denominative verb formed from ~Grund~ ("ground, bottom, foundation") with derivational umlaut (u → ü):

~Grund~ traces to OHG ~grunt~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grundu~, from Proto-Germanic *~grunduz~, from PIE *~gʰr̥mtu-~. Cognate with West Frisian ~grûn~, Dutch ~grond~, and English ~ground~.

The OHG verb ~grunten~ meant "to fathom" — to sound the depth of water, to investigate the bottom of a matter. This sense still echoes in ~ergründen~ ("to fathom, to get to the bottom of something"). ~gründen~, by contrast, shifted from probing the ground to creating it: "to found, to establish" — to put something on a secure foundation. The same metaphor appears in English ~ground~ (as in "to ground one's argument"). The umlaut — ~Grund~ → ~gründen~ — is a hallmark of denominative weak verbs in German: it signals that the verb was derived from the noun rather than inherited directly. The noun ~Grund~ itself remains semantically rich: it serves simultaneously as "ground" (the earth underfoot), "bottom" (the floor of a lake), "reason" (the ground of an argument), and "foundation" (the basis of a building) — all the more remarkable because these senses converge in a single word that goes back to a PIE root simply meaning "earth, soil." Cognate with Dutch ~gronden~.
en.grüßen:
From Middle High German ~grüezen~, from Old High German ~gruozzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grōtijan~. The noun ~Gruß~ ("greeting") is a deverbal form from Middle High German ~gruoȥ~. Cognate with Dutch ~groeten~, West Frisian ~groetsje~, and English ~greet~ (from the same Proto-West Germanic root, without any prefix).

The semantic range of ~grüßen~ is narrower than its English cognate ~greet~: where English ~greet~ can mean both "to welcome" and "to react with emotion" (as in "the news was greeted with dismay"), German ~grüßen~ remains anchored in the act of salutation — a wave, a nod, a spoken hello. The compound ~begrüßen~ adds the ~be-~ prefix to make the greeting act outwardly directed and transitive: one ~grüßt~ in general, but one ~begrüßt~ a specific person or proposal. The Proto-West Germanic root *~grōtijan~ likely carried the original sense of physical approach or hailing — a greeting as an act of reaching toward someone.
en.gucken:
From Middle High German ~gücken~, ~guggen~ ("to look inquisitively, to peek, to peer"), of uncertain origin. Perhaps from a specialized use of MHG ~gücken~, ~gucken~ ("to cuckoo, to call or cry like a cuckoo"), from Old High German ~guckōn~, ~gukkōn~ ("to cuckoo") — the semantic link being the cuckoo's habit of poking its head out from cover, or the notion of peeping out curiously. The common pronunciation with initial ~k-~ (also spelled ~kucken~) is often attributed to influence from Low German ~kieken~ (from Middle Low German ~kīken~), though this pronunciation extends surprisingly far southward (compare Luxembourgish ~kucken~ and Dutch ~kijken~).

~Gucken~ is the everyday, colloquial word for "to look" in northern and central Germany, while southern German and Austrian varieties prefer ~schauen~. It rarely appears in formal writing, where ~sehen~, ~blicken~, or ~schauen~ take its place. Despite its informal register, ~gucken~ is enormously productive: ~angucken~ ("to look at"), ~zugucken~ ("to watch"), ~weggucken~ ("to look away"), ~durchgucken~ ("to look through"), ~Guckloch~ ("peephole"). The compound ~Kuckuck~ ("cuckoo") — if the etymological connection holds — would make ~gucken~ one of the few verbs potentially derived from a bird's call. The spelling debate between ~gucken~ (standard) and ~kucken~ (reflecting actual pronunciation) has generated more heat among German orthography enthusiasts than one might expect for a monosyllabic verb.
en.gönnen:
From MHG ~gunnen~, from OHG ~giunnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gaunnan~ ("to grant"), from the prefix *~ga-~ + *~unnan~, from Proto-Germanic *~unnaną~ ("to grant"), from PIE *~h₃neh₂-~ ("to enjoy"). Cognate with Dutch ~gunnen~, Yiddish ~ginen~. The PIE root also appears in Greek ~oninēmi~ ("to be of use, to help") and ~aponēto~ ("to have joy of something").

~Gönnen~ is one of those German words that English simply cannot translate with a single term. It means "to not begrudge someone something" — to be genuinely happy that another person has or enjoys something. ~Ich gönne dir den Erfolg~ means "I'm happy for your success" with no trace of envy.
