The negation ~missgönnen~ ("to begrudge, to envy") and the noun ~Missgunst~ ("envy, resentment") expose the default expectation: ~Gunst~ ("favor, goodwill") is the positive baseline. The reflexive ~sich etwas gönnen~ means "to treat oneself, to allow oneself a pleasure" — again, granting oneself permission to enjoy. The PIE root *~h₃neh₂-~ ("to enjoy") has been remarkably faithful to its original meaning across five millennia, even as the social dimension — the emphasis on non-envious generosity — is a distinctly Germanic innovation.
en.haben:
From Middle High German ~haben~, from Old High German ~habēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~habbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~habjaną~, from PIE *~keh₂p-~ ("to grasp, seize"). Cognate with Dutch ~hebben~, English ~have~, Danish ~have~, Old Norse ~hafa~, Gothic 𐌷𐌰𐌱𐌰𐌽 (~haban~).

~Haben~ and Latin ~habēō~ ("to have") are one of the most celebrated false-cognate pairs in Indo-European linguistics. Despite near-identical spelling, identical meaning, and centuries of scholarly confusion, the two words descend from entirely different PIE roots: ~haben~ from *~keh₂p-~ ("to grasp, seize") and ~habēō~ from *~gʰeh₁bʰ-~ ("to grab, take"). The resemblance is pure coincidence — the kind of accident that makes historical linguistics treacherous. ~Haben~'s actual Latin cousin is ~capiō~ ("I take, I seize"), the ancestor of English ~captive~, ~capture~, ~capable~, ~captain~, and ~accept~ (from ~ad-~ + ~capiō~). German ~akzeptieren~ thus descends from the same PIE root as ~haben~, via Latin rather than Germanic inheritance. The same root also gives German ~heben~ ("to lift") — to have and to heave are, at bottom, the same word: to grasp something is both to possess it and to lift it.

Perhaps the deepest irony is that Germanic and Romance languages independently chose their respective "have" verbs — etymologically unrelated — to build compound past tenses: German ~ich habe gesungen~ and French ~j'ai chanté~ are structurally identical constructions ("I have sung"), yet ~haben~ and ~avoir~ (from ~habēō~) share no common ancestor. This parallel development is a hallmark of the Western European ~Sprachbund~ — a linguistic area in which neighboring but unrelated languages converge on shared grammatical structures through centuries of contact. The ~Sprachbund~ also produced the definite article, the relative clause with a relative pronoun, and the distinction between ~sein~ and ~haben~ as perfect auxiliaries (compare French ~je suis allé~ / ~j'ai mangé~ with German ~ich bin gegangen~ / ~ich habe gegessen~) — all features that feel natural to European speakers but are typologically unusual worldwide. That two unrelated "have" verbs independently became the scaffolding for compound tenses across the Roman ~limes~ is a reminder that grammar can be borrowed just as readily as vocabulary.
en.haften:
From ~Haft~ + ~-en~. ~Haft~ descends from MHG ~haft~, ~hafte~ ("captivity"), from OHG ~haft~, ~hafta~, immediately cognate with Middle Dutch ~hachte~, Middle Low German ~hafte~, Old English ~hæft~ ("captive"), and further with Latin ~captus~ ("seized") and Old Irish ~cacht~ ("captive"). The Latin and Germanic forms both derive from PIE *~kh₂p-tó-~ (from *~keh₂p-~ "to seize, grasp"), making ~haften~ and English ~captive~ cognates from the same root.

~Haften~ means "to stick, adhere, cling" and "to be liable, to be responsible" -- two senses united by the "captivity" root. The "adhesion" sense developed from the idea of being held fast, clinging to a surface. The legal meaning is especially productive: ~haftbar~ ("liable"), ~Haftpflicht~ ("liability"), ~Haftpflichtversicherung~ ("liability insurance"), and ~Haft~ itself now primarily means "custody, imprisonment." That Latin ~captus~ and Germanic ~haft~ are cognates is beautifully confirmed by the same semantic range: both moved from "seized" to "held" to "bound."
en.halten: From Middle High German ~halten~, from Old High German ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldaną~. Cognate with Bavarian ~hoidn~, Low German ~holden~, Dutch ~houden~, English ~hold~, Danish ~holde~, Icelandic ~halda~, Swedish ~hålla~.
en.handeln: From Middle High German ~handeln~, from Old High German ~hantalōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~handulōn~, a denominative verb derived from Proto-Germanic *~handuz~ ("hand") with the frequentative suffix *~-lōną~. The underlying noun ~Hand~ traces from MHG ~hant~, from OHG ~hant~, from Proto-West Germanic *~handu~, from Proto-Germanic *~handuz~. The semantic development from "to handle (with the hand)" to "to act, deal, trade" reflects a concrete-to-abstract broadening typical of denominative verbs. Cognate with Dutch ~handelen~, English ~handle~, Danish ~handle~, Swedish ~handla~.
en.hassen:
From Middle High German ~hazzen~, from Old High German ~hazzon~, from Proto-Germanic *~hatāną~, a stative-fientive verb derived from *~hataz~ ("hate, hostility"), from Proto-Indo-European *~keh2d-~ ("grief, hatred").

Cognate with English ~hate~, Dutch ~haten~, Old Norse ~hata~, Gothic ~hatan~. The PIE root *~keh2d-~ also produced Greek ~kēdos~ ("care, grief, mourning") and Welsh ~cas~ ("hatred"). The Proto-Germanic noun *~hataz~ survives in Old English ~hete~ and is the source of English ~hate~. Interestingly, the Greek descendant ~kēdos~ developed in the opposite emotional direction, coming to mean "care, concern" and eventually "funeral rites" -- suggesting that the PIE root may have originally denoted intense emotional engagement rather than a specific negative emotion.
en.hauen:
From MHG ~houwen~, from OHG ~houwan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hauwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hawwaną~, from PIE *~kewh₂-~ ("to beat, hew, forge"). Cognate with English ~hew~.

~Hauen~ means "to hit, to strike, to chop, to hew" and is one of German's oldest action verbs. Originally a class VII strong verb (~hauen~, ~hieb~, ~gehauen~), it has partially weakened in colloquial speech, where ~haute~ competes with ~hieb~ as the past tense. In everyday German, ~hauen~ is the child's word for hitting: ~Er hat mich gehauen!~ ("He hit me!") is the universal playground complaint. But the verb also has a vigorous literary and technical life: ~in Stein hauen~ ("to carve in stone"), ~Holz hauen~ ("to chop wood"), ~hau ab!~ ("beat it!, get lost!"). The English cognate ~hew~ has retained the more dignified "chop, carve" meaning. The PIE root *~kewh₂-~ ("to beat, forge") also connects to Latin ~cūdere~ ("to strike, to forge").
en.heben:
From Middle High German ~heben~, ~heven~, from Old High German ~heffen~, ~heven~, from Proto-West Germanic *~habbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~habjaną~, from PIE *~keh₂p-~ ("to seize, take hold of"). A class VI strong verb; the past tense ~hob~ and participle ~gehoben~ descend from the strong ablaut paradigm. Cognate with English ~heave~, Dutch ~heffen~, Danish ~hæve~, Swedish ~häva~.

The curious shift from "to seize" (PIE) to "to lift" (Germanic) reflects the same conceptual move as in English ~heave~ — the act of taking hold of something is inseparable from lifting it. The same PIE root *~keh₂p-~ also produced Latin ~capiō~ ("I take, I seize"), giving English ~capture~, ~capable~, ~captain~, and — via the frequentative ~acceptāre~ — German ~akzeptieren~. So ~heben~ and ~akzeptieren~, despite looking nothing alike, are distant PIE cousins: both descend from the same ancient root meaning "to grasp." The derived noun ~Hebel~ ("lever") preserves the physical mechanics of the verb: a lever is the tool that lifts. Cognate with Dutch ~heffen~, English ~heave~.
en.heilen:
From Middle High German ~heilen~, from Old High German ~heilen~ (causative, "to make whole") and ~heilēn~ (inchoative, "to become whole"), from Proto-West Germanic *~hailijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hailijaną~ ("to heal, make whole, save"), derived from *~hailaz~ ("whole, healthy, uninjured"), from PIE *~kéh₂ilos~ ("healthy, whole"). Cognate with English ~heal~ (from OE ~hǣlan~), Dutch ~helen~, Old Norse ~heila~.

The adjective ~heil~ ("whole, sound") is the source of both ~heilen~ and English ~whole~ (from OE ~hāl~, with the same meaning). The ~w-~ in ~whole~ is a late English spelling addition; historically ~hale~ and ~whole~ are the same word. English ~hale~ ("robust, healthy") preserves the original form. The connection between wholeness and health runs deep: to ~heilen~ is to restore something to its ~heile~ ("whole") state. The word ~heilig~ ("holy") is also from ~heil~ -- to be holy is, etymologically, to be whole and inviolate.
en.heiraten:
From Middle High German ~hīrāten~, derived from MHG ~hīrāt~ ("marriage"), whence modern German ~Heirat~. The noun comes from Old High German ~hīrāt~ ("wedding, marriage"), from Proto-West Germanic *~hīwarād~. The compound literally meant something like "household counsel" or "household arrangement," from *~hīwa-~ ("household, family") + *~rād~ ("counsel, plan"). Cognate with Old English ~hīred~ ("family, household"), whence the English word ~hird~ (a Scandinavian royal retinue, re-borrowed through Old Norse ~hirð~ from Old English).

The etymology reveals that marriage in Germanic society was fundamentally a household arrangement -- not a matter of romantic love but of establishing a domestic partnership. The ~-rāt~ element is the same word that appears in ~Rat~ ("counsel, advice") and in the ~-rad~ that underlies names like ~Konrad~ ("bold counsel"). English ~hird~ -- a king's household guard -- preserves the same Proto-West Germanic compound but specialized toward the military-household meaning. Modern English has no direct cognate for ~heiraten~; instead it uses ~marry~, borrowed from French ~marier~, itself from Latin ~marītāre~. The German word thus preserves a distinctly Germanic conception of marriage as domestic establishment, while English adopted the Roman view.
en.heißen: From Middle High German ~heiȥen~, from Old High German ~heiȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haitan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haitaną~, tentatively from PIE *~ḱeyd-~ ("to call"). The original sense was "to command, order" (still preserved in the construction ~jemanden etwas heißen~), with the meaning "to be called, be named" developing from the passive use: "to be called (something)" became the dominant sense. Cognate with Bavarian ~haßn~, ~hoaßn~, Yiddish ~heysn~, Dutch ~heten~, Low German ~heten~, ~heiten~, Old Norse ~heita~, Gothic ~haitan~. Compare also English ~hest~, ~behest~.
en.helfen: From Middle High German ~hëlfen~, from Old High German ~hëlfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~helpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~helpaną~, from PIE *~ḱelb-~, *~ḱelp-~. A class III strong verb with the characteristic ~e~-~a~-~o~ ablaut pattern (~helfen~, ~half~, ~geholfen~). Cognate with Low German ~helpen~, Dutch ~helpen~, English ~help~, Danish ~hjælpe~, Norwegian ~hjelpe~.
en.heranziehen:
Compound of ~heran-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dewk-~ ("to pull, to lead"). Cognate with Old English ~teon~ ("to pull, to draw"), Latin ~ducere~ ("to lead"), and English ~tow~, ~tug~, and ~duke~ (via Latin).
- ~heran-~: From ~her~ ("hither, toward the speaker") + ~an~ ("on, at"). The element ~her~ descends from OHG ~hera~, from Proto-Germanic *~hiz~ ("this"), from Proto-Indo-European *~kis~ ("this, here"). The combined prefix conveys movement approaching the speaker.

The compound means "to draw near, to pull closer," and by extension "to consult, to cite" (drawing a source toward oneself) or "to train, to raise" (drawing a person upward toward competence). The PIE root *~dewk-~ has an astonishingly wide range of descendants: Latin ~ducere~ yielded ~duke~, ~duchess~, ~conduct~, ~produce~, ~educate~, and ~deduce~. To ~heranziehen~ a source in an argument is, etymologically, to lead it toward you.
en.herausfinden:
~heraus-~ + ~finden~, where ~heraus-~ is itself a compound of ~her-~ ("hither, toward the speaker") + ~aus-~ ("out"). Compound of ~her-~ + ~aus-~ + ~finden~:

- ~finden~: From MHG ~vinden~, from OHG ~findan~, from Proto-Germanic *~finþaną~, from PIE *~pent-~ ("to tread, to go, to find the way").
- ~her-~: From PIE *~ḱís~ ("this, here"). The prefix indicates direction toward the speaker.
- ~aus-~: From PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). The prefix adds a sense of outward extraction.

~Herausfinden~ means "to find out, to discover, to figure out." The double prefix creates a vivid spatial metaphor: to find something ~out~ and bring it ~here~ — to extract hidden knowledge and draw it toward oneself. Compare English ~find out~, which Wiktionary notes as a semantic parallel. The PIE root *~pent-~ ("to tread, to go") reveals an ancient connection between finding and walking: you ~find~ things by treading the ground, by going along a ~path~ (English ~path~ is from the same root). Latin ~pōns~ ("bridge," originally "path over water") is a cognate, as is Greek ~póntos~ ("sea" — the thing you cross). The double-prefix pattern (~heraus-~, ~herein-~, ~herüber-~) is characteristically German: it specifies both the origin point and the direction of motion in a single verb, achieving a spatial precision that English typically requires a phrasal verb to match.
en.herausgeben:
Compound of ~heraus-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~geben~, from OHG ~geban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebaną~ ("to give"), from Proto-Indo-European *~gʰebʰ-~ ("to give"). Cognate with English ~give~ (via Norse), Dutch ~geven~.
- ~heraus-~: From ~her~ ("hither, toward the speaker") + ~aus~ ("out"). The element ~her~ descends from OHG ~hera~, from Proto-Germanic *~hiz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~kis~ ("this, here"). The element ~aus~ descends from OHG ~uz~, from Proto-Germanic *~ut~, from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). Together they mean "out toward the speaker."

The compound literally means "to give out (toward the speaker)," yielding the modern senses of "to publish" (giving a book out to the world), "to return, hand over" (giving change back), and "to edit" (in the sense of preparing for publication). A ~Herausgeber~ is an editor or publisher. Dutch ~uitgeven~ ("to publish, to spend") shows the same semantic development with its own prefix ~uit-~ ("out"), confirming that the metaphor of publication as "giving out" runs deep in the Germanic languages.
en.herausstellen:
Compound of ~heraus-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from Proto-Indo-European *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand"). Cognate with English ~stall~ and ~still~, Dutch ~stellen~.
- ~heraus-~: From ~her~ ("hither, toward the speaker") + ~aus~ ("out"). The element ~her~ descends from OHG ~hera~, from Proto-Germanic *~hiz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~kis~ ("this, here"). The element ~aus~ descends from OHG ~uz~, from Proto-Germanic *~ut~, from Proto-Indo-European *~ud~ ("upwards, away, out"). Together they mean "out toward the speaker."

The compound literally means "to place out (toward the speaker)," hence "to expose, to highlight, to emphasize." The reflexive ~sich herausstellen~ means "to turn out, to become apparent" -- something places itself out into the open. This metaphor of truth as spatial emergence is ancient: what is hidden is inside, what is revealed comes out. The PIE root *~stel-~ also produced Greek ~stellein~ ("to set, to send") and Latin ~locus~ (from an older *~stlocus~), making ~herausstellen~ a distant cousin of English ~locate~.
en.herrschen:
From MHG ~hērschen~, ~hērsen~, from OHG ~hērrisōn~, ~hērisōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hairisōn~ ("to be strong, to dominate"). The surface form was remodeled after ~Herr~ already during the MHG period.

~Herr~ itself descends from MHG ~hērre~, OHG ~hēriro~ / ~hērro~, originally the comparative of ~hēr~ ("grey-haired, noble, venerable"), from Proto-Germanic *~hairaz~ ("grey"). Cognates: Dutch ~heer~, Old English ~hār~, English ~hoar~ and ~hoary~, Old Norse ~hárr~. The same root also gives modern German ~hehr~ ("sublime, noble") — so ~Herr~, ~herrschen~, ~herrlich~, and ~Herrschaft~ all descend from an ancestral word meaning "grey-haired," the authority of an elder embedded in each.
en.herstellen:
Compound of ~her-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb — the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~.
- ~her-~: From MHG ~her~, from OHG ~hera~, from Proto-Germanic *~hiri~ ("here, hither"), from PIE *~ḱís~ ("this, here"). This separable prefix conveys motion toward the speaker — "hither" — as opposed to ~hin-~, which conveys motion away.

~herstellen~ had a long first life as a purely spatial verb: in the 16th century it meant only "to place here, to set down toward the speaker" — the directional counterpart of ~hinstellen~ ("to place over there"). Its modern sense of "to manufacture, to produce" is surprisingly recent, developing only around 1900 as a shortening of ~wiederherstellen~ ("to restore, to reconstruct"). The original ~wiederherstellen~ meant to bring something back to its former state — to restore it to where it had been. When speakers began dropping the ~wieder-~ prefix and using ~herstellen~ alone, the sense broadened from "restore" to "make" in general. This makes ~herstellen~ one of the few common German verbs whose dominant modern meaning is the product of a late semantic compression rather than centuries of gradual development. The noun ~Hersteller~ ("manufacturer, producer") followed the verb into this new sense and is now indispensable in business and product labeling. Cognate with Dutch ~herstellen~ (in the narrower sense of "restore").
en.herunterladen:
A calque of English ~download~. Compound of ~herunter-~ ("down") + ~laden~ ("to load"):

- ~laden~: From MHG ~laden~ (strong verb), from OHG ~hladan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaþaną~ ("to load"), from PIE *~kleh₂-~ ("to put, lay out"). Cognate with English ~laden~ and ~lade~. Related to Lithuanian ~klóti~ ("to lay, spread, pave"), Russian ~klad~ ("treasure" -- something laid away).
- ~herunter-~: From ~her~ ("hither, toward the speaker") + ~unter~ ("under"), ultimately from PIE *~ḱís~ ("this, here") + *~n̥dʰér~ ("under").

~Herunterladen~ is a modern German calque (loan-translation) of English ~download~, mapping ~down-~ to ~herunter-~ and ~-load~ to ~-laden~. The strong conjugation of ~laden~ (~lädt~, ~lud~, ~geladen~) carries over, producing forms like ~er lädt herunter~, ~er lud herunter~, ~er hat heruntergeladen~. The competing anglicism ~downloaden~ also exists in colloquial German but is considered less standard. The informal contraction ~runterladen~ is common in everyday speech. The antonym ~hochladen~ ("to upload") follows the same calque pattern. These digital-age compounds demonstrate German's productive capacity for translating English technical vocabulary through native morphology rather than simply borrowing -- a tradition stretching back to seventeenth-century language purists like Philipp von Zesen.
en.hervorheben:
Compound of ~hervor-~ + ~heben~:

- ~heben~: From MHG ~heben~, ~heven~ (rarer ~heffen~), from OHG ~heffen~, ~heven~, from Proto-West Germanic *~habbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~habjaną~ ("to lift, to heave"), from PIE *~keh₂p-~ ("to seize, to grasp"). Cognate with English ~heave~, Dutch ~heffen~, Danish ~haeve~.
- ~hervor-~: A compound prefix from ~her-~ (from PIE *~ḱís~, "this (here)," conveying motion toward the speaker) + ~vor-~ (from PIE *~preh₂~, "before, in front"), together meaning "forth, out from."

~Hervorheben~ means "to emphasize, to highlight, to bring forth" — literally to lift something out into view. The noun ~Hervorhebung~ ("emphasis, highlighting") is used in typography, rhetoric, and everyday speech. The PIE root *~keh₂p-~ ("to seize, to grasp") connects lifting with grasping across the Indo-European branches: Latin ~capiō~ ("I seize") gave English ~capture~, ~capable~, ~accept~, and ~anticipate~. The related noun ~Hefe~ ("yeast") preserves the original fricative — yeast is literally "that which lifts" the dough.
en.hervorrufen:
Compound of ~hervor-~ + ~rufen~:

- ~rufen~: From MHG ~ruofen~, from OHG ~ruofan~, ~hruofan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hropan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hropaną~ ("to call, to cry out"). Cognate with Dutch ~roepen~ ("to call"), English dialectal ~roop~.
- ~hervor-~: From ~her~ ("hither, toward the speaker") + ~vor~ ("before, forward"). The element ~her~ descends from OHG ~hera~, from Proto-Germanic *~hiz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~kis~ ("this, here"). The element ~vor~ descends from OHG ~fora~, from Proto-Germanic *~fura~, from Proto-Indo-European *~per~ ("before, in front"). Together they convey "forward out toward the speaker."

The compound literally means "to call forth (toward the speaker)," hence "to cause, to evoke, to provoke." One ~ruft~ Gefühle ~hervor~ ("calls forth feelings") or ~ruft~ eine Reaktion ~hervor~ ("provokes a reaction"). The OHG form ~hruofan~ preserves an initial ~h-~ that was later lost, a common Germanic development (compare ~hring~ becoming ~Ring~). The metaphor of causation as summoning -- calling something out of hiding into existence -- is shared with English ~evoke~ (from Latin ~evocare~, "to call out") and ~provoke~ (from Latin ~provocare~, "to call forth").
en.hindern: From Middle High German ~hindern~, from Old High German ~hintaren~, from Proto-Germanic *~hindrōną~ ("to hinder"), derived from the adverb *~hinder~ ("back, behind"). The adverb itself likely comes from PIE *~ḱem-tér-~ ("down, back, through"), from *~ḱóm~ ("beside, near"), though an alternative theory derives it from *~ḱís~ ("this"). The semantic path from "behind" to "to obstruct" is intuitive: what is behind you blocks the way forward. Cognate with English ~hinder~, Dutch ~hinderen~. The related Latin ~contrā~ ("against") descends from the same PIE root, making ~hindern~ a distant cousin of English ~counter~ and ~contrary~.
en.hinnehmen:
From Middle High German ~hinnemen~, from Old High German ~hinaneman~. Compound of ~hin-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nëmen~, from OHG ~nëman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemaną~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
- ~hin-~: From Old High German ~hina~, from Proto-Germanic *~hina~ ("away from here, thither"), from PIE *~ḱís~ ("this, here"). This directional prefix indicates motion away from the speaker.

The literal sense is "to take away," but the modern meaning has shifted to "to accept, to put up with" -- often with a connotation of reluctant resignation. The PIE root *~nem-~ is the ancestor of Greek ~nemein~ ("to distribute"), which gave rise to ~Nemesis~, the goddess who distributed what was due. English ~nimble~ is a cognate through the same Germanic root. Cognate with Dutch ~nemen~, Danish ~nemme~ ("to learn, grasp").
en.hinterfragen:
A modern compound of ~hinter-~ + ~fragen~:

- ~fragen~: From MHG ~vrāgen~, from OHG ~frāgēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frāgēn~. Cognate with Dutch ~vragen~, Swedish ~fråga~, English dialectal ~frain~ ("to inquire").
- ~hinter-~: From Old High German ~hintar~ ("behind"), from Proto-Germanic *~hinder~, ultimately from PIE *~ḱís~ ("this, here") via a comparative formation. As an inseparable verbal prefix, ~hinter-~ adds a sense of going behind or beneath the surface.

The verb ~hinterfragen~ means "to question critically, to scrutinize" -- literally to ask behind something, probing beyond the surface. It is a relatively recent coinage, entering standard German in the twentieth century as part of intellectual and journalistic discourse. Unlike the simple ~fragen~ ("to ask"), ~hinterfragen~ implies skepticism and analytical depth.
en.hinterlassen:
Compound of ~hinter-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lāzen~, ~lān~, from OHG ~lāȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lātan~ ("to let, to allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lētaną~ ("to leave behind, to allow"), from PIE *~leh₁d-~ ("to let, to leave behind").
- ~hinter-~: Meaning "behind," from Proto-Germanic *~hinder~ ("behind"), from PIE *~ḱom-tero-~ ("on this side"), a comparative formation from *~ḱom~ ("beside, near"). As an inseparable prefix, ~hinter-~ conveys leaving something in one's wake or behind one.

~Hinterlassen~ means "to leave behind, to bequeath" — ~ein Erbe hinterlassen~ ("to leave an inheritance"), ~Spuren hinterlassen~ ("to leave traces"), ~eine Nachricht hinterlassen~ ("to leave a message"). The inseparable prefix ~hinter-~ distinguishes this from the separable ~hinter etwas lassen~ (which would mean "to leave behind something" in a more literal, spatial sense). The PIE root *~leh₁d-~ ("to leave behind") makes ~hinterlassen~ doubly emphatic about departure: both the prefix and the root verb encode the concept of leaving. Cognate with English ~let~ (from the same Germanic root) and Dutch ~nalaten~ ("to bequeath," using a different prefix but the same root verb). The noun ~Hinterlassenschaft~ ("estate, legacy") preserves the formal, testamentary register of the verb.
en.hinweisen:
~hin-~ + ~weisen~. Compound of ~hin-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wīsen~, from OHG ~wīsen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wīsijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wīsijaną~, a denominative of *~wīsaz~ ("wise, knowing"), from PIE *~weyd-so-s~ ("knowledgeable"), from *~weyd-~ ("to see, know").
- ~hin-~: From PIE *~ḱís~ ("this, here"), with a directional shift away from the speaker. ~Hin-~ indicates movement or pointing away from the speaker's position — toward some other place or thing.

~Hinweisen~ means "to point out, to indicate, to draw attention to." The prefix ~hin-~ directs the act of showing away from the speaker toward the referent — one ~weist hin auf~ something by directing another's knowledge or gaze toward it. The noun ~Hinweis~ ("hint, pointer, reference, note") is ubiquitous in German signage, footnotes, and official communication. The underlying PIE root *~weyd-~ ("to see, know") is the same root that gives Latin ~videō~ ("I see"), Sanskrit ~veda~ ("I know"), and English ~wise~, ~wit~, and ~wizard~. To point out is, at the deepest etymological layer, to cause someone to see — a remarkably literal metaphor. Cognate with Dutch ~aanwijzen~.
en.hinzufügen:
~hin-~ + ~zu-~ + ~fügen~. A double-prefix compound:

- ~fügen~: From MHG ~füegen~, ~fuogen~, from OHG ~fuogen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fōgijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fōgijaną~ ("to join, fit together"), from PIE *~peh₂ḱ-~ ("to join, attach, fasten").
- ~hin-~: From PIE *~ḱís~ ("this, here"). The prefix indicates motion away from the speaker.
- ~zu-~: From PIE *~doh₁~ ("to"). The prefix adds directionality toward a goal or target.

~Hinzufügen~ means "to add" — literally "to join (something) toward (a destination) away from here." The double prefix ~hinzu-~ creates a vivid spatial image: something is fitted (~fügen~) in the direction of (~zu-~) a destination that is away from the speaker (~hin-~). The root verb ~fügen~ itself is related to English ~fay~ (an archaic word meaning "to fit closely") and Dutch ~voegen~ ("to join, grout"). The PIE root *~peh₂ḱ-~ ("to fasten") also gave Latin ~pāx~ ("peace" — originally "a binding agreement") and ~pangere~ ("to fasten, fix"), making ~hinzufügen~ a distant relative of English ~peace~ and ~pact~. In computing, ~hinzufügen~ has become the standard German equivalent of "add" — the button you click to add items to a cart or entries to a list.
en.hochladen:
A modern compound of ~hoch~ + ~laden~, and a calque of English ~upload~:

- ~hoch~: From Middle High German ~hōch~, from Old High German ~hōh~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hauh~, from Proto-Germanic *~hauhaz~, from PIE *~kewk-~ ("to bend; crooked"). The semantic shift from "bent, arched" to "high" may reflect the image of an arching peak.
- ~laden~: From MHG ~laden~ (strong verb), from OHG ~hladan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaþan~. Cognate with English ~laden~ and ~load~.

This is a textbook example of a loan translation: German speakers needed a word for the digital concept of uploading and calqued it directly from English ~upload~ by substituting ~hoch~ ("up") + ~laden~ ("to load"). Its counterpart ~herunterladen~ ("to download") is calqued the same way. The computing sense dates to the 1990s, but ~laden~ itself has been loading cargo since the early medieval period.
en.hoffen:
From Middle High German ~hoffen~ (first attested 12th century), from northern Old High German *~hoffōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hopōn~. The ultimate pre-Germanic origin is uncertain. The word's early attestation is exclusively in northern and central German dialects; it was absent from Upper German, where it only became established in later Middle High German, gradually displacing the now-obsolete ~gedingen~ ("to have hope, trust"). Some scholars have proposed that all continental Germanic forms derive from Old English ~hopian~, introduced to the continent by Anglo-Saxon missionaries — which would make ~hoffen~ a remarkably late addition to the German vocabulary, arriving through religious rather than inherited channels.

Whether missionary loan or parallel inheritance, the word displaced a native competitor (~gedingen~, related to ~Ding~, "thing, assembly") and established itself as the sole common word for hope in German. Its exact PIE ancestry remains debated, with no secure cognate outside Germanic. Cognate with Dutch ~hopen~, English ~hope~, Danish ~håbe~, Swedish ~hoppas~, Yiddish ~האָפֿן~ (~hofn~).
en.holen: From Middle High German ~holen~, ~holn~, from Old High German ~holōn~, ~holēn~ ("to fetch, get, draw"), from Proto-West Germanic *~holōn~, possibly from PIE *~kelh₁-~ ("to call, cry, summon"). The semantic development would be from "to summon, call (someone to come)" → "to go and fetch, retrieve." The connection to PIE *~kelh₁-~ is uncertain but paralleled by similar "call → fetch" developments in other languages. Wiktionary directs readers to English ~haul~ for related forms. The cognate English word ~haul~ (from Old French ~haler~, of Germanic origin) shares the sense of pulling something toward oneself, pointing to an underlying idea of directed movement. Cognate with Low German ~halen~, Dutch ~halen~ ("to fetch, get"), English ~haul~ (via French).
en.hängen:
Modern German ~hängen~ is the result of the gradual merger of three historically distinct Old High German verbs, all related in meaning and ancestry:

1. OHG ~hāhan~ (strong, chiefly transitive), past ~hieng~, participle ~gehangan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hą̄han~, from Proto-Germanic *~hanhaną~ — the original strong verb meaning "to be suspended."
2. OHG ~hangēn~ (weak, intransitive) — a related form also meaning "to hang."
3. OHG ~hengen~ (weak, transitive/causative), from Proto-West Germanic *~hangijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hangijaną~ — a causative formation meaning "to hang something, to cause to hang."

Verbs 1 and 2 merged first, combining the present stem ~hang-~ from ~hangēn~ with the strong past forms (~hing~, ~gehangen~) of ~hāhan~. This merger is first attested in the north and spread southward through Middle Low German and into Central German during the 14th century. The causative verb 3 — ancestor of modern transitive ~hängen~ — was kept formally separate into the 20th century, at least by prescriptive grammarians, but colloquial usage steadily conflated all three. Today the distinction is upheld primarily in the past tense: intransitive ~hing~/~gehangen~ ("the coat was hanging") versus transitive ~hängte~/~gehängt~ ("she hung the coat"). This mirrors the English ~lie~/~lay~ distinction, which likewise collapses in casual speech. Cognate with Dutch ~hangen~, English ~hang~.
en.hören: From Middle High German ~hœren~, from Old High German ~hōren~, from Proto-Germanic *~hauzijaną~, from PIE *~h₂ḱh₂owsyéti~ ("to be sharp-eared"), from *~h₂eḱ-~ ("sharp") + *~h₂ows-~ ("ear"). The PIE root is a compound construction meaning literally "to have sharp ears" — a vivid image of acute hearing built into the very fabric of the word. Cognate with Dutch ~horen~, English ~hear~, Danish ~høre~, Gothic ~hausjan~.
en.identifizieren: Borrowed from French ~identifier~, from Medieval Latin ~identificāre~, a compound of Latin ~identicus~ ("identical") + ~faciō~ ("to make, to do"). ~Identicus~ derives from Late Latin ~identitās~ ("sameness"), from Latin ~idem~ ("the same"), from the demonstrative pronoun ~is~ ("he, it") + ~-dem~ (an emphatic particle), ultimately from PIE *~ey-~ ("he, that"). The ~-fizieren~ ending reflects the standard German adaptation of Latin ~-ficāre~ (from ~faciō~, from PIE *~dʰeh₁-~ "to put, to place, to make"). To ~identifizieren~ is thus, etymologically, "to make the same" — to establish that two things are one. The verb entered German during the Enlightenment period, when scientific taxonomy demanded precise language for classification. The reflexive form ~sich identifizieren mit~ ("to identify with") extends the etymology beautifully: to declare oneself ~the same as~ another. Cognate with Dutch ~identificeren~, English ~identify~, Spanish ~identificar~.
en.ignorieren:
From Latin ~ignōrō~ ("I do not know, I disregard"), with the German suffix ~-ieren~. The Latin verb ultimately derives from PIE *~ǵneh₃-~ ("to know, to recognize"), with the Latin privative prefix ~in-~ (becoming ~ig-~ before ~n~) negating the root. The derivation within Latin is disputed: it may be a denominative from ~ignārus~ ("ignorant"), or it may have shifted from the ~-ā-~ to ~-ō-~ stem under the influence of ~ignōtus~ ("unknown").

The PIE root *~ǵneh₃-~ is one of the most productive in all of Indo-European, giving Latin ~nōscō~ and ~cognōscō~ ("I learn, I know"), Greek ~gignōskō~ (whence English ~diagnosis~ and ~prognosis~), and the Germanic family including English ~know~, ~can~ (originally "to know how"), ~ken~ (Scottish "to know"), ~cunning~ (originally "knowing"), and German ~kennen~ ("to know a person") and ~können~ ("to be able"). The irony is that ~ignorieren~ -- a word about not knowing -- is built from one of the richest "knowing" roots in human language. German borrowed this verb from Latin in the 16th century, and it coexists with the native German synonym ~missachten~ ("to disregard"). In modern usage, ~ignorieren~ specifically implies deliberately choosing not to acknowledge something, whereas ~nicht wissen~ is simply not knowing -- a crucial distinction that the Latin original also made.
en.informieren:
Borrowed from French ~informer~, from Latin ~īnfōrmō~ ("to shape, form, train, instruct"), a compound of Latin ~in-~ ("into") + ~fōrma~ ("form, shape"). The Latin ~fōrma~ has an uncertain etymology, possibly borrowed from Etruscan, itself perhaps from Greek ~morphē~ ("shape, form"), with a dissimilation *~m~-~m~ > *~f~-~m~.

The original Latin sense of ~īnfōrmō~ was literal and physical: "to shape, give form to." The semantic shift from "to give form to" to "to instruct, inform" reflects the metaphor of education as shaping the mind — the same metaphor underlying English ~inform~ and ~formation~. German received the verb through French during the period when -~ieren~ borrowings were flooding the language (roughly 12th–17th centuries via Old French and later French contact), giving it the characteristic weak -~ieren~ conjugation that marks Latin-French loanwords in German. Like ~funktionieren~, ~präsentieren~, and ~konjugieren~, it entered the language as a fully formed loanword rather than being built from native roots. Cognate with English ~inform~, French ~informer~, Spanish ~informar~, Italian ~informare~.
en.inspirieren:
Borrowed in the sixteenth century from Latin ~īnspīrāre~ ("to breathe into, to blow upon"), from ~in-~ ("into") + ~spīrāre~ ("to breathe"). The Latin verb's earliest sense was literally "to breathe into," used of divine breath animating creation. Related to Latin ~spīritus~ ("breath, spirit"), whence German ~Spiritus~ and English ~spirit~. Also related to German ~konspirieren~ ("to conspire," literally "to breathe together") and ~transpirieren~ ("to transpire").

The theological sense -- God breathing life or creative power into a person -- was the primary meaning when the word entered German. The secular sense "to give someone a creative idea" developed later, preserving the metaphor of something being breathed into the mind from outside. English ~inspire~ arrived via Old French from the same Latin source.
en.installieren: ~Installieren~ is a borrowing from Medieval Latin ~installāre~ ("to place in a stall, to install in an office — especially to seat a canon in his choir stall"), composed of Latin ~in-~ ("in, into") and ~stallāre~, derived from ~stallus~ ("stall, seat, choir stall"). ~Stallus~ descends from Proto-Germanic *~stallaz~ ("standing place"), from Proto-Indo-European *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand still"). That PIE root is the ancestor of English ~stall~, ~stale~, and ~install~ itself (via the same Latin path), and of German ~Stall~ ("stable, livestock pen"). The original ecclesiastical meaning — "to seat a newly appointed canon in his stall in the choir" — survives in English "to install someone in office." The jump to technology ("to install software") is a 20th-century extension of the same idea: placing something in its proper location so it can function.
en.integrieren: ~Integrieren~ is borrowed from Latin ~integrāre~ ("to make whole, renew, restore"), from ~integer~ ("untouched, whole, sound, honest"), from Proto-Italic *~əntagros~, built from *~n̥-~ (the PIE negative prefix) + *~teh₂g-~ ("to touch"). That touching root also produced Latin ~tangere~ ("to touch"), ~tactus~ ("touch"), and English ~tangent~, ~tact~, ~contact~, and ~contagion~. So ~integer~ literally means "not-touched" — hence "whole, intact, uncorrupted." The noun ~Integrität~ and the mathematical term ~Integral~ share this ancestry: an integer is a number left whole, not cut into fractions. English ~entire~ comes from the same root via Old French. A cultural note: in German, ~integrieren~ carries a strong second meaning of social integration — incorporating people into a community — making its etymology ("making whole") feel especially apt.
en.interessieren: Borrowed in the 17th century from French ~intéresser~ ("to interest, to concern"), from Latin ~interesse~ ("to be between, to be present, to matter"), a compound of ~inter-~ ("between, among") + ~esse~ ("to be"), from PIE *~h₁es-~ ("to be"). The semantic leap from "to be between" to "to matter, to concern" reflects the Roman legal and financial use of ~interest~ (third-person singular present of ~interesse~) as a formula meaning "it makes a difference" — and from there, the noun ~interest~ in the financial sense of a stake or share in something. The abstract ~inter-esse~, "being-between," captures the idea of having a stake in something — of standing between pure detachment and full possession. Cognate with English ~interest~, French ~intéresser~, Spanish ~interesar~, Dutch ~interesseren~.
en.interpretieren: From French ~interpréter~, from Old French ~enterpreter~, from Latin ~interpretārī~ ("to explain, expound, interpret"), a deponent verb derived from the noun ~interpres~ ("agent, intermediary, interpreter"). Latin ~interpres~ is composed of ~inter~ ("between") and a derivation from PIE *~per-~ ("to sell, to traffic in; forward, through"), yielding an original sense of "one who goes between" -- a broker, a middleman, a go-between. The proposed reconstruction traces through *~enter-poro-~ ("going between") to *~enterpress~ via regular sound changes. German adopted the verb through French with the ~-ieren~ suffix, the standard pathway for Romance and Latin verbs into German. Cognate with English ~interpret~, French ~interpréter~, Italian ~interpretare~. The word ~Dolmetscher~ ("interpreter" in the oral-translation sense) is a fascinating contrast: it entered German from Turkic ~dilmaç~ via Hungarian, showing that German has two words for "interpreter" from opposite ends of the ancient world. ~Interpretieren~ is used for textual, musical, and legal interpretation, while ~dolmetschen~ is reserved for oral translation between languages.
en.investieren: ~Investieren~ is borrowed from Latin ~investīre~ ("to clothe, to surround, to envelop"), from ~in-~ ("in, into") + ~vestīre~ ("to clothe"), from ~vestis~ ("garment, clothing"), from Proto-Italic *~westis~, from Proto-Indo-European *~wes-~ ("to be dressed, wear clothes"). That PIE root is the source of English ~wear~, Gothic ~wasti~ ("garment"), and Old Armenian ~զgest~. The financial sense of ~investieren~ — to put capital to work — arose in 17th-century Italian banking: ~investire~ was used to describe "dressing" money in productive assets, cloaking capital in ventures that would return profit. The metaphor traveled from Italian into French ~investir~ and then into English ~invest~ and German ~investieren~. The original Latin ~investīre~ also gave English ~investiture~ (the ceremony of cloaking someone in the robes of an office) and ~investigate~ — literally "to trace footprints," via a different Latin compound.
en.jagen:
From MHG ~jagen~ ("to hurry, drive, pursue, hunt"), from OHG ~jagōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~jagōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~jakkōną~. The Proto-Germanic form derives from a ~nā~-iterative formation, likely rebuilt from PIE *~h₁eyǵʰ-~ ("to strive, chase"). Attested since the eighth century. Further pre-Germanic etymology is obscure.

~Jagen~ means "to hunt, to chase, to drive" and is one of the oldest action verbs in the Germanic lexicon, reflecting the centrality of hunting to early Germanic society. The noun ~Jagd~ ("hunt") derives from the same root and appears in countless compounds: ~Jagdhund~ ("hunting dog"), ~Jagdhorn~ ("hunting horn"), ~Jagdrevier~ ("hunting grounds"). The verb has developed rich figurative uses: ~jemanden aus dem Amt jagen~ ("to chase someone out of office"), ~einen Schauer über den Rücken jagen~ ("to send a shiver down someone's spine"). The compound ~Jäger~ ("hunter") is also famous as a military term -- ~Jäger~ units were light infantry in the Prussian and Austrian armies, originally recruited from professional hunters and gamekeepers.
en.kaufen: From Middle High German ~koufen~, from Old High German ~koufōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kaupōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~kaupōną~, a denominative verb derived from *~kaupô~ ("merchant, innkeeper"). *~kaupô~ is a loanword from Latin ~caupō~ ("innkeeper, shopkeeper, petty trader"), itself likely a common Mediterranean trade word. This Latin borrowing entered Germanic during the period of Roman-Germanic commercial contact along the Rhine and Danube frontiers. The word spread widely: Old English ~cēapian~ ("to trade, buy") yielded ~cheap~ — originally meaning "bargain, price, trade" (as in the London market district ~Cheapside~) — and the place-name element ~Chipping-~ appears in English market towns (Chipping Norton, Chipping Campden). The Danish capital ~København~ ("Copenhagen") literally means "merchants' harbor": *~kaupô~ + *~hafnō~ ("harbor"). Cognate with Dutch ~kopen~, English ~cheap~ and ~cheapen~, Danish ~købe~, Swedish ~köpa~, Icelandic ~kaupa~. Related to German ~Kauf~ ("purchase") and ~verkaufen~ ("to sell").
en.kehren: A homograph concealing two distinct verbs. ~Kehren~ (1), "to turn," comes from Middle High German ~kēren~, from Old High German ~kēran~, ~chēren~, from Proto-Germanic *~kairijaną~ or *~kausijaną~, of uncertain PIE origin — possibly connected to PIE *~gers-~ ("to twist, bend, turn"), though Kluge's etymological dictionary calls this "a difficult word to explain both etymologically and phonetically." ~Kehren~ (2), "to sweep," comes from Middle High German ~kern~, ~keren~, from Old High German ~kerian~, ~cherian~, from Proto-Germanic *~karjaną~ ("to scrape, scratch"), from PIE *~ger-~ ("to scrape"). Despite their identical modern forms, these are etymologically unrelated verbs that merged through phonological convergence. The "turn" sense survives in ~umkehren~ ("to turn around, to reverse"), ~Kehrtwendung~ ("about-face"), ~zurückkehren~ ("to return"), and the idiom ~den Rücken kehren~ ("to turn one's back"). The "sweep" sense lives in ~auskehren~ ("to sweep out"), ~Kehricht~ ("sweepings, rubbish"), and the compound ~Kehrwoche~ — the famously Swabian tradition where apartment residents take turns sweeping shared spaces on a rotating schedule. English ~car~ (via Latin ~carrus~) is not related, despite superficial similarity.
en.kennen: From Middle High German ~kennen~, from Old High German ~kennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kannijan~ ("to know"), from Proto-Germanic *~kannijaną~ ("to know"), a causative form of *~kunnaną~ with the suffix *~-janą~. *~kunnaną~ derives from PIE *~ǵn̥néh₃ti~, from *~ǵneh₃-~ ("to know"). The causative origin means ~kennen~ originally signified "to make known, to cause to know" before narrowing to "to know, be acquainted with." Cognate with Bavarian ~kennan~, Dutch ~kennen~, Low German ~kennen~, Scots and English ~ken~ ("to know").
en.kennenlernen:
A double-verb compound of ~kennen~ + ~lernen~, meaning "to get to know, to become acquainted with":

- ~kennen~: From MHG ~kennen~, from OHG ~kennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kannijan~ ("to know"), from Proto-Germanic *~kannijaną~, a causative form of *~kunnaną~ ("to know how, to be able"), from PIE *~ǵneh₃-~ ("to know"). Cognate with Scots and English ~ken~ ("to know"), Dutch ~kennen~.
- ~lernen~: From MHG ~lernen~, from OHG ~lernēn~, ~lirnēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~liznēn~. Cognate with Old English ~leornian~ (Modern English ~learn~). Related to ~lehren~ ("to teach").

~Kennenlernen~ is one of German's few double-verb compounds — two full verbs fused into one separable unit (~Ich lerne sie kennen~, "I get to know her"). The combination is semantically precise: ~kennen~ means "to know" (a person or thing, through acquaintance), while ~lernen~ means "to learn" — together, "to learn to know." The distinction between ~kennen~ ("to be acquainted with") and ~wissen~ ("to know a fact") mirrors the French ~connaître~ vs. ~savoir~ and the Spanish ~conocer~ vs. ~saber~ — all Indo-European languages that distinguish experiential knowledge from propositional knowledge. English ~ken~ (from the same root as ~kennen~) survives mainly in Scottish English and the fixed phrase "beyond my ken." The PIE root *~ǵneh₃-~ is among the most prolific in all of Indo-European: it also gives Latin ~cognōscō~ ("I learn, I recognize," whence English ~cognition~, ~recognize~), Greek ~γιγνώσκω~ (~gignōskō~, whence ~gnosis~, ~diagnosis~), and Sanskrit ~jānāti~ ("he knows").
en.kennzeichnen:
Compound of ~Kennzeichen~ + ~-en~ (verb-forming suffix). The noun ~Kennzeichen~ is itself a compound of ~kennen~ + ~Zeichen~:

- ~kennen~ ("to know"): From MHG ~kennen~, from OHG ~kennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kannijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~kannijaną~ ("to know"), a causative form of *~kunnaną~ ("to be able, to know"), from PIE *~ǵneh₃-~ ("to know"). Cognate with English ~ken~ (Scottish/dialectal "to know"), Dutch ~kennen~.
- ~Zeichen~ ("sign"): From MHG ~zeichen~, from OHG ~zeihhan~ ("sign, symbol"), from Proto-West Germanic *~taikn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taikną~ ("sign"), from PIE *~deyk̂-~ ("to show"). Cognate with English ~token~, Dutch ~teken~, Danish ~tegn~.

The verb thus means "to mark with a recognizable sign," used both concretely (to label, to flag) and abstractly (to characterize, to be indicative of). The noun ~Kennzeichen~ has a very concrete everyday use in German: it refers to a vehicle's license plate (~Kraftfahrzeugkennzeichen~), literally a "recognition sign."
en.klagen: From Middle High German ~klagen~, from Old High German ~klagōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~klagōn~ ("to lament, complain"), a denominative verb from *~klagu~ ("lamentation, complaint"). The ultimate origin is uncertain; it is often compared to Proto-Indo-Iranian *~garjʰ-~ ("to complain"), but phonological difficulties make this connection questionable, and some scholars favor an onomatopoeic origin -- the word imitating the sound of wailing. German ~klagen~ preserves both the emotional sense ("to lament, mourn") and a legal one ("to sue, file a complaint"), whence the noun ~Klage~ means both "lament" and "lawsuit." English ~claim~ is unrelated, coming instead from Latin ~clāmāre~.
en.klappen: From Middle Low German ~klappen~, from Old Saxon *~klappōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~klappōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~klappōną~. The verb is onomatopoeic — it imitates the sharp percussive sound of a flat surface snapping shut. Cognate with Dutch ~klappen~ ("to clap, snap"), English ~clap~, and Old High German ~klapfō~ ("a clapping sound"). Also related to ~klaffen~ ("to gape, stand wide open"), which represents the same root shifted toward the open position rather than the shut one. The modern senses of ~klappen~ span the acoustic original ("to clatter, snap"), a physical result ("to fold shut"), and an abstract success meaning — "es klappt" ("it works out, it clicks") — where the satisfying snap of something fitting together became a metaphor for things going smoothly.
en.kleben:
From Middle High German ~kleben~, from Old High German ~klebēn~ ("to stick"), from Proto-West Germanic *~klibēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~klibēną~, from PIE *~gleyb-~ ("to stick, adhere"). This is a durative formation from the strong verb root *~kleibēną~ ("to adhere, to climb"), with cognates in Old English ~cleofian~ ("to cleave to, adhere"), Old Norse ~klīfa~ ("to climb"), and Old High German ~-klīban~ ("to stick").

The PIE root *~gleyb-~ reveals a surprising family reunion: German ~kleben~ ("to stick"), English ~cleave~ (in the sense "to cling to"), and ~climb~ (from OE ~climban~, originally "to cling one's way upward") are all cousins. The relationship between sticking and climbing is intuitive -- to climb a cliff face, one must cling to it. Even more remarkably, English has two verbs spelled ~cleave~ with opposite meanings: "to cling to" (from *~gleyb-~, the same root as ~kleben~) and "to split apart" (from *~glewbʰ-~, a different root entirely). These are among the most famous auto-antonyms in English.
en.klettern: An iterative verb formed in Early New High German from ~kletten~, from PIE *~gleyt-~ ("to cling to, cleave, stick"), from the root *~gley-~ ("to stick, smear"). Related to ~Klette~ ("burr") -- the prickly seed casing that clings to clothing and fur -- and, surprisingly, to ~Kleid~ ("dress, garment"), which originally referred to something that clings to the body. The iterative ~-ern~ suffix emphasizes repeated action, capturing the hand-over-hand nature of climbing. The semantic connection between clinging and climbing is vivid: a climber clings to the rock face the way a burr clings to a wool coat.
en.klicken: ~Klicken~ ("to click") is attested from the 16th century, derived from Middle High German *~klicken~, as preserved in ~zerklicken~ ("to break an eggshell"). It is either cognate with or formed in parallel to Dutch ~klikken~ (15th century), English ~click~ (16th century), and French ~cliquer~ (14th century) — all ultimately imitative of the sharp, dry sound a latch, buckle, or button makes. The computing sense — pressing a mouse button — is a 20th-century semantic loan from English ~click~. ~Klicken~ is thus one of the few words in this app to have two distinct origin stories in the same form: an ancient onomatopoeic root and a modern borrowing wearing its face. The verb is wholly regular and weak, befitting something so thoroughly modern.
en.klingen:
From Middle High German ~klingen~, from Old High German ~klingan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~klingan~, from Proto-Germanic *~klinganą~. A class III strong verb. Cognate with Middle Dutch ~klinken~, English ~clink~.

The Proto-Germanic root *~klinganą~ is almost certainly of onomatopoeic origin — a phonological icon of the ringing, resonant sound metal makes when struck. English ~clink~ is a direct descendant via Old English *~clincan~. Related by sound-symbolic extension are English ~clang~, ~cling~ (in the sense of a resonant sound), and possibly ~call~. The verb's surviving class III ablaut pattern — ~klingt~/~klang~/~geklungen~ — shows the vowel series (i, a, u) characteristic of Germanic strong verbs with nasal infixes, the same pattern as ~singen~ and ~springen~. German has generalized ~klingen~ from pure sound to metaphorical resonance: a speech can ~klingen wie Musik~, and a name can ~gut klingen~ — uses that extend the root's sonic origin into the domain of general quality or impression.
en.klären: From Middle High German ~klæren~, denominal from ~klar~ ("clear, bright"). The adjective ~klar~ was borrowed into German from Middle Low German or Middle Dutch ~klār~, itself from Old French ~cler~, from Latin ~clārus~ ("bright, illustrious, renowned"). English ~clear~ arrived by the same Latin route, also via Old French ~cler~, making ~klären~ and English ~to clear~ distant cousins by borrowing rather than direct inheritance. Latin ~clārus~ also gives English ~clarity~, ~declare~, and ~clarion~. The verb covers both the literal sense (clarifying a liquid by letting it settle) and the abstract sense (clarifying a question or situation) — exactly parallel to English ~to clear up~.
en.knüpfen:
From Middle High German ~knüpfen~, from Old High German ~knupfen~, ~chnuphan~, related to ~Knopf~ ("button, knob"). ~Knopf~ descends from MHG ~knopf~, from OHG ~knopf~, from Proto-Germanic *~knuppaz~ ("lump, clod, knob"). The word belongs to a large Germanic cluster of ~kn-~ words expressing ideas of lumps, knots, and protuberances: ~Knoten~ ("knot"), ~Knochen~ ("bone"), ~Knollen~ ("tuber"), ~Knauf~ ("knob, pommel").

~Knüpfen~ means "to tie, to knot, to forge (a connection)" and carries both literal and figurative weight. One ~knüpft~ a net, a carpet (~Teppich knüpfen~ = to hand-knot a carpet), or a bond (~Kontakte knüpfen~ = to forge contacts, ~Bedingungen daran knüpfen~ = to attach conditions). The Germanic ~kn-~ cluster is one of the most expressive sound-symbolic groupings in the language family: ~knead~, ~knot~, ~knob~, ~knuckle~, ~knee~, ~knoll~ — all words evoking compressed, rounded, or lumpy shapes.
en.kochen: From Middle High German ~kochen~, from Old High German ~kohhōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kokōn~ ("to cook"), borrowed from Late Latin ~cocō~, from Classical Latin ~coquō~ ("I cook, I boil"), from PIE *~pekʷ-~ ("to cook, to ripen"). This is one of the clearest Latin loanwords in the Germanic lexicon, entering the language during the Roman period when Germanic peoples along the Rhine and Danube adopted Roman culinary techniques and vocabulary. The word replaced whatever native Germanic cooking terms existed. Cognate with Dutch ~koken~, English ~cook~ (also from Latin via Old English ~cōc~), Swedish ~koka~, and Danish ~koge~. The PIE root *~pekʷ-~ also gave Sanskrit ~pacati~ ("he cooks"), Greek ~peptein~ ("to cook, to digest") — whence English ~peptic~ and ~dyspepsia~ — and Russian ~pech'~ ("to bake"). Fascinatingly, Hittite and Lydian preserve what may be the original meaning of *~pekʷ-~: "to pound, crush (grain)," suggesting the root shifted from grain processing to cooking as agricultural societies developed. The Latin-derived ~Koch~ ("cook," the noun) was borrowed so early into Germanic that it underwent the High German consonant shift from ~k~ to ~ch~ in the verb form, proving the borrowing occurred before or during the shift (roughly 3rd-6th centuries CE).
en.kombinieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~combīnāre~, a Late Latin formation from ~con-~ ("together") + ~bīnī~ ("two by two, in pairs"), from ~bis~ ("twice"), from PIE *~dwis~ ("twice"). The Latin verb ~combīnō~ meant "to combine, to unite."

The word entered many European languages via French ~combiner~ or directly from Latin: English ~combine~, Dutch ~combineren~, Polish ~kombinować~, and German ~kombinieren~ are all Latin borrowings. The German word carries an additional colloquial meaning, "to deduce" (~kombinieren~ as in reasoning something out, the detective's art), which is idiomatic to German and not shared by the English ~combine~.
en.kommen: From Middle High German ~quemen~, ~komen~, ~kumen~, from Old High German ~queman~ (rarer ~cuman~, ~chuman~), from Proto-West Germanic *~kweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~kwemaną~, from PIE *~gʷem-~. Cognate with Low German ~kamen~, Dutch ~komen~, English ~come~, Danish ~komme~.
en.kommentieren: Derived from ~Kommentar~ with the verbal suffix ~-ieren~. In use since the 18th century. ~Kommentar~ was borrowed from Latin ~commentārius~, ~commentārium~ ("notebook, commentary"), itself from ~commentārī~ ("to ponder, discuss"), an intensive form of ~comminiscī~ ("to think, devise"), from ~com-~ ("together") + ~meminisse~ ("to remember"), from PIE *~men-~ ("to think"). The PIE root *~men-~ is one of the most productive in the Indo-European family, yielding Latin ~mens~ ("mind"), Greek ~menos~ ("spirit"), Sanskrit ~manas~ ("mind"), and an enormous number of English derivatives: ~mind~, ~mental~, ~memory~, ~mention~, ~mentor~, ~mania~, ~mnemonic~, ~amnesia~, and ~monastery~ (from Greek ~monazein~, "to live alone," via the idea of contemplation). The German ~-ieren~ suffix naturalizes the Latin verb into standard German conjugation. The word ~Kommentar~ in German has the same double life as English ~commentary~: it can be a scholarly annotation or a casual remark. The phrase ~kein Kommentar~ ("no comment") is a direct calque of the English journalistic formula.
en.kommunizieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~commūnicāre~ ("to share, to make common, to impart"), derived from the adjective ~commūnis~ ("common, shared, public"), which itself is from ~com-~ ("with, together") + ~mūnus~ ("duty, gift, service"), from PIE *~mey-~ ("to change, exchange"). The ~-ieren~ suffix is the standard German adaptation of the Latin/French verbal ending ~-āre~/~-er~, used productively since the Middle Ages to integrate foreign verbs.

This is one of the most internationally recognizable roots in European languages: English ~communicate~, French ~communiquer~, Italian ~comunicare~, Spanish ~comunicar~ all descend from the same Latin source. The underlying PIE root *~mey-~ ("to exchange") also yielded Latin ~mūtāre~ ("to change," whence English ~mutation~, ~mutual~, ~commute~) and even ~immune~ (literally "not subject to duty"). The German word entered the language during the Early Modern period as part of the massive wave of Latin and French scholarly vocabulary. Unlike many such borrowings that remained confined to academic registers, ~kommunizieren~ has become thoroughly colloquial, though German also retains the native equivalent ~mitteilen~ ("to share with, to inform"), which offers a neat semantic parallel: ~mit-~ ("with") + ~teilen~ ("to divide, share") mirrors Latin ~com-~ + ~mūnicāre~.
en.konfrontieren:
Borrowed from Medieval Latin ~confrontāre~ ("to border on, to be adjacent"), composed of Latin ~con-~ ("with, together") and ~frōns~ (genitive ~frontis~, "forehead, front"). The word entered German likely via French ~confronter~ in the 17th century.

The original Latin sense was spatial: two properties ~confronting~ each other simply shared a boundary. The metaphorical shift to "facing someone with unpleasant facts or an adversary" developed in French legal and diplomatic language and was borrowed wholesale into German. ~Konfrontieren~ means "to confront, to face with" — ~jemanden mit der Wahrheit konfrontieren~ ("to confront someone with the truth"). The Latin root ~frōns~ ("forehead") reveals the underlying image: confrontation is a forehead-to-forehead encounter, a face-to-face meeting. This same root gives English ~front~, ~frontier~, ~affront~ (literally "to strike in the face"), and ~effrontery~ ("shamelessness," literally "without a forehead," i.e., without the capacity to blush). The reflexive ~sich konfrontiert sehen~ ("to find oneself confronted") is a staple of journalistic German.
en.kontaktieren:
Formed from ~Kontakt~ + ~-ieren~. The noun ~Kontakt~ was borrowed from Latin ~contāctus~ ("a touching, contact"), the past participle of ~contingere~ ("to touch upon"), from ~con-~ ("together") + ~tangere~ ("to touch"), from PIE *~teh₂g-~ ("to touch"). The ~-ieren~ suffix adapts the noun into a verb meaning "to establish contact with." A doublet of the less common ~kontakten~.

The word is a relatively modern formation, reflecting the influence of Latin-derived vocabulary in German bureaucratic and business language. English ~contact~ (as a verb) followed a similar path, though its use as a verb was once controversial among English prescriptivists.
en.kontrollieren:
Borrowed from French ~contrôler~, from Old French ~contreroller~, ~contreroler~, from ~contrerole~, from Medieval Latin ~contrarotulum~ ("a counter-roll or register used to verify accounts"), composed of Latin ~contrā~ ("against, opposite") + Medieval Latin ~rotulus~, from Latin ~rotula~ ("a little wheel"), diminutive of ~rota~ ("a wheel"), from PIE *~Hret-~ ("to run, roll"). The circumflex in French ~contrôler~ marks the loss of an older ~s~ (from ~contresroller~), a pattern visible across French (compare ~hôpital~ from ~hospital~).

The semantic journey is remarkable: from a physical wheel (~rota~) to a small scroll (~rotula~, because scrolls are rolled up) to a counter-register used to double-check accounts (~contrarotulum~) to the act of checking itself (~contrôler~) to the broader sense of mastery and dominance (~kontrollieren~, "to control"). German preserves both major senses: "to check, inspect" (the older meaning, closer to the Latin) and "to control, direct" (the newer meaning, influenced by English). The German suffix ~-ieren~ is the standard adaptation pattern for Romance verbs entering German. Related: ~Kontrolle~ ("inspection, control"), ~Kontrolleur~ ("inspector").
en.konzentrieren: ~Konzentrieren~ ("to concentrate") is borrowed from French ~concentrer~, formed from Latin ~con-~ ("together, with") + the root of ~centrum~ ("center, midpoint"), from Ancient Greek ~κέντρον~ (~kéntron~, "sharp point, goad, center of a circle"), from Proto-Hellenic *~kéntron~, from Proto-Indo-European *~ḱent-~ ("to prick, sting"). The Greek root is also the source of English ~center~ and ~centrifuge~. The underlying image of ~konzentrieren~ is therefore "to bring to a point" — to gather dispersed things to a single sharp focus. In chemistry, a ~konzentrierte Lösung~ (a concentrated solution) is one from which the solvent has been reduced so the dissolved substance is dense and undiluted. In everyday use, ~sich konzentrieren~ means "to focus one's attention," preserving the original geometrical metaphor of converging toward a center point.
en.konzipieren:
From Late Middle High German ~concipieren~, borrowed from Latin ~concipere~ ("to take in, to conceive, to grasp"), from ~con-~ ("together") + ~capere~ ("to take, to seize"). Latin ~capere~ descends from Italic ~kapiō~, from Proto-Italic *~kapjō~, from PIE *~kap-~ ("to seize, grab"). Cognate with Albanian ~kap~ ("to grab"), Welsh ~cael~ ("to get"), Breton ~kavout~ ("to find"), Lithuanian ~kampt~ ("to take hold"), Greek ~kaptō~ ("to gulp down"), and — remarkably — English ~have~ and ~heave~, which descend from the same PIE root through the Germanic branch.

German ~konzipieren~ means "to conceive, to draft, to design" — to seize upon an idea and give it initial shape. The noun ~Konzept~ ("concept, draft, rough plan") is the more everyday derivative. Latin ~concipere~ carried a vivid physical sense: to take things together, to gather in — and from this, to conceive (a child, an idea, a plan). The same Latin root, through the alternate prefix form ~-cipere~, produced an enormous family of English words: ~accept~ (~ad-~ + ~capere~), ~except~, ~intercept~, ~receive~ (via French, from ~re-~ + ~capere~), ~perceive~, ~deceive~, and ~capable~. The ~-ieren~ suffix is the hallmark of Latin and French loans in German, corresponding to French ~-er~ and English ~-ate~.
en.kopieren: From Medieval Latin ~copiāre~ ("to transcribe, to reproduce"), derived from the noun ~Kopie~, which was borrowed from Latin ~copia~ ("abundance, plenty"). Latin ~copia~ is itself composed of ~co-~ (from ~cum~, "with, together") + ~ops~ ("wealth, resources, power"), from PIE *~h₃ep-~ ("to work, produce"). The semantic chain from "abundance" to "copy" runs through the medieval scribal sense: a ~copia~ was originally a transcript made from an abundant supply of text, then any reproduction. The German verb entered the language via the Latinate ~-ieren~ suffix, the standard mechanism for adapting foreign verbs into German conjugation. Cognate with English ~copy~ and French ~copier~, all from the same Latin source. The modern sense has expanded well beyond manuscripts to encompass photocopying, digital duplication, and even imitating behavior. The related noun ~Kopie~ is used in everyday German exactly like English "copy."
en.korrigieren: From Middle High German ~corrigieren~, borrowed from Latin ~corrigere~ ("to set right, to correct"), a compound of ~con-~ ("together, with") + ~regere~ ("to direct, to rule"), from PIE *~h₃reg-~ ("to straighten, to direct"). The Latin prefix ~con-~ intensifies the meaning, giving the sense of "thoroughly setting straight." The verb entered German during the medieval period of heavy Latin influence on scholarly and administrative language. Cognate with English ~correct~ (via Old French from the same Latin source), as well as with ~regime~, ~regal~, ~regulate~, and ~rex~, all from the same PIE root. The German ~-ieren~ suffix marks this as a loanword verb, following the standard pattern for adapting foreign verbs into the German conjugation system. Interestingly, the native German equivalent would be something like ~berichtigen~ (from ~richtig~, "right, correct"), which itself descends from the same PIE root *~h₃reg-~ via the Germanic branch, making ~korrigieren~ and ~berichtigen~ distant etymological cousins that converged on the same meaning from Latin and Germanic paths respectively.
en.kosten:
~kosten~ carries two entirely unrelated Latin etymologies that have converged on a single German spelling.

~kosten~ "to cost": From Middle High German ~kosten~, borrowed from Old French ~coster~ (modern French ~coûter~), from Latin ~cōnstō~ ("to stand firm, be fixed, cost"), equivalent to ~con-~ + ~stō~ ("to stand"), from PIE *~steh₂-~ ("to stand"). The metaphor is of a price "standing firm" — a cost being fixed and settled. English ~cost~ traveled the same Old French route. Latin ~cōnstō~ is the same verb that underlies English ~constant~.

~kosten~ "to taste, to try": From Old High German ~kostōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~kustōną~ ("to try, taste"), from Proto-Indo-European *~ǵews-~ ("to taste, try"). This is an entirely unrelated word that happens to be spelled identically in modern German. The PIE root *~ǵews-~ also underlies Latin ~gustus~ ("taste"), which gave English ~gusto~, ~disgust~ (via Italian ~disgusto~, originally "a distaste for"), and ~gust~. The Proto-Germanic root *~kustōną~ also underlies Old English ~costnian~ ("to tempt, try, prove"), and English ~choose~ shares the same PIE source via a different derivational path through Proto-Germanic *~keusaną~. Two entirely different Latin and PIE roots thus converged on the same German spelling — one concerned with standing firm (prices), the other with tasting and testing.
en.kreieren: Borrowed from French ~créer~, itself from Latin ~creō~ ("I create, bring forth"), with the German verb-forming suffix ~-ieren~. Latin ~creō~ derives from PIE *~ḱreh₁-~ ("to grow, bring into being"), from the root *~ḱer-~ ("to grow, become bigger"), which also yielded Latin ~crēscō~ ("I grow") -- the source of English ~crescent~, ~increase~, and ~crescendo~. German ~kreieren~ coexists with the native ~schaffen~ and ~erschaffen~, but carries a more refined or artistic connotation, often used in fashion, cuisine, and the arts. The word entered German during the period of strong French cultural influence in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.
en.kriegen: From Middle High German ~kriegen~ ("to war, to strive for, to conquer property or land"), a verbalization of ~Krieg~ ("war"), from OHG ~krig~, from Proto-West Germanic *~krīg~. The origin of the noun *~krīg~ is debated: it may be related to OHG ~krēg~ ("stubbornness, defiance") or to a pre-Germanic substrate word. The semantic evolution is striking: the original meaning was "to wage war, to strive for" — one ~kriegte~ land or possessions through military effort. Over time, the "striving to obtain" sense overshadowed the military one, and in colloquial modern German ~kriegen~ simply means "to get, to receive," making it the everyday synonym for ~bekommen~. The older martial sense survives fossilized in the noun ~Krieg~ and in formal compound verbs like ~bekriegen~ ("to wage war on"). Cognate with Bavarian ~kriagn~, Dutch ~krijgen~ ("to get, to receive" — the same semantic shift occurred independently), and Hunsrik ~krien~. The Dutch parallel is particularly illuminating: both German and Dutch independently drifted from "to fight for" to "to obtain," suggesting that the shift was natural and perhaps inevitable.
en.kritisieren: From French ~critiquer~ with the verb ending replaced by the standard German ~-ieren~ suffix for naturalized foreign verbs. French ~critiquer~ derives from ~critique~ ("critical analysis"), from Latin ~criticus~, from Ancient Greek ~κριτικός~ (~kritikós~, "able to judge, discerning"), from ~κρίνω~ (~krínō~, "to separate, to decide, to judge"), from PIE *~krey-~ ("to sift, to separate, to distinguish"). The PIE root reveals that criticism was originally an act of sorting — separating the wheat from the chaff, the worthy from the unworthy. The same root gives English ~crisis~ (a decisive moment of separation), ~criterion~ (a standard for judging), ~crime~ (an act judged as wrongful — via Latin ~crīmen~), ~discern~ (to separate with the eyes), and ~secret~ (something separated away — via Latin ~sēcernere~). German borrowed the word during the 17th-century vogue for French intellectual vocabulary, and the ~-ieren~ suffix slots it neatly into German's productive system for assimilating Romance verbs. Cognate with English ~criticize~, Italian ~criticare~, Spanish ~criticar~. The journey from PIE "to sift grain" to modern "to write a book review" spans about six thousand years.
en.kämpfen: From Middle High German ~kempfen~, from Old High German *~kempfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kampijan~, a denominal verb from *~kamp~ ("field, battlefield"), itself borrowed from Latin ~campus~ ("field, open plain"). The word originally denoted the battlefield itself; the sense shifted early in High German from "the place of battle" to "the act of fighting." Latin ~campus~ is the common ancestor of English ~camp~, ~champion~ (via Old French ~champion~, "fighter in the field"), and the modern word ~campus~ ("university grounds," originally "the field" at Princeton). The Germanic borrowing was purely military — the word entered the Germanic vocabulary through Roman contact and immediately took on the specialized sense of armed contest rather than pastoral plain.
en.können: From Middle High German ~künnen~, ~kunnen~, from Old High German ~kunnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kunnan~, from Proto-Germanic *~kunnaną~, from PIE *~ǵn̥néh₃ti~, from *~ǵneh₃-~ ("to know"). A preterite-present verb whose original meaning was "to know (how)" and shifted to "to be able to." Cognate with Dutch ~kunnen~, English ~can~, Old Norse ~kunna~, Gothic ~kunnan~.
en.kümmern: ~Kümmern~ ("to concern, bother; to care for") descends from Middle High German ~kumbern~, derived from the noun ~kumber~ ("debris, rubble, obstruction, distress"). That noun was probably borrowed from Old French *~combre~ ("obstruction, barrier"), from Medieval Latin ~combrus~ ("barricade"), traced either to Latin ~cumulus~ ("heap") or to Gaulish *~komberū~ — from Proto-Celtic *~kombereti~ ("to bring together"), itself from *~kom-~ and *~bereti~ ("to bear"). An alternative derivation traces it to Proto-West Germanic *~kumbr~, Proto-Germanic *~kumbraz~ ("burden, trouble, sorrow"). Cognates include Luxembourgish ~këmmeren~ and Dutch ~kommer~ ("distress"). The related noun ~Kummer~ ("grief, sorrow") preserves the older sense of obstruction that has hardened into emotional weight. The reflexive construction ~sich um jemanden kümmern~ ("to take care of someone") shows the verb's shift from passive obstruction to active concern — etymologically, to be ~encumbered~ by something came to mean attending to it.
en.kündigen: From Middle High German ~kündigen~ ("to make known, to announce"), derived from the adjective ~kündic~ (modern ~kundig~, "knowledgeable, skilled"), from OHG ~kundig~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kunþīg~, itself from Proto-Germanic *~kunþaz~ ("known"), the past participle of *~kunnaną~ ("to know, to be able"), from PIE *~ǵneh₃-~ ("to know"). The modern meaning of "to terminate (a contract), to give notice, to quit" only became standard in the 19th century, clipped from longer compounds like ~aufkündigen~ ("to renounce"), ~abkündigen~ ("to announce the end of"), and ~verkündigen~ ("to proclaim"). The semantic journey from "to make known" to "to give notice of termination" is logical: one ~kündigt~ by making one's departure known. The PIE root *~ǵneh₃-~ is one of the most prolific in Indo-European: it produced Latin ~gnōscere~, ~cognōscere~ (whence English ~cognition~, ~recognize~), Greek ~gnōsis~ ("knowledge"), Sanskrit ~jnā~ ("to know"), English ~know~ and ~can~ (from the same Germanic *~kunnaną~), and German ~kennen~, ~können~, and ~Kunst~ ("art," literally "that which one knows how to do"). The related noun ~Kunde~ has split into two meanings: ~der Kunde~ ("customer" — originally "one who is known, a familiar") and ~die Kunde~ ("news, tidings" — information that is made known).
en.kürzen: A deadjectival verb formed from ~kurz~ ("short"), which descends from Middle High German ~kurz~, from Old High German ~kurz~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kurt~, borrowed from Latin ~curtus~ ("shortened, cut short"). Latin ~curtus~ derives from PIE *~(s)ker-~ ("to cut"), the same root behind English ~shear~, ~short~, ~shirt~, and ~skirt~. The native Germanic cognate of ~curtus~ was OHG *~skurz~, which survives in ~Schurz~ ("apron") -- originally something cut short. The verb ~kürzen~ thus means "to shorten," forming a neat pair with ~verlängern~ ("to lengthen"). English ~curt~ (meaning brusquely short in manner) is a doublet borrowed directly from the same Latin source.
en.lachen:
From Middle High German ~lachen~, from Old High German ~lahhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlahhjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlahjaną~, from PIE *~kleg-~ ("to cry out, sound"). Cognate with Dutch ~lachen~, English ~laugh~, Danish ~le~, Swedish ~le~, Gothic ~hlahjan~.

The initial ~hl-~ cluster in Proto-Germanic (preserved in Gothic ~hlahjan~) was lost in all the modern West Germanic languages — Old High German simplified it to ~l-~, and English did the same (via Old English ~hlæhhan~ → ~laugh~). The PIE root *~kleg-~ originally meant simply "to make a sound, to cry out," with no specification of whether the sound was joyful or mournful — laughter and lamentation share the same ancestral noise. The spelling of English ~laugh~ (with ~gh~) fossilizes the Old English velar fricative that German still pronounces as ~ch~ in ~lachen~ — a rare case where German pronunciation better preserves the Proto-Germanic sound than English spelling does.
en.laden:
Two historically distinct verbs have merged under a single infinitive in Modern German, distinguished by context and sometimes by register.

~laden~ (strong, "to load") derives from Middle High German ~laden~ (strong verb), from Old High German ~hladan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaþaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~kleh₂-~ ("to put, lay out"). Cognate with Lithuanian ~klṓti~ ("to lay, spread"), Russian ~клад~ ("treasure, hoard"), and English ~lade~ (as in ~bill of lading~). The English adjective ~laden~ — "loaded, burdened" — is a direct borrowing of the German past participle.

~laden~ (weak, "to invite, summon") derives from Middle High German ~laden~ (weak verb), from Old High German ~ladon~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laþōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~laþōną~, from *~laþō~ ("invitation"), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂lot-eh₂~, a root shared with Hittite ~halzai~ ("to invoke, call out") and Luwian ~ḫalta~ ("to call"). This root is an isogloss found only in Germanic and Anatolian, making it one of the rarer traces of the Indo-Hittite connection. ~Einladen~ ("to invite") keeps this second meaning alive as the commonest modern form.

The two verbs converged in form in Middle High German, creating a rich ambiguity: in certain contexts ~Laden~ can mean both a physical shop (something "loaded" with goods) and an invitation to enter.
en.lagern:
Derived from the noun ~Lager~ ("camp, store, warehouse"), from MHG ~lëger~, from OHG ~lëgar~, from Proto-West Germanic *~legr~, from Proto-Germanic *~legrą~. Cognate with English ~lair~ (a sleeping place for an animal — originally any place of lying down), Dutch ~leger~, Danish ~lejr~, Swedish ~läger~. The modern German form with ~-a-~ is of East Central German origin, influenced by the related word ~Lage~ ("position, situation"). The original front-vowel form survives in some rare alternate plurals.

The verb ~lagern~ means "to store, to warehouse" (transitive) and "to be stored, to camp, to lie" (intransitive/reflexive). The semantic path from "lying place" to "storage" is natural: goods are laid down for safekeeping. English borrowed ~lager~ directly from German in the 19th century — ~Lagerbier~ is beer that has been ~gelagert~ (stored, aged) in cool cellars for weeks or months, as opposed to top-fermented ales. The darker compound ~Konzentrationslager~ ("concentration camp") tragically demonstrates the word's range, from the innocuous sense of "encampment" to one of history's most terrible coinages.
en.landen: Denominal from ~Land~ ("land, country"), via Middle Low German ~landen~. The noun ~Land~ descends from Proto-West Germanic *~land~, from Proto-Germanic *~landą~, from Proto-Indo-European *~lendʰ-~ ("land, heath"). An earlier native German verb ~länden~ was displaced by the borrowed Low German form. Both the German noun ~Land~ and the English noun and verb ~land~ share the same Proto-Germanic ancestor *~landą~, making ~landen~ and English ~to land~ direct cognates — two sister-languages coining the same verb from the same noun independently.
en.lassen: From Middle High German ~lāzen~, ~lān~, from Old High German ~lāȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lātan~ ("to let, allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lētaną~ ("to leave behind, allow"), from PIE *~leh₁d-~ ("to let, leave behind"). Cognate with Bavarian ~lassn~, Dutch ~laten~, English ~let~.
en.laufen: From Middle High German ~loufen~, from Old High German ~hloufan~, ~loufan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaupan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaupaną~. A class VII strong verb. The initial ~hl-~ cluster was lost during the Old High German period. Cognate with Dutch ~lopen~, English ~leap~ and ~lope~, Danish ~løbe~.
en.lauten: ~Lauten~ ("to read, run, be worded; to sound") descends from Middle High German ~lūten~ ("to sound, ring"), Old High German ~hlūtēn~, and Proto-West Germanic *~hlūdēn~. It is equivalent to ~Laut~ ("sound") plus the verbal suffix ~-en~. ~Laut~ itself comes from the Proto-Germanic adjective *~hlūdaz~ ("loud"), the same root that gives English ~loud~, Dutch ~luid~, and Old Norse ~hljóð~.
