A subtle distinction in modern German: ~lauten~ means "to read as, to run" (as in ~das Urteil lautet~ — "the verdict reads"), while ~läuten~ ("to ring bells") is historically the factitive (causative) form of the same root — "to make sound." Over time, ~läuten~ absorbed the general sense of sounding, while ~lauten~ narrowed to the meaning of textual or verbal content. They share an ancestor but now describe entirely different acts.
en.leben: From Middle High German ~lëben~, from Old High German ~lebēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~libbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~libjaną~, from PIE *~leyp-~ ("to leave, cling, linger"). The semantic development from "to cling, stick" to "to remain alive" parallels the related ~bleiben~ ("to remain"), which shares the same PIE root. Cognate with Old Saxon ~libbian~ (Middle Low German ~leven~), Dutch ~leven~, English ~live~, West Frisian ~libje~, Old Norse ~lifa~ (Swedish ~leva~), Gothic ~liban~.
en.legen: From Middle High German ~legen~, from Old High German ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjaną~, a causative derivation of *~ligjaną~ ("to lie") with the suffix *~-janą~, from PIE *~logʰéyeti~. The causative formation — "to cause to lie," hence "to lay" — mirrors exactly the relationship between ~liegen~ ("to lie") and ~legen~ ("to lay"), and between English ~lie~ and ~lay~. The same causative pattern appears in German ~setzen~ (from ~sitzen~, "to sit") and ~stellen~ (from ~stehen~, "to stand"): in each pair, a strong intransitive verb of posture spawns a weak transitive causative. This systematic grammatical pairing descends from Proto-Indo-European. Cognate with Dutch ~leggen~, English ~lay~, Danish ~lægge~, Swedish ~lägga~.
en.lehnen:
From a merger of two interrelated Old High German verbs:

1. MHG ~linen~/~lenen~ (intransitive "to lean"), from OHG ~hlinēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlinēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlināną~ ("to lean"), from PIE *~k̂ley-~ ("to lean, incline"). Cognate with Latin ~clīnō~ ("to slope, incline") and Greek ~klinō~ ("to bend, lean").
2. MHG ~leinen~ (transitive/causative "to lean something"), from OHG ~hleinen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlainijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlainijaną~, the causative form of the above.

The merger occurred in Central and Low German dialects, where the vowels converged to ~ē~. Some Upper German dialects still distinguish the transitive and intransitive forms. Cognate with Dutch ~leunen~ and English ~lean~.

A second, archaic ~lehnen~ ("to lend, borrow"), from OHG ~lēhanōn~, survives only in the derivative ~entlehnen~ ("to borrow a word or concept") and in compounds like ~Lehnwort~ ("loanword") and ~Lehnübersetzung~ ("calque, loan translation").
en.lehren:
From Middle High German ~lēren~, from Old High German ~lēren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laiʀijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~laizijaną~ ("to teach"), a causative derived from *~lizaną~ ("to learn"), from PIE *~leys-~ ("track, furrow, trace, trail"). The underlying metaphor is that teaching means "to cause someone to follow a track." Cognate with Low German ~lehren~, Dutch ~leren~, Afrikaans ~leer~, Danish ~lære~, Norwegian ~lære~, Swedish ~lära~, and archaic English ~lear~ ("to learn, to teach").

Closely related to German ~lernen~ ("to learn"), which descends from the same Proto-Germanic root *~lizaną~ through a different derivational path. English ~lore~ ("accumulated knowledge") is another sibling, from Proto-Germanic *~laizō~ ("teaching, lore"). The PIE root *~leys-~ ("track, furrow") reveals an agricultural metaphor at the heart of education: to learn was to follow a furrow, and to teach was to cause someone to follow one. In some Middle English dialects, ~learn~ could itself mean "to teach" (a usage that survives dialectally in "I'll learn you!"), preserving the original ambiguity between the causative and stative senses that German resolved by splitting into two separate verbs.
en.leiden: ~leiden~ comes from Middle High German ~līden~, from Old High German ~līdan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~līþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~līþaną~ ("to go, travel"). The original sense was purely spatial: to go, to travel, to pass through. Over time the meaning narrowed to "to endure" and then "to suffer" — a trajectory from journeying to hardship that mirrors the lived experience of passage. The semantic shift was reinforced by convergence with Middle High German ~leiden~ ("to be sorry, suffer, be hated"), itself from Old High German ~leidēn~, and by the noun ~Leid~ ("sorrow"), whose surface similarity made the merger feel natural. A classic false friend: ~leiden~ is NOT cognate with English ~lead~ — it is instead cognate with English ~lithe~ ("flexible, supple"), Dutch ~lijden~, and Low German ~lieden~. The spatial ancestor survives in the compound prefix ~-leiten~ ("to guide, conduct") and in place names such as ~Leiden~ (the Dutch city: "the watery passage").
en.leisten: From Middle High German ~leisten~, from Old High German ~leisten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laistijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~laistijaną~, a denominative of *~laistaz~ ("track, trace, footprint"), from Proto-Indo-European *~leys-~ ("to track, pursue; track, furrow, trace, trail"). The verb originally meant "to track, follow" — to follow in someone's footsteps — and evolved into "to perform, carry out, accomplish." This semantic journey from literal path-following to figurative achievement is a remarkably concrete-to-abstract shift: to fulfill an obligation is to follow the track it has laid out. English ~last~ (in "lasting shoe") is a direct cognate (OE ~lǣstan~, "to follow, perform"), and the shoemaker's ~last~ — the foot-shaped form used to make shoes — is the noun *~laistaz~ itself, a "footprint" in wood. Cognate with English ~last~ (OE ~lǣstan~), Old Frisian ~lēsta~, Old Saxon ~lēstian~, Dutch ~leisten~ (archaic).
en.leiten: ~Leiten~ ("to lead, guide, conduct") descends from Middle High German ~leiten~, Old High German ~leiten~, and Proto-West Germanic *~laidijan~. It is the causative of the older strong verb *~līþaną~ ("to pass, go, travel"), built with the Proto-Germanic causative suffix *~-janą~: "to cause to go, to make travel." The PIE root is *~leyt-~ ("to go, depart, die"), which also produced Latin ~lītus~ ("shore" — the place where the sea "goes"), Avestan ~iriθiieiti~ ("to die, pass away"), and Tocharian A ~litā-~ ("to pass on"). The English cognate ~lead~ (to guide) is its exact parallel — Old English ~lǣdan~ from the same Proto-Germanic causative. A false-friend trap: ~leiten~ ("to guide") is unrelated to ~leiden~ ("to suffer"), which comes from a completely different root meaning "to permit, allow."
en.lenken: From Middle High German ~lenken~, from Old High German ~lanca~ ("hip, flank"), related to ~Gelenk~ ("joint"). The noun ~Gelenk~ descends from MHG ~gelenke~ ("joint, bend, waist"), from OHG ~gilenki~, from Proto-Germanic *~gahlankiją~, from *~hlankaz~ ("flexible, lank, slender"). Cognate with English ~lank~ (related to ~link~) and Hunsrik ~lenke~. The semantic journey from "hip/joint" to "to steer, guide, direct" traveled through the idea of bending and turning at a joint. A joint is a point of articulation that allows redirection of movement, so ~lenken~ came to mean directing or steering something along a new path. The compound ~Lenkrad~ ("steering wheel," literally "steering-wheel") is the everyday reminder of this etymology, as is ~Ablenkung~ ("distraction, deflection"). The relationship between ~lenken~ and ~Gelenk~ mirrors the English connection between ~joint~ and ~articulate~ (from Latin ~articulus~, "small joint"). English ~lank~ preserves the physical sense of the root: something long, thin, and flexible. The related OHG ~hlanka~ ("side, flank, hip") also produced the French loanword ~flanc~ (whence English ~flank~), showing a Germanic word that traveled into Romance.
en.lernen: From Middle High German ~lernen~, from Old High German ~lernēn~, ~lirnēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~liʀnēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~lizaną~, from PIE *~leys-~ ("track, furrow, trace, trail"). The PIE root conveys the image of following a track or tracing a path — and from this physical metaphor, Germanic developed the meaning of acquiring knowledge: to learn is to follow the trail laid down by those who came before. This same root underlies ~lore~ and ~lear~ (archaic English for "learning, knowledge"). The historically close verb ~lehren~ ("to teach") shares the same Germanic stem in its causative function — "to cause to learn." ~Lernen~ is attested in German since the 8th century. Cognate with Old English ~leornian~ (Modern English ~learn~), Old Frisian ~lernia~, Middle Low German ~lernen~, and Dutch ~lernen~.
en.lesen: From Middle High German ~lësen~, from Old High German ~lësan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lesan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lesaną~ ("to gather"), from PIE *~les-~ ("to gather"). The semantic shift from "to gather" to "to read" — paralleled in Latin ~legō~ ("I gather; I read") — reflects how reading was originally conceived as gathering letters or words. Cognate with Low German ~lesen~, Dutch ~lezen~, Afrikaans ~lees~, English ~lease~ (archaic, "to gather").
en.leuchten:
From OHG ~liuhten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~liuhtijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~liuhtijaną~, a factitive/denominative verb derived from *~leuhtą~ ("light") or *~leuhtaz~ ("light, bright"), both from PIE *~lewk-~ ("bright, to shine, to see"). Cognate with MHG ~liuhten~. The verb is attested since the 8th century.

The PIE root *~lewk-~ is one of the great etymological treasures of the Indo-European family. It produced Latin ~lūx~ ("light"), whence English ~lucid~, ~translucent~, ~elucidate~, and the name ~Lucifer~ ("light-bearer"); Greek ~leukos~ ("white"), whence ~leukemia~ (a disease of "white" blood cells); Sanskrit ~roká-~ ("light"); and Lithuanian ~laũkas~ ("pale"). The Germanic branch developed *~lewk-~ into the noun *~leuhtą~ ("light") and the adjective *~leuhtaz~ ("bright"), whence both the German verb ~leuchten~ ("to shine, to glow") and the noun ~Licht~ ("light"). English ~light~ is a cognate. The modern German word ~Leuchtturm~ ("lighthouse," literally "shining tower") and ~Leuchtstoff~ ("fluorescent material") build directly on this ancient root.
en.lieben:
Formally from Middle High German ~lieben~, an alteration (modeled on the adjective ~liep~) of the rarer MHG ~liuben~ ("to make dear, treat in a friendly way"), from Old High German ~liuben~. Denominative of the adjective ~lieb~ ("dear, beloved"), from MHG ~liep~, from OHG ~liob~, from Proto-West Germanic *~leub~, from Proto-Germanic *~leubaz~ ("dear, beloved"), from PIE *~lewbʰ-~ ("to desire, love").

The Modern German verb ~lieben~ has a complicated history: the Old High German simplex ~liobōn~ ("to love") apparently left no direct continuation, and the modern sense was derived in late Middle High German from the noun ~Liebe~ ("love") on the model of ~minnen~ ("to love"), from ~Minne~ (the courtly word for love), which had acquired a sexual overtone by that period. As a result, ~lieben~ is absent from most traditional German dialects, which prefer constructions like ~lieb haben~ or ~gern haben~ instead. English ~love~ derives from a related but distinct Proto-Germanic root *~lubō~ (noun), while the adjective ~lief~ (archaic English, "beloved, dear") is a direct cognate of German ~lieb~. Cognate with Dutch ~lieven~, English ~lief~, archaic English ~love~ (the verb, via *~lubōną~).
en.liefern:
From Middle High German ~lieveren~ (attested before 1350), probably borrowed via Middle Dutch and Middle Low German ~lēveren~ (the ~ie~ vocalism in German being due to hypercorrection), ultimately from Old French ~livrer~, from Latin ~līberō~ ("I free, release, deliver"), from ~līber~ ("free"). The word was part of the technical vocabulary of the Hanseatic League, the powerful trading network of northern European merchants, which spread it from Low German into standard High German — a linguistic footprint of medieval commercial power.

The route from Latin "to free" to German "to deliver" is semantically transparent: to hand over goods is to release them to another party. The same Latin root ~līberō~ gives English ~liberate~, ~deliver~ (via Old French ~delivrer~), and the name ~Liberia~. Note that German ~liefern~ ("to deliver") is therefore a distant cousin of ~liberally~ and ~liberty~ — trading and freedom share a root. Cognate with Dutch ~leveren~, Swedish ~leverera~, French ~livrer~.
en.liegen: From Middle High German ~ligen~, from Old High German ~ligen~ (from earlier ~liggen~, with the stem generalized from the second- and third-person singular), from Proto-West Germanic *~liggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~ligjaną~, from PIE *~legʰ-~ ("to lie down"). A class V strong verb. Cognate with Low German ~liggen~, Dutch ~liggen~, English ~lie~, Danish ~ligge~, Gothic ~ligan~.
en.loben:
From Middle High German ~loben~, from Old High German ~lobēn~/~lobōn~ ("to praise, approve, hold dear"), from Proto-West Germanic *~lubōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~lubō~, from PIE *~lewbʰ-~ ("to love, desire, praise"). Cognate with Old English ~lofian~ ("to praise"), Old Norse ~lofa~ ("to praise, promise"), Dutch ~loven~ ("to praise"), Swedish ~lova~ ("to promise, praise").

~Loben~ is related to the noun ~Lob~ ("praise") and to ~lieb~ ("dear, beloved") and ~Liebe~ ("love") -- all from PIE *~lewbʰ-~. The root reveals an ancient equation between praising and loving: to praise someone was to declare one's affection. English ~love~ comes from the same PIE root via a different branch (OE ~lufu~). Old English ~lofian~ ("to praise") survives fossilized in the word ~loft~ (originally a raised gallery in a church from which hymns of praise were sung) and in ~aloft~. The compound ~Lobgesang~ ("hymn, song of praise") echoes the medieval world where ~loben~ was the language of devotion.
en.locken:
From Middle High German ~locken~, from Old High German ~lockōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lokkōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~lukkōną~ ("to entice, to lure"). Related to ~lügen~ ("to lie"), Latin ~laciō~ ("I entice, I lure"), and Slovak ~lákať~ ("to allure"). There is a second, homonymous verb ~locken~ ("to curl") from OHG ~lochōn~, derived from the noun ~Locke~ ("curl"), but the two verbs are etymologically distinct.

The connection between ~locken~ ("to lure") and ~lügen~ ("to lie") hints at a deep Germanic association between enticement and deception -- to lure someone is, at root, a form of lying. The Latin cognate ~laciō~ likewise means "to entice" and is the ancestor of English ~lace~ (originally a snare or noose) and ~delicious~ (through ~dēliciae~, "enticements, delights") -- though these Latin connections are disputed. English has no direct Germanic cognate of ~locken~ in common use; it instead uses ~lure~, borrowed from Old French. German ~Lockvogel~ ("decoy bird," literally "lure-bird") preserves the original concrete sense of baiting traps, while the modern figurative use -- ~ein Angebot, das Kunden lockt~ ("an offer that attracts customers") -- shows how trapping became marketing.
en.lohnen: From Middle High German ~lōnen~, from Old High German ~lōnōn~, a denominal verb from ~lōn~ ("reward, wage"), from Proto-West Germanic *~laun~, from Proto-Germanic *~launą~, from Proto-Indo-European *~leh₂w-~ ("gain, prize, booty"). Cognate with Old English ~lēan~ ("reward, recompense"). The English cognate ~lean~ in this sense is now obsolete, but it survived long enough to be recorded in Old English poetry. The PIE root also underlies the idea of payment as something captured or won — an early conceptual link between labor and the spoils of contest.
en.lächeln:
From Middle High German ~lecheln~, formed from ~lachen~ ("to laugh") + the diminutive suffix ~-eln~:

- ~lachen~: From MHG ~lachen~, from OHG ~lahhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlahhjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlahjaną~ ("to laugh"). Cognate with English ~laugh~, Dutch ~lachen~, and Old Norse ~hlæja~.
- ~-eln~: A diminutive verbal suffix indicating a lesser, gentler, or more tentative version of the base action.

~Lächeln~ is a diminutive of ~lachen~ — literally "to laugh a little." The vowel change from ~a~ to ~ä~ (umlaut) is triggered by the diminutive suffix, just as ~Haus~ becomes ~Häuschen~. This is one of the rare cases where German's diminutive system extends to verbs rather than nouns. The distinction between ~lachen~ (open, audible laughter) and ~lächeln~ (a quiet, gentle smile) exactly parallels the diminutive's function: softening and miniaturizing. English ~laugh~ and German ~lachen~ are exact cognates, but English has no equivalent diminutive verb — ~smile~ comes from an entirely different root (Proto-Germanic *~smīlāną~). The Mona Lisa's enigmatic expression is a ~Lächeln~, never a ~Lachen~.
en.löschen: Two etymologically distinct verbs have merged under this spelling. The first, meaning "to extinguish," descends from Middle High German ~leschen~, Old High German ~lescan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~leskan~, from Proto-Germanic *~leskaną~ ("to go out, be extinguished"). Cognate with Dutch ~lessen~. The original Proto-Germanic verb meant the fire goes out on its own; the transitive causative meaning — to put out a fire — developed later. The second, meaning "to delete, unload (cargo)," was borrowed from Low German ~lössen~, ~lossen~, from Middle Low German ~lössen~, derived from the ancestor of ~los~ ("free, rid of"). The spelling ~löschen~ for this second verb arose from later association with the first: unloaded cargo was figuratively "extinguished" from the ship's manifest. Today the digital-age meaning "to delete a file" continues exactly this metaphor.
en.lösen: From Middle High German ~lœsen~, ~lōsen~, from Old High German ~lōsen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lausijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lausijaną~, a causative of *~leusaną~ ("to lose, let loose"), from Proto-Indo-European *~lewh₁-~ ("to cut, sever, separate, loosen, lose"). The verb is denominative via the adjective *~lausaz~ ("loose, free") — literally "to make loose." The same PIE root underlies English ~lose~ and ~loose~, Latin ~luere~ ("to release, atone"), Ancient Greek ~lúō~ ("to loosen, liberate"), and Sanskrit ~lūna~ ("cut, severed"). The semantic range of ~lösen~ — from untying a knot to solving a problem to buying a ticket — all trace back to the original idea of releasing something that was bound. Cognate with Dutch ~lozen~, English ~loose~ (via Old Norse ~lauss~), obsolete English ~leese~ (from Old English ~līesan~), Danish ~løse~, Faroese ~loysa~, Icelandic ~leysa~, Norwegian ~løse~, Swedish ~lösa~.
en.machen: From Middle High German ~machen~, from Old High German ~mahhōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~makōn~, from PIE *~mag-~ ("to knead, mix, make"). Cognate with Dutch ~maken~, Low German ~maken~, English ~make~.
en.malen:
From Middle High German ~mālen~, from Old High German ~mālōn~, ~mālēn~, a denominative verb from the noun ~māl~ ("spot, stain, mark"), from Proto-West Germanic *~mālijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~mēlijaną~, itself derived from *~mēlą~ ("mark, spot"). The ultimate PIE root is *~melh₂-~ ("dark color"), which also gives Lithuanian ~mėlas~ ("black") and Greek ~melas~ ("dark-colored, black"), the source of English ~melancholy~ (literally "black bile") and ~melanin~.

The semantic path from "mark, stain" to "to paint" is intuitive: the earliest painting was essentially making marks. Cognate with Dutch ~maal~ ("mark") and the German noun ~Mal~ ("mark, time, occasion") and ~Mahl~ ("meal" -- originally a "marked time" for eating). Be careful not to confuse ~malen~ ("to paint") with ~mahlen~ ("to grind"), which despite their similar sound have completely different origins: ~mahlen~ comes from Proto-Germanic *~malaną~ ("to grind"), from PIE *~melh₂-~ ("to grind"). Ironically, the "to paint" and "to grind" roots may ultimately be related at the deepest PIE level, both involving the notion of rubbing or crushing, since dark pigments were made by grinding minerals -- but at the Proto-Germanic stage they were already distinct words.
en.markieren:
Borrowed from French ~marquer~ ("to mark, to indicate"), from Frankish *~markōn~ ("to mark, to note"), from Proto-Germanic *~markō~ ("boundary, border, sign"), from PIE *~merǵ-~ ("edge, boundary"). The German verb was formed with the standard ~-ieren~ suffix for naturalized Romance verbs.

The word's journey is circular: a Frankish Germanic root traveled into Old French, was reshaped by Romance phonology, and then returned to German with a French suffix. The native German noun ~Marke~ ("brand, mark, stamp") was separately borrowed from French ~marque~, which has the same Frankish origin. English ~mark~ is a direct cognate of the original Germanic root, having never taken the detour through French. In modern internet-era German, ~markieren~ has acquired the additional meaning "to tag" someone on social media.
en.meinen: From Middle High German ~meinen~, from Old High German ~meinen~, from Proto-Germanic *~mainijaną~ ("to mean, think; lament"), from PIE *~moyn-éye-ti~ ("to intend, give an opinion"), a causative formation from the root *~meyn-~. The semantic range of the PIE root covered intending, opining, and expressing — a cluster that persists across its descendants. The Germanic cognates include both the intellectual sense ("to mean, think") and an emotional one: ~meinen~ is also related to English ~moan~, where the root's sense of "lamenting, expressing" took a sorrowful turn. The Irish cognate ~mían~ ("wish, desire") preserves yet another facet of the original meaning. Cognate with Dutch ~menen~, West Frisian ~miene~, English ~mean~ and ~moan~, and Old Irish ~mían~.
en.meistern:
From Middle High German ~meisteren~, from Old High German ~meistarōn~ ("to master, to direct"), derived from the noun ~Meister~ ("master"), from OHG ~meistar~, from Proto-West Germanic *~maistar~, borrowed from Latin ~magister~ ("master, teacher, chief"), from PIE *~meg-~ ("great") via the superlative suffix *~-is-tero-~. The noun ~Meister~ is thus a doublet of ~Magister~ and ~Maestro~, all from the same Latin source.

The verb means "to master, to overcome, to manage successfully" — ~eine Herausforderung meistern~ ("to master a challenge"), ~eine schwierige Situation meistern~ ("to handle a difficult situation"). The Latin source ~magister~ literally meant "the greater one" (from ~magnus~ "great"), a formation parallel to ~minister~ ("the lesser one," from ~minus~ "less") — the master commands while the minister serves. This pair reveals Roman social hierarchy frozen in morphology. In the German trades, ~Meister~ carries enormous prestige: the ~Meisterbrief~ ("master craftsman's certificate") is the highest qualification in the vocational system, and many trades legally require it to open a business. The compound ~Meisterstück~ ("masterpiece") originally referred to the work a journeyman had to produce to earn the ~Meister~ title — a tradition dating to the medieval guild system that is still practiced in some crafts today.
en.melden: From Middle High German ~mëlden~, from Old High German ~mëldōn~, ~mëldēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~melþōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~melþōną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~meldʰ-~ ("to ask, pray, speak"). The same PIE root underlies Lithuanian ~maldýti~ ("to implore"), Proto-Slavic *~modlìti~ ("to ask, pray"), Hittite ~māldi~ ("to recite, make a vow"), and Old Armenian ~małtʻ~ ("prayer, supplication"). The original sense was religious or supplicatory speech; ~melden~ retains the communicative dimension while shedding the devotional one, now meaning simply "to report, announce, register." A curious parallel: the PIE *~meldʰ-~ root appears across Indo-European in prayer and invocation contexts, but in German it survives only in the bureaucratic sense of filing a report. Cognate with Dutch ~melden~, Low German ~mellen~, English ~meld~ (a partial cognate, via Dutch ~melden~, used in card games to mean "to declare").
en.merken: From Middle High German ~merken~, from Old High German ~merchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~markijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~markijaną~ ("to mark, notice"), a denominal verb from *~marką~ ("mark, sign"). Cognate with Dutch ~merken~, Icelandic ~merkja~, Old English ~marc~, and modern English ~mark~ (including ~remark~, which entered English via French but traces to the same Germanic root). The core semantic movement is from physical inscription — leaving a mark on a surface — to cognitive attention: to notice something is, in an older idiom, to leave a mental mark on it. German ~Marke~ ("brand, token, stamp") and ~merken~ share this common ancestor, making the act of noticing etymologically an act of marking.
en.messen:
From Middle High German ~mezzen~, from Old High German ~mezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~metan~, from Proto-Germanic *~metaną~, from PIE *~med-~ ("to measure, to take appropriate measures"). A class V strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~meten~, English ~mete~ (as in "to mete out"), Old English ~metan~.

The PIE root *~med-~ is spectacularly productive across the Indo-European family: it gives Latin ~medeor~ ("I heal" — originally "I take the right measure"), whence English ~medicine~, ~remedy~, and ~moderate~; Greek ~μέδω~ ("I rule over, I measure") and ~μέτρον~ (~metron~, "measure"), whence ~meter~, ~metric~, ~geometry~, and ~symmetry~; and Latin ~meditārī~ ("to reflect upon"), whence ~meditate~. The semantic thread connecting all these descendants is "taking the right measure" — whether of a physical quantity (~messen~), of a punishment (~mete out~), of a disease (~medicine~), or of one's thoughts (~meditate~). German ~Maß~ ("measure") and ~Meter~ (borrowed back from Greek via French) both descend from the same ancient root, one through native inheritance and one through learned borrowing.
en.mieten: From Middle High German ~mieten~, from Old High German ~mietan~, a denominative verb formed from the noun ~Miete~ ("rent, reward"). The noun ~Miete~ descends from MHG ~miete~, from OHG ~mieta~, from Proto-West Germanic *~miʀdu~ ("reward"), from Proto-Germanic *~mizdō~, from PIE *~misdʰéh₂~ ("reward, compensation"). The PIE root reveals a deep connection between rental payment and the ancient concept of reciprocal reward. Cognate with Gothic ~mizdo~ ("reward"), Old English ~meord~ ("reward"), Greek ~misthos~ ("wages, pay"), and Sanskrit ~midha~ ("reward, prize"). The semantic development from "reward" to "rent" reflects the narrowing of a general concept of compensation into a specific financial transaction. English ~meed~ ("a deserved share, reward"), now archaic and poetic, is the direct English cognate, sharing the same Proto-Germanic ancestor. The modern German word ~Miete~ has also taken on the secondary meaning of an earthen clamp for storing root vegetables, though that ~Miete~ is a separate word entirely, borrowed from Low German and ultimately from Latin ~mēta~ ("cone").
en.minimieren: Derived from ~Minimum~ with the verb-forming suffix ~-ieren~. ~Minimum~ is borrowed from Latin ~minimum~, the neuter superlative of ~minimus~ ("smallest, least"), from ~minor~ ("smaller"), ultimately from PIE *~mey-~ ("small"), the same root that gave English ~minus~, ~minor~, ~minute~, ~diminish~, and ~minister~ (originally "a lesser servant"). The suffix ~-ieren~ is the standard German mechanism for creating verbs from Latin and French borrowings, corresponding to French ~-er~ and English ~-ize~. The antonym ~maximieren~ is built on the same pattern from Latin ~maximum~.
en.mischen:
From Middle High German ~mischen~ ("to mix"), from Old High German ~misken~ ("to mix"), from Proto-Germanic *~miskijaną~ ("to mix"), from PIE *~meyḱ-~ ("to mix, knead"), via the inchoative present *~miḱ-sḱé-~. Cognate with English ~mix~, Latin ~misceō~ ("to mix"), Greek ~mísgo~ ("to mix"), Old Church Slavonic ~měšati~ ("to mix"), Lithuanian ~maišyti~ ("to mix"), Sanskrit ~miśrá~ ("mixed"), Welsh ~mysgu~ ("to mix").

The PIE root *~meyḱ-~ has an astonishingly wide reach across the Indo-European family, appearing in nearly every branch with the same core meaning. The Latin descendant ~misceō~ gave English ~miscellaneous~ and ~promiscuous~ (literally "thoroughly mixed"). The Germanic path through *~miskijaną~ produced both German ~mischen~ and (via Old English *~mixian~) English ~mix~. French ~mélange~ ("mixture") is also probably of Germanic origin, borrowed from Frankish *~misken~ with a fossilized Germanic suffix. The German verb covers both the general sense "to mix" and the specialized card-game sense "to shuffle."
en.mitbringen:
From ~mit-~ + ~bringen~. Compound of:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringaną~, from a pre-Germanic compound root *~bʰrenḱ-~, likely from PIE *~bʰer-~ ("to carry") + *~h₁neḱ-~ ("to bring, reach"). Cognate with English ~bring~, Dutch ~brengen~, Gothic ~briggan~.
- ~mit-~: From MHG ~mit-~, from OHG ~mit-~, ~miti-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~midi~, from Proto-Germanic *~midi~, from PIE *~me~ ("in the middle of, near"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys accompaniment — doing something along with someone or bringing something along.

~bringen~ is one of the most unusual verbs in Germanic: it has a mixed past tense combining both strong (ablaut: ~brachte~) and weak (dental suffix: ~-te~) features — the only known case of this mixture in Proto-Germanic. This anomaly arises from its compound PIE origin. ~mitbringen~ ("to bring along") is among the most common separable-prefix verbs in everyday German — one ~bringt~ a gift ~mit~ to a party, or ~bringt~ experience ~mit~ to a job. The question ~Was soll ich mitbringen?~ ("What should I bring?") is a fixture of German social life. Cognate with Hunsrik ~mitbringe~.
en.mitmachen:
Compound of ~mit-~ + ~machen~:

- ~machen~: From MHG ~machen~, from OHG ~mahhōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~makōn~, from PIE *~mag-~ ("to knead, mix, make"). Cognate with English ~make~, Dutch ~maken~, Low German ~maken~.
- ~mit-~: From MHG ~mit(e)~, from OHG ~mit(i)~, from Proto-Germanic *~midi~ ("with, among"), from PIE *~me~ ("in the middle of, near"). Cognate with English ~mid~ (as in ~amid~, ~midway~), Greek ~meta~ ("with, after").

The literal sense is "to make along with, to do along," meaning "to participate, to join in." The verb is remarkably versatile in colloquial German: ~eine Reise mitmachen~ ("to go along on a trip"), ~eine Mode mitmachen~ ("to follow a fashion"), ~viel mitmachen müssen~ ("to have had to endure a lot"), and ~da mache ich nicht mit~ ("I'm not going along with that"). The English cognate ~make~ and German ~machen~ have tracked closely in meaning for over a millennium, both expanding from a concrete PIE sense of kneading or mixing into the most general-purpose "doing" verb in their respective languages. Cognate also with Hunsrik ~mitmache~, carried to Brazil by German settlers in the 19th century.
en.mitnehmen:
Compound of ~mit-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nëmen~, from OHG ~nëman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemaną~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
- ~mit-~: From PIE *~me~ ("in the middle of, near"). This separable prefix adds the sense of accompaniment or inclusion — taking something along.

~Mitnehmen~ means "to take along, to take with" — ~Nimm einen Regenschirm mit!~ ("Take an umbrella along!"). But it has a vivid colloquial extension: ~mitgenommen~ (past participle) means "worn out, battered, exhausted" — someone who has been "taken along" by life's hardships looks ~mitgenommen~. The fast-food phrase ~zum Mitnehmen~ ("to go, takeaway") has become ubiquitous. The PIE root *~nem-~ is fascinating because it meant both "to give" and "to take" one's allotted portion — the ambiguity reflects a world where distribution and receipt were two sides of the same act. Greek ~νέμω~ ("to distribute, to allot") and ~Nemesis~ (the goddess of rightful distribution turned retribution) descend from the same root. Cognate with Dutch ~meenemen~, Low German ~mitnehmen~, Danish ~nemme~ ("to learn, to grasp" — from the "take in" sense).
en.mitteilen:
From Middle High German ~miteteilen~, equivalent to ~mit-~ + ~teilen~:

- ~teilen~: From MHG ~teilen~, from OHG ~teilen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dailijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~dailijaną~, from PIE *~deh₂y-~ ("to divide, to share").
- ~mit-~: From PIE *~me~ ("in the middle of, near"). The prefix adds the sense of sharing or participating.

~Mitteilen~ literally means "to share a part with" — to divide something (information, news) and give a portion to someone else. The derived noun ~Mitteilung~ ("communication, notification, message") is standard in formal and administrative German. The compound is transparent in its structure: ~mit~ ("with") + ~teilen~ ("to divide") = to divide with someone, i.e., to share information. The related noun ~Mitleid~ ("compassion," literally "suffering-with") shows the same prefix in its empathic sense. Cognate with Dutch ~meedelen~.
en.montieren: Borrowed from French ~monter~ with the addition of the German verbal suffix ~-ieren~. The French source traces from Middle French ~monter~, from Old French ~monter~, from Vulgar Latin *~montāre~, from Latin ~mons~ (~montem~, "mountain"). The original French sense was "to go up, to climb," from the idea of ascending a mountain. The semantic shift to "to mount, to assemble" arose from the notion of placing something up or putting components together, as one might mount a horse or mount a piece of equipment into position. The ~-ieren~ suffix is German's standard mechanism for adapting French and Latin verbs, paralleling patterns like ~reparieren~, ~organisieren~, and ~installieren~. English ~mount~ is a direct cognate from the same French source, and the technical sense of ~montieren~ in German closely mirrors English ~to mount~ in mechanical and engineering contexts. In film, ~Montage~ (borrowed from French) preserves the assembly metaphor, referring to the cutting and joining of shots into a unified sequence.
en.motivieren: Borrowed from French ~motiver~, itself derived from ~motif~ ("motive") + the verbal suffix ~-er~. French ~motif~ descends from Medieval Latin ~motivus~ ("moving, impelling"), from Latin ~motus~, the past participle of ~movere~ ("to move"), from PIE *~mewh₁-~ ("to push away, move"). The German verb adds the standard ~-ieren~ suffix used for French and Latin loanwords. The word entered German during the 18th century as part of the broader wave of French intellectual vocabulary that shaped the language of Enlightenment discourse. English ~motivate~ arrived via the same Latin root but took a slightly different path through the adjective ~motive~. The PIE root *~mewh₁-~ is extraordinarily productive: it also yielded Latin ~momentum~ (a "movement," giving English ~moment~ and ~momentum~), ~mobile~ ("movable"), ~emotion~ (literally "a moving out"), and ~motor~. German ~Motiv~ serves double duty as both "motive" (reason for action) and "motif" (recurring artistic theme), preserving a unity that English splits between two borrowed words.
en.mögen: From Middle High German ~mugen~, from Old High German ~mugan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~magan~, from Proto-Germanic *~maganą~, from PIE *~megʰ-~ ("to be able, have power"). A preterite-present verb: its present-tense conjugations descend from original past-tense forms, which accounts for the absence of personal endings in the first- and third-person singular (~mag~, not *~magt~). Cognate with Dutch ~mogen~, English ~may~, Icelandic ~mega~, Gothic ~magan~.
en.müssen: From Middle High German ~müeȥen~, from Old High German ~muoȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~mōtan~, from Proto-Germanic *~mōtaną~. A preterite-present verb derived from a secondary perfect of PIE *~mēd-~ ("to measure, ensure compliance, take care of"). Alternatively connected to the same root as Proto-Germanic *~mōtą~ ("meeting"). The preservation of the original diphthong can still be seen in Bavarian ~muass~. Cognate with Dutch ~moeten~, English ~must~, Old Norse ~mega~.
en.nachdenken:
~nach-~ + ~denken~. Compound of ~nach-~ + ~denken~:

- ~denken~: From MHG ~denken~, from OHG ~denken~, ~thenken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þankijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þankijaną~, from PIE *~tong-éye-ti~, causative of *~teng-~ ("to think, feel, perceive, know").
- ~nach-~: From PIE *~h₂neḱ~ ("to reach, to attain"). The separable prefix adds a sense of following after, pursuing.

~Nachdenken~ means "to reflect, to ponder, to think carefully about." Where ~denken~ is simply "to think," ~nachdenken~ is to think ~after~ — to follow a thought to its conclusion, to trace its implications. The prefix ~nach-~ here conveys pursuit: the thinker chases after an idea, turning it over repeatedly. The construction ~über etwas nachdenken~ ("to reflect on something") is one of the most common intellectual verbs in German. As a neuter noun, ~das Nachdenken~ ("reflection, contemplation") is used without an article in the elegant phrase ~nach reiflichem Nachdenken~ ("after mature reflection"). Cognate with Hunsrik ~nohdenke~, Dutch ~nadenken~ ("to think over, to ponder").
en.nachfragen:
A compound of ~nach-~ + ~fragen~:

- ~fragen~: From MHG ~vrāgen~, from OHG ~frāgēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frāgēn~. Cognate with Dutch ~vragen~, Swedish ~fråga~.
- ~nach-~: From Old High German ~nāh~ ("near, close"), from Proto-West Germanic *~nāhw~, from Proto-Germanic *~nēhwaz~, from PIE *~h₂neḱ-~ ("to reach, attain"). As a verbal prefix, ~nach-~ adds a sense of "after" or "following up."

The verb means "to inquire further, to follow up with a question" -- asking after something that has already been stated or left unclear. The PIE root *~h₂neḱ-~ also gave rise to Latin ~nancīscor~ ("I obtain") and Sanskrit ~naśati~ ("to reach"), all sharing the idea of reaching or arriving at something. Cognate with Dutch ~navragen~.
en.nachgehen:
From Middle High German ~nāchgān~, from Old High German ~nāhgān~, from Proto-West Germanic *~nāhwagān~. Compound of ~nach-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gān~, ~gēn~, from OHG ~gān~, ~gēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gān~, from Proto-Germanic *~gāną~, from PIE *~ǵʰeh₁-~ ("to leave, go").
- ~nach-~: From OHG ~nāh~ ("near, close"), from Proto-West Germanic *~nāhw~, from Proto-Germanic *~nēhwaz~, from PIE *~h₂neḱ-~ ("to reach, attain"). As a prefix, it conveys "after, following."

The literal meaning is "to go after," which branches into several figurative senses: "to pursue," "to investigate" (as in ~einer Sache nachgehen~), and "to be slow" (of a clock). The spelling with ~-h-~ in ~gehen~ was introduced by analogy with ~sehen~, where the ~h~ had become silent but was retained in writing. Cognate with Dutch ~nagaan~.
en.nachlesen:
Compound of ~nach-~ + ~lesen~:

- ~lesen~: From MHG ~lësen~, from OHG ~lësan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lesan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lesaną~ ("to gather"), from PIE *~les-~ ("to gather"). Cognate with English ~lease~ (with an obsolete meaning "to gather"), Dutch ~lezen~ ("to read"), Afrikaans ~lees~ ("to read").
- ~nach-~: From PIE *~h₂neḱ~ ("to reach, attain"). This separable prefix conveys the idea of following after, reviewing, or looking something up after the fact.

~Nachlesen~ means "to look up, to read up on, to check a reference" -- literally "to read after." The semantic development of ~lesen~ from "to gather" to "to read" is one of the most famous in Germanic linguistics: just as Latin ~legere~ independently shifted from "to gather" to "to read" (whence English ~legible~, ~lesson~, ~lecture~), Germanic speakers transferred the concept of gathering scattered items to gathering meaning from scattered letters. The parallel evolution in two unrelated branches of Indo-European suggests something deep about how humans conceptualize reading: as picking up meaning, symbol by symbol, just as one gathers grapes from a vine -- and indeed, German ~Weinlese~ ("grape harvest") preserves the original "gathering" sense.
en.nachvollziehen:
Compound of ~nach-~ + ~vollziehen~:

- ~vollziehen~: Itself a compound of ~voll~ ("full") + ~ziehen~ ("to pull, draw"), from MHG ~volleziehen~, from OHG ~follaziohan~. ~Voll~ descends from OHG ~foll~, from Proto-Germanic *~fullaz~, from PIE *~pl̥h₁nós~ ("full"). ~Ziehen~ from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhaną~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Compare Dutch ~voltrekken~.
- ~nach-~: From MHG ~nāch~, from OHG ~nāh~, from Proto-Germanic *~nēhw~ ("near, close"), from PIE *~h₂neḱ-~ ("to reach, attain"). As a prefix, it means "after, following, re-."

The literal sense is "to carry out after" — to retrace and reconstruct in one's mind what someone else has done or thought. ~Nachvollziehen~ means "to comprehend, to understand by mentally retracing" and fills a gap that English struggles to express in a single word. It is not mere understanding but empathetic reconstruction — following the same mental path another person walked. The word is a mid-20th-century coinage, surprisingly young for such a deeply embedded concept. The base verb ~vollziehen~ means "to carry out, to execute" (a law, a sentence) — itself a calque of Latin ~exsequī~ ("to follow out"). The PIE root *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead") also yielded Latin ~dūcere~ ("to lead"), whence English ~duke~, ~conduct~, and ~education~.
en.nachweisen:
~nach-~ + ~weisen~. Compound of ~nach-~ + ~weisen~:

- ~weisen~: From MHG ~wīsen~, from OHG ~wīsen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wīsijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wīsijaną~ ("to show, to point out"), from PIE *~weyd-~ ("to see").
- ~nach-~: From PIE *~h₂neḱ~ ("to reach, attain"). The prefix adds the sense of following after, tracing back, or verification through pursuit.

~Nachweisen~ means "to prove, to demonstrate, to detect, to verify" — literally "to show after" or "to point out by following the trail." The prefix ~nach-~ contributes a forensic quality: one does not merely show something (~weisen~), one traces it, follows the evidence, and then shows it to be true. The derived noun ~Nachweis~ ("proof, evidence, certificate, verification") is ubiquitous in German bureaucracy, science, and law — one needs a ~Nachweis~ for everything from vaccination status (~Impfnachweis~) to proof of income (~Einkommensnachweis~) to scientific detection (~DNA-Nachweis~). The verb can take a dative object: ~jemandem etwas nachweisen~ ("to prove something against someone"), often in a legal or investigative context — "to catch someone out." The PIE root *~weyd-~ ("to see") thus achieves its most rigorous expression here: ~nachweisen~ is seeing that has been verified through systematic pursuit. Cognate with Dutch ~nawijzen~ (rare).
en.nehmen: From Middle High German ~nëmen~, from Old High German ~nëman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemaną~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due"). A class IV strong verb. Cognate with Low German ~nehmen~, Dutch ~nemen~, English ~nim~ ("to take, filch"), Danish ~nemme~ ("to learn, grasp"), Swedish ~förnimma~ ("to perceive").
en.neigen:
From a merger of MHG ~nīgen~ (intransitive, strong) and ~neigen~ (transitive, weak), from OHG ~nīgan~ and ~neigen~ respectively, from Proto-Germanic *~hnīganą~ (strong, intransitive: "to bow, to bend") and its causative *~hnaigijaną~ (weak, transitive: "to make bow, to incline"). The strong verb *~hnīganą~ descends from PIE *~kneygʷʰ-~ ("to bend, droop"). Cognate with Dutch ~nijgen~ and ~neigen~, Old English ~hnīgan~ ("to bow") and ~hnǣgan~ ("to cause to bow"), Old Norse ~hnīga~ and ~hneiga~.

The strong forms (~neigen~, ~nieg~, ~geniegen~) vanished from the standard language by the 16th century, though they survive in dialects — making ~neigen~ an example of a once-strong verb that went entirely weak. The modern verb means "to incline, to lean, to tend" — both physically (to tilt) and metaphorically (~dazu neigen~, "to tend to, to be inclined to"). The noun ~Neigung~ ("inclination, tendency") carries both senses. The PIE root *~kneygʷʰ-~ also produced Latin ~conīveō~ ("I blink, I overlook" — literally "I bend together [the eyelids]") and ~nītor~ ("I lean on"), showing how the concept of bending diverged into remarkably different physical actions across the Indo-European branches.
en.nennen: From Middle High German ~nennen~, ~nemmen~, ~nemnen~, from Old High German ~nemnen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~namnijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~namnijaną~, a denominative verb derived from Proto-Germanic *~namô~ ("name"), from PIE *~h₁nómn̥~ ("name"). The verb literally means "to name" — that is, "to perform the act of giving a name" — and has not meaningfully drifted from its PIE origin across more than five millennia. ~Nennen~ exhibits the same mixed conjugation as ~bringen~ and ~denken~: it forms its past tense ~nannte~ with a vowel change plus a weak dental suffix, rather than following either a purely strong or purely weak paradigm. Like ~kennen~ (its close relative via the same PIE root *~ǵneh₃-~ "to know"), ~nennen~ has umlaut suppression in the past tense. Cognate with Old English ~nemnan~, Middle English ~nemnen~, Swedish ~nämna~, and Hunsrik ~nenne~.
en.nerven: A modern denominative verb formed from the noun ~Nerv~ ("nerve"), via the colloquial expression ~auf die Nerven gehen~ ("to get on someone's nerves"). ~Nerv~ was borrowed from Latin ~nervus~ ("sinew, tendon"), partly through French ~nerf~. Latin ~nervus~ comes from earlier *~neuros~ (by metathesis), from Proto-Italic *~(s)nēuros~, from PIE *~snḗh₁wr̥~ ("sinew, tendon"). Cognate with Greek ~neuron~ (whence English ~neuron~ and ~neurotic~) and Old English ~sinu~ ("sinew"). The meaning shift from "sinew" to "nerve" occurred in medical Latin; the further shift to "to annoy" is a twentieth-century colloquial development unique to German, where the anatomical term became a metaphor for irritation.
en.notieren: From Middle High German ~notieren~, borrowed from Latin ~notō~ ("I mark, note, observe"), from ~nota~ ("a mark, sign, note"). ~Nota~ is likely related to ~nōscō~ ("I come to know"), from PIE *~ǵneh₃-~ ("to know"), the same root behind English ~know~, ~notice~, Greek ~gnōsis~, and Sanskrit ~jñā~. The verb entered German through medieval scholarly Latin, reflecting the transmission of literate culture from Roman to Germanic lands. In modern German, ~notieren~ means "to jot down, to note," and also has a specialized financial sense: "to be quoted at" (a price), paralleling English ~note~ in stock-exchange usage. Cognate with French ~noter~, Italian ~notare~, English ~note~.
en.nutzen: From Middle High German ~nutzen~, ~nützen~, from Old High German ~nuzzōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~nutōną~, from *~nutō~ ("use"), from *~nutiz~ ("useful"), from *~neutaną~ ("to enjoy, use, catch"), from PIE *~newd-~ ("to acquire, make use of"). The verb was originally restricted to Upper German and East Central German dialects, the former preferring the conjugation ~nutzen~, the latter preferring ~nützen~. By way of hypercorrection, ~nützen~ has now become a chiefly southern conjugation; Low German and West Central German originally used other verbs (namely ~brauchen~ for the transitive function) and prefer ~nutzen~ in modern standard German. Cognate with modern ~Nutzen~ (noun, "benefit, use").
en.nähen:
From MHG ~nāian~, ~næjen~, from OHG ~nājen~, ~nāian~, from Proto-Germanic *~nēaną~ ("to sew"). Cognate with German ~Nadel~ ("needle") and English ~needle~ (both from the instrument noun formed on the same root), Dutch ~naaien~, Gothic ~nēþla~ ("needle").

The PIE root *~(s)neh₁-~ ("to spin, to sew") is notable for its optional initial ~s-~ — the so-called ~s-mobile~, a mysterious phonological feature of Proto-Indo-European where certain roots could appear with or without an initial ~s-~ with no change in meaning. The ~s-~less variant produced Latin ~nēre~ ("to spin") and Greek ~nêma~ ("thread"), whence English ~nematic~ (describing thread-like liquid-crystal arrangements). The instrument noun *~neh₁-dleh₂~ ("that which sews") traveled through Proto-Germanic *~nēþlō~ to produce both German ~Nadel~ and English ~needle~ — one of the clearest cases where a common tool name reveals a shared craft tradition stretching back to the prehistoric Indo-European homeland.
en.nähern:
From the comparative form ~näher~ ("nearer") with the verbal suffix ~-n~, itself from the adjective ~nah~ ("near"), from MHG ~nāch~, from OHG ~nāh~, from Proto-West Germanic *~nāh~, from Proto-Germanic *~nēhwaz~ ("near"), from PIE *~h₂neḱ-~ ("to reach, attain"). Cognate with English ~nigh~ (from OE ~nēah~), Dutch ~na~.

~Nähern~ is almost always reflexive: ~sich nähern~ ("to approach, to draw near"). The word is a denominal verb built from a comparative: "to make nearer." The PIE root *~h₂neḱ-~ also produced Sanskrit ~naśati~ ("to reach, attain") and Latin ~nancīscī~ ("to obtain"). German ~nach~ ("after, toward") is a doublet of ~nah~ -- both descend from the same root, but ~nach~ specialized as a preposition while ~nah~ remained an adjective. The English cognate ~nigh~ survives mainly in poetry and the fixed phrase "nigh impossible."
en.nützen: From Middle High German ~nützen~, a variant of ~nutzen~, from Old High German ~nuzzōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~nutōną~, *~nutjōną~, derived from *~nutō~ ("use, enjoyment"), from *~nutiz~ ("useful"), from *~neutaną~ ("to enjoy, use"), from PIE *~newd-~ ("to acquire, make use of"). The ~ü~ form arose in East Central German dialects through umlaut, while Upper German dialects preferred ~nutzen~ without umlaut. By hypercorrection, ~nützen~ became the standard southern form, while northern Germany adopted ~nutzen~. Both forms coexist in modern standard German with identical meaning. Cognate with Lithuanian ~nauda~ ("benefit"), Russian ~nuzhda~ ("need"). English ~enjoy~ is unrelated, but the archaic English ~nait~ ("to use") descends from the same Proto-Germanic verb *~neutaną~.
en.offenbaren:
From ~offenbar~ ("manifest, obvious") + ~-en~. ~Offenbar~ is composed of ~offen~ ("open") + ~-bar~ ("-able, carrying"):

- ~offen~: From MHG ~offen~, from OHG ~offan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~opan~, from Proto-Germanic *~upanaz~, originally a past participle meaning "lying on top" and hence "uncovered, open," derived from *~upp~ ("up"), from PIE *~upo~ ("up, over"). Cognate with English ~open~, Dutch ~open~, Swedish ~öppen~.
- ~-bar~: From OHG ~-bāri~, from Proto-Germanic *~-bērijaz~ ("carrying, bearing"), from *~beraną~ ("to bear"), from PIE *~bʰer-~ ("to carry, bear").

The literal sense of ~offenbar~ is thus "carrying openness" -- something that bears itself openly, that is evident. ~Offenbaren~ means "to reveal, to disclose," and in religious usage, "to reveal" in the divine sense, as in die ~Offenbarung~ des Johannes (the Book of Revelation). The past participle may appear as either ~offenbart~ (the prescribed form) or ~geoffenbart~ (with the ~ge-~ prefix that is normally suppressed in inseparable-prefix verbs).
en.optimieren: A denominative verb formed from the noun ~Optimum~ ("optimum, the best state"), with the productive ~-ieren~ suffix. ~Optimum~ was borrowed from Latin ~optimum~, the neuter of ~optimus~ ("best"), the superlative of ~bonus~ ("good"). ~Optimus~ derives from an earlier Latin *~opitumus~, from ~ops~ ("power, resources, wealth"), from PIE *~h₃ep-~ ("to work, to produce"). The same root gave Latin ~opus~ ("work"), ~opera~ ("effort, work"), ~opulentus~ ("wealthy"), and ~officium~ ("duty, office") — whence English ~operate~, ~opulent~, and ~office~. German ~optimieren~ thus etymologically means "to make the most powerful, to bring to peak performance." The word entered German in the 20th century as a technical term from mathematics and engineering, then spread to everyday language. The related adjective ~optimal~ and noun ~Optimierung~ ("optimization") are ubiquitous in modern German business and technology discourse. A nice irony: the PIE root *~h₃ep-~ ("to work") also produced Latin ~opus~, so ~optimieren~ is, at its deepest root, about ~working~ something into its best state. Cognate with Dutch ~optimaliseren~, English ~optimize~, French ~optimiser~.
en.ordnen:
From Middle High German ~ordenen~, from Old High German ~ordinōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ordinōn~ ("to order, put in order"), borrowed from Latin ~ōrdināre~ ("to arrange, set in order"), from ~ōrdō~ ("row, rank, order"), from Proto-Italic *~ordō~, probably from PIE *~h₂er-~ ("to fit together"). Doublet of ~ordinieren~ ("to ordain"). Cognate with English ~order~ (via Old French, from the same Latin source), Dutch ~ordenen~, and Italian ~ordinare~.

The journey from PIE *~h₂er-~ ("to fit together") to modern ~ordnen~ traces a remarkable semantic stability: the idea of fitting things into their proper arrangement has survived over five millennia. Latin ~ōrdō~ originally described a row of threads in a loom — a warp that held everything in place — before generalizing to any row, rank, or sequence. The derivative ~Ordnung~ ("order, tidiness") is practically a national concept: ~Ordnung muss sein~ ("there must be order") is the quintessential German maxim.
en.organisieren: Borrowed from French ~organiser~ (attested from the 17th century), from Medieval Latin ~organizāre~, from Latin ~organum~ ("instrument, tool, organ"), itself a borrowing from Ancient Greek ~órganon~ ("tool, instrument"), from PIE *~werǵ-~ ("to work"). The Greek ~órganon~ is the same word that gave the world the philosophical term "organon" (as in Aristotle's logical works, his tool for reasoning), the musical ~organ~, and the biological ~organ~. The semantic path from "tool" to "organization" runs through the idea of coordinating instruments or parts into a working whole — as individual pipes are organized into a functioning organ. German received ~organisieren~ through French in the same period as many other learned ~-ieren~ borrowings flooded the language. The ~-ieren~ suffix, entering via Old French contact from the 12th century onward, marks it as a loanverb and guarantees weak conjugation. Cognate with English ~organize~, French ~organiser~, Spanish ~organizar~, Italian ~organizzare~.
en.orientieren: Borrowed from French ~orienter~, itself from ~orient~ ("east, the direction of the rising sun"), from Old French ~oriant~, from Latin ~oriens~ ("rising"), the present active participle of ~orior~ ("to rise, to be born"), from Italic *~orjōr~, from PIE *~h₃er-~ ("to stir, to rise"). The underlying image is vivid: medieval European maps placed east at the top (toward the ~Orient~, where the sun rises), so ~orienter~ originally meant "to align a map with east at the top" — that is, to find one's bearings by the sunrise. The reflexive ~sich orientieren~ ("to orient oneself, to get one's bearings") preserves this navigational metaphor. German added the standard ~-ieren~ suffix for French-derived verbs, and the Hunsrik cognate ~orïenteere~ shows the same borrowing pattern among German emigrants in Brazil. Cognate with English ~orient~, Italian ~orientare~, Spanish ~orientar~. The Latin root *~h₃er-~ also gives us Greek ~ὄρνῡμι~ ("to stir up") and Sanskrit ~ṛṇóti~ ("to move").
en.packen: Borrowed from Middle Low German ~packen~, from Old Saxon *~pakko~, from Proto-West Germanic *~pakkō~, from Proto-Germanic *~pakkô~ ("bundle, pack"). The ultimate origin is debated: the word may derive from an early contact with Vulgar Latin *~paccō~ or Celtic languages, or it may be a native Germanic formation; the absence of the expected High German consonant shift (~p~ should have become ~pf~ or ~f~ in OHG) confirms it entered High German from the north via Low German trade routes rather than descending through the native High German line. ~Packen~ means "to pack" but also "to grab, to seize" — the two senses unite in the physical act of grasping and bundling: you ~packst~ things into a suitcase, but you also ~packst~ someone by the arm. The colloquial ~Das packen wir!~ ("We can handle it!") extends the grabbing sense to mastery over a situation. The imperative ~Pack dich!~ ("Beat it!") adds another layer: to pack yourself away, to bundle yourself off. Cognate with English ~pack~, Dutch ~pakken~, Swedish ~packa~, Danish ~pakke~. The noun ~Pack~ in German also means "rabble, riffraff" — a pejorative use where people are treated as a mere bundle, a mass without individuality.
en.passen: Borrowed from French ~passer~, with Dutch ~passen~ as an intermediary and reinforcing influence; compare the German noun ~Pass~. French ~passer~ derives from Middle French ~passer~, from Old French ~passer~, from Vulgar Latin *~passāre~, a denominative verb from Latin ~passus~ ("step, pace"), from ~pandō~ ("to spread, stretch out"), from PIE *~pet-~ ("to spread, extend"). The Latin word ~passus~ measured the Roman double-step — roughly 1.5 meters — and gave English ~pace~ and the unit ~passus~ in Roman surveying. German ~passen~ entered primarily through Dutch trade contact and retains the Dutch sense of "to fit, to suit" alongside the French-influenced sense of "to pass." A notable false friend lurks nearby: English ~pass~ and German ~passen~ overlap in sports contexts (passing a ball), but ~passen~ most commonly means "to fit" or "to suit," not "to pass by." The noun ~Pass~ (mountain pass, passport) is a separate borrowing from the same Latin root. Cognate with French ~passer~, Dutch ~passen~, Italian ~passare~, English ~pass~, ~pace~.
en.passieren:
Borrowed from French ~passer~, from Old French ~passer~, from Vulgar Latin *~passāre~, derived from Latin ~passus~ ("step"), the past participle of ~pandere~ ("to spread out, stretch"), from Proto-Italic *~patnō~, from PIE *~peth₂-~ ("to spread out"). A "step" (Latin ~passus~) was thus literally "a spreading" — of the legs in walking.

German ~passieren~ carries two distinct meanings that developed along different semantic lines: "to pass (a place, a border, an obstacle)" and "to happen, occur." The second sense ("Was ist passiert?" — "What happened?") is a calque of the French intransitive use of ~passer~ in the same sense, particularly in the construction ~il se passe quelque chose~ ("something is happening"). English ~pass~ is a direct cognate from the same Old French source. The Latin unit of measurement the ~passus~ (roughly 1.48 meters, a double step) gave the world the ~mille passuum~ — "a thousand paces" — from which English ~mile~ derives. Cognate with English ~pass~, Dutch ~passeren~, Spanish ~pasar~, Italian ~passare~.
en.pflanzen: From Middle High German ~pflanzen~, from Old High German ~pflanzōn~, a denominative verb from ~pflanza~ ("plant"), which was borrowed from Latin ~planta~ ("a shoot, cutting, plant"). The phonological change from Latin ~pl-~ to German ~pfl-~ is part of the High German consonant shift and is one of the most distinctive markers of Old High German. The same shift turned Latin ~piper~ into German ~Pfeffer~ and Latin ~platea~ into ~Platz~. Latin ~planta~ itself is of uncertain origin but may be related to ~planus~ ("flat") -- a plant being something pressed flat into the soil. English ~plant~ was also borrowed from Latin but shows no consonant shift, having come via Old English from the same Latin source. Cognate with Luxembourgish ~Planz~.
en.pflegen: From Middle High German ~phlëgen~, from Old High German ~plëgan~ ("to take care of, be responsible for, stand up for"), from Proto-West Germanic *~plehan~. The Proto-West Germanic verb is a strong class-5 verb meaning "to stand up for, be responsible for," and its past tense forms survive in the archaic German literary past tense ~pflog~ (rarely used today but occasionally found in elevated or ironic registers). Cognate with Dutch ~plegen~, Old English ~plēon~, Old Norse ~plega~, and Old Saxon ~plegan~. The descendants of *~plehan~ in the reconstruction include a borrowing path into Medieval Latin as *~pleviō~ ("to pledge"), which fed into Old French ~plege~ and ultimately into English ~pledge~ — making ~pflegen~ and English ~pledge~ distant relatives, the one meaning "to tend, nurture, maintain" and the other "a solemn promise," both rooted in the ancient idea of taking responsibility for something.
en.planen:
Denominal verb derived from the noun ~Plan~, which was borrowed from French ~plan~ ("plan, ground plan"). French ~plan~ derives either from the verb ~planter~ ("to plant, set firmly") or from Latin ~planus~ ("flat, level"), the latter being the more widely accepted source. Latin ~planus~ traces from PIE *~pleh₂-~ ("flat, broad"). The architectural sense — a flat drawing representing a structure — then generalized to the modern sense of any scheme or design.

Interestingly, English ~plan~ entered the language via the same French source as German ~Plan~, meaning both languages acquired the word independently through the same medieval and early-modern channel of French architectural and technical vocabulary. The related Latin ~planus~ also gives German ~Plan~ its second meaning of "plain, flat terrain" (as in ~Hochebene~), though the planning sense has long since become dominant. Cognate with Dutch ~plannen~, English ~plan~, Swedish ~planera~, French ~planifier~.
en.platzieren: From French ~placer~ ("to place") with the German suffix ~-ieren~, a verb-forming ending used to adapt foreign (especially French and Latin) words into German conjugation patterns. A later spelling reform reanalyzed the word as ~Platz~ + ~-ieren~, aligning it visually with its semantic root. The noun ~Platz~ descends from MHG ~plaz~, borrowed from Old French ~place~, from Latin ~platēa~ ("broad street"), from Greek ~plateia hodos~ ("broad way"), from PIE *~pleth₂-~ ("broad, flat"). This makes ~platzieren~ a case of round-trip borrowing: a Latin word traveled into French, was borrowed into German as a noun (~Platz~), and then the French verb derived from the same Latin root was borrowed separately with ~-ieren~, only for German speakers to later reinterpret the verb as derived from their own noun. Cognate with English ~place~ (via Norman French), Dutch ~plaatsen~, Italian ~piazzare~. The PIE root *~pleth₂-~ also gives English ~flat~, ~plaza~, and ~platypus~ ("flat-footed"), making this humble rental-office verb a distant cousin of the duck-billed platypus.
en.positionieren: Derived from ~Position~ ("position") with the verb-forming suffix ~-ieren~. ~Position~ was borrowed from Latin ~positiō~ ("a placing, position"), from ~positus~ ("placed"), the past participle of ~pōnō~ ("I place, put"), from an older form *~posinō~, a compound of *~po-~ (from PIE *~h₂epo~ "off, away") + *~sinō~ ("I let, place"). The PIE root *~tḱey-~ ("to settle, dwell") has been proposed as the ultimate source. German ~positionieren~ means "to position" and has gained currency in both physical and abstract senses (as in ~sich positionieren~, "to take a stance"). The suffix ~-ieren~ marks it as a learned borrowing, distinguishing it from native Germanic compounds like ~hinstellen~.
en.posten:
Borrowed from English ~post~ (in the internet sense "to post a message"), itself from English ~post~ ("mail, dispatch"), from French ~poste~, from Italian ~posta~ ("station, mail"), from Vulgar Latin *~posta~, from Latin ~posita~ (feminine past participle of ~pōnere~ "to place"), originally referring to a relay station where horses were ~placed~ for messengers.

~Posten~ is one of the youngest verbs in the German language, entering common usage in the 1990s with the rise of internet forums and then social media. It means "to post" a message, photo, or update online. The irony of its etymology is rich: a word that began with Roman relay riders placing fresh horses at stations along imperial roads now describes placing digital content on servers. The German language already had an older ~posten~ (from the noun ~Posten~, "post, position, guard duty"), but the internet sense is unmistakably an English borrowing. The ~Duden~ dictionary officially recognized the internet sense in the early 2000s. Unlike many English borrowings that German speakers resist (the infamous ~Denglisch~ debate), ~posten~ has been widely accepted, perhaps because the German alternatives — ~veröffentlichen~ ("to publish"), ~einstellen~ ("to place in") — feel too formal for the casual act of sharing a photo of one's lunch.
en.probieren:
From Latin ~probō~ ("to approve, test, examine"), from ~probus~ ("good, fit"), from PIE *~pro-bʰuH-s~ ("being in front"), composed of *~pro-~ ("forward") + *~bʰuH-~ ("to be"). A doublet of ~proben~ ("to rehearse") and ~prüfen~ ("to test, examine"), all three descending from the same Latin source by different routes and at different periods.

The meaning "to try, to taste, to sample" represents the examining/testing sense of Latin ~probō~ rather than the approving sense. The German proverb ~Probieren geht über Studieren~ ("Trying beats studying") captures the empirical spirit the word carries. The ~-ieren~ suffix marks it as a learned Latin borrowing, distinguishing it from the more natively adapted ~prüfen~ (which entered earlier and underwent more thorough sound changes, passing through Old French ~prover~ and Middle High German ~prüeven~). The same Latin root gives English ~prove~, ~probe~, ~probable~, ~probation~, and ~approbation~. The Italian ~provare~, Spanish ~probar~, and French ~prouver~ are all sisters of ~probieren~.
en.produzieren: Borrowed from Latin ~prōdūcō~ ("to lead forth, bring out, extend, produce"), a compound of ~prō-~ ("forward, forth") + ~dūcō~ ("to lead, draw"), from Proto-Italic *~doukō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dewk-~ ("to draw, pull"). English ~tow~ (as in to tow a boat) is a cognate via the Germanic branch of the same PIE root. The Latin verb's core meaning was spatial — "to bring something forward" or "to extend" — which extends naturally to agricultural and theatrical production: ~prōdūcere~ was used of bringing a crop to fruition or of staging a performance. German borrowed ~produzieren~ with the productive ~-ieren~ suffix, which always creates weak verbs from Latin and French loanwords. Cognate with English ~produce~, French ~produire~, Spanish ~producir~, Italian ~produrre~. The related Latin ~dux~ ("leader," from the same ~dūcō~) gave Italian ~duce~ and English ~duke~ — a "leader" is, etymologically, someone who draws others forward.
en.profitieren: Borrowed from French ~profiter~ ("to benefit, profit"), from Old French ~profit~ (modern French ~profit~), from Latin ~prōfectus~ ("advance, progress, growth, increase, profit"), the past participle of ~prōficiō~ ("to go forward, advance, make progress, be useful"), a compound of ~prō-~ ("forward") + ~faciō~ ("to make, do"). The Latin root sense is fundamentally one of forward motion: profit is progress. English ~profit~ arrived via the same Old French channel. German ~profitieren~ was formed with the standard ~-ieren~ suffix, marking it as a French loanverb with weak conjugation. Cognate with English ~profit~, French ~profiter~, Spanish ~profitar~, Italian ~profittare~.
en.prägen: ~Prägen~ ("to mint, coin; to shape, leave an impression") derives from Middle High German ~bræchen~ / ~præchen~ ("to press in, to depict"), from Old High German ~giprāhhan~ / ~prāhhan~ ("to press in or scratch by breaking the surface"). These forms arose from the first and third person plural preterite forms of ~prēchan~ / ~prāchun~ ("to break"), making ~prägen~ a verb that is likely related to ~brechen~ ("to break"). The noun ~Prägung~ ("imprint, coinage, stamp") shares the root. The semantic development moves from physically breaking or incising a surface — the action of a die striking metal to mint a coin — to the broader sense of leaving a lasting impression: ~ein Erlebnis prägt einen~ ("an experience shapes you"). The abstract sense dominates in modern usage, but the metalworking origin remains visible in ~Münzprägung~ ("coin minting").
en.präsentieren: Inherited from Middle High German ~prēsentieren~, borrowed from Old French ~présenter~ (modern French ~présenter~). The French verb derives from Latin ~praesentō~ ("to place before, present"), a denominative of ~praesens~ ("present, at hand"), itself the present active participle of ~praesum~ ("to be before, preside over"), from ~prae-~ ("before") + ~sum~ ("to be"), from PIE *~h₁es-~ ("to be"). The ~-ieren~ suffix — German's productive borrowing suffix for foreign verbs — arrived through Old French contact beginning in the 12th–13th centuries and became the standard treatment for learned Latin and French borrowings, distinguishing them from native Germanic strong verbs (compare ~schreiben~, borrowed from Latin ~scrībō~ directly in the Roman period and fully assimilated into the strong-verb system). A word that has traveled from PIE "to be" through Latin "to be before" through French "to place before" to modern German boardrooms and PowerPoint slides. Cognate with French ~présenter~, English ~present~ (verb), Spanish ~presentar~, Italian ~presentare~.
en.prüfen: From Middle High German ~prüeven~ (first attested in the 13th century), ultimately from Latin ~probāre~ ("to test, approve, prove"), a denominative of ~probus~ ("good, honest, upright"), from Proto-Italic *~proβwos~, from Proto-Indo-European *~pro-bʰuH-wó-s~ ("being foremost, excellent"), from *~pro-~ ("forward") + *~bʰuH-~ ("to be"). Unlike the ~-ieren~ borrowings, ~prüfen~ entered German very early — before the ~-ieren~ suffix pattern was productive — and was fully assimilated into the native weak-verb system. The route was almost certainly through Middle Dutch or Middle Low German, which would explain the umlaut (~ü~ rather than ~o~): the form *~prōfijan~ underwent High German umlaut to produce ~prüeven~. English ~prove~ arrived from the same Latin source via Old French, as did ~probe~ (a more direct Latin borrowing). That ~prüfen~, ~prove~, and ~probe~ are all triplets from the same Latin root — with ~prüfen~ the oldest German survivor — illustrates how a single Latin verb can splinter into phonologically distinct words across centuries of borrowing. Cognate with English ~prove~, ~probe~, Dutch ~proeven~, French ~prouver~.
en.punkten:
From the noun ~Punkt~ ("point, dot"), from Middle High German ~punct~, borrowed from Late Latin ~pūnctus~ ("point, dot"), from Latin ~pungō~ ("to prick, puncture"), from PIE *~pewǵ-~ ("to prick, punch").

~Punkten~ is a relatively modern back-formation meaning "to score, to gain points." While ~Punkt~ has been in German since the medieval period, the verb ~punkten~ only became common in the 20th century, initially in sports journalism. The PIE root *~pewǵ-~ ("to prick") also produced Latin ~pugnus~ ("fist") and Greek ~pugmḗ~ ("fist, boxing") — whence English ~pugilist~. The semantic journey from "prick" to "point" to "score" is delightful: a tiny puncture mark became a dot, a dot became a unit of scoring, and the noun became a verb. German ~Punkt~ is also a false friend for English speakers: ~Punkt drei Uhr~ means "at three o'clock sharp."
en.qualifizieren:
From Medieval Latin ~qualificāre~ ("to characterize, to designate"), a compound of Latin ~quālis~ ("of what kind") + ~-ficāre~ (from ~facere~ "to make"), from PIE *~kʷo-~ ("who, what," the interrogative stem) + *~dʰeh₁-~ ("to put, to make"). Borrowed into German in the early modern period.

~Qualifizieren~ means "to qualify" -- both transitively ("to make suitable, to describe") and reflexively (~sich qualifizieren~, "to become qualified"). The PIE interrogative stem *~kʷo-~ is one of the most productive roots in the family: it generated Latin ~quis~ ("who"), ~quod~ ("what"), ~quandō~ ("when"), English ~who~, ~what~, ~when~, ~where~, ~which~, ~how~, German ~wer~, ~was~, ~wann~, ~wo~, ~welch~, and ~wie~. Every question word in both languages traces back to this single interrogative particle.
en.raten:
From Middle High German ~rāten~, from Old High German ~rātan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rādan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rēdaną~ ("to decide, advise, interpret"), from PIE *~Hreh₁dh-~ ("to think, arrange"). Cognate with Dutch ~raden~, English ~read~ (originally "to counsel, interpret"), Danish ~råde~.

The dual meanings "to advise" and "to guess" both descend from the original Germanic sense of deliberation and interpretation. English ~read~ underwent an even more dramatic semantic shift: from "to interpret, counsel" (as in Old English ~rǣdan~) to the modern sense of deciphering written text. The German noun ~Rat~ ("counsel, advice") and ~Rathaus~ ("town hall," literally "counsel house") preserve the older advisory meaning. The archaic English word ~rede~ ("counsel") is a direct cognate.
en.rauchen: From Middle High German ~rouchen~, ~röuchen~, from Old High German ~rouhhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raukijan~, derived from the noun *~rauki-~ ("smoke"), from Proto-Germanic *~raukiz~, from *~reukaną~ ("to smoke"), possibly from PIE *~h₁rewg-~ ("to belch, emit"). The noun survives as modern ~Rauch~ ("smoke"). Cognate with English ~reek~, Dutch ~rook~ ("smoke"), Swedish ~rök~, Danish ~røg~. In Old English, ~rēocan~ meant simply "to smoke" or "to steam," but its descendant ~reek~ has taken on a strongly negative connotation ("to stink"). The specific sense "to smoke tobacco" entered German in the late seventeenth century as a calque of French ~fumer~, itself a semantic extension from "to emit smoke" to "to inhale smoke." The related verb ~räuchern~ ("to smoke food, to fumigate") preserves the umlauted form that was once a variant of ~rauchen~ itself.
en.reagieren: Borrowed from Medieval Latin ~reagō~ ("to act back, react"), a compound of ~re-~ ("back, again") + ~agō~ ("to drive, act"), from Proto-Italic *~agō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂eǵ-~ ("to drive"). Latin ~agō~ is one of the most productive roots in the Latin lexicon: it underlies ~agent~, ~agenda~, ~action~, ~exact~, and ~navigate~ (via ~navis~ + ~agere~, "to drive a ship"). The Medieval Latin ~reagō~ is itself a scholarly coinage of the early modern period, formed in chemistry and natural philosophy to describe the behavior of substances upon one another. German borrowed ~reagieren~ directly from this Neo-Latin scientific vocabulary in the 17th–18th century, using the standard ~-ieren~ suffix that naturalizes Latin verbs into the German weak-verb system. Cognate with English ~react~, French ~réagir~, Dutch ~reageren~, Spanish ~reaccionar~.
en.realisieren:
Borrowed from French ~réaliser~, from Middle French ~real~ ("actual") + ~-iser~, from Old French ~reel~, from Latin ~reālis~ ("of or relating to things"), from ~rēs~ ("thing, event, deed, fact").

The French verb ~réaliser~ entered German carrying the sense "to make real, to implement" — its core meaning. The sense "to become aware of" (~to realize~ in the English cognitive sense) is a semantic loan from English ~realize~ and is colloquially common but considered an anglicism, sometimes frowned upon by purists. So ~realisieren~ in formal German means "to bring something into existence," not merely to notice it — an important distinction for learners translating English sentences. The Latin ~rēs~ is one of the most ancient and productive roots in the language: it also underlies ~real~, ~republic~ (~rēs pūblica~, "public matter"), and ~reify~.
en.recherchieren: Borrowed from French ~rechercher~ ("to search again, investigate"), from Old French ~recerchier~, a compound of ~re-~ ("again") + ~cerchier~ ("to look for, search"), from Latin ~circāre~ ("to go around"), from ~circus~ ("circle"), from Greek ~kirkos~, from PIE *~(s)ker-~ ("to turn, bend"). The word thus carries the literal sense of "searching again" -- going back over ground already covered. English ~research~ comes from the same French source. Interestingly, ~recherchieren~ in German specifically means investigative research (as a journalist or detective would do), while academic research is typically ~forschen~. French ~recherché~ ("sought after, exquisite") comes from the same root and has been borrowed directly into English as a term for something elaborately refined.
en.rechnen:
From Middle High German ~rechenen~, from Old High German ~rehhanōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rekanōn~ ("to count, explain"), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₃reǵ-~ ("to make straight or right"). Cognate with Low German ~rekenen~, Dutch ~rekenen~, English ~reckon~, Danish ~regne~.

The PIE root *~h₃reǵ-~ is also the ancestor of Latin ~rectus~ ("straight, right") and its English descendants — ~correct~, ~direct~, ~erect~. German ~recht~ ("right, correct") is a sibling from the same root. The semantic link is not accidental: to reckon is to make things come out right, to straighten the accounts. English ~reckon~ (from Old English ~recenian~) shares this ancestry directly — it was first attested meaning "to explain, set in order" before narrowing to calculation and then to the colloquial sense "to suppose, figure." The connection between rightness and reckoning runs deep: justice was long conceived as a kind of arithmetic, and ~Gericht~ ("court of law") shares the same ~recht~ root. Cognate with Dutch ~rekenen~, English ~reckon~, Latin ~rectus~.
en.rechtfertigen:
From Middle High German ~rëhtvertegen~/~rëhtvertigen~, derived from MHG ~rëhtvertec~/~rëhtvertic~ ("just, proper, righteous"). The compound consists of ~recht~ + ~fertigen~:

- ~recht~: From MHG ~rëht~, from OHG ~rëht~, from Proto-West Germanic *~reht~ ("right, straight"), from Proto-Germanic *~rehtaz~, from PIE *~h₃reǵ-~ ("to straighten, direct"). Cognate with English ~right~, Dutch ~recht~, Latin ~rēctus~.
- ~fertigen~: From MHG ~vertigen~/~vertegen~ ("to prepare, make ready"), from the adjective ~fertig~, from OHG ~fartīg~, derived from ~fart~ ("journey"). The original sense was "to make ready for a journey."

The compound literally meant "to make right" or "to prepare righteousness," evolving into the modern legal and moral sense of "to justify, to defend one's actions." The derived noun ~Rechtfertigung~ is the standard German equivalent of English ~justification~. In theology, ~Rechtfertigung~ is the key term for the Protestant doctrine of justification by faith (~sola fide~), central to Luther's Reformation. English ~justify~ comes from Latin ~iustificāre~, a calque of the same concept: ~iustus~ ("just") + ~facere~ ("to make").
en.reden: From Middle High German ~reden~, from Old High German ~redōn~, ~rediōn~ ("to speak"), a denominal verb from ~reda~, ~redia~ ("speech, word, opinion, view, mind"), from Proto-Germanic *~raþjǭ~ ("accountability, speech"). Cognate with Latin ~ratiō~ ("account, reason, proof") — a striking parallel: the Latin noun that gives us English ~ratio~, ~rational~, and ~reason~ is a cognate of the German word for everyday talk. The shared PIE root carried the sense of organized, accountable speech — speaking with clarity and purpose. English ~rede~ ("counsel, advice") is also a cognate, surviving in archaic or dialectal use, and the same root underlies German ~Rat~ ("counsel, advice"). To speak (~reden~), in this etymology, is to render an account.
en.reduzieren: Borrowed from Latin ~redūcō~ ("to lead back, bring back, restore, reduce"), a compound of ~re-~ ("back") + ~dūcō~ ("to lead, draw"), from Proto-Italic *~doukō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dewk-~ ("to draw, pull") — the same root underlying ~produzieren~ and, via the Germanic branch, English ~tow~. The original Latin sense of ~redūcō~ was entirely spatial and military: "to lead troops back, to withdraw." The mathematical sense — reducing a fraction, reducing an equation — is a metaphorical extension of the idea of "bringing back" to a simpler or more fundamental state. German borrowed ~reduzieren~ through French ~réduire~ or directly from the Latin learned vocabulary, applying the standard ~-ieren~ suffix. Cognate with English ~reduce~, French ~réduire~, Spanish ~reducir~, Italian ~ridurre~.
