The Latin ~dux~ ("leader," from the same ~dūcō~) underlies English ~duke~ — making ~reduzieren~ and ~Herzog~ surprising distant relatives by way of the PIE root *~dewk-~.
en.reflektieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~reflectere~ ("to bend back, to turn back"), composed of ~re-~ ("back, again") + ~flectere~ ("to bend"). Entered German in the early seventeenth century with the optical meaning "to reflect (light)," then developed the intellectual meaning "to reflect (upon), to contemplate" in the second half of that century, and by the mid-eighteenth century acquired the further sense "to aspire to, to have designs on" (~auf etwas reflektieren~).

~Reflektieren~ is a remarkable case of semantic layering across centuries: the physical meaning (a mirror ~reflektiert~ light), the intellectual meaning (~über etwas reflektieren~, "to reflect on something"), and the aspirational meaning (~auf eine Stelle reflektieren~, "to have one's eye on a position") coexist in modern German. The Latin root ~flectere~ ("to bend") also produced English ~flexible~, ~deflect~, ~inflect~, and ~genuflect~. The aspirational third sense is uniquely German -- neither English ~reflect~ nor French ~refléchir~ developed this usage, making it a false friend in that particular register.
en.regeln: ~Regeln~ is a denominal verb formed directly from the noun ~Regel~ ("rule, regulation") with the infinitive suffix ~-n~. ~Regel~ entered German in Old High German as ~regula~, borrowed from Latin ~rēgula~ ("straight stick, ruler, rule"), a diminutive of Latin ~regere~ ("to rule, direct, keep straight"), from Proto-Italic *~regō~ and Proto-Indo-European *~h₃reǵ-~ ("to straighten, make right"). That PIE root is extraordinarily productive: it yielded Latin ~rēx~ ("king"), Sanskrit ~rājati~ ("to rule"), and — via French — English ~rule~, ~royal~, ~rector~, and ~correct~. The cognate English ~rail~ (as in a fence rail) also descends from ~rēgula~ via Old French. So when one ~regelt~ a Situation, one is etymologically "putting it on a straight stick."
en.regen:
From MHG ~regen~ ("to make protrude, to erect, to excite, to move"), from Proto-Germanic *~hragjaną~ ("to make stand, wake up, incite"). By synchronic analysis, the causative of MHG ~ragen~ ("to project, to tower").

~Regen~ (the verb, not to be confused with ~Regen~ the noun meaning "rain," which has a completely different etymology) means "to stir, to move, to rouse." It is most commonly encountered in the reflexive ~sich regen~ ("to stir, to move, to show signs of life"): ~Kein Blatt regt sich~ ("Not a leaf stirs"), ~der Widerstand regt sich~ ("resistance is stirring"). The proverb ~Sich regen bringt Segen~ ("To stir brings blessing," i.e., hard work pays off) is one of Germany's most beloved rhyming proverbs. The derived adjective ~rege~ ("lively, active, brisk") preserves the positive-energy sense. The compound ~an-regen~ ("to stimulate, to inspire") and its noun ~Anregung~ ("stimulus, suggestion") are among the verb's most productive descendants.
en.registrieren:
Borrowed from Medieval Latin ~registrāre~ ("to record, enter in a register"), from ~registrum~ ("register, list"), a singular form of Late Latin ~regesta~ ("items recorded"), neuter plural of ~regestus~, past participle of Latin ~regerere~ ("to carry back, to record"), from ~re-~ ("back") + ~gerere~ ("to carry, bear"). The ~-ieren~ suffix is the standard German adaptation of French/Latin ~-er~ / ~-āre~ verb endings, used productively for learned and technical borrowings since the Middle High German period.

The word entered German during the late medieval expansion of administrative and legal Latin. Cognate in formation with French ~registrer~, Italian ~registrare~, English ~register~. Some scholars note that the meaning of ~registrum~ was influenced by association with unrelated Latin ~regere~ ("to rule"), giving it an added connotation of official authority. The verb remains firmly technical and administrative in register: one ~registriert~ births, trademarks, and domain names. The reflexive ~sich registrieren~ ("to register oneself") has gained new life in the digital age as the standard verb for creating online accounts.
en.regnen: From Middle High German ~regenen~, from Old High German ~reganōn~, a denominative verb derived from the noun ~regan~ ("rain"), from Proto-West Germanic *~regn~, from Proto-Germanic *~regną~. The PIE origin is uncertain but possibly *~Hreǵʰ-~ ("to flow water"), which may also be the source of Latin ~rigō~ ("I irrigate, moisten") and Albanian ~rrjedh~ ("to flow"). The verb is impersonal in German (~es regnet~, "it rains"), a construction inherited from Proto-Germanic, where weather verbs lacked a real subject. Cognate with English ~rain~, Dutch ~regenen~, Danish ~regne~, Swedish ~regna~. The English expression "it's raining cats and dogs" has no German equivalent; instead, German says ~es gießt wie aus Eimern~ ("it pours as if from buckets").
en.reichen:
From Middle High German ~reichen~, from Old High German ~reihhen~, ~reichen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raikijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~raikijaną~, from PIE *~Hroyǵ-éye-ti~, a causative o-grade of *~Hreyǵ-~ ("to stretch out, reach"). Cognate with Lithuanian ~ráižytis~ ("to keep stretching"), Old Irish ~rigid~ ("to stretch").

The PIE causative formation — "to cause to stretch" — evolved into the broad semantic range of Modern German ~reichen~: "to reach, extend," "to hand (something) to someone," "to suffice, be enough." The sense of "sufficiency" (as in ~das reicht~, "that's enough") developed from the physical image of something extending far enough to arrive at its goal. English ~reach~ (from the same root) covers the first two senses but not the third; no single English word captures all three meanings. The ~er-~ prefixed form ~erreichen~ ("to achieve, attain") strips away the sense of sufficiency and focuses on completion — the goal is not just approached but fully reached. Cognate with Dutch ~reiken~, English ~reach~, Danish ~række~.
en.reinigen: From Middle High German ~reinegen~, ~reinigen~, a denominative verb derived from the adjective ~rein~ ("pure, clean"). ~Rein~ descends from MHG ~reine~, from OHG ~reini~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hrainī~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrainiz~, from PIE *~krey-~ ("to divide, to sift, to separate"). The semantic path from "sifting" to "clean" is intuitive: purity is what remains after impurities have been sifted away. The same PIE root *~krey-~ produced Greek ~krī́nō~ ("to separate, to decide, to judge") — whence English ~crisis~, ~critic~, and ~criterion~ — and Old Irish ~criathar~ ("sieve"). English ~riddle~ (the sieve, not the puzzle) is also a cognate. The Germanic *~hr-~ cluster (from PIE *~kr-~) lost its initial ~h~ in High German but survives in Old Norse ~hreinn~ ("clean, pure"), which via Norse settlers entered Old French as ~raïncer~ ("to cleanse"), giving English ~rinse~. So ~reinigen~ and ~rinse~ are distant cousins, reunited through a chain of borrowings. Cognate with Dutch ~reinigen~, Swedish ~ren~ ("clean"), Icelandic ~hreinn~.
en.reisen: From Middle High German ~reisen~ ("to set out on a campaign, to travel"), from Old High German ~reisōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raisōn~, derived from a form akin to Proto-Germanic *~raizijaną~ ("to cause to rise"). The Proto-Germanic root *~rīsaną~ ("to rise") is the direct ancestor of both Old English ~rīsan~ and thus English ~rise~. German ~reisen~ is a cognate of English ~rise~, with the German verb retaining an older meaning "to fall" alongside the travel sense — both descending from a root about directional movement, setting out, departing. The semantic shift from "rising up, setting out" to "traveling" happened through the military context: a ~Reise~ was originally a campaign, a sortie, an armed departure. Only later did the word shed its martial sense and become the everyday word for a journey.
en.reiten: From Middle High German ~rīten~, from Old High German ~rītan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rīdan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rīdaną~, from PIE *~Hreydʰ-~ ("to set in motion, ride"), likely a suffixed form of *~h₃reyH-~ ("to move, churn"). Cognate with English ~ride~, Dutch ~rijden~, Danish ~ride~, Swedish ~rida~, Old Irish ~réidid~ ("to ride, drive"). German ~reiten~ has narrowed to mean riding on horseback (or on an animal), whereas English ~ride~ has broadened to include vehicles. For traveling by car or bus, German uses ~fahren~. In Dutch, ~rijden~ has taken the opposite path from German, primarily meaning "to drive (a vehicle)" rather than horseback riding. The past participle ~geritten~ preserves the old strong conjugation pattern with vowel change (ei to i), a relic of the Indo-European ablaut system.
en.reißen:
From Middle High German ~rīȥen~, from Old High German ~rīȥan~ ("to scratch"), from a conflation of two similar Proto-West Germanic verbs:

- *~wrītan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wrītaną~, from PIE *~wer-~ ("to cut, scratch, tear, sketch an outline")
- *~hrītan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrītaną~

Cognate with Dutch ~rijten~ ("to rip, tear"), Low German ~rieten~ ("to rip, tear"), Luxembourgish ~räissen~ ("to scratch, tear").

The merger of two distinct verbs into one is a rare etymological event, and it left ~reißen~ with an unusually broad semantic field: "to tear, rip, pull, snatch, sketch." The sketching sense (as in ~Grundriss~ = "floor plan," literally "ground-sketch") preserves the older "scratch, inscribe" meaning from *~wrītaną~. This same root gave English ~write~ — which originally meant "to scratch, carve" (runes into wood or stone) before shifting to its modern meaning. Thus German ~reißen~ ("to tear") and English ~write~ are distant cousins, both descending from the act of scratching marks into a surface. The connection is also visible in Swedish ~rita~ ("to draw, sketch") and Icelandic ~ríta~ ("to scratch, write"). Compare also Dutch ~wrijten~ ("to argue, quarrel"), where the "scratching" metaphor extends to verbal conflict.
en.rennen:
From a merger of two Old High German verbs: transitive/causative ~rennan~ ("to make something run or flow"), from Proto-Germanic *~rannijaną~, the causative of *~rinnaną~ ("to run, to flow"); and intransitive ~rinnan~ ("to run, to flow"), from Proto-Germanic *~rinnaną~, from PIE *~h₃reyH-~ ("to undulate, churn"). A similar merger of transitive and intransitive roots occurred in Dutch ~rennen~ and English ~run~.

The PIE root *~h₃reyH-~ connects running with flowing water — a vivid equation of rapid human motion with the surge of a stream. This root also produced Middle Irish ~rian~ ("river, way"), Proto-Slavic *~rěka~ ("river," whence Russian ~reká~), Latin ~rīvus~ ("stream," whence English ~river~ and ~rival~ — originally "one who shares the same stream"), and, most strikingly, the Celtic-Gaulish ~Rēnos~ ("that which flows"), which is the source of the name of the river ~Rhine~. The semantic collapse of two distinct Proto-Germanic verbs — causative *~rannijaną~ and intransitive *~rinnaną~ — into a single modern verb is mirrored in English ~run~, where the transitive ("to run a business") and intransitive ("to run fast") coexist in one lemma. The German noun ~Rinne~ ("gutter, channel") preserves the original water-flow sense.
en.reparieren:
Borrowed in the 16th century from Latin ~reparāre~ ("to restore, renew, repair"), from ~re-~ ("back, again") + ~parāre~ ("to prepare, provide, arrange"), from PIE *~perh₃-~ ("to grant, provide"). Compare English ~repair~ (via Old French ~reparer~, from the same Latin source).

The Latin root ~parāre~ ("to prepare") is spectacularly productive in European languages. From it descend English ~prepare~, ~compare~, ~separate~, ~apparatus~, and ~pare~ — each prefixed form taking the "arrange, make ready" sense in a different direction. Unlike the native German word ~flicken~ ("to patch, mend"), which evokes homespun repair, ~reparieren~ carries a more professional, technical connotation — one ~flickt~ a torn sock but ~repariert~ a broken machine.
en.repräsentieren: Borrowed from Latin ~repraesentāre~ ("to bring before, make present, represent"), a compound of ~re-~ ("again, back") + ~praesentāre~ ("to present, show"), from ~praesēns~ ("present, at hand"), from ~prae-~ ("before") + the present participle of ~esse~ ("to be"). The Latin verb originally meant "to make something present again" -- to bring an absent thing vividly before the mind. German ~repräsentieren~ entered the language through learned Latin usage, with the ~ä~ reflecting German phonological adaptation. It carries both the political sense ("to represent a constituency") and the social sense ("to make an impressive appearance"), the latter being particularly prominent in German. The suffix ~-ieren~ marks it as a learned Latinate borrowing, as with ~positionieren~ and ~reservieren~.
en.reservieren: Borrowed from French ~réserver~, from Latin ~reservāre~ ("to keep back, save up"), a compound of ~re-~ ("back, again") + ~servāre~ ("to preserve, keep, guard"). Latin ~servāre~ derives from a denominative to Proto-Italic *~serwā~ or *~serwom~ ("observation, heedance"), ultimately from PIE *~ser-~ ("to guard, watch over"). The same Latin root gives English ~reserve~, ~preserve~, ~conserve~, and ~observe~ (literally "to watch toward"). In German, ~reservieren~ is used primarily for booking tables, seats, and hotel rooms, where English would say "to reserve." The related noun ~Reservierung~ ("reservation") and ~Reserve~ ("reserve, backup") are also thoroughly naturalized in German. Cognate with French ~réserver~, Italian ~riservare~, Spanish ~reservar~.
en.retten: From Middle High German ~retten~, from Old High German ~hretten~ or ~retten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hraddjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hradjaną~ ("to save, rescue"). Cognate with Dutch ~redden~, English ~redd~ (dialectal for "to tidy, clear"), and the partially related English ~rid~ — the latter borrowed partly from an Old English form of the same root, ~hreddan~ ("to deliver, rescue"). The English connection is a surprise: when speakers say "to rid oneself of something," they are reaching for a word that shares ancient ancestry with German ~retten~ ("to rescue, save").
en.richten:
From Middle High German ~richten~, from Old High German ~rihten~ ("to straighten, set right, direct"), from Proto-West Germanic *~rihtijan~ ("to make straight"), from Proto-Germanic *~rihtijaną~, a denominative verb derived from the adjective *~rehtaz~ ("straight, right"), from PIE *~h₃reǵtós~ ("right, straightened"), from *~h₃reǵ-~ ("to stretch out, straighten, direct"). The same PIE root *~h₃reǵ-~ underlies Latin ~regō~ ("I rule, direct"), Sanskrit ~rájati~ ("rules"), and ultimately English ~right~, ~reign~, and ~regiment~. A causative denominative: "to make (something) straight/right."

The modern semantic range of ~richten~ extends in several directions from the original sense of straightening: "to direct" (~den Blick richten~, "to direct one's gaze"), "to judge" (literally "to set right according to law"), "to execute" (~hinrichten~, still in use), and "to set up" (~Richtfest~, the topping-out ceremony when roof beams are set straight). The judicial sense is ancient: Gothic ~garaihtjan~ already carried it. Cognate with Dutch ~richten~, English ~right~, Old English ~rihtian~, Gothic ~raihtjan~.
en.riechen:
From MHG ~riechen~, from OHG ~riohhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~reukan~, from Proto-Germanic *~reukaną~, from PIE *~h₁rewg-~ ("to belch, to roar"). Cognate with English ~reek~, Dutch ~rieken~, Danish ~ryge~ ("to smoke"), Swedish ~ryka~ ("to steam, smoke"). Related to German ~Rauch~ ("smoke") and ~Geruch~ ("smell, odor").

The semantic journey from PIE "to belch" to German "to smell" is a wonderfully circuitous one. The original sense involved emitting something — vapor, smoke, belching gas. Proto-Germanic *~reukaną~ shifted toward the emission of smoke or fumes, and the meaning then pivoted from the emitter to the perceiver: from "to give off fumes" to "to detect fumes" to "to smell" in general. English ~reek~ preserves an intermediate stage: it means "to stink" — still focused on the emission, but specifically an unpleasant one. German ~riechen~ generalized to cover all smelling, pleasant or foul. The noun ~Geruch~ ("smell, odor") and the related ~Rauch~ ("smoke") both spring from the same root, preserving the ancient link between smoke and sensation. Greek ~erygganō~ ("I belch") is a cousin from the same PIE root, confirming the original oral-emission sense.
en.rollen:
From Middle High German ~rollen~, borrowed from Old French ~roeler~, from Medieval Latin ~rotulāre~ ("to roll"), from Latin ~rotula~ ("small wheel"), diminutive of ~rota~ ("wheel"). The Latin ~rota~ derives from Proto-Indo-European *~ret-~ ("to run, to roll"). Cognate with Dutch ~rollen~, English ~roll~ (also borrowed from Old French). The related noun ~Rolle~ entered German in the fifteenth century, likewise via Old French ~rolle~ and probably through Middle Dutch ~rolle~.

The semantic extension of ~Rolle~ from "scroll, cylinder" to "theatrical role" was a later semantic loan from French ~role~, originally referring to the rolled-up script an actor read from. English ~role~ followed the same French path.
en.rufen:
From Middle High German ~ruofen~, from Old High German ~ruofan~/~hruofan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hrōpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrōpaną~. A class VII strong verb. The origin of *~hrōpaną~ is uncertain. One theory connects it to *~hrōþiz~ ("glory, fame," the first element of names like ~Hrodulf~/~Rudolf~), though the phonetic relationship is problematic. Another theory proposes a connection to the raven's call — comparing Ancient Greek ~kórax~ ("raven"), Latin ~corvus~ ("raven"), and Lithuanian ~šárka~ ("magpie") — treating the root as an imitation of a carrying cry that was generalized to all calling out. Cognate with Dutch ~roepen~.

The historical forms show an initial ~hr-~ cluster — Old High German ~hruofan~ — that was reduced to ~r-~ as Germanic languages systematically shed their initial ~h~ before consonants. The same process affected ~hring~ → ~Ring~ and ~hrōþ~ → ~Ruhm~. A parallel weak form of ~rufen~ also existed in Middle High German and persisted into the 19th century; its legacy can be seen in the modern present tense, which lacks the umlaut that would be expected from a purely strong paradigm (~du rufst~, ~er ruft~ rather than the historically expected *~du rüfst~, *~er rüft~).
en.ruhen:
From MHG ~ruo(we)n~, from OHG ~ruowēn~ ("to rest"), derived from the noun ~ruowa~ ("rest, peace, quiet"), from Proto-West Germanic *~rōu~ ("calm, rest"), from Proto-Germanic *~rōwō~, from PIE *~h₁reh₁-~ ("to rest"). Cognate with ~Ruhe~ ("rest, peace, quiet"), Dutch ~rusten~ (from a different formation on the same base), Gothic ~rimis~ ("rest").

~Ruhen~ means "to rest, to lie still, to be at peace" — both physically and metaphorically. One can say ~die Arbeit ruht~ ("work has ceased"), ~hier ruht...~ ("here lies..." on a gravestone), or ~auf sich ruhen~ ("to be at ease with oneself"). The PIE root *~h₁reh₁-~ had a remarkably narrow semantic range: it meant simply "to be still, to rest," and this sense has remained stable across millennia and language branches. The phrase ~Ruhe in Frieden~ ("rest in peace") is the German equivalent of Latin ~requiēscat in pāce~, though the German and Latin terms derive from entirely different PIE roots — a case of cultural convergence in funerary language.
en.runden:
From ~rund~ + ~-en~. ~Rund~ descends from MHG ~runt~, borrowed from Old French ~ront~, from Latin ~rotundus~ ("round"), from ~rota~ ("wheel, disk"), from PIE *~Hróth₂o~ (from *~ret-~ "to run, to roll").

~Runden~ means "to round (off), to make round" and is especially common in mathematical and commercial contexts: ~auf ganze Zahlen runden~ ("to round to whole numbers"), ~abrunden~ and ~aufrunden~ ("to round down" and "to round up"). The etymological journey is striking: a PIE root meaning "to roll" produced Latin ~rota~ ("wheel"), which produced ~rotundus~ ("round"), which was borrowed into Old French as ~ront~, which was borrowed into Middle High German as ~runt~ -- making this one of the few everyday German adjectives that arrived via Latin and French rather than directly from Proto-Germanic. English ~round~ took the same French detour.
en.rutschen: From Middle High German ~rutzen~, ~rützen~, an onomatopoeic formation imitating the sound of sliding, with a later shift from ~-tz-~ to ~-tsch-~ (as also seen in ~fletschen~). Perhaps related to Old High German ~rūtezzen~. English ~rutch~ and ~rutsch~ are borrowed from German or its Low German cognates. The noun ~Rutsche~ ("slide, chute") derives from the verb, and the festive New Year's greeting ~Guten Rutsch!~ ("Happy New Year!," literally "good slide") is a beloved German idiom, though some etymologists have proposed a folk-etymological connection to Yiddish ~Rosh~ (as in ~Rosh Hashanah~, "head of the year").
en.räumen:
From Early New High German ~reumen~, a Central German umlaut variant of the regular form ~raumen~, from MHG ~rūmen~, from OHG ~rūmen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rūmijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rūmijaną~, a factitive verb derived from the adjective *~rūmaz~ ("roomy, spacious"), from PIE *~(H)rewH-~ ("to tear open"). Cognate with Bavarian ~rama~. The form ~raumen~ (without umlaut) was standard in Upper German regions until the 17th century and survives in dialects.

The verb means "to clear, to vacate, to tidy up" — etymologically, "to make room." The adjective ~geräumig~ ("spacious") preserves the original adjectival sense. The noun ~Raum~ ("room, space") is a sibling from the same Proto-Germanic root. English ~room~ is a cognate (from Old English ~rūm~, from the same *~rūmaz~), as is the archaic ~roomy~. The PIE root *~(H)rewH-~ ("to tear open") — also the source of Latin ~rūs~ ("country," i.e., open land) — reveals the original image: spaciousness as something torn or cleared open. The related ~aufräumen~ ("to tidy up, to clear away") and ~einräumen~ ("to put away, to concede") extend the root with different prefixes.
en.rücken: From Middle High German ~rücken~, from Old High German ~rucchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rukkijan~. Cognate with Old Norse ~rykkja~, Old English ~roccian~, and modern English ~rock~ (as in "to rock back and forth"). The verb means "to move, to shift, to push" — often with the sense of a short, deliberate motion: ~einen Stuhl rücken~ ("to move a chair"), ~näher rücken~ ("to move closer"), ~in den Mittelpunkt rücken~ ("to move into the spotlight"). The noun ~Rücken~ ("back"), from MHG ~rücke~, from OHG ~rucki~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hrugi~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrugjaz~, is a separate word etymologically — cognate with English ~ridge~ — though folk etymology naturally connects the two (one moves by pushing with one's back). The verb ~rücken~ is also the base of ~verrückt~ ("crazy," literally "shifted, displaced") — perhaps the most entertaining derivative, implying that a ~verrückter~ person has been mentally "moved out of place."
en.rühren: From Middle High German ~rüeren~, from Old High German ~ruoren~, ~hruoren~, from Proto-Germanic *~hrōzijaną~. The initial ~hr-~ cluster, common in older Germanic, was gradually simplified in German (compare English loss of initial ~wr-~ in ~write~). Cognate with Dutch ~roeren~ and the now-rare English ~rear~ ("to move, stir"), which survives mainly in the phrase "to rear up." The semantic range has stayed remarkably stable across a millennium: ~rühren~ still means "to stir" both literally (a pot) and figuratively (emotions). The compound ~anrühren~ preserves an older nuance of "to touch," while ~berühren~ ("to touch, to affect") shows the emotional dimension at full strength.
en.sagen: From Middle High German ~sagen~, from Old High German ~sagēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~saggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sagjaną~, ultimately from PIE *~sekʷ-~ ("to say, tell"). Cognate with Low German ~seggen~, Dutch ~zeggen~, English ~say~, Danish ~sige~, Swedish ~säga~.
en.sammeln:
From Middle High German ~samelen~, ~samlen~, regarded as formed by dissimilation from ~samenen~, which ultimately derives from Proto-Germanic *~samnōną~ ("to bring together, gather, collect, assemble"). Alternatively, inherited from Old High German *~samalōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~samalōn~. The Proto-Germanic root *~samnōną~ is derived from *~samanai~ ("together") combined with the verbal suffix *~-ōną~, and *~samanai~ goes back to Proto-Indo-European *~sem-~ ("one, same, together").

The PIE root *~sem-~ is one of the most productive in Indo-European: it underlies Latin ~semel~ ("once"), ~similis~ ("similar"), ~simul~ ("at the same time"), and English ~same~, ~seem~, ~simple~, and ~some~. The idea of oneness and togetherness are the same idea — to make something one is to bring its parts together. German ~zusammen~ ("together") preserves *~sem-~ in its ~sam-~ portion. The same root appears in Greek ~homos~ ("same"), whence ~homogeneous~, ~homologous~, and the prefix ~homo-~. Cognate with Dutch ~verzamelen~, Swedish ~samla~, Danish ~samle~, Gothic ~samjan~.
en.schaden:
From Middle High German ~schaden~, from Old High German ~scadōn~, denominal from the noun ~Schaden~ ("damage, harm"). The noun derives from OHG ~scado~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaþō~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaþô~ ("injury, harm"), whence also the verb *~skaþōną~ ("to injure, harm"). Cognate with English ~scathe~ (via Old Norse), Dutch ~schade~, Swedish ~skada~, Danish ~skade~.

The English cognate ~scathe~ survives mainly in the negative adjective ~unscathed~ ("without harm") and the derived ~scathing~ ("harshly critical"). German ~schaden~ takes the dative case -- ~das schadet ihm~ ("that harms him") -- a pattern typical of verbs expressing disadvantage or harm. The idiomatic expression ~Es kann nicht schaden~ ("It can't hurt") is the German equivalent of the English shrug-and-try-it phrase.
en.schaffen:
Two historically distinct verbs have merged under a single infinitive in Modern German, distinguished only by their conjugation:

The strong verb (~schuf~, ~geschaffen~, "to create") descends from Middle High German ~schaffen~, from Old High German ~scaffan~, a byform of ~skephen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skappjan~. The form with ~-f-~ was chiefly back-formed from the past participle ~giscaffan~ but was also influenced by the weak verb below. The older form ~skephen~ survives in the archaic variant ~schöpfen~ ("to create"). Cognate with Dutch ~scheppen~, English ~shape~.

The weak verb (~schaffte~, ~geschafft~, "to manage, accomplish") descends from Middle High German ~schaffen~, from Old High German ~skaffōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skapōn~, from the same root as the strong verb. The semantic distinction between the two conjugations — strong for divine or artistic creation, weak for practical accomplishment — only established itself clearly in Modern German.
en.schalten:
From Middle High German ~schalten~, from Old High German ~scaltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaldan~ ("to push, shove"). Further etymology is debated: according to Pokorny, from PIE *~(s)kelH-~ ("to split, separate"), which would make it related to English ~shell~. Cognate with Old Saxon ~skaldan~ ("to push").

The semantic development from "to push, shove" to modern "to switch, shift (gears), to manage" is striking. The original physical sense of shoving a lever or bolt evolved into the technical meaning of operating a switch or gear mechanism. In modern German, ~schalten~ is the standard verb for switching electrical devices, shifting gears in a car (~Schaltgetriebe~ = "manual transmission"), and by extension, for managing or directing (~schalten und walten~ = "to do as one pleases," literally "to switch and govern"). The compound ~Schalter~ means both "switch" and "counter/window" (at a bank or post office) — two kinds of interfaces where something gets directed. The idiom ~schalten und walten~ was already proverbial in Middle High German, pairing two near-synonyms for emphasis.
en.schauen: From Middle High German ~schouwen~, from Old High German ~scouwōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~skawwōną~ ("to look, see, watch"), from PIE *~(s)kewh₁-~ ("to heed, look, feel, take note of"). The same PIE root, via Old English ~scēawian~, gives modern English ~show~ — a striking semantic reversal: the Germanic descendants of this root divide between the intransitive "to look" (German ~schauen~) and the transitive "to make visible" (English ~show~). The verb is characteristic of southern German, Austrian, and Swiss usage, where ~Schau!~ and ~schau mal!~ serve as colloquial equivalents of standard German ~guck mal!~ Cognate with Old English ~scēawian~, English ~show~, Dutch ~schouwen~ ("to inspect").
en.scheiden: From Middle High German ~scheiden~, from Old High German ~sceidan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaiþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaiþaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~skeyt-~ ("to cut, separate"). Cognate with Dutch ~scheiden~, West Frisian ~skiede~, and English ~shed~ (as in "to shed" skin or tears, originally "to separate"). The PIE root produced a rich network of cognates across the Indo-European family: Irish ~scian~ ("knife"), Lithuanian ~skiesti~ ("to separate"), Old Church Slavonic ~cěditi~ ("to filter, strain"), Greek ~skhizein~ ("to split," source of English ~schism~ and ~schizophrenia~), and Sanskrit ~chyati~ ("he cuts off"). The German noun ~Entscheidung~ ("decision") literally means "a cutting apart" of alternatives.
en.scheinen: From Middle High German ~schīnen~, from Old High German ~skīnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skīnan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skīnaną~, from PIE *~(s)ḱeh₁y-~ ("to shine, shimmer"). A class I strong verb. The modern verb covers two related senses — "to shine" (physical light) and "to seem, appear" — a duality that reflects the ancient metaphor of appearances as light cast onto the observer. This ambiguity between shining and seeming is shared by Latin ~lucēre~ ("to shine") and its derivative ~illūsiō~. The German cognate ~Schein~ ("shine; appearance; certificate") preserves the full range, and the pejorative ~zum Schein~ ("for appearances only") shows the "seeming" sense at its most skeptical. Cognate with Dutch ~schijnen~, Low German ~schienen~, English ~shine~, Danish ~skinne~, and the Esperanto borrowing ~ŝajni~ ("to seem").
en.scheitern: A verbal formation from ~Scheiter~, the plural of ~Scheit~ ("log, billet of firewood"), from Middle High German ~schīt~, from Old High German ~scīt~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skīd~ ("piece of wood"), from Proto-Germanic *~skīdą~, from PIE *~skeyd-~ ("to split, to separate"), an extension of *~skey-~ ("to cut"). In the 16th century, the compounds ~zuscheitern~ and ~zerscheitern~ appeared first, meaning "to break into pieces (logs)" — like a ship shattering against rocks into firewood-sized fragments. The shortened form ~scheitern~ likely arose by analogy with phrases like ~zu Scheitern gehen~ ("to go to pieces, to fall apart"). The semantic shift from "to splinter like a log" to "to fail" is vivid and dramatic: a project that ~scheitert~ doesn't merely not succeed — it breaks apart catastrophically, like a vessel dashed on the shoals. The root noun ~Scheit~ is cognate with Old Norse ~skíð~ ("plank, billet, ski") — yes, ~Ski~ is a doublet of ~Scheit~, both meaning "a piece of split wood." One piece you burn for warmth; the other you strap to your feet and slide downhill. Also cognate with English ~shide~ (archaic "a piece of split wood"), Greek ~σχίζα~ ("splinter"), and Lithuanian ~skìedra~ ("chip"). The PIE root *~skey-~ also yields ~scheiden~ ("to separate, to divorce") and English ~shed~ — all preserving the ancient concept of cutting apart.
en.schenken:
From Middle High German ~schenken~, from Old High German ~scenken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skankijan~ ("to pour, serve"). Related to ~Schenke~ ("tavern, inn") and ~Schenkel~ ("thigh, shank"). Cognate with Dutch ~schenken~ ("to pour; to give"), English ~skink~ (archaic, "to pour, to serve drink").

The semantic journey from "to pour" to "to give as a gift" is one of German's most vivid etymological stories. In medieval Germanic culture, the act of pouring a drink for a guest was itself the paradigmatic act of generosity — the host who poured was giving. Over time, the meaning broadened from pouring drink to giving any gift, while the original "pouring" sense retreated to fixed expressions like ~Wein einschenken~ ("to pour wine") and the noun ~Schenke~ ("tavern," originally a place where drinks were poured). Dutch ~schenken~ preserves both senses equally — "to pour" and "to give" — making the Dutch usage a living fossil of the semantic transition. English ~shank~ (the leg bone) is a cognate of ~Schenkel~ from the same root, the original connection being the tilting motion of pouring.
en.schicken: From Middle High German ~schicken~ ("to outfit oneself, fit in, arrange appropriately"), from Old High German *~skihhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skikkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skikkijaną~ ("to order, send"), the causative of the verb underlying Middle High German ~geschehen~ ("to happen, rush"). The modern sense "to send" developed from the earlier "to dispatch, make ready to go" — to arrange something so that it departs. Related to English ~chic~ (via French, ultimately from the same Germanic source), which preserves the original sense of fitting arrangement. The family of words also includes ~geschickt~ ("skilled, adept") — skill being a matter of fitting arrangement — and ~Schicksal~ ("fate, destiny"), where fate is the dispatch one cannot refuse.
en.schieben: From Middle High German ~schieben~, from Old High German ~skioban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skeuban~, from Proto-Germanic *~skeubaną~, from PIE *~skewbʰ-~ ("to push, to shove, to thrust"). The word is attested since the 9th century. As a class II strong verb, ~schieben~ displays the ablaut series ~ie~ > ~o~ > ~o~ (~schieben~, ~schob~, ~geschoben~). ~Schieben~ means "to push, to shove" — physically moving something by applying force from behind. But it also means "to slide" (as in sliding a drawer), "to traffic" or "to profiteer" in colloquial usage (~Schwarzmarktgeschäfte schieben~, "to run black-market deals"), and in the expression ~die Schuld auf jemanden schieben~ ("to shift the blame onto someone"), where the pushing is metaphorical. The phrase ~auf die lange Bank schieben~ ("to put off, to procrastinate" — literally "to push onto the long bench") may originate from the medieval practice of storing unresolved court documents on a long bench, where they gathered dust. Cognate with English ~shove~ (which underwent the Great Vowel Shift while German kept the diphthong), Dutch ~schuiven~, Low German ~schuven~, Danish ~skubbe~. The PIE root *~skewbʰ-~ may also be related to Lithuanian ~skubėti~ ("to hurry" — to push oneself along). The derived noun ~Schub~ ("push, thrust, batch") and ~Schublade~ ("drawer" — literally "push-load," something you push and pull) round out the family.
en.schießen: From Middle High German ~schiezen~, from Old High German ~sciozan~, ~sciozzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skeotan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skeutaną~ ("to shoot, to shove"), possibly from PIE *~(s)kewd-~ ("to throw, shoot, pursue"). A class II strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~schießen~, ~schoss~, ~geschossen~. The Proto-Germanic root yielded an impressive family of cognates: English ~shoot~, Dutch ~schieten~, Swedish ~skjuta~, Danish ~skyde~, Icelandic ~skjóta~, and Old Norse ~skjóta~. The related noun ~Schuss~ ("shot") entered English directly as a skiing term — a straight, fast downhill run, preserving the original sense of rapid forward motion. The zero-grade form Proto-Germanic *~skutiz~ ("a shooting, a shot") is the ancestor of ~Schuss~. The original meaning of ~schießen~ was broader than firearms: it meant any rapid, propulsive motion — shooting an arrow, of course, but also shooting a bolt (on a door), or a plant shooting up from the ground. This last sense survives in the compound ~aufschießen~ and in the idiom ~ins Kraut schießen~ ("to run to seed," literally "to shoot into the herb"), used figuratively when something proliferates out of control.
en.schildern: Borrowed in the 16th century from Middle Low German or Dutch ~schilderen~ ("to paint, to coat"). The Dutch verb derives from the noun ~schilder~ ("painter"), itself from ~schild~ ("shield"). In medieval times, shields were decorated with coats of arms, and the craftsman who painted these heraldic designs onto shields was called a ~schilder~. This artisan gradually expanded beyond heraldic work to paint other subjects, and the verb broadened accordingly. Dutch ~schild~ descends from Middle Dutch ~schilt~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skeldu~, from Proto-Germanic *~skelduz~ ("shield"), from PIE *~(s)keyt-~ ("shield, cover"). Cognate with English ~shield~ and Danish ~skjold~. The semantic shift from "to paint a shield" to "to paint" to modern German "to describe, to depict (in words)" is a beautiful case of metaphorical expansion: first painting concrete images on shields, then painting pictures generally, and finally "painting pictures with words." English ~depict~ follows a parallel trajectory from the physical to the verbal.
en.schlafen: ~schlafen~ comes from Middle High German ~slāfen~, from Old High German ~slāfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~slāpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slēpaną~. Cognate with English ~sleep~, Dutch ~slapen~, Low German ~slapen~, and Gothic ~𐍃𐌻𐌴𐍀𐌰𐌽~ (slēpan) — all of identical meaning across the Germanic family. The Proto-Germanic origin is uncertain: it may be related to *~slapaz~ ("weak, lax, limp"), the ancestor of German ~schlaff~ ("slack, limp, flaccid"), connecting sleep to the physical limpness of the sleeping body. This possible German-internal cognate pair — ~schlafen~ / ~schlaff~ — captures something real: sleep is the state of going slack. An older theory derives the root from PIE *~sleh₁-~, possibly related to ~languor~ and Old English ~slæc~ ("lazy"), but the connection remains debated. The Class VII strong verb ~schlafen~, ~schlief~, ~geschlafen~ belongs to the reduplicating class, whose Präteritum (~schlief~) reflects an ancient pattern of vowel modification rather than a simple ablaut grade.
en.schlagen: From Middle High German ~slahen~, from Old High German ~slahan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~slahan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slahaną~, from a Proto-Indo-European *~slak-~ ("to hit, strike, throw"), apparently of onomatopoeic origin. The modern ~-g-~ in the infinitive and present tense is a secondary insertion by analogy with the past-tense forms (~schlug~) and the related noun ~Schlag~ ("blow, strike, beat") — a phenomenon known as Grammatischer Wechsel (Verner's Law alternation later regularized by analogy). The same root produced an extraordinary range of modern German compounds: ~vorschlagen~ ("to suggest," literally "to strike forward"), ~nachschlagen~ ("to look up in a reference"), ~beschlagen~ ("to shoe a horse"), ~erschlagen~ ("to strike dead"), ~einschlagen~ ("to hammer in; to take a path"). English ~slay~ and ~slog~ both derive from the same Proto-Germanic root (Old English ~slēan~, "to strike, beat, smite"). Cognate with Low German and Dutch ~slaan~, Old English ~slēan~, Danish ~slå~, Swedish ~slå~, Norwegian ~slå~.
en.schließen: From Middle High German ~slieȥen~, from Old High German ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slūtaną~, *~sleutaną~ ("to lock, shut"). The PIE root is not definitively established, but the Germanic root carried the sense of fastening or locking — the sliding of a bolt into place. A class II strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~schließen~, ~schloss~, ~geschlossen~. The modern spelling with ~sch-~ reflects the High German consonant shift from earlier *~sl-~, while Dutch ~sluiten~ preserves the unshifted cluster. One peculiarity of English: the Wiktionary entry lists ~slot~ as a cognate of ~schließen~. The relevant ~slot~ here is not the common English word (which comes from Old French ~esclot~ via Old Norse) but rather the archaic and dialectal English ~slot~ meaning "a bar, bolt, or fastening," directly from the same Proto-Germanic root. Cognate with Old Saxon ~slūtan~, Dutch ~sluiten~, archaic English ~slot~ (a bolt).
en.schmecken:
From Middle High German ~smecken~, from Old High German ~smecchen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~smakkijan~ ("to taste"), from Proto-West Germanic *~smakku~ ("a taste"). Cognate with English ~smack~ (as in "it smacks of"), Dutch ~smaken~, Lithuanian ~smagù~ ("cheerful, enjoyable, pleasant").

The English expression "it smacks of" — meaning "it has the flavor or character of" — is a direct semantic and etymological cognate of German ~schmecken~. Both descend from the same Proto-West Germanic root *~smakku~, and both retain the original sense of tasting or perceiving flavor. In regional German (especially southern dialects and Austrian German), ~schmecken~ means not just "to taste" but also "to smell" — a reminder that the Proto-Germanic root encompassed sensory perception more broadly than modern Standard German allows. The noun ~Geschmack~ ("taste, flavor") is a collective formation with the ~Ge-~ prefix, literally "the totality of what is tasted." The relationship between ~schmecken~ and English ~smack~ is one of the more surprising Germanic cognate pairs hiding in plain sight.
en.schneiden: ~schneiden~ comes from Middle High German ~snīden~, from Old High German ~snīdan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~snīþan~ ("to cut"). Cognate with Dutch ~snijden~ ("to cut, carve, intersect"), Low German ~snieden~, Swedish ~snida~ ("to carve, engrave"), Icelandic ~sníða~ ("to trim, tailor"), and the archaic English ~snithe~ ("to cut") — a word related to the modern English adjective ~snide~, whose original sense was "cutting" in the literal sense before shifting to "cutting" in the figurative one. No secure PIE root has been established for the Proto-West Germanic ancestor; the word may be a Germanic innovation. The Class I strong verb pattern (~schneiden~, ~schnitt~, ~geschnitten~) shows the characteristic *ei / *i / *i ablaut, and the dental alternation between German ~-d-~ and English ~-th-~ (Proto-Germanic *þ) reflects a regular West Germanic sound change.
en.schonen: From Middle High German ~schōnen~, derived from the adjective ~schön~ ("beautiful"). The adjective traces to Old High German ~scōni~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaunī~, from Proto-Germanic *~skauniz~, whose original meaning was likely "to look at, to behold" and hence "worth beholding." The semantic development from "beautiful" to "to spare, to take care of" reflects the idea that one treats beautiful things gently. Cognate with English ~sheen~ (originally "beautiful, bright"). The reflexive ~sich schonen~ ("to take it easy, to spare oneself") is common in medical contexts, while ~Schonzeit~ ("closed season") refers to periods when hunting is forbidden to let animal populations recover.
en.schreiben: From Middle High German ~schrīben~, from Old High German ~scrīban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrīban~, from Proto-Germanic *~skrībaną~, an early borrowing from Latin ~scrībō~ ("I write"), from Proto-Italic *~skreiβō~, from PIE *~(s)kreybʰ-~ ("to scratch, cut, incise"). The word entered Germanic through contact with the Roman world along the Rhine frontier; before this borrowing, Germanic peoples used *~wrītaną~ ("to scratch, carve") for writing-related activities — the ancestor of English ~write~. Because this borrowing occurred during the Roman period (roughly 1st–4th century CE) — well before the ~-ieren~ suffix became productive through Old French contact in the 12th–13th century — ~schreiben~ was fully assimilated into the strong-verb system (class I: ~schreiben~, ~schrieb~, ~geschrieben~), following the same ablaut pattern as native verbs like ~treiben~. Later Latin and French borrowings received the weak ~-ieren~ treatment instead (~studieren~, ~konjugieren~). Cognate with Dutch ~schrijven~, Low German ~schrieven~.
en.schreien:
From Middle High German ~schrīen~, from Old High German ~scrīan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrīan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skrīaną~ ("to cry, scream"). A class I strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~schreien~, ~schrie~, ~geschrien~. Cognate with Dutch ~schreien~ ("to cry, weep"), Frisian ~skrieme~ ("to weep"), and related to English ~scream~ (via Middle Dutch ~scremen~) and Old Norse ~skræma~ ("to terrify"), whence Swedish ~skrämma~ ("to frighten") and Danish ~skræmme~ ("to scare").

The semantic spread across Germanic languages is revealing: German ~schreien~ means "to scream, to shout, to cry out" (a loud vocalization), while Dutch ~schreien~ means "to cry, to weep" (a quiet lamentation). English ~scream~ preserves the loud sense. The shift from "loud cry" to "weeping" in Dutch parallels what happened to English ~cry~ itself, which originally meant "to call out" (from Old French ~crier~, from Latin ~quiritāre~ "to wail") before acquiring its modern weeping sense. The expression ~zum Schreien~ ("hilarious," literally "enough to make you scream") captures the verb's emotional intensity, while ~Schreihals~ ("screamer, loudmouth," literally "scream-throat") is one of German's more vivid compound insults.
en.schulen: Derived from the noun ~Schule~ with the verbal suffix ~-en~. From Middle High German ~schuole~, from Old High German ~scuola~, borrowed from Latin ~schola~ ("leisure for learning, school"), itself from Ancient Greek ~scholē~ (~σχολή~, "leisure, rest, holding back"), from PIE *~seǵʰ-~ ("to hold"). The Greek word originally denoted the leisure required for intellectual pursuits, since only those free from labor could devote time to study. Latin ~schola~ entered Germanic languages during the Christianization period, when monastic schools became centers of education. The semantic irony is delicious: the root means "leisure," yet school has become synonymous with hard work for generations of students. English ~school~ is a cognate borrowed from the same Latin source, and even ~scholar~ traces back to Greek ~scholē~. The German verb ~schulen~ means "to train, to school" and carries a more specialized, professional sense than simply "to teach" (~lehren~).
en.schweigen:
From MHG ~swīgen~, from OHG ~swīgēn~ ("to be silent"), from Proto-West Germanic *~swīgēn~. The verb was originally weak but developed strong forms in early Middle High German (~sweic~, ~swigen~, ~geswigen~). Cognate with Old Saxon ~swīgōn~ (weak), Old English ~swīgian~ ("to be silent").

The Old Norse cognates ~svígja~ ("to bow, yield") and ~svigna~ ("to bend, give way") suggest an original semantic field around yielding -- from which "falling silent" may have developed as a metaphor for yielding in conversation. The transition from weak to strong conjugation in Middle High German is unusual; most German verbs have moved in the opposite direction over the centuries. Modern ~schweigen~ conjugates as a strong verb (~schwieg~, ~geschwiegen~) with no trace of its originally weak past. The noun ~Schweigen~ carries almost poetic weight: ~das Schweigen brechen~ ("to break the silence"), ~Schweigen ist Gold~ ("silence is golden," the second half of the proverb ~Reden ist Silber, Schweigen ist Gold~). In legal contexts, ~Schweigepflicht~ ("duty of confidentiality") binds doctors, lawyers, and priests.
en.schwimmen:
From Middle High German ~swimmen~, from Old High German ~swimman~ ("to swim"), from Proto-West Germanic *~swimman~, from Proto-Germanic *~swimmaną~ ("to swim, float"), from PIE *~swem-~ ("to be in motion, to swim"). A class III strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~schwimmen~, ~schwamm~, ~geschwommen~. Cognate with English ~swim~, Dutch ~zwemmen~, Low German ~swimmen~, Danish ~svømme~, and Swedish ~simma~.

The PIE root *~swem-~ originally meant general motion in or through a fluid medium. The modern English cognate ~swim~ descends unchanged from Old English ~swimman~, making this one of the most transparent Germanic cognate pairs. The German-Dutch consonant correspondence ~schw-~/~zw-~ is perfectly regular: it appears in ~Schwester~/~zuster~ ("sister"), ~schwarz~/~zwart~ ("black"), ~Schwert~/~zwaard~ ("sword"), and ~Schwein~/~zwijn~ ("pig"). Dutch underwent a further sound shift from ~sw-~ to ~zw-~ that German did not. The expression ~ins Schwimmen kommen~ ("to start swimming," figuratively "to become uncertain, to flounder") captures the helplessness of being cast into deep water. The noun ~Schwamm~ ("sponge") — etymologically the thing that absorbs in water — derives from the same ablaut series.
en.schätzen: ~Schätzen~ ("to estimate, value; to appreciate") is a denominal verb from ~Schatz~ ("treasure, darling"). ~Schatz~ descends from Middle High German ~schaz~, Old High German ~scaz~, and Proto-West Germanic *~skatt~ ("cattle, treasure") — an early Germanic word in which cattle served as a measure of wealth. Cognates include Old Norse ~skattr~ (whence Danish ~skat~ and Swedish ~skatt~, both meaning "tax"), Gothic ~skatts~, and Old English ~sċeatt~. The Scandinavian descendants shifted decisively from "treasure" to "tax" — the compulsory yielding of wealth to authority — while German retained the positive sense of treasure and added the intellectual sense of estimating or valuing. A German speaker who ~schätzt~ something either places a rough value on it or genuinely appreciates it. The card game Skat, despite its resemblance to ~Schatz~, is actually borrowed from Italian ~scarto~ ("discard") — a false cognate that trips up many players.
en.schütteln: From Middle High German ~schütelen~, ~schütteln~, from Old High German ~scutilōn~, an iterative (frequentative) formation from ~schütten~ ("to pour, to shake"), indicating repeated or vigorous shaking. The base verb ~schütten~ derives from Old High German ~scuttēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~skudjaną~, perhaps from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)kewt-~ ("to shake, jolt"). Cognate with Dutch ~schudden~ ("to shake"). The iterative ~-eln~ suffix marks ~schütteln~ as describing repeated back-and-forth motion, distinguishing it from the single decisive motion of ~schütten~. The compound ~Kopfschütteln~ ("head-shaking") is frequently used as a noun meaning bewilderment or disapproval.
en.schützen: From late Middle High German ~schützen~ ("to protect"), attested only from around 1300, and debated in origin. Wiktionary notes two competing derivations: either from the root of ~schießen~ ("to shoot"), with an original sense of "to advance a barrier (to shoot something forward as a bulwark)," from Proto-Germanic *~skeutaną~, from PIE *~(s)kewd-~ ("to advance, propel"); or from ~schütten~ ("to pour, heap up"), with an original sense of "to pile up a protective wall," from Proto-Germanic *~skudjaną~, from PIE *~(s)kewt-~ ("to shake, jolt"). Both derivations converge on the notion of physical construction or projection of a barrier. The late attestation of the simplex makes the etymology difficult to resolve. A striking false friend: English ~shoot~ and German ~schützen~ share a possible common ancestor but have traveled in very different semantic directions — one projects a missile, the other stops one. Cognate with Dutch ~schutten~ ("to dam, pen in, lock [a canal]"), which preserves the barrier sense more literally.
en.sehen: From Middle High German ~sëhen~, from Old High German ~sëhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwaną~, from PIE *~sekʷ-~ ("to see, notice"). Cognate with Low German ~sehn~, Dutch ~zien~, English ~see~, Danish ~se~, Gothic ~saihwan~.
en.sein:
From Middle High German ~sīn~, from Old High German ~sīn~ ("to be"). Traces to Proto-Germanic *~wesaną~ ("to be"), suppleted with *~beuną~ ("to be, exist, become"). Cognate with Dutch ~zijn~, Low German ~sien~.

~Sein~ is the most radically suppletive verb in German. ~Suppletion~ (from Latin ~supplēre~, "to fill up, to supplement") occurs when an inflectional paradigm draws its conjugations from etymologically unrelated roots — not merely irregular forms of a single root (as ablaut produces ~singen~, ~sang~, ~gesungen~ from one PIE ancestor), but genuinely different words that merged into one paradigm because the original paradigm had gaps that needed filling. Three distinct PIE roots contribute conjugations to ~sein~:

- *~h₁es-~ ("to be, to exist"): Supplies the third-person present forms ~ist~ (from PGmc *~isti~) and ~sind~ (from PGmc *~sinti~), as well as the infinitive ~sein~ itself (via OHG ~sīn~, from PGmc *~sīną~, a contracted form).
- *~bʰuH-~ ("to grow, to become, to come into being"): Supplies the first- and second-person present forms ~bin~ and ~bist~ (from PGmc *~beuną~). The original sense of becoming or coming into existence lingers in the ~b-~ forms: ~ich bin~ is, at its etymological root, "I am becoming."
- *~h₂wes-~ ("to dwell, to stay, to remain"): Supplies the entire past tense ~war~, ~warst~, ~waren~ and the past participle ~gewesen~ (from PGmc *~wesaną~). To say ~ich war~ is, etymologically, to say "I dwelt" — existence conceived as inhabiting a place.

English ~to be~ preserves exactly the same three-way suppletion: ~is~ and ~am~ from *~h₁es-~, ~be~, ~been~, and ~being~ from *~bʰuH-~, and ~was~ and ~were~ from *~h₂wes-~. German and English thus carry the same three-headed fossil in their most fundamental verb.

The same phenomenon extends into the Romance copulae, and the parallels are striking. Latin ~esse~ ("to be") was itself already suppletive: its present tense ~sum~, ~est~, ~sunt~ descended from *~h₁es-~, while its perfect tense ~fuī~, ~fuit~ and future participle ~futūrus~ came from *~bʰuH-~ — the same two roots that supply German ~ist~ and ~bin~. The Romance languages inherited this Latin suppletion and then deepened it by recruiting Latin ~stāre~ ("to stand") as a third contributor. Most Romance languages — Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, Catalan, Galician — maintained ~stāre~ as a separate copula alongside the ~esse~-derived one, splitting "to be" into two verbs: Spanish ~ser~/~estar~, Italian ~essere~/~stare~, Portuguese ~ser~/~estar~, each distinguishing essential being from temporary state. French is the exception: it merged everything into a single verb ~être~, and ~stāre~ survives only as a fossil in the past participle ~été~ — and in the otherwise-extinct legal verb ~ester~ (~ester en justice~, "to appear in legal proceedings"), a frozen relic of ~stāre~ preserved in the amber of juridical French. Within each Romance copula, the older two-way suppletion persists: Italian ~sono~/~è~ (from *~h₁es-~), ~fui~ (from *~bʰuH-~), ~stato~ (from ~stāre~); French ~suis~/~est~/~sont~ (from *~h₁es-~), ~fus~/~fut~ (from *~bʰuH-~), ~été~ (from ~stāre~).

That both Germanic and Italic independently maintained the *~h₁es-~ / *~bʰuH-~ suppletion — and that both language families later recruited a third root (~*h₂wes-~ in Germanic, ~stāre~ in Romance) to patch further gaps — suggests that copulae are uniquely prone to this kind of merger. The likeliest explanation is frequency: the copula is the most commonly used verb in virtually every Indo-European language, and high-frequency words resist regularization. Speakers memorize ~bin~/~ist~/~war~ as individual wholes rather than deriving them from rules, so multiple roots can coexist within one paradigm indefinitely, each reinforced by constant use. The copula is also semantically porous — the boundary between "to be," "to exist," "to dwell," "to become," and "to stand" is thin enough that neighboring verbs are perpetually absorbed into its paradigm, filling whatever slots need filling. The result is that the most basic verb in the language is, paradoxically, the least regular — a patchwork of three ancient words stitched together so long ago that only etymology can see the seams.
en.senden: ~Senden~ ("to send, broadcast") descends from Middle High German ~senden~, Old High German ~senten~, and Proto-West Germanic *~sandijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sandijaną~. The Proto-Indo-European root is *~sont-eye-~, a causative of *~sent-~ ("to walk, travel") — so the original sense was "to cause to go." Cognates include Dutch ~zenden~, Danish ~sende~, Swedish ~sända~, Gothic ~sandjan~, and English ~send~. A notable feature of ~senden~ is its double past tense: ~sendete~ is used for broadcasting (radio, television), while ~sandte~ — a historically strong-influenced form — is used for physically or formally sending a letter or message. This split preserved an archaic form that might otherwise have been regularized. The verb is related to Old English ~sand~ ("a sending, mission, message"), visible in the old word ~errand~.
en.senken: From Old High German ~senken~, from Proto-Germanic *~sankwijaną~, a causative of *~sinkwaną~ ("to sink"), from PIE *~sengʷ-~ ("to sink, to fall"). As a causative, ~senken~ originally meant "to cause to sink, to make something go down" — the active counterpart to the intransitive ~sinken~. This causative relationship (where a weak verb means "to cause the action of the corresponding strong verb") is one of the great structural features of Germanic: ~sinken~/~senken~ parallels ~trinken~/~tränken~ ("to drink"/"to water, to give drink"), ~springen~/~sprengen~ ("to jump"/"to blow up, to scatter"), and ~fallen~/~fällen~ ("to fall"/"to fell"). The vowel alternation (~i~ > ~e~) in these pairs reflects the Proto-Germanic causative *~-jan-~ suffix, which triggered umlaut (here rounding *~a~ to *~e~ after the high front vowel was lost). Modern ~senken~ means "to lower" — prices (~Preise senken~), one's gaze (~den Blick senken~), or a coffin into the ground (~einen Sarg ins Grab senken~). The derived noun ~Senke~ means "a depression in the terrain" — a place that has been made to sink. Cognate with Gothic ~sagqjan~ ("to cause to sink"), Old Norse ~søkkva~.
en.servieren: Borrowed from French ~servir~ with the addition of the German ~-ieren~ verb suffix. French ~servir~ descends from Old French ~servir~, from Latin ~servire~ ("to serve, to be a slave to"), from Latin ~servus~ ("slave, servant"). The ultimate origin of ~servus~ is debated: it may derive from Proto-Italic *~serwos~ ("guardian"), from PIE *~ser-~ ("to watch over, to protect"), which would make the original meaning something like "guardian" rather than "slave," a striking semantic reversal. The German borrowing is specifically tied to the sense of serving food and drink; broader senses of "to serve" are covered by the native ~dienen~. Cognate with English ~serve~ (via Old French from the same Latin source), ~service~, ~servant~, and also with ~serf~ (from the same Latin ~servus~). The word entered German during the period of heavy French cultural influence on German courtly and culinary vocabulary in the 17th and 18th centuries.
en.setzen: From Middle High German ~setzen~, from Old High German ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjaną~ ("to set, to place down"), from PIE *~sodéyeti~. A causative derivation of *~sitjaną~ ("to sit") with the suffix *~-janą~: literally "to cause to sit," hence "to set, place." The causative relationship between ~sitzen~ ("to sit," intransitive) and ~setzen~ ("to set," transitive) is a productive pattern preserved from Proto-Indo-European, paralleling English ~sit~/~set~. Cognate with Dutch ~zetten~, English ~set~, Yiddish ~zetsn~.
en.sichern:
A denominative verb derived from the adjective ~sicher~ ("safe, secure, certain"). Old High German ~sihhurōn~ ("to justify, to vouch for, to guarantee"), attested from the 9th century, developed into Middle High German ~sichern~ ("to make safe, to give assurance").

~sicher~ itself entered Germanic as a loanword: from OHG ~sihur~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sikur~, borrowed from Latin ~sēcūrus~ ("worryless, carefree, secure"). The Latin adjective is a compound of ~sē-~ ("without, apart from") + ~cūra~ ("care, concern") — literally "without care." Latin ~sē-~ in turn derives from PIE *~sue-~ ("self, one's own"), the reflexive particle.

The word's journey from the Roman world into Germanic is a vivid example of cultural transmission along the Rhine frontier: Roman administrators and soldiers brought the legal and military vocabulary of ~sēcūrus~ into contact with Germanic-speaking populations, and the word was adopted wholesale. Its sibling entered English via French: ~secure~ and ~sure~ both descend from the same Latin root, as does ~sinecure~ (from Latin ~sine cūrā~, "without care" — a position requiring no work). The OHG sense of ~sihhurōn~ — "to justify oneself, to give surety" — is notably more active and legally weighted than the modern sense of "to secure, to safeguard": it meant to perform the act of pledging or vouching, not merely to make safe. The modern passive-causative sense ("to secure a building, to back up a file") is the later development.
en.sicherstellen:
A compound of ~sicher~ + ~stellen~, meaning "to ensure, to secure, to confiscate":

- ~sicher~: From MHG ~siher~, from OHG ~sihur~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sikur~ ("secure, sure"), borrowed from Latin ~sēcūrus~ ("worryless; carefree; secure"), from ~sē-~ ("without") + ~cūra~ ("care"). Cognate with English ~secure~ and ~sure~ (both from the same Latin source via different routes).
- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stallijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stallijaną~, from PIE *~stelh₂-~ ("to put, to stand").

~Sicherstellen~ is not a standard prefix verb but an adjective-verb compound: to place something in a state of security. Its meanings range from "to ensure" (~die Versorgung sicherstellen~, "to ensure the supply") to "to confiscate, to seize as evidence" (~die Polizei stellte die Waffe sicher~, "the police confiscated the weapon"). The confiscation sense is particularly interesting: to ~sicherstellen~ evidence is to "place it in safety" — to secure it from tampering, a bureaucratic euphemism that frames seizure as preservation. The adjective ~sicher~ is itself a fascinating Latin borrowing: it entered Proto-West Germanic from Latin ~sēcūrus~ during the Roman period, making it one of the earliest Latin loanwords in German. English received the same Latin word twice — once as ~sure~ (via Old French ~seur~) and once as ~secure~ (directly from Latin) — while German inherited it through direct contact along the ~limes~.
en.siegen: Derived from the noun ~Sieg~ ("victory") with the verbal suffix ~-en~. ~Sieg~ traces to Middle High German ~sige~, ~sic~, from Old High German ~sigu~, from Proto-Germanic *~segaz~, *~seguz~ ("victory"). The word is deeply embedded in Germanic personal names: ~Siegfried~ ("victory-peace"), ~Siegmund~ ("victory-protection"), and ~Sieglinde~ ("victory-shield"). The Proto-Germanic root may be connected to Proto-Indo-European *~segʰ-~ ("to hold, overpower"), also the source of Greek ~ekhein~ ("to hold") and the ~-seige~ in English ~besiege~. The exclamation ~Sieg Heil~ was appropriated by the Nazi regime, permanently darkening the word's connotations in modern German, though compound names like ~Siegfried~ remain in common use.
en.singen: ~singen~ comes from Middle High German ~singen~, from Old High German ~singan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~singwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~singwaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~sengʷʰ-~ ("to recite, intone, chant"). The PIE root is connected to Ancient Greek ~ὀμφή~ (omphḗ, "divine voice, oracle"), suggesting the deep cultural link between singing and prophecy in early Indo-European thought — the poet-singer as the mouth of the gods. ~Singen~ is one of the most stable words in the Germanic family: Old English ~singan~, Gothic ~𐍃𐌹𐌲𐌲𐍅𐌰𐌽~ (siggwan), Old Norse ~syngja~, Dutch ~zingen~, and Swedish ~sjunga~ all carry the same meaning with minimal semantic drift across three thousand years. The Class III strong verb ablaut (~singen~, ~sang~, ~gesungen~) is among the most-cited examples in every German grammar, making it something of a paradigm case. The English cognate ~sing~ underwent the same vowel alternation (sing, sang, sung), reflecting the shared Proto-Germanic inheritance.
en.sinken:
From Middle High German ~sinken~, from Old High German ~sinkan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sinkwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sinkwaną~, from PIE *~sengʷ-~ ("to sink, to drop"). A class III strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~sinken~, ~sank~, ~gesunken~. Cognate with Low German ~sinken~, Dutch ~zinken~, English ~sink~, Danish ~synke~, Swedish ~sjunka~. The Armenian ~ankanim~ ("to fall") is a distant cognate from the same PIE root.

The related causative verb ~senken~ ("to lower, to sink something") represents the same ablaut alternation seen across Germanic: the intransitive strong verb ~sinken~ ("it sinks") versus the transitive weak causative ~senken~ ("to make it sink"). This pattern — strong intransitive vs. weak causative — is one of the most productive in Germanic morphology: compare ~trinken~/~tränken~, ~springen~/~sprengen~, ~finden~/~finden~ (lost causative). The Proto-Germanic labiovelar *~kw~ in *~sinkwaną~ simplified to ~k~ in West Germanic, but its trace appears in the Old Norse descendant ~søkkva~ (Icelandic ~sökkva~, Faroese ~søkka~), where the labiovelar survives as ~kv~.
en.sitzen: From Middle High German ~sitzen~, from Old High German ~sizzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sittjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sitjaną~, from PIE *~sédyeti~, from the root *~sed-~ ("to sit"). A class V strong verb. The same PIE root *~sed-~ underlies ~setzen~ — the causative pair is exactly parallel to English ~sit~/~set~: ~sitzen~ (intransitive, "to be sitting") : ~setzen~ (transitive, "to cause to sit, place"). ~besitzen~ ("to possess," literally "to sit upon") derives from this verb through the ~be-~ prefix. Ancient Greek ~hézomai~ ("I sit") is a formal cognate. Cognate with Dutch ~zitten~, Low German ~sitten~, English ~sit~, Danish ~sidde~.
en.sollen: From Middle High German ~scholn~, ~schuln~, from Old High German ~sculan~, ~skulan~, ~scolan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skulan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skulaną~, from PIE *~skel-~ ("to be obligated, owe, be guilty"). The change of ~sk~ to ~s~ observed in many Germanic languages is probably attributable to the word's common appearance in unstressed position. Cognate with Dutch ~zullen~, English ~shall~ and ~should~, Danish ~skulle~, Swedish ~skall~ and ~skulle~.
en.sorgen: From Middle High German ~sorgen~, from Old High German ~sorgēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~surgāną~, a denominative verb derived from *~surgō~ ("worry, sorrow, care"), ultimately from PIE *~swergʰ-~ ("to watch over, worry; be ill, suffer"). The semantic duality of the PIE root — watchful care and anxious suffering — is still visible in Modern German: ~sorgen für~ ("to care for, provide for") carries the positive sense of attentiveness, while ~sich sorgen um~ ("to worry about") carries the negative sense of anxiety. The noun ~Sorge~ preserves both meanings simultaneously. The same root took only the suffering side in English ~sorrow~. Cognate with Dutch ~zorgen~, English ~sorrow~, Danish ~sørge~, Swedish ~sörja~.
en.sortieren: Borrowed from Italian ~sortire~, itself from Latin ~sortīrī~ ("to cast lots, to select by lot"), a derivation of the noun ~sors~ (genitive ~sortis~, "lot, fate, share"), which also gave English ~sort~, ~sorcery~ (originally divination by lots), and ~consort~. The ~-ieren~ suffix is the standard German adaptation of Romance ~-ire~/~-ir~ verb endings, seen in hundreds of loanwords like ~studieren~, ~telefonieren~, and ~organisieren~. Though Latin ~sors~ originally concerned fate and chance, ~sortieren~ has lost all mystical connotations and simply means "to sort" or "to arrange." Cognate with French ~sortir~ (which shifted meaning to "to go out") and Spanish ~sortear~ ("to raffle, to dodge").
en.sparen:
From Middle High German ~sparn~, from Old High German ~sparēn~, ~sparōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sparēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~sparaz~ ("sparing, frugal"), from Proto-Indo-European *~speh₁-~ ("to thrive, prosper; to leave untouched"). Cognate with English ~spare~, Dutch ~sparen~, Swedish ~spara~, Icelandic ~spara~.

English ~spare~ is the direct cognate, descended from Old English ~sparian~, and the two verbs share the same fundamental sense: to leave something untouched, to refrain from consuming or harming it. The same PIE root *~speh₁-~ produced Latin ~prosperus~ ("lucky, favorable") — whence English ~prosper~ and ~prosperity~. The original meaning hovered between restraint and abundance: to spare something was both to hold back and to ensure it remained plentiful. Old Church Slavonic ~sporŭ~ ("plentiful") and Sanskrit ~sphirá~ ("thick, abundant") preserve the prosperity side of the root; German ~sparsam~ ("frugal, economical") and English ~spare~ (adj., "extra, scanty") preserve the restraint side. ~Sparen~ and English ~spare~ thus meet in the same paradox: to save is both to go without and to ensure you have enough.
en.speichern: ~Speichern~ ("to store, save") is a denominal verb formed from ~Speicher~ ("storehouse, granary, attic"). ~Speicher~ descends from Middle High German ~spīcher~, Old High German ~spīhhāri~, and Proto-West Germanic *~spīkārī~, borrowed from Late Latin ~spīcārium~ ("granary"), itself derived from ~spīca~ ("ear of grain, spike"). The Latin word also gives English ~spike~. The semantic journey is remarkable: a word for storing grain in a granary became the standard German word for saving a file to a hard drive or the cloud. Computer ~Speicher~ ("memory, storage") follows the same path. The ear-of-grain sense is preserved in the related English word ~spice~, which entered English via Old French from the same Latin root — both grain and seasoning were stored together in ancient storehouses.
en.spenden: From Middle High German ~spenden~, from Old High German ~spentōn~ ("to consume, use, spend"), from Proto-West Germanic *~spendōn~ ("to spend"), borrowed from Latin ~expendere~ ("to weigh out, pay out"). The PIE root is *~(s)pend-~ ("to pull, stretch, spin"), which also produced Latin ~pendere~ ("to hang, weigh") and ~pondus~ ("weight"). English ~spend~ is a direct cognate, descending from Old English ~spendan~, which was borrowed from the same Proto-West Germanic form. Both the German and English words share this remarkable path through church Latin, where monastic communities would ~expendere~ their resources in acts of charity. However, the two languages diverged semantically: English ~spend~ generalized to mean any outlay of money or effort, while German ~spenden~ narrowed to mean specifically "to donate, to give charitably." So a German speaker who wants to say "spend money" must use ~ausgeben~, while ~spenden~ carries the moral weight of generosity. The Latin root also produced English ~expend~, ~dispense~, ~pension~, and ~ponder~, all connected by the original image of weighing something out on a scale.
en.sperren:
From Middle High German ~sperren~, from Old High German ~sperran~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sparrijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sparrijaną~ ("to bar, to block"), a denominative verb derived from *~sparrô~ ("wooden beam, log"). Cognate with English ~spar~ (the verb "to bolt, bar," and the noun for a wooden beam), Dutch ~sperren~.

The original concrete sense of barring a passage with a wooden beam expanded into a wide range of figurative blocking: ~absperren~ ("to cordon off"), ~einsperren~ ("to lock up"), ~aussperren~ ("to lock out"). In computing, ~sperren~ means "to lock" or "to block" access. The related noun ~Sperre~ ("barrier, blockade") and adjective ~sperrig~ ("bulky, unwieldy") both retain the idea of obstruction.

An interesting typographic meaning survives: ~gesperrt drucken~ means to print with spaced-out letters, the traditional German method of emphasis before bold and italic type became standard.
en.spezialisieren:
Borrowed from French ~spécialiser~, derived from ~spécial~ ("special"), from Latin ~speciālis~ ("particular, individual"), from ~speciēs~ ("appearance, form, kind"), from PIE *~speḱ-~ ("to look, to observe"). The word entered German through French in the 18th century.

The verb means "to specialize" — ~sich auf etwas spezialisieren~ ("to specialize in something"). The PIE root *~speḱ-~ ("to look") produced an extraordinary family of words across the Indo-European languages: Latin ~specere~ ("to look") gave English ~spectacle~, ~spectrum~, ~specimen~, ~species~, ~special~, ~specific~, ~inspect~, ~respect~, ~suspect~, ~prospect~, and ~aspect~ — all built on the idea of looking. The semantic journey from "looking" to "special" passed through "outward appearance" (Latin ~speciēs~) to "a particular kind" and then "pertaining to a particular kind." The reflexive usage ~sich spezialisieren~ is standard in German, while the non-reflexive ~spezialisieren~ (with a direct object: ~ein Krankenhaus auf Herzchirurgie spezialisieren~) is rarer. The noun ~Spezialist~ and the adjective ~speziell~ entered German through the same French-Latin channel.
en.spiegeln:
From Middle High German ~spiegeln~, ~spiegelen~, a denominative verb from the noun ~Spiegel~ ("mirror"). ~Spiegel~ descends from MHG ~spiegel~, from OHG ~spiegal~, ~spēgal~, from Proto-West Germanic *~spēgl~ ("mirror"), an early borrowing from Latin ~speculum~ ("mirror"), which derives from ~specere~ ("to look at"), from PIE *~speḱ-~ ("to see, to observe"). Compare Middle Dutch ~spiegelen~ (modern Dutch ~spiegelen~).

The borrowing of Latin ~speculum~ into Germanic must have occurred during the Roman occupation of the Rhineland, when Germanic peoples first encountered Roman glass mirrors -- a luxury technology that replaced the polished metal surfaces they had previously used. The PIE root *~speḱ-~ is extraordinarily productive: from Latin it gave English ~spectacle~, ~species~, ~specimen~, ~spectrum~, ~speculate~, ~suspect~, ~respect~, ~inspect~, and ~spy~; from the Germanic side, it is the source of German ~spähen~ ("to spy, to scout"). The verb ~spiegeln~ means both "to mirror, to reflect" and figuratively "to reflect upon" (~widerspiegeln~, "to reflect back"). The noun ~Spiegel~ is also familiar as the name of the famous German news magazine ~Der Spiegel~ ("The Mirror"), founded in 1947 with the aspiration of reflecting society back to itself.
en.spielen: From Middle High German ~spil(e)n~, from Old High German ~spilōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~spilōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~spilōną~, a denominative verb derived from the noun *~spilą~ ("play, game, dance"), from Proto-West Germanic *~spil~ ("dance"). The ultimate pre-Germanic origin is uncertain. The semantic range of Old High German ~spilōn~ was broader than modern ~spielen~, encompassing dance, bodily movement, and entertainment generally. Cognate with Dutch ~spelen~, Low German ~spelen~.
en.sprechen: From Middle High German ~sprëchen~, from Old High German ~sprëhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekaną~, probably from PIE *~spregʰ-~ ("to crackle"). The semantic development from an onomatopoeic root meaning "to crackle, make noise" to "to speak" parallels the evolution seen in many languages where words for speech derive from words for sound. Cognate with Dutch ~spreken~, English ~speak~.
en.springen:
From Old High German ~springan~, from Proto-Germanic *~springaną~, from PIE *~sprenǵʰ-~, a nasalized form of *~sperǵʰ-~ ("to move energetically, to hasten, to spring"). A class III strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~springen~, ~sprang~, ~gesprungen~. Cognate with Low German ~springen~, Dutch ~springen~, West Frisian ~springe~, English ~spring~, Danish ~springe~, and Greek ~spérkhō~ ("to set in rapid motion, to hasten"). Related to Sanskrit ~spṛháyati~ ("to be eager"), Albanian ~shpërthej~ ("to pop open, explode"), and Lithuanian ~sprę̃gti~ ("to push in").

The verb covers a wider semantic range in German than its English cognate: beyond "to jump, to leap, to bounce," it also means "to break, to burst, to pop" (as in glass cracking) and, in southern dialects and Swiss German, "to run, to dash" (~spring mal schnell zum Bäcker!~ for "run to the bakery quickly!"). The "bursting" sense is the original Proto-Germanic meaning alongside "jumping" — both share the image of sudden, forceful motion. The noun ~Sprung~ ("jump; crack") preserves this dual meaning: a ~Sprung in der Schüssel~ is a crack in a bowl. Derived: ~Springer~ ("jumper; knight" in chess — because the knight leaps), ~Sprungbrett~ ("diving board, springboard").
en.spüren: From Middle High German ~spüren~, ~spuren~, ~spurn~, from Old High German ~spurien~, ~spurren~, from Proto-Germanic *~spurjaną~ ("to feel, touch; to search, examine"). That verb is a derivative of *~spurą~ ("track, trace"), the ancestor of modern ~Spur~. English ~spur~ (the boot-heel implement) comes from a closely related Proto-Germanic root *~spurô~ ("to kick"), both branches ultimately tracing to Proto-Indo-European *~sperH-~ ("to kick, step"). The original sense of ~spüren~ was following an animal's tracks — reading the earth for signs — and only later generalized to the interior act of sensing or feeling. Cognate with dialectal English ~speer~ ("to ask, inquire"), Swedish ~spörja~, and Dutch ~speuren~ ("to track, sniff out").
en.stammen:
A denominative verb derived from ~Stamm~ ("trunk, stem, tribe, stock") with the infinitive suffix ~-en~. ~Stamm~ derives from Middle High German ~stam~, from Old High German ~stam~, from Proto-Germanic *~stamnaz~, *~stamniz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~steh₂-~ ("to stand, stay"). Cognate with English ~stem~.

The PIE root *~steh₂-~ ("to stand") is among the most generative in the language: it underlies German ~stehen~ ("to stand"), ~Staat~ ("state"), ~Stadt~ ("city"), ~Stall~ ("stable"), ~Stahl~ ("steel," via the notion of something solid and standing), Latin ~stāre~ ("to stand"), English ~stand~, ~stay~, ~station~, ~statute~, ~status~, ~stable~, and ~understand~ (to stand among or under). The concrete image of a tree trunk — the standing, upright part from which branches and roots extend — gave ~Stamm~ its cluster of meanings: a tribe is the stock from which descendants branch; a grammatical stem is the standing root form. ~Stammen~ thus means literally "to have one's trunk" in something, to originate from a standing point. English ~stem~ (from Old English ~stefn~, ~stemn~) is the direct cognate, preserving the same tree-and-origin duality. Cognate with English ~stem~, Dutch ~stam~, Swedish ~stam~, Danish ~stamme~.
en.starten: Borrowed from English ~start~, with the German verbal suffix ~-en~ added; compare Dutch ~starten~. English ~start~ itself traces from Middle English ~sterten~ ("to leap up suddenly"), from Old English ~styrtan~ ("to leap up, start"), from Proto-West Germanic *~sturtijan~ ("to startle, move, set in motion"), from PIE *~(s)ter-~ ("to be stiff"). The original sense was sudden bodily movement — a startled leap or a jolt — rather than the modern sense of beginning. That native English cognate is German ~stürzen~ ("to plunge, tumble, overturn"), from Old High German ~sturzen~, which descended from the same Proto-West Germanic root. So ~starten~ and ~stürzen~ are cousins, both going back to the same image of sudden, violent movement. The modern sense of "to begin (an engine, a race, a program)" developed first in English and entered German via mechanical and sporting vocabulary in the 19th and 20th centuries. Cognate with Dutch ~storten~ ("to pour, dump, collapse"), German ~stürzen~, Danish ~styrte~.
en.stattfinden:
Attested in Old High German as ~stat findan~ and in Middle High German as ~stat vinden~, documented since the 9th century. A compound of the noun ~Statt~ ("place, stead") used as a separable verbal particle and the strong verb ~finden~ ("to find"):

- ~Statt~: An orthographic variant of ~Stadt~ ("city") and a doublet of ~Stätte~ ("place, site").
