From OHG ~stat~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stadi~, from Proto-Germanic *~stadiz~, from PIE *~stéh₂tis~ ("a standing, a standing place"), from *~steh₂-~ ("to stand"). Cognate with English ~stead~ (as in ~homestead~, ~instead of~), Dutch ~stad~, and Old English ~stede~. The noun ~Statt~ is not a standard verb prefix and should not be treated as one: it is an unproductive lexical fossil occurring only in this compound and in the preposition ~statt~ ("instead of").
- ~finden~: From MHG ~vinden~, from OHG ~findan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~finþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~finþaną~, from PIE *~pent-~ ("to go, pass; path, bridge"). Cognate with English ~find~, Dutch ~vinden~, Danish ~finde~.

~stattfinden~ means literally "to find a place" — an event ~findet statt~ when it finds its place in time and space, when it locates itself in the world. This spatial metaphor is deeply embedded in German idiom: German speakers say an event "takes place" by having it actively seek and find its location. The PIE root *~pent-~ also gives English ~path~ and ~find~ — reflecting the ancient association between traveling a path and encountering something along the way. The related noun ~Stätte~ survives in elevated or archaic usage (~Kultstätte~, "place of worship"; ~Gedenkstätte~, "memorial site"), while ~Stadt~ narrowed to mean "city" specifically — making ~Statt~ the sole surviving instance where the word retains its fully general sense of "place" in productive modern usage.
en.stechen: From Middle High German ~stëchen~, from Old High German ~stëhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stekaną~ ("to stab, prick"), from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)teg-~ ("to stick, poke"). Cognate with Dutch ~steken~ ("to stab, sting"), Old Norse ~stinga~ ("to sting"), and English ~stick~ (originally "to stab, pierce"). The card-game term ~Stich~ ("trick," literally "stab") derives from this verb, preserving the idea of one card defeating another. The compound ~Mückenstich~ ("mosquito bite," literally "mosquito sting") and the expression ~in See stechen~ ("to put to sea") show the verb's wide semantic range from piercing to departing.
en.stecken: Modern ~stecken~ reflects a merger of two historically distinct verbs. The transitive weak verb ("to put, stick something in") derives from Middle High German ~stecken~, from Old High German ~steckōn~, a geminate (doubled-consonant) derivative of ~stëhhan~ — the ancestor of modern ~stechen~ ("to prick, stab"). The strong intransitive verb (archaic ~stak~/~gestocken~, "to be stuck, be fixed in place") belonged to the same root family but followed a strong conjugation before the two merged in Early Modern German. Both trace to Proto-West Germanic *~stekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stikaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)teyg-~ ("to be sharp, stab, goad"). English ~stick~ and ~steak~ (the latter originally "meat on a stick") are cognates, as is the archaic English verb ~stitch~ in its older sense of "to pierce." The merger of the transitive and intransitive forms parallels that of ~hängen~, where a strong intransitive and a weak transitive likewise fused over time.
en.stehen: From Middle High German ~stān~, ~stēn~, from Old High German ~stān~, ~stēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stān~, from Proto-Germanic *~stāną~, from PIE *~steh₂-~ ("to stand"). The ~-h-~ was introduced into the spelling by analogy with ~sehen~, in which it had become mute but was retained in writing. The past-tense conjugations (~stand~, ~standen~) derive from Old High German ~stuond~, ~stuonden~, themselves suppletive forms from Proto-West Germanic *~standan~; their ~a~-vocalism arose by analogy with other strong verbs such as ~binden~ (~band~) and ~finden~ (~fand~), dating back to Middle High German. Cognate with Dutch ~staan~, English ~stand~, Danish ~stå~, Swedish ~stå~.
en.stehlen: From Middle High German ~stëln~, from Old High German ~stelan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stelan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stelaną~ ("to steal"), from Proto-Indo-European *~stel-~ ("to rob, steal"). This is a common Germanic verb with remarkably consistent meaning: cognate with Gothic ~stilan~, Old Norse ~stela~, Old English ~stelan~ (whence English ~steal~), Dutch ~stelen~, Swedish ~stjäla~, Danish ~stjæle~, and Icelandic ~stela~. The reflexive ~sich stehlen~ ("to steal away, to sneak") preserves an older sense of furtive movement rather than theft. The idiomatic ~jemandem die Show stehlen~ ("to steal the show from someone") is a calque from English, demonstrating how borrowed idioms travel between related languages.
en.steigen: From Middle High German ~stîgen~, from Old High German ~stīgan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stīgan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stīganą~, from PIE *~steygʰ-~ ("to walk, climb"). A class I strong verb (~steigen~, ~stieg~, ~gestiegen~). The same PIE root gave Ancient Greek ~steíkhō~ ("I go, march") and Old Church Slavonic ~stignǫ~ ("I come"). The cognate English word ~sty~ (as in "pig sty") originally meant an enclosure approached by a path — preserving the sense of going or climbing to a place. The archaic English verb ~sty~ ("to climb") shares the same root directly. Cognate with Dutch ~stijgen~, Low German ~stiegen~, Danish ~stige~, Swedish ~stiga~, Icelandic ~stíga~, Gothic ~steigan~.
en.steigern: ~Steigern~ is the causative of ~steigen~ ("to climb, rise"), formed with the German ~-ern~ suffix that creates intensive or causative verbs: "to make (something) climb, to cause to rise, to increase." ~Steigen~ descends from Middle High German ~stîgen~, Old High German ~stīgan~, Proto-West Germanic *~stīgan~, and Proto-Germanic *~stīganą~, from Proto-Indo-European *~steygʰ-~ ("to walk, climb, stride"). That ancient root surfaces in English ~stair~ (from Old English ~stǣġer~, literally "that by which one climbs") and in the obsolete English verb ~sty~ ("to climb"), as well as in Ancient Greek ~στείχω~ (~steíkhō~, "I go"). The ~-ern~ suffix is especially transparent in German: compare ~fördern~ ("to promote," causative of ~fahren~ in its older sense), ~steigern~ ("to increase"), and ~ändern~ ("to alter"). In grammar, ~steigern~ takes on a second, technical life: it means "to form the comparative and superlative of an adjective" — that is, to make an adjective "climb" through its degrees of comparison.
en.stellen: From Middle High German ~stellen~, from Old High German ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put"). A causative weak verb meaning "to place, set" — the transitive counterpart to ~stehen~ ("to stand"). Cognate with Dutch ~stellen~, Low German ~stellen~, English ~stell~ ("to place, fix"), Swedish ~ställa~, Danish ~stille~, Yiddish ~shteln~.
en.sterben:
From Middle High German ~stërben~, from Old High German ~stërban~, from Proto-Germanic *~sterbaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)terp-~ ("to lose force, become numb, be motionless") or *~sterbʰ-~ ("to become stiff"), both traceable to the root *~ster-~ ("stiff, rigid"). A class III strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~sterven~, Low German ~starven~/~sterven~, West Frisian ~stjerre~, English ~starve~.

The false-friend relationship between German ~sterben~ and English ~starve~ is one of the most striking semantic divergences in the Germanic family. Both words descend from the same Proto-Germanic root, which originally meant "to become stiff, to become rigid" — a reference to the bodily stiffening of death. German ~sterben~ has retained the general sense of dying from any cause. English ~starve~ narrowed dramatically to dying specifically of hunger, a restriction that solidified by the early modern period, though dialectal English retained the broader sense ("to starve of cold") well into the 19th century. The underlying PIE root is also connected to German ~starr~ ("stiff, rigid") and to English ~stark~ and ~stare~, all preserving some trace of the original sense of rigidity. The ablaut series ~stirbt~/~starb~/~gestorben~ follows the class III pattern shared with ~werfen~ and ~verderben~.
en.steuern:
From MHG ~stiuren~, from OHG ~stiuren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stiurijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stiurijaną~.

The root noun ~Steuer~ carries two distinct meanings that share a single ancestor: (1) ~das Steuer~ ("rudder, helm," neuter), from PGmc *~stiuriją~, cognate with English ~steer~ and Dutch ~stuur~; and (2) ~die Steuer~ ("tax," feminine), whose semantic path ran from "helm/rudder" to "supporting post" to "support" to "financial support" to "tax." Both ultimately derive from the same PGmc verbal root. ~Steuern~ thus means both "to steer, navigate, control" and, in archaic or Swiss usage, "to pay a tax." The English cognate ~steer~ shares the navigation sense exactly. That the word for navigating a ship and the word for paying taxes share a single ancestor makes ~steuern~ one of German's richest semantic clusters.
en.stimmen: From Middle High German ~stimmen~, from Old High German *~stimmen~ (attested in OHG ~gistimmen~, "to bring into harmony"), a denominative derived from the noun ~Stimme~ ("voice, vote"). ~Stimme~ derives from MHG ~stimme~, from OHG ~stimma~, ~stimna~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stebnu~, from Proto-Germanic *~stebnō~ or *~stamnijō~, from Proto-Indo-European *~stomen-~ ("mouth, muzzle"). Cognate with Old English ~stefn~, ~stemn~ ("voice"), Old Frisian ~stifne~, ~stemme~, Old Saxon ~stemna~, Dutch ~stem~ ("voice, vote"), Gothic ~stibna~ ("voice"), and Ancient Greek ~stóma~ ("mouth") — the last being particularly striking as a cross-family cognate that connects German ~Stimme~ to the Greek anatomical term. English ~steven~ (archaic, "voice, clamor") is a direct cognate. The word ~stimmen~ spans a wide semantic range today: "to tune" (an instrument), "to be correct" (~das stimmt~, "that's right"), "to vote," and "to put in a mood" (~jemanden fröhlich stimmen~, "to cheer someone up"). All of these meanings radiate from the central idea of a voice — bringing things into accord, casting a voice in a vote, being "in tune." Cognate with Dutch ~stemmen~, Danish ~stemme~, Swedish ~stämma~.
en.stoppen:
Borrowed from Middle Low German ~stoppen~, from Old Saxon *~stoppōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stoppōn~. The ultimate origin is debated: possibly from Proto-Germanic *~stuppōną~ ("to stop, close"), from PIE *~(s)tewp-~ ("to push, stick"), or possibly borrowed from Latin ~stuppō~ ("to stop up, plug"). The direction of borrowing between Germanic and Latin is itself disputed.

The original concrete meaning was "to plug, to stop up" (a hole, a leak). The figurative sense "to halt, to cease" developed from the image of stopping a flow by plugging it. Only in this figurative meaning did ~stoppen~ enter standard High German, reinforced by the influence of English ~stop~. The older High German doublet ~stopfen~ ("to stuff, to plug") preserves the original concrete sense. A third relative, ~staffieren~ ("to decorate, furnish"), arrived via a more circuitous Romance route. The compound ~Stoppuhr~ ("stopwatch") reflects the verb's secondary meaning "to measure time with a stopwatch."
en.stoßen: ~stoßen~ comes from Middle High German ~stōzen~, from Old High German ~stōzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stautan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stautaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~stówd-e-ti~, an o-grade intensive formation from *~(s)tewd-~ ("to hit, push"). The PIE root also produced Latin ~tundere~ ("to beat, pound") — whose past participle ~tūsus~ gives English ~contuse~ and ~obtuse~ — and Sanskrit ~tudáti~ ("to strike, goad"). The related Proto-Germanic formation *~stautijaną~ (a frequentative meaning "to push repeatedly") is the likely ancestor of English ~stutter~ and Dutch ~stotteren~, where the repeated-action meaning of the frequentative maps neatly onto the repeated-syllable nature of stuttering. Cognate with Old Norse ~stauta~ and ~steyta~ (whence Danish ~støde~), Gothic ~𐍃𐍄𐌰𐌿𐍄𐌰𐌽~ (stautan), Dutch ~stoten~, and dialectal English ~stot~ ("to bounce, ricochet").
en.strahlen: A 16th-century denominative verb from the noun ~Strahl~ ("ray, beam"), with the verbal suffix ~-en~. ~Strahl~ traces to Middle High German ~strāle~, from Old High German ~strāla~ ("arrow"), from Proto-West Germanic *~strālu~ ("arrow"). Cognate with Dutch ~straal~ and the rare English ~streal~. The semantic journey from "arrow" to "ray of light" is a remarkable metaphor: arrows fly in straight lines from a source, just as light beams radiate from the sun. This metaphorical extension happened in the 17th century when ~Strahl~ began to describe light and sunbeams, then expanded in the 18th century to water jets (~Wasserstrahl~) and around 1800 to heat (~Wärmestrahlung~, "radiation"). Today ~strahlen~ means both "to radiate/beam" and figuratively "to beam with joy."
en.streben: From Middle High German ~strëben~, from Old High German ~strëbēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stribēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~stribāną~ ("to strive, resist"). The verb carries connotations of earnest, directed effort toward a goal. The derived noun ~Streber~ ("overachiever, teacher's pet") has decidedly negative connotations in German, unlike the neutral English ~striver~. The compound ~Bestreben~ ("endeavor") and the participial adjective ~strebsam~ ("ambitious, industrious") maintain more positive associations. Cognate with Dutch ~streven~ ("to strive") and English ~strive~ (via Old French ~estriver~, itself borrowed from a Germanic source related to ~streben~).
en.streichen:
From Middle High German ~strīchen~, from Old High German ~strīhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~strīkan~, from Proto-Germanic *~strīkaną~ ("to stroke, to rub"), from PIE *~streyg-~ ("to stroke, rub, press"). A class I strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~strijken~ ("to iron, to stroke"), English ~strike~.

The relationship between ~streichen~ and English ~strike~ is a case study in how cognates can drift apart while remaining connected by a shared core meaning. Both descend from the same Proto-Germanic root meaning "to move in one direction, to rub, to stroke." English ~strike~ hardened the action into a blow; German ~streichen~ softened it into a gliding motion. Modern ~streichen~ means "to stroke, to caress" (~einem Kind über den Kopf streichen~, "to stroke a child's head"), "to spread" (~Butter aufs Brot streichen~, "to spread butter on bread"), "to paint" (~die Wand streichen~, "to paint the wall"), and "to cross out, to cancel, to delete" (~ein Wort streichen~, "to strike out a word"). This last meaning — crossing out — is the closest to English ~strike~ (as in "strike from the record"). In a delightful irony, German borrowed the English cognate back as ~Streik~ ("labor strike") and ~streiken~ ("to go on strike") in the 19th century, because the native ~streichen~ had drifted too far from the combative sense. The noun ~Strich~ ("line, stroke, dash") and the diminutive ~streicheln~ ("to pet, to caress") round out the family.
en.streiten:
From MHG ~strīten~, from OHG ~strītan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~strīdan~, from Proto-Germanic *~strīdaną~, probably from Pre-Germanic *~streydʰh₁-~, a compound of PIE *~strey-~ ("to resist") + *~dʰeh₁-~ ("to put, place"). Cognate with Low German ~striden~, Dutch ~strijden~ ("to fight, struggle"), and English ~stride~.

The English cognate ~stride~ reveals a remarkable semantic divergence: while German ~streiten~ means "to quarrel, to argue, to dispute," English ~stride~ means "to walk with long steps." The connection lies in the physical posture of combat -- striding aggressively toward an opponent. Dutch ~strijden~ ("to fight, to battle") preserves the martial sense more faithfully than either German or English. In modern German, ~streiten~ typically refers to verbal conflict (~sich streiten~ = "to argue") rather than physical combat, though the legal compound ~Streit~ ("dispute, lawsuit") maintains a formal register.
en.studieren: ~Studieren~ ("to study, attend university") descends from Middle High German ~studieren~, borrowed from Latin ~studēre~ ("to be eager, to apply oneself, to strive after"), from Proto-Italic *~studēō~ and Proto-Indo-European *~(s)tewd-~, from the root *~(s)tew-~ ("to push, strike, hit"). Related Latin words include ~tundō~ ("to beat, strike"), and the English cognate ~stub~ (something you strike your foot against) carries the same root. The semantic journey is striking: from "to push or knock toward something" to "to strive after" to "to pursue earnestly" to "to study." In modern German, ~studieren~ is typically reserved for university-level study (one ~studiert~ at a ~Universität~), whereas ~lernen~ covers school learning more generally. A false friend: English speakers sometimes assume ~studieren~ means "to study" in the broad sense, but a German schoolchild ~lernt~, not ~studiert~.
en.stärken: ~Stärken~ ("to strengthen") is a denominal verb inherited from Middle High German ~sterken~ and Old High German ~sterken~, formed from the adjective ~stark~ ("strong") with the verbal suffix ~-en~. The adjective ~stark~ and the English adjective ~stark~ are direct cognates, both from Proto-Germanic *~starkuz~ ("stiff, strong"), from Proto-Indo-European *~(s)terg-~ ("rigid, stiff"). The semantic split between German and English is a classic false-friend trap: German ~stark~ still means "strong," while English ~stark~ shifted toward "bare, utter, rigid" — as in ~stark naked~ or ~stark raving mad~. English kept the "stiffness" nuance and lost the "strength" nuance. The same root also gives English ~starch~ (via the stiffening property of the substance). So ~stärken~ is cognate with both English ~stark~ and ~starch~, words that feel nothing alike.
en.stören:
From Middle High German ~stören~, from Old High German ~stōren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~staurijan~. Related to Proto-Germanic *~steuran~ and cognate with Dutch ~storen~, Old English ~styrian~ ("to stir, to move, to agitate"), English ~stir~.

The connection between ~stören~ ("to disturb") and English ~stir~ is direct: both descend from the same Germanic root meaning "to move, to agitate." To disturb someone is etymologically to stir them from their rest — to set them in unwanted motion. The derived noun ~Störung~ ("disturbance, malfunction") appears everywhere from psychology (~psychische Störung~, "psychological disorder") to technology (~Netzwerkstörung~, "network disruption"), while the compound ~zerstören~ intensifies the disturbance to total destruction. Cognate with Dutch ~storen~, English ~stir~.
en.stürzen:
From Middle High German ~stürzen~, from Old High German ~sturzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sturtijan~ ("to fall down, tumble"), of uncertain ultimate origin. Possibly from PIE *~ster-~ ("to be stiff") via a dental extension *~sterd-~, though the derivation is debated. Cognate with English ~start~ — a doublet descended from the same Proto-West Germanic source, though the meanings have diverged radically.

The connection between ~stürzen~ ("to fall, to plunge, to overthrow") and English ~start~ ("to begin, to set in motion") is one of the more surprising cognate pairs in Germanic. Both descend from Proto-West Germanic *~sturtijan~, but English preserved the sense of sudden, jerky motion (a startled horse ~starts~), while German kept the sense of precipitous falling. The verb covers an enormous semantic range: intransitively, "to fall, to crash, to plunge, to rush" (~er stürzte vom Dach~, "he fell from the roof"; ~sie stürzte aus dem Zimmer~, "she rushed out of the room"); transitively, "to hurl, to overturn, to overthrow" (~eine Regierung stürzen~, "to overthrow a government"). The reflexive ~sich auf etwas stürzen~ ("to pounce on something, to dive into something") adds eagerness to the violence. The derived noun ~Sturz~ ("fall, plunge, crash") and the compound ~Absturz~ ("crash, precipitous drop") — used equally for aircraft and computer programs — extend the imagery into every domain of German life.
en.stützen: Attested as a simplex verb only since the 17th century. Probably modeled after Middle Low German ~stütten~, as a back-formation from the inherited compound ~unterstützen~, from Middle High German ~understüzzen~, from Old High German ~untarstuzzen~, ultimately from Proto-West Germanic *~stuttijan~. The verb is related to Proto-Germanic *~stuþs~ ("post, prop"), whence English ~stud~ (in the architectural sense of "upright post"). Cognate with Dutch ~stutten~ ("to support"), and, through Low German borrowing, Norwegian ~støtte~, Swedish ~stötta~, and probably dialectal English ~stut~ ("to support"). The semantic core of "propping up" has remained remarkably stable across a millennium. In modern German, ~stützen~ covers both physical support ("to brace, to prop") and abstract support ("to back, to substantiate"), a metaphorical extension shared with its English cousin ~support~ (though the two words are unrelated etymologically). The fact that the simplex ~stützen~ is a relatively late back-formation from ~unterstützen~ reverses the usual pattern, where prefixed verbs derive from simpler roots -- here the compound came first.
en.suchen: From Middle High German ~suochen~, ~süechen~, from Old High German ~suohhen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sōkijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sōkijaną~, from PIE *~seh₂g-~ ("to seek out"). The modern form without umlaut reflects the Upper German dialect, which resisted umlaut of long rounded vowels before velars; Central German forms show the expected umlaut (compare Luxembourgish ~sichen~). Cognate with Dutch ~zoeken~, Low German ~söken~, Danish ~søge~, English ~seek~, Gothic ~sōkjan~.
en.tanzen:
From Middle High German ~tanzen~, borrowed via Central German dialects from Old French ~dancier~ (modern French ~danser~). The adapted onset ~t-~ (rather than ~d-~) in Upper German dialects and standard German proves that the word was not borrowed directly from French but entered through Middle Low German or Middle Dutch ~dansen~, passing through Central German before reaching the south, where the High German consonant shift changed ~d-~ to ~t-~.

The ultimate origin of the Old French word is disputed, but it may derive from Frankish *~dansōn~ ("to draw along, to pull"), which would make it a Germanic word that traveled to France with the Franks and then boomeranged back into German. If this Frankish etymology is correct, ~tanzen~ is a remarkable round-trip borrowing: a Germanic word exported to Romance and then re-imported in altered form. English ~dance~ was likewise borrowed from Old French ~dancier~, making ~tanzen~ and ~dance~ doublets of sorts -- both borrowed from the same French source, but through different routes and at different times. The word appears in German texts from around the 12th century, coinciding with the age of courtly ~Minnesang~, when French cultural influence on German aristocratic life was at its peak.
en.tauchen:
From Middle High German ~tūchen~, ~touchen~, from Old High German *~tūhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dūkan~, from Proto-Germanic *~dūkaną~ ("to duck, to dive under"). A class II strong verb that became weak during the early modern period. The word had fallen out of use in Upper German dialects but was reintroduced from Central German ~dūchen~/~tūchen~ and Middle Low German ~dūken~, which had remained common. In the course of this revival, the originally strong conjugation was regularized to weak. Cognate with Dutch ~duiken~, Swedish ~dyka~. A Low German consonant variant of the same root survives as German ~ducken~ ("to duck, to crouch"), cognate with and synonymous with English ~duck~ (the verb).

The etymology of Proto-Germanic *~dūkaną~ is uncertain. One hypothesis connects it to *~duganą~ ("to be fit, to avail"), suggesting a Pre-Germanic form *~dʰugʰnéh₂-~ with an original meaning of "to fall" that evolved to "to happen, be fitting" (compare ~Zufall~, literally "falling-to," meaning "chance, coincidence"). The transitive sense "to immerse, to submerge, to bathe" (~die Äpfel in heißes Karamell tauchen~, "to dip the apples in hot caramel") coexists with the intransitive "to dive" — a neat semantic pairing of agent and object. The derivative ~auftauchen~ ("to emerge, to surface, to turn up") is among the most common compounds: things, people, and problems all ~tauchen auf~ in German.
en.tauschen:
From Middle High German ~tiuschen~, derived from ~tūschen~, related to ~täuschen~ ("to deceive"), both of uncertain further origin. The semantic shift ran from "to deceive" to "to trick someone into buying one's goods" to "to trade, exchange, barter."

The shared ancestry of ~tauschen~ ("to exchange") and ~täuschen~ ("to deceive") reveals a deeply cynical medieval view of commerce: trading was, at its root, a form of trickery. The two verbs differentiated only gradually, with ~tauschen~ losing its negative connotation while ~täuschen~ retained it. The pair is a classic example of semantic bifurcation, where a single word splits into two with divergent meanings. In modern German, the two words are completely distinct: ~tauschen~ is neutral ("to swap, exchange, barter"), while ~täuschen~ is negative ("to deceive, mislead"). The derivative ~enttäuschen~ ("to disappoint") literally means "to un-deceive" -- to strip away an illusion, revealing a less pleasant reality.
en.teilen: From Middle High German ~teilen~ and Old High German ~teilen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dailijan~ ("to divide, part, deal"), from Proto-Germanic *~dailijaną~, a denominative of *~dailiz~ ("part, share"), from PIE *~dʰ(h₁)-oy-lo-~ (alternatively *~dʰayl-~), tentatively from *~dʰeh₁-~ ("to put, set, place"), though the precise PIE derivation is uncertain. The semantic core of "dividing into parts" is consistent and stable across the entire recorded history of the word. The same root yields ~Teil~ ("part"), ~Abteil~ ("compartment"), and the legal term ~Anteil~ ("share, stake"). Cognate with Dutch ~delen~, English ~deal~ (in both the commercial sense of "to deal cards / goods" and the older sense of "a part, a great deal"), Danish ~dele~, Swedish ~dela~.
en.teilnehmen:
A calque of Latin ~participō~ ("to share in, partake of"), itself from ~pars~ ("part") + ~capere~ ("to take"). Compound of ~Teil~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~Teil~: From MHG ~teil~, from OHG ~teil~, from Proto-West Germanic *~daili~, from Proto-Germanic *~dailiz~, tentatively from PIE *~dʰeh₁-~ ("to put, do") via a stem meaning "portion, watershed." Cognate with English ~deal~ (as in "a great deal," a portion), Dutch ~deel~.
- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nëmen~, from OHG ~nëman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemaną~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due"). A class IV strong verb.

Unlike standard prefix compounds (~ab-~, ~an-~, ~be-~, etc.), ~teilnehmen~ is built from a noun (~Teil~, "part") plus a verb — a verbal noun compound rather than a prefixed form. The noun ~Teil~ in this construction is considered grammaticalized, which is why it is written lowercase when separated: ~ich nehme teil~. The parallel calques across neighboring languages are striking: Dutch ~deelnemen~, Icelandic ~taka þátt~, and English ~take part~ all replicate the same Latin model independently, each "translating" Latin ~participō~ into native materials. Cognate with Dutch ~deelnemen~.
en.testen: Borrowed from English ~test~, plus the German verbal suffix ~-en~. English ~test~ comes from Middle English ~test~, ~teste~, from Old French ~test~, ~teste~ ("an earthen vessel, especially a pot in which metals were tried"), from Latin ~testum~ ("earthen pot, lid of a clay vessel"), related to Latin ~testa~ ("clay pot, potsherd") and ~terra~ ("earth, land"). The leap from clay pot to examination came via assaying: medieval metallurgists placed ore in a ~testum~ to fire and analyze it, and this concrete act of proving metal by heat became the general metaphor for any examination of quality. The word entered German in the twentieth century as a scientific and technical borrowing, making ~testen~ one of the youngest verbs in the standard vocabulary — a word born in the laboratory.
en.thematisieren:
From ~Thema~ + ~-isieren~:

- ~Thema~: Borrowed from Latin ~thema~, from Ancient Greek ~théma~ ("that which is placed, a proposition"), from the verb ~tithénai~ ("to put, place"), from PIE *~dʰeh₁-~ ("to put, place, set").
- ~-isieren~: The standard German suffix for creating verbs from adjectives or nouns, borrowed from French ~-iser~, ultimately from Greek ~-ízein~.

~Thematisieren~ means "to bring up as a topic, to make something a theme of discussion, to address (an issue)." The PIE root *~dʰeh₁-~ ("to put, place") is one of the most prolific roots in the entire family: it produced Greek ~thésis~ ("a placing," whence English ~thesis~), Latin ~facere~ ("to do, make," from an extended form, whence English ~fact~, ~factory~, ~fashion~), Sanskrit ~dádhāti~ ("places"), and Lithuanian ~dė́ti~ ("to put"). The original Greek ~théma~ meant simply "something placed down" — a proposition laid on the table for discussion — which is exactly what ~thematisieren~ does: placing something into the arena of discourse.
en.tippen: From Low German, where the standard High German equivalent would have been ~tüpfen~ ("to dab, dot"). The word has two distinct semantic branches in modern German. In the sense of "to type" or "to tap," it derives from the onomatopoeic imitation of light touching. In the sense of "to bet, to guess" (as in ~Lotto tippen~), it comes from the noun ~Tipp~, itself borrowed from English ~tip~ ("a piece of advice, a hint"). The coexistence of these two meanings -- tapping keys and placing bets -- makes ~tippen~ a minor source of amusement: ~Ich tippe auf Bayern~ could mean either "I'm typing about Bayern" or "I'm betting on Bayern."
en.tragen: From Middle High German ~tragen~, from Old High German ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganą~ ("to draw, drag"), from PIE *~dʰregʰ-~ ("to pull, draw, drag"). A class VI strong verb whose semantic range broadened from "to drag, pull" to "to carry, bear, wear." Cognate with Dutch ~dragen~, English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Danish ~drage~, Icelandic ~draga~, Portuguese ~trazer~.
en.trainieren: From English ~train~ with the German ~-ieren~ suffix, attested since the first half of the 19th century, when organized athletic training and horse racing adopted English terminology. The English word ~train~ descends from Old French ~traïner~ ("to pull out, to draw"), from Vulgar Latin *~traginō~, from *~tragō~, from Latin ~trahō~ ("to pull, to draw"), ultimately from PIE *~dʰregʰ-~ ("to pull, to draw, to drag"). The original sense of "pulling" or "drawing along" evolved through the notion of "pulling a person through exercises" to "training." The same Latin root gives German ~Traktor~ (something that pulls) and ~Attraktion~ (something that draws you in). The ~-ieren~ suffix is the standard German tool for naturalizing foreign verbs — compare ~telefonieren~, ~funktionieren~, ~finanzieren~. Unlike many ~-ieren~ verbs borrowed from French, ~trainieren~ came via English, reflecting the rise of British sporting culture in nineteenth-century Continental Europe.
en.transportieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~trānsportāre~ ("to carry across") in the sixteenth century, with the German verb-forming suffix ~-ieren~. The Latin compound consists of ~trāns~ ("across"), from PIE *~terh₂-~ ("to cross over"), and ~portāre~ ("to carry"), from PIE *~per-~ ("to carry forth").

The word is one of many Latin-derived ~-ieren~ verbs that coexist with native Germanic synonyms: ~befördern~ ("to convey, transport") is the Germanic alternative. The noun ~Transport~ and the suffix ~-ieren~ together illustrate how German readily adapted Latin vocabulary through a French-influenced verbal pattern, creating a hybrid word that is Latin in root but Germanic in conjugation.
en.trauen: From Middle High German ~trūwen~, from Old High German ~trūwēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~trūwēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~trūwāną~ ("to trust, believe"). The PIE root is *~deru-~ ("to be firm, hard, solid"), which also produced the words for ~tree~ and ~true~ in English, both referring to things that are firm and reliable. The semantic web is stunning: from a single root meaning "solid" grew German ~trauen~ ("to trust"), ~Trost~ ("comfort, consolation"), ~treu~ ("faithful"), and ~Trauung~ ("wedding ceremony," literally an act of entrusting). English ~trust~, ~true~, ~tree~, ~truce~, and ~troth~ all descend from the same PIE root, as do Gothic ~trausti~ ("alliance, pact") and Old Norse ~traust~ ("confidence"). The German verb ~trauen~ has a delightful range: ~ich traue dir~ means "I trust you," ~sich trauen~ means "to dare" (to trust oneself), and ~trauen~ in a church context means "to marry" (to entrust two people to each other). The related ~zutrauen~ means "to believe someone capable of," while ~misstrauen~ means "to distrust." German ~Trost~ and English ~trust~ are doublets from the same Proto-Germanic root *~traustą~.
en.treffen: From Middle High German ~treffen~, from Old High German ~treffan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~drepan~, from Proto-Germanic *~drepaną~ ("to strike, push, beat"), from PIE *~dʰrebʰ-~, *~dʰreb-~ ("to crack, crush, strike, kill"). The Proto-Germanic root carried a primary meaning of violent striking — preserved most clearly in Old Norse ~drepa~ ("to kill, strike") and in German ~betreffen~ ("to concern, affect," literally "to strike upon"). Modern German ~treffen~ has largely shed its violent sense, shifting toward "to meet" and "to hit a target," but the original force remains audible in phrases like ~ein Unglück trifft jemanden~ ("a misfortune strikes someone"). The High German consonant shift from *~dr-~ to ~tr-~ obscures the cognate relationship with Old English ~drepan~ ("to strike"). Cognate with Old Norse ~drepa~, Old English ~drepan~.
en.treiben: ~treiben~ comes from Middle High German ~trīben~, from Old High German ~trīban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~drīban~, from Proto-Germanic *~drībaną~. This is the cognate of English ~drive~: the two words share a Proto-West Germanic ancestor, separated by the High German consonant shift that regularly converted Proto-Germanic *d to German *t. The semantic range in modern German is wide — to drive (animals), to drift (on water), to pursue (a hobby or trade: ~Sport treiben~), to force or compel, and to sprout (~Knospen treiben~) — but all share the underlying sense of impelling motion. Cognate with Dutch ~drijven~, Low German ~drieven~, Swedish ~driva~, and Danish ~drive~. The Class I strong verb ablaut pattern (~treiben~, ~trieb~, ~getrieben~) is one of the most regular in the language, directly reflecting the Proto-Germanic vowel grades *ei / *ai / *i.
en.trennen: From Middle High German ~trennen~, from Old High German ~trennen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~trannijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trannijaną~. Cognate with Low German ~trennen~ and Dutch ~tornen~ (archaic ~tarnen~, from Middle Dutch ~ternen~, a metathesis of an older form ~trennen~). The deeper etymology is uncertain; the Proto-Germanic root is not securely connected to any PIE root, and proposed connections to a root meaning "to tear" remain speculative. Dutch ~tornen~ has narrowed to mean specifically "to unpick stitches" — a particularly apt descendant for a verb of separation.
en.treten: From Middle High German ~trëten~, from Old High German ~trëtan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tredan~, from Proto-Germanic *~trudaną~. A class V strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~treten~, ~trat~, ~getreten~. The Proto-Germanic root is probably a root aorist with alternating vocalism: present singular *~tred-~, non-singular *~trud-~, from a possible pre-Germanic *~dret-~, which may be an extension of PIE *~dr-~ ("to run"), related to roots like *~drem-~ ("to run, sleep") and Sanskrit ~drā~ ("to run, make haste"). The semantic shift from "to run" to "to step, tread" reflects a narrowing from general movement to deliberate foot placement. The same Proto-West Germanic base *~tredan~ gives English ~tread~, so the two verbs are direct cognates with essentially the same meaning. Derived forms extend the core sense in vivid directions: ~eintreten~ ("to step in, to occur"), ~auftreten~ ("to appear, perform"), ~betreten~ ("to enter, to step on") — every social entry and stage appearance in German involves the language of footsteps. Cognate with Dutch ~treden~, English ~tread~, Danish ~træde~, Old Norse ~troða~.
en.trinken: ~trinken~ comes from Middle High German ~trinken~, from Old High German ~trinkan~ (attested since the 8th century), from Proto-West Germanic *~drinkan~, from Proto-Germanic *~drinkaną~. This is the same word as English ~drink~: the two are exact cognates separated only by the High German consonant shift, which regularly converted Proto-Germanic *d to German *t (the "dental" portion of the Second Sound Shift). The semantic identity across all Germanic branches is remarkable — Old Saxon ~drinkan~, Old Dutch ~drinkan~, Old English ~drincan~, Old Frisian ~drinka~, Old Norse ~drekka~, and even Gothic ~𐌳𐍂𐌹𐌲𐌺𐌰𐌽~ (drigkan) all mean "to drink." No non-Germanic cognate has been securely identified, suggesting the word may be a Germanic innovation, possibly derived from an older root for "drawing liquid." The compound ~ertrinken~ ("to drown") shows how the same root covers both drinking and the lethal overconsumption of water.
en.trocknen: A deadjectival verb from the adjective ~trocken~ ("dry"), formed with the verbal suffix ~-en~. ~Trocken~ traces to Middle High German ~trucken~, from Old High German ~truckan~, ~trokkan~ ("dried out, parched"), from Proto-West Germanic *~drukn~, from Proto-Germanic *~druknaz~, *~druhnaz~ ("dry"), from Proto-Indo-European *~dʰerǵʰ-~ ("to strengthen, become hard or solid"), an extension of *~dʰer-~ ("to hold, hold fast"). The PIE root connects dryness with firmness and solidity, reflecting how drying hardens materials. The form ~trucken~ was originally predominant, but the word eventually standardized with the western variant ~-o-~. Cognate with Old Saxon ~drokno~ ("dry," adverb) and Old English ~gedrycnan~ ("to dry up"). The compound ~Trockenheit~ ("drought, dryness") and ~Wäschetrockner~ ("clothes dryer") are common modern derivatives.
en.träumen:
From Middle High German ~troumen~ and Old High German ~troumen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~draumijan~, a denominative verb from the noun *~draum~ (whence ~Traum~). The noun derives from Proto-Germanic *~draumaz~, from PIE *~dʰrewgʰ-~ ("to deceive, to harm"), suggesting that the ancient Indo-Europeans viewed dreams as fundamentally deceptive experiences. Cognate with Dutch ~dromen~, English ~dream~, and Swedish ~drömma~.

The connection between dreaming and deception runs deep: the PIE root *~dʰrewgʰ-~ also yields Old English ~drēogan~ ("to endure, to suffer") and Sanskrit ~druh-~ ("to harm"). The Germanic ~Traum~/~dream~ words originally may have meant "apparition" or "phantom" rather than the neutral "sleep vision" we intend today. Curiously, Old English ~drēam~ originally meant "joy, music, revelry" -- it only acquired the sense of "sleep vision" in Middle English, possibly under influence from Old Norse ~draumr~. This means German ~Traum~ and English ~dream~ are etymological cognates that arrived at the same meaning through different semantic paths -- German kept the original sense while English took a detour through "music" before circling back.
en.tun: From Middle High German ~tuon~, from Old High German ~tuon~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~dōną~, from PIE *~dʰeh₁-~ ("to put, set, place"). One of the most irregular verbs in German, ~tun~ belongs to the small class of Germanic anomalous verbs that resist classification into strong or weak paradigms. Its Proto-Germanic present tense derives from the PIE perfective *~dʰéh₁t~ (though the o-grade *~dʰóh₁t~ is unexpected), while its past tense derives from the reduplicated imperfective *~dʰédʰeh₁ti~. This dual PIE origin accounts for the verb's highly irregular conjugation. Cognate with English ~do~, Dutch ~doen~, Danish ~gidde~ (archaic).
en.tätigen:
Derived from the adjective ~tätig~ ("active") with the verbal suffix ~-en~. ~Tätig~ itself derives from the noun ~Tat~ ("deed"), from MHG ~tāt~, OHG ~tāt~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dādi~, from Proto-Germanic *~dēdiz~, from PIE *~dʰéh₁-tis~ ("a placing, a doing"), built on the root *~dʰeh₁-~ ("to put, to place, to do"). This is the same root that gives German ~tun~ ("to do") — so ~Tat~ and ~tun~ are, at the deepest level, the same word. Cognate with Dutch ~daad~, English ~deed~, Danish ~dåd~, Gothic *~dēþs~, Old Norse ~dáð~.

The PIE root *~dʰeh₁-~ is one of the most prolific in the Indo-European family. It produced Greek ~títhēmi~ ("I place"), whence ~thésis~ ("a placing, a proposition") and English ~theme~, ~thesis~, ~apothecary~, and ~thesaurus~ (originally "a storehouse" — a place where things are put). In Latin, the zero-grade *~dʰh₁-~ yielded ~facere~ ("to do, to make") via an Italic sound change, giving English ~fact~, ~factory~, ~faculty~, ~affair~, and ~perfect~ (literally "thoroughly done"). Sanskrit ~dádhāti~ ("he places") shows the reduplicated present that was the original PIE formation.

The verb ~tätigen~ is a relatively modern coinage, attested from the 18th century, and belongs to commercial and legal register: ~einen Kauf tätigen~ ("to make a purchase"), ~eine Investition tätigen~ ("to make an investment"), ~einen Anruf tätigen~ ("to make a call"). In everyday speech, Germans strongly prefer simpler alternatives — ~kaufen~, ~investieren~, ~anrufen~ — making ~tätigen~ a hallmark of bureaucratic German (~Amtsdeutsch~). The journey from PIE *~dʰeh₁-~ ("to place") through Germanic ~Tat~ ("deed") to the stiff formality of ~eine Überweisung tätigen~ ("to execute a bank transfer") is a small history of civilization itself: from the concrete act of placing something with one's hands to the abstract machinery of modern commerce.
en.töten:
From Middle High German ~tœten~, ~tœden~, from Old High German *~tōten~, ~tōden~, from Proto-West Germanic *~daudijan~ ("to make dead, to kill"), from Proto-Germanic *~daudijaną~, a factitive (causative) verb derived from *~daudaz~ ("dead") + *~-janą~ (the factitive suffix meaning "to make X"). Cognate with Dutch ~doden~, Old Norse ~deyða~ (Icelandic ~deyða~), Gothic ~dauþjan~. The surface analysis is transparent: ~tot~ ("dead") + ~-en~, literally "to make dead." Compare English ~deaden~, which follows the same derivation pattern from ~dead~ but has a weaker meaning ("to dull, to muffle" rather than "to kill").

The High German consonant shift transformed the initial ~d~ of the Proto-Germanic root to ~t~: *~daudaz~ became OHG ~tōt~, hence ~töten~ rather than the expected *~döden~ (which is precisely the Dutch form: ~doden~). In colloquial German, ~umbringen~ is strongly preferred over ~töten~; the latter sounds clinical and formal, appropriate for news reports (~zehn Menschen wurden getötet~, "ten people were killed") but jarring in everyday speech. The word ~Neuntöter~ ("red-backed shrike," literally "nine-killer") reflects folk belief that this small bird impales nine prey items on thorns before eating — a grisly name for a songbird.
en.umbauen:
Compound of ~um-~ + ~bauen~:

- ~bauen~: From Middle High German ~būwen~, from Old High German ~būan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~būan~, from Proto-Germanic *~būaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~bʰuH-~ ("to become, to grow, to dwell"). Cognate with English ~build~ (via a different Germanic branch) and, more surprisingly, with English ~be~ (from PIE *~bʰuH-~ in its "to become" sense).
- ~um-~: From Middle High German ~umbe~, from Old High German ~umbi~, from Proto-Germanic *~umbi~, from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂m̥bʰi~ ("around, on either side of"). As a separable prefix, it conveys the sense of rebuilding or restructuring.

The separable ~UMbauen~ means "to rebuild, to renovate, to remodel" -- literally "to build around" in the sense of restructuring what already exists. The PIE root *~bʰuH-~ is one of the most productive in the Indo-European family, generating ~be~, ~been~, ~booth~, ~bower~, ~build~, ~neighbor~ (literally "near-dweller"), and German ~Bauer~ ("farmer," literally "one who dwells/cultivates").
en.umfassen:
From ~um-~ + ~fassen~. Compound of ~um-~ + ~fassen~:

- ~fassen~: From MHG ~vazzen~, from OHG ~fazzōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fatōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fatōną~ ("to fetch, hold"), from Proto-Indo-European *~pod-néh₂-ti~, from *~pod-~ ("to fetch"). Cognate with English ~fetch~.
- ~um-~: From Proto-Indo-European *~h₂m̥bʰi~ ("around, on either side of"). This separable prefix in ~umfassen~ is used in its inseparable sense ("to encompass, surround").

The connection between ~fassen~ and English ~fetch~ is one of the more surprising Germanic cognate pairs: both descend from the same PIE root meaning "to get, obtain." ~Umfassen~ — literally "to grasp around" — developed its abstract sense of "to comprise, encompass, include" via the image of something being held within an encircling grip. The related noun ~Umfang~ ("scope, extent, circumference") shares this spatial metaphor. ~Fassen~ itself has a rich idiomatic life in German: ~sich fassen~ ("to compose oneself"), ~Fassung~ ("composure; version of a text"), and ~unfassbar~ ("incomprehensible, literally ungraspable").
en.umgeben:
Compound of ~um-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~geben~, from OHG ~geban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebanan~, from PIE *~ghebh-~ ("to give"). Cognate with English ~give~ (though the English form was heavily influenced by Old Norse ~gefa~), Dutch ~geven~, Swedish ~giva~.
- ~um-~: From MHG ~um(b)(e)-~, from OHG ~umbi~, from Proto-West Germanic *~umbi~, from Proto-Germanic *~umbi~ ("around"), from PIE *~h₂mbhi~ ("around, on either side of"). Cognate with Latin ~ambi-~ (as in ~ambiguous~, literally "going around both sides") and Greek ~amphi-~ (as in ~amphitheater~).

As an inseparable compound (stressed on ~-ge-~), ~umgeben~ means "to surround," literally "to give around." The metaphor is of encircling something by "giving" or placing oneself all around it. The PIE root *~ghebh-~ has a surprisingly broad semantic range: alongside "to give," it also carried notions of "to grab, to seize" in some branches (compare Latvian ~gabties~, "to snatch," and Old Church Slavonic ~gabati~, "to seize"). The shift between "to give" and "to grab" may reflect different perspectives on the same physical exchange. The participle ~umgeben~ also functions as an adjective meaning "surrounded" (~von Bergen umgeben~, "surrounded by mountains"), one of those German participles that has essentially become a standalone word in its own right.
en.umgehen:
~um-~ + ~gehen~. Compound of ~um-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gān~, ~gēn~, from OHG ~gān~, ~gēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gān~, from Proto-Germanic *~gāną~, from PIE *~ǵʰeh₁-~ ("to leave").
- ~um-~: From PIE *~h₂m̥bʰi~ ("around, on either side of"). The prefix in its separable use adds a sense of going around or circulating; in its inseparable use, a sense of bypassing or circumventing.

~Umgehen~ is one of the classic German separability pairs, where stress placement alone changes meaning. The separable ~umgehen~ (stress on ~um-~) means "to circulate, go around, deal with" — as in ~mit jemandem umgehen~ ("to deal with, to treat someone") or a rumor ~geht um~ ("goes around, circulates"). The inseparable ~umgehen~ (stress on ~ge-~) means "to circumvent, bypass, avoid" — as in ~ein Gesetz umgehen~ ("to circumvent a law"). Both meanings trace to the same spatial logic of going around something, but the separable form emphasizes circulation while the inseparable emphasizes avoidance. This stress-based distinction — inseparable meaning derived from uninterruptible prefix fusion — is one of the most productive patterns in German morphology. The PIE root *~ǵʰeh₁-~ ("to leave") produces the paradox that "going" originally meant "leaving" — direction is always relative to a starting point. Cognate with Dutch ~omgaan~.
en.umsetzen:
Compound of ~um-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~satjaną~ ("to set, place down"), from PIE *~sodéyeti~. A causative of *~sitjaną~ ("to sit"): literally "to cause to sit."
- ~um-~: From MHG ~um(b)-~, from OHG ~umbi-~, from Proto-Germanic *~umbi-~, from PIE *~h₂m̥bʰi~ ("around, on either side of"). This separable prefix adds a sense of turning around, repositioning, or encircling.

The core sense of ~umsetzen~ is "to re-place, set in a new position" — moving a plant to a new pot, a player to a new position, or a plan into action (~in die Tat umsetzen~, "to implement"). This last sense, "to implement, realize," developed in Early New High German and is now the dominant meaning in business and administrative German. The financial sense, ~Umsatz~ ("turnover, revenue"), derives from this same root — money that has been "turned over," set around in circulation. Cognate with Middle English ~umsetten~ ("to surround, beset").
en.umstellen:
Compound of ~um-~ + ~stellen~:

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand"). Cognate with English ~stall~ and Dutch ~stellen~.
- ~um-~: From OHG ~umbi~, from Proto-Germanic *~umbi~ ("around"), from PIE *~h₂m̥bʰi~ ("around, on either side of").

~Umstellen~ is a textbook example of how German prefix stress determines meaning. With stress on ~um-~ (separable: ~er stellt die Möbel um~), it means "to rearrange" — literally "to place around." With stress on ~-stellen~ (inseparable: ~die Polizei umstellt das Gebäude~), it means "to surround, to encircle." Same prefix, same root, but different stress patterns yield different semantics, different syntax, and different past participles (~umgestellt~ vs. ~umstellt~).
en.umwandeln:
Compound of ~um-~ + ~wandeln~:

- ~wandeln~: From MHG ~wandeln~, from OHG ~wantalōn~ ("to change, to wander"), from Proto-West Germanic *~wandalōn~, a frequentative derived from Proto-Germanic *~wandijaną~ ("to turn, to change"), from PIE *~wendʰ-~ ("to turn, to wind"). Cognate with English ~wend~ (as in "to wend one's way"), ~wander~, ~wind~ (the verb), Dutch ~wandelen~ ("to walk, to stroll").
- ~um-~: From MHG ~um(be)~, from OHG ~umbi~, from Proto-Germanic *~umbi~, from PIE *~h₂m̥bʰi~ ("around, on either side of"). As a separable prefix here, it conveys the sense of changing something over, transforming.

~Umwandeln~ means "to transform, to convert, to transmute" — literally to turn something around, to change it into something else. The noun ~Umwandlung~ ("transformation, conversion") is used in chemistry (~Stoffumwandlung~, "material conversion"), law (~Umwandlung einer Strafe~, "commutation of a sentence"), and biology (~Umwandlung von Energie~, "energy conversion"). The PIE root *~wendʰ-~ ("to turn") is one of the great motion roots of Indo-European, producing English ~wind~ (the verb), ~wander~, ~wend~, and through the Gmc tribal name *~Wandilōz~ ("the wanderers"), the name of the ~Vandals~, the Germanic tribe whose sack of Rome in 455 CE made their name synonymous with destruction.
en.unterbrechen:
From MHG ~underbrëchen~, from OHG ~untarbrehhan~, a calque of Latin ~interrumpere~ ("to break between"):

- ~brechen~: From MHG ~brëchen~, from OHG ~brëhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~brekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~brekaną~, from PIE *~bʰreg-~ ("to break"). Cognate with English ~break~, Dutch ~breken~, Old Saxon ~brekan~.
- ~unter-~: From PIE *~n̥dʰér~ ("under, beneath"). In this compound it carries the sense of "between, in the middle of."

~Unterbrechen~ is a rare example of a documented calque — a word-for-word translation — from Latin into Old High German. Latin ~inter-~ ("between") + ~rumpere~ ("to break") was rendered as OHG ~untar-~ + ~brehhan~, producing a Germanic compound with identical internal structure. The verb means "to interrupt" — to break into the middle of something. The noun ~Unterbrechung~ ("interruption") follows naturally. The PIE root *~bʰreg-~ is one of the most broadly preserved roots in the family: it appears in Latin ~frangere~ ("to break," whence English ~fracture~, ~fragment~, and ~fraction~), and it has remained semantically stable — meaning "to break" — in an unbroken line from PIE through Proto-Germanic to modern German and English. Compare Dutch ~onderbreken~, another parallel calque.
en.unterbringen:
Compound of ~unter-~ + ~bringen~:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringaną~, from PIE *~bʰrenk-~ ("to bring"). Cognate with English ~bring~, Dutch ~brengen~, Afrikaans ~bring~, Gothic ~briggan~.
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under~, from OHG ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~ ("under, between"), from PIE *~n̥dʰér~ ("under, beneath"). As a separable prefix, it means "under" or "among," here conveying the idea of placing something into shelter or accommodation.

The literal sense is "to bring under (a roof)," i.e., to accommodate, to house, to place somewhere. From this spatial meaning, ~unterbringen~ has developed figurative uses: ~Waren unterbringen~ ("to store goods"), ~ein Kind unterbringen~ ("to place a child," as in foster care), and ~eine Bemerkung unterbringen~ ("to slip in a remark"). The underlying metaphor of bringing something under shelter or into a contained space applies equally to people, objects, and words. English ~bring~ is the direct cognate of ~bringen~, and the two verbs have maintained remarkably parallel meanings for over a millennium. The PIE root *~bʰrenk-~ is notable for being one of the few Proto-Indo-European roots whose Germanic reflexes show an anomalous nasal infix that has never been fully explained -- ~bringen~ is simply an etymological puzzle that linguists continue to debate.
en.unterhalten:
From Middle High German ~underhalten~. Compound of ~unter-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldaną~, of uncertain PIE origin — possibly from *~ḱel-~ ("to hide, conceal") or *~kelH-~ ("to rise"). Cognate with English ~hold~, Dutch ~houden~, Icelandic ~halda~, Swedish ~hålla~.
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under-~, from OHG ~untar-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~under~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from PIE *~n̥dʰér~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix (as in this verb), it carries a sustaining or supporting sense rather than a purely spatial one.

The verb ~unterhalten~ is notable for bearing two quite different meanings: "to entertain, to converse with" (reflexive ~sich unterhalten~) and "to maintain, to support" (e.g. a family or a building). Both senses derive from the underlying image of "holding something up from below" — supporting it. The entertainment sense arose from the idea of sustaining someone's attention or spirits. The noun ~Unterhaltung~ similarly means both "entertainment" and "maintenance" (as in ~Straßenunterhaltung~ = "road maintenance"). Cognate with Hunsrik ~unnerhalle~. The inseparable prefix distinguishes ~unterhalten~ from separable ~untergehen~ ("to go under, to perish") — a morphological contrast that encodes whether ~unter-~ modifies the verb's meaning structurally or merely directionally.
en.unterliegen:
From Middle High German ~unterligen~, from Old High German ~untarliggan~ ("to lie beneath"), equivalent to ~unter-~ + ~liegen~:

- ~liegen~: From MHG ~ligen~, from OHG ~ligen~ (earlier ~liggen~), from Proto-West Germanic *~liggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~ligjaną~, from PIE *~legʰ-~ ("to lie down").
- ~unter-~: From PIE *~n̥dʰér~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix here, it conveys the sense of being beneath or being subjected to.

~Unterliegen~ means "to be subject to" (~einer Pflicht unterliegen~, "to be subject to an obligation") or "to be defeated, to succumb" (~dem Gegner unterliegen~, "to lose to the opponent"). The physical image is powerful: the one who lies underneath has been pinned, overcome, forced down. This is the exact semantic equivalent of English ~underlie~ (and the related ~succumb~, from Latin ~sub-~ + ~cumbere~, "to lie under"), confirming that the metaphor of spatial position as power relation is deeply rooted in Indo-European thought. Cognate with Dutch ~onderliggen~, English ~underlie~, Gothic ~𐌻𐌹𐌲𐌰𐌽~.
en.unternehmen:
From Middle High German ~undernëmen~, from Old High German ~untarnëman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~underneman~. Compound of ~unter-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nëmen~, from OHG ~nëman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemaną~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
- ~unter-~: From PIE *~n̥dʰér~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix here, ~unter-~ conveys the sense of support or undertaking from below.

The modern meaning of "to undertake, to attempt, to do" was first attested in the 16th century. The derived noun ~Unternehmen~ ("enterprise, company") and ~Unternehmer~ ("entrepreneur") emerged later, turning the verbal concept of "taking something on from underneath" into the institutional foundations of capitalism. English ~undertake~ is a precise calque: ~under-~ + ~take~ mirrors ~unter-~ + ~nehmen~ exactly, and both languages extended the meaning from "to take upon oneself" to more specialized senses (English ~undertaker~ famously narrowing to the funeral business, while German ~Unternehmer~ stayed with commerce). Cognate with Dutch ~ondernemen~ and obsolete English ~undernim~.
en.unterrichten:
From Middle High German ~underrihten~, from Old High German compound of ~untar-~ + ~rihten~. Equivalent to ~unter-~ + ~richten~:

- ~richten~: From MHG ~richten~, from OHG ~rihten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rihtijan~ ("to right, to straighten, to direct"), related to ~recht~ ("right, correct"). Cognate with English ~right~ (both the adjective and the verb).
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under-~, from OHG ~untar-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~under~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~ ("under, between, among"), from PIE *~ndher~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix, ~unter-~ here conveys a sense of "between" or supportive direction, as in guiding someone.

The literal meaning is something like "to direct (someone) underneath" or "to set right among," which evolved into "to instruct, to teach." The verb ~unterrichten~ is an inseparable compound (stress falls on ~-rich-~, not ~unter-~), distinguishing it from the separable ~unter|richten~ (which does not exist as a standard verb). Cognate with Middle Low German ~underrichten~ and Saterland Frisian ~unnergjuchte~. The semantic connection between "directing" and "teaching" is natural: a teacher directs the understanding of students, setting their knowledge "right." English ~instruct~ follows the same logic, from Latin ~instruere~ ("to build up, to arrange, to inform").
en.unterscheiden:
From Middle High German ~underscheiden~, equivalent to ~unter-~ + ~scheiden~. Compound of ~unter-~ + ~scheiden~:

- ~scheiden~: From MHG ~scheiden~, from OHG ~sceidan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaiþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaiþaną~, from PIE *~skey-~ ("to split, dissect"). Cognate with Dutch ~scheiden~, English ~shed~, Ancient Greek ~skhízō~ ("to split").
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under-~, from OHG ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from PIE *~n̥dʰér~ ("under, beneath"). In this compound, ~unter-~ does not mean "beneath" but rather "between" — the sense of being positioned in the space separating two things, which is the conceptual basis for distinguishing them.

The semantic logic of ~unterscheiden~ — "to distinguish, differentiate" — mirrors that of ~entscheiden~ ("to decide"): both rely on the metaphor of cutting. Where ~entscheiden~ cuts options apart (deciding between them), ~unterscheiden~ cuts between two things to reveal the gap that separates them. Latin ~discernere~ ("to distinguish") uses the same spatial logic: ~dis-~ ("apart") + ~cernere~ ("to sift, separate"). English ~discern~ and ~discriminate~ belong to the same conceptual family. Cognate with Dutch ~onderscheiden~.
en.unterschreiben:
Compound of ~unter-~ + ~schreiben~:

- ~schreiben~: From MHG ~schrīben~, from OHG ~scrīban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrīban~, from Proto-Germanic *~skrībaną~, an early borrowing from Latin ~scrībō~ ("I write"), from PIE *~(s)kreybʰ-~ ("to scratch, cut, incise"). Cognate with English ~scribe~, ~shrive~, Dutch ~schrijven~.
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under~, from OHG ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from PIE *~n̥dʰér~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix here, it conveys "under, beneath."

~Unterschreiben~ means "to sign" (a document) -- literally "to write under." The concept is universal: English ~subscribe~ is a calque from Latin ~subscrībere~ ("to write under"), Dutch has ~ondertekenen~ ("to sign under"), and French uses ~souscrire~. All preserve the ancient practice of writing one's name beneath the text of a document to indicate consent. The root verb ~schreiben~ is itself a fascinating cultural borrowing: the Germanic peoples had no native word for writing (their runic inscriptions used ~ritzen~, "to scratch"), so when they encountered Roman literacy, they borrowed the Latin verb wholesale. The original PIE root *~(s)kreybʰ-~ meant "to scratch" or "to cut" -- a reminder that the earliest writing was carved, not inked.
en.unterstellen:
Compound of ~unter-~ + ~stellen~, with two distinct etymologies and meanings depending on stress:

- ~stellen~: From Middle High German ~stellen~, from Old High German ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from Proto-Indo-European *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand").
- ~unter-~: From Middle High German ~unter~, from Old High German ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from Proto-Indo-European *~n̥dʰér~ ("under, beneath"). As a separable prefix it means "under" literally; as an inseparable prefix it takes on abstract meanings.

The separable ~UNTERstellen~ means "to put underneath, to shelter" (~das Auto unterstellen~, "to put the car under cover"). The inseparable ~unterSTELLen~ has two meanings: "to assume, to suppose" (calqued from Latin ~suppōnere~, literally "to place under," the same metaphor that gives English ~suppose~) and "to impute, to accuse" (~jemandem etwas unterstellen~, "to accuse someone of something"). Dutch ~veronderstellen~ preserves the same Latin-influenced semantic development.
en.unterstreichen:
Compound of ~unter-~ + ~streichen~:

- ~streichen~: From MHG ~strichen~, from OHG ~strihhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~strikan~, from Proto-Germanic *~strikanan~, from PIE *~streyg-~ ("to stroke, to rub, to press"). Cognate with English ~strike~ and Dutch ~strijken~.
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under-~, from OHG ~untar-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~under~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~ ("under, beneath"), from PIE *~ndher~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix here, it conveys the spatial sense of "underneath."

The literal meaning is "to stroke underneath," which is to say, to draw a line under something, to underline. English ~underline~ and ~underscore~ are exact calques of the same concept. The figurative extension to "to emphasize, to stress" follows naturally from the typographic practice of underlining important text. The base verb ~streichen~ itself has a remarkably wide semantic field: "to stroke, to paint, to spread (butter), to cross out, to cancel, to roam." All these meanings trace back to the basic motion of moving something across a surface. The cognate English ~strike~ shares the physical-motion core but developed its own distinctive range of meanings ("to hit, to strike a match, to go on strike").
en.unterstützen:
From Middle High German ~understüzzen~, from Old High German ~untarstuzzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~understuttijan~. Compound of ~unter-~ + ~stützen~:

- ~stützen~: Attested as a simplex only since the 17th century, probably after Middle Low German ~stütten~ as a back-formation from the inherited compound ~unterstützen~ itself. Ultimately from Proto-West Germanic *~stuttijan~. Related to Proto-Germanic *~stuþs~, whence English ~stud~ ("protrusion; post").
- ~unter-~: From MHG ~under-~, from OHG ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from PIE *~n̥dʰér~ ("under, beneath"). As an inseparable prefix, ~unter-~ adds a sense of support from below.

The simplex ~stützen~ was probably extracted from this compound rather than the reverse: ~unterstützen~ is the historically primary form. Cognate with Dutch ~stutten~ ("to support"), and, by borrowing from Low German, Norwegian ~støtte~, Swedish ~stötta~.
en.untersuchen: First attested in the 15th century as ~undersuochen~. The existence of an unattested Old High German *~untarsuohhen~ is probable; compare Old Saxon ~undarsōkian~ (whence Low German ~ünnersöken~), Old Dutch ~undersuoken~ (whence Dutch ~onderzoeken~), and Old English ~undersecan~ (whence archaic English ~underseek~), all from Proto-West Germanic *~undersōkijan~ ("to search under or between"). Equivalent to ~unter-~ + ~suchen~. ~Unter-~ derives from PIE *~n̥dʰér~ ("under, beneath"). ~Suchen~ derives from Old High German ~suohhen~, from Proto-Germanic *~sōkijaną~, from PIE *~seh₂g-~ ("to seek out"). The spatial metaphor — searching beneath the surface — evolved naturally into the modern sense of "to investigate, examine." Dutch ~onderzoeken~, English ~underseek~ (archaic), and Old Saxon ~undarsōkian~ are direct structural parallels. Cognate with Dutch ~onderzoeken~, Low German ~ünnersöken~.
en.unterzeichnen:
Compound of ~unter-~ + ~zeichnen~:

- ~zeichnen~: Derived from the noun ~Zeichen~ ("sign, symbol"), from Middle High German ~zeichen~, from Old High German ~zeihhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taikn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taikną~ ("sign"), from Proto-Indo-European *~deyḱ-~ ("to show"). Cognate with English ~token~ and Dutch ~teken~.
- ~unter-~: From Middle High German ~unter~, from Old High German ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from Proto-Indo-European *~n̥dʰér~ ("under, beneath"). Here the prefix literally means "under" -- one writes one's name under the text of a document.

~Unterzeichnen~ ("to sign") is always inseparable and carries a formal register, used for treaties, contracts, and official documents. The verb literally means "to draw beneath" -- to place one's mark under the text. Compare English ~undersign~ and the related noun ~Unterzeichner~ ("signatory"). The PIE root *~deyḱ-~ ("to show") also produced Latin ~dīcere~ ("to say," originally "to point out"), Greek ~deiknynai~ ("to show"), and English ~teach~ (originally "to show").
en.unterziehen:
Compound of ~unter-~ + ~ziehen~:

- ~ziehen~: From Middle High German ~ziehen~, from Old High German ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhaną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Cognate with obsolete English ~tee~ ("to draw, lead"), Gothic ~tiuhan~, and from the same PIE root as Latin ~dūcere~ ("to lead"), which gave English ~duke~, ~conduct~, ~produce~, and ~educate~.
- ~unter-~: From Middle High German ~unter~, from Old High German ~untar~, from Proto-Germanic *~under~, from Proto-Indo-European *~n̥dʰér~ ("under, beneath").

The separable ~UNTERziehen~ means "to put on underneath" (clothing). The inseparable ~unterZIEHen~ means "to subject to" or reflexively ~sich unterziehen~ ("to undergo"), as in ~sich einer Operation unterziehen~ ("to undergo surgery"). The metaphor of being "pulled under" something captures the sense of submitting to a process. The PIE root *~dewk-~ is remarkably productive: through Latin alone it generated ~duct~, ~duke~, ~educate~, ~deduce~, ~introduce~, ~reduce~, and ~seduce~.
en.variieren:
Borrowed from French ~varier~, from Latin ~variāre~ ("to change, to make different, to diversify"), from ~varius~ ("diverse, variegated, changeable"), from PIE *~weh₁-ro-~ ("bent, crooked") or possibly *~weh₂-ro-~. The word entered German in the 16th century.

Latin ~varius~ originally described something spotted or multicolored — a surface with contrasting patches — before generalizing to "diverse, changeable." The same root produced English ~vary~, ~various~, ~variety~, and ~varicose~ (veins that are "bent" or "twisted"). ~Variieren~ in German means "to vary, to modify, to diversify" and is used in both everyday and technical contexts: recipes ~variieren~, opinions ~variieren~, and musical themes ~variieren~ (the ~Variation~ being a fundamental form in classical music). Bach's ~Goldberg-Variationen~, composed in Leipzig, gave the word particular cultural resonance in German-speaking lands. The derived noun ~Variante~ ("variant") and the adjective ~variabel~ ("variable") entered German through the same Latin-French channel.
en.verabschieden:
From ~ver-~ + ~Abschied~ + ~-en~. A denominal verb formed from ~Abschied~ ("farewell, parting"):

- ~Abschied~: From MHG ~abeschit~, equivalent to ~ab-~ ("away, off," from PIE *~h₂epó~) + ~Schied~ ("separation, divorce"), from ~scheiden~ ("to separate"), from OHG ~sceidan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skaiþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skaiþaną~, from PIE *~skeyd-~ ("to split, to separate"). The same root as ~scheitern~ above.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds completeness or formality to the act.

~Verabschieden~ is a double-prefix construction (~ver-~ + ~ab-~) built on a noun rather than directly on the verb ~scheiden~. It means "to say goodbye to" (reflexive: ~sich verabschieden~), "to see off, to dismiss," and — in parliamentary usage — "to pass, to adopt (a law)." The parliamentary sense is the most surprising: when the Bundestag ~verabschiedet ein Gesetz~, it "sends the law on its way" — the legislative body bids farewell to the bill as it leaves the chamber and enters the world as binding law. The reflexive ~sich aus der Politik verabschieden~ ("to take leave of politics") preserves the original farewell sense in a formal register. The root verb ~scheiden~ is cognate with English ~shed~ (to separate, to cast off), Greek ~σχίζω~ ("to split," whence ~schism~), and Lithuanian ~skíesti~ ("to separate").
en.verankern:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~ankern~:

- ~ankern~: From Middle High German ~ankern~, a denominative verb from the noun ~Anker~ ("anchor"). ~Anker~ traces to Old High German ~anker~, ~ankero~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ankurō~, from Proto-Germanic *~ankurô~. The Germanic word was borrowed from Latin ~ancora~ (also spelled ~anchora~), itself from Greek ~ankyra~ ("anchor"), related to ~ankos~ ("bend, hook"), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂enk-~ ("to bend").
- ~ver-~: From Middle High German ~ver-~, from Old High German ~far-~, ~fir-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Indo-European *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion or intensification.

~Verankern~ means "to anchor firmly" both literally (a ship) and figuratively (a principle in a constitution: ~in der Verfassung verankert~, "enshrined in the constitution"). The ~ver-~ prefix intensifies the anchoring, emphasizing permanence. The PIE root *~h₂enk-~ ("to bend") also produced Latin ~angulus~ ("angle, corner"), English ~angle~ and ~ankle~, and German ~Angelhaken~ ("fishhook") -- all reflecting the concept of a bent or hooked shape.
en.veranlassen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~Anlass~ + ~-en~:

- ~Anlass~: From MHG ~anelāz~, ~anlāz~, a deverbal noun from ~anlassen~ ("to start, set going"), literally "a letting-on." The verb ~lassen~ descends from MHG ~lāzen~, from OHG ~lāȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lātan~ ("to let, allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lētaną~ ("to leave behind, allow"), from PIE *~leh₁d-~ ("to let, leave behind"). Cognate with English ~let~ and Dutch ~laten~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a causative, completive sense — bringing about a cause.

~Veranlassen~ means "to cause, to prompt, to arrange for something to happen" — an elegant three-layered word. At its core is ~lassen~ ("to let"), prefixed with ~an-~ to form ~Anlass~ ("occasion, cause" — literally "a letting-on"), then further prefixed with ~ver-~ to create a causative verb: "to create the occasion for." The noun ~Veranlassung~ ("instigation, cause") appears frequently in official correspondence: ~auf Veranlassung des Ministers~ ("at the instigation of the minister"). The PIE root *~leh₁d-~ has a satisfying doublet in English: ~let~ (Germanic path) and ~late~ (from the same root via the adjective meaning "slow, tardy" — one who has been "left behind").
en.veranstalten:
From ~ver-~ + ~Anstalt~ + ~-en~, attested from the early 18th century. Compound of ~ver-~ + the denominal verb derived from ~Anstalt~:

- ~Anstalt~: From MHG ~anstalt~, a deverbal noun from ~anstellen~ ("to set up, arrange"), itself from ~an-~ + ~stellen~. ~Stellen~: from MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix conveys thoroughness and completion — to bring an arrangement fully into being.

~Veranstalten~ means "to organize, to arrange, to stage" — exclusively for events and occasions: concerts, festivals, competitions, demonstrations. One ~veranstaltet~ a wedding reception but not a bookshelf. The word is structurally layered: ~stellen~ ("to place") became ~anstellen~ ("to set up") became ~Anstalt~ ("institution, arrangement") became ~veranstalten~ ("to arrange an event") — each derivation adding a level of organizational complexity. The derived nouns ~Veranstalter~ ("organizer") and ~Veranstaltung~ ("event") are ubiquitous in German cultural life. ~Anstalt~ itself followed a separate semantic path from "arrangement" to "institution," particularly ~Irrenanstalt~ ("insane asylum") and ~Strafanstalt~ ("penal institution"), giving it a darker coloring that ~veranstalten~ has entirely escaped. The colloquial accusation ~was veranstaltest du für einen Lärm?~ ("what kind of racket are you staging?") adds a note of mock-theatrical indignation.
en.verarbeiten:
From ~ver-~ + ~arbeiten~:

- ~arbeiten~: From OHG ~arbeiten~, from Proto-Germanic *~arbaidijaną~, ultimately from PIE *~h₃órbʰos~ ("orphan"). The semantic arc from "orphan" through "hardship" to "labor" reflects the lot of the fatherless in ancient society — whose work was the defining condition of their existence. Cognate with Dutch ~arbeiden~, English ~orphan~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a thoroughgoing, transformative sense — working something all the way through until it is processed into a new state.

The prefix ~ver-~ applied to ~arbeiten~ produces a verb meaning "to process, to work through, to come to terms with." Material is ~verarbeitet~ when it is transformed into a product; grief or trauma is ~verarbeitet~ when one has worked through it emotionally — a usage that would have been familiar to Freud, for whom the German term for psychological processing was literally the verb for industrial transformation. The metaphor of the factory and the metaphor of mourning share the same word. Cognate with Dutch ~verwerken~.
en.verbauen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~bauen~:

- ~bauen~: From MHG ~būwen~, from OHG ~būan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~būan~, from Proto-Germanic *~būaną~, from PIE *~bʰuH-~ ("to be, to grow, to dwell"). Cognate with English ~build~ (via a different derivation), Dutch ~bouwen~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, *~fur-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). As a verbal prefix, it can mean "away" (consuming), "wrongly" (spoiling), or "completion."

The prefix ~ver-~ gives ~verbauen~ two distinct senses, both involving the "using up" or "spoiling" meanings of the prefix. First, "to use up in building" — ~das ganze Geld verbauen~ ("to spend all the money on construction"). Second, and more commonly, "to block or obstruct by building" — ~jemandem die Aussicht verbauen~ ("to block someone's view by building"). The figurative extension ~sich die Zukunft verbauen~ ("to ruin one's future," literally "to build over one's future") is especially vivid: the image is of someone erecting walls that block their own path forward. A third sense, "to build incorrectly" (~ein schlecht verbautes Haus~), draws on the ~ver-~ prefix's "wrongly" meaning. This semantic range — consuming, obstructing, and botching — illustrates why ~ver-~ is often called the most versatile and unpredictable of all German prefixes.
en.verbergen:
From Middle High German ~verbergen~, from Old High German ~firbergan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frabergan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~bergen~:

- ~bergen~: From MHG ~bërgen~, from OHG ~bergan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bergan~, from Proto-Germanic *~berganą~ ("to take care of, preserve, protect"), from PIE *~bʰergʰ-~ ("to take care of, protect, preserve"). Cognate with Old English ~forbeorgan~ ("to forbear, refrain"), Dutch ~verbergen~ ("to hide").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies the base verb's sense of concealment.

The base verb ~bergen~ has a fascinating semantic range: "to recover, to salvage, to rescue." It is the word used for recovering bodies from wreckage, salvaging a sunken ship, or rescuing mountaineers. The noun ~Burg~ ("castle, fortress") is a relative, from Proto-Germanic *~burgaz~ -- a place that shelters and protects. ~Verbergen~ takes the protective sense of ~bergen~ and turns it inward: to shelter something from sight, to conceal. The past participle ~verborgen~ ("hidden, secret") has become a common adjective in its own right.
en.verbessern:
From ~ver-~ + ~bessern~:

- ~bessern~: A denominative from the adjective ~besser~ ("better"), from MHG ~beȥȥer~, from OHG ~beȥȥiro~, from Proto-West Germanic *~batiʀō~, from Proto-Germanic *~batizô~ (the comparative of *~bataz~, "good"), from PIE *~bʰed-~ ("good"). Cognate with English ~better~, Dutch ~beter~, Sanskrit ~bhadrá~ ("blessed, fortunate, good").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a completive sense — improving something all the way, bringing it to a better state.

The chain runs from PIE *~bʰed-~ ("good") through Proto-Germanic's comparative form *~batizô~ ("better"), to OHG ~beȥȥiro~, to ~besser~, to the denominative verb ~bessern~ ("to better, to improve"), and finally to ~verbessern~ with the completive ~ver-~ prefix driving it toward "to improve thoroughly, to correct." In a pedagogical context, ~verbessern~ is the standard word for correcting a student's work — a teacher ~verbessert~ an essay. The Sanskrit cognate ~bhadrá~ ("fortunate, good") is a reminder that the PIE root for goodness spread from the steppes to the subcontinent. Cognate with Dutch ~verbeteren~.
en.verbieten:
From Middle High German ~verbieten~, from Old High German ~farbiotan~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~bieten~:

- ~bieten~: From MHG ~bieten~, from OHG ~biotan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~beudan~, from Proto-Germanic *~beudaną~, from PIE *~bʰewdʰ-~ ("to be aware, to make aware").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here reverses or negates the action of offering — what is offered is now forbidden.

~Verbieten~ means "to forbid, to prohibit, to ban." The semantic relationship between ~bieten~ ("to offer") and ~verbieten~ ("to forbid") is one of German's most elegant prefix transformations: the ~ver-~ prefix negates the offering, turning "I present this to you" into "I withhold this from you."
