The derived noun ~Verbot~ ("prohibition, ban") is formed with the ~-t~ suffix (compare ~Gebot~, "commandment," from ~gebieten~) and is ubiquitous in German public life — ~Rauchverbot~ ("smoking ban"), ~Fahrverbot~ ("driving ban"), ~Verbotsschild~ ("prohibition sign"). The English cognate ~forbid~ (from Old English ~forbēodan~) is a perfect structural parallel: ~for-~ corresponds to ~ver-~, and ~bid~ to ~bieten~, both from the same PGmc *~beudaną~. So ~verbieten~ and ~forbid~ are not merely similar — they are the same compound, independently preserved in two sister languages. Cognate with Dutch ~verbieden~, English ~forbid~.
en.verbinden:
From Middle High German ~verbinden~, from Old High German ~firbintan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frabindan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~binden~:

- ~binden~: From MHG ~binden~, from OHG ~bintan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bindan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bindaną~, from PIE *~bʰendʰ-~ ("to bind, tie"). A class III strong verb with ablaut ~binden~, ~band~, ~gebunden~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a completive or intensive sense — binding something thoroughly or together.

The ~ver-~ prefix amplifies the root sense of binding into "to connect, unite, link" and, in a medical context, "to bandage." The noun ~Verband~ ("association, bandage, compound") derives from the same compound. English ~band~, ~bond~, and ~bind~ all descend from the same PIE *~bʰendʰ-~, as does Sanskrit ~bandhana~ ("binding, bond") and Latin ~offendimentum~ ("chin-strap"). The cognate Old English ~forbindan~ ("to bind up, bandage") followed nearly the same compounding strategy. Cognate with Dutch ~verbinden~, Old English ~forbindan~, Sanskrit ~bandhana~.
en.verbleiben:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~bleiben~:

- ~bleiben~: From MHG ~blīben~, ~belīben~ ("to remain, stay"), from OHG ~bilīban~, from Proto-Germanic *~bilībaną~ ("to remain"), from PIE *~leyp-~ ("to stick, to adhere"). Cognate with Dutch ~blijven~ ("to remain"), archaic English ~belive~ ("to remain, stay").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a sense of continuity and formality — remaining in a fixed state.

~Verbleiben~ is the formal, administrative cousin of ~bleiben~. While ~bleiben~ is the everyday word for "to stay," ~verbleiben~ appears in official contexts: ~im Amt verbleiben~ ("to remain in office"), ~Wir verbleiben mit freundlichen Grüßen~ ("We remain with friendly greetings" — a formal letter closing). The noun ~Verbleib~ ("whereabouts") is standard in missing-person reports. The PIE root *~leyp-~ ("to stick, adhere") also produced Greek ~lípos~ ("grease, fat") and English ~life~ (via Proto-Germanic *~lībą~, originally "that which persists, continued existence"). The semantic chain from "to stick" to "to remain" to "to stay alive" is beautifully logical.
en.verbrauchen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~brauchen~:

- ~brauchen~: From MHG ~brūchen~, from OHG ~brūhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~brūkan~ ("to use, employ, consume"), from Proto-Germanic *~brūkaną~, from PIE *~bʰruHg-~ ("to make use of, to enjoy"). Cognate with English ~brook~ ("to tolerate, endure") and Dutch ~bruiken~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, *~fur-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here it carries the sense of "away, through, completely" — using something until it is gone.

The compound means "to consume, to use up, to expend" — ~Strom verbrauchen~ ("to consume electricity"), ~Benzin verbrauchen~ ("to use up gasoline"). The ~ver-~ prefix pushes ~brauchen~ ("to need, to use") past mere usage into complete consumption: what is ~verbraucht~ is spent, exhausted, used up. The English cognate ~brook~ (as in "I will not brook any interference") has narrowed dramatically from "to use" to "to tolerate," surviving mainly in literary English. The noun ~Verbraucher~ ("consumer") is the standard German term in economics and law, parallel to ~Verbraucherschutz~ ("consumer protection"). The PIE root *~bʰruHg-~ is distinct from the similar-looking *~bʰewg-~ ("to enjoy, to bend"), though both involve a sense of deriving benefit — a coincidence that has confused more than one etymologist.
en.verbreiten:
From ~ver-~ + ~breiten~, a deadjectival verb from ~breit~ ("broad, wide"):

- ~breiten~: From the adjective ~breit~, from MHG ~breit~, from OHG ~breit~, from Proto-West Germanic *~braid~, from Proto-Germanic *~braidaz~ ("broad, wide"). Cognate with English ~broad~, Dutch ~breed~, Swedish ~bred~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here adds the sense of spreading outward or making widely known.

~Verbreiten~ means "to spread, to disseminate, to propagate" — etymologically, "to make broad." The reflexive ~sich verbreiten~ ("to spread oneself") is used for diseases, rumors, species, and ideas — anything that extends its range. The derived noun ~Verbreitung~ ("distribution, spread, prevalence") is standard in scientific and media contexts. German ~breit~ and English ~broad~ are direct cognates from PGmc *~braidaz~, though they have diverged phonologically: the German diphthong ~ei~ (from earlier ~ai~) corresponds to English ~oa~ (from earlier ~ā~). The adjective ~verbreitet~ ("widespread, common") functions as a participial adjective — ~weit verbreitet~ ("widely spread") is one of German's most common collocations. Cognate with Dutch ~verbreiden~.
en.verbrennen:
From Middle High German ~verbrennen~, from Old High German ~firbrinnan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frabrinnan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~brennen~:

- ~brennen~: From MHG ~brennen~, a merger of two OHG verbs: ~brennan~ ("to set on fire," transitive) from Proto-West Germanic *~brannijan~, and ~brinnan~ ("to be on fire," intransitive) from *~brinnan~. The transitive *~brannijan~ was the causative of *~brinnan~, both ultimately from PIE *~bʰrewh₁-~ ("to boil, brew"), making ~brennen~ an unexpected cousin of English ~brew~ and ~broth~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of complete destruction -- burning something up entirely.

The archaic English cognate ~forburn~ ("to burn up completely") preserves the same prefix-plus-verb pattern, though it fell out of use centuries ago. Cognate with Dutch ~verbranden~. The completive sense of ~ver-~ in ~verbrennen~ is the same one found in ~verbrauchen~ ("to use up") and ~verschwinden~ ("to vanish") -- things that are gone for good.
en.verbringen:
From Middle High German ~verbringen~, equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~bringen~:

- ~bringen~: From MHG ~bringen~, from OHG ~bringan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~bringan~, from Proto-Germanic *~bringaną~, from PIE *~bʰrenk-~ ("to bring").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here adds the sense of completion or passing through a duration.

~Verbringen~ means almost exclusively "to spend (time)" — ~den Sommer am Meer verbringen~ ("to spend the summer at the sea"). The ~ver-~ prefix transforms the physical act of bringing into a temporal one: time is "brought through" to completion. This is one of the cases where ~ver-~ functions as a perfectivizing prefix, marking the passage of a duration from beginning to end. The word is notably more formal than the colloquial alternative ~Zeit totschlagen~ ("to kill time"), and it carries no negative connotation — one ~verbringt~ time pleasantly or productively. Cognate with Dutch ~doorbrengen~ (a calque using ~door-~ instead of ~ver-~).
en.verdanken:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~danken~:

- ~danken~: From MHG ~danken~, from OHG ~dankōn~ ("to thank"), from Proto-West Germanic *~þankōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~þankōną~, derived from *~þankaz~ ("thought, gratitude"). Cognate with English ~thank~, Dutch ~danken~, Danish ~takke~, Old English ~þancian~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). In this compound, the prefix shifts the direction of gratitude — not thanking someone directly, but owing something to someone.

~Verdanken~ means "to owe, to have to thank for" — ~Das verdanke ich dir~ ("I owe that to you," "I have you to thank for that"). Unlike ~danken~ ("to thank"), which is the active expression of gratitude, ~verdanken~ describes the relationship of indebtedness: it identifies the source to which something is owed. The PIE root *~teng-~ ("to think, to feel") links thinking and thanking in a profound way: to thank someone is literally to think of them, to hold them in mind. This thought-gratitude connection is perfectly preserved in English, where ~think~ and ~thank~ are doublets from the same root. The German noun ~Dank~ ("thanks, gratitude") and ~Gedanke~ ("thought") are likewise siblings, both descending from Proto-Germanic *~þankaz~.
en.verdeutlichen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~deutlich~ + ~-en~:

- ~deutlich~: From MHG ~diuticlīche~ (adverb), from ~diuten~ ("to interpret, explain"), from OHG ~diuten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þiudijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þiudijaną~, derived from *~þeudō~ ("people"). Cognate with Old English ~þēod~ ("people, nation") and English ~Dutch~ (originally meaning simply "of the people").
- ~ver-~: Inseparable prefix from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here it functions as a causative intensifier, meaning "to make (something) so."

~Verdeutlichen~ means "to clarify, to make clear." The deep history of ~deutlich~ is remarkable: its ancestor *~þeudō~ meant "people," and the derived verb *~þiudijaną~ meant "to make popular, to make accessible to the people" -- hence "to interpret, to explain in plain language." Over centuries, "explained for ordinary folk" became simply "clear, plain." The prefix ~ver-~ intensifies this into "to make clear." English ~Dutch~ and German ~deutsch~ are the same word: both originally meant "of the people, vernacular," as opposed to Latin, the language of the clergy.
en.verdienen:
From MHG ~verdienen~, from OHG ~firdionōn~, ~firthiniōn~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~dienen~, and a calque of Latin ~deservio~:

- ~dienen~: From MHG ~dienen~, from OHG ~dionōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~þiunōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~þewaz~ ("servant, bondsman"), from PIE *~tekʷ-~ ("to run, flee"). Cognate with Old English ~þēow~ ("servant"), Gothic ~þius~ ("servant").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix strengthens the root toward completion — serving all the way through, thereby earning.

The fact that ~verdienen~ is a documented calque of Latin ~deservio~ ("to serve fully, to deserve") is linguistically remarkable. Latin ~deservio~ is itself built from ~de-~ ("thoroughly") + ~servio~ ("to serve") — the same structural logic as ~ver-~ + ~dienen~. Both languages independently hit upon the same conceptual move: to earn or deserve something is to have served for it completely. The modern German word carries both senses — ~verdienen~ means both "to earn" (money) and "to deserve" (merit) — just as English ~deserve~ preserves only the merit sense while ~earn~ covers the financial one. Cognate with Dutch ~verdienen~.
en.vereinbaren:
From Middle High German ~vereinbæren~, analyzable as ~ver-~ + ~ein~ + ~-bar~ + ~-en~:

- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here conveys completion or thoroughness — making something fully ~ein~ ("one").
- ~ein~: From MHG ~ein~, from OHG ~ein~, from Proto-Germanic *~ainaz~, from PIE *~h₂óynos~ ("one"). Cognate with English ~one~, Dutch ~een~, Latin ~ūnus~.
- ~-bar~: An adjectival suffix meaning "able to be, capable of" (compare English ~-able~).
- ~-en~: The verbal infinitive suffix.

The morphological logic is exquisite: to ~vereinbaren~ is literally "to make-one-able" — to bring multiple parties or positions into unity, to make agreement possible. The word means "to agree upon, to arrange, to reconcile" and is the standard verb for scheduling appointments (~einen Termin vereinbaren~), reaching agreements (~eine Regelung vereinbaren~), and reconciling contradictions (~diese Aussagen lassen sich schwer vereinbaren~, "these statements are hard to reconcile"). The derived noun ~Vereinbarung~ ("agreement, arrangement") is ubiquitous in legal and business German. The related verb ~verlautbaren~ ("to announce, to make public") follows an identical formation pattern: ~ver-~ + ~laut~ + ~-bar~ + ~-en~.
en.vereinen:
~ver-~ + ~einen~. From ~ver-~ + ~ein~ + ~-en~:

- ~ein~: From MHG ~ein~, from OHG ~ein~ ("one"), from Proto-Germanic *~ainaz~, from PIE *~h₁óynos~ ("one"). Cognate with English ~one~, Dutch ~een~, Latin ~ūnus~ (from the same PIE root via Italic).
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix conveys thoroughness and completion — to make fully one.

~Vereinen~ means "to unite, to join together, to combine." The word achieved its greatest rhetorical weight in the phrase ~die Vereinten Nationen~ ("the United Nations") and in the historic context of German reunification: ~das vereinte Deutschland~ ("united Germany"). The closely related verb ~vereinigen~ — built with the same components but an additional ~-ig-~ suffix — is nearly synonymous but tends toward more abstract or political usage (~Wiedervereinigung~, "reunification"). The derived noun ~Verein~ ("club, association, society") is one of the most characteristically German institutions: from sports clubs to choral societies to rabbit-breeding associations, the ~Verein~ is the organizing principle of German civic life. The wry observation ~die Deutschen gründen einen Verein~ ("Germans found a club") whenever two or more share a hobby captures something real about German associational culture. The PIE root *~h₁óynos~ ("one") ultimately gives both German ~ein~ and Latin ~ūnus~, making ~vereinen~ and English ~unite~ (from Latin ~unīre~) etymological cousins expressing the same idea through entirely different derivational paths.
en.vereinfachen:
A denominative verb formed with ~ver-~ + ~einfach~ + ~-en~:

- ~einfach~: From ~ein-~ ("one") + ~-fach~ ("fold"), literally "onefold" -- compare English ~onefold~ and ~simple~ (from Latin ~simplex~, also literally "one-fold").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix functions as a verbalizer, turning an adjective into a verb meaning "to make [adjective]."

The ~ver-~ prefix in its verbalizing role appears across dozens of German adjectives: ~vereinfachen~ ("to simplify"), ~vergrößern~ ("to enlarge"), ~verbessern~ ("to improve"). The underlying adjective ~einfach~ entered German in the 16th century, modeled on Latin ~simplex~, and ~vereinfachen~ followed naturally. The English cognate of ~-fach~ survives in ~manifold~, ~twofold~, and ~threefold~.
en.verfassen:
From Middle High German ~verfazzen~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~fassen~:

- ~fassen~: From MHG ~vazzen~, from OHG ~fazzōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fatōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fatōną~ ("to fetch, hold"), from PIE *~pod-~ ("to fetch"), itself possibly related to *~ped-~ ("to walk, step"). Cognate with English ~fetch~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix can indicate completion, transformation, or the result of an action.

The semantic journey from "to grasp, seize" (~fassen~) to "to compose, write" (~verfassen~) runs through the intermediate sense of "to put into a form" -- one still visible in ~fassen~ itself, which can mean "to formulate, express." The ~ver-~ prefix adds completive force, so ~verfassen~ is roughly "to fully formulate" and hence "to author, compose, draft." The derivative ~Verfassung~ means both "constitution" (a nation's foundational document) and "state, condition" (one's physical or mental constitution) -- the same word for a country's governing text and for one's personal well-being.
en.verfolgen:
From ~ver-~ + ~folgen~, a calque of Latin ~persequor~:

- ~folgen~: From MHG ~volgen~, from OHG ~folgēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fulgēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fulgāną~. Cognate with English ~follow~, Dutch ~volgen~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds persistent, forward direction — following relentlessly onward.

The calque of Latin ~persequor~ — itself ~per-~ ("through, thoroughly") + ~sequor~ ("to follow") — shows the same structural thinking as ~ver-~ + ~folgen~. In English, the Latin-derived descendant is ~persecute~ (via the past-participial stem ~persecutus~), which has narrowed to its most extreme sense; German ~verfolgen~ covers the full range from "to pursue" (a goal) to "to follow" (a story) to "to prosecute" (in law) to "to persecute" (historically). The historical weight of ~verfolgen~ in German is thus considerable: the noun ~Verfolgung~ ("pursuit, persecution") carries the legacy of political and religious persecution across centuries. Cognate with Dutch ~vervolgen~, Swedish ~förfölja~.
en.verfügen:
From Middle High German ~vervüegen~, ~ferfüegen~, from ~ver-~ + ~fügen~:

- ~fügen~: From MHG ~füegen~, ~fuogen~, from OHG ~fuogen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fōgijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fōgijaną~, from PIE *~peh₂ḱ-~ ("to join, attach"). Cognate with Dutch ~voegen~, and distantly with English ~fair~ (from the same PIE root via a different branch), Latin ~pāx~ ("peace"), and English ~peace~ and ~pact~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, wrongly, or completion.

The compound ~verfügen~ developed two distinct senses from the core meaning of "to join, arrange completely": "to decree, to order" (arranging something officially into place) and — as ~verfügen über~ — "to have at one's disposal" (having something joined to one's hand, within reach). The PIE root *~peh₂ḱ-~ ("to join") also underlies English ~fay~ (archaic, "to join two surfaces perfectly") and the peace/pact cluster through Latin. Cognate with German Low German ~verfögen~, Dutch ~vervoegen~, Danish ~forføje~, Swedish ~förfoga~.
en.vergeben:
From Middle High German ~vergeben~, from Old High German ~fargeban~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gëben~, from OHG ~gëban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebaną~, from PIE *~gʰebʰ-~ ("to give").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here adds the sense of giving away completely or giving over.

~Vergeben~ means both "to forgive" (~jemandem vergeben~, "to forgive someone") and "to award, to assign" (~einen Auftrag vergeben~, "to award a contract," ~eine Stelle ist schon vergeben~, "the position is already taken"). The forgiveness sense derives from the idea of giving something away completely — releasing the debt, surrendering the grievance. The awarding sense derives from the idea of giving something out, distributing it. The derived noun ~Vergebung~ ("forgiveness") is elevated and theological, while ~Vergabe~ ("allocation, awarding") is administrative. English ~forgive~ (from Old English ~forġiefan~) is a structural cognate: ~for-~ corresponds to ~ver-~, and ~give~ to ~geben~, both from PGmc *~gebaną~. The parallel is exact — ~vergeben~ and ~forgive~ are the same compound word preserved independently in two branches of West Germanic. Cognate with Dutch ~vergeven~, English ~forgive~.
en.vergehen:
From Middle High German ~vergān~, ~vergāngēn~, from Old High German ~firgān~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fragān~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From MHG ~gān~, ~gēn~, from OHG ~gān~, ~gēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gān~, from Proto-Germanic *~gāną~, from PIE *~ǵʰeh₁-~ ("to leave"). Cognate with English ~go~, Dutch ~gaan~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here it conveys the sense of passing away, disappearing.

The literal sense is "to go away completely," yielding "to pass (of time), to fade, to expire." The past participle ~vergangen~ is used as an adjective meaning "past" (~die vergangene Woche~, "last week"), and the noun ~Vergangenheit~ means "the past" -- literally "the state of having gone away." The verb also has a darker reflexive use: ~sich an jemandem vergehen~ means "to commit an offense against someone," where "going away" from proper conduct becomes transgression. English preserves a striking parallel in ~to pass away~ (to die) and ~the past~ (what has gone), showing that both Germanic languages independently developed this metaphor of time as motion. The ~ver-~ prefix here works in its "away, to disappearance" sense, the same one found in ~verschwinden~ ("to vanish"), ~verlieren~ ("to lose"), and ~vergessen~ ("to forget").
en.vergessen:
From Middle High German ~vergëȥȥen~, from Old High German ~firgëȥȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fragetan~ ("to give up, forget"). Compound of ~ver-~ + the extinct root *~getan~:

- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a sense of losing or going wrong — "seizing wrongly" → "losing one's grip on."
- *~getan~: From Proto-Germanic *~getaną~ ("to find a way, attain, acquire"), from PIE *~gʰe(n)d-~ ("to take, seize"). Cognate with Latin ~prehendō~ ("to snatch, seize"), Ancient Greek ~χανδάνω~ (~khandánō~, "to hold, contain"). The simplex is extinct in modern German — it survives only inside compounds.

The original sense of *~fragetaną~ was "to give up one's grasp on, release." From "releasing grip" → "losing hold" → "forgetting" is a natural semantic slide. What makes ~vergessen~ a superb teaching example is that English ~forget~ follows the identical path: from Old English ~forġietan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fragetan~ — the same compound, the same PIE root, the same metaphor. The German prefix ~ver-~ and the English prefix ~for-~ are cognates (both from *~fra-~); the root *~getan~ is the ancestor of the English dialectal ~yet~ and archaic ~get~ in the sense of "to reach, find." Cognate with Dutch ~vergeten~, West Frisian ~fergette~, English ~forget~.
en.vergleichen:
From Middle High German ~verglīchen~, from Old High German ~firgalīhhōn~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~gleichen~:

- ~gleichen~: From MHG ~gelīchen~, from OHG ~gilīhhōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~galīkōną~, a denominative of *~galīkaz~ ("having the same form or body"), from *~ga-~ (collective prefix) + *~-līkaz~ ("body, form"), from PIE *~leyg-~ ("image, likeness, similarity"). Cognate with English ~like~ (verb), Dutch ~lijken~, Swedish ~lika~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds completive and comparative force — bringing a likening all the way through, converting resemblance into systematic analysis.

The PIE root *~leyg-~ ("image, likeness") gave both German ~gleich~ ("same, equal") and English ~like~. Old English ~līcian~ ("to please, resemble") is the same verb as ~gleichen~, but the two diverged over time: English ~like~ shifted toward approval and pleasure while German ~gleichen~ stayed with resemblance. ~Vergleichen~ with its completive ~ver-~ prefix became the word for deliberate comparison — not merely noticing similarity but measuring it. The legal noun ~Vergleich~ ("comparison; out-of-court settlement") reflects this: parties to a dispute are ~verglichen~, brought into formal equivalence and resolved. Cognate with Dutch ~vergelijken~, English ~like~.
en.vergrößern:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~größer~ (comparative of ~groß~) + ~-n~:

- ~groß~: From MHG ~grōȥ~, from OHG ~grōȥ~ ("big, thick, coarse"), from Proto-Germanic *~grautaz~, from PIE *~gʰrewd-~ ("to grind, crush"), from *~gʰer-~ ("to rub, grind"). Cognate with Old Saxon ~grōt~, Dutch ~groot~, English ~great~.
- ~ver-~: Inseparable prefix from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here it functions as a causative intensifier: "to make (more so)."

~Vergrößern~ means "to enlarge, to magnify, to increase." Like ~nähern~ (from the comparative ~näher~), it is built from a comparative form: ~größer~ ("bigger") becomes a verb meaning "to make bigger." The underlying adjective ~groß~ has a surprising origin in PIE *~gʰrewd-~ ("to grind"): the original sense was "coarse, coarse-grained" (as in ground material), which shifted to "thick, large, great." English ~great~ is the cognate, and its semantic journey parallels the German: from "coarse, thick" to "big" to "important, magnificent." The related German words ~Grieß~ ("semolina"), ~Grütze~ ("groats"), and ~grob~ ("coarse") all preserve the original grinding sense.
en.verhalten:
From Middle High German ~verhalten~, from Old High German ~firhaltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frahaldan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~halten~:

- ~halten~: From MHG ~halten~, from OHG ~haltan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~haldan~, from Proto-Germanic *~haldaną~, from PIE *~kel-~ ("to drive, to herd").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of holding something in check or directing one's manner of holding.

The reflexive ~sich verhalten~ ("to behave, to conduct oneself") is the dominant modern usage — literally "to hold oneself in a particular way." The impersonal ~es verhält sich so~ ("the matter stands thus, it is the case that") uses ~verhalten~ to describe how facts are configured relative to each other. The noun ~Verhalten~ ("behavior") and adjective ~verhalten~ ("restrained, subdued") both descend from the same source. Martin Heidegger famously used ~sich verhalten zu~ ("to comport oneself toward") as a key term in ~Sein und Zeit~: ~Dasein~ is the being that can ~sich verhalten~ toward its own existence — that is, relate to and take a stance on its own being. The legal jargon sense ~sich zu etwas verhalten~ ("to take a position on, to address") extends the metaphor of holding to judicial deliberation. Cognate with Middle Low German ~vörhōlden~, Dutch ~verhouden~ ("to be in proportion"), English ~forhold~ (archaic).
en.verhandeln:
From ~ver-~ + ~handeln~. Compound of:

- ~handeln~: From OHG ~hantalōn~ ("to grasp, take in hand, handle"), from MHG ~handeln~ ("to do, carry out, conduct"), from ~Hand~ ("hand"), from Proto-West Germanic *~handu~, from Proto-Germanic *~handuz~. The semantic journey from "grasping with the hand" to "dealing, trading" occurred via merchant language in the 16th century.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of thoroughness and completion.

The progression from physical handling to abstract negotiation is mirrored in English ~handle~ (from the same Germanic root). German ~verhandeln~ literally means "to deal through completely" -- to negotiate until a matter is resolved. The noun ~Verhandlung~ ("negotiation, trial") reveals the word's importance in legal and diplomatic language. Cognate with English ~handle~, Dutch ~verhandelen~.
en.verheiraten:
From ~ver-~ + ~heiraten~. Compound of:

- ~heiraten~: From MHG ~hīrāten~, derived from ~hīrāt~ ("marriage"), from OHG ~hīrāt~ ("wedding, marriage"), from Proto-West Germanic *~hīwarād~. This compound word originally meant something like "household arrangement" -- from *~hīwą~ ("household, family") + *~rādą~ ("counsel, plan").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of completion -- the definitive act of joining in marriage.

The Old English cognate ~hīred~ meant "family, household, retinue," showing the same root but a different semantic path. The reflexive form ~sich verheiraten~ ("to get married") uses ~ver-~ to mark the irrevocable change of state, much as ~sich verlieben~ marks the irreversible plunge into love. The root *~hīwą~ is also the ancestor of English ~hide~ (a unit of land sufficient to support one household).
en.verhindern:
From MHG ~verhindern~, from OHG ~firhintaren~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~hindern~:

- ~hindern~: From MHG ~hindern~, from OHG ~hintaren~. Denominative from ~hinter~ ("behind, in back of"). ~Hinter~ is from OHG ~hintar~, from Proto-Germanic *~hinder~, from Proto-Indo-European *~ḱem-tér-~ ("down, back, along, through"), related to *~ḱóm~ ("beside, near, by, with"). Cognate with Old English ~hinder~ ("in the rear"), Gothic ~hindar~ ("beyond").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a completive or intensifying sense — blocking something all the way, preventing it entirely.

The structural metaphor of ~verhindern~ is vivid: to place something ~hinter~ ("behind") an action, using ~ver-~ to make the hindrance complete, thereby preventing it. The English cognate ~hinder~ (as both verb and adjective) descends directly from the same Proto-Germanic root, and the archaic English adverb ~hinder~ (meaning "in the rear") is the same word. Cognate with English ~hinder~, Dutch ~verhinderen~.
en.verkaufen:
From Middle High German ~verkoufen~, from Old High German ~firkoufen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frakōpōn~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~kaufen~:

- ~kaufen~: From MHG ~koufen~, from OHG ~koufōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kaupōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~kaupōną~, itself a denominative of *~kaupô~ ("merchant, innkeeper"), borrowed from Latin ~caupō~ ("innkeeper, shopkeeper, petty trader") — likely a pre-Latin Mediterranean loanword. The noun *~kaupô~ also yields modern German ~Kaufmann~ ("merchant") and the place-name element ~-kauf~ in towns like ~Kaufbeuren~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix often adds a sense of completion, disposal, or doing something definitively away.

A remarkable Latin borrowing that spread across all of Germanic: Latin ~caupō~ (the lowly innkeeper who sold food and drink) gave Proto-Germanic *~kaupōną~, which became ~kaufen~ (to buy), ~verkaufen~ (to sell), Dutch ~kopen~ / ~verkopen~, and — via a different route — English ~cheap~ (originally "a bargain, a market," from the same root, as in the London place name ~Cheapside~). Cognate with Dutch ~verkopen~, Yiddish ~פֿאַרקויפֿן~ (~farkoyfn~).
en.verknüpfen:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~knüpfen~:

- ~knüpfen~: From MHG ~knüpfen~, from OHG ~knupfen~, related to ~Knopf~ ("button, knob"), from Proto-Germanic *~knuppaz~ ("lump, clod, knob"), from PIE *~gnewbʰ-~ (an extension of *~gen-~, "to compress, to pinch"). Cognate with Dutch ~knoop~ ("knot, button"), Old Irish ~gnobh~ ("knot"), Lithuanian ~gniáubti~ ("to embrace").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). In this verb, ~ver-~ adds a sense of completion or togetherness to the action.

The basic verb ~knüpfen~ means "to tie, to knot," while ~verknüpfen~ intensifies this to "to link, to connect, to combine." In modern usage, ~verknüpfen~ frequently appears in computing contexts (~Dateien verknüpfen~, "to link files") and abstract reasoning (~Ideen verknüpfen~, "to connect ideas"). The relationship between ~knüpfen~, ~Knopf~ ("button"), and ~Knoten~ ("knot") reveals a whole family of Germanic words built around the tactile notion of pressing and tying. English ~knot~ is a cousin from the same Germanic stock, and the silent ~k~ in English ~knot~, ~knob~, and ~knack~ preserves an ancient consonant cluster that German still pronounces.
en.verkünden:
From Middle High German ~verkunden~, from Old High German ~farkunden~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frakunthijan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~kunden~:

- ~kunden~: From MHG ~kunden~, from OHG ~kunden~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kunthijan~ ("to make known"), from Proto-Germanic *~kunthijanan~, a causative formed from *~kunthaz~ ("known"), which derives from *~kunnanan~ ("to know"), from PIE *~gneh₃-~ ("to know"). Cognate with English ~kithe~ (Scottish dialectal, "to make known"), Dutch ~verkondigen~, and Old English ~forcythan~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~far-~, ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth, completely"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix here intensifies the action, giving a sense of making something widely or completely known.

The PIE root *~gneh₃-~ is extraordinarily productive across Indo-European languages: it gives English ~know~, ~can~ (originally "to know how"), ~kenning~, ~cunning~, Latin ~gnoscere~ (whence ~recognize~, ~cognition~), Greek ~gnosis~, and Sanskrit ~jnana~. The semantic core of ~verkunden~ is "to make known completely" or "to announce publicly," used especially in solemn, official, or religious contexts: ~das Evangelium verkunden~ ("to proclaim the Gospel"), ~ein Urteil verkunden~ ("to pronounce a verdict").
en.verkürzen:
From ~ver-~ + ~kürzen~. Compound of:

- ~kürzen~: A deadjectival verb from ~kurz~ ("short"), from MHG ~kurz~, from OHG ~kurz~, from Proto-West Germanic *~kurt~, borrowed from Latin ~curtus~ ("shortened, cut short"). Latin ~curtus~ itself comes from PIE *~(s)ker-~ ("to cut").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix functions as a verbalizer and intensifier, meaning "to make thoroughly [adjective]."

The Latin origin of ~kurz~ makes it one of the earliest Latin loanwords in Germanic, adopted during the Roman military presence along the Rhine and Danube. English ~curt~ (blunt, rudely brief) comes from the same Latin source but has narrowed to a purely figurative sense, while German ~verkürzen~ retains both physical shortening and the abstract sense of "to shorten, abridge." Cognate with Dutch ~verkorten~, English ~curtail~ (from Old French ~courtald~, also from Latin ~curtus~).
en.verlangen:
From Middle High German ~verlangen~, equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~langen~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~langen~:

- ~langen~: From MHG ~langen~, from OHG ~langēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~langōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~langōną~, a denominative verb derived from the adjective *~langaz~ ("long"), from PIE *~dlongʰos~ ("long"). The base verb ~langen~ means "to reach (for), to extend" — literally "to make long, to stretch out toward."
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds an intensifying or completive force — "longing fully, reaching outward."

The connection to ~lang~ ("long") is preserved transparently in ~verlangen~: to desire something is to "stretch out toward" it, to reach for what is not yet within grasp. The same spatial metaphor of extension underlies English ~long~ (as in "to long for"), which is a cognate from the same PIE *~dlongʰos~. Latin ~longus~ ("long") descends from the same root, making ~verlangen~ and English ~longing~ etymological relatives by a common PIE ancestor. Cognate with Dutch ~verlangen~.
en.verlassen:
From Middle High German ~verlāzen~, from Old High German ~firlāzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fralātan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fralētaną~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lāzen~, ~lān~, from OHG ~lāȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lātan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lētaną~, from PIE *~leh₁d-~ ("to let, leave behind").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix here adds a completive or intensive sense — leaving finally, leaving behind entirely.

The ~ver-~ prefix intensifies ~lassen~'s sense of "letting go" into a more complete, often emotionally weighted act of abandonment. The reflexive construction ~sich auf jemanden verlassen~ ("to rely on someone") developed from the notion of fully entrusting oneself to another — abandoning oneself to their care — which is semantically the mirror image of the active sense "to abandon." This double life (to abandon / to rely on) makes ~verlassen~ one of the most semantically rich reflexive pairs in German. Cognate with Dutch ~verlaten~, archaic English ~forlet~ ("to abandon, forsake"), Middle Low German ~vorlaten~.
en.verlaufen:
From Middle High German ~verloufen~, from Old High German ~farlaufan~, ~farloufan~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~laufen~:

- ~laufen~: From MHG ~loufen~, from OHG ~loufan~, ~hloufan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~hlaupan~, from Proto-Germanic *~hlaupaną~, from PIE *~klewb-~ ("to spring, stumble").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here adds the sense of running its course, going astray, or passing through time.

~Verlaufen~ means "to proceed, to run its course" (~Die Verhandlungen verliefen gut~, "The negotiations went well"), "to get lost" (reflexive: ~sich verlaufen~, "to lose one's way"), and "to fade, to dissipate" (~Die Farbe verläuft~, "The color bleeds/fades"). All three meanings derive from the image of running forward and away: events run their course, a person runs off the path, colors run past their boundaries. The derived noun ~Verlauf~ ("course, progress, progression") is standard in medical (~Krankheitsverlauf~, "course of an illness"), temporal (~im Verlauf des Tages~, "over the course of the day"), and narrative contexts. Cognate with Dutch ~verlopen~.
en.verlegen:
From Middle High German ~verlegen~ ("to misplace, obstruct"), from Old High German ~firleggen~ ("to cover"). Compound of ~ver-~ + ~legen~:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjaną~ ("to lay, place in a resting position"), the causative of *~ligjaną~ ("to lie"), from PIE *~logʰéyeti~, from the root *~legʰ-~ ("to lie down"). Cognate with English ~lay~, Danish ~laegge~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of "away, wrongly" (in "misplace") and of completion or transformation (in "publish, lay down").

~Verlegen~ is a master class in German polysemy. A single verb covers: to misplace (~die Brille verlegen~), to lay or install (~ein Kabel verlegen~), to reschedule (~eine Besprechung verlegen~), to transfer (~Arbeitskräfte verlegen~), and to publish (~ein Buch verlegen~). The publishing sense gave rise to ~Verlag~ ("publishing house") and ~Verleger~ ("publisher"). As an adjective, ~verlegen~ means "embarrassed" -- from an entirely separate etymology tracing back through MHG to OHG ~firlegan~ ("sluggish, adulterous"), from ~firliggen~ ("to lie wrongly"), literally preserving the medieval association between idleness and moral failing.
en.verleihen:
From Middle High German ~verlīhen~, from Old High German ~firlīhan~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~leihen~:

- ~leihen~: From MHG ~līhen~, from OHG ~līhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~līhwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~līhwaną~, from PIE *~leykʷ-~ ("to leave behind"). A class I strong verb. Cognate with English ~loan~ (via Old Norse ~lán~, from Proto-Germanic *~laihną~, derived from the same PIE root), Dutch ~lenen~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a conferring sense — lending or granting something out completely, placing it with another.

The PIE root *~leykʷ-~ ("to leave behind") is the ancestor of both ~leihen~ and English ~loan~. The conceptual link is direct: to lend is to leave something with another temporarily. English ~loan~ entered the language through Old Norse ~lán~, a Norse borrowing that displaced the native Old English cognate ~lǣn~ ("loan, grant"). The semantic range of ~verleihen~ is broader than simple lending: one can ~verleihen~ a prize (to award it), a quality (to lend it to something), or a tool (to loan it out). The bestowing and lending senses both flow from the root idea of leaving something with another. Cognate with Dutch ~verlenen~, English ~loan~.
en.verletzen:
From ~ver-~ + ~letzen~, an archaic verb meaning "to harm, to injure":

- ~letzen~: From MHG ~letzen~, from OHG ~lezzen~, from Proto-Germanic *~latjaną~. Related to ~letzt~ ("last") and ~Leid~ ("suffering").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here intensifies the harmful action.

~Verletzen~ means "to injure, to hurt, to violate" — one can ~verletzen~ a person (physically or emotionally), a law, a treaty, or someone's rights. The base verb ~letzen~ is archaic in modern German but survives in the adjective ~letzt~ ("last") — the conceptual bridge being "the final blow," the one that finishes. MHG ~letzen~ had the remarkable enantiodromic property of meaning both "to refresh, to comfort" (as in a farewell feast, a ~Letze~) AND "to harm, to injure" — opposite meanings in a single word. The refreshment sense survives in the Swiss German expression ~Letzi~ (a farewell party) and in the archaic noun ~Labsal~ / ~Letze~ ("refreshment at parting"). The ~ver-~ prefix resolved this ambiguity by fixing the meaning firmly on the harmful side. The derived noun ~Verletzung~ ("injury, violation") is among the most common words in medical and legal German. Cognate with Dutch ~kwetsen~ (a different root) and English ~last~ (a distant relative via the same PGmc root).
en.verlieben:
From ~ver-~ + ~lieben~. Compound of:

- ~lieben~: From ~Liebe~ ("love"), from MHG ~liebe~ ("joy, affection"), from OHG ~liob~, ~lioba~ ("fortune, joy, pleasantness"), from Proto-West Germanic *~leubu~ ("love"), from Proto-Germanic *~lubō~, from PIE *~lewbʰ-~ ("to love, desire").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the inchoative sense of entering a new state.

The reflexive ~sich verlieben~ ("to fall in love") captures the moment of transformation: the ~ver-~ prefix signals an irrevocable crossing into a new emotional state. English ~love~ descends from the same PIE root *~lewbʰ-~ via a different Germanic branch (Old English ~lufu~). The PIE root also gave Latin ~lubēre~, ~libēre~ ("to please"), source of English ~libido~. Cognate with Dutch ~verliefd~ ("in love").
en.verlieren:
From Middle High German ~verliesen~, from Old High German ~firliosan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fraleusan~, from Proto-Germanic *~fraleusaną~. Compound of ~ver-~ + the archaic root verb ~liesen~ ("to lose"):

- ~liesen~: The archaic simplex survives only in compounds. From Proto-Germanic *~leusaną~, from PIE *~leu-~ ("to detach, divide, cut"). Cognate with Ancient Greek ~λύω~ (~lúō~, "to loosen, untie, dissolve"), Latin ~solvō~ ("to loosen, dissolve"), and ultimately related to English ~lose~, ~loose~, ~loss~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix reinforces and doubles down on the meaning of loss already present in the root — a semantically emphatic pattern sometimes called pleonastic prefixation.

The semantic reinforcement — the prefix ~ver-~ piling "wrongly" or "away" onto a root already meaning "to lose" — is a vivid Germanic habit of underlining loss through compounding. The archaic simplex ~liesen~ survives in ~Verlies~ ("dungeon"), originally a place where things (or people) are "lost." A notable cognate is English ~forlorn~, from Old English ~forloren~, the past participle of ~forleosan~ ("to lose entirely"), built from the same Proto-Germanic components. Cognate with Dutch ~verliezen~, English ~forlorn~.
en.verlängern:
From ~ver-~ + ~längern~ (to lengthen), a denominative verb from ~länger~ (longer), comparative of ~lang~ (long). Compound of ~ver-~ + the root adjective ~lang~:

- ~lang~: From MHG ~lanc~, from OHG ~lang~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lang~, from Proto-Germanic *~langaz~, from PIE *~dlongʰos~ ("long"). Cognate with English ~long~, Dutch ~lang~, Latin ~longus~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). In this word, the prefix carries an intensive or augmentative force — making something thoroughly longer.

The verb is formed by a common German derivational pattern: adjective comparative + ~-ern~ suffix (as in ~vergrößern~, ~verbessern~). The umlaut in ~längern~ comes from the comparative suffix ~-er~, which triggered i-mutation of the root vowel ~a~ to ~ä~. The PIE root *~dlongʰos~ appears across the Indo-European world with remarkable semantic consistency — Latin ~longus~, Greek ~dólikhos~ ("long"), Sanskrit ~dīrgháḥ~ ("long") — making "long" one of the most stable adjective meanings over five thousand years of linguistic change.
en.vermeiden:
From MHG ~vermīden~, from OHG ~firmīdan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~meiden~:

- ~meiden~: From MHG ~mīden~, from OHG ~mīdan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~mīþan~, from Proto-Germanic *~mīþaną~, from PIE *~meyth₂-~ ("to change, switch"). A class I strong verb. Cognate with Dutch ~mijden~, Old English ~mīþan~ ("to avoid, conceal"), Old Saxon ~mīthan~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies the act of shunning — avoiding something completely, keeping well away.

The PIE root *~meyth₂-~ ("to change, switch") gives the original sense of switching away from something, turning aside — which in Germanic narrowed specifically to avoidance and shunning. The expected Grammatischer Wechsel forms of the past tense (*~mitt~, *~gemitten~) were levelled out in modern German — possibly to avoid confusion with the preposition ~mit~ and the adverb ~mitten~. Cognate with Dutch ~vermijden~, Old English ~mīþan~.
en.vermissen:
From MHG ~vermissen~, from OHG ~firmissen~, equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~missen~:

- ~missen~: From MHG ~missen~, from OHG ~missan~ ("to miss, to lack"), from Proto-Germanic *~missijaną~ ("to miss, to fail"), from *~missō~ ("wrongly, amiss"), from PIE *~mey(t)-~ ("to change, to exchange"). Cognate with English ~miss~, Old English ~missan~, Old Norse ~missa~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies the sense of lacking, of being without.

~Vermissen~ means "to miss (someone or something absent)" — to feel the lack of a person, an object, or a quality. This is the emotional missing: ~Ich vermisse dich~ ("I miss you"), ~Wir vermissen ihn sehr~ ("We miss him greatly"). It should not be confused with ~verpassen~ ("to miss" in the sense of failing to catch a train or opportunity). The Proto-Germanic root *~missō~ ("wrongly, amiss") reveals the original sense: to miss is to err, to go wrong, to fail to hit the mark. This same root produced English ~miss~ in all its senses (missing a target, missing a person), ~mistake~ (a miss-taking), and ~amiss~ ("wrongly"). The emotional sense — the ache of absence — is a medieval semantic development, extending the idea of "lacking" from a factual observation to a felt experience.
en.vermitteln:
From MHG ~vermitteln~. By surface analysis, ~ver-~ + ~Mittel~ + ~-n~:

- ~Mittel~: From MHG ~mittel~, from OHG ~mittil~, from Proto-Germanic *~midlą~ ("middle"), a derivative of *~midjaz~ ("middle"), from PIE *~médʰyos~ ("middle"). Cognate with Dutch ~middel~, English ~middle~, Latin ~medius~ ("middle"), Sanskrit ~madhya~ ("middle").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a completive or channeling sense — mediating something all the way through to resolution.

The chain ~médʰyos~ → ~midlą~ → ~mittil~ → ~Mittel~ → ~vermitteln~ traces the concept of "the middle" becoming "the means" (the thing in the middle position between two parties) and then "to mediate" (to act as that middle thing). The PIE root *~médʰyos~ is extraordinarily productive: it yields Latin ~medius~ (→ English ~medium~, ~mediation~, ~mediocre~), Greek ~mésos~ ("middle"), Sanskrit ~madhya~, and — through Proto-Germanic — English ~mid~, ~middle~, and ~midst~. German ~vermitteln~ is thus the verbalized form of this ancient spatial concept, applied to the social act of standing between two parties. Cognate with Dutch ~bemiddelen~, English ~mediate~ (via Latin, as a parallel form).
en.vermuten:
~ver-~ + ~Mut~/~muten~. From Middle Low German ~vormōden~, composed of ~vor-~ ("for") + ~mōden~ ("to want, ask for"), synchronically analyzable as ~ver-~ + ~muten~, from ~Mut~ + ~-en~:

- ~Mut~: From MHG ~muot~, from OHG ~muot~, from Proto-West Germanic *~mōdd~, from Proto-Germanic *~mōdaz~ ("mind, sense, courage, anger"), from PIE *~mō-tó-s~, a lengthened-grade nominal formation from *~meh₁-~ ("to endeavor; will, temper"), which may be a semantic extension of *~meh₁-~ in its more basic sense "to measure." Cognate with English ~mood~, Dutch ~moed~, and possibly Latin ~mōs~ ("will, humor, custom").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix here conveys an attempt or approximation — a mental reaching-forward.

~Vermuten~ means "to assume, suppose, suspect" — literally something like "to have a presentiment about, to pre-sense." The semantic path from ~Mut~ ("courage, mood, mind") to ~vermuten~ ("to suppose") runs through the older meaning of ~Mut~ as "mind, inner state": to ~ver-muten~ was to cast one's mind forward toward something not yet certain. The English cognate ~mood~ preserves the psychological sense but has shed the intellectual one, while German split the word's inheritance: ~Mut~ narrowed to "courage" in most modern contexts, while ~vermuten~ kept the cognitive dimension alive. A false friend lurks nearby: English ~mood~ shares the same Proto-Germanic ancestor as ~Mut~, but ~vermuten~ has nothing to do with "being moody."
en.vermögen:
From Middle High German ~vermügen~ or ~vermugen~, from Old High German ~firmugan~ or ~firmagan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~framagan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~mögen~:

- ~mögen~: From MHG ~mugen~, from OHG ~mugan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~magan~, from Proto-Germanic *~maganą~, from PIE *~megʰ-~ ("to be able, to have power"). Cognate with English ~may~, Dutch ~mogen~, Icelandic ~mega~, and Gothic ~magan~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix intensifies the base verb, adding a sense of thoroughgoing ability.

The PIE root *~megʰ-~ is the source of English ~may~, ~might~, and ~machine~ (via Greek ~mēkhanē~, "contrivance," from ~mekhos~, "means"). In German, ~vermögen~ means "to be capable of" and also functions as a noun meaning "fortune, wealth, assets," reflecting the idea that one's ability is one's true wealth. The OHG forms show both ~a~-grade (~firmagan~) and ~u~-grade (~firmugan~), reflecting the ablaut patterns of this ancient preterite-present verb. English ~may~ and German ~mögen~ are thus siblings, both descending from the same Proto-Germanic modal verb, though English lost the ~ver-~ compound entirely.
en.vernichten:
From MHG ~vernihten~, from ~nihten~, from ~niht~ ("nothing"). Surface analysis: ~ver-~ + ~nicht~ + ~-en~:

- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This inseparable prefix adds a sense of completion and thoroughness -- here transforming "nothing" into the act of making nothing.

~Vernichten~ means "to annihilate, to destroy utterly, to exterminate" -- literally "to make into nothing." It is one of the most absolute verbs in the German language, admitting no degrees: what is ~vernichtet~ is gone completely. The word's etymology mirrors that of English ~annihilate~, which derives from Latin ~ad-~ + ~nihil~ ("nothing") -- both languages independently built "destroy" from "nothing," but through their own linguistic materials. The derived adjective ~vernichtend~ ("devastating, crushing") is common in both military and figurative contexts: ~eine vernichtende Niederlage~ ("a crushing defeat"). The noun ~Vernichtung~ ("annihilation, destruction") carries heavy historical weight in German.
en.verpassen:
~ver-~ + ~passen~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~passen~:

- ~passen~: Borrowed from French ~passer~ ("to pass"), via Dutch ~passen~, ultimately from Vulgar Latin *~passāre~, from Latin ~passus~ ("step").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). The prefix adds a sense of missing, passing beyond, or failing.

~Verpassen~ means "to miss (an opportunity, a bus, a deadline)" — one of the most common verbs of everyday frustration. The prefix ~ver-~ here carries its sense of "going past, going wrong": to ~verpassen~ a train is to let it ~pass beyond~ you. The root ~passen~ itself has a layered borrowing history, entering German from French via Dutch during the medieval trade networks of the Low Countries. Latin ~passus~ ("step") comes from ~pandō~ ("to spread, extend"), from PIE *~peth₂-~ ("to spread out"). A second, colloquial meaning of ~verpassen~ is "to give someone something unpleasant" — ~jemandem eine Ohrfeige verpassen~ ("to give someone a slap") — where the ~ver-~ prefix shifts from "missing" to "inflicting." Cognate with Dutch ~verpassen~.
en.verpflichten:
From Middle High German ~verphlichten~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~Pflicht~ + ~-en~:

- ~Pflicht~: From OHG ~pfliht~, from Proto-West Germanic *~plihti~, related to ~pflegen~ ("to care for, tend"), from MHG ~phlëgen~, from OHG ~plëgan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~plehan~. The noun ~Pflicht~ ("duty, obligation") is built on the same root as ~pflegen~ — duty as the care one owes. Cognate with English ~plight~ (in the sense of a sworn obligation, as in "to plight one's troth"), Dutch ~plicht~, Danish ~pligt~, Yiddish ~פֿליכט~ (~flikht~).
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a completive or binding sense — bringing someone fully under an obligation.

English ~plight~ in the phrase "to plight one's troth" (to pledge faithfulness) is the same word as ~Pflicht~: both descend from Proto-West Germanic *~plihti~ ("care, responsibility, duty"). The modern English ~plight~ in the sense of "a bad situation" is a different word (from Old French ~pleit~, "condition") — a false friend within English itself. ~Verpflichten~ binds someone to a duty completely, committing them through the totality of their care. Cognate with Dutch ~verplichten~.
en.verraten:
From Middle High German ~verrāten~, from Old High German ~firrātan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frarādan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~raten~:

- ~raten~: From MHG ~rāten~, from OHG ~rātan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~rādan~, from Proto-Germanic *~rēdaną~, from PIE *~Hreh₁dʰ-~ ("to arrange, to consider").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries a sense of wrongdoing, misdirection, or reversal — turning counsel into betrayal.

The semantic shift in ~verraten~ is dramatic: ~raten~ means "to advise, to guess," but ~verraten~ means "to betray, to give away (a secret)." The ~ver-~ prefix twists counsel into treachery — advice given to the wrong party becomes betrayal. This negative transformation by ~ver-~ is paralleled in English ~bewray~ (archaic "to betray," from ~be-~ + ~wray~ "to accuse"). Cognate with Dutch ~verraden~. The root verb ~raten~ is cognate with English ~read~ (which originally meant "to advise, to interpret") and Danish ~råde~ ("to advise, to rule") — a remarkable case where "reading" and "advising" share an ancestor in the PIE concept of arranging or making sense of things.
en.verringern:
From ~ver-~ + archaic ~ringern~ ("to make lighter, soften, weaken"), a verbal formation from ~gering~ ("little, low"):

- ~gering~: From MHG ~geringe~, from OHG ~giringi~, ~ringi~ ("low, plain, simple, light, easy"), from Proto-Germanic *~ringwaz~ ("small, light, quick"), of uncertain origin, though possibly from PIE *~wrengʷʰ-~ ("to squeeze, wring"). Cognate with Yiddish ~גרינג~ (~gring~, "light, easy").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds completive force.

The intermediate verb ~ringern~ ("to lessen, make small") is now archaic, leaving ~verringern~ as the surviving compound. The adjective ~gering~ ("little, insignificant") is still very much alive, but its direct verbal derivative has been supplanted by the prefixed form. The possibly related PIE root *~wrengʷʰ-~ ("to squeeze, wring") would suggest an original image of something being squeezed small -- though this connection remains uncertain. The Greek cognate ~ρίμφα~ (~rhímpha~, "fast, quickly") preserves the "quick, light" sense of the Proto-Germanic ancestor rather than the "small" sense that prevailed in German.
en.versammeln:
From ~ver-~ + ~sammeln~. Cognate with Dutch ~verzamelen~. Compound of:

- ~sammeln~: From MHG ~sameln~, ~samlen~, probably by dissimilation from ~samenen~, ultimately from Proto-Germanic *~samnōną~ ("to gather, collect"). Alternatively inherited from OHG *~samalōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~samalōn~ ("to bring together, assemble").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies the base verb, giving the sense of gathering completely, assembling as a whole.

The Proto-Germanic root *~samnōną~ is also the source of English ~same~ (things gathered together are alike) and ~some~ (a gathered portion). The ~Ver-sammlung~ ("assembly") has been a key word in German political life since the medieval ~Reichsversammlung~ (imperial assembly). Dutch ~verzamelen~ ("to collect") is an exact cognate, while English ~assemble~ took a parallel path through Old French from Latin ~assimulāre~.
en.verschaffen:
From ~ver-~ + ~schaffen~. Compound of:

- ~schaffen~ (weak verb, "to manage, procure"): From MHG ~schaffen~ (strong verb), from OHG ~scafan~, ~scaffen~, a byform of ~skephen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skappjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~skapjaną~, of uncertain PIE origin — possibly related to *~skabaną~ ("to shave, scrape") via a semantic shift from "to scrape, hollow out" to "to form, shape." The strong verb form ~skephen~ survives in the archaic variant ~schöpfen~ ("to create"). Cognate with Dutch ~scheppen~, English ~shape~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fra-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a sense of completion or procurement — bringing something about fully.

~verschaffen~ means "to procure, obtain, provide" (~sich Zugang verschaffen~ = "to gain access," ~jemandem Arbeit verschaffen~ = "to find someone work"). It uses the weak conjugation of ~schaffen~ (~schaffte~, ~geschafft~, meaning "to manage, accomplish") rather than the strong conjugation (~schuf~, ~geschaffen~, meaning "to create"). This strong/weak split in ~schaffen~ is one of the most interesting in German: ~Gott schuf die Welt~ ("God created the world") versus ~Ich habe es geschafft~ ("I managed it"). ~verschaffen~ always takes the weak form, focusing on the practical act of procurement rather than divine creation. The etymological connection to English ~shape~ — both from Proto-Germanic *~skapjaną~ — is obscured by centuries of sound change but the semantic parallel of "forming, making" is still visible.
en.verschenken:
From ~ver-~ + ~schenken~. Compound of:

- ~schenken~: From MHG ~schenken~, from OHG ~scenken~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skankijan~ ("to pour, serve"), from Proto-Germanic *~skankijaną~, a causative from *~skankaz~ ("askew, crooked"), from PIE *~(s)keng-~ ("to limp, be crooked").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of giving away completely, parting with something irrevocably.

The semantic journey from "crooked" to "give as a gift" is one of the most entertaining in German etymology: the original meaning was "to pour drink" (tilting the vessel at an angle), then "to serve drink at a tavern," then "to give freely" (because the innkeeper sometimes poured one on the house). German ~Geschenk~ ("gift") and ~einschenken~ ("to pour a drink") preserve both ends of this journey. English ~skink~ (an archaic word for "to pour drink") is a distant cousin through the same Germanic root.
en.verschicken:
From ~ver-~ + ~schicken~. Compound of:

- ~schicken~: From MHG ~schicken~ ("to arrange, order, put in place"), from Proto-West Germanic *~skikkijan~ ("to arrange, put in order"), related to Proto-Germanic *~skikkijaną~. The original meaning was "to arrange, put in order," and the modern sense "to send" developed from the idea of dispatching something to its proper place.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of sending away to a distance.

The semantic shift from "arrange" to "send" happened in the late medieval period, when arranging goods for transport naturally came to mean dispatching them. The related noun ~Schicksal~ ("fate, destiny") preserves the older sense of "arrangement" -- fate as what has been arranged for you. English ~ship~ (as in "to ship goods") underwent a strikingly similar shift from a vessel to the act of sending. Cognate with Dutch ~beschikken~ ("to arrange, dispose").
en.verschieben:
From Middle High German ~verschieben~, from Old High German ~firskioban~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~schieben~:

- ~schieben~: From MHG ~schieben~, from OHG ~skioban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skeuban~, from Proto-Germanic *~skeubaną~, from PIE *~skewbʰ-~ ("to push, to shove").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix conveys completion or transformation of the base action — pushing something into a different position, whether spatially or temporally.

~Verschieben~ means both "to move, to shift (physically)" and "to postpone, to defer" — a beautiful case where spatial displacement became temporal displacement. You can ~einen Schrank verschieben~ ("shift a wardrobe") or ~einen Termin verschieben~ ("postpone an appointment"), and the underlying metaphor is identical: pushing something to a different place. Cognate with Dutch ~verschuiven~. The root verb ~schieben~ is cognate with English ~shove~, Low German ~schuven~, and Danish ~skubbe~ — all from the same PIE root describing forceful pushing.
en.verschließen:
From ~ver-~ + ~schließen~. Compound of:

- ~schließen~: From MHG ~slieȝen~, from OHG ~sliozan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sleutan~, from Proto-Germanic *~slūtaną~, *~sleutaną~ ("to lock, shut"). The original sense was specifically "to lock with a bolt or bar."
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of completeness -- locking something up tight.

The Proto-Germanic root gave Dutch ~sluiten~ ("to close"), while English ~slot~ (originally "a bar to lock a door") preserves the same source with a narrowed meaning. German ~Schlüssel~ ("key") is a derivative, literally "the thing that locks." The ~ver-~ prefix in ~verschließen~ adds the nuance of sealing away, locking up completely -- one cannot simply ~schließen~ a secret, but one can ~verschließen~ it. The past participle ~verschlossen~ has also come to describe a person's character: reserved, closed-off.
en.verschwinden:
From Middle High German ~verswinden~, from Old High German ~firswintan~, ~farswindan~. Equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~schwinden~:

- ~schwinden~: From MHG ~swinden~, from OHG ~swintan~, from Proto-Germanic *~swindaną~. A class III strong verb. The simplex ~schwinden~ still exists in literary German with the meaning "to dwindle, wane, fade." Cognate with Dutch ~zwinden~ ("to disappear"), Danish ~svinde~, Swedish ~svinna~, Norwegian ~svinne~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds completive and directional force — dwindling away entirely, disappearing to completion.

The most surprising cognate is English ~swindle~. The English verb is a back-formation from ~swindler~, which was borrowed directly from German ~Schwindler~ — a person who causes things (including money) to vanish, from ~schwindeln~ ("to be dizzy, to swindle"). So English ~swindle~ traces back through German to the same Proto-Germanic root *~swindaną~ that underlies ~verschwinden~. To make something disappear and to defraud someone have always been close neighbors. Cognate with Dutch ~verzwinden~, English ~swindle~ (via German ~Schwindler~).
en.versehen:
From Middle High German ~versehen~, from Old High German ~firsehan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frasehwan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~sehen~:

- ~sehen~: From MHG ~sehen~, from OHG ~sehan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwaną~, from PIE *~sekʷ-~ ("to see, to perceive").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries multiple semantic loads depending on the construction: completion (to provide fully), misdirection (to see wrongly), and anticipation (to foresee).

~Versehen~ splits into fascinatingly divergent meanings all rooted in altered seeing. As a transitive verb, ~mit etwas versehen~ means "to provide with, to equip with" — to see to it that something is furnished. As a reflexive ~sich versehen~, it means "to make a mistake" — to see wrongly, to misjudge. The archaic reflexive ~sich eines Besseren versehen~ meant "to expect better" — to foresee. The noun ~Versehen~ ("oversight, mistake") crystallizes the negative sense: a failure of seeing. Cognate with Old English ~forsēon~ ("to overlook, to despise") and Dutch ~verzien~ — both sharing the "wrongly seen" semantics. The Old English cognate's meaning of "to despise" (literally "to look past someone") shows how the ~ver-~ prefix could push "seeing" toward contemptuous disregard.
en.versenden:
From MHG ~versenden~, from OHG ~firsenten~, equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~senden~:

- ~senden~: From MHG ~senden~, from OHG ~senten~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sandijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sandijaną~, a causative of *~sinþaną~ ("to go, to travel"), from PIE *~sent-~ ("to go"). Cognate with English ~send~, Dutch ~zenden~, Danish ~sende~, Gothic ~sandjan~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix adds a sense of sending away, dispatching completely.

The literal sense is "to send away" — to dispatch, to ship. ~Versenden~ means "to send out, to ship, to dispatch" and is the standard verb for mailing packages, shipping goods, or distributing mailings. It is more formal and comprehensive than simple ~senden~ ("to send"), implying distribution to multiple recipients or dispatch through a logistics system. The noun ~Versand~ ("dispatch, shipping") is ubiquitous in e-commerce. The PIE root *~sent-~ ("to go") underwent a causative transformation in Proto-Germanic: *~sandijaną~ means literally "to cause to go" — a beautifully transparent etymology for the act of sending. The same root appears in Old Irish ~sét~ ("path, way"), Armenian ~əntʿanac~ ("way"), and Latin ~sentīre~ ("to feel" — originally perhaps "to go mentally toward"), whence English ~sense~, ~sentence~, and ~sentiment~. Cognate with Dutch ~verzenden~ ("to send, to ship").
en.versetzen:
From Middle High German ~versetzen~, from Old High German ~firsezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~frasattjan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, a causative derived from Proto-Germanic *~sitjaną~ ("to sit"). Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~ ("away, forth"), from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix carries the sense of displacement -- setting something elsewhere.

Cognate with Dutch ~verzetten~ ("to move, shift") and archaic English ~forset~. Like ~verlegen~, ~versetzen~ is richly polysemous: to move or displace (~einen Baum versetzen~), to transfer (~nach Karlsruhe versetzt werden~), to promote to the next school grade (~er wird versetzt~), to put into a state (~in Angst versetzen~ -- "to frighten"), to pawn (~das Silberbesteck versetzen~), to deal a blow (~einen Klaps versetzen~), and colloquially to stand someone up (~er hat mich versetzt~). The common thread is displacement -- setting something or someone into a different position, state, or situation. In literary German, ~versetzte er~ was once a standard speech tag meaning "he replied, retorted," now largely archaic. The proverbial ~der Glaube kann Berge versetzen~ ("faith can move mountains") preserves the physical-displacement sense at its most dramatic.
en.versichern:
From Middle High German ~versichern~, equivalent to ~ver-~ + ~sichern~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~sichern~:

- ~sichern~: From ~sicher~ + ~-n~, where ~sicher~ descends from MHG ~siher~, from OHG ~sihur~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sikur~ ("secure, sure"), borrowed from Latin ~sēcūrus~ ("without care, carefree"), from ~sē-~ ("without") + ~cūra~ ("care, concern").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix adds a sense of completion or thoroughness.

The insurance sense of ~versichern~, which emerged in the 17th century, is a calque (loan translation) of Italian ~assicurare~ ("to insure"), itself built from ~a-~ + ~sicuro~ ("secure"). This means German and Italian independently constructed the same metaphor from the same Latin root: "to make secure." English ~assure~ and ~insure~ followed the same pattern through French. The dual meaning of ~versichern~ elegantly mirrors this history: "to assure" (to make someone feel secure in a belief) and "to insure" (to make someone financially secure against loss). The noun ~Versicherung~ ("insurance") is one of the most common German words in daily life. The ultimate Latin root ~cūra~ ("care") also produced English ~cure~, ~curious~, ~accurate~, and ~secure~, all revolving around the concept of care and its absence.
en.versorgen:
From ~ver-~ + ~sorgen~:

- ~sorgen~: From MHG ~sorgen~, from OHG ~sorgēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~surgāną~, from *~surgō~ ("worry, sorrow, care"), from PIE *~swergʰ-~ ("to watch over, worry; be ill, suffer"). The semantic duality of watchful care and anxious suffering is present in the root itself. Cognate with Dutch ~zorgen~, English ~sorrow~, Danish ~sørge~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix adds a thoroughgoing, providing sense — caring for something completely, ensuring that needs are fully met.

~Versorgen~ takes the dual root of ~sorgen~ — care and concern — and resolves it firmly toward the positive pole: to provide for, to supply, to look after. While ~sorgen~ can still tip toward worry (~sich sorgen um~, "to worry about"), ~versorgen~ almost exclusively denotes active provision. A hospital ~versorgt~ patients; a government ~versorgt~ the population with electricity; parents ~versorgen~ their children. The ~ver-~ prefix converts the act of caring into the act of ensuring that care is carried through to its object. Cognate with Dutch ~verzorgen~.
en.versprechen:
From Middle High German ~versprechen~ ("to guarantee"), from Old High German ~firsprehhan~ ("to defend verbally, to warrant"), from Proto-West Germanic *~frasprekan~. Analyzable as ~ver-~ + ~sprechen~:

- ~sprechen~: From MHG ~sprëchen~, from OHG ~sprëhhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sprekan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sprekaną~, probably from PIE *~spregʰ-~ ("to crackle, make noise"). Cognate with Dutch ~spreken~, English ~speak~.
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix originally gave a completive or forward-directed sense — speaking forth, speaking on behalf of.

~Versprechen~ carries two opposite meanings held in grammatical tension. When transitive (~ich verspreche dir~, "I promise you"), it means to commit — to speak something forth and bind yourself to it. When reflexive (~ich verspreche mich~, "I misspeak"), it means to err in speaking — a later transparent derivation exploiting the prefix's sense of deviation. The verb thus embodies the double nature of ~ver-~: forward completion (a promise) and errant deviation (a slip of the tongue) are both housed in a single form. The sense "to promise" is attested from Old High German; the reflexive "to misspeak" is a later development. Cognate with Low German ~vorspreken~.
en.verstecken:
From Middle High German ~verstecken~, from Old High German ~firstekken~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~stecken~:

- ~stecken~: From MHG ~stecken~, from OHG ~steckōn~, a geminated (consonant-doubled) derivative of OHG ~stehhan~ (whence modern ~stechen~, "to sting, prick, stab"). The semantic development from "to pierce, stick" to "to be fixed in place, to put into" runs through the idea of a pointed object driven into a surface and remaining there.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies and completes the action: to stick something away so thoroughly that it cannot be found.

~Verstecken~ means "to hide, to conceal." The reflexive ~sich verstecken~ ("to hide oneself") is the verb every German child knows from the game ~Verstecken~ ("hide and seek"). The related noun ~Versteck~ ("hiding place") drops the verbal suffix. Cognate with Dutch ~versteken~ ("to hide"). The underlying verb ~stecken~ preserves a fascinating grammatical fossil: transitively it has always been weak (~steckte~), but intransitively it once had strong past-tense forms (~stak~, Konjunktiv II ~stäke~). These strong forms, though still technically attested, have become archaic and unintelligible to many native speakers — a rare case of a strong-verb pattern dying within recorded history.
en.verstehen:
From Middle High German ~verstān~, ~virstēn~, ~vorstēn~, ~ferstān~, from Old High German ~firstān~, ~firstēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~frastāną~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~stehen~:

- ~stehen~: From MHG ~stān~, ~stēn~, from OHG ~stān~, ~stēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stān~, from Proto-Germanic *~stāną~, from PIE *~steh₂-~ ("to stand").
- ~ver-~: From MHG ~ver-~, from OHG ~fir-~, ~far-~, from Proto-Germanic *~fra-~, from PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This prefix often adds a sense of away, wrongly, or completion.

The semantic development from "to stand before, against" to "to understand" parallels English ~understand~ (literally "to stand among") and Latin ~comprehendere~ ("to grasp"). The notion is of positioning oneself in relation to something in order to grasp it — a spatial metaphor for cognition shared across the Germanic languages. Cognate with Bavarian ~versteh~, Dutch ~verstaan~, Middle Low German ~vorstān~. Compare also archaic English ~forstand~ ("to understand").
en.versterben:
From MHG ~versterben~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~sterben~:

- ~sterben~: From MHG ~stërben~, from OHG ~stërban~, from Proto-Germanic *~sterbaną~, from PIE *~sterbʰ-~ ("to be stiff, become stiff"). Cognate with Dutch ~sterven~, Low German ~starven~, Frisian ~stjerre~, and English ~starve~.
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This inseparable prefix adds a sense of completion and finality -- dying to the end, dying completely.

~Versterben~ is the formal, euphemistic, and respectful way to say "to die" in German -- roughly equivalent to English "to pass away." Where ~sterben~ is neutral and direct, ~versterben~ adds a veil of formality appropriate for obituaries, official announcements, and solemn contexts. The past participle ~verstorben~ functions as an adjective meaning "deceased" (~der verstorbene Professor~, "the late professor"). The English cognate ~starve~ underwent a dramatic semantic narrowing: while German ~sterben~ retained the general meaning "to die," English ~starve~ specialized to mean "to die of hunger" -- a shift that occurred in Middle English. The PIE root *~sterbʰ-~ ("to be stiff, become stiff") connects death to rigor mortis -- a grimly literal etymology.
en.verstoßen:
From MHG ~verstōzen~, from OHG ~firstōzan~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~stoßen~:

- ~stoßen~: From MHG ~stōzen~, from OHG ~stōzan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stautan~, from Proto-Germanic *~stautaną~, from PIE *~(s)tewd-~ ("to push, hit"). Cognate with Gothic ~stautan~, Old Norse ~stauta~, Dutch ~stoten~ ("to push, bump"), and dialectal English ~stot~ ("to bounce"). Non-Germanic cognates include Latin ~tundō~ ("I beat") and Sanskrit ~tudáti~ ("to strike, goad").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). This inseparable prefix adds a sense of completion, excess, or wrongful action -- here intensifying the thrust into an act of expulsion.

~Verstoßen~ means "to expel, to cast out, to violate (a law or rule)" -- the ~ver-~ prefix transforms a physical push into an act of permanent exclusion. ~Ein verstoßenes Kind~ ("a cast-out child") carries immense emotional weight. The legal sense ~gegen ein Gesetz verstoßen~ ("to violate a law") shifts the metaphor: one pushes through or against a boundary that should hold firm. The PIE root *~(s)tewd-~ connects ~stoßen~ to Latin ~tundō~ ("I beat, I pound"), whence English ~contusion~ -- a bruise being literally a place where flesh has been struck.
en.verstärken:
Compound of ~ver-~ + ~stärken~:

- ~stärken~: From MHG ~sterken~, from OHG ~sterken~, a denominative verb from ~stark~ ("strong"), from Proto-West Germanic *~stark~, from Proto-Germanic *~starkuz~, from PIE *~ster-~ / *~sterg-~ ("stiff, rigid, strong").
- ~ver-~: From PIE *~per~ ("before, in front, first"). Here the prefix intensifies the base verb, carrying the sense of thorough or complete transformation.

~Verstärken~ means "to reinforce, to amplify, to strengthen" — the ~ver-~ prefix pushes ~stärken~ ("to strengthen") toward completion or intensification. You ~verstärkst~ a wall (reinforce it), a signal (amplify it), a team (bolster it), or a tendency (intensify it). The derived noun ~Verstärker~ ("amplifier") is the standard term in electronics and music — a guitar ~Verstärker~ is an amp. ~Verstärkung~ means "reinforcement" in both military and general senses. The root adjective ~stark~ is cognate with English ~stark~ (which shifted from "strong" to "utter, bare, harsh" — a ~stark~ contrast originally meant a ~strong~ contrast) and Dutch ~sterk~ ("strong"). The PIE root *~ster-~ / *~sterg-~ ("stiff, rigid") also connects to English ~stare~ (with rigid eyes) and ~stork~ (the stiff-legged bird), Latin ~re-stringere~ ("to restrict" — whence ~strict~), and Greek ~στερεός~ ("solid, firm" — whence ~stereo~).
en.versuchen:
From ~ver-~ + ~suchen~. Compound of ~ver-~ + ~suchen~:
