English ~wont~ (as in "as was his wont") descends from Old English ~ġewunian~ ("to be accustomed to, dwell"), the same verb as Proto-West Germanic *~wunēn~: to be ~wont~ is to be "dwelt," habituated into a place or practice. English ~win~ (from the same *~wenh₁-~ root via *~winnaną~) preserves the striving sense — to win was originally to labor, struggle toward the desired goal, not merely to come first. The cluster of ~wohnen~, ~Wonne~, ~wünschen~, ~gewinnen~, and English ~wont~ and ~win~ all radiate from the same ancient notion: the thing you love enough to reach for, dwell in, or be shaped by. Cognate with Dutch ~wonen~, Afrikaans ~woon~, English ~wont~, Gothic ~wunan~.
en.wollen: From Middle High German ~wollen~, ~wöllen~, ~wullen~, ~wellen~, from Old High German ~wollen~, ~wellen~, from a blend of Proto-West Germanic *~willjan~ and *~walljan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wiljaną~ and *~waljaną~, ultimately from PIE *~welh₁-~ ("to wish, choose"). A preterite-present modal verb. Cognate with Low German ~willen~, Dutch ~willen~, English ~will~, West Frisian ~wolle~, Danish ~ville~, Swedish ~vilja~. Related to ~wählen~ ("to choose"), from the same PIE root.
en.wundern:
From Middle High German ~wundern~, from Old High German ~wuntarōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~wundrōną~, derived from the noun *~wundrą~ ("wonder, miracle"), whence German ~Wunder~. The noun *~wundrą~ may derive from PIE *~wenh₁-~ ("to desire, wish for, strive after, win, love") via a form *~wn̥h₁-tro-m~, suggesting the original sense was something "wished for" or "longed for" -- hence marvellous.

The verb's reflexive usage, ~sich wundern~ ("to be surprised, to wonder"), is the most common in modern German. The non-reflexive transitive ~das wundert mich~ ("that surprises me") places the wondering person as the object, not the subject -- a grammatical pattern shared by several German verbs of emotion (~freuen~, ~ärgern~, ~langweilen~). English ~wonder~ is not a direct cognate; it derives from Old English ~wundrian~, from Proto-Germanic *~wundrōną~, making it in fact a sibling -- both descend from the same Proto-Germanic verb, though their respective noun bases (~Wunder~ vs. ~wonder~) diverged phonetically through the High German consonant shift.
en.wählen: From Middle High German ~weln~, from ~welen~, ~wellen~, from Old High German ~wellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~walljan~, from Proto-Germanic *~waljaną~, ultimately from PIE *~welh₁-~ ("to wish, choose"). The modern umlaut vowel ~ä~ developed from the influence of the second- and third-person singular forms in Middle High German. As the ~wollen~ entry notes, ~wählen~ and ~wollen~ share the same PIE root — an elegant reminder that willing and choosing were once a single concept in Proto-Indo-European. Cognate with English ~well~ (in the archaic sense "to wish well"), Dutch ~willen~ (from the same root via a parallel path).
en.währen:
From Middle High German ~wëren~, ~wërn~, from Old High German ~wërēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~weʀēn~, related to OHG ~wesen~ ("to be, exist"). The deeper connection runs through Proto-Germanic *~wēraz~ ("true"), from PIE *~weh₁ros~ ("true"), from *~weh₁-~ ("true, trustworthy").

The semantic thread connecting "to last, endure" with "true" is the idea that what is true is what stands firm and persists. This same root gave German ~wahr~ ("true"), ~Wahrheit~ ("truth"), and the now-archaic ~Wesen~ ("being, essence"). The famous German proverb ~Was lange währt, wird endlich gut~ ("What lasts long turns out good in the end") captures the verb's core meaning. Latin ~vērus~ ("true"), Irish ~fíor~ ("true"), and Slavic *~vě̀ra~ ("faith") are all cognates from the same PIE root, showing that the ancient link between duration and truth runs across the entire Indo-European family.
en.wünschen: From Middle High German ~wünschen~, ~wunschen~, from Old High German ~wunsken~ ("to choose, wish, desire"), from Proto-West Germanic *~wunskijan~, from Proto-Germanic *~wunskijaną~, derived from *~wunskaz~ ("wish, desire"), from PIE *~wenh₁-~ ("to wish, love"). The same PIE root underlies German ~Wonne~ ("bliss, rapture") and English ~win~ — both preserving the old sense of "desired thing, pleasure." The connection between wishing and winning reflects a Proto-Germanic world in which what one wished for was what one strove to obtain. Cognate with Dutch ~wensen~, English ~wish~, Danish ~ønske~, and — across a vast linguistic distance — Sanskrit ~vāṃchati~ ("to wish, desire").
en.würdigen:
From ~würdig~ ("worthy") + ~-en~. ~Würdig~ derives from ~Würde~ ("dignity, worth") + ~-ig~. ~Würde~ descends from MHG ~wirde~, from OHG ~wirdī~, ultimately related to Proto-Germanic *~werþaz~ ("valuable, worthy"), from PIE *~wert-~ ("to turn"). Cognate with English ~worth~ and ~worthy~.

~Würdigen~ means "to honor, to appreciate, to acknowledge the worth of" -- and, in a less common construction, ~keines Blickes würdigen~ ("to not deem worthy of a single glance"), which expresses withering contempt. The PIE root *~wert-~ ("to turn") connects ~Würde~ to Latin ~vertere~ ("to turn"), whence English ~convert~, ~divert~, and ~versus~; the Germanic branch produced ~werden~ ("to become," literally "to turn into"), ~-wärts~ ("-ward," as in ~vorwärts~), and English ~worth~. The development from "turning" to "exchanging" to "having value" to "being worthy" to "dignity" traces a beautiful arc across five millennia of semantic evolution.
en.zahlen:
From Middle High German ~zaln~, ~zalen~, from Old High German ~zalōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~talōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~talōną~ ("to tell, recount; to count, reckon"). Cognate with Bavarian ~zoin~, Dutch ~talen~, Swedish ~tala~, English ~tale~.

~Zahlen~ ("to pay") and ~zählen~ ("to count") are siblings from the same Proto-Germanic root *~talōną~. This root carried both counting and narrating from the beginning — a duality visible in English ~tell~ (to count: ~teller~ at a bank, ~telling beads~; to narrate: to tell a story) and ~tale~ (a story, but also archaic "a count, tally"). The semantic path from "to count" to "to pay" is natural: payment requires counting out the correct amount. In medieval German, ~zalen~ meant both to count and to pay, and the modern distinction between ~zahlen~ and ~zählen~ reflects the same productive split that gave English ~tell~ and ~tale~ their divergent senses. Cognate with Dutch ~betalen~ (via the same root), English ~tale~ and ~tell~, Danish ~tale~ ("to speak"), Swedish ~tala~ ("to speak").
en.zaubern:
From Middle High German ~zoubern~, from Old High German ~zouberōn~, ~zoubarōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taubrōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taubrōną~, derived from *~taubrą~ ("magic, sorcery"). Cognate with English ~tiver~ (a red pigment, perhaps originally used in magical rituals) and Dutch ~toveren~ ("to do magic"), Old Norse ~taufr~ ("magic, sorcery").

The origin of Proto-Germanic *~taubrą~ is uncertain, but it may be linked to *~tawjaną~ ("to make, prepare"), suggesting that magic was originally understood as a kind of crafting or making. The Gothic Bible translator Wulfila used ~taujan~ for "to do, make," and the idea of magic as fabrication -- making something appear from nothing -- fits this connection. The noun ~Zauber~ ("magic, spell") and the fairy-tale figure ~Zauberer~ ("wizard, sorcerer") both stem from this root. Interestingly, the old Germanic word for magic survived where many other pagan-era terms did not, resisting replacement by Latin or Christian vocabulary.
en.zeichnen: Derived from the noun ~Zeichen~ ("sign, symbol"), from Middle High German ~zeichen~, from Old High German ~zeihhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taikn~, from Proto-Germanic *~taikną~, *~taiknaz~ ("sign"), from Proto-Indo-European *~deyḱ-~ ("to show, point"). Cognate with Dutch ~teken~, English ~token~, Danish ~tegn~, Swedish ~tecken~. The PIE root *~deyḱ-~ is extraordinarily productive: it also underlies German ~zeigen~ ("to show"), Latin ~dīcere~ ("to say," giving English ~dictate~, ~judge~, ~index~), Greek ~deiknynai~ ("to show"), and Sanskrit ~diś-~ ("direction"). To draw (~zeichnen~) is etymologically to make a sign — and the sign itself descends from the same ancestral root as the act of pointing and showing. English ~token~ and German ~Zeichen~ are cognates across a millennium of linguistic divergence: both mean "a sign left behind to be read."
en.zeigen: From Middle High German ~zeigen~, from Old High German ~zeigōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~taigōn~, possibly from Proto-Germanic *~taikōną~ (an iterative formation). An intensive derivative of Old High German ~zīhan~ ("to accuse"), whence modern ~zeihen~ ("to blame"). Ultimately from PIE *~deyḱ-~ ("to point out, show"), whence also ~Zeichen~ ("sign") and English ~teach~. In Modern German, ~zeigen~ absorbed the synonymous but entirely unrelated Middle High German ~zöugen~ (cognate with Gothic ~ataugjan~).
en.zerstören:
From Middle High German ~zerstœren~, equivalent to ~zer-~ + ~stören~:

- ~stören~: From MHG ~stören~, from OHG ~stōren~, from Proto-West Germanic *~staurijan~. Cognate with English ~stir~.
- ~zer-~: From PIE *~dwís~ ("twice, doubly, in two"). The prefix expresses shattering, breaking apart, or total destruction.

~Zerstören~ means "to destroy" — etymologically, "to disturb to pieces." The ~zer-~ prefix is one of German's most violent: it takes the relatively mild ~stören~ ("to disturb") and escalates it to total annihilation. The same prefix appears in ~zerbrechen~ ("to shatter"), ~zerreißen~ ("to tear apart"), ~zerfallen~ ("to disintegrate"), and ~zerschlagen~ ("to smash") — in each case converting a moderate action into a catastrophic one. The derived noun ~Zerstörung~ ("destruction") and the agent noun ~Zerstörer~ ("destroyer" — also the German word for the naval warship class) are transparently formed. The semantic distance between ~stören~ ("to disturb") and ~zerstören~ ("to destroy") — the difference between an annoying noise and total devastation — is carried entirely by the prefix ~zer-~, making it one of the most powerful morphological intensifiers in the German language. Cognate with Dutch ~verstoren~ (which uses ~ver-~ instead of ~zer-~).
en.zeugen:
From Middle High German ~ziugen~, ~geziugen~, from Old High German ~giziugon~, from ~ziug~ ("equipment, gear"), ultimately from the root of ~ziehen~ ("to pull, draw, move"). The underlying noun ~Zeug~ comes from MHG ~ziuc~ ("stuff, gear"), from OHG ~giziug~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teug~, from Proto-Germanic *~teugą~, related to *~teuhaną~ ("to pull"), from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead").

The original meaning of ~zeugen~ was "to produce, bring forth through technical activity" -- literally to pull something into existence using tools (~Zeug~). From this developed two distinct modern senses: "to beget, procreate" (producing a child) and "to testify, bear witness" (producing evidence). The noun ~Zeuge~ ("witness") comes from the verb, as does ~Zeugnis~ ("testimony, certificate"). English ~tug~ descends from the same PIE root *~dewk-~. Cognate with Dutch ~tuig~ ("equipment, gear"), Old Norse ~tygi~ ("gear"). The semantic range of ~zeugen~ -- from making things, to making children, to making a case in court -- is a remarkable study in conceptual extension.
en.ziehen: From Middle High German ~ziehen~, from Old High German ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhaną~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). A class II strong verb. Cognate with Low German ~tehn~, Gothic ~tiuhan~, Old English ~tēon~, Old Norse ~toga~, West Frisian ~tsjen~. Compare obsolete English ~tee~ ("to draw, lead, proceed"); also related to English ~tie~, from the same PIE root.
en.zielen:
From ~Ziel~ ("target, goal") + ~-en~. ~Ziel~ descends from MHG ~zil~, from OHG ~zil~, from Proto-West Germanic *~til~, from Proto-Germanic *~tilą~ ("aim, goal, fixed point"). Cognate with Old English ~til~ ("suitable, fitting"), whence English ~till~ (preposition: "up to a fixed point").

~Zielen~ means "to aim" — both physically (~auf ein Ziel zielen~, "to aim at a target") and figuratively (~auf etwas zielen~, "to be directed at something"). The verb is a relatively transparent denominal formation: to aim is to direct oneself toward a fixed point (~Ziel~). The Proto-Germanic *~tilą~ connects to a semantic field of fitness and suitability rather than spatial targets — Old English ~til~ meant "suitable, good, apt," and the preposition ~till~ ("up to") preserves the sense of reaching a fixed endpoint. German ~Ziel~ narrowed to the spatial and aspirational senses: the place you aim for, the goal you strive toward. The noun ~Zielscheibe~ ("target disc") makes the archery metaphor explicit, while ~Ziellinie~ ("finish line") extends it to racing.
en.zitieren:
Borrowed from Latin ~citāre~ ("to summon, to call forth, to set in motion"), a frequentative of ~ciēre~ ("to move, stir"), from PIE *~keyh₂-~ ("to set in motion"). The Latin verb's primary sense was legal: to summon a person to appear before a court. The scholarly sense "to quote a text as evidence" developed as a metaphorical extension -- calling a written authority to testify, as it were.

German borrowed ~zitieren~ with the ~-ieren~ suffix typical of Latin and French loan verbs. It retains both the quotation sense ("to cite a passage") and, more formally, the summoning sense ("to call someone to appear"). English ~cite~ arrived via Old French from the same Latin source, while ~excite~ and ~incite~ are formed from the same root with different prefixes.
en.zubereiten:
Compound of ~zu-~ + ~bereiten~:

- ~bereiten~: From MHG ~bereiten~, from OHG ~bireiten~, derived from the adjective ~bereit~ ("ready"), which descends from OHG ~bireiti~, ~reiti~, from Proto-West Germanic *~raidī~, from Proto-Germanic *~raidaz~ ("ready, arranged"), from PIE *~h₂reh₁dʰ-~ ("to put in order, arrange"). Cognate with English ~ready~, Dutch ~bereid~ ("prepared"), Gothic ~garaiþs~ ("arranged, ordered"), and Swedish ~redo~ ("ready").
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo~, from OHG ~zuo~, from Proto-Germanic *~tō~ ("to"), from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it means "to, toward" and often conveys purposeful completion.

The compound means "to prepare" — specifically food: ~eine Mahlzeit zubereiten~ ("to prepare a meal"), ~einen Cocktail zubereiten~ ("to mix a cocktail"). While ~vorbereiten~ means "to prepare" in a general sense (preparing for an exam, a trip), ~zubereiten~ is reserved almost exclusively for culinary preparation, making it one of the more semantically specialized ~zu-~ compounds. The underlying adjective ~bereit~ ("ready") connects German and English more closely than most speakers realize: English ~ready~ descends from the exact same Proto-Germanic root *~raidaz~. The Old English form ~ġerǣde~ still wore its Germanic prefix ~ge-~ openly, while German replaced it with ~be-~. Both prefixes carry a sense of collective completion, and both languages independently chose to prefix the same root to create their word for "prepared."
en.zugeben:
Compound of ~zu-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gëben~, from OHG ~gëban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebaną~, from PIE *~gʰebʰ-~ ("to give"). Cognate with English ~give~ (from Old Norse ~gefa~, displacing OE ~giefan~), Dutch ~geven~.
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo~, from OHG ~zuo~, from Proto-Germanic *~tō~ ("to"), from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys "to, toward" or "in addition."

~Zugeben~ means both "to admit, to confess" and "to add" (an ingredient). The confession sense is "to give to" the truth, to yield toward an admission. The culinary sense is more literal: giving something into the mixture. The duality mirrors the English verb ~concede~, from Latin ~concēdere~ ("to yield, to give way"), which likewise spans both "to admit a point" and "to grant, to hand over."
en.zugreifen:
Compound of ~zu-~ + ~greifen~:

- ~greifen~: From MHG ~grīfen~, from OHG ~grīfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grīpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grīpaną~ ("to grab, grasp"), from PIE *~gʰreyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). Cognate with English ~gripe~ and ~grip~, Dutch ~grijpen~, Gothic ~greipan~. The Lithuanian cognate ~griebti~ ("to seize") confirms the PIE pedigree.
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo~, from OHG ~zuo~, from Proto-Germanic *~tō~ ("to"), from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it adds a sense of reaching toward and seizing.

The literal sense is "to grab toward" — to reach out and take hold. ~Zugreifen~ means "to grab it, to help oneself" (as when food is offered: ~greifen Sie zu!~ — "help yourself!"), "to seize an opportunity," or "to take decisive action." The English cognate ~grip~ preserves the physical sense most transparently, while ~gripe~ has narrowed to mean "to complain" (originally "to clutch, to cause a gripping pain"). The noun ~Zugriff~ ("access, grasp") is ubiquitous in computing and law alike. The PIE root *~gʰreyb-~ also appears in Lithuanian ~graibýti~ ("to seize"), confirming that the sense of grasping has been stable for over five thousand years.
en.zukommen:
Compound of ~zu-~ + ~kommen~:

- ~kommen~: From MHG ~komen~, ~quemen~, from OHG ~queman~ (rarer ~cuman~, ~chuman~), from Proto-West Germanic *~kweman~, from Proto-Germanic *~kwemanan~, from PIE *~gwem-~ ("to come, to go"). Cognate with English ~come~, Dutch ~komen~, Danish ~komme~.
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo-~, from OHG ~zuo~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tō~, from Proto-Germanic *~tō~ ("to"), from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to"). This prefix adds a directional sense of motion toward something.

The initial ~qu-~ of Old High German ~queman~ (reflecting the original labio-velar *~gw-~) was gradually lost during the Middle High German period, yielding modern ~kommen~. This same PIE root *~gwem-~ also produced Latin ~venire~ ("to come"), showing how the labio-velar *~gw~ developed differently in ~Italic~ versus ~Germanic~ branches. The verb ~zukommen~ has two main modern senses: "to come toward someone" and "to be entitled to, to be due to" (~jemandem steht etwas zu~), the latter reflecting a metaphorical extension where something "comes to" a person by right.
en.zulassen:
From Middle High German ~zuolāȥen~, equivalent to ~zu-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lāȥen~, from OHG ~lāȥȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lētan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lētaną~, from PIE *~leh₁d-~ ("to let, to leave, to be weary").
- ~zu-~: From PIE *~doh₁~ ("to"). The prefix adds the sense of granting access or permitting something toward a goal.

~Zulassen~ means "to allow, to permit, to admit" — from allowing a possibility (~eine Ausnahme zulassen~, "to allow an exception") to admitting a person (~zum Studium zugelassen~, "admitted to study") to certifying a product (~ein zugelassenes Medikament~, "an approved medication"). The derived noun ~Zulassung~ is the standard word for official authorization — a vehicle registration (~Fahrzeugzulassung~), a professional license, or a university admission. The prefix ~zu-~ here implies opening a gate toward someone: what is ~zugelassen~ has been let through. Cognate with Dutch ~toelaten~.
en.zulegen:
From ~zu-~ + ~legen~. Compound of:

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjaną~, a causative of *~ligjaną~ ("to lie"), from PIE *~logʰéyeti~, a causative formation from *~legʰ-~ ("to lie down"). Cognate with English ~lay~, Danish ~laegge~.
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo-~, from OHG ~zuo-~, from Proto-Germanic *~tō~, from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it indicates direction toward, addition, or closure.

The literal meaning "to lay toward, add to" generates several figurative senses: ~sich zulegen~ can mean "to acquire, get oneself" (laying something toward one's own pile), or "to pick up speed" (~zulegen~ in the sense of adding pace). The causative relationship between ~legen~ ("to lay," transitive) and ~liegen~ ("to lie," intransitive) mirrors exactly the English pair ~lay~/~lie~, both descending from the same PIE causative formation. English ~lay~ comes from Old English ~lecgan~, from the same Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~.
en.zunehmen:
Compound of ~zu-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nëmen~, from OHG ~nëman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemaną~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due, to allot, to distribute"). A class IV strong verb. Cognate with English ~nim~ ("to take, filch"), Dutch ~nemen~, Danish ~nemme~ ("to learn, grasp"), and Swedish ~förnimma~ ("to perceive").
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo~, from OHG ~zuo~, from Proto-Germanic *~tō~ ("to"), from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys addition, increase, or closure.

The compound means "to increase, to grow, to gain weight" — the moon ~nimmt zu~ ("waxes"), a patient ~nimmt zu~ ("gains weight"), and traffic ~nimmt zu~ ("increases"). The image is of something "taking to itself," accumulating more. Its antonym ~abnehmen~ ("to decrease, to lose weight") uses the opposite prefix ~ab-~ ("away"). The PIE root *~nem-~ had the original sense of distributing or allotting — it also produced Greek ~nemein~ ("to distribute, to pasture"), whence ~Nemesis~ (the goddess who distributes what is due) and ~nemesis~ (one's deserved comeuppance). The semantic journey from "distributing fairly" (PIE) to "taking" (Germanic) to "increasing" (German compound) shows how a single root can travel through three thousand years of speakers who each nudged its meaning a little further from the original.
en.zuordnen:
From ~zu-~ + ~ordnen~. Compound of:

- ~ordnen~: From MHG ~ordenen~, from OHG ~ordinōn~, borrowed from Latin ~ōrdināre~ ("to put in order, arrange"), from ~ōrdō~ ("row, rank, order"). The Latin source also gave rise to German ~ordinieren~ (a learned doublet) and, via French, to English ~ordain~.
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo-~, from OHG ~zuo-~, from Proto-West Germanic *~tō~, from Proto-Germanic *~tō~, from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it conveys direction toward or assignment.

~zuordnen~ means "to assign, allocate, classify" — to place something ~toward~ its proper order or category. It is the standard verb for mapping, assigning, or attributing in both everyday and technical German (~einer Kategorie zuordnen~ = "to assign to a category"). The fact that ~ordnen~ is a Latin loanword naturalized so thoroughly that it takes native Germanic prefixes (~zu-~, ~an-~, ~ein-~, ~unter-~) illustrates how deeply Latin administrative vocabulary penetrated German during the medieval period. The noun ~Zuordnung~ ("assignment, mapping") is ubiquitous in mathematics and data science, where it denotes any mapping from one set to another.
en.zurückführen:
Compound of ~zurück-~ + ~führen~:

- ~führen~: From MHG ~vüeren~, from OHG ~fuoren~, a causative of ~fahren~ ("to travel, go"). The verb ~fahren~ descends from Proto-West Germanic *~faran~, from Proto-Germanic *~faraną~, from PIE *~per-~ ("to carry, bring forth"). Cognate with English ~fare~ and Dutch ~varen~.
- ~zurück-~: A compound prefix from ~zu~ + ~rück~, where ~zu~ derives from Proto-Germanic *~tō~, from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to"), and ~rück~ from ~Rücken~ ("back"), ultimately related to PIE *~(s)krewk-~ ("heap, hill"). Together they convey "back, returning."

As a causative, ~führen~ originally meant "to cause to go," i.e., "to lead," while ~fahren~ simply meant "to go, travel." This causative pair is one of the clearest in German. The compound ~zurückführen~ means "to lead back, to trace back, to attribute to," and the intellectual sense of tracing an idea or phenomenon back to its origin is perhaps the most common usage today: ~Das lässt sich auf X zurückführen~ ("That can be traced back to X"). English ~fare~ preserves the intransitive root: a bus ~fare~ is the cost of going. The PIE root *~per-~ also produced Greek ~poros~ ("passage"), English ~ford~ (a crossing), and ~ferry~ (to carry across water). The noun ~Führer~ ("leader") is simply the agent noun of ~führen~, though its historical associations have made the word nearly unusable in modern German outside of compound forms like ~Reiseführer~ ("travel guide") or ~Führerschein~ ("driver's license").
en.zurückgreifen:
Compound of ~zurück-~ + ~greifen~:

- ~greifen~: From MHG ~grīfen~, from OHG ~grīfan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~grīpan~, from Proto-Germanic *~grīpaną~ ("to grab, grasp"), from PIE *~gʰreyb-~ ("to grab, grasp"). Cognate with English ~grip~, ~gripe~, Dutch ~grijpen~.
- ~zurück-~: From PIE *~doh₁~ ("to") + *~(s)krewk~ ("heap, hill"); literally "to the back." The prefix means "back, returning."

The literal sense is "to grab back" — to reach back for something already known or available. ~Zurückgreifen~ is typically used figuratively: ~auf etwas zurückgreifen~ means "to fall back on something, to resort to, to draw upon" (a resource, an experience, a method). Unlike ~zugreifen~ (which means to seize something present), ~zurückgreifen~ implies reaching into the past or into reserves. The spatial metaphor is vivid: one turns around and grasps something behind oneself. There is no single English equivalent — "fall back on," "resort to," "draw upon," and "have recourse to" all approximate its meaning. The prefix ~zurück-~ is itself a compound (from ~zu~ + an old form related to ~Rücken~, "back"), making ~zurückgreifen~ a triple-layered word: "to-back-grasp."
en.zurückkehren:
Compound of ~zurück-~ ("back") + ~kehren~ ("to turn"):

- ~kehren~: From MHG ~kēren~ ("to turn"), from OHG ~kēren~, from Proto-Germanic *~kaizwijaną~ ("to turn, direct"), of uncertain PIE origin. Kroonen derives it from PIE *~geys-~ ("to turn, become"), comparing Hittite ~kīša(ri)~ ("to happen, occur, become"). Cognate with Danish ~køre~ ("to drive"), Swedish ~köra~ ("to drive"), Icelandic ~keyra~ ("to drive").
- ~zurück-~: From MHG ~ze rücke~ ("to the back"), from ~zu~ + ~Rücken~ ("back"). Ultimately from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to") + *~(s)krewk~ ("heap, hill; back").

There are two homonymous verbs ~kehren~ in German: one meaning "to sweep" (from Proto-Germanic *~karjaną~) and one meaning "to turn" (from *~kaizwijaną~). ~Zurückkehren~ uses the "turn" sense -- to turn back, to return. The Scandinavian cognates evolved differently: Danish ~køre~ and Swedish ~köra~ now mean "to drive (a vehicle)," preserving only the sense of directing motion. The base verb ~kehren~ in the "turn" sense is largely archaic in Germany proper (replaced by ~umkehren~, ~umdrehen~, ~wenden~), but remains current in Swiss German. The derivative ~Rückkehr~ ("return") is the standard noun for returning from a journey or exile.
en.zurückziehen:
From ~zurück-~ + ~ziehen~. Compound of:

- ~ziehen~: From MHG ~ziehen~, from OHG ~ziohan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teuhan~, from Proto-Germanic *~teuhaną~, from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead"). Cognate with English ~tug~, Gothic ~tiuhan~, Dutch ~tijgen~.
- ~zurück-~: From MHG ~zerucke~, a compound of ~zuo~ ("to") + ~rücke~ ("back"), literally "to the back." The first element comes from Proto-Germanic *~tō~, from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to"); the second from MHG ~rücke~, ~rugge~ ("back, ridge"), from Proto-Germanic *~hrugjaz~.

The literal meaning "to pull back" extends to military retreat (~Truppen zurückziehen~), withdrawing money (~Geld zurückziehen~), and the reflexive ~sich zurückziehen~ ("to withdraw, retire"). The PIE root *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead") also gave Latin ~dūcere~ ("to lead"), source of English ~duke~, ~conduct~, ~produce~, and ~educate~ -- all words about pulling or leading in various directions. Cognate with Dutch ~terugtrekken~ ("to pull back").
en.zusammenarbeiten:
Compound of ~zusammen-~ + ~arbeiten~:

- ~arbeiten~: From OHG ~arbeiten~, from Proto-Germanic *~arbaidijaną~ ("to toil, to labor"), ultimately from PIE *~h₃órbʰos~ ("orphan"). Cognate with Dutch ~arbeiden~, Old Saxon ~arvedian~, Gothic ~arbaidjan~.
- ~zusammen-~: From MHG ~zusamen~, from OHG ~zisamane~, from Proto-Germanic *~tō~ + *~samanē~ ("together"), ultimately from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to") + *~sem~ ("one, together"). As a separable prefix, it conveys the sense of doing something jointly.

~Zusammenarbeiten~ means "to collaborate, to work together." The noun ~Zusammenarbeit~ ("collaboration, cooperation") is indispensable in diplomatic, scientific, and corporate contexts. The etymology of ~arbeiten~ is one of the most poignant in all of Germanic: PIE *~h₃órbʰos~ meant "orphan" — a child bereft of parents who must toil for others. This same root gave Greek ~orphanos~ ("orphaned"), Latin ~orbus~ ("deprived"), and English ~orphan~. The journey from "orphan's drudgery" to "work" in general reflects the social reality of the ancient Indo-European world, where the parentless child's lot defined the concept of hard labor. Old High German ~arabeit~ still meant "hardship, toil, suffering" rather than neutral "work" — the modern sense of productive activity is a relatively recent semantic elevation.
en.zusammenfassen:
Compound of ~zusammen~ + ~fassen~:

- ~zusammen~: From MHG ~zesamen~, from OHG ~zisamane~, ultimately from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to give, put") + *~sem~ ("together, one").
- ~fassen~: From MHG ~vazzen~, from OHG ~fazzōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~fatōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~fatōną~ ("to fetch, hold, seize"). Cognate with English ~fetch~, Dutch ~vatten~. The PIE root may be *~pod-~ ("to fetch"), though the connection to *~ped-~ ("to walk, stumble, fall") is semantically uncertain.

The literal image is of grasping things together into one bundle. From this concrete sense, ~zusammenfassen~ developed its primary modern meaning: "to summarize" -- gathering scattered ideas into a single coherent statement. The noun ~Zusammenfassung~ is the standard German word for "summary" or "abstract."
en.zusammenstellen:
Compound of ~zusammen-~ ("together") + ~stellen~ ("to put, place"):

- ~stellen~: From MHG ~stellen~, from OHG ~stellen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stalljan~ ("to put, position"), from PIE *~stel-~ ("to place, put, stand").
- ~zusammen-~: From MHG ~zesamen~, from OHG ~zisamane~, from Proto-Germanic *~tō~ + *~samanē~ ("together"), ultimately from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to") + *~sem~ ("together, one").

The compound literally means "to place together" and hence "to compile, compose, assemble." One ~stellt zusammen~ a team, a menu, a report, or a playlist. The PIE root *~sem~ ("together, one") is extraordinarily productive across the Indo-European family: it gives English ~same~, ~some~, and ~similar~ (via Latin ~similis~), Greek ~homos~ ("same," whence ~homogeneous~), and Sanskrit ~sama~ ("even, equal"). The separable nature of the prefix is visible in main clauses: ~Sie stellt die Liste zusammen~. The derivative ~Zusammenstellung~ ("compilation, composition") is the standard noun for any curated collection.
en.zustimmen:
Compound of ~zu~ + ~stimmen~:

- ~stimmen~: From MHG ~stimmen~, from OHG *~stimmen~ (attested in the compound ~gistimmen~), derived from the noun ~Stimme~ ("voice, vote"). The noun descends from OHG ~stimma~, ~stimna~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stebnu~, from Proto-Germanic *~stebnō~ or *~stamnijō~, from PIE *~stomen-~ ("mouth, muzzle"). Cognate with Gothic ~stibna~ ("voice"), Old English ~stefn~, ~stemn~ ("voice"), Dutch ~stem~, and Greek ~stoma~ ("mouth," whence English ~stomach~).
- ~zu-~: From MHG ~zuo~, from OHG ~zuo~, from Proto-Germanic *~tō~ ("to"), from PIE *~doh₁~ ("to"). As a verbal prefix, it means "to, toward."

The literal sense is "to voice toward," i.e., to add one's voice in support. The verb ~stimmen~ itself has a remarkable range of meanings: "to tune (an instrument)," "to be correct" (~das stimmt!~ "that's right!"), "to vote," and "to put in a mood" (~traurig stimmen~, "to make sad"). All trace back to the idea of a voice being in harmony or accord. The compound ~zustimmen~ narrows this to the specific act of expressing agreement. The related noun ~Zustimmung~ ("consent, approval") is a staple of legal and political German. The connection between ~Stimme~ ("voice") and Greek ~stoma~ ("mouth") reveals that the PIE speakers conceptualized the voice as emanating from the mouth-opening, a straightforward anatomical observation preserved in two wildly divergent word families across five millennia.
en.zwingen:
From Middle High German ~twingen~, from Old High German ~dwingan~, from Proto-Germanic *~þwinganą~, *~þwinhaną~. What appears to be a triple consonant shift (Germanic *~þ~ to OHG ~d~ to MHG ~t~ to modern ~z~) is actually a regular development that affected most words with OHG ~dw-~ and ~tw-~, compare ~Zwerg~ ("dwarf"). A class III strong verb with the ablaut pattern ~zwingen~, ~zwang~, ~gezwungen~. Cognate with Dutch ~dwingen~, Norwegian ~tvinge~, Swedish ~tvinga~, Icelandic ~þvinga~, and English ~twinge~.

The English cognate ~twinge~ — a sharp, sudden pain that grips or squeezes — preserves the original physical sense of the Proto-Germanic verb: to press, to squeeze, to constrain. German ~zwingen~ elevated this physical compression into abstract compulsion: "to force, to compel, to make someone do something." The noun ~Zwang~ ("compulsion, coercion") and the adjective ~zwingend~ ("compelling, cogent") carry the same force. The medieval ~Zwinger~ — the narrow space between inner and outer castle walls where attackers were "squeezed" and trapped — survives today as the name of Dresden's famous ~Zwinger~ palace complex, built on the site of such a fortification.
en.zählen:
From Middle High German ~zel(e)n~, ~zellen~, from Old High German ~zellan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~talljan~, from Proto-Germanic *~taljaną~ ("to enumerate, count; to recount, tell"). The modern long vowel continues the second- and third-person singular of the present (where West Germanic gemination did not occur); it was reinforced by the lengthened vowel in ~Zahl~ (from MHG ~zale~, "number").

The Proto-Germanic root *~taljaną~ carried both senses — "to count" and "to recount, narrate" — from the very beginning. Modern German split these into two verbs: ~zählen~ retained "to count," while ~erzählen~ (via the ~er-~ prefix) specialized in "to narrate." English underwent the same bifurcation: ~tell~ (from the same root) means both "to count" (as in ~teller~ at a bank, or ~telling beads~) and "to narrate." The word ~tale~ ("a story" but also archaic "a count, tally") shares this dual heritage. Cognate with Dutch ~tellen~, English ~tell~ and ~tale~, Danish ~tælle~, Gothic ~talzjan~.
en.ähneln:
A 17th-century alteration of the earlier verb ~ähnlichen~, from Middle High German ~anelīchen~, equivalent to ~ähnlich~ ("similar") + the verbal suffix ~-en~. The modern shortened conjugation in ~-eln~ may reflect a folk-etymological association with ~Ahn~ ("ancestor"), as if "to resemble" were derived from "to take after one's forebears" -- reanalyzed as ~Ahn~ + ~-eln~. Compare French ~s'apparenter à~ ("to be related to"), which shows a similar conceptual link between family resemblance and similarity.

The adjective ~ähnlich~ itself derives from OHG ~analīh~ ("similar"), literally "of one form" (~ana~ "on" + ~līh~ "body, form"), the same compound pattern that produced English ~alike~ and ~like~. The word ~Ahn~ ("ancestor"), despite the folk-etymological association, actually traces to Proto-Germanic *~anô~ ("forefather"), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₂en-~ ("ancestor") -- cognate with Latin ~anus~ ("old woman"), a connection that would surely surprise speakers of both languages.
en.ändern: From Middle High German ~endern~, a denominative verb derived from ~ander~ ("other, different"). ~Ander~ descends from MHG ~ander~, from OHG ~andar~, from Proto-West Germanic *~anþar~, from Proto-Germanic *~anþeraz~, from PIE *~h₂énteros~ ("other"). The PIE root *~h₂énteros~ is a comparative formation from *~h₂ent-~ ("front, forefront"), and its original sense was "the one opposite, the one facing" — i.e., the second of two — before broadening to "other." The semantic journey from "second" to "different" to "to change" completes in ~ändern~. Cognate with Hunsrik ~ennre~, English ~other~, Dutch ~ander~, Old Norse ~annarr~ (Swedish ~annan~, Danish ~anden~).
en.ärgern:
From Middle High German ~argern~ ("to worsen"), from Old High German ~argerōn~, derived from the adjective ~arg~ ("bad, wicked"). Cognate with English ~eerie~.

The adjective ~arg~ traces from MHG ~arc~, from OHG ~arg~, from Proto-West Germanic *~arg~, from Proto-Germanic *~argaz~, from Proto-Indo-European *~h₃erǵʰ-~ ("to copulate; ardent"). The semantic journey from "ardent, lustful" to "bad, wicked" reflects a moral judgment on excessive passion -- what was once merely "passionate" became "wicked" as Germanic Christianity imposed new values on old vocabulary. Cognates include Lithuanian ~eržùs~ ("ardent, stubborn") and Hittite ~arkari~ ("to copulate"). The modern German meaning of ~ärgern~ has softened considerably from "to make worse" to "to annoy, to irritate," and the reflexive ~sich ärgern~ ("to be annoyed") is among the most common expressions of everyday frustration. Dutch ~erg~ ("bad, severe") preserves the older adjective sense.
en.äußern:
From MHG ~ūzeren~ ("to bring out, express"), deadjectival from ~äußer~ ("outer, external"), itself the comparative of ~aus~ / ~außen~ ("out, outside"). ~Aus~ derives from MHG ~ūȥ~, from OHG ~ūȥ~, from Proto-West Germanic *~ūt~, from Proto-Germanic *~ūt~, from PIE *~úd~ ("upwards, away, out"). A doublet of English ~out~; compare Dutch ~uit~, Low German ~ut~, Danish ~ud~.

The meaning of ~äußern~ is "to express, to utter (one's thoughts or feelings)" — to bring something inner to the outside. The cognate English verb ~utter~ (as in "to utter a word") followed the same semantic path from the same root: PGmc *~ūtizô~ (comparative of *~ūt~) yielded both German ~äußer~ and English ~outer~ / ~utter~. The physical image of moving speech outward from within is thus embedded in the very shape of the word.
en.öffnen:
From Middle High German ~offenen~, from Old High German ~offanōn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~opanōn~, from Proto-Germanic *~upanōną~ ("to open, to lift"). The underlying adjective *~upanaz~ ("open") was probably originally a past participle of a lost verb *~ūpaną~, derived from *~upp~ ("up"), with the original sense "lying on top" → "clear, unobstructed" → "open." The Modern German umlaut (~offen~ → ~öffnen~) arose from the verbal suffix, producing the standard denominal pattern of forming a verb from an adjective.

The directional metaphor — "open" as "lifted up" — is shared across many cultures, as in English ~uplift~ (the door or lid is raised, revealing what was underneath). The adjective ~offen~ ("open") and the verb ~öffnen~ ("to open") remain distinct in German, as in the difference between ~das Fenster ist offen~ ("the window is open") and ~öffne das Fenster~ ("open the window"). Cognate with Dutch ~openen~, English ~open~, Danish ~åbne~.
en.üben:
From Middle High German ~üeben~, ~uoben~, from Old High German ~uoben~, from Proto-Germanic *~ōbijaną~ ("to do, practice"), the causative-iterative formation of a lost strong verb *~afaną~, from PIE *~h₃ep-~ ("to labor, work, toil"). The same PIE root gives Latin ~opus~ ("work") and ~opera~ ("effort, service") — whence English ~opera~, ~operate~, and ~opus~. Cognate with Dutch ~oefenen~ (with an ~n~-suffix extension), Old Saxon ~ōbian~, Old Norse *~œfa~ (Icelandic ~æfa~). The Swedish ~öva~ and Danish ~øve~ are borrowed from Middle Low German ~ôven~ rather than inherited.

The verb's core meaning, "to practice, to drill, to exercise," has remained remarkably stable across a thousand years of German. The reflexive ~sich üben in~ ("to practice at, to exercise in") preserves the iterative force of the original Proto-Germanic causative: not a one-time doing but repeated, deliberate effort. The derived noun ~Übung~ ("exercise, practice") and the adjective ~üblich~ ("usual, customary") both reflect this deep association between repeated action and established habit. German proverb: ~Übung macht den Meister~ ("Practice makes the master") — a direct calque of the Latin ~exercitium facit magistrum~.
en.überarbeiten:
Compound of ~über-~ + ~arbeiten~:

- ~arbeiten~: From Old High German ~arbeiten~, from Proto-Germanic *~arbaidijaną~, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *~h₃órbʰos~ ("orphan"), from which English ~orphan~ is also derived. Cognate with Dutch ~arbeiden~, Old Saxon ~arvedian~, Gothic ~arbaidjan~.
- ~über-~: From Middle High German ~über~, from Old High German ~ubar~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Indo-European *~upéri~ ("over, above"). In this compound, the prefix conveys the sense of "over again" or doing something excessively.

The verb has two distinct meanings depending on stress: ~UMarbeiten~ (separable, "to rework") versus ~überARbeiten~ (inseparable, "to revise" or reflexively "to overwork oneself"). The reflexive ~sich überarbeiten~ ("to work oneself to exhaustion") reflects the ~über-~ prefix in its "excessively" sense. The surprising PIE connection between "work" and "orphan" reflects an ancient reality: orphans were society's laborers, and the original sense of the Germanic root was "hardship, toil."
en.übergeben:
~über-~ + ~geben~. Compound of ~über-~ + ~geben~:

- ~geben~: From MHG ~gëben~, from OHG ~gëban~, from Proto-West Germanic *~geban~, from Proto-Germanic *~gebaną~, from PIE *~gʰebʰ-~ ("to give").
- ~über-~: From PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). The inseparable prefix adds a sense of transfer across a boundary.

~Übergeben~ means "to hand over, to deliver, to transfer" — and, colloquially, "to vomit." The formal sense is transparent: to give ~over~, to pass something across from one person to another. The colloquial meaning follows the same spatial metaphor — to ~give over~ the contents of one's stomach — and is shared by Dutch ~overgeven~, which means both "to surrender" and "to throw up." The inseparable prefix ~über-~ creates a fixed, non-literal meaning; contrast the separable ~übergeben~ (stressed on ~über~), which would mean "to give excessively" but is rarely used. The noun ~Übergabe~ ("handover, transfer") is common in legal and military contexts. English ~overgive~ existed in Middle English with the same sense of "to surrender" but has since fallen out of use, replaced by the French-derived ~surrender~. Cognate with Dutch ~overgeven~, English ~overgive~ (archaic).
en.übergehen:
Compound of ~über-~ + ~gehen~:

- ~gehen~: From Middle High German ~gān~, ~gēn~, from Old High German ~gān~, from Proto-West Germanic *~gān~, from Proto-Germanic *~gāną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~ǵʰeh₁-~ ("to leave"). Cognate with Dutch ~gaan~, English ~go~, Swedish ~gå~.
- ~über-~: From Middle High German ~über~, from Old High German ~ubar~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Indo-European *~upéri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix it conveys "passing over" or "skipping."

Like many ~über-~ verbs, ~übergehen~ splits into two distinct words depending on stress. The separable ~ÜBERgehen~ means "to go over, to pass into" (as in ~in eine neue Phase übergehen~, "to transition into a new phase"). The inseparable ~überGEHen~ means "to skip over, to ignore" (as in ~jemanden übergehen~, "to pass someone over"). This stress-based semantic distinction is a hallmark of German prefix verbs that challenges learners and delights linguists.
en.überlassen:
Compound of ~über-~ + ~lassen~:

- ~lassen~: From MHG ~lāzen~, ~lān~, from OHG ~lāȥan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~lātan~ ("to let, allow"), from Proto-Germanic *~lētaną~ ("to leave behind, allow"), from PIE *~leh₁d-~ ("to let, leave behind"). Cognate with English ~let~, Dutch ~laten~. Related to Latin ~lassus~ ("tired") -- the connection being the sense of "letting go" from weariness.
- ~über-~: From MHG ~über-~, from OHG ~ubar-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber-~, from PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix (stressed on ~-lassen~), it conveys "over to someone."

Like ~übersetzen~, ~überlassen~ splits by stress. As an inseparable verb (stress on ~-lassen~: ~er überlässt~), it means "to relinquish, leave to someone, leave up to someone." As a separable verb (stress on ~über-~: ~er lässt über~), it is an informal alternative to ~übriglassen~ ("to leave over, spare"). The inseparable sense captures a deliberate act of ceding control -- one "leaves something over" to another person's judgment or possession. The PIE root *~leh₁d-~ connects ~lassen~ not only to English ~let~ but also to Latin ~lassus~ ("weary"), suggesting that the original Proto-Indo-European concept involved the weariness of releasing one's grip.
en.überleben:
Compound of ~über-~ + ~leben~, calqued from Latin ~supervīvō~:

- ~leben~: From MHG ~lëben~, from OHG ~lebēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~libbjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~libjaną~, from PIE *~leyp-~ ("to leave, cling, linger"). The semantic path from "cling, remain" to "be alive" reflects a view of life as persistence. Cognate with English ~live~, Dutch ~leven~, Swedish ~leva~, Gothic ~liban~.
- ~über-~: From MHG ~über~, from OHG ~ubar~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above").

The fact that ~überleben~ is a calque of Latin ~supervīvō~ (itself from ~super-~ + ~vīvō~) makes it a structural parallel to English ~survive~ (from Old French, ultimately from the same Latin source). Three languages, three different root verbs for "live," but the same metaphorical prefix meaning "over, beyond."
en.überlegen:
From Middle High German ~überlegen~, past participle of ~überligen~. By surface analysis, ~über-~ + ~legen~.

- ~legen~: From MHG ~legen~, from OHG ~leggen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~laggjan~, from Proto-Germanic *~lagjaną~ ("to lay, cause to lie"), from PIE *~logʰéyeti~. A causative denominative: "to cause to lie." Cognate with English ~lay~, Dutch ~leggen~.
- ~über-~: From MHG ~über~, from OHG ~ubiri~, ~ubari~, from Proto-Germanic *~ubiri~, from PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix in the "to ponder" sense, ~über-~ lends the sense of turning something over in the mind — laying thought upon thought.

~Überlegen~ has two distinct existences in Modern German, distinguished only by stress and separability. The inseparable verb (stress on ~-legen~) means "to reflect, consider, ponder" — one lays thoughts over a problem. The adjective ~überlegen~ (also stress on ~-legen~, but derived differently) means "superior, dominant." The two arose independently: the verb from the idea of placing something mentally "over" a problem, the adjective from the literal sense of lying over or on top of someone (as in a wrestling hold). The MHG past participle ~überlegen~ from ~überligen~ ("to lie over, overpower") gave rise to the adjective; the thoughtful verb derives from ~Überlegung~ ("reflection, consideration"). The noun ~Überlegung~ is the standard German word for deliberate, careful consideration — "after much ~Überlegung~" is a formula in formal prose. Cognate with Dutch ~overleggen~.
en.übermitteln:
A relatively modern formation, coined in the early twentieth century. Compound of ~über-~ + ~mitteln~:

- ~mitteln~: A denominative verb from ~Mittel~ ("means, medium"), from MHG ~mittel~, from OHG ~mittil~, from Proto-Germanic *~midlą~ or *~midilą~ ("middle"), cognate with English ~middle~ and Dutch ~middel~.
- ~über-~: From MHG ~über-~, from OHG ~ubar-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uberi-~, from PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). This prefix adds the sense of transmitting across a distance or barrier.

The verb means "to convey, to transmit, to communicate," literally "to mediate (something) across." It is related to the older verb ~vermitteln~ ("to mediate, to arrange"), which uses the ~ver-~ prefix instead. The noun ~Mittel~ also gives rise to ~Mittelalter~ ("Middle Ages"), ~Mittelpunkt~ ("center"), and ~Lebensmittel~ ("groceries," literally "means of life"). Despite its recent coinage, ~übermitteln~ filled a genuine lexical gap, providing a formal verb for the transmission of messages, data, or information across channels. English speakers might use "to transmit" or "to convey" in the same contexts.
en.übernehmen:
Compound of ~über-~ + ~nehmen~:

- ~nehmen~: From MHG ~nëmen~, from OHG ~nëman~, from Proto-West Germanic *~neman~, from Proto-Germanic *~nemaną~, from PIE *~nem-~ ("to give or take one's due").
- ~über-~: From MHG ~über~, from OHG ~ubiri~, ~ubari~, from Proto-Germanic *~ubiri~, from PIE *~upéri~, ultimately from *~upo~ + *~-i~. Meaning "over, across, above." Cognate with Ancient Greek ~hupér~ ("above"), Latin ~super~ ("above"), Sanskrit ~upári~, Dutch ~over~, English ~over~, Swedish ~över~.

The semantic range of ~übernehmen~ illustrates how "taking over" can mean taking on responsibility (~eine Aufgabe übernehmen~), assuming control (~ein Unternehmen übernehmen~), or adopting something (~eine Idee übernehmen~). The verb can also be reflexive — ~sich übernehmen~ means "to overextend oneself," a sense that turns the completive force of ~über-~ against the subject. Cognate with Dutch ~overnemen~.
en.überprüfen:
From ~über-~ + ~prüfen~:

- ~prüfen~: From MHG ~prüeven~, ultimately from Latin ~probāre~ ("to test, approve"), a denominative of ~probus~ ("good, upright"), from PIE *~pro-bʰuH-wó-s~ ("being foremost"). Entered German before the ~-ieren~ suffix was productive, fully assimilated into the native weak-verb system. Cognate with English ~prove~ and ~probe~.
- ~über-~: From MHG ~über~, from OHG ~ubiri~, ~ubari~, from Proto-Germanic *~ubiri~, from PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). Here the prefix adds thoroughness and coverage — to check something over, to go over it comprehensively.

~Überprüfen~ ("to check over, verify, review, audit") extends ~prüfen~'s sense of testing with the thoroughness of ~über-~: where ~prüfen~ tests a student or a hypothesis, ~überprüfen~ reviews a complete file, audits an account, or re-examines a decision from above. The verb is ubiquitous in administrative and legal German. That its root ~prüfen~ derives ultimately from Latin ~probus~ ("upright, good") means that every act of ~überprüfen~ is etymologically an inquiry into whether something meets the standard of uprightness. Cognate with Dutch ~controleren~ (parallel function, different root).
en.überraschen:
~über-~ + ~raschen~. Compound of ~über-~ + ~raschen~:

- ~raschen~: A now-rare verb derived from the adjective ~rasch~ ("quick, swift"), from MHG ~rasch~, ~rasche~, from OHG ~rask~, ~rasc~ ("quick, prompt, powerful"), from Proto-Germanic *~raskuz~, possibly from PIE *~Hreth₂-~ ("to run, roll").
- ~über-~: From PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix here, ~über-~ conveys the sense of coming upon someone from above or beyond expectation.

~Überraschen~ means "to surprise" — literally "to rush over someone quickly," capturing the sudden, overwhelming quality of surprise. The adjective ~rasch~ ("quick, swift") survives independently in modern German, but the verb ~raschen~ from which ~überraschen~ was formed is essentially extinct outside this compound. The noun ~Überraschung~ ("surprise") is one of the most common German nouns. English ~rash~ (meaning "hasty, impetuous") is a cognate of ~rasch~, borrowed into English from Middle Low German or Middle Dutch — so when someone acts ~rashly~, they are being etymologically ~rasch~. The underlying image of surprise as speed — something happening too fast to anticipate — is preserved intact in this compound.
en.überreichen:
Compound of ~über-~ + ~reichen~:

- ~reichen~: From Middle High German ~reichen~, from Old High German ~reihhen~, related to the adjective ~reich~ ("rich, powerful"). The adjective traces to Old High German ~rīhhi~, from Proto-Germanic *~rīkijaz~, a borrowing from Celtic *~rīgos~ ("king"), from Proto-Indo-European *~h₃rēǵs~ ("king, ruler"), also the source of Latin ~rēx~. The verb's original sense was "to make powerful, to extend," hence "to reach, to hand over."
- ~über-~: From Middle High German ~über~, from Old High German ~ubar~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Indo-European *~upéri~ ("over, above"). Here the prefix conveys the sense of handing something across or over to someone.

~Überreichen~ is always inseparable and carries a formal, ceremonial tone -- one ~überreicht~ an award, a gift, or a document. The more casual equivalent is simply ~geben~ ("to give"). Cognate with Dutch ~overhandigen~, which uses a different metaphor ("over-handing") for the same formal concept.
en.überschreiten:
Compound of ~über-~ + ~schreiten~. Compare Dutch ~overschrijden~, Swedish ~överskrida~, Danish ~overskride~:

- ~schreiten~: From MHG ~schrīten~, from OHG ~skrītan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~skrīþan~ ("to step, stride"), from Proto-Germanic *~skrīþaną~ ("to walk, glide, crawl"), from PIE *~(s)kreyt-~ ("to stride"), an extension of *~(s)krey-~ ("to go about"). Cognate with Lithuanian ~skrìsti~ ("to rush, run"), Latvian ~skrìet~ ("to run, fly"). Related to ~Schritt~ ("step").
- ~über-~: From MHG ~über-~, from OHG ~ubar-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber-~, from PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix here, it conveys "across, beyond."

The semantic range of ~überschreiten~ mirrors the physical act of stepping beyond a boundary: "to cross" (a border, a threshold) and "to exceed" (a limit, a deadline). The verb preserves the formal, elevated register of its base verb ~schreiten~, which in modern German sounds literary compared to the everyday ~gehen~ -- one ~schreitet~ through a palace, one ~geht~ to the store. The Proto-Germanic ancestor *~skrīþaną~ originally meant "to walk, glide, crawl," a broad range of locomotion that narrowed to stately striding in German while Old Norse ~skríða~ retained the "crawl, slide" sense (whence Icelandic ~skríða~ "landslide").
en.übersehen:
Compound of ~über-~ + ~sehen~:

- ~sehen~: From MHG ~sëhen~, from OHG ~sëhan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sehwan~, from Proto-Germanic *~sehwaną~ ("to see"), from PIE *~sekʷ-~ ("to see, to notice"). Cognate with English ~see~, Dutch ~zien~, Danish ~se~, Gothic ~saihwan~.
- ~über-~: From MHG ~über~, from OHG ~ubari~, from Proto-Germanic *~ubiri~, from PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix here, it conveys "over" in the sense of looking past something.

~Übersehen~ means "to overlook, to miss" — literally to see over something and thus past it. This is an inseparable compound (stress on ~-seh-~): ~Ich habe den Fehler übersehen~ ("I overlooked the error"). Contrast with the separable ~übersehen~ (stress on ~über-~), which means "to survey, to have an overview of" — ~Man kann das ganze Tal übersehen~ ("One can survey the entire valley"). This stress-dependent meaning split is a hallmark of German prefix verbs: the same written form produces antonymic senses depending on whether the prefix is separable (stressed) or inseparable (unstressed).
en.übersetzen:
Sixteenth-century formation, probably modeled on Latin ~trādūcere~ ("to lead across, translate"), perhaps through Middle Low German ~ōversetten~. Compound of ~über-~ + ~setzen~:

- ~setzen~: From MHG ~setzen~, from OHG ~sezzen~, from Proto-West Germanic *~sattjan~, a causative derived from Proto-Germanic *~sitjaną~ ("to sit"). Cognate with English ~set~, Dutch ~zetten~.
- ~über-~: From MHG ~über-~, from OHG ~ubar-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber-~, from PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). As an inseparable prefix (stressed on the second syllable), it conveys "across" in an abstract sense.

German ~übersetzen~ is a fascinating case of stress distinguishing meaning. With stress on ~über-~ (separable prefix: ~er setzt über~), it means "to cross, ferry across" -- literally to set something over a body of water. With stress on ~-setzen~ (inseparable prefix: ~er übersetzt~), it means "to translate." The separable sense has Old High German roots (~ubarsezzen~), while the inseparable "translate" sense is a sixteenth-century calque of Latin ~trādūcere~, which similarly meant both "to lead across" physically and "to translate" metaphorically. This Latin-inspired semantic extension made German ~übersetzen~ part of a pan-European pattern: compare French ~traduire~, Italian ~tradurre~, all from the same Latin source. Cognate with Dutch ~overzetten~ (separable sense) and English ~overset~ (archaic).
en.überstehen:
Compound of ~über-~ + ~stehen~:

- ~stehen~: From Middle High German ~stān~, ~stēn~, from Old High German ~stān~, from Proto-West Germanic *~stān~, from Proto-Germanic *~stāną~, from Proto-Indo-European *~steh₂-~ ("to stand"). Cognate with English ~stand~, Dutch ~staan~, Latin ~stāre~.
- ~über-~: From Middle High German ~über~, from Old High German ~ubar~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Indo-European *~upéri~ ("over, above"). Here the prefix conveys surviving through or getting past something.

The inseparable verb ~überSTEHen~ means "to survive, to endure, to get through" -- literally "to stand over" a difficulty. The separable ~ÜBERstehen~ means "to protrude, to jut out." The survival sense captures a vivid image: standing firm while hardship passes over you, still standing when it is done. The past participle ~überstanden~ appears frequently in the relieved exclamation ~Das wäre überstanden!~ ("That's over and done with!").
en.übertragen:
From Middle High German ~übertragen~, at least in part a calque of Latin ~transferre~. Analyzable as ~über-~ + ~tragen~:

- ~tragen~: From MHG ~tragen~, from OHG ~tragan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~dragan~, from Proto-Germanic *~draganą~ ("to draw, drag, carry"), from PIE *~dʰregʰ-~ ("to pull, draw, drag"). A class VI strong verb. Cognate with English ~draw~ and ~drag~, Dutch ~dragen~, Icelandic ~draga~.
- ~über-~: From MHG ~über~, from OHG ~ubari~, from Proto-West Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). Cognate with English ~over~, Dutch ~over~, Latin ~super~, Greek ~ὑπέρ~ (~hyper~).

~Übertragen~ is a Germanic calque of Latin ~transferre~ (~trans-~ + ~ferre~, "to carry across"), and the parallel is exact: ~über-~ ("over, across") + ~tragen~ ("to carry") = ~transferre~. English ~transfer~ is the Latin borrowing; ~übertragen~ is the native Germanic translation of the same concept. The verb's semantic range is wide: to transfer funds, to broadcast a signal, to transmit a disease, and — in the phrase ~im übertragenen Sinne~ ("in the figurative sense") — to carry a meaning metaphorically from one domain to another. That last usage is itself an act of ~übertragen~: figurative language carries a word across from its literal territory. Compare English ~carry over~, Dutch ~overdragen~. Cognate with Dutch ~overdragen~.
en.übertreffen:
From Middle High German ~übertreffen~. Compound of ~über-~ + ~treffen~:

- ~treffen~: From Middle High German ~treffen~, from Old High German ~treffan~, from Proto-West Germanic *~drepan~, from Proto-Germanic *~drepaną~ ("to strike, hit"). Cognate with English ~drub~ ("to beat"). The semantic shift from "to hit, strike" to "to meet" (the primary modern sense of ~treffen~) happened because meeting someone was conceived as "striking upon" them.
- ~über-~: From Middle High German ~über~, from Old High German ~ubar~, from Proto-Germanic *~uber~, from Proto-Indo-European *~upéri~ ("over, above"). Here the prefix conveys exceeding or surpassing.

~Übertreffen~ is always inseparable and means "to surpass, to exceed, to outdo." The metaphor is hitting above or beyond the mark. The reflexive ~sich selbst übertreffen~ ("to surpass oneself") is common in both praise and ironic criticism.
en.überwachen:
Compound of ~über-~ + ~wachen~:

- ~wachen~: From MHG ~wachen~, from OHG ~wahhēn~, from Proto-West Germanic *~wakēn~, from Proto-Germanic *~wakāną~ ("to be awake, to watch"), from PIE *~weǵ-~ ("to wake, to be strong, to be lively"). Cognate with English ~wake~ and ~watch~.
- ~über-~: From MHG ~über-~, from OHG ~ubar-~, from Proto-Germanic *~uberi-~, from PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). The prefix adds the sense of watching over something from above, implying authority and control.

The PIE root *~weǵ-~ connects wakefulness with vigor and strength, reflecting the ancient understanding that being alert was itself a form of power. ~Überwachen~ means "to supervise, to monitor, to surveil," and its noun ~Überwachung~ ("surveillance") has acquired pointed political connotations in German, especially given the country's experiences with state surveillance under both the Nazi regime and the East German Stasi. The term ~Überwachungsstaat~ ("surveillance state") is a powerful compound that resonates deeply in German political discourse. English ~watch~ and ~wake~ are direct Germanic cousins, and the connection extends to Latin ~vigil~ ("watchful") and ~vigor~ ("liveliness"), both from the same PIE root. The related adjective ~wach~ ("awake") preserves the simplest form of this ancient word.
en.überwinden:
From Middle High German ~überwinden~, from Old High German ~ubarwintan~, an originally Upper German form alongside Central German ~überwinnen~, ~ubarwinnan~. The ~nn~-form descends from Proto-West Germanic *~ubarwinnan~. Compound of ~über-~ + ~winden~ (or possibly ~winnen~):

- ~winden~: From MHG ~winden~, from OHG ~wintan~, from Proto-Germanic *~windaną~ ("to wind, to wrap"), from PIE *~wendʰ-~ ("to turn, wind, braid"). Cognate with English ~wind~ (the verb, "to coil"), Dutch ~winden~, Danish ~vinde~.
- ~über-~: From PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). The prefix conveys mastery and supremacy — to come over and above an obstacle.

Whether ~überwinden~ derives from ~winden~ ("to wind, twist") or from the now-lost simplex ~winnen~ ("to win," surviving only in the compound ~gewinnen~) is one of the more intriguing puzzles in German word history. The ~nd~ form may be a mere phonetic alteration of the ~nn~ form, or it may reflect a genuine reanalysis connecting the verb to ~winden~ — twisting free from an opponent's grip as a metaphor for overcoming. Isolated MHG compounds like ~anewinden~ ("to attack") and ~widerwinden~ ("to repulse") support the fighting sense for ~winden~. Modern ~überwinden~ means "to overcome, to get over, to surmount": one overcomes fear (~Angst überwinden~), conquers obstacles (~Hindernisse überwinden~), and gets over grief (~Trauer überwinden~). The reflexive ~sich überwinden~ ("to bring oneself to, to force oneself") captures the internal struggle beautifully. Cognate with Dutch ~overwinnen~ ("to conquer, to defeat").
en.überzeugen:
From ~über-~ + ~zeugen~:

- ~zeugen~: From MHG ~ziugen~, ~geziugen~, from OHG ~giziugon~, a denominative of ~Zeug~ ("stuff, gear, equipment, tools"). ~Zeug~ itself comes from MHG ~ziuc~, from OHG ~giziug~, from Proto-West Germanic *~teug~, from Proto-Germanic *~teugą~. This is the nominal derivative of *~teuhaną~ ("to pull, lead"), which also underlies ~ziehen~ ("to pull, draw"), from PIE *~dewk-~ ("to pull, lead").
- ~über-~: From MHG ~über~, from OHG ~ubari~, ~ubar~, from Proto-Germanic *~ubar~, from PIE *~upéri~ ("over, above"). The prefix here carries the sense of mastery or going beyond — convincing someone by rising above their resistance.

The original sense of ~zeugen~ was "to bear witness, to attest" — itself from the earlier meaning of producing or showing one's ~Zeug~ (one's evidence, one's gear). ~Überzeugen~ therefore literally means "to over-witness" or "to attest so thoroughly that one overcomes the other's doubt." The noun ~Zeuge~ ("witness") and the verb ~zeugen~ ("to beget, to produce") are related — all sharing the core idea of bringing something forth or pulling it into being. A curious English near-parallel: ~convince~ ultimately derives from Latin ~vincere~ ("to conquer"), reflecting the same metaphor of overcoming resistance through argument. Cognate with Dutch ~overtuigen~.
